lessons.title: Management of Acute Coronary Syndrome
lessons.subtitle: Facilitator-led chalk talk · 20–25 min (core) / 35–45 min (with cases) · IM attendings & senior residents teaching residents
objectives: Select appropriate early medical interventions for oxygenation, pain, antiplatelet, and anticoagulation in patients presenting with ACS.
Determine timing of revascularization in STEMI vs NSTEMI, with and without high-risk features.
Select appropriate discharge medical therapies for ACS based on ACS type, revascularization method, and comorbidities.

prework.q: 1. A patient with STEMI arrives at a hospital with NO cath lab. What single time threshold decides whether you transfer for PCI vs give fibrinolytics?
prework.a: If the patient can reach a PCI-capable hospital with a door-to-balloon time <120 min, transfer for primary PCI. If not, give fibrinolytics within 90 min (no contraindications), then transfer for delayed angiography (3–24 h).

prework.q: 2. Name the four "very high-risk" NSTEMI features that mandate immediate angiography (<2 h), regardless of GRACE/TIMI score.
prework.a: Cardiogenic shock; hemodynamic instability (e.g., acute MR or VSD); refractory angina despite maximal medical therapy; unstable arrhythmia (VT/VF).

prework.q: 3. Which two ACS medications carry a 30-day mortality benefit but are started before discharge rather than acutely?
prework.a: ACE inhibitor/ARB (all STEMI; NSTEMI with LVEF <40%, HTN, DM, or CKD) and an MRA (LVEF <40%, HF, or DM — once tolerating a beta-blocker + ACEi/ARB).

segments.label: Hook
segments.html: "It's 2 a.m. A 58-year-old grabs his chest in triage. In the next 90 minutes you'll make four or five decisions that decide how much heart muscle he keeps. Let's build the timeline of ACS care — the same spine for STEMI, NSTEMI, and unstable angina — so you never freeze at the bedside." Project the timeline slide: O → A → H/E → Pain → DC, with 0 / 90 min / 2 h / 24 h / 48 h / 72 h tick marks.

segments.label: Segment 1 — Early treatment, identical for all ACS
segments.html:
ASK — "Before you know the troponin or the cath plan, what do you give everyone with suspected ACS?" (Let them build the list; reveal the timeline letters one at a time.)
AgentKey pointWatch out
Aspirin325 mg chewed (or PR) once, then 81 mg daily. All chest pain.Active major bleeding, true hypersensitivity.
AnticoagulationHeparin if PCI planned (quick on/off). Enoxaparin if medical management of NSTEMI (↓ death/MI/recurrent angina vs heparin — ESSENCE 1997).HIT, pork hypersensitivity; avoid enoxaparin if CrCl <30.
Nitroglycerin1st-line anti-anginal. SL 0.4 mg q5min ×3; escalate to IV drip if persistent.RV/inferior MI, SBP <90, severe AS, HCM, recent PDE5 inhibitor (24 h).
Beta-blockerInitiate within 24 h (PO > IV). Improves mortality.Cardiogenic shock, HR <45, high-grade AV block, SBP <100.
MorphineLast-line — only when maxed on nitrates + beta-blocker.Increases mortality and masks refractory angina.
GP IIb/IIIa (eptifibatide)Not routine. High thrombus burden, under interventionalist direction.Bleeding; need for it = consider urgent PCI.
Pearl — oxygen is not benign. Give supplemental O2 only for SpO2 <90%, respiratory distress, or other high-risk hypoxemia. Routine O2 in normoxic STEMI may be harmful (AVOID trial, 2015). (The original board read "SpO2 >90%" — corrected to <90%.)

segments.label: Segment 2 — Timing of revascularization
segments.html:
STEMI = time is myocardium. Click "risk stratification" only after the STEMI branch.

ASK — "STEMI at a PCI-capable hospital: what's the target, and when does the target change?"
  • PCI-capable hospital: primary PCI within 90 min (door-to-balloon), if symptom onset <12 h.
  • Non-PCI hospital: transfer for PCI if achievable in <120 min; otherwise fibrinolytics <90 min then transfer for delayed angiography (3–24 h). PCI beats lytics — lytics fail to reperfuse ~35% and carry higher death/MI/stroke/bleed.
  • Onset >12 h: still PCI unless totally occluded infarct with no ongoing ischemia.
ASK — "NSTEMI: you can't activate the lab for everyone. How do you decide who goes now vs who can wait?"
Risk tierTriggerStrategy
Very highCardiogenic shock, hemodynamic instability, refractory angina, VT/VFImmediate PCI <2 h
HighGRACE >140, TIMI 6–7, new ST depression, rising troponin, age >75Early invasive <24 h
Mod–lowGRACE 109–140, TIMI 3–5Delayed invasive 25–72 h (no mortality difference vs <24 h)
Very lowGRACE <109, TIMI 0–1Ischemia-guided — medical mgmt; angiography only if refractory angina, objective ischemia, or high prognostic risk
Pearl. GRACE estimates death within 6 months / in-hospital; TIMI estimates 14-day composite (death, MI, urgent revascularization). Use either to anchor the conversation — they sort, they don't dictate.

segments.label: Segment 3 — Late / discharge therapies
segments.html:
ASK — "Post-PCI, the patient is stable. Walk me through every pill that should be on the discharge list and why."
AgentWhenIndication / caveat
High-intensity statin<24 hAtorvastatin 80 / rosuvastatin 20–40 — all ACS; plaque stabilization + anti-inflammatory (MIRACL 2001).
P2Y12 inhibitorpost-PCI / DAPTTicagrelor or prasugrel preferred over clopidogrel; ~12 mo DAPT. Hold 5 d pre-CABG.
ACE inhibitor/ARBbefore d/cAll STEMI; NSTEMI if LVEF <40%, HTN, DM, or CKD. 30-day mortality benefit.
MRAbefore d/cLVEF <40%, HF, or DM — once tolerating BB + ACEi/ARB. Watch K⁺ and renal function.

segments.label: Retrieval close
segments.html: ASK — Erase the board. "Rebuild the ACS timeline from memory: what's given to everyone, what decides revascularization timing for STEMI vs NSTEMI, and what gets added before discharge?" Have the team reconstruct the three take-home points aloud.

cases.title: Case 1 — 61 y/o, HTN/DM/HLD, chest pain. T 36.4, HR 114, BP 105/78, SpO₂ 94%. ECG: ST elevations I, aVL, V1–V4.
cases.reveal: Emergently. Anterior LAD STEMI — activate the cath lab, primary PCI within 90 min at a PCI-capable hospital.

cases.title: Case 2 — 45 y/o, DM2, crushing substernal pain. HR 110, BP 82/45, SpO₂ 82% RA. ECG: ST depressions V2–V4.
cases.reveal: Nitrates, morphine, and beta-blockers are contraindicated with hypotension. Consider GP IIb/IIIa (eptifibatide) under interventionalist guidance.

cases.title: Case 3 — 41 y/o, anxiety, one episode of chest pain. HR 85, BP 145/85, SpO₂ 94% RA. ECG: precordial T-wave inversions. hs-troponin I 25 → 110 (H) → 15 ng/L.
cases.reveal: NSTEMI — elevated/rising troponin + chest pain + ECG change. (Original case listed troponin in μg/dL; corrected to ng/L.)

mcqs.stem: A 60-year-old man with chest pain has an ECG showing 2-mm ST elevation in II, III, aVF. He arrives at a hospital without a cath lab. The nearest PCI center is a confirmed 150-minute door-to-balloon transfer. SBP 130, no bleeding history. Best next step?
opts: Administer fibrinolytics now, then transfer for angiography in 3–24 h
Start heparin and observe for spontaneous reperfusion
Give morphine and arrange outpatient stress test
mcqs.exp:
B. Inferior STEMI; transfer would exceed the 120-min threshold, so fibrinolytics within 90 min (no contraindications) followed by transfer for delayed angiography is correct.

A — a 150-min transfer misses the <120-min window, so lytics first. C — "wait and see" abandons reperfusion in an evolving STEMI. D — outpatient management of an active STEMI is dangerous and morphine masks ischemia.

mcqs.stem: A 72-year-old woman with NSTEMI has GRACE 165, new ST depression, and a rising troponin. She is pain-free and hemodynamically stable on medical therapy. Optimal timing of angiography?
mcqs.exp:
B. High-risk NSTEMI features (GRACE >140, new ST depression, dynamic troponin) → early invasive within 24 h.

A — reserved for very-high-risk features (shock, instability, refractory angina, VT/VF), none present. C — for mod-low risk (GRACE 109–140). D — for very low risk (GRACE <109, TIMI 0–1).

mcqs.stem: A 55-year-old man is admitted with NSTEMI, BP 84/52, cool extremities, lactate 4.2. Echo shows a new large anterior wall motion abnormality. Most appropriate revascularization timing?
opts: Delayed invasive at 48 h after diuresis
mcqs.exp:
C. Cardiogenic shock is a very-high-risk feature mandating immediate angiography regardless of risk score.

A/B — delaying revascularization in shock increases mortality. D — fibrinolytics are for STEMI when timely PCI is unavailable, not for NSTEMI, and are contraindicated in shock.

mcqs.stem: A 64-year-old undergoes successful DES to the RCA for STEMI. Discharge EF is 35%; he has type 2 diabetes, Cr 1.1, K 4.2, and tolerates a beta-blocker and lisinopril. Which agent provides additional 30-day mortality benefit and should be added before discharge?
mcqs.exp:
B. An MRA (spironolactone/eplerenone) is indicated post-MI with LVEF <40%, HF, or diabetes once the patient tolerates a beta-blocker + ACEi/ARB, and confers a 30-day mortality benefit (EPHESUS).

A — amlodipine has no mortality benefit in ACS. C — digoxin is for rate/symptom control, not mortality. D — diuretics treat congestion, not mortality.

mcqs.stem: Per the 2025 ACC/AHA ACS guideline, which lipid strategy is recommended for a patient whose LDL-C is 92 mg/dL despite maximally tolerated high-intensity statin after MI?
opts: No change; statin alone is sufficient
Switch to a moderate-intensity statin to reduce side effects
Add a non-statin agent (e.g., ezetimibe or a PCSK9 inhibitor)
Stop the statin and start niacin
mcqs.exp:
C. The 2025 guideline recommends adding a non-statin (ezetimibe, evolocumab, alirocumab, inclisiran, or bempedoic acid) when LDL-C ≥70 mg/dL on maximally tolerated statin.

A — LDL ≥70 on max statin warrants escalation. B — de-escalating intensity post-ACS is wrong. D — niacin is not a recommended add-on; it lacks outcome benefit and the statin should continue.

anki: Early ACS therapy given to ALL patients (no contraindications)?
Aspirin 325 mg chewed, anticoagulation (heparin if PCI planned / enoxaparin if medical mgmt), nitroglycerin, beta-blocker <24 h; morphine last-line.
When is supplemental oxygen indicated in ACS?
SpO2 <90%, respiratory distress, or high-risk hypoxemia. Routine O2 in normoxic STEMI may be harmful (AVOID 2015).
Heparin vs enoxaparin in ACS — when each?
Heparin if PCI planned (quick on/off peri-procedure). Enoxaparin for medical management of NSTEMI (lowers death/MI/angina vs heparin, ESSENCE).
Contraindications to nitroglycerin in ACS?
RV/inferior MI, SBP <90, severe AS, HCM, PDE5 inhibitor within 24 h.
Why is morphine last-line in ACS?
Increases mortality and masks refractory angina (an indication for urgent PCI).
STEMI at a PCI-capable hospital — reperfusion target?
Primary PCI within 90 min (door-to-balloon), if onset <12 h.
STEMI at a non-PCI hospital — transfer vs lytics?
Transfer if PCI achievable <120 min; else fibrinolytics <90 min then transfer for angiography (3-24 h).
NSTEMI very-high-risk features that mandate immediate PCI (<2 h)?
Cardiogenic shock, hemodynamic instability, refractory angina, unstable arrhythmia (VT/VF).
NSTEMI high-risk features → early invasive (<24 h)?
GRACE >140, TIMI 6-7, new ST depression, rising troponin, age >75.
NSTEMI very-low-risk strategy?
GRACE <109 or TIMI 0-1 → ischemia-guided: medical mgmt, angiography only if refractory angina / objective ischemia / high prognostic risk.
GRACE vs TIMI — what each estimates?
GRACE: in-hospital / 6-month mortality. TIMI: 14-day composite (death, MI, urgent revascularization).
Two ACS meds started within 24 h (beyond early Tx)?
High-intensity statin and a P2Y12 inhibitor (post-PCI).
Preferred P2Y12 inhibitor in ACS (2025 guideline)?
Ticagrelor or prasugrel over clopidogrel; default DAPT >=12 mo if not high bleeding risk.
ACEi/ARB indications in ACS?
All STEMI; NSTEMI if LVEF <40%, HTN, DM, or CKD. 30-day mortality benefit.
MRA indications post-ACS?
LVEF <40%, HF, or DM — once tolerating beta-blocker + ACEi/ARB. Watch K+/renal.
2025 ACS guideline — lipid escalation rule?
High-intensity statin for all; add non-statin (ezetimibe/PCSK9i/inclisiran/bempedoic acid) if LDL-C >=70 on max statin. Radial > femoral access.
lessons.dot:
#ACS: * [STEMI / NSTEMI / UA], * [anterior/inferior/lateral/posterior] territory, sx onset * h ago. Killip * [I-IV]. likely 2/2 plaque rupture; r/o aortic dissection, PE, perimyocarditis, Takotsubo, demand ischemia (type 2 MI).
risk: GRACE * (→ * % in-hosp/6-mo death) | TIMI * (→ * % 14-d composite). very-high-risk features: shock / hemodynamic instab / refractory angina / VT-VF = * [none/present].
EARLY (all ACS): ASA 325 chewed → 81 qd *; anticoag * [heparin gtt if PCI planned / enoxaparin 1mg/kg q12 if med mgmt, hold CrCl<30]; NTG * [SL 0.4 q5 x3 → IV gtt] (hold RV/inferior MI, SBP<90, AS, HCM, PDE5i<24h); BB * [metoprolol] <24h (hold shock/HR<45/AVB/SBP<100); morphine PRN last-line; O2 only SpO2<90.
REVASC: STEMI → PCI <90 min if PCI-capable; if not, transfer if D2B <120 else lytics <90 → angio 3-24h. NSTEMI → * [immediate <2h (very-high) / early invasive <24h (GRACE>140, ST dep, ↑trop, age>75) / delayed 25-72h (GRACE 109-140) / ischemia-guided (GRACE<109, TIMI 0-1)]. radial > femoral; complete revasc.
LATE/DISCHARGE: high-intensity statin * [atorva 80 / rosuva 20-40] <24h (add ezetimibe/PCSK9i if LDL≥70 on max); P2Y12 * [ticagrelor 90 BID / prasugrel / clopidogrel] x ~12mo DAPT (hold 5d pre-CABG); ACEi/ARB * [lisinopril] (all STEMI; NSTEMI if EF<40/HTN/DM/CKD); MRA * [spironolactone 25] if EF<40/HF/DM & tolerating BB+ACEi.
2/p: cardiac rehab referral; lipid panel 4-8 wk; secondary prevention; f/u cards.
takehome: STEMI = emergent reperfusion. PCI <90 min if PCI-capable; transfer if door-to-balloon <120 min is achievable; otherwise fibrinolytics <90 min then transfer for delayed angiography.
NSTEMI = risk-stratify, then time the cath. Very high → immediate (<2 h); high → early invasive (<24 h); mod-low → delayed (25–72 h); very low → ischemia-guided strategy is acceptable.

Layer the meds. Early (all ACS): aspirin, anticoagulation, nitrates, beta-blocker — never delaying emergent revascularization. Within 24 h: high-intensity statin + P2Y12. Before discharge: ACEi/ARB and MRA where indicated.

extras.label: 2025 ACC/AHA ACS Guideline — Update Feb 2025
extras.html:
The original talk (Oct 2023) predates the 2025 ACC/AHA/ACEP/NAEMSP/SCAI ACS guideline, which consolidated STEMI and NSTE-ACS into a single document. The standing teaching above remains correct; these are the practice-shifting additions, also captured on the appended deck slide.

Antiplatelet. Ticagrelor or prasugrel is preferred over clopidogrel for STEMI/NSTE-ACS undergoing PCI (Class 1). Default DAPT is ≥12 months if not high bleeding risk. Bleeding-reduction options: PPI if GI-bleed risk; transition to ticagrelor monotherapy ≥1 month post-PCI; if long-term anticoagulation is needed, stop aspirin 1–4 weeks post-PCI and continue a P2Y12 inhibitor (preferably clopidogrel).
Lipids. High-intensity statin for all ACS, with the option of concurrent ezetimibe. Add a non-statin (ezetimibe, evolocumab, alirocumab, inclisiran, bempedoic acid) if LDL-C ≥70 mg/dL on maximally tolerated statin; further intensification is reasonable if LDL-C 55–<70.
Procedure & revascularization. Radial access preferred over femoral (↓ bleeding, vascular complications, mortality). Intravascular imaging for complex lesions (Class 1). Complete revascularization recommended in STEMI and NSTE-ACS; in cardiogenic shock, revascularize the culprit only (no routine non-IRA PCI at the same sitting). Microaxial flow pump reasonable in selected AMI-cardiogenic shock.
Supportive. RBC transfusion to a hemoglobin of 10 g/dL may be reasonable in ACS with anemia (not actively bleeding). Check a lipid panel 4–8 weeks after starting/adjusting therapy; refer to cardiac rehabilitation (home-based option acceptable).

Source: Rao SV, et al. 2025 ACC/AHA/ACEP/NAEMSP/SCAI Guideline for the Management of Patients With Acute Coronary Syndromes. Circulation 2025;151. doi:10.1161/CIR.0000000000001309.

  2. Top Things to Know: 2025 ACS Guideline. American Heart Association, professional.heart.org.

lessons.title: AF with RVR
lessons.subtitle: Optimized lesson package · Facilitator-led interactive chalk talk for IM residents · 15–20 min (talk) / 30–35 min (with cases)
lessons.credit: Adapted from TeachIM.org — Management of AF with RVR (Buchanan C, Prouse A, Zhang Y; April 2021), CC BY-NC 4.0. Optimized June 2026: guideline currency verified, one deck error corrected, pedagogy restructured, assessments added. Companion deck: AF_RVR_Optimized.pptx (original interactive presenter board, surgically corrected).
objectives: Recognize atrial fibrillation with a rapid ventricular rate on the ECG.
Evaluate a patient with new AF for cardiac and non-cardiac triggers.
Build a framework for rate control in AF with RVR — anchored on hemodynamic stability.

prework.q: Q1. What three ECG features define AF with RVR?
prework.a: Irregularly irregular rhythm, narrow QRS (<120 ms), no discernible P waves — with a ventricular rate >100 bpm. (It can be wide-complex if there's an underlying bundle branch block or aberrancy.)

prework.q: Q2. What makes a patient with AF with RVR "unstable," and what do you do about it?
prework.a: Sustained hypotension (SBP <90), or a SBP drop >30 mmHg with chest pain, altered mentation, or dyspnea — i.e., hemodynamic compromise attributable to the rhythm. Unstable → immediate synchronized cardioversion (120–200 J); sedation/analgesia if feasible but never let it delay shocking.

prework.q: Q3. Name the four rate-control drug classes and which two are first-line.
prework.a: Beta-blockers (metoprolol) and non-dihydropyridine calcium-channel blockers (diltiazem) are first-line; digoxin and amiodarone are second-line for specific scenarios (BP-limited, heart failure, shock). Avoid non-DHP CCBs in HFrEF.

segments.label: Hook (2 min)
segments.html: Open with the board's case: "68-year-old with HTN, diabetes, and COPD, dyspneic and palpitating; here's his ECG." Have a learner read it cold. The whole talk hangs off two decisions you'll make in the first 30 seconds: is this AF, and is he stable?

segments.label: Objective 1 — Recognize & the pathophysiology
segments.html:
ASK: "Walk me through this rhythm strip" — then click DIAGNOSIS. Narrow QRS (<120 ms), irregularly irregular, no P waves, rate >100.

Click the Pathophysiology tab: multiple atrial micro-reentry circuits fire at ~400 bpm; the AV node's refractory period is the gatekeeper that determines how many impulses reach the ventricle — which is exactly why AV-nodal blockers (BB, CCB, digoxin) are the levers for rate control.

Pearl: AF with RVR is usually narrow-complex, but it can be wide-complex with an underlying bundle branch block or rate-related aberrancy — don't let a wide QRS talk you out of the diagnosis when the rhythm is irregularly irregular.

segments.label: Objective 2 — Evaluate the trigger
segments.html:
ASK: "New AF — what set it off? Sort your work-up into cardiac and non-cardiac." Click each category; emphasize testing in context, not a shotgun panel.
CardiacNon-cardiac
Valvular or structural heart disease → TTE; decompensated HF → ±BNP; new ischemia → ECG ± troponinPulmonary disease (incl. PE) → TTE, ±CXR, ±OSA screen; drugs/toxins → ±urine tox, EtOH level; metabolic → BMP+Mg, CBC; endocrine → TSH with reflex T4

The universal new-AF work-up the take-home emphasizes: TTE, a chemistry panel (BMP+Mg), and TSH — plus CBC and ECG/troponin as indicated.

segments.label: Objective 3 — Management: stability first
segments.html:
ASK: "Before any drug — what one question decides everything?" Click "1": is the patient UNSTABLE?

Unstable = sustained hypotension (SBP <90) or a SBP drop >30 mmHg with chest pain, altered mentation, or dyspnea. Unstable → synchronized DCCV 120–200 J; give sedation/analgesia if you can but don't delay; start anticoagulation.

ASK: "Stable patient — what's the rate target, and IV or oral?" Click "2".

Goal HR <110. Acute: if HR >130 give IV agents; chronic/sub-acute: HR 110–130 can be brought down with oral agents.

ASK: "Four agents — where do they act and what's the catch for each?" Click "3", then each drug.
AgentLine / nicheBP effect & caution
Metoprolol (BB)First-line↓↓BP; caution in decompensated HFrEF, sepsis. IV 5 mg q5min ×3; PO 12.5–37.5 mg q6h
Diltiazem (non-DHP CCB)First-line↓↓BP; avoid in HFrEF, caution in sepsis. IV 10–20 mg bolus + drip; PO 30–90 mg q6h
DigoxinSecond-lineBP-neutral (↔) with positive inotropy — useful when BP won't tolerate a BB/CCB; caution in elderly and renal failure. IV 0.25–0.5 mg load then 0.25 mg q6h
AmiodaroneSecond-lineMild ↓BP; favored in shock; caution as a rate-control agent in chronic AF (it can chemically cardiovert). IV 150 mg bolus then drip; lung/liver/thyroid toxicity long-term

If one agent at escalating doses fails, add a second (e.g., a CCB on top of a BB). Start anticoagulation (heparin/enoxaparin) for anyone heading to cardioversion or with high thromboembolic risk by CHA2DS2-VASc.

2023 currency note: The acute approach above is unchanged in the 2023 ACC/AHA/ACCP/HRS AF guideline — BB/CCB first-line, lenient resting HR <110 acceptable when asymptomatic with preserved LVEF (RACE II), DCCV for instability. Around cardioversion: if AF has lasted ≥48 h or for an unknown duration, give ≥3 weeks of therapeutic anticoagulation first OR do a TEE to exclude thrombus, then anticoagulate ≥4 weeks afterward regardless of score. Long-term stroke prophylaxis is still guided by CHA2DS2-VASc (2023 ACC); the 2024 ESC guideline shifted to CHA2DS2-VA (dropping the sex category).

segments.label: Close (2 min)
segments.html: Hide the board. Have the group rebuild: the three ECG features, the universal new-AF work-up, the stability criteria + what to do when unstable, the rate target, and which agent they'd reach for in HFrEF, in shock, and when the BP is borderline.

cases.title: Case 1 — Stable RVR
cases.stem: 68-year-old man with HTN and DM, new AF with RVR, HR 160s, BP 120s/60s, dyspnea and palpitations.
cases.reveal: Stable AF with RVR. With HR >130, give IV metoprolol 5 mg or IV diltiazem 10 mg. Work-up: CBC, BMP/Mg, TSH, ECG, troponin, CXR.

cases.title: Case 2 — RVR in septic shock
cases.stem: 54-year-old woman with HTN and alcohol use disorder, admitted with septic shock on norepinephrine 0.1, MAPs in the 70s. New AF, HR 150s.
cases.reveal: Despite the pressors, she is in stable AF with RVR (the rhythm isn't the cause of her shock). Given septic shock, amiodarone is preferred over metoprolol or diltiazem because it is more hemodynamically neutral. Treat the sepsis in parallel.

cases.title: Case 3 — RVR with HFrEF
cases.stem: 72-year-old man with DM, HTN, and HFrEF (EF 25%) with AF with RVR, HR 160s, BP 110s/60s, no orthopnea or PND.
cases.reveal: Stable AF with RVR. Avoid CCBs in HFrEF. A beta-blocker can be used in compensated HFrEF — give IV metoprolol 5 mg.

cases.title: Case 4 — The one who decompensates
cases.stem: 68-year-old on an esmolol drip in the ICU; called for HR 160s, BP drops to 85/palp, and the patient is unresponsive.
cases.reveal: This patient is now UNSTABLE. Place pads and prepare for synchronized DCCV at 120–200 J. All cardioverted patients should receive anticoagulation.

mcqs.stem: A 70-year-old man presents with palpitations. ECG shows an irregularly irregular narrow-complex tachycardia at 150 bpm with no P waves. BP is 132/78 and he is mentating normally with no chest pain. What is the most appropriate initial management?
opts: IV diltiazem or metoprolol for rate control
mcqs.exp: B. This is AF with RVR and he is hemodynamically stable, so the priority is rate control with a first-line AV-nodal blocker (IV diltiazem or metoprolol). Cardioversion (A) is for unstable patients. Adenosine (C) transiently blocks the AV node and can unmask atrial activity but does not treat AF and won't sustain rate control. Up-front chemical cardioversion (D) raises stroke risk if AF has lasted ≥48 h without anticoagulation and isn't the first move in a stable patient.

mcqs.stem: A 64-year-old woman with HFrEF (EF 30%) develops AF with RVR at 150 bpm, BP 108/70, no chest pain or hypoxia. Which rate-control agent should be avoided?
mcqs.exp: B. Non-dihydropyridine calcium-channel blockers (diltiazem, verapamil) are negative inotropes and are contraindicated in HFrEF — they can precipitate decompensation. A beta-blocker (A) is appropriate once the patient is compensated; digoxin (C) and amiodarone (D) are reasonable, BP-friendly options in heart failure.

mcqs.stem: A 58-year-old man in septic shock on norepinephrine develops AF with RVR at 155 bpm; MAP is 68. Which agent best balances rate control and hemodynamics?
mcqs.exp: C. In the setting of shock, amiodarone is the most hemodynamically neutral rate-control choice; diltiazem (A) and metoprolol (B) can drop an already marginal blood pressure. Cardioversion (D) is unlikely to hold while the underlying driver (sepsis, catecholamines) persists and is reserved for instability attributable to the rhythm — here the shock is septic, not rate-driven. Treat the sepsis concurrently.

mcqs.stem: A 75-year-old man with AF at 150 bpm becomes acutely hypotensive (SBP 80) with chest pain and confusion. What is the immediate next step?
opts: IV fluids and reassess in 30 minutes
mcqs.exp: C. He meets criteria for instability (hypotension plus ischemia and altered mentation attributable to the tachyarrhythmia), so synchronized cardioversion is indicated immediately; sedation should not delay it. AV-nodal blockers (A, D) would further lower his blood pressure, and waiting (B) is unsafe in an unstable patient. Anticoagulate after cardioversion.

mcqs.stem: A patient with new AF of unknown duration is hemodynamically stable and you plan elective cardioversion. Regarding anticoagulation, which is correct?
opts: No anticoagulation is needed if CHA2DS2-VASc is 0
Anticoagulate for ≥3 weeks beforehand or perform a TEE, then ≥4 weeks afterward
Anticoagulate only if the post-procedure rhythm is AF
Heparin during the procedure is sufficient with no further therapy
mcqs.exp: B. When AF has lasted ≥48 hours or for an unknown duration, the embolic risk around cardioversion requires either ≥3 weeks of therapeutic anticoagulation beforehand or a transesophageal echo to exclude left-atrial thrombus, followed by ≥4 weeks of anticoagulation afterward regardless of the CHA2DS2-VASc score (A) — because atrial mechanical stunning persists after the rhythm normalizes (C). Peri-procedural heparin alone (D) is inadequate.

anki: Three ECG features of AF with RVR?
Irregularly irregular rhythm, narrow QRS (<120 ms), no discernible P waves; ventricular rate >100.
When is AF with RVR wide-complex?
With an underlying bundle branch block or rate-related aberrancy.
What determines the ventricular rate in AF?
The AV node's refractory period (atria fire ~400 bpm; the AV node gates conduction) — which is why AV-nodal blockers control the rate.
Universal work-up for new AF?
TTE, chemistry panel (BMP + Mg), TSH — plus CBC and ECG +/- troponin; add CXR/OSA/tox/EtOH per context.
Cardiac triggers to consider in new AF?
Valvular/structural heart disease, decompensated HF, new ischemia.
Non-cardiac triggers in new AF?
Pulmonary disease (incl. PE), drugs/toxins/alcohol, metabolic (electrolytes), endocrine (hyperthyroidism), OSA.
Criteria for an UNSTABLE patient with AF with RVR?
Sustained hypotension (SBP <90) or SBP drop >30 mmHg with chest pain, altered mentation, or dyspnea — attributable to the rhythm.
Management of unstable AF with RVR?
Synchronized DCCV 120-200 J; sedation/analgesia if feasible but don't delay; anticoagulate.
Acute vs chronic heart-rate goals?
Acute: HR <130 (IV meds if >130). Chronic: HR <110 (oral meds for 110-130). Lenient <110 acceptable if asymptomatic with preserved EF.
First-line rate-control agents?
Beta-blocker (metoprolol) and non-dihydropyridine CCB (diltiazem).
Second-line rate-control agents and their niches?
Digoxin (BP-neutral, positive inotropy — when BP won't tolerate a BB/CCB) and amiodarone (favored in shock).
Which rate-control class is contraindicated in HFrEF?
Non-dihydropyridine CCBs (diltiazem, verapamil) — negative inotropes. Use a beta-blocker once compensated.
Best rate-control agent in septic shock / hypotension?
Amiodarone (most hemodynamically neutral); digoxin is an alternative when BP-limited.
Long-term toxicities of amiodarone?
Lung, liver, and thyroid toxicity — especially with continued long-term use.
Anticoagulation rule around cardioversion (AF >=48 h or unknown)?
>=3 weeks therapeutic anticoagulation before OR a TEE to exclude thrombus; then >=4 weeks after regardless of CHA2DS2-VASc.
What guides long-term stroke prophylaxis in AF?
CHA2DS2-VASc (2023 ACC/AHA); the 2024 ESC uses CHA2DS2-VA (drops the sex category).
lessons.dot:
#AF w/ RVR: irregularly irregular, narrow QRS <120ms, no P waves, vent rate * bpm. new vs known. likely 2/2 * [HF/ischemia/valvular, PE/pulm, sepsis, thyroid, EtOH/tox, electrolyte, post-op]. HDS vs HDUS.
- STEP 1 stability: UNSTABLE = SBP <90 or SBP↓ >30 + [CP / AMS / SOB] attributable to rhythm → synchronized DCCV 120-200J (sedate if feasible, do NOT delay) + anticoag
- STEP 2 rate target (if stable): goal HR <110. acute HR >130 → IV meds; HR 110-130 → PO meds. lenient <110 ok if asx + preserved EF (RACE II)
- STEP 3 agent (AV-nodal): [metoprolol IV 5mg q5min x3 / PO 12.5-37.5 q6h — 1st line, ↓↓BP, caution decomp HFrEF/sepsis] [diltiazem IV 10-20mg bolus+drip / PO 30-90 q6h — 1st line, ↓↓BP, AVOID HFrEF] [digoxin IV 0.25-0.5mg load then 0.25 q6h — BP-neutral + inotropy, caution elderly/renal] [amiodarone IV 150mg bolus+drip — favored in shock, caution chronic AF (may cardiovert), lung/liver/thyroid tox]. fail 1 agent → add 2nd class
- w/u: ECG, TTE, BMP+Mg, CBC, TSH±fT4, ±troponin/BNP, ±CXR, ±tox/EtOH, ±OSA screen
- anticoag: CHA₂DS₂-VASc = * [≥2 M / ≥3 F → OAC]. cardiovert + AF ≥48h/unknown → ≥3wk therapeutic anticoag OR TEE first, then ≥4wk after regardless of score. (2024 ESC: CHA₂DS₂-VA)
- monitor: tele, q*h vitals, recheck BMP/Mg, reassess HR/BP after each dose, hold AV-nodal if HDUS
takehome: AF with RVR is an irregularly irregular, narrow-complex (<120 ms) tachycardia with no P waves and a rate >100 — wide-complex only with BBB/aberrancy.
Work up new AF for cardiac and non-cardiac triggers; the universal tests are TTE, a chemistry panel (BMP+Mg), and TSH.
Decide stability first: unstable (hypotension or end-organ symptoms from the rhythm) → synchronized DCCV 120–200 J without delay.
If stable, rate-control to HR <110 — IV agents if HR >130; beta-blockers and CCBs are first-line, digoxin/amiodarone second-line, and avoid CCBs in HFrEF.
Anticoagulate everyone who is cardioverted (≥4 weeks after) and guide long-term therapy with CHA2DS2-VASc.
lessons.refs:
1. Joglar JA, et al. 2023 ACC/AHA/ACCP/HRS Guideline for the Diagnosis and Management of Atrial Fibrillation. Circulation. 2024;149(1):e1-e156.
  2. Van Gelder IC, et al. Lenient versus Strict Rate Control in Patients with Atrial Fibrillation (RACE II). N Engl J Med. 2010;362(15):1363-1373.
  3. Andrade JG, et al. Contemporary Atrial Fibrillation Management: A Comparison of the Current AHA/ACC/HRS, CCS, and ESC Guidelines. Can J Cardiol. 2017;33(8):965-976.
  4. Van Gelder IC, et al. 2024 ESC Guidelines for the management of atrial fibrillation. Eur Heart J. 2024;45(36):3314-3414.

lessons.title: AKI in Cirrhosis
lessons.subtitle: Optimized lesson package · Facilitator-led interactive chalk talk for IM residents · 30–45 min
lessons.credit: Adapted from TeachIM.org — AKI in Cirrhosis (Yilin Zhang, MD; ~2020–2021), CC BY-NC 4.0. Optimized June 2026: guideline currency verified (terlipressin, HRS-AKI nomenclature), deck typo fixed, pedagogy restructured, assessments added. Companion deck: AKI_in_Cirrhosis_Optimized.pptx (original interactive board, corrected).
objectives: Differentiate hepatorenal syndrome (HRS) from other causes of AKI in cirrhosis.
Describe the systemic pathophysiology of compensated and decompensated cirrhosis in terms of kidney perfusion.
Identify the initial treatment of HRS.
Apply the framework through a series of short cases.

prework.q: Q1. In a cirrhotic with new AKI, what is the single most common category — pre-renal, intrarenal, or post-renal — and what two things must you always rule out?
prework.a: Pre-renal (60–70% of AKI in cirrhosis). Always evaluate for infection — including a diagnostic paracentesis for SBP — and rule out GI bleeding. Post-renal/obstruction is <1%.

prework.q: Q2. What defines hepatorenal syndrome physiologically, and how do you confirm it at the bedside?
prework.a: HRS is a pre-renal AKI that does NOT respond to volume. Confirm by stopping diuretics and giving an albumin challenge (1 g/kg/day, max 100 g/day, ×48 h): no improvement in creatinine, with no shock, no nephrotoxins, and no structural kidney disease, supports HRS.

prework.q: Q3. What is the only definitive treatment for HRS?
prework.a: Liver transplant. Medical therapy (albumin + a vasoconstrictor) and HD/TIPS are bridges or temporizing measures.

segments.label: Objective 1 — The differential
segments.html:
ASK: "Cirrhotic patient, creatinine just doubled. Before labs — what are your buckets?" Build pre-renal / intrarenal / post-renal, then click to reveal prevalence.
CategoryPrevalenceCauses
Pre-renal60–70%Volume responsive: GI bleed, over-diuresis, GI losses. Not volume responsive: HRS, cardiorenal. Plus sepsis (SBP, pneumonia, UTI), medications.
Intrarenal30–40%ATN, AIN, glomerulonephritis
Post-renal<1%Obstruction (intrinsic/extrinsic), neurogenic bladder
Pearl: AKI increases mortality 2–6 fold in cirrhosis. The mandatory early workup is a diagnostic paracentesis (SBP can present without abdominal pain) and ruling out GI bleed. FeUrea (not FeNa) when on diuretics — FeUrea <35% suggests pre-renal. Granular casts and low urine Na are unreliable for ATN in advanced cirrhosis (persistent RAAS activation reclaims urinary sodium).

segments.label: Objective 2 — Pathophysiology
segments.html:
Click each number on the board to build the cascade. The core idea: cirrhosis is a vasodilatory state.

Compensated: ↓liver metabolism of vasodilators → ↓vascular tone, especially splanchnic → splanchnic pooling → ↓effective arterial volume (EAV) → heart raises CO to preserve perfusion.

Decompensated: the same vasodilation worsens; CO can no longer compensate → ↓BP → RAAS, ADH, and sympathetic activation → renal vasoconstriction and sodium/water retention → hyponatremia and pre-renal AKI. HRS is the kidney being squeezed by systemic vasodilation, not by intrinsic renal disease.

segments.label: Objective 3 — Diagnosis & treatment of HRS
segments.html:
ASK: "Pre-renal AKI that won't budge with volume — how do you prove it's HRS?" Stop diuretics + 48-h albumin challenge (1 g/kg/day); no Cr improvement, no shock, no nephrotoxins, no structural disease.
Updated terminology (use this when you teach): The board labels HRS "Type 1 / Type 2." Since 2015 (ICA) and the 2021 AASLD guidance, HRS-1 → HRS-AKI (diagnosed by ICA-AKI criteria: ↑Cr ≥0.3 mg/dL within 48 h or ≥50% within 7 days — the old fixed "Cr >2.5" threshold was dropped) and HRS-2 → HRS-NAKI (non-AKI; includes HRS-CKD, often with refractory ascites). The board's schematic is still excellent for teaching the physiology — just name it with current terms.

Treatment targets the physiology — refill the arterial volume and restore tone:

StepDetail
AlbuminRaises effective arterial volume (up to ~50 g/day for ongoing expansion)
VasoconstrictorRestores vascular tone. US, 2026: terlipressin (FDA-approved 2022) or norepinephrine (ICU); the midodrine + octreotide + albumin regimen is the older, less effective outpatient/ward option (midodrine 5–7.5 mg TID, goal ↑MAP >15)
TIPSCan give delayed renal improvement; most HRS patients are too ill to qualify
HemodialysisBridge to transplant in selected patients
Liver transplantThe ONLY definitive therapy; some need combined liver-kidney transplant
2026 update — terlipressin: The original lesson says terlipressin is "available in other countries but not in the US." As of September 2022 (CONFIRM trial), the FDA approved terlipressin for HRS-AKI — the first drug ever approved for HRS in the US. It improves HRS reversal vs placebo. Avoid in ACLF grade 3, pre-existing hypoxia, or SCr >5 mg/dL (respiratory-failure safety signal; benefit unlikely at very high Cr).

segments.label: Objective 4 — Cases
segments.html: Work the three case-handout cases in small groups; answers below and in the facilitator answer sheet.

segments.label: Close (2 min)
segments.html: Hide the board; have the group rebuild the four take-home points from memory.

cases.title: Case 1 — Fever + confusion in HCV cirrhosis
cases.stem: 55 F, HCV cirrhosis, fever and increased confusion, more ascites, ↓UOP, on lactulose. Afebrile, HR 92, BP 92/60. Diffusely edematous, distended nontender abdomen. Na 127, BUN 30, Cr 2.4 (baseline 1.2); WBC 12k, Hct 30, Plt 90k; Tbili 4, alb 2; UA RBCs + hyaline casts; urine Na <10.
cases.reveal: Low urine Na = pre-renal pattern; differential includes GI bleed, volume loss, sepsis, meds, HRS. She is decompensating (↑Cr, ↑Tbili, worse encephalopathy) — get a diagnostic paracentesis for SBP (can occur without pain): 500 cells, 90% PMNs. Start an albumin challenge while awaiting results.

cases.title: Case 2 — Listed for transplant, rising Cr
cases.stem: 48 M, HCV/EtOH cirrhosis, actively listed, worsening Cr/Tbili/INR despite diuretics. HR 60, BP 92/45. Tight distended abdomen. Na 126, BUN 40, Cr 2.4 (baseline 1.0); Tbili 11, INR 5; UA 1+ protein, hyaline + occasional granular casts; urine Na >65, FeUrea 30%.
cases.reveal: Use FeUrea (not FeNa) on diuretics — FeUrea <35% suggests pre-renal. Granular casts and low urine Na are unreliable for ATN in advanced cirrhosis. Paracentesis: 450 cells, 10% PMNs (no SBP). RUQ US with Doppler (PVT — negative). Bladder pressure 12 mmHg (normal). Albumin challenge: no Cr improvement after 1 g/kg ×48 h.

cases.title: Case 3 — PSC flare, heavy NSAID use
cases.stem: 45 M, PSC cirrhosis, week of worsening abdominal pain self-treated with Tylenol and ibuprofen, ↓UOP, dark urine. HR 90, BP 120/80. Mild RUQ tenderness, no Murphy's, no CVA tenderness. Na 132, K 5.8, Cr 3.6 (baseline 0.9); UA hyaline + WBC casts, no bacteria; urine Na 28, urine Cr 60.
cases.reveal: Intrinsic/intrarenal AKI. FeNa ~1.5%; WBC casts + heavy NSAID use → high suspicion for AIN (acute interstitial nephritis). (Note FeNa is interpretable here as he is not on diuretics.)

mcqs.stem: A 58-year-old man with alcohol-associated cirrhosis and ascites has a creatinine rise from 0.9 to 2.3 mg/dL over 5 days. Diuretics are stopped and albumin 1 g/kg/day is given for 48 hours with no improvement. BP 96/58, no shock; UA bland, no proteinuria; renal ultrasound normal; no recent contrast or nephrotoxins. Diagnostic paracentesis shows 120 PMNs/µL. Which is the most appropriate next step?
opts: Diagnose HRS-AKI and start a vasoconstrictor plus albumin
Diagnose ATN and provide supportive care
mcqs.exp: A. He meets HRS-AKI criteria: AKI in cirrhosis with ascites, no response to diuretic withdrawal + 48-h albumin, no shock, no nephrotoxins, no structural disease, and <250 PMNs so no SBP (B is wrong). Bland sediment argues against ATN (C). HD (D) is a bridge, not the first step. Begin albumin + a vasoconstrictor (terlipressin or norepinephrine).

mcqs.stem: Which statement about terlipressin for HRS-AKI is correct in the United States in 2026?
opts: It is not approved in the US; use midodrine and octreotide instead
It is FDA-approved (2022) but should be avoided in ACLF grade 3, hypoxia, or SCr >5 mg/dL
It is FDA-approved and is the definitive cure for HRS
It is approved only for type 2 HRS
mcqs.exp: B. The FDA approved terlipressin for HRS-AKI in September 2022 (so A is outdated — exactly the original lesson's error). No drug cures HRS; only liver transplant is definitive (C wrong). It is indicated for HRS-AKI, not the old "type 2"/HRS-NAKI (D). Key safety limits: avoid in ACLF grade 3, pre-existing hypoxia, or SCr >5 mg/dL because of a respiratory-failure signal and low likelihood of benefit.

mcqs.stem: A 60-year-old woman with cirrhosis is admitted with SBP (ascitic PMNs 480/µL), Tbili 5, Cr 1.4. In addition to cefotaxime, which intervention most reduces her risk of HRS and death?
opts: IV albumin 1.5 g/kg on day 1 and 1 g/kg on day 3
mcqs.exp: B. In SBP, albumin (1.5 g/kg day 1 + 1 g/kg day 3) reduces HRS and mortality, with greatest benefit at Tbili >4, Cr >1, or BUN >30 (Sort et al., NEJM 1999) — she qualifies. Diuretics (A) worsen the pre-renal state. TIPS (C) and FFP (D) have no role here.

mcqs.stem: A cirrhotic on furosemide and spironolactone has AKI. Urine Na is 70 mEq/L. Why is FeNa misleading here, and what is more useful?
opts: FeNa is always accurate in cirrhosis; no substitute is needed
Diuretics raise urinary sodium, falsely elevating FeNa; use FeUrea (<35% suggests pre-renal)
FeNa is low because of contrast nephropathy
Urine Na is irrelevant in AKI
mcqs.exp: B. Loop and thiazide-type diuretics force renal sodium excretion, inflating urine Na and FeNa regardless of volume status. FeUrea is not affected the same way; FeUrea <35% supports a pre-renal cause. Conversely, in advanced cirrhosis a low urine Na/FeNa can occur even in ATN because persistent RAAS activation reclaims urinary sodium — interpret these indices cautiously.

mcqs.stem: A patient with cirrhosis develops AKI after a week of ibuprofen for pain. UA shows WBC casts and no bacteria; FeNa 1.5% (not on diuretics). The most likely diagnosis and best initial step are:
opts: Pre-renal azotemia; give large-volume normal saline boluses
Acute interstitial nephritis from NSAIDs; stop the NSAID
Post-renal obstruction; place a Foley and consult urology
mcqs.exp: C. WBC casts plus a clear NSAID exposure point to AIN; the elevated FeNa (interpretable off diuretics) argues against a purely pre-renal HRS picture (A, B). The first and most important step is removing the offending drug. A cautious volume challenge is reasonable since NSAIDs also cause pre-renal injury, but aggressive saline (B) is not the answer and there's no obstruction (D).

anki: Most common category of AKI in cirrhosis, and prevalence?
Pre-renal — 60–70%. Intrarenal 30–40%, post-renal <1%.
Two things you must always rule out in a cirrhotic with new AKI?
Infection (diagnostic paracentesis for SBP — can be painless) and GI bleeding.
How much does AKI raise mortality in cirrhosis?
2–6 fold.
Physiologic definition of HRS?
Pre-renal AKI driven by systemic (splanchnic) vasodilation that does NOT respond to volume — no intrinsic renal disease.
How is HRS confirmed at the bedside?
Stop diuretics + albumin challenge 1 g/kg/day (max 100 g/day) ×48 h: no Cr improvement, with no shock, no nephrotoxins, no structural kidney disease.
Modern HRS nomenclature (vs. old type 1/2)?
HRS-1 → HRS-AKI (ICA-AKI criteria: ↑Cr ≥0.3 mg/dL in 48 h or ≥50% in 7 d; old fixed Cr >2.5 dropped). HRS-2 → HRS-NAKI / HRS-CKD. (ICA 2015; AASLD 2021.)
Core medical treatment of HRS-AKI?
Albumin to expand effective arterial volume + a vasoconstrictor to restore tone.
US vasoconstrictor options for HRS-AKI in 2026?
Terlipressin (FDA-approved 2022) or norepinephrine (ICU). Midodrine + octreotide + albumin is the older, less effective ward regimen.
Terlipressin — when to avoid?
ACLF grade 3, pre-existing hypoxia, or SCr >5 mg/dL (respiratory-failure signal; benefit unlikely).
Only definitive treatment for HRS?
Liver transplant (some need combined liver-kidney transplant). TIPS and HD are bridges.
Albumin regimen that reduces HRS and death in SBP?
1.5 g/kg on day 1 + 1 g/kg on day 3; greatest benefit if Tbili >4, Cr >1, or BUN >30 (Sort, NEJM 1999).
Ascitic fluid threshold diagnosing SBP?
≥250 PMNs/µL → start empiric cefotaxime/ceftriaxone (fluoroquinolone if penicillin-allergic).
Why use FeUrea instead of FeNa in cirrhosis?
Diuretics raise urinary Na and inflate FeNa; FeUrea <35% supports pre-renal AKI.
Why can urine Na be low even in ATN in advanced cirrhosis?
Persistent RAAS activation (aldosterone) reclaims urinary sodium; granular casts can also appear with severe hyperbilirubinemia — both unreliable for ATN here.
Pathophysiology one-liner for decompensated cirrhosis → AKI?
↓Hepatic metabolism → splanchnic vasodilation → ↓effective arterial volume → RAAS/ADH/sympathetic activation → renal vasoconstriction + Na/water retention → hyponatremia + pre-renal AKI.
AIN clue in a cirrhotic with AKI?
WBC casts + NSAID exposure (intrarenal cause); stop the offending drug.
lessons.dot:
#AKI in cirrhosis: Cr * (baseline *), AKI stage * [1/2/3 by ICA-AKI: ↑Cr ≥0.3 in 48h or ≥50% in 7d]. likely 2/2 * [pre-renal (hypovolemia/GIB/over-diuresis/sepsis-SBP/HRS-AKI/cardiorenal), intrarenal (ATN/AIN/GN), post-renal (rare)]. decomp cirrhosis w/ * [ascites/HE/varices/jaundice].
- core w/u: H&P [meds (diuretics, NSAIDs, contrast, nephrotox), GIB, sepsis sx] + UA w/ micro + urine lytes [FeUrea <35% pre-renal if on diuretics; FeNa unreliable] + renal US + DIAGNOSTIC PARACENTESIS (SBP can be painless; ≥250 PMN = SBP) + r/o GIB
- HRS-AKI dx (ICA): cirrhosis + ascites + AKI + NO response to diuretic hold + albumin 1 g/kg/d (max 100 g) x48h + no shock + no nephrotox + no structural dz (bland UA, no proteinuria/hematuria, nl US)
- tx HRS-AKI: albumin (vol expansion) + vasoconstrictor * [terlipressin (FDA 2022; avoid ACLF-3/hypoxia/SCr>5) OR norepi (ICU) OR midodrine+octreotide+albumin (older/ward)] → goal ↑MAP >15; TIPS select; HD bridge; DEFINITIVE = liver transplant (±combined liver-kidney)
- SBP present → cefotaxime/ceftriaxone x5d + albumin 1.5 g/kg d1 + 1 g/kg d3 (esp Tbili>4 / Cr>1 / BUN>30) — ↓HRS & mortality
- AIN (WBC casts + NSAID) → stop offending drug; cautious volume challenge
- monitoring: strict I/O & daily wt, hold nephrotoxins/NSAIDs/diuretics, renally dose meds, daily BMP, MAP goal, lactulose/HE care
- labs: BMP, CBC, LFTs/INR, UA w/ micro, urine Na/Cr/urea, ascitic fluid cell count+cx, lactate, +/- RUQ US w/ Doppler (PVT)
takehome: AKI in cirrhosis is usually pre-renal and almost never obstructive — always tap the belly (SBP) and rule out GI bleed.
HRS is a pre-renal AKI that does not respond to volume; confirm with diuretic withdrawal + a 48-hour albumin challenge, after excluding shock, nephrotoxins, and structural disease.

Treat with albumin + a vasoconstrictor — in the US (2026) terlipressin (FDA-approved 2022) or norepinephrine; midodrine/octreotide is the older regimen. The only definitive therapy is liver transplant.
In SBP, albumin (1.5 g/kg day 1 + 1 g/kg day 3) reduces HRS and death.
lessons.refs:
1. Regner KR, Singbartl K. Kidney Injury in Liver Disease. Crit Care Clin. 2016;32:343-355.
  2. Ge PS, Runyon BA. Treatment of Patients with Cirrhosis. NEJM. 2016;375:767-77.
  3. Sort P, et al. Effect of IV Albumin on Renal Impairment and Mortality in Cirrhosis with SBP. NEJM. 1999;341:403-409.
  4. Runyon BA. Renal Failure in Cirrhosis. NEJM. 2009;361(13):1279-1290.
  5. Angeli P, et al. (International Club of Ascites). Diagnosis and management of AKI in patients with cirrhosis: revised consensus. J Hepatol. 2015;62:968-974.
  6. Biggins SW, et al. AASLD Practice Guidance: Diagnosis, Evaluation, and Management of Ascites, SBP, and HRS. Hepatology. 2021;74(2):1014-1048.
  7. Wong F, et al. Terlipressin plus Albumin for HRS-AKI (CONFIRM). NEJM. 2021;384:818-828.
  8. US FDA. Terlipressin (Terlivaz) approval for HRS-AKI, September 2022.

lessons.title: AKI in a Renal Transplant
lessons.subtitle: Optimized lesson package · Facilitator-led interactive chalk talk for IM residents · 30–45 min
lessons.credit: Adapted from TeachIM.org — AKI in a Patient with a Renal Transplant (Yilin Zhang, MD; expert review Iris DeCastro, MD, Transplant Nephrology; 2020), CC BY-NC 4.0. Optimized June 10, 2026: standing teaching verified current, three deck typos corrected, a 2024 practice-update slide appended, pedagogy restructured and assessments added. Companion decks: AKI_RTx_Optimized.pptx (presenter board) and AKI-in-Renal-Transplant-Learner-Board-1.pptx (learning board, corrected).
objectives: Differentiate pre-renal, intra-renal, and post-renal causes of AKI in a kidney-transplant recipient — keeping both transplant-specific and usual causes on the list.
Name the transplant-specific complications that cause allograft dysfunction and how timing from transplant narrows the differential.
Evaluate acute allograft dysfunction with a focused "toolbox" (renal duplex US, viral PCR, CNI trough, biopsy + DSAs) and know when to involve transplant nephrology.

prework.q: 1. A transplant recipient develops AKI. Before you reach for transplant-specific tests, what standard framework should still structure your differential?
prework.a: The same pre-renal / intra-renal / post-renal framework used in any AKI. Transplant-specific causes are added to — not substituted for — volume depletion, NSAIDs/ACEi, sepsis, ATN, AIN, contrast, and obstruction.

prework.q: 2. List as many transplant-specific causes of allograft dysfunction as you can.
prework.a: CNI toxicity (acute & chronic), renal artery stenosis/thrombosis, viral infection (BK, CMV, adenovirus), acute rejection (hyperacute, cellular, antibody-mediated), and ureteral stenosis (± lymphocele/hematoma/urinoma).

prework.q: 3. Which single test rules post-renal obstruction in or out, and which best evaluates vascular causes?
prework.a: Renal ultrasound detects hydronephrosis (obstruction); adding duplex assesses arterial/venous flow for renal artery stenosis or thrombosis.

segments.label: Hook (2 min)
segments.html:
Put the case on the board: "A 33-year-old man, 6 months out from a kidney transplant for IgA nephropathy (CMV D+/R+), on tacrolimus, prednisone, and mycophenolate, now has a Cr of 3.4 (baseline 1.5)." Ask the room: "His nephrologist is worried about rejection — but what would you miss if rejection is the only thing you think about?" The point of the talk: a transplant patient gets every cause of AKI a non-transplant patient does, plus five transplant-specific ones.

ASK — "What's your one-line framework for any AKI?" Elicit pre/intra/post-renal before revealing the board's categories.

segments.label: Segment 1 — The two-axis differential (Objective 1)
segments.html:
Use the board's first slide. Reveal that each compartment splits into typical/non-transplant and transplant-specific causes. Have learners rapid-fire the usual causes first (≤2 min), then build the transplant-specific list.

CompartmentUsual causesTransplant-specific
Pre-renalVolume depletion, sepsis, cardiorenal, hepatorenal, NSAIDs/ACEiAcute CNI toxicity; renal artery stenosis / artery-or-vein thrombosis
Intra-renalATN, AIN, contrast nephropathy, glomerulonephritisAcute rejection; viral (BK, CMV, adenovirus); chronic CNI toxicity; recurrent/de novo GN
Post-renal3 C's — calculi, clot, cancer; bladder dysfunction; BPHUreteral stenosis; extrinsic compression (lymphocele, perinephric hematoma, urinoma)
Pearl: Acute CNI toxicity is a pre-renal/hemodynamic lesion — it constricts both the afferent and efferent arteriole. Chronic CNI toxicity is an intra-renal structural lesion (fibrosis). Same drug, two compartments.

segments.label: Segment 2 — Timing narrows it (Objective 2)
segments.html:
ASK — "It's post-op day 1 and the new graft makes no urine. What two things top your list?" (Hyperacute rejection from pre-formed DSAs; vascular thrombosis.)
TimingLead withKey clue / test
< 24 hHyperacute rejection; renal artery/vein thrombosisPre-formed DSAs; renal duplex
First 3 monthsAcute CNI toxicity; acute rejection (cellular/humoral)↑CNI trough; biopsy + DSAs (± dd-cfDNA)
3 months – 2 yearsRenal artery stenosis; BK nephropathy (peak ~10–13 mo); CMV nephritis (>3 mo)Duplex; plasma BK PCR; CMV PCR
Any timeLate rejection (esp. non-adherence); ureteral stenosis; all usual causesBiopsy; renal US
ASK — "Patient is 11 months out, asymptomatic, creeping creatinine, bland-ish urine. What virus, and what's the single best test?" (BK; plasma BK PCR.)
Pearl: BK progresses viruria → viremia → nephropathy. Per the 2024 Second International Consensus, plasma (serum) BK PCR is the preferred screening/diagnostic test — screen monthly to ~9 months, then q3 months to 2 years. Sustained DNAemia or biopsy-proven nephropathy → reduce immunosuppression, the primary treatment.
ASK — "Which CMV serostatus is highest risk?" (Donor-positive / recipient-negative, D+/R−.)

segments.label: Segment 3 — The toolbox & calling for help (Objective 3)
segments.html:
Reveal the "toolbox" icon: renal duplex US (hydronephrosis + flow), viral studies (plasma + urine BK/CMV PCR), CNI trough, and allograft biopsy with DSAs (± donor-derived cell-free DNA). Emphasize: a normal CNI trough does NOT exclude chronic CNI toxicity, and biopsy + DSAs remain the standard for rejection. Involve transplant nephrology early.

Pearl: Always reconcile the med list. Azole antifungals (fluconazole, voriconazole), diltiazem, and macrolides inhibit CYP3A4 and raise CNI levels — a classic precipitant of acute CNI toxicity (see Case 1).

segments.label: Retrieval close (3 min)
segments.html: Erase the board. Ask learners to rebuild, from memory: the five transplant-specific causes, the timeline buckets, and the four toolbox tests. Whoever names the azole–CNI interaction unprompted wins.

cases.title: Case 1 — Numbness, hot toe, recent fluconazole
cases.stem: ROS: new numbness/tingling in hands & feet; pain and redness of the left great toe. Meds: fluconazole 2 weeks ago for a yeast infection. Exam: BP 160/92, no graft tenderness. Labs: Na 135, K 6.9, FeNa 0.7%; UA clear, hyaline casts, no protein/WBC/RBC.
cases.reveal: CNI trough elevated at 14 (goal 6–8). Diagnosis: acute CNI toxicity causing neurotoxicity, a gout flare, hypertension, and hyperkalemia. Fluconazole inhibits CYP3A4 → raised tacrolimus levels. Hold/adjust the CNI, treat hyperkalemia and gout, and manage the interaction.

cases.title: Case 2 — Fevers, dysuria, graft tenderness; post-biopsy hematuria
cases.stem: ROS: 2 days of fevers/chills, abdominal pain, dysuria, nausea/vomiting. Exam: T 39.4 °C, HR 115, BP 160/92, tenderness over the graft. (Renal biopsy yesterday for uptrending Cr, complicated by hematuria.) Labs: FeNa 1.2%, urine Pr:Cr 2.6; UA cloudy, + leukocyte esterase, + nitrite, +WBC/RBC/protein/bacteria.
cases.reveal: CNI trough in range; renal duplex normal flow, no hydronephrosis, perinephric stranding; biopsy shows acute cellular rejection, no DSAs; viral studies negative. Diagnosis: acute cellular rejection plus sepsis from acute graft pyelonephritis — two processes at once. Treat the infection and coordinate rejection management with transplant nephrology.

cases.title: Case 3 — Sudden suprapubic pain, anuria for 12 h
cases.stem: ROS: acute suprapubic/abdominal pain, no urine output for 12 hours. Exam: VSS, abdomen tender over graft/suprapubic region. Labs: FeNa 5.7%, urine Pr:Cr 0.6; UA not collected (anuric).
cases.reveal: Renal duplex: normal arterial flow but graft hydronephrosis with 700 cc in the bladder and retained clot. Diagnosis: acute clot retention → obstructive uropathy, on a background of chronic CNI toxicity (biopsy: glomerulosclerosis + interstitial fibrosis). The post-biopsy clot obstructs outflow — relieve the obstruction (catheter/irrigation).

mcqs.stem: A 40-year-old woman is 5 weeks post-deceased-donor kidney transplant on tacrolimus, mycophenolate, and prednisone. She started clarithromycin for bronchitis 4 days ago. Cr rises from 1.3 to 2.4; she has a fine tremor and headache. K 5.8. Which is the most likely cause?
opts: Acute CNI toxicity from a drug interaction
mcqs.exp: B. Macrolides (clarithromycin) inhibit CYP3A4, raising tacrolimus levels; tremor, headache, hypertension, hyperkalemia, and a hemodynamic AKI within the first months are classic acute CNI toxicity. Check a trough (expected high). A is possible but the temporal link to clarithromycin and neuro symptoms point to CNI toxicity; rejection would more typically show graft pain/proteinuria. C peaks later (~10–13 mo) and is usually asymptomatic. D presents with refractory HTN over months, not acutely after an antibiotic. E would show hydronephrosis and ↓UOP.

mcqs.stem: An 11-month-post-transplant man has an asymptomatic Cr rise from 1.4 to 2.0. UA is relatively bland. Plasma BK PCR is markedly elevated. What is the most appropriate next step in management?
mcqs.exp: C. The cornerstone of BK nephropathy treatment is reducing immunosuppression (typically the antiproliferative ± CNI), often confirmed with biopsy. A treats rejection and would worsen BK. B targets CMV, not BK. D is not the acute management. E is unnecessary at this level of dysfunction. Per the 2024 consensus, sustained plasma DNAemia or biopsy-proven nephropathy triggers stepwise immunosuppression reduction.

mcqs.stem: A patient is anuric 12 hours after an allograft biopsy and has new suprapubic pain. Vitals stable. What is the best initial test?
mcqs.exp: C. Anuria plus suprapubic pain after a biopsy screams obstruction — renal US will show hydronephrosis and a clot-filled bladder (clot retention/obstructive uropathy), which is rapidly reversible. The others address intra-renal causes and miss the time-sensitive post-renal lesion. Always rule out obstruction first when UOP drops abruptly.

mcqs.stem: Which donor/recipient CMV serostatus carries the highest risk of CMV disease after transplant?
mcqs.exp: D. A CMV-seronegative recipient receiving a seropositive organ (D+/R−) has no prior immunity and the highest risk of primary CMV disease. CMV nephritis typically occurs after 3 months and can present with fever, malaise, arthralgias, leukopenia, and thrombocytopenia; diagnose with CMV PCR ± biopsy.

mcqs.stem: A transplant recipient with a slowly rising creatinine over a year has a tacrolimus trough squarely within goal range. Biopsy shows arteriolar hyalinosis, interstitial fibrosis, and tubular atrophy. What does this most likely represent?
mcqs.exp: B. Gradual, asymptomatic decline with hyalinosis/fibrosis is classic chronic CNI toxicity — and crucially, a therapeutic trough does not exclude it. A would show peritubular capillaritis/C4d with DSAs. C shows viral cytopathic changes with positive plasma BK PCR. D shows mesangial IgA with hematuria/proteinuria. E is acute, not a year-long course. Findings are nonspecific, so correlate clinically.

anki: What two complementary frameworks organize the differential for AKI in a kidney transplant recipient?
(1) Anatomic — pre-renal, intra-renal, post-renal; and (2) timing from transplant. Always keep BOTH transplant-specific and usual AKI causes on the list.
Name the 5 transplant-specific causes of allograft dysfunction.
CNI (calcineurin-inhibitor) toxicity — acute and chronic; renal artery stenosis/thrombosis; viral infection (BK, CMV, adenovirus); acute rejection; ureteral stenosis.
By what mechanism does ACUTE CNI toxicity cause AKI, and when does it typically occur?
Vasoconstriction of both afferent and efferent arterioles (a pre-renal/hemodynamic insult). Most common in the first 3 months post-transplant.
What are the clinical and lab clues to acute CNI toxicity?
Neurotoxicity (headache, tremor, paresthesias, seizures), hypertension, rarely HUS; labs show ↑K and ↑uric acid; usually an elevated CNI trough level.
Does a CNI trough within the goal range exclude chronic CNI toxicity?

No. Chronic CNI toxicity causes a gradual, asymptomatic rise in Cr and is diagnosed on biopsy (glomerulosclerosis, interstitial fibrosis, adventitial nodule — all nonspecific); a normal trough does NOT exclude it.
When does hyperacute rejection occur and what mediates it?
Within the first 24 hours; antibody-mediated by pre-formed donor-specific antibodies (DSAs).
When does acute rejection most commonly occur, and what late trigger should you suspect?
Within the first 6 months; later episodes suggest medication non-adherence. Types: acute cellular (T-cell mediated) and acute humoral/antibody-mediated.
How is acute rejection diagnosed?
Biopsy is the standard. Acute cellular → biopsy; acute antibody-mediated → biopsy + DSAs (± donor-derived cell-free DNA). Clues: graft pain, fever, hypertension, proteinuria (esp. >1 g/d), hematuria.
What is the typical timing and progression of BK polyomavirus nephropathy?
Can occur anytime but peaks ~10–13 months post-transplant. Progression: BK viruria → viremia → BK nephropathy.
What is the preferred test to screen for and diagnose BK, per the 2024 consensus?

Plasma (serum) BK PCR is the preferred screening/diagnostic test — screen monthly until ~9 months, then q3 months to 2 years. Urine BK PCR/decoy cells are a sensitive early screen; biopsy confirms nephropathy.
Which CMV donor/recipient serostatus carries the highest risk of CMV disease?
CMV donor-positive / recipient-negative (D+/R−). CMV nephritis typically occurs after 3 months; clues: fever, malaise, arthralgias, ↓WBC and ↓platelets; diagnose with CMV PCR ± biopsy.
When does renal artery stenosis typically present in a transplant, and how is it diagnosed?
Most common between 3 months and 2 years (atherosclerosis, trauma, ischemia; risk rises with CMV infection). Clues: hypertension, edema, ↓RBCs. Diagnosis: renal duplex ultrasound.
When does vascular (renal artery/vein) thrombosis occur after transplant?
In the immediate post-transplant period; relatively rare. Artery: kinking or rejection. Vein: external compression or extension of a DVT. Diagnosis: renal duplex.
Name the post-renal transplant-specific causes of AKI and the key test.

Ureteral stenosis (surgical anastomosis stenosing over time) and extrinsic compression from lymphocele, perinephric hematoma, or urinoma → obstructive uropathy. Diagnose with renal ultrasound (hydronephrosis).
What tests make up the "toolbox" for working up AKI in a transplant?
Renal duplex ultrasound, viral studies (plasma + urine BK/CMV PCR), CNI trough level, and allograft biopsy with DSAs (± donor-derived cell-free DNA). Involve transplant nephrology early.
A transplant patient on tacrolimus develops AKI, ↑K, gout flare, and tremor after starting fluconazole. Mechanism?

Acute CNI toxicity. Azole antifungals (e.g., fluconazole) inhibit CYP3A4 and raise tacrolimus/cyclosporine levels, precipitating neurotoxicity, hypertension, ↑K and AKI. Always review for CNI drug interactions.
What is the role of donor-derived cell-free DNA (dd-cfDNA) in 2024 practice?

A non-invasive adjunct that rises before creatinine; used to help exclude rejection (especially antibody-mediated) in acute allograft dysfunction or stable function (ASTS, Oct 2024). It supports, but does not replace, biopsy + DSAs.
Why must "usual" AKI causes still be evaluated in a transplant recipient?

Transplant patients still develop pre-renal (volume depletion, NSAIDs/ACEi, sepsis, cardiorenal/hepatorenal), intra-renal (ATN, AIN, contrast, GN), and post-renal (calculi, clot, cancer, BPH) AKI — transplant-specific causes are ADDED to, not substituted for, the standard differential.
lessons.dot:
#Allograft dysfunction: AKI in kidney tx (*___ mo post-tx, baseline Cr *___ → now *___), 2/2 [transplant-specific +/- usual cause]
fx: keep BOTH lists — pre/intra/post-renal AND tx-specific; timing narrows
likely 2/2 *[acute CNI tox / chronic CNI tox / acute rejection (cellular vs AMR) / hyperacute / BK / CMV / adenovirus / RAS / a-or-v thrombosis / ureteral stenosis / lymphocele-hematoma-urinoma / + usual: prerenal-ATN-AIN-contrast-obstruction]
timeline: <24h [hyperacute / thrombosis]; <3mo [acute CNI tox / rejection]; 3mo-2yr [RAS / BK peak ~10-13mo / CMV >3mo]; anytime [late rejection-nonadherence / ureteral stenosis]
core w/u: renal duplex US [hydronephrosis? arterial/venous flow?] + UA/micro + CNI trough *[goal ___] + plasma & urine BK PCR + CMV PCR
  - acute CNI tox: ↑trough, tremor/HA/szr, HTN, ↑K, ↑uric acid → reduce/hold CNI, fix interaction
  - chronic CNI tox: gradual asx ↑Cr, NORMAL TROUGH DOES NOT EXCL → bx (hyalinosis/IF-TA)
  - rejection: graft pain/fever/HTN/proteinuria >1g/d → bx + DSAs (± dd-cfDNA); cellular=Tcell, AMR=Ab/C4d
  - BK: viruria→viremia→nephropathy; dx plasma BK PCR (2024 consensus) → REDUCE immunosupp
  - CMV: D+/R- highest risk, fever/↓WBC/↓plt → CMV PCR ± bx
targeted only: allograft biopsy + DSAs if susp rejection/chronic CNI tox; dd-cfDNA to help excl AMR (ASTS 2024)
med rec: *[azole antifungal / diltiazem / macrolide] ↑ CNI → tox; hold nephrotoxins (NSAID/contrast/ACEi)
AVOID: tx-specific tunnel vision (still r/o volume/obstruction/sepsis); trusting nl trough to excl chronic CNI tox
red flags → escalate: anuria/obstruction, hyperacute pattern, sepsis, refractory ↑K
dispo: involve transplant nephrology early; *[admit / obs]; monitor UOP, K, CNI trough, graft US
takehome: Keep both lists — a transplant patient gets every usual AKI cause plus five transplant-specific ones.
Five transplant-specific causes: CNI toxicity (acute + chronic), renal artery stenosis/thrombosis, viral (BK, CMV, adenovirus), rejection, ureteral stenosis.
Timing narrows it: <24 h hyperacute/thrombosis; first 3 mo acute CNI tox & rejection; 3 mo–2 yr RAS, BK (peak ~10–13 mo), CMV.
Toolbox: renal duplex US, plasma + urine BK/CMV PCR, CNI trough, biopsy + DSAs (± dd-cfDNA). Loop in transplant nephrology early.
Reconcile meds — azole antifungals and other CYP3A4 inhibitors raise CNI levels and trigger acute toxicity. A normal trough does not exclude chronic CNI toxicity.

  5. Limaye AP, Brennan DC. Clinical manifestations and diagnosis of BK virus-induced (polyomavirus) nephropathy. UpToDate.

Transplantation. 2024;108(9):1834-1866. · American Society of Transplant Surgeons. Statement on the appropriate use of donor-derived cell-free DNA in kidney transplant recipients. Oct 2024.
