lessons.title: Abnormal Uterine Bleeding
lessons.subtitle: Optimized lesson package · Facilitator-led interactive chalk talk for IM residents · ~25–40 min total
lessons.credit: Adapted from TeachIM.org — Abnormal Uterine Bleeding (Zarling N, deQuillfeldt N, Fainstad B, Brett M; April 2022), CC BY-NC 4.0. Optimized June 2026: FIGO currency verified, one deck correction made (PALM-COEIN "I"), pedagogy restructured, assessments added. Companion deck: Abnormal_Uterine_Bleeding_Optimized.pptx (original interactive board, surgically corrected).
objectives: Define abnormal uterine bleeding (AUB) using the four FIGO bleeding parameters.
Build a differential for AUB with the PALM-COEIN mnemonic, separating structural from non-structural causes.
Order the correct baseline and cause-specific work-up — including when to add transvaginal ultrasound and endometrial biopsy.

prework.q: Q1. A patient says her periods are "heavy and long." Which four parameters define a normal menstrual cycle, and what are the rough FIGO cutoffs for each?
prework.a: Frequency 24–38 days (abnormal = <24 frequent, >38 infrequent, none = amenorrhea) · Duration ≤8 days · Regularity cycle-to-cycle variation ≤7–9 days (age-dependent) · Volume subjective (interferes with life) or objective (soaking a pad/tampon every 2 h, flooding, overnight changes). Any deviation = AUB.

prework.q: Q2. Expand PALM-COEIN. Which letters are structural vs non-structural, and what does the "I" stand for?
prework.a: PALM = structural: Polyp, Adenomyosis, Leiomyoma (fibroid), Malignancy/hyperplasia. COEIN = non-structural: Coagulopathy, Ovulatory dysfunction, Endometrial, Iatrogenic (hormonal contraception/IUD/implant, anticoagulants, SERMs, dopamine antagonists), Not yet classified. The "I" is Iatrogenic — not "idiopathic."

prework.q: Q3. Which three labs (plus one exam) does EVERY patient with AUB get, before any cause-specific testing?
prework.a: Pelvic exam + three labs: pregnancy test, CBC, and iron studies. Pregnancy is always ruled out first; CBC screens for anemia/thrombocytopenia; ferritin <50 ng/mL is low in pre-menopausal patients and warrants treatment even without anemia.

segments.label: Hook (2 min)
segments.html: "A 32-year-old tells you her periods are 'heavier than they used to be.' Before you order a single test — what four questions tell you whether this is even abnormal, and what's the one test you must send no matter what the story is?" (The four FIGO parameters; the must-send is a pregnancy test.) This launches Objectives 1 and 3 at once.

segments.label: Objective 1 — Define AUB: the four parameters
segments.html:
ASK: "What features of a menstrual history would make you call a bleeding pattern abnormal?" Collect answers, then click each button (Duration / Frequency / Regularity / Volume) to reveal the cutoffs.
ParameterNormalAbnormal
Duration≤8 daysProlonged >8 days
FrequencyEvery 24–38 daysFrequent <24 d, infrequent >38 d, absent (amenorrhea = no menses ×3 mo)
RegularityCycle-to-cycle variation ≤7–9 dVariation >9 d (≤9 d if age <25 or >42; ≤7 d if 26–41); intermenstrual spotting
VolumeDoes not interfere with lifeSubjective interference, or objective: soaking pad/tampon q2h, overnight changes, flooding/clots
Pearl: Ask about all four every time — patients (and notes) collapse them into "heavy," but the parameter that's off steers the differential. Heavy+regular suggests a structural or coagulopathy cause; irregular/infrequent points to ovulatory dysfunction.

segments.label: Objective 2 — Build the differential: PALM-COEIN
segments.html:
ASK (first, always): "Before any mnemonic — what is the one diagnosis you rule out in every reproductive-age patient with AUB?" Click "Pregnant?" → reveal. Then: go letter by letter; have the group guess a cause for each before you click.
PALM — structuralCOEIN — non-structural
P Polyp (endometrial > cervical)C Coagulopathy (vWD, thrombocytopenia, platelet dysfunction from liver/renal disease)
A Adenomyosis (endometrium into myometrium; "boggy," tender uterus)O Ovulatory dysfunction (PCOS, thyroid, hyperprolactinemia, perimenopause, POI, HPA-axis stress)
L Leiomyoma (fibroid)E Endometrial (endometritis/PID, STI; local hemostatic disorders)
M Malignancy / hyperplasia (endometrial in at-risk patients; also cervical/vaginal/vulvar)I Iatrogenic — hormonal contraception (IUD, implant/Nexplanon), anticoagulants, SERMs (tamoxifen, raloxifene), steroids, dopamine antagonists
N Not yet classified (AVMs, isthmocele)
Corrected here: The classic teaching error is calling the "I" idiopathic. FIGO's "I" is Iatrogenic — the board now reflects this. The textbook example is new-Nexplanon spotting.

segments.label: Objective 3 — Work it up: baseline → cause-specific
segments.html:
ASK: "What does EVERY patient with AUB get, before you go down any one branch?" Click Exam + Labs to reveal.

Baseline for all-comers: pelvic exam (visualize vulva/vagina/cervix for a non-uterine source; bimanual for an enlarged/tender uterus or adnexal mass) plus three labs — pregnancy test, CBC, iron studies (ferritin <50 ng/mL is low pre-menopause).

Structural branch: transvaginal ultrasound to image uterus/ovaries/endometrial stripe. Add endometrial biopsy when hyperplasia/cancer risk is present — AUB is the most common symptom of endometrial cancer.

ASK: "Who needs the endometrial biopsy?" Click to reveal the risk factors.

Biopsy triggers: any postmenopausal bleeding; age >45 with AUB; chronic anovulation/obesity; unopposed-estrogen exposure (incl. tamoxifen); persistent AUB despite treatment in younger patients with risk factors.

Non-structural branch: directed by history — personal/family bleeding history, surgical/dental bleeding, medication and procedure review, full ROS.

Clue on history/examSuspectTargeted test
Heavy menses since menarche; family bleeding hx; postpartum/dental/surgical bleedingCoagulopathy (≈20% of HMB)vWF panel, PT/INR, PTT, factor activity
Hirsutism, insulin resistance, irregular cyclesPCOS / androgen excessTotal testosterone (± 17-OH-progesterone, DHEAS)
Galactorrhea / breast dischargeProlactinomaProlactin
Weight ↑, fatigue, hair loss / Weight ↓, heat intoleranceHypo- / hyperthyroidismTSH
Hot flashes, age 40s, rising cycle lengthPerimenopauseFSH (± LH)
Dyspareunia, discharge, light irregular bleedingEndometritis / PID / STIGonorrhea + chlamydia testing
New contraceptive/IUD/implant, anticoagulant, SERMIatrogenicMed & procedure review; address underlying cause
Bonus (HPO axis): Hypothyroidism → ↑TSH provides negative feedback → ↓GnRH → ↓FSH/LH → anovulation. Perimenopause → failing ovarian estradiol → loss of feedback → ↑GnRH → ↑FSH (why FSH rises).

segments.label: Retrieval close (2 min)
segments.html: Board erased: have the group rebuild, from memory — (1) the four parameters, (2) PALM vs COEIN with the "I" = Iatrogenic, (3) the universal exam + 3 labs, (4) the two triggers for endometrial biopsy.

cases.title: Case 1 — 32-year-old with "heavy periods" ×12 mo
cases.stem: Periods every 26–28 days lasting 8–9 days; soaks a pad every 2 hours on heavy days, with blood soaking through clothing. Previously 4–5 days and lighter. Pelvic exam: mildly enlarged uterus, otherwise normal. Labs: iron deficiency without anemia, negative pregnancy test.
cases.reveal: Yes — it is high volume (q2h soaking, flooding) and prolonged duration (>8 days). Frequency and regularity are normal.

cases.title: Case 2 — 39-year-old with "irregular periods" ×2 yr
cases.stem: Increasingly infrequent periods every 40–60 days, variable volume. FHx celiac disease and lupus. ROS: fatigue, hair loss, 15-lb unintentional weight gain over 2 years. Pelvic exam: unremarkable. Labs: negative pregnancy test, normal CBC and coagulation, no iron deficiency.
cases.reveal: Infrequent (oligomenorrhea, cycles >38 d) and irregular (variable cycle length).

mcqs.stem: A 28-year-old woman reports menses every 27 days lasting 9 days, soaking a pad every 1–2 hours on her two heaviest days. She has bled heavily since menarche, had a postpartum hemorrhage, and her mother has frequent nosebleeds. Pregnancy test negative; Hgb 10.8, platelets normal. Which is the most appropriate next test?
mcqs.exp: C — von Willebrand panel. Heavy menstrual bleeding since menarche plus a personal (postpartum hemorrhage) and family bleeding history strongly suggest an inherited bleeding disorder (≈20% of HMB; vWD most common). A is for structural suspicion (enlarged uterus, focal findings) — absent here. B is for hyperplasia/cancer risk (age >45, postmenopausal bleeding, anovulation/obesity, unopposed estrogen) — this patient has none. D MRI is not a first-line AUB test. E thyroid/prolactin fit ovulatory dysfunction (irregular cycles), but hers are regular.

mcqs.stem: A 58-year-old woman who reached menopause at 51 has one episode of vaginal spotting. Exam is unremarkable. What is the most appropriate next step?
opts: Reassure; bleeding this remote from menopause is benign
mcqs.exp: B — transvaginal ultrasound and/or endometrial biopsy. Any postmenopausal bleeding is endometrial cancer until proven otherwise and mandates evaluation (TVUS for endometrial stripe; biopsy if stripe >4 mm or bleeding persists/recurs). A and D dangerously delay diagnosis. C treats without excluding malignancy. E menopause is already established clinically; FSH won't change the need for evaluation.

mcqs.stem: A 24-year-old with a levonorgestrel IUD placed 6 weeks ago reports irregular light spotting. Pregnancy test negative; exam normal. Under PALM-COEIN, which category best fits, and what is the next step?
opts: Iatrogenic (I) — counsel and continue; spotting typically settles by 3–6 months
mcqs.exp: B — Iatrogenic. New-IUD/implant spotting is the prototypical iatrogenic cause; it is expected and usually resolves within 3–6 months — reassure and continue. (This is precisely the "I" the corrected board now labels Iatrogenic, not "idiopathic.") The other categories don't fit a low-risk young patient with a clear temporal trigger; STI testing would be added only with discharge/dyspareunia or risk factors.

mcqs.stem: A 35-year-old with obesity, oligomenorrhea (cycles 45–70 days), hirsutism, and acanthosis nigricans has AUB. Beyond pregnancy test, CBC, and iron studies, which test best targets the most likely cause?
mcqs.exp: C — total testosterone. The picture is androgen excess / PCOS (ovulatory dysfunction): obesity, oligomenorrhea, hirsutism, insulin resistance. Workup also includes TSH and prolactin. Of note, chronic anovulation = unopposed estrogen, so this patient is also at risk for endometrial hyperplasia and may warrant biopsy. A/B/E target structural or bleeding-disorder causes; D FSH fits perimenopause/POI, less likely at 35 with these features.

mcqs.stem: A 47-year-old with AUB has a transvaginal ultrasound showing a thickened endometrium. Which single factor most strongly mandates endometrial biopsy?
opts: Age >45 with AUB (and chronic anovulation/unopposed-estrogen risk)
mcqs.exp: B. Endometrial sampling is indicated for AUB with hyperplasia/cancer risk — age >45, postmenopausal bleeding, chronic anovulation/obesity, unopposed estrogen (incl. tamoxifen), or persistent bleeding despite treatment. A thickened stripe in a 47-year-old with these risks crosses the threshold. The other options are routine findings that don't, by themselves, drive biopsy.

anki: Four FIGO parameters defining a normal menstrual cycle
Frequency (24-38 d), Duration (<=8 d), Regularity (variation <=7-9 d), Volume (no interference)
FIGO normal menstrual frequency
Every 24-38 days (frequent <24, infrequent >38, absent = amenorrhea)
Normal menstrual duration cutoff
<=8 days; >8 days = prolonged
Expand PALM (structural causes of AUB)
Polyp, Adenomyosis, Leiomyoma (fibroid), Malignancy/hyperplasia
Expand COEIN (non-structural causes of AUB)
Coagulopathy, Ovulatory dysfunction, Endometrial, Iatrogenic, Not yet classified
What does the "I" in PALM-COEIN stand for?
Iatrogenic (NOT idiopathic) - hormonal contraception/IUD/implant, anticoagulants, SERMs, dopamine antagonists
First step in evaluating any reproductive-age patient with AUB
Rule out pregnancy
Exam + 3 labs every AUB patient needs
Pelvic exam + pregnancy test, CBC, iron studies
Ferritin threshold considered low in a pre-menopausal patient
<50 ng/mL (treat even without anemia)
Imaging test for the structural work-up of AUB
Transvaginal ultrasound
Indications for endometrial biopsy in AUB
Postmenopausal bleeding; age >45; chronic anovulation/obesity; unopposed estrogen (incl. tamoxifen); persistent AUB despite treatment
Most common symptom of endometrial cancer
Abnormal uterine bleeding
Approx. % of heavy menstrual bleeding due to a bleeding disorder
~20% (higher in adolescents); vWD most common
Clues suggesting a coagulopathy cause of AUB
HMB since menarche, postpartum hemorrhage, surgical/dental bleeding, strong family bleeding history
Targeted tests for suspected ovulatory dysfunction
TSH, prolactin, +/- FSH; total testosterone (+/- 17-OH-progesterone, DHEAS) if androgen excess
How hypothyroidism causes menstrual irregularity (HPO axis)
Increased TSH -> negative feedback -> decreased GnRH -> decreased FSH/LH -> anovulation
Why FSH is elevated in perimenopause
Falling ovarian estradiol removes negative feedback -> increased GnRH -> increased FSH/LH
lessons.dot:
#AUB (abnl uterine bleeding): *pt, reproductive-age, w/ *[heavy/prolonged/irregular/infrequent] bleeding
  pattern: freq *[q__d, nml 24-38] / dur *[__d, nml <=8] / regularity *[var __d] / vol *[soak pad q__h]
  PREGNANCY TEST *[neg] -- always first
likely 2/2 *[PALM structural: polyp/adenomyosis/leiomyoma/malignancy-hyperplasia |
  COEIN nonstructural: coagulopathy/ovulatory dysfxn/endometrial/IATROGENIC/not-yet-classified]
core w/u (all): pelvic exam *[non-uterine source? boggy/enlarged uterus?] + bHCG + CBC *[Hgb __] + iron studies *[ferritin __; <50 = low]
structural: TVUS *[endo stripe __mm]; endometrial bx if *[postmenopausal bleed / age >45 / chronic anovulation-obesity / unopposed estrogen incl tamoxifen / persists despite rx]
targeted (by clue):
  - coagulopathy [HMB since menarche / fam bleeding hx] -> vWF panel, PT/PTT, factor activity
  - ovulatory [irregular] -> TSH, prolactin, +/- FSH; total testosterone (+/- 17-OHP, DHEAS) if androgen excess
  - endometrial [dyspareunia/discharge] -> GC/chlamydia
  - iatrogenic [new IUD/implant/AC/SERM] -> med review; counsel new-device spotting settles 3-6mo
red flags -> escalate: postmenopausal bleeding (CA until proven o/w); hemodynamic instability; severe anemia
rx (non-structural HMB): levonorgestrel IUD or tranexamic acid 1st-line; treat underlying cause (thyroid, prolactin, etc.)
plan: *[TVUS ordered / bx / labs / GYN referral]; iron repletion if def; f/u *[__]
takehome: AUB is defined by abnormality in any of four parameters — duration (>8 d), frequency (<24 or >38 d), regularity (variation >7–9 d by age), or volume.

After ruling out pregnancy, use PALM-COEIN: PALM = structural (Polyp, Adenomyosis, Leiomyoma, Malignancy/hyperplasia); COEIN = non-structural (Coagulopathy, Ovulatory dysfunction, Endometrial, Iatrogenic, Not yet classified).
Every patient gets a pelvic exam plus three labs: pregnancy test, CBC, and iron studies.
Add transvaginal ultrasound for the structural branch; endometrial biopsy for hyperplasia/cancer risk (postmenopausal bleeding, age >45, chronic anovulation/obesity, unopposed estrogen).
~20% of heavy menstrual bleeding reflects a bleeding disorder — screen when bleeding dates to menarche or there's a personal/family bleeding history.

  2. Munro MG, Critchley HOD, Broder MS, Fraser IS; FIGO Working Group. FIGO classification system (PALM-COEIN) for causes of abnormal uterine bleeding in nongravid women of reproductive age. Int J Gynecol Obstet. 2011;113(1):3–13.
  3. ACOG Practice Bulletin No. 128: Diagnosis of abnormal uterine bleeding in reproductive-aged women. Obstet Gynecol. 2012;120(1):197–206 (reaffirmed).
  4. Davis E, Sparzak PB. Abnormal Uterine Bleeding. StatPearls; and Abnormal uterine bleeding in pre-menopausal women. Am Fam Physician. 2019;99(7):435–443.
  5. Kadir RA, Economides DL, Sabin CA, Owens D, Lee CA. Frequency of inherited bleeding disorders in women with menorrhagia. Lancet. 1998;351(9101):485–489.

lessons.title: Acid Base Disorders
lessons.duration: 15 min framework + 10–15 min cases (45 min total with all cases)
lessons.subtitle: Optimized lesson package · Facilitator-led interactive chalk talk for IM residents · ~15 min framework + 10–15 min cases (45 min total with all cases)
lessons.credit: Adapted from TeachIM.org — Acid Base Disorders (Zhang Y; Mar 2021; initial expert review Andy Luks MD), CC BY-NC 4.0. Optimized June 2026: framework verified current (no guideline changes — core physiology), three content errors corrected on BOTH boards, pedagogy restructured, assessments added. Companion decks: Acid_Base_Optimized.pptx (presenter board) and ABG-Learner.pptx (detailed learning board, corrected).
objectives: Identify the components of a standard ABG and their normal values.
Apply a step-wise approach to interpreting acid-base disorders.
Generate a differential diagnosis for metabolic acidosis, respiratory acidosis, metabolic alkalosis, and respiratory alkalosis.
Calculate expected compensation for each primary disorder — and recognize a mixed disorder when compensation doesn't match.

prework.q: Q1. A patient's pH is exactly 7.40. Can they still have an acid-base disorder?
prework.a: Yes — a normal pH with abnormal PCO<sub>2</sub> or HCO<sub>3</sub> means two (or more) opposing primary processes. Compensation never fully normalizes the pH, so a "perfect" pH with deranged values is a mixed disorder until proven otherwise.

prework.q: Q2. Why must you calculate an anion gap on every chemistry panel, even when the pH is alkalemic?
prework.a: An anion gap metabolic acidosis can hide behind any primary disorder — and an elevated AG is never compensatory. Classic example: salicylate poisoning presents as respiratory alkalosis + concurrent AGMA.

prework.q: Q3. Your patient's albumin is 2.0 g/dL and the anion gap is 11. Is that normal?
prework.a: No — expected AG ≈ 2.5 × albumin ≈ 5. An AG of 11 is ~6 points above expected: a real anion gap acidosis hidden by hypoalbuminemia.

segments.label: Hook (2 min)
segments.html: Write 6.98 / 41 / 58 / 10 on the board. "This ABG has three primary acid-base disorders hiding in it, and by the end of this talk every one of you will find all three in under two minutes." (It's Case 5 — return to it at the close.)

segments.label: Objective 1 — Components of the ABG
segments.html:
ASK: "What's the format of an ABG, and what are the normal values?" — click each component to reveal.
ComponentNormalDerangement
pH7.40 ± 0.05<7.35 acidemia; >7.45 alkalemia
PCO240 ± 4>44 respiratory acidosis; <36 respiratory alkalosis
PO2>80 (RA)see the hypoxemia talk (A–a gradient)
HCO324 ± 2<22 metabolic acidosis; >26 metabolic alkalosis
Base excess0 ± 2>+2 metabolic alkalosis; <−2 metabolic acidosis (H+ needed to restore pH at normal PCO2)
ASK: "What's the difference between acidemia and acidosis?" — -emia is the blood pH; -osis is a process. Several -oses can coexist with any pH.

segments.label: Objective 2 — The step-wise approach
segments.html:
1. Acid-base status: pH → acidemia / normal / alkalemia.
  2. Primary process: which deranged value (PCO2 respiratory, HCO3 metabolic) explains the pH? Both deranged in the pH's direction → concurrent processes. Normal pH + deranged values → mixed disorder.
  3. Anion gap — in everyone: AG = Na − (Cl + HCO3); expected AG ≈ 2.5 × albumin. If AGMA → delta-delta (step 3a).
  4. Compensation: calculate the expected compensatory change; mismatch = another primary process.
Pearl — the "residual HCO3" delta-delta: take measured AG − expected AG and add that difference back to the measured HCO3. It asks: "what's left after the AGMA is accounted for?" Normal → pure AGMA. High → concurrent metabolic alkalosis. Low → concurrent NAGMA.

segments.label: Objective 3 — Differentials
segments.html:
ASK: before each reveal — "Build me the differential." Start with acidemia.
DisorderDifferential
AGMAMUDPILES — Methanol, Uremia, DKA/ketoacidosis, Paraldehyde, INH/Iron, Lactic acidosis, Ethylene glycol (& EtOH), Salicylates
NAGMAGut HCO3 loss (diarrhea, ileostomy), renal loss (RTA, early renal failure, ↓aldosterone), excess Cl (NS). Urinary AG: "neGUTive" = GI loss; positive = renal
Respiratory acidosis↓CO2 elimination (obstructive/intrinsic lung disease, neuromuscular weakness, ↓drive — opioids/benzos) or ↑production (sepsis, overfeeding, hyperthyroidism)
Metabolic alkalosisIVF/Cl-responsive: vomiting/NGT, thiazide/loop diuretics, contraction. Non-responsive: ↑aldosterone, genetic disorders, milk-alkali, hypokalemia
Respiratory alkalosisAnxiety/pain, fever/sepsis, CNS disease, salicylates, liver disease, PE

segments.label: Objective 4 — Compensation
segments.html:
ASK: "Which organ compensates for which, and how fast?" Lungs for metabolic (minutes); kidneys for respiratory (2–3 days). PCO2 and HCO3 always move in the same direction.
Primary disorderExpected compensation
Metabolic acidosisWinter's: PCO2 = 1.5 × HCO3 + 8 (±2) ≈ thumb rule: PCO2 ≈ last two digits of pH
Metabolic alkalosisPCO2 = 0.7 × HCO3 + 21
Respiratory acidosis (per ↑10 PCO2)HCO3 ↑1 acute · ↑4 chronic
Respiratory alkalosis (per ↓10 PCO2)HCO3 ↓2 acute · ↓5 chronic
Pearl: compensation never overcompensates and never fully corrects pH to 7.40. Don't memorize the whole table — metabolic acidosis is the most common derangement, so own Winter's formula and the thumb rule; look up the rest.

segments.label: Close — retrieval (2 min)
segments.html: Return to the hook ABG (6.98/41/10 + chemistry) and have learners run the four steps aloud to find all three disorders (AGMA + NAGMA + respiratory acidosis). Then have them rebuild the step-list from memory.

cases.title: Case 1
cases.stem: 65M with COPD, fever and cough. SaO2 90% on 30% FiO2. ABG 7.36/60/60/36. Na 142, Cl 97, Cr 1.2, HCO3 32 (panel), albumin 3.0.
cases.reveal: pH is low-normal.

cases.title: Case 2
cases.stem: 45M with IBD, abdominal pain, multiple loose bowel movements. ABG 7.34/35/–/18. Na 127, Cl 100, HCO3 18, Cr 2.4, albumin 4.0.
cases.reveal: Acidemia (borderline). PCO<sub>2</sub> and HCO<sub>3</sub> both low → the metabolic acidosis is primary.

cases.title: Case 3
cases.stem: 34F found down, SaO2 89% on RA, pinpoint pupils. ABG 7.25/60/60/26, base excess +1. Na 136, Cl 100, HCO3 26, Cr 1.4, albumin 4.0.
cases.reveal: Acidemia (7.25). PCO<sub>2</sub> 60 elevated, HCO<sub>3</sub> upper-normal → respiratory acidosis.

cases.title: Case 4
cases.stem: 24-year-old man with a history of depression, brought in unresponsive. RR 34, SaO2 99% RA. ABG 7.56/22/110/17. Na 137, Cl 99, HCO3 18, Cr 1.2, albumin 4.0, lactate 1.2, bland urine.
cases.reveal: Alkalemia — PCO<sub>2</sub> low → respiratory alkalosis.

cases.title: Case 5
cases.stem: 72F with Roux-en-Y gastric bypass: 3 days of dyspnea, abdominal pain, nausea, diarrhea; somnolent after IV morphine. ABG 6.98/41/58/10. Na 146, Cl 121, HCO3 7, K 18/98 panel, lipase 3290, lactate 2.7, albumin 2.1, urine + ketones.
cases.reveal: Severe acidemia. HCO<sub>3</sub> very low → metabolic acidosis; PCO<sub>2</sub> "normal" at 41 is inappropriate (see compensation) → concurrent respiratory process.

mcqs.stem: A 68-year-old man with severe COPD is seen for a routine visit. He feels at baseline. ABG: pH 7.36, PCO2 58, PO2 62, HCO3 32. Which best describes his acid-base status?
opts: Respiratory acidosis with concurrent metabolic acidosis
Primary metabolic alkalosis with respiratory compensation
mcqs.exp: B. PCO2 ↑18 with HCO3 ↑~8 (24→32) matches chronic compensation (↑4 per 10) and a near-normal pH. A: acute compensation would raise HCO3 only ~2 (to 26) and the pH would be much lower (~7.27). C: HCO3 is high, not low. D: primary metabolic alkalosis would push pH above 7.45 and PCO2 compensation tops out lower (0.7×32+21≈43, not 58). E: pH is not alkalemic.

mcqs.stem: A 30-year-old woman presents with tinnitus, vomiting, and hyperpnea (RR 32). ABG: pH 7.51, PCO2 20, PO2 105, HCO3 15. Na 140, Cl 102, albumin 4.0. Which best describes her acid-base status?
opts: Primary respiratory alkalosis with appropriate renal compensation
Primary metabolic acidosis with respiratory compensation
Primary respiratory alkalosis with a concurrent anion gap metabolic acidosis
Metabolic alkalosis from vomiting with respiratory compensation
mcqs.exp: C. Alkalemic pH with PCO2 20 = primary respiratory alkalosis; AG = 140 − (102+15) = 23 — and an elevated AG is never compensatory, so there is a concurrent AGMA. Classic salicylate toxicity. A: acute renal compensation drops HCO3 ~2 per 10 (expect ~20); 15 with AG 23 is an acidosis, not compensation alone. B: pH 7.51 means the alkalosis dominates — compensation can't make the pH alkalemic. D: pure AGMA can't produce pH 7.51. E: HCO3 is low, not high.

mcqs.stem: A 28-year-old man with type 1 diabetes has 2 days of vomiting and polyuria. ABG: pH 7.32, PCO2 28. Na 134, Cl 88, HCO3 14, albumin 4.0, glucose 480. After calculating the anion gap and delta-delta, which combination is present?
mcqs.exp: C. AG = 134 − (88+14) = 32; ΔAG = 32 − 12 = 20; residual HCO3 = 14 + 20 = 34 > 26 → hidden metabolic alkalosis (vomiting). A: residual HCO3 would be ~24. B: residual HCO3 would be <22. D: the AG is frankly elevated. E: Winter's = 1.5×14+8 = 29 ≈ measured 28 — respiratory compensation is appropriate, no respiratory acidosis.

mcqs.stem: A 45-year-old man with a high-output ileostomy: pH 7.31, PCO2 30. Na 138, Cl 111, HCO3 15, albumin 4.0. Urinary anion gap is negative. Which best describes the disorder?
opts: NAGMA from GI bicarbonate loss with appropriate respiratory compensation
mcqs.exp: B. AG = 138 − (111+15) = 12 (normal) → NAGMA; Winter's = 1.5×15+8 = 30.5 ≈ measured 30 (appropriate); negative UAG = "neGUTive" = GI loss, matching the ileostomy. A: AG is normal. C: RTA gives a positive UAG. D: measured PCO2 matches Winter's — no concurrent respiratory process. E: HCO3 is low and pH acidemic.

mcqs.stem: A 56-year-old with cirrhosis and septic shock: lactate 6.0, albumin 2.0. Chemistry: Na 132, Cl 100, HCO3 18 (AG 14). The resident notes "anion gap only mildly elevated at 14 (normal 8–12)." What is the most accurate interpretation?
opts: The AG of 14 is essentially normal; no AGMA is present
Expected AG here is ~5, so an AG of 14 represents a significant anion gap acidosis
An AG cannot be interpreted in liver disease
The low albumin falsely elevates the measured AG
A urinary anion gap is needed to confirm AGMA
mcqs.exp: B. Expected AG ≈ 2.5 × albumin = 5; the measured 14 is ~9 above expected — a substantial AGMA (lactic acidosis) masked by hypoalbuminemia. A: ignores the albumin correction. C: AG remains interpretable with the correction. D: backwards — hypoalbuminemia lowers the AG. E: UAG distinguishes causes of NAGMA, not AGMA.

anki: Normal values: pH, PCO2, HCO3, PO2 on an ABG?
pH 7.40 ± 0.05 (acidemia <7.35, alkalemia >7.45); PCO2 40 ± 4; HCO3 24 ± 2; PO2 >80 mmHg on room air.
"-emia" vs "-osis"?
-emia describes the blood pH (acidemia/alkalemia); -osis is a metabolic or respiratory PROCESS that would derange pH if unchecked — several -oses can coexist with any pH.
What are the steps of ABG interpretation?
1) Acid-base status (pH). 2) Primary process (match pH against PCO2 and HCO3). 3) Check anion gap in everyone (+ delta-delta if AGMA). 4) Assess compensation.
Anion gap: formula and normal value?
AG = Na − (Cl + HCO3); normal ≈ 8–12, but EXPECTED AG ≈ 2.5 × albumin (g/dL) — hypoalbuminemia lowers the "normal" gap and can hide an AGMA.
Why calculate an anion gap in every patient regardless of primary process?
An AGMA can be present even when the primary disorder is respiratory or a metabolic alkalosis — and an elevated AG is never a compensatory change.
How does the "residual HCO3" (delta-delta) method work?
Add (measured AG − expected AG) back to the measured HCO3. Result normal → pure AGMA; high (>26) → concurrent metabolic alkalosis; low (<22) → concurrent NAGMA.
Differential for anion gap metabolic acidosis?
MUDPILES: Methanol, Uremia, DKA/ketoacidosis, Paraldehyde, INH/Iron, Lactic acidosis, Ethylene glycol (+EtOH), Salicylates.
Differential for non-gap metabolic acidosis (NAGMA)?
HCO3 loss via gut (diarrhea, ileostomy) or kidney (RTA, early renal failure, hypoaldosteronism) + excess Cl (normal saline).
How does the urinary anion gap separate NAGMA causes?
UAG = Na + K − Cl in urine. "neGUTive" = GI HCO3 loss; positive = renal loss (RTA). Unreliable in renal dysfunction.
Differential for respiratory acidosis?

Decreased CO2 elimination (obstructive/intrinsic lung disease, neuromuscular weakness, decreased respiratory drive — opioids/benzos) or increased CO2 production (sepsis, overfeeding, hyperthyroidism).
Differential for respiratory alkalosis?
Anxiety/pain, fever/sepsis, CNS disease, drugs (salicylates), liver disease, PE — all drive hyperventilation.
Metabolic alkalosis: chloride-responsive vs non-responsive causes?
IVF(Cl)-responsive: vomiting/NGT losses, diuretics (thiazide/loop), contraction alkalosis. Non-responsive: hyperaldosteronism, genetic disorders, milk-alkali, hypokalemia.
Two general rules of compensation?
PCO2 and HCO3 move in the SAME direction; respiratory compensation is fast (minutes), metabolic (renal) compensation slow (2–3 days); compensation never overshoots or fully normalizes the pH.
Winter's formula (and the bedside shortcut)?
Expected PCO2 = 1.5 × HCO3 + 8 (± 2) for metabolic acidosis. Thumb rule: PCO2 should ≈ the last two digits of the pH (e.g., pH 7.32 → PCO2 ~32).
Expected PCO2 in metabolic alkalosis?
PCO2 ≈ 0.7 × HCO3 + 21 — hypoventilation raises PCO2, but rarely much above ~55.
Expected HCO3 change in respiratory acidosis (per 10 mmHg PCO2 rise)?
Acute: HCO3 ↑ ~1; chronic (2–3 d): HCO3 ↑ ~4 — a COPD patient with PCO2 60 and HCO3 32 is chronically compensated.
Expected HCO3 change in respiratory alkalosis (per 10 mmHg PCO2 fall)?
Acute: HCO3 ↓ ~2; chronic: HCO3 ↓ ~5.
What does a "base excess" on the ABG represent?
The number of H+ ions required to restore normal pH if PCO2 were normal: positive (> +2) → metabolic alkalosis; negative (< −2) → metabolic acidosis.
What does measured PCO2 ≠ expected PCO2 (by Winter's) mean?
A second primary respiratory process: measured > expected → concurrent respiratory acidosis; measured < expected → concurrent respiratory alkalosis.
lessons.dot:
#Acid-base: ABG *pH/*PCO2/*PO2/*HCO3 (nl 7.40±.05/40±4/>80/24±2)
status: [acidemia <7.35 / nl 7.35-7.45 / alkalemia >7.45]; primary: [met acidosis HCO3<22 / resp acidosis PCO2>44 / met alkalosis HCO3>26 / resp alkalosis PCO2<36 / mixed — nl pH w/ abnl values]
AG: *Na − (*Cl + *HCO3) = * vs expected 2.5×alb (*alb) — [no AGMA / AGMA → MUDPILES: *]
ΔΔ (if AGMA): residual HCO3 = HCO3 + (AG − expected AG) = * → [~24 pure AGMA / >26 + met alkalosis / <22 + NAGMA]
NAGMA w/u: UAG [neGUTive = GI loss (diarrhea/ileostomy) / positive = renal (RTA)] — unreliable if renal dysfxn
compensation: [met acidosis → Winter's 1.5×HCO3+8 ±2 (≈ last 2 digits of pH) / met alk → 0.7×HCO3+21 / resp acidosis per ↑10 PCO2: HCO3 +1 acute +4 chronic / resp alk per ↓10: −2 acute −5 chronic]; measured vs expected: [appropriate / measured PCO2 > expected → + resp acidosis / < expected → + resp alkalosis]
impression: * [primary dx] 2/2 * [etiology]; [+ concurrent: *]
plan: tx underlying cause *; recheck [ABG/BMP] in *h; AVOID: bicarb push for pure resp acidosis; trust no "compensation" that normalizes pH
takehome: Same routine every time: pH → primary process → anion gap in every patient → compensation. A normal pH with abnormal PCO2/HCO3 is a mixed disorder.
Expected AG ≈ 2.5 × albumin — hypoalbuminemia hides anion gaps. If AGMA, run the residual-HCO3 delta-delta to find the second metabolic process.
PCO2 and HCO3 move in the same direction when compensating; lungs compensate in minutes, kidneys in days; compensation never overshoots.
Winter's formula (1.5 × HCO3 + 8) ≈ the thumb rule (PCO2 ≈ last two digits of pH) — the one compensation calculation to memorize.
A measured value that doesn't match the expected compensation is a second primary diagnosis — like Case 5's "normal" PCO2 of 41 hiding a respiratory acidosis.
lessons.refs:
1. Tolwani AJ, Saha MK, Wille KM. "Metabolic Acidosis and Alkalosis." Textbook of Critical Care, 7th ed. Elsevier, 2017:726-742.
  2. Haber RJ. A Practical Approach to Acid-Base Disorders. West J Med. 1991;155:146-151.
  3. Bear RA. Clinical approach to the diagnosis of acid-base disorders. CMA Journal. 1979;120:173-182.
  4. Robinson MT, Heffner AC. "Acid-Base Disorders." Emergency Medicine Clinical Essentials, 2nd ed. Elsevier Saunders, 2013:1357-1364.
  5. Kaufman DA. "Interpretation of ABGs." American Thoracic Society clinical education.

AreaWhat changed

lessons.title: Acute Dizziness
lessons.subtitle: Optimized lesson package · Facilitator-led interactive chalk talk for IM residents · 30–45 min total
lessons.credit: Adapted from TeachIM.org — Acute Dizziness (Meara Melton MD; ed. Brandon Fainstad MD; March 2021), CC BY-NC 4.0. Optimized June 2026: guideline currency verified against 2023 GRACE-3, one deck typo corrected, pedagogy restructured, assessments added. Companion deck: Acute_Dizziness_Optimized.pptx (original interactive board, surgically corrected).
objectives: Use timing and triggers (modified TiTrATE) to sort acute dizziness into four limited differential categories.
Perform a bedside Dix-Hallpike maneuver to diagnose episodic, positionally-triggered dizziness (BPPV).
Perform the HINTS exam to identify concerning features in continuous, spontaneous dizziness (acute vestibular syndrome).
Construct a four-step history-and-exam process to separate benign peripheral from dangerous central causes.

prework.q: Q1. Patients describe dizziness vaguely ("lightheaded," "woozy"). Why does the modern approach use timing and triggers instead of asking "what kind of dizziness?"
prework.a: Symptom-quality categories (vertigo / presyncope / disequilibrium / lightheadedness) are unreliable — patients give inconsistent, overlapping descriptions, and quality does not map cleanly to cause. Timing (episodic vs continuous, and how long) plus triggers (positional, spontaneous, trauma/toxin) is reproducible and points directly to the right bedside test. This is the TiTrATE approach (Newman-Toker, Neurol Clin 2015).

prework.q: Q2. A patient has continuous vertigo for 2 days. The head impulse test shows a corrective (catch-up) saccade. Is that reassuring or concerning?
prework.a: Reassuring — in a patient with continuous (acute vestibular) syndrome, an abnormal head impulse (a catch-up saccade) localizes to the peripheral vestibular nerve and points away from stroke. The dangerous finding is a normal/intact head impulse (no saccade) in someone with ongoing vertigo. This is the counter-intuitive heart of HINTS.

prework.q: Q3. Which is more dangerous to miss: a positive Dix-Hallpike, or a normal head impulse test in a patient with continuous vertigo?
prework.a: The normal head impulse. A positive Dix-Hallpike diagnoses benign BPPV. A normal head impulse in continuous vertigo is one of three HINTS findings that flag a central cause (posterior-circulation stroke) — time-sensitive and potentially fatal.

segments.label: Hook (2 min)
segments.html: "Two patients, same complaint: 'the room is spinning.' One has been spinning for 10 seconds every time she rolls over in bed; the other has been spinning continuously for two days. Same symptom — but they get completely different exams and one might be having a stroke." Ask the room which is which, then build the framework. (These become Cases 1 and 3.)

segments.label: Objective 1 — Timing + Triggers narrow the field
segments.html:
ASK: "Before you touch the patient — what two questions sort dizziness into a short list?" Click "Timing," then "Trigger." Timing splits episodic (seconds–hours) vs continuous (hours–days); triggers split positional, spontaneous, and trauma/toxin.

The four working categories: (1) Episodic, positionally triggered (lasts seconds–minutes → BPPV, orthostasis); (2) Spontaneous episodic (minutes–hours → vestibular migraine, Ménière, vasovagal/panic, TIA); (3) Continuous from trauma/toxin (meds, barotrauma); (4) Continuous spontaneous = acute vestibular syndrome (vestibular neuritis vs stroke).

CategoryTypical durationKey bedside stepLead diagnoses
Episodic, positionalSeconds–minutesDix-Hallpike; orthostaticsBPPV; orthostatic hypotension
Spontaneous episodicMinutes–hoursHistory; Weber/RinneVestibular migraine, Ménière, vasovagal/panic, TIA
Continuous, trauma/toxinPersistentMed review; otoscopyDrug-induced; barotrauma/TM perforation
Continuous, spontaneous (AVS)Hours–daysHINTS examVestibular neuritis vs posterior-circulation stroke
Pearl: Always probe cardiovascular risk factors, hearing loss/tinnitus, focal neuro symptoms, substance use, and medication changes — these reset your pre-test probability before the exam even begins.

segments.label: Objective 2 — Dix-Hallpike for positional dizziness
segments.html:
For episodic + positionally-triggered symptoms, the Dix-Hallpike diagnoses posterior-canal BPPV (canalith debris in the semicircular canal). A positive test (transient upbeat-torsional nystagmus + vertigo on lying back with the head turned 45°) = BPPV. A negative test → check orthostatics and reconsider other causes.

ASK: "Your Dix-Hallpike is positive — what do you do right now?" Click to reveal: treat at the bedside with the Epley canalith-repositioning maneuver.
Pearl (2023 GRACE-3): For pc-BPPV diagnosed by a positive Dix-Hallpike, perform the Epley at the time of diagnosis — strong recommendation, moderate-certainty evidence. The half-somersault maneuver is a reasonable home option but is less effective than Epley in small head-to-head trials (better patient adherence is its trade-off). Test characteristics of Dix-Hallpike: sensitivity ~79%, specificity ~75% — better at ruling in than ruling out.

segments.label: Objective 3 — HINTS for continuous spontaneous dizziness
segments.html:
Reserve HINTS for the patient with acute, persistent (continuous), spontaneous vertigo and nystagmus — the acute vestibular syndrome. It is not a test for triggered or episodic dizziness. Three components:

ComponentReassuring (peripheral)Concerning (central)
Head ImpulseCorrective catch-up saccade present (abnormal VOR = nerve problem)Normal/intact head impulse — no saccade (a brain problem)
I: NystagmusHorizontal, unidirectionalBidirectional (direction-changing) or vertical/torsional
Test of SkewNo vertical realignment on cover-uncoverVertical skew deviation (disconjugate)

Interpretation: ANY concerning finding → central (stroke/TIA) until proven otherwise. ALL three reassuring → peripheral (vestibular neuritis / labyrinthitis). The mnemonic INFARCT captures the dangerous trio: Impulse Normal, FAst-phase Alternating, Refixation on Cover Test.

ASK: "Your patient with 2 days of continuous vertigo has a normal head impulse and bidirectional nystagmus. MRI is read as negative at hour 4. Are you reassured?" Reveal: No. HINTS performed by a trained examiner out-performs early MRI-DWI, which misses up to ~15–20% of posterior-fossa strokes in the first 24–48 h (Kattah, Stroke 2009).
Pearl (2023 GRACE-3 caution): HINTS is only reliable in the correct patient (continuous AVS with nystagmus) and in trained hands. Applied to the wrong patient or by an untrained clinician it is inaccurate and can falsely reassure. Don't HINTS a patient without nystagmus or with merely positional symptoms.

segments.label: Objective 4 — Put it together; don't reach for the CT
segments.html:
The four-step process: (1) Timing → (2) Triggers → (3) the matching targeted exam (Dix-Hallpike or HINTS) → (4) diagnosis/disposition.

Pearl (2023 GRACE-3): Strong recommendation against routine noncontrast head CT to distinguish central from peripheral causes — CT is insensitive for acute posterior-circulation ischemia. If a central cause is suspected, the right test is MRI with DWI (and MRA), plus stroke-pathway activation — not a reflexive CT.

segments.label: Retrieval close (3 min)
segments.html: Close the laptop. Ask learners to rebuild the algorithm from memory: the two sorting questions, the test that matches each branch, and the single most dangerous finding (normal head impulse in continuous vertigo). Then have them state the take-home points before you show them.

cases.title: Case 1 — Episodic, positionally triggered
cases.stem: Two days of brief spinning (<1 min) triggered by turning her head, with nausea, interfering with daily activities. No hearing change, tinnitus, headache, vision change, weakness, or falls.
cases.reveal: Episodic, positionally triggered.

cases.title: Case 2 — Continuous, spontaneous (the dangerous one)
cases.stem: Sudden onset of persistent vertigo ~1 hour ago; cannot do anything because of severity. History of BPPV but couldn't do Epley at home this time. No hearing change, tinnitus, or vision change. Adds DM to her risk profile.
cases.reveal: Continuous, spontaneous — acute vestibular syndrome.

cases.title: Case 3 — Continuous, spontaneous (the benign mimic)
cases.stem: Three days of continuous room-spinning, lying in bed unable to work, nausea without vomiting. Tried half-somersault and Epley at home without relief. No hearing change, tinnitus, headache, vision change, weakness, or falls.
cases.reveal: Continuous, spontaneous — acute vestibular syndrome (same category as Case 2).

mcqs.stem: A 58-year-old man reports 3 days of continuous vertigo, nausea, and unsteadiness. He has spontaneous left-beating horizontal nystagmus. On horizontal head impulse testing there is no corrective saccade in either direction. Cover-uncover shows a small vertical realignment. Which is the most appropriate next step?
opts: MRI brain with DWI/MRA and stroke-pathway activation
mcqs.exp: D. A normal/intact head impulse (no catch-up saccade) plus a skew deviation in a patient with continuous vertigo is a central HINTS pattern — posterior-circulation stroke until proven otherwise. The right test is MRI-DWI/MRA, not CT. A falsely reassures a central pattern. B treats BPPV, which is positional/episodic, not continuous. C — noncontrast CT is insensitive for early posterior-fossa ischemia (GRACE-3 strong rec against using it to discriminate). E doesn't address the time-critical stroke concern.

mcqs.stem: A 34-year-old woman has recurrent 20-second episodes of spinning whenever she rolls over in bed or tips her head back, for one week. Between episodes she is asymptomatic. Neuro exam is normal. Which test best establishes the diagnosis?
mcqs.exp: B. Brief, positionally-triggered, episodic vertigo is the classic profile of posterior-canal BPPV, diagnosed by Dix-Hallpike. A HINTS is for continuous vertigo with nystagmus and would be inappropriate (and inaccurate) here. C imaging is unnecessary with a typical history and normal exam. D orthostatics are a reasonable adjunct if Dix-Hallpike is negative, but won't diagnose BPPV. E is not a first-line bedside test.

mcqs.stem: The Dix-Hallpike in the patient above is positive with transient upbeat-torsional nystagmus. What is the best immediate management?
opts: Order an MRI of the internal auditory canals
mcqs.exp: B. GRACE-3 gives a strong recommendation to perform Epley canalith-repositioning at the time of diagnosis for pc-BPPV. A/D chronic vestibular suppressants/benzodiazepines impair central compensation and don't treat the mechanical cause; meclizine for weeks is inappropriate. C/E are unnecessary for typical BPPV.

mcqs.stem: Which patient is the appropriate candidate for a HINTS exam?
opts: A patient with 15-second spinning episodes triggered by head movement
A patient with lightheadedness on standing and a 25-mmHg orthostatic SBP drop
A patient with continuous vertigo for 36 hours and spontaneous nystagmus
A patient with brief presyncope and palpitations
A patient with chronic imbalance and no nystagmus
mcqs.exp: C. HINTS is validated only for the acute vestibular syndrome: continuous, spontaneous vertigo with nystagmus. A is positional/episodic (Dix-Hallpike). B is orthostatic hypotension. D suggests a cardiac/presyncopal cause. E — without nystagmus, HINTS is uninterpretable; GRACE-3 warns it is inaccurate when applied to the wrong patient.

mcqs.stem: A 47-year-old woman with continuous vertigo for 2 days has an entirely reassuring HINTS (abnormal head impulse with catch-up saccade, unidirectional horizontal nystagmus, no skew) and normal hearing. She is diagnosed with vestibular neuritis. Regarding corticosteroids, which statement is most accurate?
opts: Steroids are strongly indicated and clearly improve quality-of-life outcomes
Steroids are contraindicated in vestibular neuritis
Steroids may be offered via shared decision-making within 3 days of onset, but evidence is weak
Steroids are only useful after 2 weeks of symptoms
High-dose steroids for 6 weeks are standard of care
mcqs.exp: C. GRACE-3 suggests shared decision-making on short-term steroids for vestibular neuritis presenting within 3 days — a conditional recommendation on very-low-certainty evidence (trials used weak endpoints like caloric recovery, not quality of life). A overstates the data; B is wrong (not contraindicated); D/E are not supported. Note: acute idiopathic sensorineural hearing loss is a clear steroid indication.

anki: What does TiTrATE stand for?
Timing, Triggers, And a Targeted Examination — the evidence-based approach to acute dizziness.
Why avoid sorting dizziness by symptom quality (vertigo vs lightheaded)?
Patient descriptions are vague, inconsistent, and don't map to cause; timing + triggers are reproducible and point to the right test.
Four categories in the modified TiTrATE framework?
(1) Episodic positionally triggered, (2) spontaneous episodic, (3) continuous from trauma/toxin, (4) continuous spontaneous (acute vestibular syndrome).
Duration of triggered episodic vs spontaneous episodic vestibular syndromes?
Triggered episodic: seconds–minutes. Spontaneous episodic: minutes–hours.
Which test for episodic, positionally triggered dizziness?
Dix-Hallpike maneuver (then orthostatics if negative).
What does a positive Dix-Hallpike diagnose?
Posterior-canal BPPV — canalith debris in the semicircular canal; transient upbeat-torsional nystagmus + vertigo.
Dix-Hallpike sensitivity/specificity?
~79% sensitive, ~75% specific — better at ruling in than ruling out.
Best immediate treatment for pc-BPPV (2023 GRACE-3)?
Epley canalith-repositioning maneuver at the time of diagnosis (strong recommendation).
Epley vs half-somersault maneuver?
Epley more effective in small trials; half-somersault is a home option with better adherence but lower efficacy.
When is the HINTS exam appropriate?
Only in acute vestibular syndrome: continuous, spontaneous vertigo WITH nystagmus.
Three components of HINTS?
Head Impulse, Nystagmus, Test of Skew.
Head impulse: which result is concerning in continuous vertigo?
A NORMAL/intact head impulse (no catch-up saccade) = central. An abnormal impulse with catch-up saccade = peripheral/reassuring.
Nystagmus findings — reassuring vs concerning?
Reassuring: horizontal, unidirectional. Concerning: bidirectional (direction-changing) or vertical/torsional.
Test of skew — reassuring vs concerning?
Reassuring: no vertical realignment on cover-uncover. Concerning: vertical skew deviation (disconjugate).
HINTS interpretation rule?
ANY concerning finding → central (stroke). ALL reassuring → peripheral (vestibular neuritis).
Why not rely on early MRI or noncontrast CT to exclude posterior stroke?
MRI-DWI misses ~15–20% of posterior-fossa strokes in the first 24–48 h; noncontrast CT is insensitive for early posterior ischemia (GRACE-3 strong rec against CT for this).
Steroids in vestibular neuritis (2023 GRACE-3)?
Offer via shared decision-making within 3 days of onset; weak/very-low-certainty evidence. Acute idiopathic SNHL is a clearer steroid indication.
lessons.dot:
#Acute dizziness/vertigo: sort by TIMING + TRIGGER, not symptom quality. timing * [episodic sec-min / episodic min-hr / continuous]. trigger * [positional / spontaneous / trauma-toxin]. category * [1 episodic-positional / 2 spontaneous-episodic / 3 continuous trauma-toxin / 4 continuous-spontaneous = AVS]. r/o central [posterior-circ stroke/TIA]; excl mimics [orthostasis, med tox, vestibular migraine, Meniere, vasovagal/panic, intox].
- core hx: CV risk [HTN/DM/AF/CAD/smoking], hearing loss/tinnitus, focal neuro sx, HA, meds/changes [AED/sedative/antiHTN/analgesic], substance use, recent trauma/barotrauma
- if CATEGORY 1 (episodic, positional): *Dix-Hallpike → +ve = pc-BPPV → tx Epley at bedside [strong rec GRACE-3]; -ve → orthostatics [SBP ↓≥20 / DBP ↓≥10 w/in 3min] + reconsider
- if CATEGORY 4 (continuous, spontaneous + nystagmus): *HINTS by trained exam ONLY
  - Head Impulse: catch-up saccade = peripheral / NORMAL-intact impulse = CENTRAL
  - Nystagmus: horiz unidirectional = peripheral / bidirectional or vertical = CENTRAL
  - Skew: none = peripheral / vertical skew = CENTRAL
  - ANY central finding → stroke pathway: *MRI-DWI + MRA [NOT noncontrast CT — insensitive, GRACE-3 rec against], ASA/statin, consider tPA/EVT per window
  - ALL reassuring → vestibular neuritis → antiemetic + short vestibular suppressant (limit days); steroids only via SDM if ≤3d [weak evidence]
- AVOID: routine noncontrast head CT to r/o posterior stroke; HINTS in pts w/o nystagmus or w/ only positional sx [inaccurate]
- red flags → central w/u: new HA/neck pain, focal deficit, truncal ataxia (can't sit/stand), normal head impulse w/ continuous vertigo, direction-changing or vertical nystagmus, skew
- dispo: * [home w/ tx if clearly peripheral / admit-stroke pathway if any central feature]
takehome: Timing + triggers (modified TiTrATE) sort acute dizziness into four categories and tell you which bedside test to do — before you touch the patient.
Episodic + positional → Dix-Hallpike; if positive (pc-BPPV), treat with Epley at the bedside.
Continuous + spontaneous (with nystagmus) → HINTS; ANY concerning finding (normal head impulse, bidirectional/vertical nystagmus, skew) = central until proven otherwise.
HINTS by a trained examiner out-performs early MRI-DWI for posterior stroke — but only in the right patient, and only with nystagmus present.
Don't reach for noncontrast CT to exclude posterior-circulation stroke — it's insensitive (2023 GRACE-3 strong rec against).

lessons.title: Acute Kidney Injury
lessons.subtitle: Optimized lesson package · Facilitator-led interactive chalk talk for IM residents · 25–35 min
lessons.credit: Adapted from TeachIM.org — Acute Kidney Injury (Mack Holmberg, MD; expert review Raj Mehrotra, MD, MS, Univ. of Washington Nephrology; 2020), CC BY-NC 4.0. Optimized June 10, 2026: framework verified current (KDIGO 2012 unchanged), two deck typos corrected on both boards, a framework-notes slide appended, pedagogy restructured and assessments added. Companion files: AKI_Optimized.pptx (presenter board), AKI-Learner-Board-Final.pptx (learning board, corrected), AKI-learner-handout.pdf.
objectives: Build a framework that differentiates the cause of AKI as pre-renal, intra-renal, or post-renal.
Diagnose and manage common AKI presentations using that framework and the bedside tests (history, urinalysis, FeNa, renal ultrasound).

prework.q: 1. Give the KDIGO definition of AKI and explain why eGFR shouldn't be reported during one.
prework.a: AKI = ↑Cr ≥0.3 mg/dL in 48 h, OR ↑Cr ≥1.5× baseline in 7 days, OR urine output <0.5 mL/kg/h. eGFR equations assume a steady-state GFR, which is false during AKI, so the number is invalid.

prework.q: 2. A patient is oliguric with a bland UA, normal renal US, and FeNa 0.3%. Which bucket, and why?
prework.a: Pre-renal: bland sediment + no hydronephrosis + FeNa <1% while oliguric all point to a perfusion problem rather than intrinsic injury or obstruction.

prework.q: 3. Name the three intra-renal compartments and one classic urine finding for each.
prework.a: Glomerular — dysmorphic RBCs / RBC casts; Tubular (ATN) — muddy-brown granular casts; Interstitial (AIN) — pyuria with WBC casts. (Vascular is the fourth sub-bucket: MAHA / cholesterol emboli.)

segments.label: Hook (2 min)
segments.html:
Ask the room: "How do you know a patient has an AKI?" Steer to an abrupt rise in Cr (often with falling urine output) and define it formally with KDIGO. Then drop the key point that anchors the whole talk: eGFR is meaningless during AKI because the equations assume a stable GFR.

ASK — "What's your framework for an AKI differential?" If they don't say pre/intra/post, introduce it and build from there.

segments.label: Segment 1 — Pre- and post-renal first (Objective 1)
segments.html:
The board deliberately covers the simpler left side before the busy intra-renal middle. Pre-renal = low effective arterial blood volume; post-renal = obstruction.

BucketDiagnosed byCauses
Pre-renalHistory + bland UA + no hydronephrosis + FeNa <1% (if oliguric)Hypovolemia, sepsis, HF (cardiorenal), cirrhosis (hepatorenal); vasogenic (NSAIDs/CNIs constrict afferent; ACEi/ARB dilate efferent); altered vasculature (RVT, IAH)
Post-renalHydronephrosis on renal USUrethra (stricture, BPH), bladder (clot, malignancy, neurogenic), ureter (calculi, malignancy/LAN, retroperitoneal fibrosis)
Pearl: Unilateral ureteral obstruction does not cause AKI unless it's the single functioning kidney — you need bilateral obstruction. Rule out obstruction early; it's the most reversible cause.
ASK — "Your patient is on furosemide and the FeNa is 2%. Can you trust it?" (No — use FeUrea <35% on diuretics.)

segments.label: Segment 2 — Intra-renal by compartment (Objective 1)
segments.html:
Break the intra-renal bucket anatomically: glomerular → tubulointerstitial → vascular.

CompartmentKey entitiesUrine clue
GlomerularGN — immune-complex, pauci-immune, anti-GBM; crescentic = RPGNDysmorphic RBCs, RBC casts, proteinuria
TubularATN (ischemic / toxic incl. contrast) — most common; tubular obstruction (myoglobin, light chains, crystals)Muddy-brown granular casts
InterstitialAIN — beta-lactams, NSAIDs, PPIs, immunotherapy; infection; Sjögren's/sarcoidPyuria, WBC casts (eosinophils insensitive)
VascularMAHA (TTP/HUS, scleroderma renal crisis, malignant HTN, HELLP); cholesterol emboliSchistocytes on smear; ± eosinophilia (emboli)
ASK — "Septic patient, fluid-resuscitated, still rising Cr at 24 h, now anuric, muddy-brown casts. Diagnosis?" (Ischemic ATN.)

segments.label: Segment 3 — Apply it (Objective 2)
segments.html: Run the six cases below. For each, ask for the bucket, the diagnosis/etiology, and the next management step before revealing. Reinforce the management-by-bucket rule: pre-renal → perfusion; intra-renal → treat cause + pull nephrotoxins; post-renal → relieve obstruction.

segments.label: Retrieval close (3 min)
segments.html: Erase the board. Have learners rebuild the three buckets, the three discriminating tests, and the four intra-renal compartments from memory.

cases.title: Case 1 — 35F, 1 week of vomiting/diarrhea, low UOP
cases.stem: Cr 1.5 (baseline 0.7), BUN 45, FeNa <0.2%; UA hyaline casts; renal US normal.
cases.reveal: Pre-renal azotemia from hypovolemia (BUN:Cr >20, very low FeNa, bland UA). Control nausea and give aggressive rehydration.

cases.title: Case 2 — 70M, worsening obstructive voiding after starting allergy meds
cases.stem: Cr 2.0 (baseline 0.8), BUN 40, FeNa 1.5%; UA normal; renal US bilateral moderate hydronephrosis.
cases.reveal: Post-renal AKI from BPH plus anticholinergic-induced retention. Stop the anticholinergic, start tamsulosin, and consider a urinary catheter to decompress.

cases.title: Case 3 — 23-year-old transman, SLE off therapy, arthralgias + dark urine
cases.stem: Cr 3.6 (baseline 0.9), BUN 30, FeNa 2.8%; UA dysmorphic RBCs and RBC casts; renal US normal.
cases.reveal: Acute glomerulonephritis from a lupus flare. Induction therapy with steroids plus cyclophosphamide or mycophenolate (coordinate with nephrology/rheumatology).

cases.title: Case 4 — 40F, day 5 of amoxicillin/clavulanate, rash, low UOP
cases.stem: Cr 2.5 (baseline 0.9), BUN 30, FeNa 2.2%; UA proteinuria, WBCs & WBC casts, no eosinophils; renal US normal.
cases.reveal: Acute interstitial nephritis from the beta-lactam. Discontinue the antibiotic, repeat labs in ~3 days, and consider biopsy ± steroids if not improving. (Absence of urine eosinophils does not exclude AIN.)

cases.title: Case 5 — 42M, DM2, pneumonia with severe sepsis, rising Cr despite fluids, now anuric
cases.stem: Cr 3.4 (2.5 on admission, baseline 1.2), FeNa 2.3%; UA muddy-brown casts; renal US normal.
cases.reveal: Ischemic ATN from prolonged hypoperfusion (hypovolemia + sepsis). Monitor electrolytes, maintain euvolemia, avoid overhydration now that he's anuric, and avoid nephrotoxins.

cases.title: Case 6 — 36M, diffuse myalgias + dark urine after a new intense workout
cases.stem: Cr 3.6 (baseline 1.2), BUN 30, FeNa 3%, CK 20,000; dipstick 3+ blood with no RBCs on micro; renal US normal.
cases.reveal: Pigment (myoglobin) tubular obstruction from rhabdomyolysis, plus a hemodynamic hit from NSAIDs. Dipstick reads myoglobin as "blood" without RBCs. Aggressive IV fluid resuscitation and stop the NSAIDs.

mcqs.stem: A 68-year-old man on chronic furosemide presents with AKI. He looks dry. Cr 2.4 (baseline 1.1), FeNa 2.5%, BUN:Cr 28, bland UA, no hydronephrosis. Which test best clarifies the etiology?
mcqs.exp: B. Diuretics drive sodium wasting and falsely raise FeNa, so it can't distinguish pre-renal here. FeUrea <35% supports a pre-renal state on diuretics. The clinical picture (dry, high BUN:Cr, bland UA) already favors pre-renal; the others don't address the diuretic confounder.

mcqs.stem: A 75-year-old woman with new anuria and suprapubic fullness has Cr 3.0 (baseline 1.0). What is the single most important first test?
mcqs.exp: B. Abrupt anuria with suprapubic fullness screams obstruction — renal US shows hydronephrosis (and a distended bladder), and relief is curative. Rule out the reversible post-renal cause before pursuing intrinsic workup.

mcqs.stem: A 30-year-old develops AKI 5 days into a course of a PPI and an NSAID, with low-grade fever, a rash, pyuria, and WBC casts. Which is most likely?
mcqs.exp: C. Drug exposure (NSAID, PPI), fever, rash, sterile pyuria, and WBC casts are classic AIN. A shows muddy-brown casts without the allergic features. D shows dysmorphic RBCs/RBC casts. B/E have bland sediment. Stop the offending drug; consider steroids if no improvement.

mcqs.stem: A 50-year-old man has AKI after a CT with iodinated contrast and a stormy ICU course with hypotension. UA shows muddy-brown granular casts; FeNa 3%. Best description?
mcqs.exp: B. Ischemic plus toxic (contrast) injury with muddy-brown granular casts and an elevated FeNa while injured is ATN. Pre-renal would have FeNa <1% and bland urine; the other options have distinct sediment or imaging findings. Supportive care; remove ongoing nephrotoxins.

mcqs.stem: Which statement about evaluating AKI is correct?
opts: eGFR accurately reflects function during AKI
A unilateral obstructed ureter reliably causes AKI
FeNa <1% in an oliguric patient suggests a pre-renal state
Absence of urine eosinophils excludes AIN
Renal US is unnecessary if the FeNa is elevated
mcqs.exp: C. A low FeNa while oliguric supports avid sodium retention (pre-renal). A is false — eGFR assumes steady state. B is false — you need bilateral obstruction (or a single functioning kidney). D is false — eosinophils are insensitive and nonspecific. E is false — always rule out obstruction with US.

anki: What is the KDIGO definition of AKI?
An abrupt drop in GFR, defined as ↑Cr ≥0.3 mg/dL within 48 h, OR ↑Cr ≥1.5× baseline within the prior 7 days, OR urine output <0.5 mL/kg/h. Characterize as non-oliguric, oliguric, or anuric.
Why is eGFR not meaningful during an AKI?
eGFR equations assume a steady-state (stable) GFR. In AKI, GFR is changing rapidly, so the reported eGFR does not reflect true kidney function.
What are the three anatomic categories of AKI?
Pre-renal (decreased effective arterial blood volume), intra-renal/intrinsic (glomerular, tubulointerstitial, vascular), and post-renal (obstruction).
How is pre-renal AKI diagnosed at the bedside?
Suggestive history (volume loss, low EABV), a bland urinalysis, no hydronephrosis on US, and — if oliguric — a FeNa <1% (FeUrea <35% is more reliable if the patient is on diuretics).
What three features point toward an intra-renal etiology?
"Active" urine sediment (supports intra-renal), an elevated FeNa while oliguric (rules out pre-renal), and a normal renal ultrasound (rules out post-renal).
What are the causes of decreased effective arterial blood volume (pre-renal)?

True hypovolemia, sepsis (distributive), heart failure (cardiorenal), and cirrhosis (hepatorenal). Also altered vasculature: renal vein thrombosis, renal venous hypertension (CHF), and intra-abdominal hypertension.
What is "vasogenic" pre-renal AKI and its classic drug causes?
A change in arteriolar tone reducing GFR: afferent constriction from NSAIDs and calcineurin inhibitors; efferent dilation from ACE inhibitors and ARBs.
List the three subcategories of intra-renal AKI.
Glomerular (glomerulonephritis), tubulointerstitial (acute tubular necrosis, acute interstitial nephritis, tubular obstruction), and vascular (MAHA/TTP-HUS, cholesterol emboli).
How is acute glomerulonephritis recognized on urinalysis?
Dysmorphic RBCs and/or RBC casts, proteinuria, leukocyturia, ± hypertension. GN is grouped into immune-complex, pauci-immune, and anti-GBM; crescentic forms = RPGN.
What is the most common intra-renal cause of AKI, and its urine finding?
Acute tubular necrosis (ATN) — ischemic (prolonged hypoperfusion) or toxic (including iodinated contrast). Urine shows muddy-brown granular casts.
What drugs and findings suggest acute interstitial nephritis (AIN)?

Antimicrobials (esp. beta-lactams), NSAIDs, PPIs, immunotherapy; also infection and systemic disease (Sjögren's, sarcoidosis). UA: pyuria, WBC casts, proteinuria; eosinophils are insensitive/nonspecific.
What causes tubular obstruction within the kidney?
Pigment/cast nephropathy (myoglobin, hemoglobin, light chains/paraproteins) and crystal nephropathy (uric acid, acyclovir, ethylene glycol, methotrexate).
How is post-renal AKI diagnosed and why look early?
Hydronephrosis on renal ultrasound. Rule it out early because obstruction is typically rapidly reversible once relieved.
Does unilateral ureteral obstruction cause AKI?
No — not unless it obstructs a single functioning kidney. AKI from ureteral obstruction requires bilateral obstruction (or obstruction of the only working kidney).
A patient on diuretics has a FeNa of 2% but seems volume-depleted. What test is more reliable?
Fractional excretion of urea (FeUrea); a value <35% supports a pre-renal state even when diuretics raise the FeNa.
A rhabdomyolysis patient (CK 20,000) has dipstick 3+ blood but no RBCs on microscopy. Mechanism of AKI?

Pigment (myoglobin) cast nephropathy — heme-pigment tubular obstruction/toxicity. Dipstick detects myoglobin as "blood" without RBCs. Treat with aggressive IV fluids and stop nephrotoxins (e.g., NSAIDs).
What are the management principles by AKI category?
Pre-renal → restore perfusion (volume/hemodynamics). Intra-renal → treat the cause, remove nephrotoxins (NSAIDs, contrast, ACEi/ARB), supportive care. Post-renal → relieve the obstruction.
In ATN that is now anuric, what fluid pitfall should you avoid?
Avoid overhydration — once anuric, aggressive fluids cause volume overload. Maintain euvolemia, monitor electrolytes, and avoid nephrotoxins.
lessons.dot:
#AKI: Cr *___ from baseline *___ (KDIGO: ↑Cr ≥0.3/48h OR ≥1.5x/7d OR UOP <0.5 mL/kg/h), *[non-oliguric / oliguric / anuric]
fx: pre / intra / post — eGFR NOT valid in AKI
likely 2/2 *[prerenal-↓EABV / intrarenal-(GN/ATN/AIN/vascular) / postrenal-obstruction]
core w/u: hx + vol exam + UA w/ micro (sediment) + FeNa *[<1% if oliguric → prerenal] (FeUrea <35% if on diuretics) + renal US [hydronephrosis?] + BMP/CBC
  - prerenal: ↓EABV [hypovolemia/sepsis/HF/cirrhosis]; vasogenic [NSAID/CNI afferent, ACEi/ARB efferent] → restore perfusion
  - intrarenal: glomerular [dysm RBC/RBC casts] / tubular ATN [muddy-brown casts, ischemic vs toxic incl contrast] / AIN [drug, pyuria/WBC casts] / vascular [MAHA, emboli] → treat cause
  - postrenal: hydronephrosis on US [BPH/stone/clot/malignancy/RPF] → relieve obstruction (cath/stent/PCN); NOTE unilateral ≠ AKI unless single kidney
targeted only: FeUrea (on diuretics); UPEP/SPEP/light chains (cast neph); CK/UA-blood-no-RBC (rhabdo); complement/ANCA/anti-GBM (GN); smear/LDH/hapto/plt (MAHA)
mgmt: *[IVF / diuretic-hold]; D/C nephrotoxins [NSAID, contrast, ACEi/ARB, aminoglycoside]; renally dose meds; maintain euvolemia (AVOID overhydration if anuric); monitor K/HCO3/PO4
red flags → urgent: refractory hyperK, acidosis, volume overload, uremic sx → consider RRT (AEIOU)
dispo: *[admit / obs]; nephrology consult if intrinsic/unclear/RRT
takehome: AKI = an abrupt rise in Cr (KDIGO thresholds); eGFR is meaningless during AKI.
Three buckets: pre-renal (↓EABV), intra-renal (glomerular / tubulointerstitial / vascular), post-renal (obstruction).
Three quick discriminators: history + bland UA + FeNa <1% (if oliguric) → pre-renal; active sediment → intra-renal; hydronephrosis on US → post-renal.
Rule out obstruction early with renal US — it's the most reversible cause; unilateral obstruction needs the single functioning kidney to cause AKI.
ATN (ischemic/toxic) is the most common intra-renal cause; remove nephrotoxins and maintain euvolemia.

1. Levey AS, Eckardt K-U, Dorman NM, et al. Nomenclature for Kidney Function and Disease: Executive Summary and Glossary From a KDIGO Consensus Conference. Kidney Int Rep. 2020;5(7):965-972.
  2. Rangaswami J, Bhalla V, Blair JEA, et al. Cardiorenal Syndrome: Classification, Pathophysiology, Diagnosis, and Treatment Strategies: A Scientific Statement From the AHA. Circulation. 2019;139(16):e840-e878.
  3. Cited in optimization: KDIGO Clinical Practice Guideline for Acute Kidney Injury. Kidney Int Suppl. 2012;2(1):1-138. · Mercado MG, Smith DK, Guard EL. Acute Kidney Injury: Diagnosis and Management. Am Fam Physician. 2019;100(11):687-694.
