lessons.title: Acute Liver Failure
lessons.subtitle: Optimized lesson package · Facilitator-led interactive chalk talk for IM residents · 20 min (35–40 min with cases)
lessons.credit: Adapted from TeachIM.org — Acute Liver Failure (Mowry, Kriss; sec. eds. Mun, Haydek; exec. ed. Zhang; Apr 2022), CC BY-NC 4.0. Optimized June 2026: guideline currency verified, two deck typos corrected, pedagogy restructured, assessments added. Companion deck: Acute_Liver_Failure_Optimized.pptx (original interactive board, surgically corrected).
objectives: Define acute liver failure (ALF) and distinguish it from acute liver injury (ALI).
Evaluate a patient with ALF and identify the most common causes by category.
Build a framework for the initial ICU management of ALF — complications, NAC, prognostication, and transfer.

prework.q: 1. What three criteria, all required, define acute liver failure?
prework.a: (1) Acute liver injury (onset <26 weeks, no chronic liver disease — except HBV, AIH, Wilson’s), (2) coagulopathy (INR >1.5), and (3) hepatic encephalopathy. The encephalopathy is what converts "acute liver injury" into "acute liver failure."

prework.q: 2. What are the two most common causes of ALF in the US, and which single drug leads?
prework.a: Drugs/toxins and viral hepatitis (>50% combined). Acetaminophen is the single most common cause in the US/Europe (~50% of cases). Alcohol alone does NOT cause ALF.

prework.q: 3. One empiric drug should be started in essentially every ALF patient. What is it, and why even in non-acetaminophen cases?
prework.a: N-acetylcysteine (NAC). It replenishes glutathione in acetaminophen toxicity and improves transplant-free survival even in non-acetaminophen ALF, especially early-stage disease with low-grade encephalopathy.

segments.label: Hook (0:00–0:02)
segments.html:
"A previously healthy 29-year-old comes in with AST in the 9,000s and an INR of 2.3 — but they're awake and oriented. Do they have acute liver failure? Not yet. The moment they get confused on hospital day 2, everything changes — the clock, the ICU, the transplant call." The talk turns on that single distinction and what to do once you cross it.

ASK — "What's the one finding that separates acute liver injury from acute liver failure?" Let them arrive at hepatic encephalopathy.

segments.label: Objective 1 — Definition (0:02–0:08)
segments.html:
ALF is rare but lethal (20–80% mortality by etiology). Reveal the three required criteria one at a time:

CriterionDetail
1. Acute liver injury↑AST/ALT and bilirubin; onset <26 weeks; no chronic liver disease (exceptions: HBV, autoimmune hepatitis, Wilson’s).
2. CoagulopathyINR >1.5 (no anticoagulant). Marker of synthetic dysfunction and a key prognostic factor.
3. Hepatic encephalopathyUsually the last to appear; the criterion that defines ALF. Graded I–IV (West Haven); higher grade = higher cerebral-edema risk.
Pearl. West Haven grades: I = altered sleep-wake/attention; II = lethargy + asterixis; III = somnolence/stupor, gross disorientation; IV = coma. Grade III–IV warrants intubation for airway protection.

segments.label: Objective 2 — Etiologies & evaluation (0:08–0:14)
segments.html:
ASK — "Give me a differential by category, most to least common in the US." Then reveal each category and its work-up.
CategoryCausesInitial work-up
Drugs/toxins (~50%)Acetaminophen (lead cause), supplements, Amanita mushrooms; statins/AEDs/antibioticsAPAP & salicylate level, tox screen, med/supplement review vs LiverTox
ViralHepatitis A, B, D, E; rarely HSV/VZVViral hepatitis serologies + PCRs; HIV (co-transmission risk)
Ischemic/vascularBudd-Chiari, hepatic artery thrombosis, shock liverAssess for shock/hypoperfusion; liver US with Doppler
OtherAutoimmune hepatitis, HELLP / acute fatty liver of pregnancy, Wilson’sANA, anti-smooth-muscle Ab, IgG; pregnancy test; ceruloplasmin, 24-h urine copper; liver biopsy if unrevealing
Pearl. Alcohol does NOT cause ALF, though it can worsen another insult (e.g., acetaminophen). For Wilson’s in a young patient, an alkaline phosphatase : total-bilirubin ratio <4 is a clue; ceruloplasmin can be normal acutely.

segments.label: Objective 3 — Management (0:14–0:24)
segments.html:
All ALF goes to the ICU. Four steps:

ASK — "What complications kill ALF patients, and which one makes us avoid IV fluids and avoid correcting the INR?"
StepKey actions
1. Address complicationsCerebral edema — q1–2h neuro checks, intubate for grade III–IV HE, head-of-bed >30°, hypertonic saline/mannitol, hyperventilate if intubated, minimize IVF; arterial ammonia >150–200 flags high ICH risk. Hypoglycemia — q1–2h glucose, dextrose infusion. Multiorgan failure — vent/pressors/RRT; TTE to exclude cardiogenic shock. Coagulopathy — vitamin K ok; give FFP/factors ONLY for active bleeding (preserves INR’s prognostic value). Infection — high risk; some give prophylactic abx/antivirals.
2. Give NACStart empirically in all ALF — improves transplant-free survival even in non-acetaminophen cases (greatest in early/low-grade HE). Low threshold for acyclovir if febrile/HSV suspected.
3. PrognosticateWith hepatology, apply King’s College Criteria (± MELD) to identify those unlikely to recover spontaneously → transplant candidates.
4. TransferSend to a transplant-capable center EARLY — before progression precludes safe transfer.
Pearl. Two counterintuitive ALF rules: don’t reflexively correct the INR (it’s your prognostic compass, and these patients are often not truly "auto-anticoagulated"), and go easy on IV fluids even with rising creatinine — volume can worsen cerebral edema.

segments.label: King’s College Criteria
segments.html:
Acetaminophen ALFNon-acetaminophen ALF
Arterial pH <7.3 OR all of: INR >6.5 (PT >100), Cr >3.3 mg/dL, and grade III–IV HEINR >6.5 (PT >100) OR any 3 of: age <10 or >40; HAV/HBV/drug-related; jaundice >7 d before HE; INR >3.5 (PT >50); total bilirubin >18 mg/dL

segments.label: Retrieval close (0:24–0:25)
segments.html: Board off: "Rebuild the three ALF criteria, the top two etiology categories, the one empiric drug, and the two counterintuitive ICU rules." Fill gaps from what they reconstruct.

cases.title: Case 1 — Acetaminophen
cases.stem: 29-year-old with recurrent pancreatitis, taking 1000 mg acetaminophen q4h, presents with 24 h of nausea, vomiting, anorexia, RUQ pain. Exam: stable, alert & oriented ×4, no asterixis. Labs: AST 9290, ALT 4550, ALP 118, Tbili 2.3, INR 2.3. On HD2: confused, oriented to self only, asterixis.
cases.reveal: Acute liver INJURY — not ALF. Despite massive transaminases and INR 2.3, there is no encephalopathy at presentation, so the third criterion isn’t met. On HD2 the new confusion/asterixis completes the triad → acute liver failure.

cases.title: Case 2 — Fulminant hepatitis B with rising ICP
cases.stem: 35-year-old with ALF from fulminant hepatitis B; initially grade 1 HE, normal head CT. On HD2: new nausea/vomiting unresponsive to antiemetics. VS: HR 58, BP 160/88, RR 20, SpO2 94% on 2L. Exam: somnolent but arousable, oriented to self, mild asterixis. Labs: glucose 180; ABG 7.2/54/80/23; ammonia 202.
cases.reveal: Repeat head CT to evaluate for elevated ICP. The bradycardia + hypertension (Cushing reflex) and ammonia >200 predict intracranial hypertension. CT shows early cerebral edema without midline shift or herniation.

cases.title: Case 3 — Shock liver
cases.stem: 69-year-old with CHF, HTN, T2DM admitted with abdominal pain and fatigue. VS: HR 145, BP 82/52. Exam: moderate distress, cold extremities, oriented to self only, asterixis. Labs: Cr 2.4 (baseline 0.9), ALT 3600, AST 5600, INR 2.2; troponin <0.04, BNP 2400; TTE LVEF 10–20%. Hepatitis serologies negative.
cases.reveal: Cardiogenic shock → shock liver (ischemic hepatitis). The hypotension, cold extremities, and severely reduced LVEF point to hypoperfusion; massive AST/ALT with AST > ALT is classic for ischemic injury.

cases.title: Case 4 — Non-APAP ALF meeting KCC
cases.stem: 59-year-old, ESRD s/p kidney transplant on chronic immunosuppression, presents to a small community hospital with fever, chills, and ALF. VS: T 38.8 °C, HR 98, BP 117/58. Exam: jaundiced, lethargic, mild asterixis. Labs: WBC 13K, AST 2300, ALT 1960, Tbili 22, INR 4.2, Cr 1.8. APAP level and urine tox negative.
cases.reveal: Empiric acyclovir — the patient is febrile and immunosuppressed; cover HSV until the PCR returns. (Not Tylenol, not prednisone, not charcoal.)

mcqs.stem: A 24-year-old woman presents 30 h after an acetaminophen overdose. AST 6200, ALT 5800, INR 2.1, Cr 1.0, alert and fully oriented, no asterixis. Which statement is correct?
opts: She meets criteria for acute liver failure
She has acute liver injury; she will meet ALF criteria only if encephalopathy develops
NAC is not indicated this late after ingestion
FFP should be given to correct the INR
Alcohol co-ingestion is the likely driver
mcqs.exp: B. Without hepatic encephalopathy she has acute liver injury, not failure. A wrong: HE is required. C wrong: NAC is still indicated in established acetaminophen hepatotoxicity regardless of timing. D wrong: correct the INR only for active bleeding — it’s a prognostic marker. E wrong: alcohol does not cause ALF.

mcqs.stem: A patient with ALF and grade III encephalopathy has worsening somnolence. Arterial ammonia is 210 µmol/L. Which intervention is most appropriate to address the leading concern?
opts: Fresh frozen plasma to normalize INR
Lactulose titrated to 6 stools/day as sole therapy
mcqs.exp: C. Ammonia >150–200 with worsening HE signals intracranial hypertension/cerebral edema — treat with hypertonic saline/mannitol, HOB >30°, q1h neuro checks, and airway protection. A wrong: minimize IVF — volume worsens cerebral edema. B wrong: don’t correct INR without bleeding. D wrong: lactulose alone is inadequate for ICH in ALF. E wrong: RRT is used for renal failure/severe metabolic derangement, not as first-line ammonia clearance here.

mcqs.stem: Which patient with ALF should be referred to a transplant center earliest?
opts: A 30-year-old with acetaminophen ALF, arterial pH 7.36, INR 3.0, grade I HE
A 45-year-old with acetaminophen ALF, arterial pH 7.25 after resuscitation
A 28-year-old with acute liver injury, INR 1.4, no encephalopathy
A 50-year-old with stable chronic hepatitis C cirrhosis
A 35-year-old with mild transaminitis and normal INR
mcqs.exp: B. In acetaminophen ALF, an arterial pH <7.3 after adequate resuscitation alone meets King’s College Criteria — a strong predictor of death without transplant. A wrong: pH normal and the full alternative KCC set isn’t met. C/E wrong: acute injury without HE is not ALF. D wrong: stable chronic cirrhosis is a different pathway, not ALF.

mcqs.stem: A 26-year-old presents with ALF; ALP 90, total bilirubin 28 mg/dL (ALP:Tbili <4), Coombs-negative hemolytic anemia, and a low uric acid. Which diagnosis should you pursue?
mcqs.exp: B — Wilson’s disease. Young patient, very high bilirubin with a low alkaline phosphatase (ALP:Tbili ratio <4), Coombs-negative hemolysis, and low uric acid are classic for acute Wilsonian ALF. Send ceruloplasmin (may be normal acutely), 24-h urine copper, and slit-lamp exam. The other options don’t fit this constellation.

mcqs.stem: Which statement about NAC in acute liver failure is most accurate?
opts: NAC is only beneficial in acetaminophen-induced ALF
NAC should be withheld until the etiology is confirmed
NAC improves transplant-free survival in non-acetaminophen ALF, especially with low-grade encephalopathy
NAC is contraindicated once encephalopathy develops
NAC reliably reverses established cerebral edema
mcqs.exp: C. NAC improves transplant-free survival in non-acetaminophen ALF, with the greatest benefit in early-stage disease and low-grade HE; it should be started empirically in all ALF. A/B/D wrong: give it early and broadly. E wrong: NAC does not treat cerebral edema.

anki: Three required criteria for ALF?
Acute liver injury (<26 wks, no chronic liver disease) + coagulopathy (INR >1.5) + hepatic encephalopathy.
What separates ALF from acute liver injury?
Hepatic encephalopathy.
Exceptions allowing ALF despite preexisting liver disease?
HBV, autoimmune hepatitis, Wilson's disease.
Most common ALF cause in the US?
Acetaminophen (drugs/toxins ~50%).
Two categories causing >50% of ALF?
Drugs/toxins and viral hepatitis.
Does alcohol cause ALF?
No — but it can worsen another insult (e.g., acetaminophen).
West Haven HE grades I–IV?
I altered sleep-wake/attention; II lethargy + asterixis; III stupor/gross disorientation; IV coma.
Empiric drug for all ALF?
N-acetylcysteine (NAC) — improves transplant-free survival even in non-APAP ALF.
When is NAC benefit greatest in non-APAP ALF?
Early-stage disease with low-grade (I–II) hepatic encephalopathy.
Why avoid correcting INR in ALF?
INR is a key prognostic marker; reverse only for active bleeding (vitamin K is ok).
Why minimize IV fluids in ALF?
Can precipitate/worsen cerebral edema (even with rising creatinine).
Ammonia threshold predicting intracranial hypertension?
Arterial ammonia >150–200 µmol/L.
HE grade prompting intubation?
Grade III–IV (airway protection).
King's College Criteria for acetaminophen ALF?
pH <7.3, OR all of: INR >6.5, Cr >3.3 mg/dL, grade III–IV HE.
King's College Criteria for non-APAP ALF?
INR >6.5, OR ≥3 of: age <10/>40; HAV/HBV/drug; jaundice >7 d before HE; INR >3.5; Tbili >18.
Wilson's clue in acute ALF?
ALP:total-bilirubin ratio <4, young age, Coombs-negative hemolysis; ceruloplasmin may be normal acutely.
Most important ALF disposition step?
Early transfer to a liver-transplant center before progression precludes safe transfer.
lessons.dot:
#Acute liver failure (ALF): acute liver injury + INR>1.5 + hepatic encephalopathy *[grade I-IV]
 dx criteria met: injury <26wk *[y/n], no chronic liver dz (exc HBV/AIH/Wilson) *, INR *>1.5, HE grade *
  -- if NO encephalopathy → this is acute liver INJURY, not failure
 likely 2/2 *[APAP/other drug-toxin/viral/ischemic-shock liver/AIH/preg(HELLP,AFLP)/Wilson/cryptogenic]
 core w/u: APAP + salicylate lvl, utox, med/supp review (LiverTox); viral hep serologies+PCR + HIV; liver US w/ doppler; ANA/ASMA/IgG; preg test; ceruloplasmin + 24h urine Cu; +/- liver bx
ICU MANAGEMENT:
 #1 complications:
   - cerebral edema: q1-2h neuro checks; intubate if HE gr III-IV; HOB>30; hypertonic saline/mannitol; hyperventilate if intubated; MINIMIZE IVF; arterial NH3 *>150-200 = high ICH risk
   - hypoglycemia: q1-2h glucose, D10/D5 infusion
   - MOF: pressors/vent/RRT prn; TTE to r/o cardiogenic
   - coagulopathy: vit K ok; FFP/factors ONLY if active bleeding (preserve INR prognostic value)
   - infection: high risk; consider empiric abx/antivirals; acyclovir if febrile/HSV susp
 #2 NAC: start empirically ALL ALF (transplant-free survival benefit, even non-APAP)
 #3 prognosticate w/ hepatology: King's College Criteria *[met y/n] +/- MELD *
 #4 TRANSFER early to LT-capable center if potential transplant candidate (before progression precludes transfer)
 -- DO NOT use negative KCC to defer transfer (specific, not sensitive)
takehome: ALF = acute liver injury (<26 wks, no chronic liver disease*) + coagulopathy (INR >1.5) + hepatic encephalopathy. HE is what makes it "failure." (*exceptions: HBV, AIH, Wilson’s.)
Drugs/toxins (esp. acetaminophen) and viral hepatitis cause >50%; also screen ischemic, autoimmune, pregnancy-related, and Wilson’s. Alcohol alone does NOT cause ALF.
ICU care: manage complications (cerebral edema, hypoglycemia, multiorgan failure, coagulopathy, infection); reverse coagulopathy only for bleeding; minimize IV fluids.
Start NAC early in all ALF — benefit extends to non-acetaminophen cases.
Prognosticate with King’s College Criteria (± MELD) and TRANSFER to a transplant center early.

extras.label: Currency Note (2026)
extras.html:
The standing teaching is current per AASLD acute-liver-failure practice guidance. These refinements are surfaced in the HTML and on the appended deck slide:

  • NAC nuance: the transplant-free survival benefit in non-acetaminophen ALF is greatest in EARLY-stage disease with low-grade (I–II) HE; benefit is not established in advanced (III–IV) HE (Lee et al., Gastroenterology 2009). Still give NAC empirically to all ALF.
  • Cerebral edema marker: arterial ammonia >150–200 µmol/L predicts intracranial hypertension and severe HE.
  • Prognostication: MELD is increasingly used alongside (sometimes instead of) King’s College Criteria. KCC is specific but not sensitive — a negative KCC should NOT defer transplant-center transfer.

1. Lee WM, Stravitz RT, Larson AM. Introduction to the revised AASLD Position Paper on acute liver failure 2011. Hepatology. 2012;55(3):965–967.

lessons.title: Acute Myeloid Leukemia
lessons.subtitle: Optimized lesson package · Facilitator-led interactive chalk talk for IM residents · 20–30 min
lessons.credit: Adapted from TeachIM.org — Acute Myeloid Leukemia (Gil, Amaya; ed. Geiger; exec. eds. Zhang, Fainstad; Dec 2023), CC BY-NC 4.0. Optimized June 2026: guideline currency verified, one deck typo corrected, WHO/ICC/ELN-2022 & targeted-therapy updates added, pedagogy restructured, assessments added. Companion deck: Acute_Myeloid_Leukemia_Optimized.pptx (original interactive board, surgically corrected + 3 appended slides).
objectives: Identify normal bone-marrow differentiation and where the pathologic mutation sits in AML.
Diagnose AML using common clinical and laboratory findings.
Recognize and initiate management of the four emergent complications of AML.
Bonus: Understand the basics of risk stratification and treatment.

prework.q: Q1. What two things are required to diagnose AML?
prework.a: (1) ≥20% blasts in bone marrow or peripheral blood, and (2) those blasts are of myeloid origin (Auer rods, MPO positivity, or myeloid immunophenotype). Certain AML-defining genetic abnormalities make the diagnosis regardless of blast count — and under WHO/ICC/ELN 2022 the 20% threshold no longer applies when such a lesion is present.

prework.q: Q2. Name the four life-threatening complications of AML.
prework.a: Leukostasis, tumor lysis syndrome (TLS), disseminated intravascular coagulation (DIC), and febrile neutropenia. A generalist often sees these before heme/onc does — early recognition saves lives.

prework.q: Q3. What empiric antibiotic do you start for febrile neutropenia, and what defines it?
prework.a: Anti-pseudomonal monotherapy — cefepime or piperacillin-tazobactam. Febrile neutropenia = a single oral temp >38.3°C (or ≥38.0°C sustained ≥1 h) with ANC <500 (or expected to fall below 500 within 48 h). Add vancomycin only for a suggestive source (line, skin/soft tissue, pneumonia) or instability.

segments.label: Hook (2 min)
segments.html: "Untreated, how long does someone with AML live?" Days to weeks. "And with our best treatment?" Only about 1 in 3 reach five years. The gap between those two numbers — and the fact that a general medicine team usually sees these patients first — is why this talk exists.

segments.label: Epidemiology (optional)
segments.html: ~1% of new cancers (~20k/yr), a disease of the elderly (median age 68, ~59% >65). Rare — but the complications are emergencies.

segments.label: Objective 1 — Pathophysiology
segments.html:
ASK: "Walk the marrow tree — where's the lesion in AML?" Click Normal → AML. Hematopoietic stem cell → myeloid vs lymphoid; myeloid stem cell → myeloblast → leukocytes (also RBCs, platelets). AML = overproliferation of myeloid stem cells OR myeloblasts.
ASK: "Given that, what CBC will you see?" Reveal that it depends where the lesion sits:
PatternWhy
Isolated leukocytosisMyeloblast affected → ↑WBC (very high or very low depending on mutation)
Leukocytosis + cytopeniasBlasts crowd out / disrupt other lines (e.g., via IL-6); blast % doesn't have to track with cytopenias
PancytopeniaMyeloid stem cell affected → proportionate decline across lines

segments.label: Objective 2 — Diagnosis
segments.html:
Diagnostic criteria — both required: (1) ≥20% blasts in marrow aspirate OR peripheral blood, and (2) myeloid origin (Auer rods, MPO+, or myeloid immunophenotype). An AML-defining cytogenetic abnormality [t(6;9), core-binding factor] is diagnostic regardless of blast count.
2022 update: WHO, ICC, and ELN 2022 dropped the ≥20% requirement when an AML-defining genetic lesion is present (e.g., PML::RARA, CBF), and made NPM1-mutated AML a distinct entity (≥10% blasts). This is the deck's "several nuanced exceptions," now formalized.
ASK: "Which CBC findings should make you think AML?" Reveal the line-by-line:
LineFindingsExam clue
WBCMedian 15k; hyperleukocytosis >100k in ~10–20%; leukopenia in ~25%; blasts on smear; Auer rods diagnosticGingival hyperplasia, leukemia cutis, Sweet syndrome
HgbAnemia common; anemia + thrombocytopenia → think DICConjunctival/palmar pallor
PlateletsThrombocytopenia in nearly all; ~25% present with plt <25kPetechiae, purpura

segments.label: Objective 3 — The four critical complications
segments.html:
ASK: For each clinical vignette on the board, have learners name the complication, its diagnosis, and first management step before revealing.
ComplicationRecognizeDiagnoseTreat
LeukostasisHeadache, vision change, dyspnea, confusion (neuro + respiratory)Clinical; WBC >100k (or >50k + symptoms)Cytoreduction (chemo/hydroxyurea) ± leukapheresis. No leukapheresis in APL.
TLSLethargy, anorexia, seizures, arrhythmia, AKI↑K, ↑uric acid, ↑phos, ↓CaIV fluids + allopurinol; rasburicase if severe
DICBleeding/bruising, "oozing from line sites"↑PT/PTT/INR, ↑D-dimer, ↓fibrinogen; schistocytesSupportive transfusions; treat the leukemia (suspect APL → ATRA/arsenic)
Febrile neutropeniaFever ± any infection sourceT >38.3°C (or ≥38°C ×1h) + ANC <500Cefepime or pip-tazo (anti-pseudomonal); add MRSA cover only if suggestive source/instability
Pearls: Leukapheresis is contraindicated in APL (worsens coagulopathy). Avoid acetaminophen in hospitalized neutropenic patients — it can mask the fever that may be the only sign of impending sepsis.

segments.label: Bonus — Risk & treatment
segments.html:
ELN sorts patients by cytogenetics + NGS into favorable / intermediate / adverse — and the spread is dramatic: ~46% vs ~18% vs ~4% 5-year OS. Treatment is systemic only (no surgery/radiation backbone), so the framework is induction (7+3 = 7 days cytarabine + 3 days anthracycline; Ven/HMA for unfit/elderly; ATRA/arsenic for APL) → consolidation (allogeneic SCT or further chemo) → maintenance, with salvage/targeted therapy or hospice for relapsed/refractory disease.

2022–25 update: NGS increasingly drives targeted therapy — quizartinib added to 7+3 for FLT3-ITD+ disease (QuANTUM-First; FDA 2023; OS 31.9 vs 15.1 mo); ivosidenib+azacitidine for newly-diagnosed unfit IDH1-mutated AML (AGILE); olutasidenib for relapsed/refractory IDH1; oral azacitidine (Onureg) as maintenance (QUAZAR); CPX-351 for therapy-related / myelodysplasia-related AML.

segments.label: Close (2 min)
segments.html: Hide the board. Have the group rebuild: the diagnostic criteria, the three CBC patterns, and the four complications with one management step each — then show the take-home slide.

cases.title: Case 1 — Fever and pancytopenia
cases.stem: 50-year-old with 6 months of worsening fatigue and recent malaise. T 38.5°C. CBC: WBC 2.3 (ANC 450), Hgb 9.2, plt 72.
cases.reveal: Neutropenic fever — a single oral T >38.3°C (or ≥38°C sustained ≥1 h) with ANC <500. Start empiric cefepime or pip-tazo and an infectious workup. Marrow ultimately shows favorable-risk AML; being young and favorable, he'd likely get 7+3.

cases.title: Case 2 — Bleeding and Auer rods
cases.stem: 62-year-old with 6 months of fatigue and new gingival bleeding. WBC 2.3 (ANC 320), Hgb 7.2, plt 16. Smear shows Auer rods and schistocytes.
cases.reveal: Auer rods (→ AML) and schistocytes (→ intravascular hemolysis). The combination with pancytopenia is worrisome.

cases.title: Case 3 — Confusion, vision changes, very high WBC
cases.stem: 75-year-old with altered mentation, headache, blurry vision, and 30-lb weight loss. WBC 153k (23% blasts), Hgb 8.2, plt 152. BMP: K 6.2, Cr 1.5, Ca 6.9, phos 5.8; uric acid 10.2.
cases.reveal: Leukostasis — AML patients are at risk with WBC >50k; seen in 10–20% of new diagnoses.

mcqs.stem: A 70-year-old presents with fatigue and bruising. WBC 30k with 28% circulating blasts; smear shows cells with Auer rods. Which finding would confirm acute myeloid leukemia regardless of the blast percentage?
opts: A core-binding factor rearrangement, e.g., t(8;21) or inv(16)
mcqs.exp: B. AML-defining genetic abnormalities — core-binding factor rearrangements [t(8;21), inv(16)], t(6;9), PML::RARA — are diagnostic of AML regardless of blast count, and under WHO/ICC/ELN 2022 the ≥20% threshold is waived in their presence. Anemia (A), thrombocytopenia (C), and splenomegaly (D) are common but nonspecific and do not establish the diagnosis.

mcqs.stem: A patient with newly diagnosed AML has WBC 180k and presents with confusion and hypoxia with diffuse pulmonary infiltrates. Which is the most appropriate immediate intervention?
opts: Packed red blood cell transfusion to improve oxygen delivery
mcqs.exp: B. This is leukostasis (neuro + respiratory symptoms with hyperleukocytosis). Treatment is urgent cytoreduction — leukapheresis and/or hydroxyurea/induction chemotherapy. RBC transfusion (A) increases viscosity and can worsen leukostasis — avoid until the WBC is reduced. Diuresis (C) doesn't address viscosity and risks volume depletion in a patient who may also have TLS. Heparin (D) is wrong and dangerous given thrombocytopenia/possible DIC. (If APL is suspected, do NOT leukapherese.)

mcqs.stem: A patient newly started on induction chemotherapy for AML develops K 6.4, phosphorus 6.8, uric acid 11, calcium 7.0, and a rising creatinine. What is the most appropriate management?
opts: Calcium gluconate and oral calcium supplementation
mcqs.exp: A. This is tumor lysis syndrome — aggressive IV fluids plus rasburicase (for a markedly elevated uric acid with renal compromise) are first-line. Repleting calcium (B) is generally avoided in TLS unless the patient is symptomatic, because it can precipitate calcium-phosphate in the setting of hyperphosphatemia. Binders alone (C) are insufficient for this severity. Observation (D) risks fatal arrhythmia and AKI.

mcqs.stem: A 58-year-old receiving AML induction has T 38.6°C and ANC 200 with no localizing symptoms. Blood pressure is normal. Which empiric regimen is most appropriate?
opts: No antibiotics; repeat the temperature in 4 hours
mcqs.exp: A. Febrile neutropenia requires immediate empiric anti-pseudomonal coverage — cefepime (or piperacillin-tazobactam) monotherapy is standard. Vancomycin (B) is not adequate Gram-negative/pseudomonal coverage and is added only for a suggestive source (line, SSTI, pneumonia) or instability. Ceftriaxone/azithromycin (C) lacks pseudomonal coverage. Delaying antibiotics (D) in neutropenic fever can be rapidly fatal — door-to-antibiotic time matters.

mcqs.stem: A 38-year-old with AML has pancytopenia, schistocytes, prolonged PT/PTT, elevated D-dimer, and low fibrinogen; the smear shows promyelocytes with Auer rods. Which statement is correct?
opts: Leukapheresis should be performed urgently for cytoreduction
This is likely APL; start ATRA without waiting for genetic confirmation
DIC here is treated definitively with heparin
mcqs.exp: B. The picture — DIC plus promyelocytes/Auer rods — strongly suggests acute promyelocytic leukemia; because early death is from coagulopathy, start ATRA immediately on clinical suspicion, before confirmatory PML::RARA testing returns. Leukapheresis (A) is contraindicated in APL — it worsens the coagulopathy. DIC management (C) is supportive (cryoprecipitate/fibrinogen, platelets) plus treating the leukemia, not heparin. APL prognosis (D) is actually excellent with treatment once the acute phase is survived.

anki: How common is AML and who gets it?
~1% of new cancers (~20k/yr in the US); predominantly elderly (median age 68, ~59% are >65). Any age can be affected.
AML prognosis with and without treatment?
Without treatment: survival is days to weeks. With treatment: only ~1 in 3 (≈30%) reach 5-year survival.
Where is the pathology in AML?
Overproliferation of myeloid stem cells OR myeloblasts. Because these are precursors to RBCs, WBCs, and platelets, any CBC pattern is possible.
Three CBC patterns AML can produce?
Isolated leukocytosis (myeloblast → ↑WBC), leukocytosis + cytopenias (blasts crowd out/disrupt other lines), and pancytopenia (myeloid stem cell affected → proportionate declines).
Diagnostic criteria for AML?

(1) ≥20% blasts in bone marrow aspirate OR peripheral blood, AND (2) cells of myeloid origin (Auer rods, MPO+, or myeloid immunophenotype). Certain defining cytogenetic abnormalities [t(6;9), CBF] are diagnostic regardless of blast count.
How did WHO/ICC/ELN 2022 change AML diagnosis?

The ≥20% blast cutoff is no longer required when an AML-defining genetic abnormality is present (e.g., PML::RARA, core-binding factor). NPM1-mutated AML is a distinct entity needing ≥10% blasts. Greater emphasis on MRD by NGS/flow.
What finding on smear is diagnostic of AML?
Auer rods (needle-like azurophilic cytoplasmic inclusions).
Physical exam findings of AML?
Gingival hyperplasia and leukemia cutis (leukemic infiltration); Sweet syndrome (acute febrile neutrophilic dermatosis); conjunctival/palmar pallor (anemia); petechiae/purpura (thrombocytopenia).
The four critical complications of AML?
Leukostasis, tumor lysis syndrome (TLS), disseminated intravascular coagulation (DIC), and febrile neutropenia.
Leukostasis — mechanism, threshold, and treatment?

Hyperleukocytosis + cytokines → ↑viscosity → impaired perfusion (neuro, respiratory symptoms). Typically WBC >100k (can occur ≥50k with symptoms). Treat by reducing WBCs: cytoreductive chemo/hydroxyurea ± leukapheresis. Leukapheresis is CONTRAINDICATED in APL (worsens coagulopathy).
Tumor lysis syndrome — labs and treatment?
Hyperkalemia, hyperuricemia, hyperphosphatemia, HYPOcalcemia; risks AKI (crystal nephropathy) and arrhythmias. Prevent/treat with IV fluids + allopurinol; rasburicase for severe/high uric acid.
DIC in AML — labs and management?
↑PT/PTT/INR and ↑D-dimer with LOW fibrinogen (key); schistocytes on smear. Supportive transfusions temporize; definitive treatment is treating the leukemia. Strongly associated with APL.
Febrile neutropenia — definition and empiric antibiotics?

Single oral T >38.3°C (or ≥38.0°C sustained ≥1 h) with ANC <500 (or expected <500 within 48 h). Empiric anti-pseudomonal monotherapy: cefepime or piperacillin-tazobactam. Add MRSA coverage (vancomycin) only for catheter/SSTI/PNA infection or instability.
Why avoid acetaminophen in hospitalized neutropenic patients?
It can mask fever, which may be the only early sign of impending sepsis.
What is APL and why is it special?
Acute promyelocytic leukemia — distinct treatment with ATRA + arsenic trioxide; excellent long-term prognosis, but the acute phase (DIC/bleeding) is the deadliest period. Avoid leukapheresis.
ELN risk stratification and prognostic spread?
Sorts patients by cytogenetics + NGS into favorable / intermediate / adverse. 5-year OS roughly 46% (favorable) vs 18% (intermediate) vs 4% (adverse).
AML treatment framework?

Induction (treat detectable cancer: 7+3 = 7 days cytarabine + 3 days anthracycline; Ven/HMA for unfit/elderly; ATRA/arsenic for APL) → consolidation (allogeneic SCT or further chemo) → maintenance; salvage/targeted therapy or hospice for relapsed/refractory disease.
Why is the adjuvant/neoadjuvant framework not used in AML?
Hematologic malignancies are treated with systemic therapy only — there's no surgery/radiation backbone, so induction/consolidation/maintenance replaces adjuvant/neoadjuvant language.
Newer targeted/maintenance agents in AML (2022–23)?

Quizartinib added to 7+3 for newly diagnosed FLT3-ITD+ (QuANTUM-First, FDA 2023); ivosidenib+azacitidine for newly dx unfit IDH1 (AGILE); olutasidenib for R/R IDH1; oral azacitidine (Onureg) maintenance (QUAZAR); CPX-351 for t-AML/AML-MR.
lessons.dot:
#AML (new dx / suspected): * yo, presenting w/ [fatigue, bleeding, infection, incidental CBC]. CBC: WBC * (* % blasts), Hgb *, plt *. smear: [blasts, Auer rods, schistocytes]. dx = >=20% myeloid blasts (Auer rod/MPO+/immunophenotype) OR AML-defining lesion [PML::RARA, CBF t(8;21)/inv(16), t(6;9), NPM1] -- WHO/ICC/ELN 2022.
- confirm: BM bx + aspirate, flow/immunophenotype, cytogenetics + NGS (FLT3-ITD/TKD, NPM1, IDH1/2, CEBPA, TP53), MPO; periph smear; HLA typing if SCT candidate
- *** screen for the 4 emergencies every new AML ***
  - leukostasis: WBC >100k (or >50k + sx: neuro/resp) -> cytoreduce [hydroxyurea / induction chemo] +/- leukapheresis; AVOID leukapheresis + minimize pRBC if APL
  - TLS: K/uric acid/phos up, Ca down, Cr up -> IVF + allopurinol; rasburicase if uric acid high/AKI; q6-8h labs, tele
  - DIC: PT/PTT/INR up, D-dimer up, FIBRINOGEN low, schistocytes -> cryo/fibrinogen + plt + FFP goals; treat leukemia; if APL -> ATRA + arsenic NOW (don't wait for genetics)
  - febrile neutropenia: T >38.3 (or >=38 x1h) + ANC <500 -> cefepime or pip-tazo STAT (door-to-abx); + vanc only if line/SSTI/PNA/unstable; cx x2, UA, CXR; NO acetaminophen (masks fever)
- supportive: transfuse plt <10k (<20k if fever, <50k if bleeding/procedure), Hgb goal *; TLS prophylaxis; central access; tumor board
- risk (ELN): [favorable ~46% / intermediate ~18% / adverse ~4% 5-yr OS] by cytogenetics + NGS
- treatment: induction [7+3 / Ven+HMA if unfit / ATRA+arsenic if APL] -> consolidation [allogeneic SCT / chemo] -> maintenance [oral azacitidine]; targeted per NGS [FLT3: midostaurin/quizartinib/gilteritinib; IDH1: ivosidenib/olutasidenib; IDH2: enasidenib]; CPX-351 if t-AML/AML-MR
- consults: heme/onc (urgent), [SCT, palliative care], goals of care
takehome: AML is a rare but high-mortality cancer of the elderly: untreated survival is days to weeks; with treatment only ~1 in 3 reach 5 years.
It arises from aberrant overproliferation of myeloid precursors that disrupts the other cell lines — expect any CBC pattern.
Diagnose with ≥20% myeloid-origin blasts in marrow or blood (Auer rods, MPO+, immunophenotype) — or an AML-defining genetic abnormality regardless of blast count (WHO/ICC/ELN 2022).
Know the four emergencies cold — leukostasis, TLS, DIC, febrile neutropenia — because the generalist usually sees them first.

lessons.title: Adrenal Insufficiency
lessons.subtitle: Optimized lesson package · Facilitator-led interactive chalk talk for IM residents · ~25–35 min
lessons.credit: Adapted from TeachIM.org — Adrenal Insufficiency (Yilin Zhang, MD; Brandon Fainstad, MD; published Oct 2020), CC BY-NC 4.0. Optimized June 2026: guideline currency verified, assay-specific cortisol cutoffs added, pedagogy restructured, assessments added. Companion deck: AdrenalInsufficiency_Optimized.pptx (original interactive presenter board, append-only).
objectives: Distinguish the physiology of primary, secondary, and tertiary adrenal insufficiency.
Know when to test and how to interpret cortisol levels (and the ACTH stimulation test) to diagnose AI.
Apply the basic principles and initial dosing of exogenous hormone replacement, including crisis management.

prework.q: Q1. Why is primary AI more likely to cause adrenal crisis than central (secondary/tertiary) AI?
prework.a: Primary (adrenal) AI loses BOTH cortisol and aldosterone. The aldosterone (mineralocorticoid) deficiency causes salt wasting → hypovolemia, hyperkalemia, and hyponatremia, which precipitate hemodynamic collapse. Central AI spares the renin–angiotensin–aldosterone axis (aldosterone is regulated by RAAS, not ACTH), so mineralocorticoid function is preserved.

prework.q: Q2. What 8 AM cortisol values rule AI in versus out, and what do you do with values in between?
prework.a: A very low morning cortisol rules it in; a clearly high value rules it out; indeterminate values require an ACTH (cosyntropin) stimulation test. The exact numeric cutoff is assay-dependent (see the update section).

prework.q: Q3. A hypotensive patient you suspect has adrenal crisis needs a definitive cortisol/ACTH work-up. Do you wait for results before treating?
prework.a: No. Adrenal crisis is treated empirically with stress-dose hydrocortisone and IV fluids immediately; you can draw cortisol/ACTH first if it doesn't delay therapy, but never withhold steroids awaiting confirmation. (Dexamethasone is an option if you must preserve a pending stim test, as it doesn't cross-react in the cortisol assay.)

segments.label: Hook (2 min)
segments.html: "A patient on chronic prednisone is admitted septic and becomes refractory to fluids and pressors. Their adrenals can't mount a stress response — and the fix is a cheap, fast dose of hydrocortisone." Frame AI as a diagnosis you must treat before you can confirm.

segments.label: Objective 1 — Physiology & the three levels
segments.html:
ASK: "Build the HPA axis with me: hypothalamus → ? → ? → ?" — CRH → ACTH → cortisol, with negative feedback. Separately, the RAAS axis (kidney JGA → renin → angiotensin → aldosterone) controls mineralocorticoid tone independent of ACTH.
LevelLesionACTHAldosterone / RAAS
Primary (adrenal)Adrenal cortexHighLost → salt wasting, hyperkalemia, hyponatremia, hypovolemia (also hyperpigmentation from ↑ACTH/MSH)
Secondary (pituitary)Pituitary ACTHLow/normalPreserved (RAAS intact)
Tertiary (hypothalamic)Hypothalamic CRHLow/normal (low even after CRH)Preserved
ASK: "Name causes at each level." — Primary: autoimmune adrenalitis (most common in resource-rich settings), infectious adrenalitis (TB, fungi, syphilis), metastases/tumor, hemorrhage, drugs (azoles, etomidate, mitotane, checkpoint inhibitors). Central: mass lesions/craniopharyngioma/metastases, radiation, infiltrative (hemochromatosis, sarcoid), infection, infarction (Sheehan), trauma/surgery — and, by far most common overall, exogenous glucocorticoids (tertiary).
Pearl — drug causes: Etomidate transiently suppresses adrenal cortisol synthesis even after a single intubation dose; azoles and mitotane inhibit steroidogenesis; rifampin/phenytoin/phenobarbital accelerate cortisol metabolism. And immune checkpoint inhibitors now cause both hypophysitis (central) and primary adrenalitis.

segments.label: Objective 2 — Diagnosis
segments.html:
ASK: "When do you test, and what's the first test?" — test when the clinical picture (fatigue, weight loss, hypotension, hyponatremia/hyperkalemia, or refractory shock) fits. First test: an 8 AM serum cortisol (cortisol is highest in the morning).

Interpretation (board values, older assay): <3 µg/dL rules AI in; <10 is suspicious; >18 rules it out. Indeterminate → ACTH (cosyntropin) stimulation test: 250 µg cosyntropin IV/IM, cortisol at baseline and 30–60 min; a subnormal peak confirms AI. Then ACTH level localizes: high ACTH = primary; low/normal = central. See the update section for modern assay-specific cutoffs (~14–15 µg/dL).

Pearl — early central AI can pass a stim test: Recent-onset central AI (e.g., just after pituitary surgery) may give a normal cosyntropin response because the adrenals haven't yet atrophied. If suspicion is high, recheck or use a different test, and treat empirically.

segments.label: Objective 3 — Treatment & crisis
segments.html:
SettingReplacement
Primary AI (chronic)Hydrocortisone 20–25 mg/24h (glucocorticoid, split dosing, larger AM dose) + fludrocortisone 0.1 mg qAM (mineralocorticoid) ± DHEA 25–50 mg qAM
Central AI (chronic)Hydrocortisone 15–20 mg/24h split — no fludrocortisone needed (RAAS intact)
Adrenal crisisHydrocortisone 100 mg IV bolus → 200 mg/24h (or 50 mg IV q6h) + aggressive IV fluids; treat empirically
ASK: "Your patient with known AI is having surgery / a serious illness — what changes?" — Stress dosing: increase glucocorticoid for physiologic stress (sick-day rules: double/triple oral dose; IV hydrocortisone for major stress). Under-replacement during stress is what precipitates crisis. Every AI patient needs an emergency injectable hydrocortisone kit and a medical-alert ID.
Pearl — at stress doses, you don't need separate mineralocorticoid: Hydrocortisone >~50 mg/day provides enough mineralocorticoid activity, so fludrocortisone can be held during high-dose stress dosing and resumed as the hydrocortisone is tapered.

segments.label: Close (2 min)
segments.html: Hide the board. Ask the group to rebuild: the three levels and which one loses aldosterone, the cortisol/stim-test pathway, and the one-line crisis treatment.

cases.title: Case 1 — Refractory shock
cases.stem: A 58-year-old on chronic prednisone 10 mg daily for polymyalgia rheumatica is admitted with pneumonia and becomes hypotensive, not responding to fluids and norepinephrine. Na 130, K 4.2, glucose 64.
cases.reveal: Tertiary (HPA-axis suppression from chronic exogenous glucocorticoids) — the most common cause of AI overall. RAAS is intact, so potassium is near-normal here despite the crisis.

cases.title: Case 2 — Hyperpigmentation and salt craving
cases.stem: A 34-year-old woman has months of fatigue, weight loss, salt craving, and darkening of palmar creases and buccal mucosa. Na 128, K 5.6, 8 AM cortisol 2 µg/dL.
cases.reveal: Primary AI — hyperpigmentation (high ACTH/MSH) plus hyperkalemia and hyponatremia signal mineralocorticoid loss. A morning cortisol of 2 rules AI in; a high ACTH localizes it to the adrenal. Check 21-hydroxylase antibodies for autoimmune adrenalitis.

cases.title: Case 3 — The indeterminate cortisol
cases.stem: A 47-year-old with fatigue has an 8 AM cortisol of 9 µg/dL. ACTH is pending.
cases.reveal: An indeterminate morning cortisol needs an ACTH (cosyntropin) stimulation test: 250 µg IV/IM, cortisol at baseline and 30–60 min. Interpret the peak against your lab's assay-specific cutoff (~14–15 µg/dL with modern specific assays, vs the older 18).

mcqs.stem: A 40-year-old woman with fatigue, hyperpigmentation, hyponatremia, and hyperkalemia is found to have a low cortisol and a high ACTH. Which additional hormone deficiency explains her electrolyte abnormalities?
mcqs.exp: B. Primary AI destroys the entire adrenal cortex, so aldosterone (a mineralocorticoid governed by RAAS) is lost along with cortisol. Aldosterone deficiency causes salt wasting → hyponatremia, hyperkalemia, and hypovolemia. This is why primary AI causes crisis more readily than central AI, which spares aldosterone. The other hormones (A, C, D) do not produce this electrolyte pattern.

mcqs.stem: A patient on chronic high-dose prednisone is admitted with hypotension and a serious infection. Which statement about their adrenal axis is correct?
opts: They have primary adrenal insufficiency with hyperkalemia
They have tertiary (HPA-suppression) AI with an intact RAAS axis
Fludrocortisone is the cornerstone of acute management
mcqs.exp: B. Exogenous glucocorticoids suppress the hypothalamic–pituitary axis (tertiary AI) — the most common cause of AI. Aldosterone (RAAS-driven) is preserved, so significant hyperkalemia is not expected (A) and fludrocortisone is not the acute priority (C). Hyperpigmentation (D) requires high ACTH, which is suppressed here. Treat with stress-dose hydrocortisone and fluids.

mcqs.stem: A hypotensive patient with suspected adrenal crisis needs a cosyntropin stimulation test, but you must treat now. Which steroid lets you start treatment without invalidating the pending test?
mcqs.exp: C. Dexamethasone does not cross-react in the serum cortisol assay, so it treats the crisis while preserving the ability to perform a stimulation test shortly after. Hydrocortisone (A) is the usual crisis drug but is measured as cortisol and would confound the test. Prednisone and methylprednisolone (B, D) also interfere with cortisol assays. Regardless of choice, never delay treatment for testing.

mcqs.stem: A patient's 8 AM cortisol is 16 µg/dL measured on a modern monoclonal (specific) immunoassay. How should this be interpreted regarding adrenal insufficiency?
opts: Diagnostic of AI because it is below 18 µg/dL
Likely sufficient — modern specific assays use a lower (~14–15 µg/dL) threshold
Requires an insulin tolerance test regardless
mcqs.exp: B. Newer specific monoclonal immunoassays and LC-MS/MS read ~20–30% lower than the older polyclonal assays that gave the historical 18 µg/dL cutoff, so ~14–15 µg/dL is an appropriate rule-out threshold. A value of 16 on such an assay is reassuring. Applying the old cutoff (A) over-diagnoses AI. There is nothing here to suggest a need to escalate to insulin tolerance testing (C) or Cushing (D).

mcqs.stem: A patient with established primary adrenal insufficiency on hydrocortisone and fludrocortisone is scheduled for major abdominal surgery. What is the most appropriate perioperative steroid plan?
opts: Continue the usual home dose unchanged
Hold all steroids the morning of surgery
Give stress-dose hydrocortisone (e.g., 100 mg IV then 50 mg q6h or 200 mg/24h)
mcqs.exp: C. Major physiologic stress requires stress-dose glucocorticoid; under-replacement precipitates adrenal crisis. Continuing the home dose (A) or holding steroids (B) is dangerous. At stress doses of hydrocortisone the mineralocorticoid effect is adequate, so a mineralocorticoid-only plan (D) is wrong. Resume the home regimen as the patient recovers.

anki: Primary vs secondary vs tertiary AI — where is the lesion?
Primary = adrenal cortex; secondary = pituitary (ACTH); tertiary = hypothalamus (CRH).
Why does primary AI cause adrenal crisis more readily than central AI?
Primary AI also loses aldosterone (mineralocorticoid) → salt wasting, hypovolemia, hyperkalemia, hyponatremia. Central AI spares the RAAS-driven aldosterone axis.
ACTH and aldosterone in primary AI?
ACTH high (loss of cortisol feedback → also hyperpigmentation); aldosterone low, renin high.
ACTH and aldosterone in central (secondary/tertiary) AI?
ACTH low/normal; aldosterone preserved (RAAS intact). Tertiary: ACTH stays low even after CRH.
Most common cause of AI overall?
Exogenous glucocorticoids (tertiary, HPA suppression).
Most common cause of primary AI in resource-rich settings?
Autoimmune adrenalitis (check 21-hydroxylase antibodies).
Drug causes of AI?
Steroids, azoles, etomidate, mitotane (synthesis); rifampin/phenytoin/phenobarbital (↑metabolism); checkpoint inhibitors (hypophysitis + adrenalitis).
First diagnostic test for AI and why that time?
8 AM serum cortisol — cortisol peaks in the morning, maximizing sensitivity.
8 AM cortisol interpretation (older assay)?
<3 µg/dL rules in; <10 suspicious; >18 rules out. Indeterminate → ACTH stimulation test.
How do modern cortisol assays change the cutoffs?
Specific monoclonal immunoassays and LC-MS/MS read ~20-30% lower, so rule-out / stim-test peak threshold is ~14-15 µg/dL, not 18. Use your lab's assay-specific value.
How is the ACTH (cosyntropin) stimulation test performed?
250 µg cosyntropin IV/IM; cortisol at baseline and 30-60 min; a subnormal peak confirms AI. Then ACTH level localizes primary vs central.
Why can early central AI pass a cosyntropin stim test?
The adrenals haven't yet atrophied and can still respond acutely. If suspicion is high, retest or use another test and treat empirically.
Replacement in primary AI?
Hydrocortisone 20-25 mg/24h split (AM-weighted) + fludrocortisone 0.1 mg qAM (mineralocorticoid) ± DHEA 25-50 mg.
Replacement in central AI?
Hydrocortisone 15-20 mg/24h split; no fludrocortisone needed (RAAS intact).
Adrenal crisis presentation and treatment?
Hypotension/shock ± fever, abdominal pain, nausea, fatigue. Treat empirically: hydrocortisone 100 mg IV then 200 mg/24h (or 50 mg q6h) + IV fluids — before confirmatory testing.
Which steroid preserves a pending cortisol stim test?
Dexamethasone — it does not cross-react in the cortisol assay (but never delay treatment for testing).
lessons.dot:
#Adrenal insufficiency: susp 2/2 * [autoimmune, infectious (TB/fungal), hemorrhage, mets, drug (steroid/azole/etomidate/CPI), central (pituitary/hypothal/exogenous steroid)]. level * [primary (adrenal, ↑ACTH, ↓aldo) / secondary (pituitary) / tertiary (hypothal/exogenous GC)]. r/o crisis [SBP*, shock, fever, abd pain, N/V, hypoNa */ hyperK */ hypoglycemia *]. HDS/HDUS.
- dx: 8a cortisol * [<3 ruled-in / 3-?? indeterminate / >cutoff ruled-out; cutoff ASSAY-SPECIFIC ~14-15 modern vs 18 old] -> if indeterminate ACTH-stim (cosyntropin 250mcg IV/IM, cortisol 0 + 30-60min, peak >= assay cutoff) -> ACTH level (↑=primary, low/nl=central) + [21-OH-Ab if primary, pituitary MRI if central].
- labs: BMP (Na/K/glu), AM cortisol, ACTH, renin/aldo (primary), TSH/free-T4 (central panhypopit), CBC.
- crisis (TREAT EMPIRICALLY, do NOT await results): hydrocortisone 100mg IV x1 -> 200mg/24h (or 50mg IV q6h) + IVF NS/D5NS aggressive; tx precipitant *. *use dexamethasone if stim test still pending (no assay cross-react).
- chronic replacement: GC hydrocortisone * [primary 20-25 / central 15-20 mg/24h] split, AM-weighted; + fludrocortisone 0.1mg qAM ONLY if primary (MCC); +/- DHEA 25-50mg.
- patient safety: SICK-DAY rules (double/triple oral dose for stress/illness); emergency IM hydrocortisone kit; medical-alert ID; stress-dose for surgery.
- f/u: *; endo referral *.
takehome: Primary AI causes adrenal crisis more readily than central AI because it also loses aldosterone (mineralocorticoid) → hypovolemia, hyperkalemia, hyponatremia. Central AI spares the RAAS axis.

Diagnose with an 8 AM cortisol — very low rules in, clearly high rules out; indeterminate values need a cosyntropin stimulation test, then ACTH to localize. Cutoffs are assay-specific (~14–15 µg/dL with modern assays, vs the older 18).
Adrenal crisis (hypotension/shock ± fever, abdominal pain, nausea, fatigue) is an emergency: give stress-dose hydrocortisone and IV fluids immediately, before confirmatory testing.

extras.label: Guideline Update Assay-specific cortisol cutoffs & GC-induced AI
extras.html:
The standing physiology and crisis teaching are unchanged and correct. Two practice refinements to surface:

TopicCurrent guidance
Cortisol cutoffs are assay-specificThe classic 8 AM "rule-out" ≥18 µg/dL and ACTH-stim peak ≥18 µg/dL derive from older polyclonal assays. Modern specific monoclonal immunoassays and LC-MS/MS read ~20–30% lower, so the rule-out / peak threshold is ~14–15 µg/dL. Apply your lab's assay-specific cutoff.
Most common causeExogenous glucocorticoids (tertiary AI). The 2024 ESE/Endocrine Society joint guideline gives a structured approach to diagnosing and safely tapering glucocorticoid-induced AI.
Adrenal crisisTreat empirically: hydrocortisone 100 mg IV then 200 mg/24h (or 50 mg q6h) + IV fluids — do not wait for confirmatory testing. Provide every AI patient an emergency hydrocortisone kit and medical-alert ID.

Sources: Bornstein SR, et al. Diagnosis and Treatment of Primary Adrenal Insufficiency: An Endocrine Society Clinical Practice Guideline, JCEM 2016; Hahner S, et al. assay-specific ACTH-stim cutoffs, J Endocr Soc 2021 (bvab022); Beuschlein F, et al. ESE/ES joint guideline on glucocorticoid-induced AI, JCEM 2024. Clinician-reviewed before teaching.

  3. Hahner S, et al. New Cutoffs for the Biochemical Diagnosis of Adrenal Insufficiency after ACTH Stimulation using Specific Cortisol Assays. J Endocr Soc. 2021;5(4):bvab022.
  4. Bancos I, et al. Diagnosis and management of adrenal insufficiency. Lancet Diabetes Endocrinol. 2015;3(3):216-226.
  5. Charmandari E, Nicolaides NC, Chrousos GP. Adrenal Insufficiency. Lancet. 2014;383(9935):2152-2167.
  6. Bastin M, et al. Effects of Etomidate on Adrenal Suppression: A Review of Intubated Septic Patients. Hosp Pharm. 2014;49(2):177-183.

lessons.title: Adult Disease Prevention Part 1
lessons.subtitle: Optimized lesson package · Facilitator-led interactive chalk talk for IM residents · 20–30 min (talk) / +10–15 min (cases)
lessons.credit: Adapted from TeachIM.org — Adult Disease Prevention Part 1 (Melton; Exec. Ed. Zhang; Section Ed. Brett; Univ. of Colorado / Univ. of Washington; published Sept 2021, last reviewed June 2025), CC BY-NC 4.0. Optimized June 2026: currency verified against USPSTF (2024 breast; 2025 cervical self-collection) and ACIP 2024; one deck typo corrected; pedagogy restructured, assessments added. Companion deck: Disease_Prevention_Part1_Optimized.pptx. Educational use only — clinician-reviewed before teaching.
objectives: Describe the disease and test characteristics that make screening worthwhile.
Use a framework (five domains) for prevention and screening in primary care.
Apply current cancer-screening recommendations and adapt them to individual patients.
Identify appropriate infectious-disease screening and immunizations.

prework.q: Q1. Name the four conditions that make a disease worth screening for.
prework.a: Common in the population; a good test exists (acceptable sensitivity/specificity, low-cost, easy, low-harm); a detectable asymptomatic period; and early treatment beats late. Net benefit must outweigh harms and cost.

prework.q: Q2. List the only five cancers with recommended screening and one population detail for each.
prework.a: Breast (women 40–74, q2y), colon (45–79), cervical (21–65), lung (50–80, ≥20 pack-years, quit <15y), prostate (men 55–69, shared decision).

prework.q: Q3. What 2025 change did the USPSTF make to cervical cancer screening?
prework.a: For ages 30–65, primary HPV testing every 5 years is now preferred, and the USPSTF endorsed self-collected (patient-collected) HPV samples for the first time. Ages 21–29 stay on cytology q3y.

segments.label: Hook (2 min)
segments.html: "More screening isn't better care — some of what we order does nothing but generate false positives and biopsies." Ask: which cancers actually have screening, and which ones don't? Park the list — there are only five, and you'll build them all.

segments.label: Objective 1 — When to screen
segments.html:
ASK: "What makes a disease worth screening for?" Take answers, then reveal disease vs test characteristics.

Screen when the disease is common, has an asymptomatic detectable window, and early treatment beats late; and the test is accurate, low-cost, easy, and low-harm. Bottom line: benefits > risks/harms/cost. Always weigh comorbidities, life expectancy, and whether you'd act on a positive result.

segments.label: Objective 2 — A framework
segments.html: Five domains of adult prevention: Cancer, Infectious Disease, Cardiovascular, Psychosocial, Family/Safety (CV/psychosocial/family-safety are covered in Part 2). Prioritize by age, sex, and risk factors. When you can't keep guidelines straight, use the USPSTF tool — and remember societies often disagree.

segments.label: Objective 3 — Cancer screening (the 5)
segments.html:
ASK: For each cancer, ask the population, modality/interval, and stop age before clicking.
CancerPopulationModality / intervalStop
BreastWomen 40–74 (Grade B)Mammogram q2y>74 or life expectancy <10y
Colon45–79 (45–49 B; 76–84 C)Colonoscopy q10y; FIT q1y; FIT-DNA q3y; CT colonography/sig q5ySelective 75–85; usually by 85
CervicalUSPSTF 21–65 (ACS 25–)21–29 cytology q3y; 30–65 primary HPV q5y (preferred), or cytology q3y, or co-test q5y>65 w/ adequate prior screen; or hysterectomy + cervix removed
Lung50–80, ≥20 pack-yr, quit <15y (B)Annual low-dose CT (Medicare needs documented SDM visit)Quit >15y or limited life expectancy
ProstateMen 55–69 (Grade C, SDM)± PSA q1–2y (no DRE for screening)>70
Pearl — Colonoscopy is the gold standard because any other positive test needs a colonoscopy to diagnose. For breast, clinical/self exams are no longer routine. For lung, lung-cancer screening is the only cancer screen that requires a documented shared-decision-making visit for Medicare coverage — and if they still smoke, cessation matters more than the scan.
Pearl — Prostate: the message is not "we never check a PSA." Individualize 55–69 with shared decision-making — PSA increases detection but a clear mortality benefit hasn't been shown, against real harms (false positives, biopsy complications, overtreatment).

Beyond the five, counsel lifestyle risk reduction: avoid tobacco, limit alcohol, healthy weight/diet, physical activity, sun protection, and STI prevention.

segments.label: Objective 4 — Immunizations & infection screening
segments.html:
ASK: "Which vaccines does everyone get, and which are age- or risk-based?"

Everyone: influenza annually; COVID-19 (at least each fall); Tdap once then Td/Tdap q10y. Age/condition-based: HPV 19–26 (preferred) or up to 45 with SDM; Hep B universal 19–59 (≥60 if risk factors); PCV21 for adults ≥50 (and 19–49 with chronic disease/immunocompromise) — or PCV15 → PPSV23; recombinant zoster ≥50 (2 doses); RSV for ≥75 (and 60–74 at higher risk) via SDM.

InfectionScreen whom
HIVOnce for all ages 15–65 (more if at risk)
Hepatitis COnce for all ages 18–79
Gonorrhea/chlamydiaSexually active women <24; older women with risk factors
Syphilis / Hep B / latent TBRisk-based (MSM, HIV, IVDU, incarceration, high-prevalence origin, congregate settings)

segments.label: Retrieval close (2 min)
segments.html: Cover the board. Have learners rebuild: the five screenable cancers with start ages, the cervical 2025 update, and the "everyone" vaccines.

cases.title: Case 1 — 54-year-old woman, current smoker, establishing care
cases.stem: 54F, HTN on amlodipine, 1 ppd since age 19 (not ready to quit), normal colonoscopy 4 years ago, mammogram 2 years ago, unsure of last (always-normal) Pap, sexually active with husband, no family cancer history.
cases.reveal: (1) Breast — bilateral screening mammogram (due, q2y). (2) Lung — LDCT after a shared-decision-making discussion (age 54, ~35 pack-years, current smoker). (3) Cervical — primary hrHPV or HPV+cytology co-test. (Colon not yet due.)

cases.title: Case 2 — 32-year-old woman, routine physical
cases.stem: 32F, hypothyroid on levothyroxine, IUD in place, last Pap 3 years ago (normal, no HPV testing done), unknown family cancer history.
cases.reveal: She's 30–65: primary hrHPV testing (preferred) or HPV+cytology co-test. (Her last test was cytology-only 3 years ago.)

cases.title: Case 3 — 56-year-old man, homeless, no care in 8 years
cases.stem: 56M, no PMH, ½ ppd ×20 years (10 pack-years), quit alcohol 2 years ago, no family cancer history, not currently sexually active.
cases.reveal: Colon cancer (age 45–79) — offer FIT, gFOBT, or FIT-DNA (or colonoscopy). Discuss prostate screening (55–69 shared decision) — but with limited life expectancy/competing needs, likely recommend against at this time. Lung: 10 pack-years does NOT meet the ≥20 pack-year threshold → not indicated.

mcqs.stem: A healthy 41-year-old woman at average risk asks about mammography. Per the 2024 USPSTF recommendation, what do you advise?
opts: Shared decision-making, screening optional in her 40s
mcqs.exp: C. In 2024 the USPSTF moved to recommend biennial mammography for all average-risk women 40–74 (Grade B), replacing the prior "individualized in the 40s" stance (D, the old recommendation). A delays screening unnecessarily. B (annual) is offered by some societies but the USPSTF interval is every 2 years. E (MRI) is reserved for high-risk patients.

mcqs.stem: A 35-year-old woman at average risk is due for cervical cancer screening. Per the USPSTF 2025 update, which is now the preferred strategy?
opts: Primary high-risk HPV testing every 5 years (clinician- or self-collected)
mcqs.exp: C. For ages 30–65, the 2025 USPSTF update makes primary hrHPV testing every 5 years the preferred strategy and, for the first time, endorses self-collected HPV samples. A and B remain acceptable alternatives but are not "preferred." D over-screens; E is wrong (screening continues through 65).

mcqs.stem: Which patient meets criteria for lung cancer screening with low-dose CT?
opts: 82-year-old, 40 pack-years, current smoker, metastatic pancreatic cancer
mcqs.exp: C. Criteria: age 50–80, ≥20 pack-years, and currently smoking or quit within 15 years. C fits. A fails the ≥20 pack-year threshold; B quit >15 years ago; D has a limited life expectancy (don't screen); E is younger than 50.

mcqs.stem: A 58-year-old man asks about prostate cancer screening. What is the most appropriate approach?
opts: Order PSA and a digital rectal exam routinely
Do not discuss PSA — screening is not recommended
Shared decision-making about PSA (men 55–69), discussing benefits and harms
mcqs.exp: C. For men 55–69 the USPSTF gives a Grade C — individualize via shared decision-making (PSA increases detection but mortality benefit is uncertain against real harms). A is wrong: DRE is not recommended for screening. B overstates (the message isn't "never check a PSA"). D is backwards (stop, not start, at 70). E ignores shared decision-making.

mcqs.stem: Which vaccine recommendation is correct for an average-risk 52-year-old adult in 2025?
opts: PCV21 (single dose) is appropriate for adults ≥50
Pneumococcal vaccination isn't indicated until age 65
Hepatitis B is not recommended at this age
Zoster vaccine is a single dose
mcqs.exp: B. Per 2024 ACIP, PCV21 (a single dose, no follow-on needed) is appropriate for all adults ≥50 (the age was lowered from 65). A is wrong (PPSV23 alone isn't preferred; if PCV15 is used it's followed by PPSV23). C reflects the old age threshold. D is wrong — Hep B is universal for 19–59. E is wrong — recombinant zoster is a 2-dose series.

anki: Four conditions that make a disease worth screening for?

(1) Common in the population; (2) a good test exists (acceptable sensitivity/specificity, low-cost, easy, low-harm); (3) a detectable asymptomatic period; (4) early treatment beats late. Bottom line: benefits must outweigh harms/cost.
What patient factors modify the decision to screen?
Comorbidities and life expectancy, patient preference, and whether diagnosis/treatment would actually be pursued if positive. Individualize by age, sex, and risk factors.
Five domains of adult disease prevention?
Cancer, Infectious Disease, Cardiovascular, Psychosocial, and Family/Safety.
Which cancers have established screening?
Only five: breast, colon, cervical, lung, and prostate.
Breast cancer screening (USPSTF 2024)?
All average-risk women ages 40–74, biennial mammography (Grade B). Stop at >74 or life expectancy <10 years. Clinical/self breast exams no longer routine.
Colon cancer screening (USPSTF)?

Average-risk adults 45–79 (45–49 Grade B; 50–75 Grade A; 76–85 Grade C, selective). Options: colonoscopy q10y, FIT/gFOBT q1y, stool FIT-DNA q3y, CT colonography or sigmoidoscopy q5y. Colonoscopy is the gold standard (needed if any other test is positive).
Cervical cancer screening — USPSTF 2025 update?

Ages 21–29: cytology alone every 3 years. Ages 30–65: high-risk HPV PRIMARY screening every 5 years is now PREFERRED; self-collected (patient-collected) HPV samples are endorsed for the first time. Alternatives 30–65: cytology q3y or HPV+cytology co-test q5y. (ACS starts at 25.)
When can cervical cancer screening stop?
After 65 with adequate normal prior screening (within 10 years), or after hysterectomy with cervix removal and no history of high-grade lesion/cervical cancer.
Lung cancer screening criteria?

Adults 50–80 with ≥20 pack-year history who currently smoke or quit <15 years ago (Grade B). Annual low-dose CT. Stop if quit >15 years or limited life expectancy. Medicare requires a documented shared-decision-making visit before the first scan.
How do you calculate pack-years?
Packs per day × years smoked (e.g., 1 ppd × 20 years = 20 pack-years).
Prostate cancer screening?

Individualized PSA-based screening in men 55–69 (Grade C, shared decision-making) — discuss harms (false positives, biopsy complications, overdiagnosis/overtreatment). Stop after 70. Digital rectal exam is NOT recommended for screening.
Adult immunizations everyone should receive?
Influenza annually; COVID-19 (at least each fall); Tdap once then Td/Tdap every 10 years.
Pneumococcal vaccination in adults (2024 ACIP)?
PCV21 for all adults ≥50, and adults 19–49 with chronic conditions/immunocompromise. If PCV15 is used instead, follow with PPSV23 (~1 year later).
Hepatitis B and HPV vaccination in adults?
Hep B: universal for adults 19–59 (≥60 only if risk factors). HPV: ages 19–26 (preferred), or up to 45 with shared decision-making.
Shingles and RSV vaccination?
Recombinant zoster (Shingrix): age ≥50, 2 doses 2–6 months apart. RSV: one dose for adults ≥75 (and 60–74 at increased risk) via shared decision-making (high relative efficacy but NNT >700).
Infection screening recommended for all adults?
HIV once for ages 15–65; hepatitis C once for ages 18–79. Others (syphilis, gonorrhea/chlamydia, hepatitis B, latent TB) are risk-based.
Gonorrhea/chlamydia screening — who?
All sexually active women <24 (Grade B); older women with risk factors (new/multiple partners, partner with an STI or concurrent partners, inconsistent barrier use).
Why does lung cancer screening uniquely require a shared-decision-making visit?

Medicare mandates a documented SDM conversation (eligibility, decision aid, counseling on radiation/overdiagnosis/false positives and on smoking cessation) before the first low-dose CT — a response to direct-to-consumer CT advertising.
lessons.dot:
#Health maintenance (adult, average risk) - Cancer + ID prevention
pt: *__yo *[F/M]; life expectancy >10y *[y/n]; pursue dx/tx if positive *[y/n]
CANCER SCREEN (only 5):

 colon: 45-79; *[colonoscopy q10y / FIT q1y / FIT-DNA q3y / CTcolonography-sig q5y] *[due/UTD]

 lung: 50-80 + >=20py + (smoking or quit<15y) -> annual LDCT *[eligible/not]; Medicare SDM visit documented *[y/n]; cessation addressed *[y]
 prostate: M 55-69 PSA *[shared decision: screen q1-2y / defer]; no DRE for screening; stop >70
 lifestyle: tobacco *__, alcohol *__, diet/activity, sun, STI prevention
IMMUNIZATIONS:
 all: influenza (annual) *__; COVID (>=fall) *__; Tdap once->Td/Tdap q10y *__
 PCV21 if >=50 (or 19-49 + chronic dz) *__; zoster (RZV) >=50 x2 *__; RSV if >=75 or 60-74 high-risk (SDM) *__
 HepB 19-59 universal *__; HPV 19-26 (up to 45 SDM) *__
ID SCREEN: HIV once 15-65 *__; HCV once 18-79 *__; risk-based [syphilis/GC-CT/HepB/LTBI] *__
resource: USPSTF tool / ACIP schedule; societies may differ (e.g., ACS cervical starts 25)
RTC: *__ for results / next due screen
takehome: Screening pays off only when the disease is common with a detectable asymptomatic phase, early treatment beats late, and the test is accurate, cheap, and low-harm — and benefits exceed harms and cost.
Use the five-domain framework and individualize by age, sex, risk, comorbidities, and life expectancy.
Only five cancers have screening: breast (40–74 q2y), colon (45–79), cervical (21–65), lung (50–80, 20 pack-yr, quit <15y), prostate (55–69, shared decision).
Immunizations: PCV21 for ≥50; RSV for ≥75 (and 60–74 at risk) via SDM; Hep B universal 19–59; flu/COVID/Tdap for all.
2025: USPSTF endorses self-collected HPV and prefers primary HPV q5y at 30–65. Use the USPSTF tool and know societies differ.

extras.label: 2024–2025 Practice Update NEW — projected on the appended deck slide
extras.html:
The deck is the June-2025 build, so most content is already current: breast (40–74 q2y, 2024 USPSTF), colon (45–79), lung (50–80/20py/15y), prostate (55–69 Grade C), PCV21 for ≥50 (2024 ACIP), and universal Hep B 19–59 were all verified. The practice-relevant addition since the deck's review:

  • USPSTF cervical cancer screening (finalized 2025), Grade A: ages 21–29 cytology alone every 3 years (unchanged); ages 30–65 high-risk HPV primary screening every 5 years is now preferred, and the USPSTF endorses self-collected (patient-collected) HPV samples for the first time (clinician- or patient-collected accepted). Alternatives at 30–65 remain cytology q3y or HPV+cytology co-test q5y.
  • Caveats: ACS still begins at 25; ASCP/CETC have raised concerns about treating self-collected and clinician-collected samples as equivalent and about the 5-year interval for self-collection. Confirm your institution's protocol.
  • RSV (reinforced): CDC now recommends RSV vaccine for all adults ≥75 and for 60–74 at increased risk (shared decision-making) — narrower than the original "≥60 SDM" framing.

lessons.refs:
Original lesson references retained (ACOG cervical 2021; ASCCP app; CMS LDCT decision memo; UpToDate chapters; USPSTF, accessed May 2024; and others as listed on the TeachIM page). Newly cited for the update:

  1. US Preventive Services Task Force. Screening for Cervical Cancer: Recommendation Statement (final, 2025) — primary hrHPV every 5 years preferred at 30–65; self-collected HPV endorsed (Grade A).
  2. US Preventive Services Task Force. Screening for Breast Cancer: Recommendation Statement. JAMA 2024 (biennial, ages 40–74, Grade B).
  3. Kobayashi M, et al. Use of 21-valent pneumococcal conjugate vaccine (PCV21) among U.S. adults: ACIP recommendations, 2024. MMWR.
  4. CDC/ACIP. RSV vaccination guidance for adults (≥75; 60–74 at increased risk, shared decision-making).
