lessons.title: Adult Disease Prevention Part 2
lessons.subtitle: Optimized lesson package · Facilitator-led interactive chalk talk for IM residents · ~30 min (talk) / +10–15 min (cases)
lessons.credit: Adapted from TeachIM.org — Adult Disease Prevention Part 2 (Melton, Guinn, Trefilek; Exec. Ed. Zhang; Section Ed. Brett; Univ. of Colorado / Washington; published Sept 2021, last reviewed May 2024), CC BY-NC 4.0. Optimized June 2026: currency verified against the 2026 ACC/AHA dyslipidemia guideline, USPSTF, and 2024 ACIP; two deck typos corrected; pedagogy restructured, assessments added. Companion deck: Disease_Prevention_Part2_Optimized.pptx. Educational use only — clinician-reviewed before teaching.
objectives: Apply the five-domain prevention framework in primary care.
Screen for substance use and mental-health conditions.
Apply cardiovascular screening — lipids, diabetes, AAA, obesity, hypertension.
Identify patients at risk for intimate partner violence, falls, and osteoporosis (plus family planning and advance-care planning).

prework.q: Q1. Which three substance-use domains should you screen all adults for, and with what question for alcohol?
prework.a: Alcohol, tobacco, and unhealthy drug use. For alcohol, the Single Alcohol Screening Question: "How many times in the past year have you had 5 (4 for women) or more drinks in a day?"

prework.q: Q2. Who gets one-time AAA ultrasound screening?
prework.a: Men 65–75 who have ever smoked (one-time abdominal ultrasound). Selective in never-smoking men in that range; not recommended for women.

prework.q: Q3. What CV-risk calculator does the 2026 ACC/AHA guideline now recommend, and why?
prework.a: PREVENT (ages 30–79) — it replaces the Pooled Cohort Equations, which overestimated 10-year risk by roughly 40–50%.

segments.label: Hook (2 min)
segments.html: "Most of what changes outcomes in a well visit isn't a scan — it's catching the drinking, the blood pressure, the fall risk, and the unsafe home." Ask: which screens would you actually run on a healthy-looking 67-year-old? Park the list; you'll fill the three remaining domains.

segments.label: Objective 1 — Framework
segments.html: Recall the five domains (Cancer, Infectious Disease — Part 1; Psychosocial, Cardiovascular, Family/Safety — here). These are average-risk recommendations; family history/comorbidities shift you from screening toward diagnostic testing.

segments.label: Objective 2 — Psychosocial
segments.html:
ASK: "How do you screen for alcohol, tobacco, and drugs in clinic?" Take answers, then reveal the tools.

Substance use (all adults, annually): Alcohol — SASQ ("How many times in the past year have you had 5 [4 for women] or more drinks in a day?") or AUDIT-C; CAGE assesses dependence, not screening. Tobacco — assess at every visit (Grade A); ask if they ever smoke and about all products. Drugs — the single question: "How many times in the past year have you used an illegal drug or a prescription medication for non-medical reasons?"

Pearl — NIAAA "unhealthy use": men 21–64 >4/day or >14/week; men ≥65 and all women >3/day or >7/week.

Mental health: USPSTF — screen all adults for depression (PHQ-2/4/9) and adults <65 for anxiety (GAD-7); no defined interval.

segments.label: Objective 3 — Cardiovascular
segments.html:
ASK: For each — HTN, obesity, diabetes, lipids, AAA — ask who and how often before clicking.
TargetWho / how
HypertensionOffice BP for all; annually if ≥40 or at risk. Confirm with ambulatory/home BP before treating.
ObesityBMI at visits (Grade B); BMI ≥30 → intensive behavioral intervention. Classes 1 (30–34.9), 2 (35–39.9), 3 (≥40).
DiabetesADA: all at 35, or any age if overweight/obese + ≥1 RF. USPSTF: 35–70 overweight/obese. A1c/FPG/OGTT; repeat ≥q3y (yearly if prediabetes).
LipidsUSPSTF: 40–75 + ≥1 RF and 10-yr risk >10% (statin; 7.5–10% selective). Repeat ~q5y low-risk, q3y high-risk; non-fasting OK to screen.
AAAOne-time abdominal US in men 65–75 who ever smoked; selective in never-smokers; not for women.
Pearl (2026 update) — The 2026 ACC/AHA dyslipidemia guideline now uses the PREVENT calculator (ages 30–79) instead of the Pooled Cohort Equations and pushes "earlier and lower, for longer" LDL targets. Refer CV-risk patients to diet/activity counseling (≥150 min/week moderate aerobic + strengthening ≥2 days/week).

segments.label: Objective 4 — Family & Safety
segments.html:
ASK: "Which safety screens do you owe a reproductive-age woman? An 80-year-old?"

Family planning: assess reproductive goals; folic acid 0.4–0.8 mg daily for anyone who may become pregnant. Falls: screen >65 (or risk factors) annually with 3 questions (unsteady? worry about falling? fallen in the past year?) → offer exercise (CDC STEADI). Osteoporosis: DEXA in women ≥65 (and postmenopausal <65 at risk); USPSTF finds insufficient evidence to screen men. IPV: screen all reproductive-age women, privately, with HITS/HARK/PVS/WAST. Advance-care planning: for adults of all ages, more so with comorbidities/older age.

segments.label: Retrieval close (2 min)
segments.html: Cover the board. Have learners rebuild the three domains and one screen each, plus the PREVENT update and AAA criteria.

cases.title: Case 1 — 26-year-old woman, annual exam, planning pregnancy
cases.stem: 26F, immunizations up to date except declined COVID, wants to conceive. BMI ~32 (175 lb, 5'2"), BP 138/82.
cases.reveal: Cervical cancer screening; STI/HIV/HCV (± syphilis by risk); COVID (± influenza by season); substance-use and depression/anxiety screening; HTN and obesity screening; diet/exercise counseling; family-planning counseling + folic acid 0.4–0.8 mg daily; IPV screening; advance-care planning. Prioritize preconception items (folic acid, BP, substance use) given her pregnancy plans.

cases.title: Case 2 — 67-year-old woman, establishing care
cases.stem: 67F, HTN on amlodipine, lives with son who manages her finances, former smoker (quit 20 years ago), normal colonoscopy 7 years ago, COVID vaccine done, tetanus ~5 years ago.
cases.reveal: Tdap (if not had within recommended window), PCV21 (≥50; PCV20 also acceptable), recombinant zoster, ± influenza; breast cancer screening; substance-use (ask alcohol/drugs) and depression/anxiety screening; obesity, diabetes and lipid screening; diet/exercise counseling; fall and osteoporosis (DEXA) screening; advance-care planning; and given a relative managing her finances, consider elder abuse/vulnerable-adult assessment.

cases.title: Case 3 — 69-year-old man, history of homelessness
cases.stem: 69M, prior homelessness, smoked in his 20s, ~2 drinks nightly, colonoscopy 7 years ago (told to repeat in 5), no meds.
cases.reveal: Colon cancer screening (colonoscopy — overdue); ± prostate screening (shared decision); Tdap, zoster, PCV21 (± influenza); HIV/HCV if not done; substance-use (his alcohol exceeds NIAAA limits) and mental-health screening; HTN, obesity, diabetes, lipid screening; AAA ultrasound (man 65–75, ever-smoked); diet/exercise counseling; fall screening; advance-care planning.

mcqs.stem: Which patient should receive one-time AAA screening with abdominal ultrasound?
opts: A 60-year-old man who smoked for 30 years
A 70-year-old man who has never smoked
A 68-year-old man who smoked 1 ppd for 25 years
A 72-year-old woman with a 40 pack-year history
mcqs.exp: C. USPSTF recommends one-time AAA ultrasound in men 65–75 who have ever smoked. C fits. A is younger than 65; B is in range but never smoked (selective, not routine); D is a woman (not recommended regardless of smoking); E is younger than 65.

mcqs.stem: A healthy 36-year-old with no risk factors and a normal BMI asks about diabetes screening. Per ADA guidance, what do you advise?
opts: Screen now — ADA recommends screening all adults starting at age 35
mcqs.exp: B. ADA recommends screening all adults beginning at age 35 regardless of risk (and at any age if overweight/obese with ≥1 risk factor). A reflects the older threshold; C and D miss the universal age-35 trigger; E starts too early without basis. Repeat every ≥3 years if normal (yearly for prediabetes).

mcqs.stem: Per the 2026 ACC/AHA dyslipidemia guideline, which tool should you use to estimate primary-prevention CV risk, and why?
opts: Pooled Cohort Equations — they remain the standard
The PREVENT equations — Pooled Cohort overestimated risk ~40–50%
ASCVD risk is no longer calculated
mcqs.exp: C. The 2026 guideline recommends the PREVENT calculator (ages 30–79) because the older Pooled Cohort Equations overestimated 10-year ASCVD risk by ~40–50%. A is outdated; B (Framingham) predates Pooled Cohort; D (CHA₂DS₂-VASc) is for AF stroke risk, not lipids; E is false.

mcqs.stem: Which screening does the USPSTF recommend for a 30-year-old woman at a routine visit?
mcqs.exp: A. USPSTF recommends anxiety screening for adults <65 and IPV screening for all reproductive-age women. AAA (B) is for men 65–75 who smoked; DEXA (C) is for women ≥65 (or younger if high risk); routine EKG (D) and vitamin D (E) are not recommended for low-risk asymptomatic adults.

mcqs.stem: A 40-year-old man asks for "the best alcohol screen." Which is the most appropriate first-line screening approach?
opts: The Single Alcohol Screening Question (or AUDIT-C)
No screening unless he reports a problem
mcqs.exp: B. The SASQ ("How many times in the past year have you had 5 [4 for women] or more drinks in a day?") or AUDIT-C are validated, sensitive screens. CAGE (A) assesses dependence, not screening. Labs (C) and a blood alcohol level (D) are not screening tools. E is wrong — USPSTF recommends screening all adults.

anki: Three psychosocial substance-use screens recommended for all adults annually?
Alcohol (USPSTF: all adults), tobacco (assess every visit, Grade A), and unhealthy drug use (all adults). Each is a question-based screen, not a lab test.
Preferred alcohol screening tools?
Single Alcohol Screening Question (SASQ: "How many times in the past year have you had 5 [4 for women] or more drinks in a day?") or AUDIT-C. CAGE assesses dependence and is NOT for screening.
NIAAA thresholds for unhealthy alcohol use?
Men 21–64: >4 drinks/day or >14/week. Men ≥65 and women of all ages: >3 drinks/day or >7/week.
Single-question drug-use screen?
"How many times in the past year have you used an illegal drug or used a prescription medication for non-medical reasons?"
USPSTF mental-health screening recommendations?
Screen all adults for depression; screen adults <65 for anxiety (no defined interval). Tools: PHQ-2/PHQ-4/PHQ-9 (depression), GAD-7 (anxiety).
Hypertension screening?
Office BP for all adults; annually if age ≥40 or increased risk. Confirm elevated readings with ambulatory or home BP before treating.
Obesity screening and intervention?
Measure BMI in all adults (Grade B). BMI ≥30 → refer to intensive multicomponent behavioral intervention. Classes: 1 (30–34.9), 2 (35–39.9), 3 (≥40).
Diabetes screening — ADA vs USPSTF?

ADA: screen all at age 35, or any age if overweight/obese (BMI ≥25, or ≥23 in Asian Americans) plus ≥1 risk factor. USPSTF: 35–70 if overweight/obese. A1c, fasting glucose, or OGTT; repeat ≥q3y (yearly if prediabetes).
Lipid disorder screening (USPSTF)?

Screen/treat ages 40–75 with ≥1 CV risk factor and 10-year CV risk >10% (Grade B); consider a statin at 7.5–10% risk. Insufficient evidence <40 or >75. Repeat ~q5y low-risk, q3y high-risk; need not be fasting for screening.
AAA screening?
One-time abdominal ultrasound in men 65–75 who have ever smoked. Selective in never-smoking men in that range. Not recommended for women.
Physical activity recommendation for adults?
150–300 min/week moderate-intensity OR 75–150 min/week vigorous aerobic activity, plus muscle-strengthening ≥2 days/week.
Folic acid for those who may become pregnant?
0.4–0.8 mg (400–800 µg) daily for all women planning or capable of pregnancy (USPSTF).
Falls screening in older adults?

Screen adults >65 (or with risk factors) at least annually with 3 questions: feel unsteady? worry about falling? fallen in the past year? A "yes" to any = at risk → offer exercise interventions (CDC STEADI).
Osteoporosis screening?
DEXA in women ≥65, and postmenopausal women <65 at increased risk. USPSTF finds insufficient evidence to screen men (under review).
Intimate partner violence screening?

Screen all reproductive-age women, annually/since last visit, in private. Tools: HITS, HARK, PVS, WAST. (USPSTF has no recommendation for screening older/vulnerable adults for abuse — but be alert to it.)
Advance care planning — who and when?

Consider for adults of ALL ages (more emphasis with multiple comorbidities or older age): durable medical power of attorney, living will, and orders for scope of treatment (e.g., MOST/POLST, varies by state).
2026 ACC/AHA dyslipidemia guideline — main change to CV risk estimation?

Use the PREVENT calculator (ages 30–79) instead of the Pooled Cohort Equations (which overestimated risk ~40–50%). Risk tiers: low <3%, borderline 3–<5%, intermediate 5–<10%, high ≥10%. Theme: "earlier and lower, for longer."
2026 ACC/AHA LDL-C targets and earlier-statin triggers?

Targets: very-high-risk <55, high-risk <70, intermediate on statin <100 mg/dL. Start statins earlier in familial hypercholesterolemia, those ≤30 with LDL ≥160, strong family ASCVD history, or high 30-year PREVENT risk.
Pneumococcal vaccination update (2024 ACIP)?
PCV21 is now an option for all adults ≥50 (and 19–49 with chronic disease), with the age lowered from 65; PCV20 remains valid. If PCV15 is used, follow with PPSV23.
lessons.dot:
#Health maintenance (adult, average risk) - Part 2: Psychosocial / CV / Family-Safety
PSYCHOSOCIAL (annual):
 alcohol: SASQ/AUDIT-C *[neg/pos]; unhealthy if M21-64 >4/day-14/wk, M>=65 & all F >3/day-7/wk
 tobacco: *[never/former/current __py]; assessed every visit; cessation offered *[y/n]
 drugs: single-item screen *[neg/pos]
 mental health: depression (PHQ-2/9) *__; anxiety (GAD-7, age<65) *__
CARDIOVASCULAR:
 HTN: office BP *__/__; if >=40 or at risk -> annual; confirm w/ home/ABPM before tx
 obesity: BMI *__ [class1 30-34.9 / 2 35-39.9 / 3 >=40]; >=30 -> intensive behavioral referral
 diabetes (ADA): screen all at 35, or any age if overweight/obese + RF; A1c/FPG *__; repeat q3y (yearly if prediab)
 lipids: 40-75 + RF; 10-yr risk by PREVENT (2026 ACC/AHA) *__% [low<3/border3-5/interm5-10/high>=10]; statin if USPSTF >10% (7.5-10% selective)
  -> LDL target *[very-high<55 / high<70 / interm-on-statin<100]; start statin earlier if FH / age<=30 LDL>=160 / high 30-yr risk
 AAA: one-time US if M 65-75 ever-smoked *[done/ordered/n-a]
 diet/activity counseling: *[y]; goal >=150min/wk mod + strengthening x2/wk
FAMILY/SAFETY:
 family planning: reproductive goals *__; folic acid 0.4-0.8mg if may conceive *[y/n]
 falls (>=65): 3-Q screen *[neg/pos] -> exercise/STEADI
 osteoporosis: DEXA if F>=65 or postmenopausal+RF *[done/ordered]; (men: insufficient evidence)
 IPV (reproductive-age F, private): HITS/HARK *[neg/pos]

RTC: *__ for results / next due
takehome: Work all five prevention domains every visit; this talk covers psychosocial, cardiovascular, and family/safety. Individualize by age, sex, and risk.
Psychosocial: screen all adults annually for alcohol (SASQ/AUDIT-C), tobacco (every visit), and drug use; depression (all) and anxiety (<65).
Cardiovascular: HTN (office BP), obesity (BMI), diabetes (ADA all at 35), lipids (40–75 + risk), and one-time AAA ultrasound in men 65–75 who ever smoked.
Family/Safety: folic acid for possible pregnancy, fall screening ≥65, DEXA in women ≥65, annual IPV screening in reproductive-age women, and advance-care planning for all.
2026 ACC/AHA: use PREVENT (not Pooled Cohort) for CV risk, with "earlier and lower for longer" LDL targets.

extras.label: 2024–2026 Practice Update NEW — projected on the appended deck slide
extras.html:
The deck's standing teaching (substance-use and mental-health screening, HTN/obesity/diabetes/lipid/AAA screening, folic acid, falls, DEXA in women, IPV, advance-care planning) was verified current. The practice-relevant additions:

  • 2026 ACC/AHA Multisociety Dyslipidemia Guideline: use the PREVENT calculator (ages 30–79) for primary-prevention CV risk — it replaces the Pooled Cohort Equations (which overestimated 10-year risk ~40–50%). Risk tiers: low <3%, borderline 3–<5%, intermediate 5–<10%, high ≥10%. Theme: "earlier and lower, for longer," with LDL-C targets of <55 (very-high-risk), <70 (high-risk), and <100 mg/dL (intermediate on statin), and earlier statins in familial hypercholesterolemia, those ≤30 with LDL ≥160, strong family ASCVD history, or high 30-year risk. The USPSTF statin threshold (40–75 + ≥1 RF, 10-yr risk >10%) is unchanged.
  • Pneumococcal (2024 ACIP): PCV21 is now an option for adults ≥50 (age lowered from 65) and 19–49 with chronic disease; the cases list "PCV20," which remains valid. If PCV15 is used, follow with PPSV23.
  • Mental health (USPSTF 2023, finalized): screen all adults for depression and adults <65 for anxiety — matches the board's framing.
  • RSV vaccine: recommended for adults ≥75, and 60–74 at increased risk, by shared decision-making.
  • Osteoporosis in men: USPSTF still finds insufficient evidence to screen men (the deck's "as of 2022" footnote remains accurate).

lessons.refs:
Original lesson references retained (NIAAA alcohol thresholds; CDC STEADI; ADA Standards 2021; UpToDate chapters; USPSTF, accessed 2021–2024; and others as listed on the TeachIM page). Newly cited for the update:

  1. 2026 ACC/AHA/Multisociety Guideline on the Management of Dyslipidemia. Circulation / JACC 2026 (PREVENT calculator; "earlier and lower for longer" LDL targets).
  2. Khan SS, et al. Predicting Risk of Cardiovascular Disease EVENTs (PREVENT) equations. Circulation 2023/2024.
  3. US Preventive Services Task Force. Statin Use for Primary Prevention of CVD in Adults (40–75, 10-yr risk >10%, Grade B).
  4. Kobayashi M, et al. Use of PCV21 among U.S. adults: ACIP recommendations, 2024. MMWR.
  5. USPSTF. Screening for Depression (all adults) and Anxiety Disorders (adults <65), 2023.

lessons.title: Alcohol-related Liver Disease
lessons.subtitle: Optimized lesson package · Facilitator-led interactive chalk talk for IM residents · 20 min (25–30 min with cases)
lessons.credit: Adapted from TeachIM.org — Alcohol-related Liver Disease (Mowry, Haydek; sec. ed. Mun; exec. ed. Zhang; Nov 2022), CC BY-NC 4.0. Optimized June 2026: guideline currency verified, one deck error corrected, pedagogy restructured, assessments added. Companion deck: Alcohol-related_Liver_Disease_Optimized.pptx (original interactive board, surgically corrected).
objectives: Identify the spectrum of alcohol-related liver disease (ALD) and the risk factors for developing it.
Select diagnostic tests that differentiate hepatic steatosis, alcohol-related hepatitis (AH), and cirrhosis.
Build a paradigm for managing the spectrum of ALD — anchored on abstinence, nutrition, and transplant referral.
Diagnose and risk-stratify acute alcohol-related hepatitis (AH) and decide who warrants corticosteroids.

prework.q: 1. Name the four stages in the spectrum of ALD, in order.
prework.a: Hepatic steatosis (fatty liver) → alcohol-related steatohepatitis (ASH) → fibrosis → cirrhosis. Steatosis and inflammation can regress with abstinence; fibrosis/cirrhosis is largely irreversible.

prework.q: 2. What single intervention most improves survival across the entire ALD spectrum?
prework.a: Complete alcohol abstinence. Nothing else — not steroids, not transplant criteria — matches its effect on the natural history of the disease.

prework.q: 3. A patient with heavy alcohol use has AST 220, ALT 95, total bilirubin 4.0. Which diagnosis does the lab pattern point to, and why?
prework.a: Alcohol-related hepatitis. AST:ALT >2 with both transaminases <400 IU/L plus hyperbilirubinemia (>3 mg/dL) in the right clinical setting is the classic AH signature. Transaminases >400–500 should make you look for another cause (e.g., ischemic or toxic injury).

segments.label: Hook (0:00–0:02)
segments.html:
"Two patients, same six-pack-a-night history. One has a fatty liver that will normalize if he stops; the other is dying of alcohol-related hepatitis with a 30–50% chance of being dead in a month. Same exposure — what separates them, and what can we actually change?" The answer threads the whole talk: where on the spectrum they sit, and whether they stop drinking.

ASK — Before the board: "What's the single most powerful lever we have in any patient with alcohol-related liver disease?" Let them land on abstinence before you reveal anything.

segments.label: Objective 1 — The spectrum & risk factors (0:02–0:08)
segments.html:
Click through the spectrum animation: normal liver → hepatic steatosis → steatohepatitis (ASH) → cirrhosis, each transition driven by continued alcohol use. Anchor the two big ideas: (1) progression is stepwise with ongoing intake, and (2) the early stages are reversible while fibrosis is not.

StageWhat it isReversible?
Hepatic steatosisEarliest, benign; seen in ~90% of heavy drinkers. A marker of altered metabolism and susceptibility.Yes, with cessation
Steatohepatitis (ASH)Inflammatory intermediary; can be indolent or present fulminantly as acute AH. Higher cirrhosis risk than steatosis alone.Inflammation yes; established fibrosis no
CirrhosisEnd-stage fibrosis from the maladaptive scarring response.Largely irreversible
ASK — "What raises a given person's risk of progressing?" Collect answers, then reveal.

Risk factors: quantity (≥2 drinks/day women, ≥3/day men), pattern (binging), genetic predisposition, obesity, female sex, smoking, and concurrent liver disease (viral hepatitis, MASLD).

Pearl. Steatosis is not just "a fatty liver" — it is biological evidence the liver is already responding to alcohol, and it flags who is set up to progress if drinking continues.

segments.label: Objective 2 — Diagnostic evaluation (0:08–0:13)
segments.html:
Who to screen: every adult with current or prior unhealthy alcohol use (screen with CAGE / AUDIT-C). How to screen: RUQ ultrasound plus basic labs — CMP, CBC, INR.

ASK — "You can't tell alcohol-related steatosis from MASLD on ultrasound or biopsy. So what clinches it?" → the clinical history of alcohol use.
EntityKey diagnostic features
Hepatic steatosisOften incidental on imaging; LFTs normal or mildly elevated; indistinguishable from MASLD — history is decisive.
Alcohol-related hepatitisClinical diagnosis: AST:ALT typically >2, both usually <400 IU/L; elevated bilirubin. Biopsy rarely needed.
Fibrosis / cirrhosisNon-invasive scores (FIB-4, APRI, FibroSure/FibroTest, ELF), elastography, or MRI; small nodular liver on US. Biopsy now usually avoidable.
Pearl. Transaminases in ALD are usually modest (<400). AST or ALT in the high hundreds or thousands should redirect you to ischemia, acetaminophen, or viral causes — not "just alcohol."

segments.label: Objective 3 — Managing the spectrum (0:13–0:18)
segments.html:
Four pillars, reveal one at a time:

ASK — "What pharmacotherapy can we offer to support abstinence, and which agent should we avoid in liver disease?"
PillarSpecifics
Alcohol cessationThe single most important factor for survival. Naltrexone (PO daily or monthly IM; hepatically metabolized, can be hepatotoxic, not studied in AH/cirrhosis) and acamprosate (PO TID; no hepatotoxicity but avoid in severe renal dysfunction) are first-line FDA-approved agents. Avoid disulfiram in ALD. Pair with psychosocial/behavioral treatment (counseling, mutual-help groups, residential programs).
NutritionHigh-protein diet; thiamine, folate, and B-complex supplementation for anyone still drinking or newly sober; low-sodium if portal hypertension; consider zinc, especially in AH.
Manage complicationsTreat ascites/SBP, varices, encephalopathy; vaccinate and screen (HCC surveillance, EGD for varices).
Transplant evaluationRefer cirrhosis that doesn't recover with abstinence — and, increasingly, carefully selected severe AH (see 2024 update).

segments.label: Objective 4 — Acute alcohol-related hepatitis (0:18–0:24)
segments.html:
Clinical diagnosis (AASLD): heavy recent use (≥3 drinks/day women, ≥4/day men for ≥60 days), onset of jaundice within 8 weeks, AST >50 with AST:ALT typically >2 (both <400 IU/L), total bilirubin >3 mg/dL. Often leukocytosis with neutrophilia and tender hepatomegaly. Evaluate for infection in every patient.

ASK — "Your AH patient has an MDF of 40 and no infection or bleeding. What's the decision, and how will you know in a week whether it's working?"
  1. Risk-stratify: Maddrey Discriminant Function (MDF, from bilirubin + INR) is most common; MDF ≥32 (or MELD >20) = severe (deck quotes 30–50% 30-day mortality).
  2. Treat: prednisolone 40 mg/day if no contraindication — short-term (28-day) survival benefit only. Adding IV NAC may further improve short-term survival (2024 ACG).
  3. Contraindications to steroids: active/known infection, AKI with Cr >2.5 mg/dL, uncontrolled GI bleed, multiorgan failure/shock, or an alternative cause of injury. (AKI is relative — reconsider if it resolves.)
  4. Reassess at day 4–7 with the Lille score: <0.45 = responder, complete the 28-day course (≈85% 6-month survival); ≥0.45 = non-responder, stop steroids and pivot to palliative care / transplant evaluation.
Pearl. Steroids in AH buy weeks, not years, and only in responders. The Lille score at day 4–7 is your honesty check — if the bilirubin isn't moving, the risks of immunosuppression have stopped being worth it.

segments.label: Retrieval close (0:24–0:25)
segments.html: Pens down, board off: "Rebuild the take-home points from memory — the spectrum, the one intervention that matters most, the lab signature of AH, and the steroid decision rule." Fill gaps from what they reconstruct.

cases.title: Case 1 — Diagnosis & evaluation of ALD
cases.stem: 60-year-old man, HTN on lisinopril, drinks a 6-pack of beer nightly, never-smoker, no drug use. Exam: BP 129/78, P 75, SpO2 94%, BMI 28, hepatomegaly. Labs: WBC 8, Hgb 14, plts 208; AST 80, ALT 55, ALP 110, Tbili 0.9, INR 1.1; HCV antibody negative within the year.
cases.reveal: RUQ ultrasound (assess degree of liver disease and complications) and calculate APRI (screens for cirrhosis in ALD). Counsel on alcohol cessation. Routine hepatology referral is not yet required; repeating labs after a period of abstinence is reasonable, but here the elevated APRI/INR warrants imaging now.

cases.title: Case 2 — Diagnosis & management of AH
cases.stem: 35-year-old woman, 1 week of progressive jaundice, new ascites, and dyspnea on exertion. Drinks 3–4 drinks/night, occasional binges; last drink ~24 h ago. Exam: T 37.9 °C, HR 90, BP 120/88, RR 20, SpO2 94% RA; jaundiced, distended abdomen with fluid wave, 2+ LE edema to the knees, confused with asterixis. Labs: Cr 0.82, WBC 14, Hgb 11, plts 108; AST 224, ALT 110, ALP 118, Tbili 3.4, INR 2.1. US: enlarged fatty liver, moderate ascites.
cases.reveal: Alcohol-related steatohepatitis (acute AH). AST:ALT >2 with transaminases in the low hundreds, elevated bilirubin, recent heavy use, and the complications (ascites, encephalopathy). The enlarged liver argues against established cirrhosis; rule out SBP and other infection (here CXR, UA, and SBP work-up are negative).

mcqs.stem: A 52-year-old man with heavy daily alcohol use is admitted with 2 weeks of jaundice. Labs: AST 180, ALT 70, total bilirubin 9.2 mg/dL, INR 1.9, Cr 0.9, WBC 13.5. CXR, UA, and ascitic fluid are negative for infection. MDF is 48. Which is the most appropriate next step?
opts: Liver biopsy to confirm the diagnosis before treatment
Defer pharmacotherapy and reassess in 2 weeks
mcqs.exp:
B — Prednisolone 40 mg daily. This is severe AH (MDF ≥32) with no contraindication (no infection, AKI, or bleed), so corticosteroids are indicated for a short-term survival benefit.

A wrong: AH is a clinical diagnosis; biopsy is rarely needed and would delay treatment. C wrong: pentoxifylline does not improve survival (STOPAH) and is not recommended over steroids. D wrong: delaying steroids in eligible severe AH forgoes the only short-term mortality benefit. E wrong: naltrexone supports abstinence but is hepatically metabolized, not studied in AH, and treats neither the acute hepatitis nor mortality.

mcqs.stem: The patient in Question 1 starts prednisolone. On day 6, repeat labs are obtained and the Lille score is 0.62. What is the most appropriate management?
opts: Continue prednisolone to complete the 28-day course
Stop prednisolone and focus on supportive care / transplant evaluation
mcqs.exp:
C — Stop prednisolone. A Lille score ≥0.45 marks a non-responder; bilirubin has not improved, so continued steroids add infection risk without benefit. Pivot to supportive care and transplant evaluation.

A wrong: completing the course is for responders (Lille <0.45). B/E wrong: escalating or changing the steroid does not rescue a Lille non-responder. D wrong: adding pentoxifylline to steroids does not confer a survival benefit.

mcqs.stem: A 45-year-old woman with alcohol use disorder is found to have hepatic steatosis on ultrasound and AST 60, ALT 48, normal bilirubin and INR. She asks what will most improve her long-term liver outcome. Which is the best answer?
mcqs.exp:
C — Complete alcohol abstinence. It is the single most important factor for survival across the ALD spectrum; at the steatosis stage, the liver can fully recover with cessation.

A wrong: vitamin E is studied in MASH, not ALD, and not for survival here. B wrong: nutrition matters (high-protein diet, vitamins) but a low-fat diet is not the key lever. D wrong: ursodeoxycholic acid has no role in ALD. E wrong: steroids are for severe acute AH, not bland steatosis.

mcqs.stem: A 58-year-old man with alcohol-related cirrhosis wants help staying sober. He has stage 3 CKD (eGFR 38) and decompensated cirrhosis. Which pharmacotherapy is most appropriate?
opts: None — counseling only is safest given his liver disease
mcqs.exp:
C — Naltrexone is the better choice here. Acamprosate is renally cleared and should be avoided in severe renal dysfunction; naltrexone is the more appropriate FDA-approved agent, with monitoring (it is hepatically metabolized and not formally studied in advanced disease, so use with care plus psychosocial support).

A wrong: disulfiram is avoided in ALD (hepatotoxicity). B wrong: acamprosate is contraindicated in severe renal dysfunction. D wrong: topiramate is non-FDA-approved second-line. E wrong: withholding all pharmacotherapy abandons an effective relapse-prevention tool — combine medication with counseling.

mcqs.stem: A 40-year-old man with heavy alcohol use presents with jaundice. Labs: AST 540, ALT 610, total bilirubin 6.0, INR 1.4. Which statement is most accurate?
opts: This lab pattern is classic for alcohol-related hepatitis
The very high transaminases should prompt evaluation for another cause
Prednisolone is indicated based on these values alone
A normal INR excludes severe liver injury
mcqs.exp:
C — Look for another cause. In AH transaminases are usually <400 IU/L with AST:ALT >2. Values in the 500–600 range with ALT > AST point away from pure AH toward ischemic, toxic (e.g., acetaminophen), or viral hepatitis — check an APAP level and acute hepatitis panel.

A wrong: the magnitude and AST:ALT pattern don't fit AH. B wrong: the ratio doesn't confirm cirrhosis. D wrong: you cannot commit to steroids before establishing the diagnosis and excluding contraindications. E wrong: a near-normal INR does not exclude serious injury.

anki: The spectrum of ALD, in order?
Hepatic steatosis → steatohepatitis (ASH) → fibrosis → cirrhosis.
Which ALD stages are reversible with abstinence?
Steatosis and inflammation regress; established fibrosis/cirrhosis is largely irreversible.
Prevalence of hepatic steatosis in heavy drinkers?
Single most important factor improving survival in ALD?
Complete alcohol abstinence.
First-line FDA-approved agents for relapse prevention in ALD?
Naltrexone and acamprosate.
Which AUD medication is avoided in ALD?
Disulfiram (hepatotoxicity).
Acamprosate caution?
Avoid in severe renal dysfunction (renally cleared).
Who to screen for ALD?
All adults >18 with current or prior unhealthy alcohol use (CAGE, AUDIT-C).
Initial ALD work-up?
RUQ ultrasound + CMP, CBC, INR.
What clinches alcohol vs MASLD steatosis?
Clinical history — they are indistinguishable on US/biopsy.
Non-invasive fibrosis tests in ALD?
FIB-4, APRI, FibroSure/FibroTest, ELF; elastography; MRI.
Lab signature of alcohol-related hepatitis (AH)?
AST:ALT >2, both usually <400 IU/L; total bilirubin >3 mg/dL; recent heavy use; jaundice ≤8 wks.
Transaminases >400–500 in a drinker — implication?
Look for another cause (ischemic, acetaminophen, viral) — not pure AH.
Severity threshold for severe AH?
MDF ≥32 (or MELD >20).
Pharmacotherapy for severe AH without contraindications?
Prednisolone 40 mg/day (short-term survival benefit); IV NAC may add short-term benefit.
Contraindications to steroids in AH?
Active infection, AKI Cr >2.5, uncontrolled GI bleed, multiorgan failure/shock, alternative cause.
Lille score: when and cutoff?
Day 4–7; <0.45 = responder (continue 28-day course, ~85% 6-mo survival); ≥0.45 = stop steroids.
Nutrition in ALD?
High-protein diet + thiamine, folate, B-complex; low-sodium if portal HTN; consider zinc in AH.
lessons.dot:
#Alcohol-related liver dz (chronic spectrum): steatosis → steatohepatitis(ASH) → fibrosis → cirrhosis
 stage *[steatosis/ASH/acute AH/compensated cirr/decompensated cirr] 2/2 *[g/day x *yrs] EtOH
 dx: hx heavy use + RUQ US *[fatty/nodular/normal] + *[FIB-4 */APRI */elastography *kPa]; AST:ALT *, Tbili *, INR *, plt *
  -- if AST/ALT >400 or ALT>AST: r/o other cause [ischemic, APAP (lvl *), viral hep panel]
PILLARS:
 #1 abstinence = #1 survival driver: 1st-line [naltrexone PO/IM (hepatic, caution adv dz) | acamprosate PO TID (AVOID if eGFR<30)]; AVOID disulfiram; + psychosocial/MAT referral
 #2 nutrition: high-protein diet + thiamine/folate/B-complex; low-Na if portal HTN; consider zinc
 #3 complications: ascites/SBP, varices(EGD), HE, HCC surveillance, viral hep vacc/screen
 #4 transplant: refer non-recovering cirr; consider early LT for select severe AH non-responders
#Acute alcohol-related hepatitis (if applicable):
 dx [AASLD clinical]: heavy use (F≥3/M≥4 drinks/d x≥60d), jaundice ≤8wk, AST:ALT *>2 (both<400), Tbili *>3
 r/o infection EVERY pt [CXR, UA, dx para for SBP]; w/u [acute hep panel, APAP, drug screen, RUQ US doppler]
 severity: MDF *[=32 thresh] / MELD *[>20] → severe = 30-50% 30d mortality
 steroids: prednisolone 40 mg/d IF no contraindic [active infxn, AKI Cr>2.5, uncontrolled GIB, MOF/shock, alt cause]
  -- ± IV NAC adjunct (short-term survival)
  -- reassess Lille day4-7: <0.45 responder → complete 28d (~85% 6-mo surv); ≥0.45 stop steroids → palliative/LT eval
 supportive: address nutrition, manage complications, d/c beta-blockers if SBP/AKI/hypotension
takehome: ALD is a spectrum — steatosis → steatohepatitis → fibrosis/cirrhosis. Early stages regress with abstinence; fibrosis does not.
Abstinence is the single most important factor for survival. Offer naltrexone or acamprosate (first-line) plus psychosocial treatment; avoid disulfiram.
AH is a clinical diagnosis: heavy recent use, jaundice ≤8 wks, AST:ALT >2 with both <400 IU/L, bilirubin >3. Rule out infection in everyone.
Risk-stratify AH with MDF ≥32 (or MELD >20); consider prednisolone 40 mg/day (± NAC) without contraindications, and use the day 4–7 Lille score to continue (<0.45) or stop (≥0.45).
Nutrition and complication management matter throughout; refer non-recovering cirrhosis — and selected severe AH — for transplant evaluation.

extras.label: 2024 Guideline & Nomenclature Update
extras.html:
The standing teaching is unchanged and remains current. Surface these newer points so the projected board reflects 2024 practice (also captured on the appended deck slide):

  • 2024 ACG Clinical Guideline on Alcohol-Associated Liver Disease (Am J Gastroenterol 2024;119:30–54) reaffirms the framework taught here: abstinence is the cornerstone; MDF ≥32 or MELD >20 defines severe AH for steroid consideration; Lille at day 4–7 guides continuation.
  • Prednisolone + NAC: adding IV N-acetylcysteine to prednisolone 40 mg/day may improve short-term (28–30 day) survival in severe AH — a reasonable adjunct, still no long-term benefit.
  • Early liver transplantation for carefully selected severe AH non-responsive to medical therapy is now endorsed (ACG/AASLD), with outcomes comparable to other indications — no longer purely experimental.
  • 2023 multi-society nomenclature change (Hepatology 2023;78:1966): NAFLD → MASLD, NASH → MASH; new MetALD category for metabolic dysfunction plus moderate alcohol use. Read the deck's "indistinguishable from NAFLD" lines as "indistinguishable from MASLD."

  3. Singal AK, Mathurin P. Diagnosis and Treatment of Alcohol-Associated Liver Disease: A Review. JAMA. 2021;326(2):165–176.
  4. Louvet A, Naveau S, Abdelnour M, et al. The Lille model: a new tool for therapeutic strategy in patients with severe alcoholic hepatitis treated with steroids. Hepatology. 2007;45(6):1348–1354. (Lille <0.45 → ~85% 6-mo survival)
  5. Rinella ME, Lazarus JV, Ratziu V, et al. A multisociety Delphi consensus statement on new fatty liver disease nomenclature (MASLD/MASH/MetALD). Hepatology. 2023;78(6):1966–1986. (newly cited)
  6. Maddrey WC, Boitnott JK, Bedine MS, et al. Corticosteroid therapy of alcoholic hepatitis. Gastroenterology. 1978;75(2):193–199.

  8. Singal AK, Bataller R, Ahn J, et al. ACG Clinical Guideline: Alcoholic Liver Disease. Am J Gastroenterol. 2018;113(2):175–194.

lessons.title: Antibiotics Part 2: Sites of Infection
lessons.subtitle: Optimized lesson package · Facilitator-led interactive chalk talk for IM residents · 45 min (+15 min cases)
lessons.credit: Adapted from TeachIM.org — Antibiotics Part 2: Sites of Infection (Yilin Zhang MD; Brandon Fainstad MD; Mike Hori MD; Sept 2021), CC BY-NC 4.0. Optimized June 2026: guideline currency verified, two spelling errors corrected, pedagogy restructured, assessments added. Companion deck: Antibiotics_Sites_of_Infection_Optimized.pptx (original "Antibiotic Man" interactive board, surgically corrected).
objectives: Identify the bacterial pathogens commonly responsible for pneumonia, UTI, intra-abdominal infection, skin/soft-tissue infection, and meningitis — and how community vs. hospital acquisition shifts the "bugs."
Differentiate CAP, HAP/VAP, and aspiration pneumonia by pathogens and empiric coverage, including when to add MRSA and Pseudomonas coverage.
Diagnose catheter-associated UTI and distinguish simple cystitis, complicated UTI, and CAUTI by host risk and microbiology.
Differentiate nonpurulent, purulent, and necrotizing soft-tissue infections by pathogen and treatment.
Differentiate community- vs. hospital-acquired intra-abdominal infection and choose first- and second-line regimens, always paired with source control.

prework.q: Q1. The 2019 IDSA/ATS CAP guideline retired "healthcare-associated pneumonia (HCAP)." So which two patient features now actually warrant empiric MRSA/Pseudomonas coverage in a patient with CAP?
prework.a: (1) Prior respiratory isolation of MRSA or Pseudomonas, and (2) hospitalization with receipt of IV antibiotics within the last 90 days — ideally validated against local risk factors. Otherwise, treat as routine CAP (ceftriaxone + azithromycin, or a respiratory fluoroquinolone).

prework.q: Q2. A patient with a chronic indwelling Foley grows 10⁵ CFU of E. coli but has no symptoms. Do you treat?
prework.a: No. CAUTI requires symptoms or signs of UTI plus the bacteriuria and a catheter (or one within 48 h). Asymptomatic bacteriuria in a catheterized patient is not treated (exceptions: pregnancy, pre-urologic procedure with mucosal trauma).

prework.q: Q3. Which soft-tissue infection is a surgical emergency, and what three exam findings should raise the alarm?
prework.a: Necrotizing soft-tissue infection (NSTI). Red flags: pain out of proportion / tenderness extending beyond the erythema, rapid progression, and systemic toxicity (also crepitus, bullae, "woody" edema, skin necrosis). Antibiotics do not substitute for urgent surgical debridement.

segments.label: Hook (2 min)
segments.html:
"Two patients, same drug question, opposite answers: a healthy 40-year-old with a productive cough, and a ventilated ICU patient on hospital day 6 with a new infiltrate. Both have pneumonia — why does only one get vancomycin and Zosyn?" The whole talk is that question, repeated across five body sites: the site tells you the likely bugs, and where the patient caught it tells you which resistant bugs to add.

ASK (frame the session): "Across all of these infections, what three organisms does hospital exposure keep adding to the list?" Reveal: MRSA, Pseudomonas, Enterococcus. That trio is the spine of the whole talk.

segments.label: Objective 1 & 2 — Pneumonia
segments.html:
ASK: "Empiric bugs for CAP?" before clicking. Reveal: S. pneumoniae, GNRs (H. flu, Moraxella), atypicals (Mycoplasma, Chlamydophila, Legionella). Empiric: ceftriaxone + azithromycin (or doxycycline), or a respiratory fluoroquinolone — regardless of severity.
TypeBugs addedEmpiric drugs
CAPS. pneumoniae, GNRs, atypicalsCeftriaxone + azithromycin/doxycycline, or respiratory FQ (levo/moxi)
HAP/VAP (>48 h)+ MRSA, + Pseudomonas, + MDR GNRs(Vanc or linezolid) + (Zosyn/cefepime/meropenem/aztreonam/levofloxacin)
AspirationS. pneumoniae/GPCs, enteric GNRs, oral anaerobesCAP coverage; add anaerobes (Unasyn/clinda/moxi) only if lung abscess or empyema
Pearl — HCAP is dead. The 2019 IDSA/ATS guideline abandoned HCAP. Add MRSA/Pseudomonas coverage to CAP only for (1) prior MRSA/Pseudomonas, or (2) hospitalization + IV antibiotics within 90 days. Draw cultures so you can de-escalate.
Pearl — anaerobes in aspiration. Newer evidence says acute aspiration events rarely involve anaerobes. Don't reflexively add metronidazole/clindamycin — reserve anaerobic coverage for lung abscess or empyema.
ASK: "A negative MRSA nares PCR — does it help?" Reveal: yes, its high negative predictive value lets you stop empiric vancomycin (most useful in low-prevalence settings).

segments.label: Objective 3 — Urinary tract infections
segments.html:
ASK: "One organism causes 75–95% of cystitis — which?" Reveal: E. coli. Others: Proteus, Klebsiella, S. saprophyticus; Staph/Enterococcus/Pseudomonas appear with healthcare exposure.
SyndromeDrugs
Simple cystitis (1st line)Nitrofurantoin 100 mg BID ×5 d, or TMP-SMX ×3 d (if local resistance <20%), or fosfomycin 3 g ×1
Simple cystitis (2nd line)Urinary FQ ×3 d, or beta-lactam (Augmentin/cefpodoxime) ×3–7 d
Pyelonephritis / complicated UTICeftriaxone (no MDR risk) → Zosyn or meropenem (MDR risk/critically ill); add vanc/dapto/linezolid if GPCs / Enterococcus
Pearl — never nitrofurantoin or fosfomycin for pyelonephritis. They don't achieve tissue/renal levels. They are bladder-only drugs.
Pearl — CAUTI is a clinical diagnosis. Symptoms/signs of UTI + bacteriuria + an indwelling/suprapubic/intermittent catheter (or one within 48 h). Colonization is the rule in catheterized patients — do not treat asymptomatic bacteriuria. Long-term catheters trend polymicrobial (Proteus common); replace the catheter and culture from a fresh one.

segments.label: Objective 4 — Skin & soft-tissue infections
segments.html:
ASK: "Nonpurulent vs purulent cellulitis — which bug drives each?" Reveal: nonpurulent = Strep (treat Strep + MSSA); purulent = S. aureus, mostly MRSA (treat MRSA).
CategoryBugsDrugs
NonpurulentStrep >> S. aureusCephalexin (PO) / cefazolin or ceftriaxone (IV)
PurulentMSSA/MRSAI&D + MRSA coverage: vanc/dapto (IV) or Bactrim/doxycycline (PO) ± cephalexin
NSTIGAS > S. aureus, GNRs, Clostridium; often polymicrobialSurgery + (vanc/dapto) + (Zosyn/meropenem) + clindamycin
Pearl — purulent SSTI is a drainage problem first. I&D is the primary treatment; culture the pus. Routine added Strep coverage is usually unnecessary. In toxic-shock-picture patients, add penicillin + clindamycin (antitoxin effect).
ASK: "Why clindamycin in NSTI and strep toxic shock?" Reveal: ribosomal protein-synthesis inhibition shuts off exotoxin production (the "Eagle effect" / antitoxin activity) — independent of its antibacterial role.

segments.label: Objective 5 — Intra-abdominal infections
segments.html:
ASK: "Community-acquired intra-abdominal infection — three bug categories?" Reveal: enteric GNRs (Enterobacterales), streptococci, and anaerobes (B. fragilis). Enterococcus and Pseudomonas are not routine in community infection.
SettingBugsDrugs
Community-acquiredEnteric GNRs, strep, anaerobes (B. fragilis)3rd-gen cephalosporin + metronidazole, or FQ + metronidazole (resistance-limited)
Hospital-acquired+ Pseudomonas, + Enterococcus, ± MRSA/CoNS(Zosyn) or (cefepime + metronidazole) or meropenem; ± vancomycin
Pearl — antibiotics never substitute for source control. Abscess drainage, cholecystectomy/cholecystostomy, or perforation repair is half the treatment. Avoid ampicillin-sulbactam empirically for IAI given high E. coli resistance.

segments.label: Retrieval close (3 min)
segments.html: Erase the board (or close the deck). Have learners rebuild the take-home grid from memory: for each of the five sites, name the baseline bugs, then say what hospital exposure adds and which drug you'd add for it. Whoever offers a regimen has to name the one organism it's covering.

cases.title: Case 1 — Pneumonia
cases.stem: 70-year-old, 4 days of productive cough, malaise, fevers. T 100.9 °F, HR 110, BP 99/53, RR 33, SpO₂ 90%. WBC 17k, lactate 2.1. CXR: focal RLL consolidation.
cases.reveal: CAP. By vital signs and age (CURB-65), manage in the hospital.

cases.title: Case 2 — Skin/soft tissue
cases.stem: 30-year-old with IVDU, painful swollen L arm 2 days after injecting. T 99 °F, HR 100s, BP 100/60. L arm red, warm, tender, with a 2×2 cm area of fluctuance.
cases.reveal: Purulent SSTI (abscess with surrounding cellulitis).

cases.title: Case 3 — Intra-abdominal
cases.stem: 21-year-old, abdominal pain, nausea/vomiting. T 103 °F, HR 140s, BP 95/55, SaO₂ 99% RA. Rigid abdomen with rebound. CT: appendicitis.
cases.reveal: Enteric GNRs, streptococci, enteric anaerobes (Bacteroides).

cases.title: Case 4a — UTI
cases.stem: 65-year-old with prior stroke (L hemiparesis, residual dysphagia) presents from SNF with confusion. T 100.5 °F, HR 110s, BP 110/60s. Grimaces with suprapubic and L-flank palpation. WBC 17k, lactate 2.1. CXR clear. UA: +LE, 50 WBCs, many bacteria.
cases.reveal: Suspected pyelonephritis / complicated UTI.

cases.title: Case 4b — Pneumonia (same patient, later)
cases.stem: The same 65-year-old (prior stroke) is readmitted 2 months later with cough and dyspnea, admitted to the ICU. CXR: RLL consolidation.
cases.reveal: CAP vs. aspiration pneumonia (dysphagia history).

cases.title: Case 5 — Skin/soft tissue (the trap)
cases.stem: 33-year-old, rapidly ascending L-leg erythema starting 12 h prior. T 103 °F, HR 150s, BP 80s/40s. Deep erythema ankle → mid-thigh with duskiness; tenderness beyond the erythema; no fluctuance. Na 123, Cr 3.5 (baseline 1.0), WBC 25k, lactate 6.7.
cases.reveal: Necrotizing soft-tissue infection — systemic toxicity, rapid spread, pain/tenderness beyond the visible margin, duskiness, and a high LRINEC-type lab profile.

mcqs.stem: A 68-year-old man is admitted with CAP. He has no history of MRSA or Pseudomonas and has not been hospitalized or received IV antibiotics in the past year. His nephew, a student, suggests "HCAP coverage" because he lives in an assisted-living facility. What is the most appropriate empiric regimen?
mcqs.exp: B. The 2019 IDSA/ATS guideline abolished HCAP; residence in a facility is no longer, by itself, a reason for broad-spectrum coverage. Empiric MRSA/Pseudomonas coverage (A, D) is reserved for prior MRSA/Pseudomonas isolation or hospitalization + IV antibiotics within 90 days. Carbapenem monotherapy (C) is excessive and misses atypicals. Standard inpatient CAP therapy is a beta-lactam + macrolide (or a respiratory fluoroquinolone).

mcqs.stem: A 74-year-old woman with a chronic indwelling Foley is admitted for a hip fracture. Routine urine culture grows 10⁵ CFU/mL E. coli. She is afebrile with no urinary symptoms and a normal WBC. What is the best next step?
opts: No antibiotics; do not treat asymptomatic bacteriuria
Exchange the catheter and start fosfomycin
mcqs.exp: C. CAUTI requires symptoms or signs of infection; this is asymptomatic catheter-associated bacteriuria, which is not treated (treating it drives resistance and C. difficile, without benefit). Exceptions are pregnancy and pre-urologic procedures with anticipated mucosal trauma — neither applies. All antibiotic options (A, B, D) are therefore wrong; nitrofurantoin/fosfomycin would also be inappropriate if true pyelonephritis were present.

mcqs.stem: A 40-year-old man has rapidly spreading leg erythema, pain out of proportion to exam extending beyond the visible margin, a dusky bulla, and hypotension. Lactate is 6. What single intervention most improves survival?
opts: Adding clindamycin to vancomycin and piperacillin-tazobactam
mcqs.exp: B. NSTI is a surgical emergency; early, aggressive debridement is the dominant determinant of survival. Broad antibiotics with clindamycin for antitoxin effect (A) are necessary but adjunctive. IVIG (C) and hyperbaric oxygen (D) are at best unproven adjuncts and must never delay the OR. Antibiotics cannot sterilize dead, non-perfused tissue.

mcqs.stem: A 60-year-old with diverticulitis and a contained perforation is started on empiric antibiotics. He was hospitalized last month and received 6 days of broad-spectrum antibiotics. Which pathogens must your empiric regimen now additionally cover compared with a first-presentation community case?
mcqs.exp: B. Recent hospitalization and broad-spectrum antibiotic exposure make this healthcare-associated IAI: empiric coverage must add Pseudomonas and Enterococcus (e.g., piperacillin-tazobactam, ± vancomycin for Enterococcus/MRSA risk). Atypicals (A) are respiratory pathogens, irrelevant to IAI. Anaerobes (D) are already covered in any IAI regimen. MRSA alone (C) misses the key additions. Source control remains essential.

mcqs.stem: A 25-year-old woman has dysuria and frequency for 2 days, no fever or flank pain, and is otherwise healthy. Local E. coli resistance to TMP-SMX is 12%. Which is the most appropriate first-line therapy?
opts: Ceftriaxone IV once, then oral cephalexin
mcqs.exp: B. Uncomplicated cystitis — first-line options are nitrofurantoin ×5 d, TMP-SMX ×3 d (appropriate here since resistance <20%), or fosfomycin ×1. Fluoroquinolones (A) are effective but reserved as second-line due to collateral harm and longer-than-needed course. Amoxicillin alone (C) has unacceptable resistance. IV/parenteral therapy (D) is unnecessary for simple cystitis. Note: 2024 WikiGuidelines make these definitions non-sex-dependent, but management of simple cystitis is unchanged.

anki: Three organisms that hospital exposure keeps adding across infection sites?
MRSA, Pseudomonas, and Enterococcus.
Empiric bugs and drugs for inpatient CAP?
S. pneumoniae, GNRs (H. flu, Moraxella), atypicals -> ceftriaxone + azithromycin/doxycycline, or a respiratory fluoroquinolone (regardless of severity).
Two features that justify adding MRSA/Pseudomonas coverage to CAP?
(1) Prior MRSA or Pseudomonas isolation; (2) hospitalization + IV antibiotics within 90 days. HCAP was retired in 2019.
HAP vs VAP definitions?
HAP: pneumonia >48 h after admission, not ventilator-associated. VAP: pneumonia >48 h after intubation.
When do you add anaerobic coverage for aspiration pneumonia?
Only for lung abscess or empyema. Routine aspiration events rarely involve anaerobes.
Value of a negative MRSA nares PCR in pneumonia?
High negative predictive value -- use it to stop empiric vancomycin (most reassuring in low-prevalence settings).
Most common cause of uncomplicated cystitis, and its share?
E. coli, 75-95% of cases. Others: Proteus, Klebsiella, S. saprophyticus.
First-line options for simple cystitis?
Nitrofurantoin 100 mg BID x5 d; TMP-SMX x3 d (if local resistance <20%); fosfomycin 3 g x1.
Two drugs that must NEVER be used for pyelonephritis?
Nitrofurantoin and fosfomycin -- they don't reach renal/tissue concentrations (bladder-only).
Definition of CAUTI?
Symptoms/signs of UTI + bacteriuria (>=10^5 CFU) in a patient with an indwelling, suprapubic, or intermittent catheter, or one removed within 48 h. Don't treat asymptomatic bacteriuria.
UTI MDR risk factors (within 3 months)?
Prior MDR/ESBL organism; inpatient/care-facility stay; recent fluoroquinolone, Bactrim, or broad-spectrum beta-lactam; travel to a high-MDR area.
Nonpurulent cellulitis -- bug and treatment?
Streptococci >> S. aureus -> cephalexin (PO) or cefazolin/ceftriaxone (IV). No role for superficial wound cultures.
Purulent SSTI -- bug and primary treatment?
S. aureus, MRSA > MSSA -> incision & drainage first; add MRSA coverage (vanc/dapto IV, or Bactrim/doxycycline PO). Culture the pus.
NSTI -- exam red flags and management?
Pain/tenderness beyond the erythema, rapid progression, systemic toxicity, crepitus, bullae, "woody" edema, necrosis -> urgent surgery + vanc + Zosyn/meropenem + clindamycin (antitoxin).
Why add clindamycin in NSTI / streptococcal toxic shock?
It inhibits ribosomal protein synthesis, shutting off exotoxin production (antitoxin / "Eagle" effect) independent of bactericidal activity.
Community-acquired intra-abdominal infection -- bugs and first-line drugs?
Enteric GNRs, streptococci, anaerobes (B. fragilis) -> 3rd-gen cephalosporin + metronidazole (or FQ + metronidazole). Enterococcus/Pseudomonas not routine.
What does hospital-acquired IAI add, and what's the non-negotiable adjunct?
Adds Pseudomonas and Enterococcus (+/- MRSA): Zosyn or cefepime+metronidazole or meropenem +/- vancomycin. Source control is essential in every IAI.
lessons.dot:
#Empiric abx by site: susp * [PNA / UTI / SSTI / IAI / meningitis], acq * [community / hospital (>48h or abx-exposed)]. MDR risk * [prior MRSA/PsA, hosp+IV abx <90d, ESBL hx, care-facility, recent FQ/BL/Bactrim]. Source control needed? * [Y/N]. Sepsis * [Y/N] → cx x2 + lactate before abx.
- PNA: CAP [S.pneumo, GNR (Hflu/Morax), atypicals] → ceftriaxone + [azithro / doxy], or resp FQ [levo/moxi]. add MRSA+PsA ONLY if [prior MRSA/PsA OR hosp+IV abx <90d OR severe]: (vanc/linezolid) + (zosyn/cefepime/mero/aztreonam). HAP/VAP (>48h) → cover MRSA+PsA. aspiration → CAP cov; anaerobes [unasyn/clinda/moxi] ONLY if abscess/empyema. MRSA nares neg → d/c vanc. severe CAP → +hydrocort <400mg/d x5-7d (NOT non-severe). narrow at 48h; tx 3-5d if stable.
- UTI: cystitis [E.coli 75-95%] → nitrofurantoin x5d / TMP-SMX x3d (R<20%) / fosfomycin x1. *NEVER nitro/fosfo for pyelo.* pyelo/complicated → ceftriaxone; if MDR risk/critically ill → zosyn or mero; +vanc/dapto/linezolid if Enterococcus/GPC. CAUTI = sx + bacteriuria + cath(≤48h) [add PsA, Enterococcus]; *do NOT treat asx bacteriuria.* tx 7-14d.
- SSTI: nonpurulent [Strep>>MSSA] → cephalexin / cefazolin. purulent [MRSA>MSSA] → I&D + (vanc/dapto, or PO Bactrim/doxy) ± cephalexin. NSTI [GAS, S.aureus, GNR, Clostridium, polymicrobial] → STAT surgery + (vanc/dapto)+(zosyn/mero)+clindamycin (antitoxin). cx pus, NOT superficial swabs.
- IAI: community [enteric GNR, strep, anaerobes (B.frag)] → 3rd-gen ceph + metronidazole (or FQ + metro). hospital/HCA [+PsA, +Enterococcus, ±MRSA] → zosyn / (cefepime+metro) / meropenem ± vanc. *pair w/ source control* [drain/cholecystectomy/repair]. avoid amp-sulbactam (E.coli R).
- de-escalate to culture+sensitivities at 48-72h; reassess duration daily; abx allergy * ; renal/wt dose-adjust * .

lessons.title: Antibiotics Part 1: Spectrum
lessons.subtitle: Optimized lesson package · Facilitator-led interactive chalk talk for IM residents · 20–25 min (+10 min cases)
lessons.credit: Adapted from TeachIM.org — Antibiotics Part 1: Spectrum (Brandon Fainstad MD; Yilin Zhang MD; expert review Diana Zhong MD MHS; updated March 2024), CC BY-NC 4.0. Optimized June 2026: content verified current, one spelling error corrected, pedagogy and assessments added. Companion deck: Antibiotics_Spectrum_Optimized.pptx (original interactive antibiogram board, surgically corrected).
objectives: Identify the spectrum of coverage for the most commonly used inpatient antibiotics, class by class.
Use the "4 questions" framework (GPC including MRSA/Enterococcus; GNR including Pseudomonas/ESBL; atypicals; anaerobes including B. fragilis) to reason about any antibiotic.
Optional: Recognize the agents that specifically cover MRSA and how to choose among them.

prework.q: Q1. What single difference separates ampicillin-sulbactam (Unasyn) from piperacillin-tazobactam (Zosyn) in spectrum?
prework.a: Piperacillin-tazobactam adds Pseudomonas coverage. Both cover GPCs, many enteric GNRs, and gut anaerobes; pip-tazo is the broadest penicillin and the antipseudomonal one.

prework.q: Q2. Which carbapenem does NOT cover Pseudomonas, and what's its niche?
prework.a: Ertapenem — no Pseudomonas coverage, but once-daily dosing makes it a convenient first-line agent for ESBL E. coli infections. Meropenem/imipenem/doripenem add Pseudomonas.

prework.q: Q3. Why should daptomycin never be used for MRSA pneumonia?
prework.a: It is inactivated by pulmonary surfactant, so it doesn't work in the lung. Use vancomycin or linezolid (often preferred) for MRSA pneumonia instead.

segments.label: Hook (2 min)
segments.html: "Your patient needs antibiotics. Before you reach for Zosyn-and-vanc reflexively — what four yes/no questions should decide the drug?" Build the framework, then hang every class on it. The whole talk is reading one antibiogram, column by column.

segments.label: The 4 questions
segments.html:
ASK: "What four coverage questions do you ask of any antibiotic?" Reveal the spine of the antibiogram:
  1. Gram positives (Staph, Strep)? → and does it cover MRSA? Enterococcus?
  2. Gram negatives (enteric GNRs)? → and Pseudomonas? ESBLs?
  3. Atypicals (Mycoplasma, Chlamydophila, Legionella)?
  4. Anaerobes? → and specifically B. fragilis (gut anaerobes)?

segments.label: Penicillins
segments.html:
General rules: no MRSA, no atypicals; GN coverage increases across the classes.

Natural penicillin (Pen G/VK) — 1st-line for Strep (GAS, susceptible S. pneumoniae), syphilis, many oral anaerobes; high Staph resistance. Antistaphylococcal (nafcillin, dicloxacillin, oxacillin) — 1st-line MSSA. Aminopenicillin + beta-lactamase inhibitor (amp-sulbactam, amox-clav) — adds Staph, many enteric GNRs (E. coli, H. flu, Moraxella), gut anaerobes; 1st-line for E. faecalis. Pip-tazo — broadest, adds Pseudomonas.

Mnemonic: E. faecaLESS is LESS resistant (~90% ampicillin-sensitive, rarely VRE); E. faecium is more resistant (~10% ampicillin-sensitive, more often VRE).

segments.label: Cephalosporins
segments.html:
General rules: no Enterococcus, no atypicals; GN coverage rises by generation (except the special 5th).

1st gen (cefazolin/cephalexin) — good GP, MSSA, minimal GN. 2nd gen (cefuroxime, cefotetan, cefoxitin) — more GN; cephamycins (cefotetan, cefoxitin) add some anaerobes. 3rd gen — ceftriaxone/cefotaxime/cefpodoxime (Strep + GNR, no anaerobes) vs ceftazidime (adds Pseudomonas, no Staph, poor CNS — not for meningitis). 4th gen cefepime — Pseudomonas + AmpC. Ceftaroline ("5th gen") — the one cephalosporin with MRSA activity.

Pearl: No oral cephalosporin covers Pseudomonas.

segments.label: Carbapenems
segments.html: Broadest class (GP, GN, anaerobe), most reliable ESBL coverage; no MRSA/atypical, poor Enterococcus. Ertapenem — once-daily, no Pseudomonas, first-line for ESBL E. coli. Meropenem/imipenem/doripenem — add Pseudomonas.

segments.label: Fluoroquinolones
segments.html:
Agent"Type"Key spectrum
CiprofloxacinUrinaryGN/PsA; poor Strep → not for respiratory infections; 2nd anti-PsA / atypical
LevofloxacinUrinary + respiratoryAdds GP (MSSA, S. pneumoniae); good for low-risk respiratory/GU
MoxifloxacinRespiratoryBest anaerobic coverage (GI/aspiration); weak PsA, poor renal penetration
Class harms: QT prolongation, aortic aneurysm/dissection, tendon rupture, encephalopathy, C. difficile — reserve FQs.

segments.label: Anti-pseudomonal (non-beta-lactam) & macrolides
segments.html: Aminoglycosides (gentamicin, tobramycin, amikacin, plazomicin) — aerobic GNR + PsA, no anaerobe/atypical; oto-/nephrotoxic; gentamicin adds GP synergy for enterococcal endocarditis. Aztreonam — monobactam, aerobic GN + PsA only; safe in penicillin allergy except ceftazidime (shared side chain). Macrolides (azithromycin) — atypicals; rising Strep resistance; fidaxomicin for C. diff.

segments.label: Anti-MRSA agents
segments.html:
ASK: "Community vs hospital MRSA — name the agents for each." Reveal:
SettingAgents & notes
CA-MRSA (PO)Clindamycin (antitoxin; C. diff risk; B. fragilis resistant); TMP-SMX (S. aureus/GN; variable Strep — add a beta-lactam for nonpurulent cellulitis); doxycycline (Staph/atypical; minimal Strep)
HA-MRSA (IV)Vancomycin (1st-line; also Enterococcus/CoNS); daptomycin (not for pneumonia — surfactant inactivation; rhabdo); linezolid (PO/IV; 1st-line VRE; often preferred for MRSA pneumonia; myelosuppression, serotonin syndrome)

segments.label: Retrieval close (3 min)
segments.html: Erase the grid. Call out an organism (MRSA, Pseudomonas, ESBL E. coli, B. fragilis, Enterococcus faecium) and have learners name two agents that cover it and one common drug that does not. Then run the take-home escalation ladders from memory.

mcqs.stem: A patient with a complicated intra-abdominal infection needs empiric coverage including Pseudomonas and gut anaerobes with a single agent. Which is most appropriate?
mcqs.exp: C. Piperacillin-tazobactam covers GPCs, enteric GNRs, gut anaerobes, and Pseudomonas in one agent. Ceftriaxone (A) lacks anaerobic and Pseudomonas coverage. Ertapenem (B) covers anaerobes but not Pseudomonas. Ampicillin-sulbactam (D) covers anaerobes but not Pseudomonas (and has rising E. coli resistance).

mcqs.stem: A patient with MRSA pneumonia is started on daptomycin. The pharmacist pages you to change it. Why?
opts: Daptomycin is inactivated by pulmonary surfactant
mcqs.exp: B. Daptomycin is inactivated by surfactant and is ineffective in the lung — never use it for pneumonia. It does cover MRSA elsewhere (A wrong). Its characteristic toxicity is rhabdomyolysis, not nephrotoxicity (C). It is IV (D). Use vancomycin or linezolid (often preferred) for MRSA pneumonia.

mcqs.stem: A urine culture grows ESBL E. coli in a stable patient without Pseudomonas risk. Which is the most appropriate first-line agent?
mcqs.exp: C. Carbapenems are the most reliable ESBL coverage; ertapenem (once-daily, no Pseudomonas needed here) is a first-line choice for ESBL E. coli. ESBLs are by definition resistant to 3rd-gen cephalosporins like ceftriaxone (A). Pip-tazo (B) and cefepime (D) are less reliable against ESBLs and generally avoided for serious ESBL infection.

mcqs.stem: A patient with severe penicillin anaphylaxis needs Gram-negative/Pseudomonas coverage. Which agent is safest, and what is the one cross-reactivity caveat?
opts: Aztreonam; avoid if a ceftazidime allergy due to a shared side chain
mcqs.exp: B. Aztreonam (a monobactam) covers aerobic GNs and Pseudomonas with minimal beta-lactam cross-reactivity — except ceftazidime, which shares an identical side chain. Cefepime (A) and ampicillin-sulbactam (D) are beta-lactams to avoid in true anaphylaxis; carbapenems (C) carry a low but real cross-reactivity risk.

mcqs.stem: Which fluoroquinolone is the best choice when reliable anaerobic coverage (e.g., aspiration) is desired, and which should NOT be used for a respiratory infection?
opts: Moxifloxacin best for anaerobes; ciprofloxacin not for respiratory infections
Ciprofloxacin best for anaerobes; levofloxacin not for respiratory
Levofloxacin best for anaerobes; moxifloxacin not for respiratory
mcqs.exp: A. Moxifloxacin has the broadest anaerobic coverage of the FQs (useful for GI/aspiration) — but weak Pseudomonas and poor renal penetration. Ciprofloxacin has poor Strep coverage, so it is not used empirically for respiratory infections. Levofloxacin and moxifloxacin are the "respiratory" FQs; ciprofloxacin is "urinary."

anki: The 4 questions to ask of any antibiotic?
(1) GPCs (Staph/Strep; +MRSA? +Enterococcus?); (2) GNRs (+Pseudomonas? +ESBL?); (3) atypicals; (4) anaerobes (+B. fragilis/gut?).
General rules for penicillins?
No MRSA, no atypicals; GN coverage increases across classes (Pen -> antistaph -> amino+BLI -> pip-tazo).
One difference between amp-sulbactam and pip-tazo?
Pip-tazo adds Pseudomonas. Both cover GPCs, enteric GNRs, and gut anaerobes.
First-line for MSSA?
Antistaphylococcal penicillins (nafcillin, oxacillin, dicloxacillin) or cefazolin (1st-gen cephalosporin).
E. faecalis vs E. faecium ampicillin/VRE mnemonic?
E. faecaLESS = LESS resistant (~90% amp-sensitive, rarely VRE). E. faecium = more resistant (~10% amp-sensitive, more often VRE).
General rules for cephalosporins?
No Enterococcus, no atypicals; GN coverage increases by generation (except the special 5th).
Which cephalosporins cover Pseudomonas? Any oral?
Ceftazidime (3rd) and cefepime (4th). No oral cephalosporin covers Pseudomonas.
Which cephalosporin covers MRSA?
Ceftaroline ("5th generation") -- the only beta-lactam with MRSA activity.
Carbapenem general rules?
Broadest (GP/GN/anaerobe), most reliable ESBL; no MRSA/atypical, poor Enterococcus.
Which carbapenem lacks Pseudomonas coverage, and its niche?
Ertapenem -- once-daily, first-line for ESBL E. coli. Meropenem/imipenem/doripenem add Pseudomonas.
Cipro vs levo vs moxifloxacin?
Cipro = urinary (GN/PsA, poor Strep, not for respiratory). Levo = urinary+respiratory (adds S. pneumo). Moxi = respiratory, best anaerobe, weak PsA/poor renal.
Fluoroquinolone class harms?
QT prolongation, aortic aneurysm/dissection, tendon rupture, encephalopathy, C. difficile.
Aztreonam spectrum and the one allergy caveat?
Aerobic GN + Pseudomonas only (no GP/anaerobe/atypical). Minimal beta-lactam cross-reactivity except ceftazidime (shared side chain).
Aminoglycoside coverage and toxicities?
Aerobic GNR + Pseudomonas; no anaerobe/atypical. Oto- and nephrotoxic. Gentamicin adds GP synergy for enterococcal endocarditis.
Oral CA-MRSA options and a key caveat for each?
Clindamycin (antitoxin; C. diff; B. fragilis resistant), TMP-SMX (variable Strep -- add beta-lactam for nonpurulent cellulitis), doxycycline (minimal Strep).
IV HA-MRSA agents?
Vancomycin (1st-line; also Enterococcus/CoNS), daptomycin (NOT for pneumonia; rhabdo), linezolid (PO/IV; 1st-line VRE; often preferred for MRSA pneumonia).
Why not daptomycin for MRSA pneumonia?
Inactivated by pulmonary surfactant.
Newer MDR Gram-negative agents (escalate with ID)?
Cefiderocol (CRE/CRAB/Stenotrophomonas/PsA); ceftazidime-avibactam, ceftolozane-tazobactam, meropenem-vaborbactam, imipenem-relebactam; aztreonam-avibactam for metallo-beta-lactamases.
lessons.dot:
#Abx spectrum reasoning: target * [GPC / GNR / atypical / anaerobe], +MRSA? * , +Enterococcus? * , +PsA? * , +ESBL? * , +gut anaerobe (B.frag)? * . PCN allergy * [none / rash / anaphylaxis]. renal/wt dose-adjust * .
- GPC ladder (MRSA/Enterococcus): MSSA/Strep [nafcillin/cefazolin] -> +MRSA [vancomycin] -> [daptomycin (NOT lung)] -> [linezolid (PO/IV, VRE, MRSA-PNA)] -> [ceftaroline (only BL w/ MRSA)]. E.faecalis [ampicillin]; E.faecium/VRE [linezolid/daptomycin].
- GNR ladder (PsA/ESBL): [ceftriaxone] -> +PsA [ceftazidime/cefepime/pip-tazo] -> ESBL [ERTAPENEM (no PsA) / meropenem (+PsA)] -> CRE/MDR ID-only [ceftazidime-avibactam, meropenem-vaborbactam, cefiderocol]. severe PCN allergy -> [aztreonam (avoid if ceftazidime allergy) / aminoglycoside].
- atypicals: [azithromycin / doxycycline / respiratory FQ (levo/moxi)]. cipro = poor Strep, NOT respiratory.
- anaerobes: gut/B.frag [metronidazole / pip-tazo / carbapenem / cefotetan-cefoxitin / moxifloxacin]. oral [pen G / clindamycin / amox-clav].
- key traps: ertapenem NO PsA; no oral cephalosporin covers PsA; daptomycin NOT for pneumonia; ceftazidime poor CNS (not meningitis); cipro not for respiratory; carbapenems poor Enterococcus; aztreonam cross-reacts w/ ceftazidime.
- de-escalate to culture/sensitivities ASAP; consult ID for advanced agents. confirm vs local antibiogram.

extras.label: Antibiogram Quick Grid summary of the board
extras.html:
Class / agentMRSAEnteroc.PsAESBLAtypicalAnaerobe
Nafcillin / cefazolin——————
Amp-sulbactam / amox-clav—+ (faecalis)———+ (gut)
Pip-tazo—+ (faecalis)+——+ (gut)
Ceftriaxone (3rd gen)——————
Ceftazidime / cefepime——+———
Ceftaroline (5th gen)+—————
Ertapenem———+—+
Meropenem—poor++—+
Ciprofloxacin——+—+—
Levofloxacin—some+—+some oral
Moxifloxacin——weak—++ (best FQ)
Aztreonam / aminoglycosides——+±——
Vancomycin / daptomycin / linezolid++ (linezolid=VRE)————
Clindamycin / TMP-SMX / doxycycline+ (CA)doxy: some——doxy: +clinda: oral

Simplified teaching grid; "+" = generally covers, "—" = generally does not. Always confirm against local susceptibilities.
