lessons.title: C. diff Infection
lessons.subtitle: Optimized lesson package · Facilitator-led interactive chalk talk for IM residents · 15 min (25–30 min with cases)
lessons.credit: Adapted from TeachIM.org — C. diff Infection (Zhang; Sept 2021), CC BY-NC 4.0. Optimized June 2026: guideline currency verified, three deck typos corrected, bezlotoxumab-discontinuation & microbiota-therapy updates added, pedagogy restructured, assessments added. Companion deck: C_diff_Infection_Optimized.pptx (original interactive board, surgically corrected).
objectives: Identify risk factors for C. diff infection (CDI), including community-acquired CDI.
Diagnose CDI and recognize the value and limitations of each stool test.
Differentiate non-severe, severe, fulminant, and recurrent CDI.
Manage non-severe, severe, fulminant, and recurrent CDI.

prework.q: 1. Why shouldn’t you test an asymptomatic patient for C. diff?
prework.a: Colonization is common (4–15% of healthy adults, ~21% of hospitalized adults, 15–30% of LTC residents). A positive test in an asymptomatic patient reflects colonization, not infection — test only patients with ≥3 unformed stools/24 h (or ileus with clinical concern).

prework.q: 2. What two lab values define SEVERE CDI?
prework.a: WBC >15,000 OR creatinine ≥1.5 mg/dL. Fulminant adds hypotension/shock or ileus/megacolon.

prework.q: 3. What is the preferred first-line antibiotic for an initial episode of CDI in current guidelines?
prework.a: Fidaxomicin (preferred over vancomycin per the 2021 IDSA/SHEA focused update). PO vancomycin remains an acceptable first-line alternative. Metronidazole is reserved for low-risk non-severe disease when neither is available.

segments.label: Hook (0:00–0:02)
segments.html:
"Your hospitalized patient has a positive C. diff PCR. Treat or not? It depends entirely on one thing the lab can’t tell you — are they actually having diarrhea? Up to a fifth of inpatients are colonized." The talk builds the discipline of testing the right patient, classifying severity, and picking the right drug.

ASK — "Who should we test for C. diff, and who should we NOT?" Anchor on symptomatic-only testing.

segments.label: Objective 1 — Epidemiology & risk factors (0:02–0:05)
segments.html:
Distinguish colonization (detection without symptoms — common) from infection. Community-acquired CDI is rising (~40% of cases).

Classic risk factorsNotable for community-acquired CDI
Age >65, healthcare exposure, antibiotic useIBD, cardiac disease, CKD — and younger patients without recent hospitalization
Pearl. A positive test never makes the diagnosis by itself — symptoms do. Sending C. diff on formed stool or in an asymptomatic patient generates false "infections" and unnecessary treatment.

segments.label: Objective 2 — Diagnosis (0:05–0:09)
segments.html:
Test when: new diarrhea (≥3 unformed stools/24 h) OR ileus (a rectal swab works in ileus). Two-step algorithm:

TestDetectsPerformance
GDH antigenEnzyme made by ALL C. diff strains (toxigenic or not)High sensitivity (94–96%); can’t distinguish toxin vs non-toxin strains
Toxin A/B (EIA/PCR)Toxins from toxigenic strainsHigh specificity (94–98%); false negatives with poor specimen handling
C. diff NAATGene encoding toxinConfirms a toxigenic strain; can’t distinguish colonization from infection — use to resolve discordant GDH/toxin results

Algorithm: GDH + / toxin + → CDI. GDH − / toxin − → rule out. Discordant/indeterminate → confirm with NAAT.

Pearl. Do NOT repeat stool tests within 7 days, and do NOT test for cure — shedding persists in ~56% after symptoms resolve, and post-infectious IBS occurs in up to 25%.

segments.label: Objective 3 — Severity classification (0:09–0:11)
segments.html:
ASK — "What separates severe from fulminant CDI?"
ClassDefinition
Non-severeAbsence of severe criteria
SevereWBC >15,000 OR Cr ≥1.5 mg/dL
FulminantSevere PLUS hypotension/shock OR ileus/megacolon
RecurrentRecurrent diarrhea + confirmatory test within 8 weeks of the initial episode (~20% of patients)

segments.label: Objective 4 — Treatment (0:11–0:15)
segments.html:
ScenarioTreatment
Non-severe / severe (initial)Fidaxomicin 200 mg BID ×10 d (preferred) or PO vancomycin 125 mg q6h ×10 d. PO metronidazole only if neither available (low-risk non-severe).
FulminantPO vancomycin 500 mg q6h (PR if ileus) + IV metronidazole 500 mg q8h; surgical consult if no improvement; FMT if not a surgical candidate.
1st recurrenceFidaxomicin (preferred) or tapered/pulsed PO vancomycin, or standard PO vancomycin.
≥2 recurrencesTapered/pulsed vancomycin, vancomycin→rifaximin, or FMT / microbiota-based therapy (see update). Refer.
ASK — "Once you’ve treated, how do you know they’re cured?" (Trick: you don’t re-test — judge clinically.)

Prevention: contact + enteric precautions until 48 h after diarrhea resolves; consider low-dose PO vancomycin 125 mg daily prophylaxis in high-risk patients on systemic antibiotics. Probiotics and stopping PPIs are NOT recommended as preventive measures.

segments.label: Retrieval close (0:15)
segments.html: Board off: "Rebuild who to test, the two-step algorithm, the severe/fulminant definitions, and the preferred first-line drug." Fill gaps from what they reconstruct.

cases.title: Case 1 — From acute to severe to recurrent CDI
cases.stem: 33-year-old with fever, abdominal pain, and 4–5 watery BMs/day for 4 days, recently treated for a UTI with Bactrim. VS: T 37.4 °C, HR 110, BP 100/50s. Na 128, BUN 18, Cr 1.7, WBC 18. Stool: GDH Ag +, toxin PCR − (culture pending).
cases.reveal: Likely CDI, but GDH+/toxin− is discordant/indeterminate → send C. diff NAAT to confirm or exclude. (NAAT returns positive.)

cases.title: Case 2 — Fulminant CDI
cases.stem: 82-year-old with recurrent UTIs admitted with fever, abdominal pain, nausea/vomiting, and constipation/obstipation ×2 d. VS: T 38.4 °C, HR 110, BP 80/50s. Na 128, BUN 18, Cr 1.7, WBC 18. KUB shows ileus.
cases.reveal: Use a rectal swab (the patient has ileus). This is fulminant CDI — severe criteria plus hypotension and ileus.

cases.title: Case 3 — Recurrence and prevention
cases.stem: 33-year-old recently treated for severe CDI with 10 days of PO vancomycin returns 5 weeks later with recurrent watery diarrhea, no interval antibiotics. C. diff testing positive; afebrile, stable vitals, normal CBC/BMP.
cases.reveal: Recurrent (non-severe) CDI. Treat with fidaxomicin (preferred) or tapered/pulsed PO vancomycin.

mcqs.stem: A 70-year-old inpatient on day 4 of ceftriaxone has one formed stool and a routine C. diff PCR sent by the night team returns positive. He has no diarrhea. What is the best next step?
opts: No CDI treatment — this likely reflects colonization
Send C. diff NAAT to confirm
mcqs.exp: C. Without diarrhea, a positive test reflects colonization, not infection — don’t treat, and don’t have sent the test in the first place. A/B/E wrong: treating colonization is inappropriate. D wrong: more testing won’t change that there are no symptoms to treat.

mcqs.stem: A 60-year-old with CDI has WBC 19,000 and Cr 1.8 but normal blood pressure and no ileus. How is this classified and treated?
opts: Fulminant — PO + PR vancomycin and IV metronidazole
mcqs.exp: B. WBC >15k or Cr ≥1.5 = severe; without hypotension/shock or ileus/megacolon it is not fulminant. Treat with fidaxomicin (preferred) or PO vancomycin. A wrong: not non-severe, and metronidazole isn’t first-line. C wrong: no fulminant features. D wrong: IV vancomycin doesn’t reach the colonic lumen — useless for CDI. E wrong: this is a first episode.

mcqs.stem: A patient with fulminant CDI has an ileus. Which regimen is most appropriate?
opts: PO vancomycin 500 mg q6h + PR vancomycin 500 mg q6h + IV metronidazole
mcqs.exp: C. Fulminant CDI with ileus needs high-dose PO vancomycin (500 mg q6h) PLUS PR vancomycin (drug may not reach the colon orally with ileus) PLUS IV metronidazole; consult surgery. A wrong: IV vancomycin doesn’t enter the gut lumen. B/D/E wrong: inadequate for fulminant disease.

mcqs.stem: A patient finishes treatment for CDI and feels well, but is about to start an unrelated course of antibiotics. Which is most appropriate for preventing recurrence?
opts: Repeat C. diff testing to confirm cure first
Consider PO vancomycin 125 mg daily prophylaxis while on the antibiotics
Give a single dose of bezlotoxumab
mcqs.exp: D. Low-dose vancomycin prophylaxis during systemic antibiotics is reasonable for high-risk patients. A wrong: never test for cure (shedding persists). B/C wrong: probiotics and PPI discontinuation are not recommended preventive measures. E wrong: bezlotoxumab was discontinued (Jan 2025) and is unavailable.

mcqs.stem: A patient has had three episodes of recurrent CDI despite appropriate antibiotics. Which durable option is now FDA-approved for preventing further recurrence?
opts: An oral fecal microbiota spore product (Vowst) or rectal fecal microbiota (Rebyota)
mcqs.exp: B. Vowst (oral, 2023) and Rebyota (rectal, 2022) are FDA-approved live biotherapeutics for preventing recurrent CDI after an antibiotic course; conventional FMT is also an option. A wrong: bezlotoxumab is discontinued. C/E wrong: chronic metronidazole or rifaximin monotherapy is not a standard durable prevention strategy. D wrong: IVIG is not standard.

anki: Colonization vs infection — who do you test?
Only symptomatic patients (≥3 unformed stools/24 h or ileus). Colonization is common.
C. diff colonization rates?
4–15% healthy adults; ~21% hospitalized; 15–30% LTC residents.
Risk factors for CDI?
Age >65, healthcare exposure, antibiotics; also IBD, cardiac disease, CKD (community-acquired).
Two-step diagnostic algorithm for CDI?
GDH antigen + toxin A/B; if discordant/indeterminate, confirm with C. diff NAAT.
What does GDH antigen detect?
An enzyme from ALL C. diff strains (high sensitivity); can't distinguish toxigenic strains.
What does the toxin assay add?
High specificity for toxigenic strains; false negatives with poor specimen handling.
Severe CDI criteria?
WBC >15,000 OR Cr ≥1.5 mg/dL.
Fulminant CDI?
Severe PLUS hypotension/shock OR ileus/megacolon.
Recurrent CDI definition?
Recurrent diarrhea + positive test within 8 weeks of the initial episode (~20%).
Preferred first-line for non-severe/severe CDI?
Fidaxomicin 200 mg BID ×10 d; PO vancomycin 125 mg q6h ×10 d acceptable alternative.
Role of metronidazole in CDI?
PO metronidazole only for low-risk non-severe CDI when fidaxomicin/vancomycin unavailable.
Fulminant CDI treatment?
PO vancomycin 500 mg q6h (PR if ileus) + IV metronidazole 500 mg q8h; surgery consult.
First-recurrence CDI treatment?
Fidaxomicin (preferred) or tapered/pulsed PO vancomycin.
Durable prevention of recurrent CDI (2022–23)?
Rebyota (rectal, live-jslm) or Vowst (oral spores, live-brpk); FMT by referral.
Bezlotoxumab status?
Discontinued by Merck effective Jan 31, 2025 — no longer available.
Do you test for cure in CDI?
No — shedding persists (~56%); post-infectious IBS up to 25%.
Probiotics or stopping PPIs for CDI prevention?
Not recommended.
lessons.dot:
#C. diff infection (CDI): *[non-severe/severe/fulminant/recurrent]
 test ONLY if symptomatic: ≥3 unformed stools/24h OR ileus (rectal swab ok)
 dx: GDH Ag *[+/-] + toxin A/B *[+/-]; if discordant/indeterminate → C. diff NAAT *
  -- do NOT repeat test <7d; do NOT test for cure (shedding ~56%)
 severity: WBC *>15k? / Cr *≥1.5? → severe if either; FULMINANT if + hypotension/shock or ileus/megacolon
 recurrence: sx + confirmatory test within 8 wk of prior episode
RF: age>65, healthcare exposure, abx; community-acq: IBD, cardiac dz, CKD
TREATMENT:
 non-severe/severe (initial): fidaxomicin 200 mg BID x10d [PREFERRED] | PO vanc 125 mg q6h x10d
   -- PO metronidazole only if low-risk non-severe & neither available
 fulminant: PO vanc 500 mg q6h (NGT ok) + IV metronidazole 500 mg q8h + PR vanc 500 mg q6h if ileus; SURGERY consult; FMT if non-surgical
 1st recurrence: fidaxomicin [pref] | tapered/pulsed PO vanc | std PO vanc
 ≥2 recurrences: tapered/pulsed vanc | vanc→rifaximin | FMT/microbiota therapy [REFER]
 recurrence PREVENTION (post-course): Rebyota (rectal) or Vowst (oral) live biotherapeutic; bezlotoxumab DISCONTINUED (Jan 2025 - unavailable)
PREVENTION/precautions: contact + enteric precautions until 48h after diarrhea resolves
 -- high-risk on systemic abx: consider PO vanc 125 mg daily ppx (cont x5d after abx)
 -- probiotics NOT rec; do NOT stop PPI for prevention
takehome: Colonization is common — test ONLY symptomatic patients, and never test for cure.
Diagnose with a two-step algorithm: GDH + toxin A/B; confirm discordant results with NAAT.
Severe = WBC >15k or Cr ≥1.5; fulminant = severe + hypotension/shock or ileus/megacolon; recurrence = symptoms + positive test within 8 weeks.
Fidaxomicin is preferred first-line for non-severe, severe, and first-recurrence CDI; PO vancomycin is an acceptable alternative. Fulminant: high-dose PO ± PR vancomycin + IV metronidazole ± surgery.
For durable recurrence prevention, use microbiota-based therapy (Rebyota, Vowst, or FMT). Bezlotoxumab is no longer available.

extras.label: 2023–2025 Treatment Update
extras.html:
The standing teaching (fidaxomicin-preferred, severity definitions, two-step testing) is current. Two material changes since publication are surfaced here and on the appended deck slide:

  • Bezlotoxumab discontinued. Merck discontinued bezlotoxumab (Zinplava) effective January 31, 2025 — it is no longer commercially available in the US. Where the deck lists "± bezlotoxumab" for recurrence prevention, treat that option as unavailable.
  • FDA-approved live biotherapeutics for preventing recurrent CDI (given after the CDI antibiotic course completes) now standardize what the deck calls "FMT": Rebyota (fecal microbiota, live-jslm; rectal; approved Nov 2022) and Vowst (fecal microbiota spores, live-brpk; oral; approved Apr 2023). Conventional FMT remains an option, usually by referral.
  • Unchanged: fidaxomicin preferred (2021 IDSA/SHEA; 2021 ACG); severity thresholds; symptomatic-only testing; no test-of-cure.

1. Johnson S, Lavergne V, Skinner AM, et al. Clinical Practice Guideline by IDSA and SHEA: 2021 Focused Update Guidelines on Management of Clostridioides difficile Infection in Adults. Clin Infect Dis. 2021;73(5):e1029–e1044.
  2. Kelly CR, Fischer M, Allegretti JR, et al. ACG Clinical Guidelines: Prevention, Diagnosis, and Treatment of Clostridioides difficile Infections. Am J Gastroenterol. 2021;116(6):1124–1147.
  3. McDonald LC, Gerding DN, Johnson S, et al. Clinical Practice Guidelines for C. difficile Infection in Adults and Children: 2017 Update (IDSA/SHEA). Clin Infect Dis. 2018;66(7):e1–e48.
  4. US FDA. Approval of REBYOTA (fecal microbiota, live-jslm) for prevention of recurrent CDI. Nov 2022. (newly cited)
  5. US FDA. Approval of VOWST (fecal microbiota spores, live-brpk), oral, for prevention of recurrent CDI. Apr 2023. (newly cited)
  6. Merck/FDA Drug Shortages. Bezlotoxumab (Zinplava) discontinuation, effective Jan 31, 2025. (newly cited)

lessons.title: COPD — Outpatient Management
lessons.subtitle: Optimized lesson package · Facilitator-led interactive chalk talk for IM residents · 20–25 min (talk) / +10–15 min (cases)
lessons.credit: Adapted from TeachIM.org — COPD – Outpatient Management (McLeod, Guinn, Brett, Fainstad; Section Ed. Steinbach; Univ. of Colorado; published Sept 2021, last reviewed May 2023), CC BY-NC 4.0. Optimized June 2026: currency verified against GOLD 2024/2025; no projected-text deck errors found (append-only edit); pedagogy restructured, assessments added. Companion deck: COPD_Outpatient_Optimized.pptx (original interactive board, all animations preserved). Educational use only — clinician-reviewed before teaching.
objectives: Define the diagnostic criteria for COPD (and the role of spirometry).
Classify patients using the 2023 GOLD ABE groups.
Choose initial and step-up inhaler therapy by GOLD group.
Categorize the benefits of non-pharmacologic therapies.
Apply an evidence-based approach to identifying and treating outpatient COPD exacerbations.

prework.q: Q1. What single test result is required to diagnose COPD, and what's the cutoff?
prework.a: Post-bronchodilator spirometry with FEV1/FVC <0.70 in a symptomatic patient with risk factors. You cannot diagnose COPD without PFTs. Supportive findings: scooped flow-volume loop, ↑TLC/RV, ↓DLCO.

prework.q: Q2. In the 2023 GOLD ABE scheme, which patients get a LABA+LAMA first-line, and when do you add an ICS?
prework.a: LABA+LAMA is first-line for Groups B and E. Add ICS (triple therapy) mainly for exacerbators with blood eosinophils ≥100; start with triple in Group E if eos ≥300. De-escalate ICS if recurrent pneumonia.

prework.q: Q3. Which COPD interventions actually reduce mortality?
prework.a: Smoking cessation has the clearest mortality benefit; long-term oxygen in resting hypoxemia and vaccinations also help. Pulmonary rehab improves symptoms/exacerbations; lung-cancer screening reduces cancer mortality in eligible smokers.

segments.label: Hook (2 min)
segments.html: "Half the inhalers we prescribe are the wrong class for the patient in front of us — and we treat COPD exacerbations like pneumonia when most don't need it." Ask the room: name the one COPD intervention that reduces mortality. Park the answers — you'll confirm smoking cessation at Objective 4.

segments.label: Objective 1 — Diagnose
segments.html:
ASK: "Give me three criteria for diagnosing COPD." Collect, then click through risk factors → symptoms → spirometry.

Risk factors: tobacco (and pipe/cigar/water-pipe/THC), other smoke/vapor/dust and occupational exposures, and host factors (genetics, prematurity, childhood respiratory infections). Symptoms: chronic progressive dyspnea, cough, sputum, recurrent LRTI. Diagnosis requires post-bronchodilator FEV1/FVC <0.70 on spirometry — mandatory.

Pearl — Supportive PFTs: scooped (concave) flow-volume loop, ↑TLC and ↑RV (air trapping), ↓DLCO (emphysema; values <60% track with more symptoms and mortality). Post-bronchodilator FEV1 grades severity. New labels: pre-COPD (symptoms/structural lesions without obstruction) and PRISm (Preserved Ratio Impaired Spirometry) — both at risk of progressing.

Initial workup adds exposure history, ambulatory pulse-ox/ABG (ABG if SpO2 <92%), and alpha-1 antitrypsin once in every COPD patient (especially if <45 or panlobular basal emphysema). CXR excludes alternatives; CT for severe FEV1 reduction, symptoms out of proportion, or lung-cancer screening.

segments.label: Objective 2 — Classify (2023 GOLD ABE)
segments.html:
ASK: "How would you sort COPD patients to decide therapy?" Reveal mMRC and CAT, then the groups.
GroupExacerbations (past year)Symptoms
A0–1 (no hospitalization)Low — mMRC 0–1 / CAT <10
B0–1 (no hospitalization)High — mMRC ≥2 / CAT ≥10
E≥2 moderate OR ≥1 requiring hospitalization(symptoms not used to split E)
Pearl — The 2023 ABE scheme replaced the old A/B/C/D grid. The key fork is exacerbation-prone or not, because that drives ICS decisions.

segments.label: Objective 3 — Inhaler therapy by group
segments.html:
ASK: "First-line inhaler for a symptomatic Group B patient?" Take answers before revealing.

Group A: a single long-acting bronchodilator (LABA or LAMA), not a short-acting agent, except with very rare symptoms. Groups B and E: LABA + LAMA first-line. Add ICS (→ triple) for ongoing exacerbations with eos ≥100; start with triple in Group E if eos ≥300. Escalation options: azithromycin (esp. ex-smokers), roflumilast (FEV1 <50% + chronic bronchitis); advanced: LVRS, endobronchial valves, transplant.

AgentBest forCaveat
LABASymptom reliefLess effect on exacerbations
LAMASymptoms AND exacerbations (LAMA > LABA for exacerbations)—
ICSFewer exacerbations if eos elevated↑ pneumonia — de-escalate if recurrent PNA
Pearl — LABA-ICS combinations, though common, have no clear place in the GOLD schema. Whatever you pick, inhaler efficacy depends on technique and adherence — teach it every visit.

segments.label: Objective 4 — Non-pharmacologic efficacy
segments.html:
ASK: "Which of these prevents death: smoking cessation, O2, pulm rehab, vaccines, lung-cancer screening?"

Smoking cessation is the standout — benefits across all COPD domains and the clearest mortality signal. Supplemental O2 for resting hypoxemia (PaO2 ≤55, or ≤59 + cor pulmonale/RHF) reduces symptoms and slows progression. Pulmonary rehab improves symptoms and exacerbation rate. Lung-cancer screening reduces mortality in eligible smokers. Vaccines (influenza, pneumococcal, COVID-19) reduce exacerbations and serious illness.

segments.label: Objective 5 — Exacerbations
segments.html:
ASK: "Name the 3 cardinal symptoms of a COPD exacerbation." Reveal, then walk the outpatient management tree.

Cardinal symptoms: ↑ dyspnea, ↑ sputum volume, ↑ sputum purulence. First rule out mimics (PE, pneumonia, heart failure, ACS) — consider CXR, D-dimer, BNP, ECG/troponin, flu swab. Outpatient management:

InterventionWhen / how
BronchodilatorsSABA ± SAMA; MDIs as effective as nebulizers if used correctly
SteroidsPrednisone 40 mg ×5 days (REDUCE) — reduces relapse/symptoms
Antibiotics×5 days if ≥2 cardinal symptoms (incl. sputum purulence) or needs ventilation; agent per local resistance (e.g., azithromycin/doxycycline; anti-pseudomonal if high-risk)
NIPPVpH <7.35 with PaCO2 >45 (hospital)
Pearl — Hospitalize for severe exacerbation (resting dyspnea, rising O2 need, confusion), severe comorbidities, or inadequate home support.

segments.label: Retrieval close (2 min)
segments.html: Cover the board. Have learners rebuild: (1) the diagnostic spirometry cutoff, (2) the ABE groups, (3) the LABA+LAMA → +ICS logic, (4) the exacerbation triad and the steroid/antibiotic rules.

cases.title: Case 1 — 60-year-old, 60 pack-years, progressive dyspnea
cases.stem: 60yo, 60 pack-year smoker, progressive dyspnea and cough, breathless walking 100 ft on level ground, no prior hospitalizations.
cases.reveal: Spirometry (pre/post bronchodilator), CBC (eosinophils), CXR; rule out alternative diagnoses.

cases.title: Case 2 — 60-year-old former smoker, acute exacerbation
cases.stem: 60yo former smoker, COPD (FEV1 60%), 1 week of ↑ dyspnea and productive cough limiting strenuous (not daily) activity; 1 prior exacerbation; no Pseudomonas history. Meds: Combivent PRN + salmeterol. SpO2 90%, RR 20, diffuse wheeze.
cases.reveal: Outpatient — he can still perform ADLs and has no severe features.

cases.title: Case 3 — 80-year-old, severe COPD on home O2
cases.stem: 80yo former smoker, COPD (FEV1 35%) on 2 L home O2, gradually worsening dyspnea affecting some ADLs, no exacerbations in the past year; no Pseudomonas. Meds: LABA-LAMA + PRN SABA.
cases.reveal: Observe inhaler technique, ensure O2 adherence, consider pulmonary rehab, and check eosinophils (consider ICS if eos >100 with frequent exacerbations); refer to pulmonology for advanced therapies (LVRS, endobronchial valves).

mcqs.stem: A 58-year-old with a 40 pack-year history reports chronic cough and exertional dyspnea. Pre-bronchodilator FEV1/FVC is 0.66; after bronchodilator it is 0.68 with FEV1 62% predicted. What is the diagnosis?
mcqs.exp: B — COPD. A post-bronchodilator FEV1/FVC <0.70 in a symptomatic patient with risk factors is diagnostic. A is unlikely given the largely fixed obstruction and smoking history. C (pre-COPD) requires symptoms/structural disease without obstruction (ratio ≥0.7); D (PRISm) is a preserved ratio (≥0.7) with reduced FEV1/FVC volumes — neither applies here. E is wrong: the ratio is below 0.70.

mcqs.stem: A patient with COPD has mMRC 3, CAT 18, and two moderate exacerbations in the past year. Which GOLD group and first-line inhaler regimen?
mcqs.exp: B — Group E, LABA+LAMA. ≥2 moderate exacerbations defines Group E regardless of symptom score. First-line is LABA+LAMA; add ICS only if blood eos ≥300 (initial) or for ongoing exacerbations with eos ≥100. LABA+ICS (D) has no clear place in GOLD. A and C under-treat; E starts triple without an eosinophil indication.

mcqs.stem: A Group E patient on LABA+LAMA continues to exacerbate. Blood eosinophils are 220 cells/µL. What is the best next step?
opts: No change; eosinophils are too low to act
mcqs.exp: A. For continued exacerbations with eos ≥100, escalate to triple therapy (LABA+LAMA+ICS). B abandons the LAMA (worse for exacerbations and not preferred over triple). C (chronic oral steroids) has no role in stable COPD. D (dupilumab) is reserved for ongoing exacerbations with eos ≥300 and chronic bronchitis on triple therapy. E is wrong — 220 is above the ≥100 escalation threshold.

mcqs.stem: A 64-year-old with COPD has 4 days of increased dyspnea, sputum volume, and purulence. He is afebrile, SpO2 91% on room air, speaking full sentences, and can do his ADLs. Best outpatient regimen?
opts: Prednisone 40 mg ×5 days + a 5-day antibiotic + increased short-acting bronchodilators
mcqs.exp: B. All three cardinal symptoms (including purulence) are present, so antibiotics are indicated; add prednisone 40 mg ×5 days (REDUCE) and step up short-acting bronchodilators. A omits proven therapy. C uses an unnecessarily long steroid course (5 days is sufficient). D is over-triage — he has no severe features. E omits steroids and uses an over-long antibiotic course.

mcqs.stem: Which COPD intervention has the best-established mortality benefit?
mcqs.exp: C — smoking cessation, the intervention with the clearest mortality benefit (and benefits across all COPD domains). Long-term oxygen in resting hypoxemia also improves survival. ICS, roflumilast, LABA, and chronic azithromycin reduce symptoms and/or exacerbations but are not established mortality-reducers in the general COPD population.

anki: What is mandatory to diagnose COPD?
Post-bronchodilator spirometry showing FEV1/FVC <0.70 in a patient with symptoms/risk factors. You cannot diagnose COPD without PFTs.
Classic spirometry/PFT findings in COPD?
Scooped (concave) expiratory flow-volume loop, FEV1/FVC <0.7; supportive: ↑TLC and ↑RV (air trapping), ↓DLCO (emphysema). Post-bronchodilator FEV1 grades severity.
What are pre-COPD and PRISm?

Pre-COPD = respiratory symptoms or structural lesions (e.g., emphysema) WITHOUT airflow obstruction (FEV1/FVC ≥0.7). PRISm = Preserved Ratio Impaired Spirometry. Both carry risk of progressing to COPD.
When should alpha-1 antitrypsin testing be done in COPD?
Once in ALL patients with COPD — especially if young (<45), panlobular basal emphysema, or a suggestive family history.
2023 GOLD ABE groups — how are they defined?
Group A: 0–1 exacerbations + low symptoms (mMRC 0–1 / CAT <10). Group B: 0–1 exacerbations + high symptoms (mMRC ≥2 / CAT ≥10). Group E: ≥2 moderate exacerbations or ≥1 requiring hospitalization.
First-line inhaler therapy by GOLD group (2023)?
Group A: a long-acting bronchodilator (LABA or LAMA). Groups B and E: LABA + LAMA. Initial triple (add ICS) in Group E if blood eos ≥300.
When is ICS added in COPD, and what is the key risk?
Add ICS (→ triple therapy) for ongoing exacerbations with blood eos ≥100 (initial ICS in Group E if eos ≥300). Key risk: pneumonia — de-escalate ICS if recurrent pneumonias.
LABA vs LAMA vs ICS roles in COPD?
LABA: symptom relief, less effect on exacerbations. LAMA: symptoms AND fewer exacerbations (LAMA > LABA for exacerbations). ICS: fewer exacerbations if eos elevated, but ↑ pneumonia risk.
Which COPD interventions reduce mortality?

Smoking cessation (clearest benefit); long-term oxygen in resting hypoxemia; and vaccinations. Pulmonary rehab improves symptoms and exacerbations; lung cancer screening reduces cancer mortality in eligible smokers.
Criteria for long-term home oxygen in COPD?
Resting PaO2 ≤55 mmHg (or SpO2 ≤88%), OR PaO2 56–59 mmHg with cor pulmonale / right heart failure / polycythemia.
Three cardinal symptoms of a COPD exacerbation?
Increased dyspnea, increased sputum volume, and increased sputum purulence.
Outpatient steroid regimen for a COPD exacerbation?
Prednisone 40 mg daily ×5 days (REDUCE trial: short course non-inferior to longer). Reduces relapse and symptoms.
When are antibiotics indicated in a COPD exacerbation, and for how long?
When ≥2 cardinal symptoms are present (must include increased sputum purulence) or the patient needs ventilation. ~5-day course; agent chosen by local resistance patterns.
Bronchodilator choice in an acute COPD exacerbation?
Short-acting: SABA ± SAMA. Metered-dose inhalers are as effective as nebulizers when used correctly.
When is NIPPV indicated in a COPD exacerbation?
Acidemic hypercapnic respiratory failure: pH <7.35 with PaCO2 >45 mmHg.
What did GOLD 2024/2025 change about ICS-containing therapy?
When ICS is indicated, triple therapy (LABA+LAMA+ICS) is preferred over LABA-ICS. Eosinophil thresholds for ICS (≥100 escalate, ≥300 initial in Group E) unchanged.
First biologic approved for COPD, and its niche?
Dupilumab (Dupixent) — add to triple therapy for ongoing exacerbations with blood eos ≥300 and chronic bronchitis. FDA-approved September 2024 (BOREAS/NOTUS).
Ensifentrine (Ohtuvayre) — what is it?
A novel inhaled dual PDE3/PDE4 inhibitor (bronchodilator + anti-inflammatory) by nebulizer; FDA-approved June 2024 (ENHANCE) — first new inhaled COPD mechanism in ~20 years.

 a1at tested once *[y/n]; eos *__ cells/uL; smoking *[active *__py / former / never]
classify (GOLD 2023): mMRC *__ / CAT *__; exacerbations last yr *__ (hosp *__)
 -> Group *[A / B / E]
maintenance inhaler: *[LAMA / LABA / LABA+LAMA / LABA+LAMA+ICS] + PRN SABA±SAMA
 step-up rule: Group A = single LABD; Group B/E = LABA+LAMA; +ICS if exac + eos>=100 (initial triple if Group E + eos>=300); de-escalate ICS if recurrent PNA
 advanced (refer pulm): roflumilast [FEV1<50% + chronic bronchitis], azithromycin [esp ex-smoker], LVRS/EBV/transplant; (2024-25) dupilumab if exac + eos>=300 + chronic bronchitis; ensifentrine (PDE3/4)
non-pharm (mortality/function): smoking cessation *[counseled/Rx]; O2 if PaO2<=55 or <=59+cor pulmonale *[y/n]; pulm rehab *[referred]; vaccines [flu/PCV/COVID/Tdap/zoster] *__; lung CA screen if eligible *[y/n]
inhaler technique reviewed + adherence *[y/n]

RTC / call for: worsening dyspnea, fever, ^sputum purulence, confusion
takehome: In a patient with risk factors, post-bronchodilator FEV1/FVC <0.7 (scooped flow-volume loop) on spirometry is required to diagnose COPD — you can't diagnose it without PFTs.
Smoking cessation is the intervention with the clearest mortality benefit; O2 for resting hypoxemia and vaccines also matter.

By 2023 GOLD groups, LABA+LAMA is first-line for most symptomatic patients; add ICS mainly for exacerbators with eos ≥100 (initial ICS in Group E if eos ≥300), and de-escalate ICS for recurrent pneumonia.
Treat outpatient exacerbations with short-acting bronchodilators, prednisone 40 mg ×5 days, and a 5-day antibiotic (per local resistance) when ≥2 cardinal symptoms include sputum purulence.
2024–25 add-ons: dupilumab (first biologic; eos ≥300 + chronic bronchitis) and ensifentrine (PDE3/4 inhaler).

extras.label: 2024–2025 GOLD Practice Update NEW — projected on the appended deck slide
extras.html:
The deck's GOLD-2023 teaching (FEV1/FVC <0.7 diagnosis, ABE groups, LABA-LAMA first-line, eosinophil-guided ICS, 5-day steroid/antibiotic for exacerbations) was verified current in the GOLD 2024 and 2025 reports. Practice-relevant additions:

  • Triple therapy preferred: when ICS is indicated, GOLD recommends LABA+LAMA+ICS over LABA-ICS (superior exacerbations, lung function, quality of life).
  • Eosinophil thresholds (unchanged but emphasized): initial ICS in Group E if eos ≥300; escalate to triple for exacerbators with eos ≥100; ICS de-escalation more likely to trigger exacerbations if eos ≥300.
  • First biologic for COPD — dupilumab (Dupixent): add to triple therapy for ongoing exacerbations with blood eos ≥300 and chronic bronchitis. FDA-approved September 27, 2024 (BOREAS, NOTUS).
  • Novel inhaled class — ensifentrine (Ohtuvayre): dual PDE3/PDE4 inhibitor (bronchodilator + anti-inflammatory) by nebulizer; FDA-approved June 26, 2024 (ENHANCE-1/-2) — the first new inhaled COPD mechanism in about 20 years.
  • Comorbidity emphasis (GOLD 2025): greater attention to cardiovascular disease and pulmonary hypertension screening, plus refinements to spirometry/imaging guidance.

Newly cited for the update:

  3. FDA. Approval of dupilumab (Dupixent) for COPD with an eosinophilic phenotype, September 27, 2024 (BOREAS, NOTUS).
  4. FDA / Verona Pharma. Approval of ensifentrine (Ohtuvayre) for COPD maintenance, June 26, 2024 (ENHANCE-1/-2).

lessons.title: Central Venous Catheter Insertion
lessons.subtitle: Optimized procedure package · Facilitator-led simulation-based mastery learning for IM residents · Pre-work ~20 min + simulation ~60 min
lessons.credit: Adapted from TeachIM.org — Central Venous Catheter Insertion (Fainstad, Longino, Held, Kramer, Ortiz-Lopez, Neumeier, Ma; sec. ed. Cool; published June 2022, materials rev. June 2024/Apr 2025), CC BY-NC 4.0. Optimized June 2026: guideline currency verified, teaching design upgraded, assessments and clinical tools added. Procedure-type lesson — TeachIM publishes no slide deck for procedures; the package is built around the original instructional guide, simulation & clinical assessment checklists, and pre-quiz (all included below).
objectives: Identify appropriate indications and relevant contraindications to performing a central venous catheter insertion.
Achieve a Minimum Passing Score (MPS) on the validated assessment checklist in a simulation setting.
Perform a CVC insertion under full or partial supervision in the clinical setting until demonstrating the Unsupervised Practice Standard (UPS) on the validated assessment checklist.

prework.q: Q1. Which transducer and which real-time technique do you use to cannulate the internal jugular vein, and why is dynamic (not static) better?
prework.a: The high-frequency linear transducer. Use the "follow-the-tip" dynamic technique: keep the transducer just caudal to the advancing needle so the tip comes into view, then slide caudally and repeat. With a static probe you can't tell whether you're seeing the needle tip or a cross-section of the shaft — so you can advance past the vein without knowing it. Confirm the wire in the vein in both short- and long-axis views before dilating.

prework.q: Q2. Before you dilate, what two things must the ultrasound confirm — and what one error during wire placement should you watch the monitor for?
prework.a: Confirm (1) the guidewire is in the IJV (not the carotid) in both short and long axis, and (2) venous (not arterial) cannulation. Watch the telemetry for ectopy while advancing the wire — ventricular ectopy means the wire is in the right atrium/ventricle; withdraw it a few centimeters. Real-time ultrasound visualization of the wire in the vein is the most reliable confirmation of venous placement (more reliable than dark blood color, ease of wire passage, or a non-pulsatile drip).

prework.q: Q3. How do you confirm correct line position and rule out a pneumothorax afterward?
prework.a: Two acceptable approaches: chest radiograph — catheter tip at the cavo-atrial junction, no pneumothorax; or bedside ultrasound — directly visualize the catheter in the vessel, inject 10 cc agitated saline and confirm turbulent flow in the right atrium within ~2 s (rapid atrial swirl sign), and confirm pleural sliding in multiple anterior interspaces to exclude pneumothorax (sens ~91%, spec ~98% vs CT).

segments.label: Hook & framing (2 min)
segments.html: "Your patient is in septic shock and needs norepinephrine now — but you place the line, and ten minutes later the nurse pages that the post-line CXR shows a new pneumothorax. Walk it back: which two moments in the procedure determine whether that happens?" Hold the answer; you'll return to it. Establish that the two highest-yield safety levers are real-time ultrasound (follow-the-tip + confirming the wire in the vein) and maintaining control of the guidewire — not needle dexterity.

segments.label: LEARN — Indications & contraindications
segments.html:
ASK: "Give me three indications for a CVC — and one common scenario where a triple-lumen CVC is actually the wrong line."
IndicationsNot a good fit for a standard triple-lumen CVC
Vasopressor / peripherally-incompatible (vesicant, TPN) infusion · inadequate peripheral access · hemodynamic monitoring (CVP, ScvO₂) · hemodialysis (via a dedicated HD catheter) · transvenous pacing / pulmonary-artery catheterMassive transfusion / rapid volume resuscitation — the long, narrow lumens of a TLC have high resistance and slow flow; use a large-bore peripheral IV or an introducer/Cordis instead.
ASK: "Which of these blocks the line: INR 4, an IJ thrombus on ultrasound, a facial maculopapular rash, or end-stage kidney disease?"
Absolute / site-specific hard stopsIncreased-risk cautions (weigh & mitigate)
Thrombus in the target vein · overlying skin infection/cellulitis at the site · distorted anatomy or hardware (e.g., contralateral) — pick another siteCoagulopathy (INR ↑, thrombocytopenia) — bleeding risk; correct only if clearly excessive, prefer a compressible site (IJ/femoral) over subclavian; uncooperative patient; severe COPD/single lung (PTX consequences). ESKD and a self-limited rash are not contraindications.
Pearl — pick the site for the patient: Real-time ultrasound makes the IJ the default for most IM bedside lines. Subclavian has the lowest infection and thrombosis rate but the highest mechanical risk (pneumothorax, non-compressible bleeding); femoral is fastest in a code but has the highest infection/thrombosis risk — avoid it for elective lines (3SITES trial, Parienti NEJM 2015).

segments.label: LEARN — Positioning & pre-procedure ultrasound
segments.html:
ASK: "What position best engorges the IJ, and what's the one thing you must check on ultrasound before you ever open the kit?"
  • Trendelenburg (head below feet) for an IJ or subclavian line engorges and stabilizes the target vein; the goal is simply to engorge the vessel for cannulation while keeping the patient comfortable and stable — drop it if not tolerated.
  • Pre-procedure scan (linear probe): confirm the IJV is well visualized and follows a predictable path relative to the internal carotid artery, then confirm patency with compression from mid-neck to clavicle. A non-compressible vein = thrombus → do not cannulate; choose another site.
  • Set up ergonomically: patient at a comfortable height, trash can and tray in reach, ultrasound screen directly in your line of sight across the patient.
Safety anchor — the IJ sits lateral/superficial to the carotid: always confirm the vein compresses and the artery pulsates. Cannulating a non-compressible structure or one that pulsates risks an arterial stick.

segments.label: SEE — Sterile prep & supply preparation
segments.html:
1. Time-out with the patient's nurse (name, DOB, procedure, site, allergies, consent) before opening any sterile supplies.
  2. Maximal sterile barrier: cap, mask, eye protection, sterile gown and gloves; full-body drape head-to-feet. (Maximal barrier + chlorhexidine + checklist is the CLABSI-prevention bundle — Pronovost NEJM 2006.)
  3. Skin prep: chlorhexidine scrub ×2, 30 s each, over an area larger than the drape aperture. (Chlorhexidine is scrubbed — unlike iodine, which is applied and allowed to dry.) Chlorhexidine is the preferred antiseptic.
  4. Sterile probe cover on the transducer; place it securely on the chest.
  5. Prepare supplies: draw up lidocaine with the largest needle/filtered catheter; prime the guidewire (retract until the curved tip is flush with the straight end of the coil); flush all lumens with sterile saline and clamp/cap each with a Luer lock.

segments.label: SEE — Cannulation, wire, dilation & catheter
segments.html:
1. Anesthetize a dermal wheal with a 21–25 g needle and the track under US guidance.
  2. Advance the introducer needle at 45–60°, maintaining negative pressure, using the follow-the-tip technique with dynamic ultrasound, until dark venous blood is aspirated; then unscrew the syringe. (If the angle is too steep — e.g., 90° — you can't follow the tip.)
  3. Introduce the guidewire: pinch with a thumb at the end of the coil while advancing, release and pull back, repeat until ≥10 cm of wire is in. Watch telemetry — withdraw a few cm if ectopy develops.
  4. Withdraw the needle over the wire; confirm the wire is in the IJV on ultrasound in both short and long axis, beyond the clavicle along the posterior wall.
  5. Nick the skin with a #11 scalpel to ~½ the blade width, contiguous with the wire puncture. Advance the dilator over the wire with a clockwise–counterclockwise twist only to the depth of the vessel; remove it and hold pressure.
  6. Advance the triple-lumen catheter over the wire; near the skin, unclamp the distal port (the one the wire exits — labeled "1," typically the brown/distal port), feed the wire out the distal port, grasp the wire tip, and advance the catheter to ~12–16 cm at the skin (R IJ, by height). Remove the wire and Luer-lock the distal port.
  7. Confirm blood return in all lumens, flush each clear, then clamp/cap. Secure with a hub fastener (≥2 sutures at the insertion site, 1–2 at the lumen junction), apply a chlorhexidine-impregnated sponge and sterile dressing.
Safety anchor — never lose the wire: Maintain control of the guidewire at every step — it must not be contaminated, advanced too far (ectopy/arrhythmia), or left in the vessel after the catheter is placed. The wire is removed after the catheter is in, not before.
Pearl — venous vs arterial confirmation: The most reliable check is real-time ultrasound visualization of the wire in the vein. Dark-vs-bright blood color, ease of wire passage, and a non-pulsatile drip are all unreliable in hypoxemia, hypotension, or shock. If you're unsure, transduce the pressure or send a blood gas before dilating.

segments.label: SEE — Clean-up & line verification
segments.html:
• Secure ultrasound and dispose of all sharps before removing the drape; reassess vitals and update the nurse.
  • Confirm placement & exclude pneumothorax — by CXR (tip at the cavo-atrial junction) or bedside ultrasound: directly see the catheter in the vessel, inject 10 cc agitated saline and confirm turbulent flow in the right atrium within ~2 s (and that the tip does not sit in the RA), plus pleural sliding in multiple anterior interspaces (sens ~91%, spec ~98% vs CT).
  • Place the "okay to use" order only after the tip is confirmed at the cavo-atrial junction (for supradiaphragmatic lines).

segments.label: PRACTICE → PROVE — Mastery learning
segments.html:
The learner familiarizes themselves with the kit and rehearses each step, then signals readiness for a graded run. With the checklist out of view, quiz the non-technical steps; then run a full simulation with it visible, correcting each mistake and having the learner repeat until correct. For any step the simulator can't physically reproduce, have the learner verbalize the intended step.

StageStandard
Simulation (MPS)Every item on the simulation assessment checklist performed correctly and independently (indications ×3, contraindications ×2, US patency, CHG scrub >30 s, prime/flush, follow-the-tip with negative pressure, wire >10 cm, wire confirmed both axes, wire control + removed after catheter, catheter to depth, blood return + Luer-lock, ≥2 sutures, verbalize CXR confirmation).
Clinical (UPS)Observed clinical assessment checklist + Global Skills Assessment and an Entrustment rating of "perform safely and effectively without direct supervision."

segments.label: Close (2 min)
segments.html: Hide the guide. Ask the learner to rebuild: three indications and the one scenario where a TLC is the wrong line; the pre-scan check that rules out a thrombus; the most reliable confirmation of venous cannulation; the two ways to confirm tip position and exclude pneumothorax; and the one rule about the guidewire.

cases.title: Case 1 — Choosing the line (and the site)
cases.stem: A 58-year-old in septic shock needs norepinephrine and central access; they also have a GI bleed and may need rapid transfusion. INR 1.8, platelets 90K. The intern asks whether the triple-lumen CVC you're about to place will cover the transfusion too, and whether the coags should be corrected first.
cases.reveal: A triple-lumen CVC is correct for the vasopressor, but it is a poor conduit for massive transfusion — the long narrow lumens have high resistance and slow flow. Place a large-bore peripheral IV or an introducer/Cordis for volume resuscitation. INR 1.8 / plt 90K are cautions, not absolute contraindications; use a compressible site (IJ) under ultrasound and don't delay a needed line in shock to chase a mild coagulopathy. Confirm IJ patency by compression before cannulating.

cases.title: Case 2 — Confirming placement
cases.stem: You've placed a right IJ triple-lumen catheter under ultrasound. Blood returns dark and non-pulsatile from all lumens. Before calling the line "okay to use," you confirm position at the bedside.
cases.reveal: Inject 10 cc agitated sterile saline through a lumen and confirm turbulent flow in the right atrium within ~2 s on a subxiphoid/apical view (rapid atrial swirl) — turbulent flow confirms the tip is in the central venous system and not, e.g., arterial or extravascular. The tip should sit at the cavo-atrial junction (on CXR: lower SVC / cavo-atrial junction, not deep in the RA). Exclude pneumothorax with anterior lung sliding in multiple interspaces (or CXR). Only then place the "okay to use" order.

mcqs.stem: Which of the following is the least appropriate indication for placing a standard triple-lumen central venous catheter?
opts: Rapid massive transfusion in hemorrhagic shock
mcqs.exp: C. A triple-lumen CVC has long, narrow, high-resistance lumens, so flow rates are slow — it is a poor choice for rapid massive transfusion, where a short large-bore peripheral IV or an introducer/Cordis is preferred. Vasopressors (A), TPN (B), and CVP monitoring (D) are all standard indications for a TLC.

mcqs.stem: On the pre-procedure scan of the right internal jugular vein, the vessel does not fully collapse with probe compression. What is the most appropriate next step?
opts: Cannulate quickly before the vein collapses
Do not cannulate this vein — suspect thrombus and select another site
mcqs.exp: C. A non-compressible vein suggests thrombus, an absolute contraindication to cannulating that vein; choose another site. Thrombus in the target vein is the classic site-specific contraindication. More Trendelenburg (A) or rushing (B) doesn't change the obstruction, and platelet count (D) is irrelevant here.

mcqs.stem: Why is dynamic "follow-the-tip" ultrasound preferred over holding the probe in a fixed position while advancing the needle?
opts: It is faster and requires less sterile setup
A static image cannot distinguish the needle tip from a cross-section of the shaft, so you can advance past the vein unknowingly
It avoids the need to confirm the wire in the vein
It eliminates the risk of arterial puncture entirely
mcqs.exp: B. With a static probe you can't tell whether the bright dot is the advancing tip or a cross-section of the shaft, so the tip can travel through the back wall of the vein and into the carotid without your knowing. Following the tip (probe just caudal to the needle, advancing in steps) keeps the tip in view. It does not remove the need to confirm the wire in the vein (C) and does not eliminate arterial puncture risk (D).

mcqs.stem: After cannulating the IJ you advance the guidewire and the monitor shows new frequent PVCs. What should you do?
opts: Continue advancing — ectopy is expected and harmless
Withdraw the guidewire a few centimeters until the ectopy resolves
Remove the wire entirely and restart
mcqs.exp: B. Ectopy means the wire tip is irritating the right atrium/ventricle. Withdraw the wire a few centimeters and the ectopy should resolve; this is why you monitor telemetry during wire placement. You do not need to abort the procedure (C) or give antiarrhythmics (A, D) for transient wire-induced ectopy.

mcqs.stem: Which finding most reliably confirms venous (rather than arterial) cannulation of the internal jugular vein before dilation?
opts: Dark (rather than bright) red blood in the syringe
Ease of passing the wire to 10–15 cm
Real-time ultrasound visualization of the wire within the IJ vein
A slow non-pulsatile drip of blood from the needle hub
mcqs.exp: C. Direct ultrasound visualization of the wire in the vein (both axes) is the most reliable confirmation. Blood color (A) and non-pulsatile flow (D) are unreliable in hypoxemia/hypotension/shock, and easy wire passage (B) can occur with arterial cannulation too. Confirming venous placement before dilating is critical — dilating an inadvertently cannulated carotid causes catastrophic injury.

anki: Common indications for a triple-lumen CVC?

Vasopressors / peripherally-incompatible infusion (TPN, vesicants), inadequate peripheral access, hemodynamic monitoring (CVP/ScvO2), transvenous pacing/PA catheter; hemodialysis via a dedicated HD catheter.
Why is a triple-lumen CVC a poor choice for massive transfusion?
Long, narrow, high-resistance lumens cause slow flow. Use a short large-bore peripheral IV or an introducer/Cordis for rapid volume/blood.
Site-specific / absolute contraindication to cannulating a vein for CVC?
Thrombus in the target vein (non-compressible on US); overlying skin infection; distorted anatomy. ESKD and a self-limited rash are NOT contraindications.
Are coagulopathy/thrombocytopenia absolute contraindications to CVC?
No, they are cautions. Correct only if clearly excessive; prefer a compressible site (IJ/femoral) over subclavian; do not delay a needed line in shock for a mild INR.
Site trade-offs: IJ vs subclavian vs femoral?

IJ = default for US-guided bedside lines. Subclavian = lowest infection/thrombosis but highest mechanical risk (PTX, non-compressible). Femoral = fastest in a code but highest infection/thrombosis; avoid for elective (3SITES, Parienti NEJM 2015).
Preferred patient position for an IJ/subclavian CVC?
Trendelenburg (head below feet) to engorge/stabilize the vein. Goal = engorge the vessel while keeping the patient comfortable/stable.
Preferred skin antiseptic for CVC, and how is it applied?
Chlorhexidine, SCRUBBED (x2, 30 s each) over an area larger than the drape aperture. (Iodine is applied and left to dry.)
Which ultrasound transducer for real-time IJ cannulation?
High-frequency LINEAR transducer.
What must the pre-procedure scan confirm?
IJV is well visualized with a predictable path relative to the carotid, and is PATENT (fully compressible mid-neck to clavicle). Non-compressible = thrombus, use another site.
"Follow-the-tip" technique, and why dynamic beats static?

Keep the probe just caudal to the advancing needle so the TIP comes into view, slide caudally, repeat. Static cannot distinguish tip from shaft cross-section, so you can pass through the vein unknowingly.
How far is the guidewire advanced, and what do you monitor?
At least 10 cm of wire. Watch telemetry, withdraw a few cm if ectopy develops (wire in RA/RV).
Most reliable confirmation of venous (vs arterial) cannulation?
Real-time ultrasound visualization of the wire IN the vein (both axes). Blood color, ease of wire passage, and non-pulsatile drip are all unreliable in shock/hypoxemia.
Maximal sterile barrier / CLABSI-prevention bundle?
Cap, mask, eye protection, sterile gown + gloves, full-body drape; chlorhexidine prep; checklist in view; CHG-impregnated dressing (Pronovost NEJM 2006).
Which port does the guidewire exit, and why?
The DISTAL port (labeled "1," typically brown), unclamp it so the wire feeds out the catheter tip; grasp the wire before advancing the catheter.
When is the guidewire removed?
AFTER the catheter is fully inserted, never before. Maintain control of the wire at every step (do not contaminate, over-advance, or retain it).
Appropriate depth for a right IJ triple-lumen catheter?
About 12-16 cm at the skin depending on patient height (~12 cm for ~5 ft, ~15 cm for ~6 ft). Tip should sit at the cavo-atrial junction.
Bedside-ultrasound confirmation of supradiaphragmatic CVC placement?
Inject 10 cc agitated saline, confirm turbulent flow in the right atrium within ~2 s (rapid atrial swirl); tip should NOT sit in the RA. Plus directly visualize the catheter in the vessel.
How do you exclude pneumothorax after a CVC, and how good is bedside US?
Anterior chest lung sliding in multiple interspaces, supine (sens ~91%, spec ~98% vs CT, Alrajhi Chest 2012), or CXR. CXR also confirms tip at cavo-atrial junction.
lessons.dot:
PROCEDURE: Central venous catheter insertion — *[triple-lumen / introducer-Cordis / HD] catheter, *R/L *[internal jugular / subclavian / femoral]
Indication: *[vasopressors / periph-incompatible infusion or TPN / inadequate access / hemodynamic monitoring / HD / transvenous pacing]
Pre-proc checklist:
 - contraindic reviewed: NO target-vein thrombus (vein FULLY COMPRESSIBLE on US), NO overlying skin infection, anatomy suitable. Cautions weighed: coags INR *__/plt *__ (compressible site chosen), *cooperative.
 - consent obtained from *[pt/surrogate] *(or emergent indication); risks (arterial puncture, pneumothorax, bleeding, arrhythmia, infection/CLABSI, malposition, failure) & alternatives discussed
 - TIME-OUT w/ RN: name/DOB/procedure/SIDE/site/allergies confirmed BEFORE opening sterile supplies
Sterile: maximal barrier (cap, mask, eye protn, sterile gown+gloves, full-body drape); chlorhexidine scrub x2 (30 s each); sterile probe cover.
Pre-scan (high-freq linear): *R/L IJV well visualized, predictable path vs carotid, PATENT to compression mid-neck→clavicle.
Technique: position *Trendelenburg. 1% lidocaine *__ mL (dermal wheal → tract under US). Introducer needle advanced 45-60° w/ negative pressure using FOLLOW-THE-TIP dynamic US until dark venous blood aspirated. Guidewire advanced >10 cm — *no/transient ectopy (wire withdrawn). Needle removed. WIRE CONFIRMED in *R/L IJV in short + long axis. #11 skin nick; dilator over wire to vessel depth; catheter advanced over wire (wire out DISTAL port, grasped) to *__ cm (~12-16 cm R IJ by height). SAFETY: wire control maintained throughout; wire removed AFTER catheter placed.
Post: blood return + flush all *3 lumens, Luer-locked/clamped; hub secured w/ *≥2 sutures; CHG sponge + sterile dressing. Sharps disposed. Vitals stable, *EBL minimal, no immediate complication.
Confirmation: *[CXR — tip at cavo-atrial junction, no PTX] / *[bedside US — catheter in vessel, agitated-saline turbulent RA flow <2 s, anterior lung sliding present b/l]. "Okay to use" order placed.
Lidocaine used (mL): *__   Performing provider: *  | Supervising: *
takehome: Match the line to the job: a triple-lumen CVC is for vasopressors, incompatible infusions, monitoring, and access — not for massive transfusion (use a large-bore peripheral or introducer).

Scan before you stick: confirm the target vein is patent and fully compressible — a non-compressible vein means thrombus, so pick another site. Thrombus and overlying infection are the hard stops; coagulopathy is a caution.

Real-time ultrasound is the core safety skill: use the high-frequency linear probe and the follow-the-tip technique, and confirm the wire in the vein in both axes before you dilate — the single best protection against dilating the carotid.
Never lose the wire: watch telemetry for ectopy, keep control of the wire at every step, and remove it only after the catheter is in.
Confirm and clear before use: tip at the cavo-atrial junction and no pneumothorax — by CXR or bedside ultrasound (agitated-saline RA swirl + anterior lung sliding).

Prove competence by mastery learning: reach the MPS (every sim checklist item correct & independent), then the UPS (clinical checklist + entrustment "without direct supervision") before unsupervised practice.

lessons.changelog:
AreaWhat changed
Currency checkVerified against current standards (June 2026): the ASA 2020 Practice Guidelines for Central Venous Access remain the major guideline and are not superseded. Real-time ultrasound guidance is reaffirmed as first-line for all sites (ACS revised recommendations; Annals of Intensive Care 2023 narrative review — US reduces complications ~3× for IJ/femoral, ~2× for subclavian). Site trade-offs unchanged (3SITES, Parienti NEJM 2015). No standing-content change required.
Content addedAdded explicit site-selection trade-offs (IJ/subclavian/femoral, with 3SITES infection/thrombosis/mechanical-risk framing); the "match the line to the job" teaching point (TLC is wrong for massive transfusion — the keyed quiz answer); the CLABSI-prevention bundle framing (maximal barrier + chlorhexidine + checklist, Pronovost NEJM 2006); and the bedside-ultrasound confirmation pathway (agitated-saline rapid atrial swirl + anterior lung sliding sens ~91%/spec ~98%).
CorrectionsNo factual errors found in the instructional guide, simulation/clinical assessment checklists, or quiz.
PedagogyRestructured into the Learn–See–Practice–Prove arc with a 2-min hook, ≥4 ASK pause-points, pearls/safety-anchor callouts, HTML tables for indications/contraindications and MPS/UPS, and a retrieval close.
AssessmentAdded 3 pre-work retrieval questions, 5 ABIM-style board MCQs with full distractor explanations, 2 case vignettes (from the quiz), and 18 spaced-repetition cards.
Clinical toolAdded an EMR-ready CVC procedure-note / time-out dotphrase (procedure-type structure).
One-pagerBuilt a dense single-page procedure reference (separate print-CSS HTML → PDF, one page).
FormatSelf-contained HTML launcher linking all original companion files (instructional guide, simulation assessment, clinical assessment, pre-quiz w/ answer key) + generated files; matches the Syncope/Thoracentesis design system. No _Optimized.pptx — TeachIM publishes no slide deck for procedures; per the no-rebuild rule none was created.
Human review neededCatheter-depth figures vary slightly across the source materials: the lesson page/quiz state ≈12–16 cm by height (used here), while the June-2024 simulation checklist lists "15–20 cm." This is a known across-document inconsistency; depth should always be individualized and confirmed by tip position at the cavo-atrial junction. Flagged for the authors to harmonize.

lessons.title: Cervical Cancer Screening
objectives: Describe HPV virology and its role in the natural history of cervical cancer.
Choose appropriate screening tests and intervals for patients at average risk.
Identify high-risk populations and apply alternate screening guidelines.
Apply risk-based guidelines to manage abnormal results in average- and high-risk individuals.

prework.q: 1. What causes nearly all cervical cancer, and where does it preferentially develop?
prework.a: Persistent infection with high-risk HPV (chiefly types 16 and 18). >90% of cancers arise at the transformation zone (squamocolumnar junction), so screening must sample that region. ~90% of new HPV infections clear within 2 years; the persistent minority drive precancer over years to decades.

prework.q: 2. For an average-risk 35-year-old, what are the acceptable screening options and intervals?
prework.a: Cytology alone every 3 years, OR HPV-based testing (primary hrHPV or co-test) every 5 years. USPSTF (2025) and ACS prefer primary hrHPV and now endorse self-collection.

prework.q: 3. What single number drives ASCCP management of an abnormal result?
prework.a: The immediate risk of CIN3+: if <4% → surveillance interval; if ≥4% → colposcopy and/or treatment, escalating with risk. "Equal management for equal risk."

segments.label: 0:00–2:00 — Hook
segments.html:
Open with the natural-history arc: persistent high-risk HPV → atypia → precancer → cancer over ~10+ years. The whole point of screening is to catch the treatable middle of that arc. Reveal the two numbers: ~90% of HPV infections clear within 2 years; the persistent ~10% are what we screen for.

ASK: "If most HPV clears on its own, why don't we screen 22-year-olds with HPV testing?" (High prevalence of transient HPV in the young → HPV testing would generate false alarms, colposcopies, and overtreatment with obstetric harms. Cytology alone for 21–29.)

segments.label: 2:00–7:00 — Objective 1: HPV virology & anatomy (Pathophysiology board)
segments.html:
ConceptKey teaching point
CausePersistent hrHPV (chiefly 16, 18) causes nearly all cervical cancer; ~15 oncogenic types exist
Risk factors for persistenceOlder age, oncogenic subtype, immunosuppression (HIV, meds), smoking, other STIs, possibly cOCP use
AnatomyEctocervix = squamous; endocervix = columnar; they meet at the squamocolumnar junction. The transformation zone (squamous metaplasia) is where HPV preferentially infects and >90% of cancers arise
Cancer typesEctocervix → squamous cell carcinoma (~75%); endocervix → adenocarcinoma (~25%, less common)
Pearl: "Sample the transformation zone" is the anatomic reason behind every screening test — that's where the disease starts. Adenocarcinoma (endocervical/glandular) is the type cytology misses most, which is why HPV-based testing is more sensitive.

segments.label: 7:00–14:00 — Objectives 2 & 3: Who and how to screen (Screening boards)
segments.html:
Three tests: cytology (Pap), primary hrHPV (with reflex cytology if positive), and co-test (both). "HPV-based testing" means primary HPV or co-test.

Average riskUSPSTFACS
21–29 yCytology q3y(ACS starts at 25)
25/30–65 yCytology q3y OR HPV-based testing q5yPrimary hrHPV q5y (preferred) > co-test q5y > cytology q3y
Stop>65 y if no CIN2+ in 25 y AND adequate prior screening (3 neg cytology in 10 y, most recent <3 y; or 2 neg HPV-based in 10 y, most recent <5 y)
Post-hysterectomyStop after total hysterectomy (cervix removed) for benign indication with no CIN2+ in 25 y
ASK: "Does HPV-vaccination status change the screening schedule?" (No — follow age-specific recommendations regardless of vaccination status.)

High-risk (immunocompromised/immunosuppressed): HIV, solid-organ/stem-cell transplant, SLE, IBD/RA on immunosuppression, chronic steroids.

HIVRecommendation
<30 yCytology at baseline, then annually ×3; if 3 normal → cytology q3y (start at diagnosis, not before 21). Co-testing not recommended (<30)
≥30 yCytology only (baseline, annually ×3, then q3y if normal) OR co-test q3y. Primary HPV with reflex not recommended. Continue for LIFE (not stopping at 65)
Pearl: For HIV/immunosuppressed patients screening does not stop at 65 and post-hysterectomy patients still need annual screening if HIV is progressing or virally unsuppressed. Non-HIV immunosuppressed (transplant, SLE, IBD/RA on immunosuppression) follow the CDC HIV protocol; IBD/RA not on immunosuppression and type 1 DM follow the general-population guideline.

segments.label: 14:00–20:00 — Objective 4: Managing abnormal results (Management boards)
segments.html:
For average-risk patients, use the ASCCP 2019 risk-based app (app.asccp.org, endorsed by ACOG). It estimates the immediate risk of CIN3+ from current + prior results and personal factors. Positive primary HPV → reflex cytology from the same specimen.

Immediate CIN3+ riskAction
<4%Surveillance by 5-year CIN3+ risk: >0.55% → 1 y; 0.15–0.55% → 3 y; <0.15% → 5 y
4–24%Colposcopy
25–59%Expedited treatment OR colposcopy (depends on desire for future pregnancy)
60–100%Expedited treatment (excision or ablation)
Pearl — Pap vs colposcopy: Cytology grades exfoliated cells (NILM → ASC-US → LSIL → ASC-H → AGC → HSIL). Colposcopy biopsies tissue for histology (No CIN / CIN1 / CIN2-3 / AIS), assessing vertical extension + p16 staining. Excision (LEEP/cone) is generally preferred over ablation for histologic HSIL and AIS because it yields tissue for pathology.

High-risk management: immunosuppressed → colposcopy for ASC-US/HPV+, or LSIL/ASC-H/AGC/HSIL regardless of HPV. History of CIN2+ → HPV-based test at 6 mo, then 3 annual HPV-based tests, then q3y for ≥25 years (even >65).

Retrieval close: board down — rebuild the average-risk schedule (21–29 vs 30–65), the 4% CIN3+ threshold, and the one feature that flips a patient to the high-risk track (immunosuppression or prior CIN2+).

lessons.casesLabel: Cases (from the original board — answers click-to-reveal)

cases.title: Case 1 — 40-year-old with a cervix; HTN, depression; presents for screening. Co-test negative, no prior abnormal Paps
cases.reveal: In 5 years — they had negative HPV-based (co-) testing.

cases.title: Case 2 — 37-year-old with a cervix; SLE on hydroxychloroquine; annual wellness exam; normal Pap 3 y ago (HPV status unknown); completed HPV vaccine series
cases.reveal: Today. Given SLE on immunosuppression, screen with cytology alone or HPV co-test at least every 3 years (high-risk protocol). HPV-vaccination status does not change the schedule.

mcqs.stem: A healthy 24-year-old requests cervical cancer screening. Which is the most appropriate strategy?
mcqs.exp: C. For ages 21–29, cytology alone every 3 years is recommended (USPSTF). A/B — HPV-based testing is avoided <30 due to high transient-HPV prevalence (overtreatment risk). D — screening begins at 21 (USPSTF) / 25 (ACS). E — colposcopy is for abnormal results, not primary screening.

mcqs.stem: A 45-year-old woman with HIV (CD4 600, virally suppressed) presents for screening. Which is correct?
opts: Primary hrHPV every 5 years; stop at 65
Cytology or co-testing, continued throughout life
Cytology every 3 years, stop at 65 like the general population
Colposcopy at baseline for all HIV patients
mcqs.exp: B. HIV patients ≥30 are screened with cytology or co-testing and continue for life (not stopping at 65). A — primary HPV with reflex is not recommended in HIV. C — they do not stop at 65. D — vaccination doesn't change the schedule. E — colposcopy is for abnormal cytology, not routine baseline.

mcqs.stem: The ASCCP app estimates a patient's immediate risk of CIN3+ at 30%. She desires future pregnancy. What does the guideline recommend?
opts: Surveillance with repeat testing in 1 year
mcqs.exp: C. An immediate CIN3+ risk of 25–59% warrants expedited treatment OR colposcopy — colposcopy is reasonable here given her desire for future pregnancy (avoids unnecessary excision). A/B — surveillance applies when immediate risk is <4%. D — hysterectomy is not first-line for a precancer risk. E — not a guideline option.

mcqs.stem: A 52-year-old average-risk woman had treatment for histologic HSIL (CIN2) 4 years ago. How long must she continue surveillance?
opts: HPV-based testing q3y for at least 25 years, even beyond age 65
No further screening if HPV-negative once
mcqs.exp: B. A history of CIN2+ confers a persistent ~2-fold increased risk for ≥25 years — surveillance is HPV-based testing q3y for at least 25 years, continuing past 65. A/E — a single negative test does not end surveillance. C — annual testing isn't required after the initial post-treatment series. D — stopping at 65 doesn't apply to a CIN2+ history.

mcqs.stem: Reflecting the 2025 USPSTF update, which statement about average-risk screening for a 40-year-old is most accurate?
opts: Primary hrHPV every 5 years is preferred and may use an FDA-approved self-collected sample
HPV testing is no longer recommended
mcqs.exp: B. The 2025 USPSTF update makes primary hrHPV every 5 years the preferred strategy for ages 30–65 and, for the first time, endorses patient self-collection on an FDA-approved platform. A/C — annual screening is not recommended for average risk. D — start remains 21 (USPSTF) / 25 (ACS). E — HPV testing is now the preferred modality, not removed.

anki: What causes nearly all cervical cancer?
Persistent high-risk HPV infection, chiefly types 16 and 18
Where do >90% of cervical cancers arise?
The transformation zone (squamocolumnar junction) - must be sampled during screening
Two cervical cancer histologies and their origin?
Squamous cell carcinoma (~75%, ectocervix); adenocarcinoma (~25%, endocervix)
What % of new HPV infections clear within 2 years?
~90% (about 70% within 12 months)
Average-risk screening, ages 21-29?
Cytology alone every 3 years (no HPV-based testing <30)
Average-risk screening, ages 30-65?
Cytology q3y OR HPV-based testing (primary hrHPV or co-test) q5y
USPSTF 2025 preferred strategy for 30-65?
Primary hrHPV every 5 years (preferred); self-collection on FDA-approved platform now endorsed
When can average-risk screening stop?
Age >65 if no CIN2+ in 25 y AND adequate prior screening (3 neg cytology/10 y or 2 neg HPV-based/10 y)
Post-hysterectomy: when stop screening?
After total hysterectomy (cervix removed) for benign indication with no CIN2+ in 25 y
Does HPV vaccination change the screening schedule?
No - follow age-specific recommendations regardless of vaccination status
Who counts as high-risk for cervical cancer?
Immunocompromised/immunosuppressed: HIV, transplant, SLE, IBD/RA on immunosuppression, chronic steroids
HIV screening: stop at 65?
No - screen with cytology or co-test, continue throughout life
ASCCP 2019 management threshold?
Immediate CIN3+ risk: <4% -> surveillance; >=4% -> colposcopy/treatment ("equal management for equal risk")
ASCCP surveillance intervals (immediate risk <4%)?
ASCCP further management (immediate risk >=4%)?
4-24% colposcopy; 25-59% colposcopy or expedited tx; 60-100% expedited treatment
Cytology result categories (increasing severity)?
NILM -> ASC-US -> LSIL -> ASC-H -> AGC -> HSIL
Excision vs ablation - which is preferred and why?
Excision (LEEP/cone) preferred for histologic HSIL and AIS - yields tissue for pathology
Surveillance after a history of CIN2+?
HPV-based test at 6 mo, then 3 annual HPV-based tests, then q3y for >=25 years (even >65)
lessons.dot:
#Cervical cancer screening: ** yo with a cervix; risk [average / high: HIV·transplant·SLE·IBD/RA-on-immunosupp·prior CIN2+ *]
hx: last screen [date/type *]; prior abnl [none / ASC-US/LSIL/HSIL/CIN* *]; HPV vax [Y/N — does NOT change schedule]
AVERAGE risk:
  21-29 → cytology q3y
  30-65 → primary hrHPV q5y (USPSTF 2025 preferred; FDA-approved self-collect option) [or co-test q5y / cytology q3y]
  stop >65 if no CIN2+ x25y + adequate prior screen (3 neg cytol/10y or 2 neg HPV/10y); post-total-hyst benign + no CIN2+ x25y → stop
HIGH risk (immunosupp/HIV): cytology or co-test, more frequent, CONTINUE FOR LIFE (no stop at 65); HIV <30 cytology only
abnl result → ASCCP app (app.asccp.org), immediate CIN3+ risk:
  <4% → surveillance [5y-risk >0.55→1y / 0.15-0.55→3y / <0.15→5y]
  4-24% → colposcopy ; 25-59% → colpo or expedited tx (per future-pregnancy desire) ; ≥60% → expedited tx (excision>ablation)
hx CIN2+ → HPV-based at 6 mo → 3 annual → q3y x ≥25 y (even >65)
plan: [order * test today] ; reflex cytology if primary HPV+ ; counsel transformation-zone sampling, HPV NL history
takehome: Persistent infection with oncogenic HPV subtypes causes nearly all cervical cancer; >90% arises at the transformation zone.

Average-risk screening: cytology alone, HPV-based testing (primary hrHPV with reflex cytology, or co-test), per age. USPSTF (2025) and ACS prefer primary hrHPV and now endorse self-collection; both agree screening can stop at 65 with adequate prior screening.
Manage abnormal results and set the surveillance interval by the immediate risk of CIN3+, calculated with the ASCCP app (4% threshold).
Immunocompromised/immunosuppressed patients are higher-risk and have unique screening and management protocols — including a lower threshold for colposcopy and lifelong screening in HIV.

extras.label: 2025 Screening Update added
extras.html:
The deck cites the USPSTF 2018 and ACS 2020 guidelines. The risk-based framework is unchanged, but the screening recommendation has moved:

  • USPSTF finalized its update in 2025 (Grade A): for average-risk ages 30–65, primary hrHPV every 5 years is now the preferred strategy; cytology q3y remains an option. Ages 21–29: cytology q3y (unchanged).
  • Self-collection endorsed for the first time: patient-collected hrHPV samples on an FDA-approved platform are non-inferior to clinician-collected (pooled CIN2+ sensitivity ~0.86, specificity ~0.81) — a meaningful access expansion for patients who decline a speculum exam.
  • ACS (2025) recommends starting at age 25 with primary hrHPV and also endorses self-collection.
  • Unchanged: HPV as the cause, ASCCP 2019 risk-based management (4% immediate-CIN3+ threshold, app.asccp.org), distinct HIV/immunosuppressed protocols, and stopping at 65 with adequate prior screening.
Practical note: self-collection requires an FDA-approved primary-HPV assay validated for self-collected specimens — confirm your lab/platform before adopting. Societies still debate the exact self-collection interval.

  9. new ASCCP Practice Advisory: Self-Collection for Cervical Cancer Screening (Oct 2025).
