lessons.title: Cholecystitis (POCUS)
objectives: Identify key structures on RUQ ultrasound — liver, gallbladder, and biliary stones.
Recognize which probe to use for biliary pathology and how to obtain long- and short-axis views.
Differentiate gallstones from sludge and polyps, and recognize the sonographic features of cholecystitis.

prework.q: 1. What single ultrasound feature tells a gallstone apart from sludge or a polyp?
prework.a: A posterior acoustic shadow. Stones cast one; sludge and polyps do not.

prework.q: 2. What is a normal gallbladder wall thickness, and name two non-cholecystitis causes of a thick wall.
prework.a: Normal <3 mm (TG18 dx threshold >4 mm). Non-specific causes: cirrhosis, hepatitis, pancreatitis, hypoalbuminemia.

prework.q: 3. Why do normal LFTs make choledocholithiasis and cholangitis unlikely here?
prework.a: Duct obstruction typically raises LFTs (cholestatic pattern); with normal LFTs, CBD pathology is unlikely and hard-to-image CBD views are low-yield.

segments.label: Hook (2 min)
segments.html: "A 55-year-old woman with severe epigastric pain, afebrile, WBC 15, normal LFTs. You have a probe and 90 seconds. Can you tell whether this is just gallstones, or cholecystitis that buys her a surgery consult — before any formal imaging?"

segments.label: Segment 1 — Anatomy & probe (Objective 1–2)
segments.html:
ASK: "Which probe, and what structures are you looking for?"

Low-frequency curvilinear probe. Find the liver, gallbladder, and the portal triad; sweep in long and short axis. A fasting GB distends and is easier to see; a non-fasting GB is contracted and obscured by bowel gas.

Pearl: Always image the GB in two planes — a single-plane finding can be artifact or an adjacent loop of bowel.

segments.label: Segment 2 — Stone vs sludge vs polyp (Objective 3)
segments.html:
ASK: "Both stones and sludge are echogenic and gravity-dependent — how do you tell them apart?"
EchogenicGravity-dependentAcoustic shadow
GallstoneYes (hyperechoic rim)YesYes
SludgeYesYes (layers)No
PolypYesNo (fixed to wall)No
Pearl: The acoustic shadow is the single most useful feature. Tumefactive sludge can form a mass-like structure and mimic a stone, but it casts no shadow (and rarely obstructs).

segments.label: Segment 3 — Is this cholecystitis? (Objective 3)
segments.html:
ASK: "The GB wall measures 7 mm. Does a thick wall mean cholecystitis?"

Not by itself. Normal wall is <3 mm (TG18 uses >4 mm), but thickening is non-specific — cirrhosis, hepatitis, pancreatitis, and hypoalbuminemia all thicken it. Put it together: stones impacted at the neck, pericholecystic fluid, a positive sonographic Murphy sign, plus systemic inflammation point to cholecystitis.

ASK: "Why is this case reassuring for the bile duct?"

Normal LFTs → choledocholithiasis and cholangitis are unlikely. The CBD is technically hard to image and low-yield when LFTs are normal.

Pearl — TG18: Definite acute cholecystitis = one local sign (A) + one systemic sign (B) + one imaging finding (C).

segments.label: Retrieval close (1 min)
segments.html: Have a learner rebuild it: "shadow = stone," "thick wall is non-specific," and "normal LFTs → don't chase the duct."

lessons.casesIntro: 55-year-old obese woman, severe epigastric pain. Afebrile, WBC 15, normal LFTs. RUQ POCUS shows echogenic material at the GB neck with layering sludge and a 7 mm wall, no pericholecystic fluid.

cases.title: The Case (click to reveal) 1
cases.stem: Overall interpretation & diagnosis?
cases.reveal: Gallstones at the GB neck with sludge and a thickened wall → acute cholecystitis in the right clinical context.

cases.title: The Case (click to reveal) 2
cases.stem: How do you confirm the echogenic material is a stone, not sludge?
cases.reveal: It casts an acoustic shadow; sludge does not.

cases.title: The Case (click to reveal) 3
cases.stem: Does a 7 mm wall confirm cholecystitis?
cases.reveal: No — thickening is non-specific (cirrhosis, hepatitis, pancreatitis, hypoalbuminemia). Combine with stones, neck impaction, sonographic Murphy, and systemic signs.

cases.title: The Case (click to reveal) 4
cases.stem: Why less concern for choledocholithiasis/cholangitis?
cases.reveal: Normal LFTs.

mcqs.stem: On RUQ ultrasound you see echogenic, gravity-dependent material in the gallbladder. Which single feature best confirms it is a gallstone rather than sludge?
mcqs.exp: C. A posterior acoustic shadow is the defining feature of a stone. Sludge is also echogenic, gravity-dependent, mobile, and layering — but casts no shadow. Polyps are echogenic but fixed and shadowless.

mcqs.stem: Which probe is most appropriate for biliary point-of-care ultrasound?
mcqs.exp: B. The low-frequency curvilinear probe provides the depth and field of view needed for abdominal/biliary imaging. Linear probes are high-resolution but too shallow; phased-array is for cardiac windows.

mcqs.stem: A cirrhotic patient with ascites and a serum albumin of 2.1 g/dL has a 5 mm gallbladder wall but no pain or fever. Best interpretation?
opts: Wall thickening is non-specific and likely from hypoalbuminemia/ascites
mcqs.exp: B. GB wall thickening is non-specific — cirrhosis, hepatitis, pancreatitis, and hypoalbuminemia all cause it. Without local/systemic inflammatory signs, this is not cholecystitis. TG18 requires A + B + C.

mcqs.stem: A patient with RUQ pain has gallstones and a positive sonographic Murphy sign, fever, and WBC 16. Per the Tokyo Guidelines (TG18), this combination establishes what?
mcqs.exp: B. Local sign (sonographic Murphy / RUQ tenderness, A) + systemic sign (fever, ↑WBC, B) + imaging (stones, C) = definite acute cholecystitis by TG18. Cholangitis would require evidence of biliary obstruction/cholestasis.

mcqs.stem: A patient with RUQ pain has gallstones on POCUS, normal LFTs, and a non-dilated CBD. Which is the most appropriate next step regarding the bile duct?
opts: No further duct workup — choledocholithiasis is unlikely with normal LFTs
HIDA scan to image the CBD
mcqs.exp: C. With normal LFTs and a non-dilated CBD, choledocholithiasis and cholangitis are unlikely; routine MRCP/ERCP is not warranted. Pursue CBD imaging only if LFTs rise or the duct dilates. (HIDA assesses cystic-duct patency, not the CBD stones.)

anki: What single sonographic feature distinguishes a gallstone from sludge or a polyp?
An acoustic (echogenic) shadow — stones cast one; sludge and polyps do not.
What do gallstones look like on ultrasound?
Gravity-dependent echogenic material with a hyperechoic rim and a posterior acoustic shadow.
What is tumefactive sludge and how is it told apart from a stone?
Discrete, layering echogenic sludge that can mimic a mass; unlike a stone it casts NO acoustic shadow (and rarely obstructs).
What is the normal gallbladder wall thickness?
<3 mm (TG18 uses >4 mm as the diagnostic threshold for cholecystitis).
Besides cholecystitis, what causes a thickened gallbladder wall?
Cirrhosis, hepatitis, pancreatitis, and hypoalbuminemia (low oncotic pressure) — wall thickening is non-specific.
Which probe is used to evaluate biliary pathology?
A low-frequency curvilinear (abdominal) probe.
Why is a non-fasting gallbladder hard to image?
It is contracted, and overlying intestinal gas obscures the view.
What is the sonographic Murphy sign?
Maximal tenderness elicited when the ultrasound probe is pressed directly over the gallbladder — a key bedside POCUS sign of cholecystitis.
What sonographic findings support acute cholecystitis?
Gallstones (often impacted at the GB neck), wall thickening, pericholecystic fluid, GB enlargement, and a positive sonographic Murphy sign.
Why is this case less concerning for choledocholithiasis or cholangitis?
Normal LFTs — these are unlikely with normal liver function tests.
When should you pursue CBD imaging / MRCP?
When LFTs are abnormal; CBD visualization is technically difficult and low-yield with normal LFTs.
What are the TG18 (Tokyo Guidelines 2018) diagnostic components for acute cholecystitis?
A: local inflammation (Murphy's sign, RUQ mass/pain/tenderness); B: systemic inflammation (fever, ↑CRP, ↑WBC); C: imaging findings. Definite dx = one A + one B + one C.
What imaging findings count under TG18 criterion C?
GB wall thickening >4 mm, pericholecystic fluid, gallstones/debris, enlarged GB (long axis ≥8 cm, short axis ≥4 cm), sonographic Murphy sign.
Classic demographic risk for gallstone disease?
The "4 Fs" — female, forty, fertile, overweight (e.g., the 55yo obese woman in this case).
Where are obstructing gallstones typically lodged in cholecystitis?
Impacted at the neck of the gallbladder (cystic duct), causing persistent pain.
lessons.dot:
#POCUS biliary (RUQ) — curvilinear probe, GB long+short axis:
gallstones [present*/absent] — gravity-dep echogenic + hyperechoic rim + ACOUSTIC SHADOW (vs sludge/polyp = no shadow)
location [body/fundus/NECK-impacted*] · sludge [none/layering*] · polyp [none*]
GB wall [<3 nl / *7] mm (TG18 dx >4) — NB nonspecific: cirrhosis/hepatitis/pancreatitis/hypoalbuminemia
pericholecystic fluid [none*/present] · GB size [nl / enlarged LA≥8 SA≥4]
sonographic Murphy [neg/POS*]
CBD [not dilated*/dilated ___mm] · LFTs [normal* → choledocho/cholangitis unlikely / abnl → image CBD, MRCP]
ASSESSMENT (TG18): [biliary colic / acute cholecystitis* = A(local) + B(systemic ↑WBC/CRP/fever) + C(imaging)]
PLAN:
 - [NPO, IVF, analgesia, abx if cholecystitis*]
 - surgery consult for cholecystectomy [early lap chole* / perc cholecystostomy if poor surgical candidate]
 - if abnl LFTs/dilated CBD → MRCP +/- ERCP, eval for cholangitis
AVOID: calling cholecystitis on wall thickness alone; chasing CBD with normal LFTs; single-plane dx
takehome: A gallstone has a hyperechoic rim and, most importantly, an acoustic shadow — that shadow distinguishes it from tumefactive sludge and polyps.

A thickened GB wall (>3 mm, or >4 mm by TG18) is suggestive but non-specific — also seen with cirrhosis, hepatitis, pancreatitis, and hypoalbuminemia. Diagnose cholecystitis by combining local + systemic + imaging signs (TG18).
Normal LFTs make choledocholithiasis and cholangitis unlikely; reserve CBD imaging for abnormal LFTs.

lessons.title: Chronic Atrial Fibrillation Management
lessons.subtitle: Optimized lesson package · Facilitator-led interactive chalk talk for IM residents · 20 min (＋15 min cases)
lessons.credit: Adapted from TeachIM.org — Chronic Atrial Fibrillation Management (Jonathan Lipsey, MD; Kelsey Flint, MD; exec. ed. Yilin Zhang, MD; published Dec 2022), CC BY-NC 4.0. Optimized June 2026: original built on the 2019 ACC/AHA/HRS + 2020 ESC guidelines; the 2023 ACC/AHA/ACCP/HRS guideline updates (AF staging, absolute-risk anticoagulation, catheter ablation as Class 1 first-line, LAAO upgrade, lifestyle pillar) added; pedagogy and assessments added. Companion deck: Chronic_AF_Management_Optimized.pptx (original interactive board, three slides appended).
objectives: Apply principles of stroke-risk assessment and prevention in atrial fibrillation (AF).
Select appropriate anticoagulants in common clinical scenarios.
Describe the goals of rate control in AF and select an appropriate management strategy.
List the factors that favor a rhythm- over a rate-control strategy.

prework.q: Q1. Name the stroke-risk and bleeding-risk tools used in AF, and state when you would start anticoagulation.
prework.a: Stroke risk: CHA₂DS₂-VASc. Bleeding risk: HAS-BLED. Anticoagulate when the estimated annual stroke risk is ≥2%/yr — roughly CHA₂DS₂-VASc ≥2 in men or ≥3 in women (2023 ACC/AHA) — and in all patients around cardioversion regardless of score. A high HAS-BLED is not a reason to withhold anticoagulation.

prework.q: Q2. What is the first-line anticoagulant for most AF, and which patients still need warfarin?
prework.a: A DOAC is first-line for most (more effective, less bleeding than warfarin). Warfarin is required only for mechanical heart valves or moderate–severe mitral stenosis (“valvular” AF). Apixaban is the go-to for ESRD/CrCl <15 or very high BMI.

prework.q: Q3. What heart-rate target defines adequate rate control, and which single factor most favors a rhythm-control strategy?
prework.a: Target HR <110 bpm for most patients (lenient control). Symptoms attributable to AF is the strongest factor favoring rhythm control.

segments.label: Objective 1 — Stroke risk & prevention
segments.html:
ASK: "Patient with AF in front of you — what's the first decision, and what tool drives it?" Elicit: stroke prevention; CHA₂DS₂-VASc. Then click each letter to build the score.
CHA₂DS₂-VAScHAS-BLED
C CHF (incl. asymptomatic LV dysfunction)1H Hypertension (uncontrolled, SBP >160)
H Hypertension (any history)1A Abnormal renal/liver function (1 each)
A₂ Age 65–74 (1) / ≥75 (2)1–2S Stroke history
D Diabetes1B Bleeding history/predisposition
S₂ Stroke/TIA/systemic embolism2L Labile INR (on warfarin)
V Vascular disease (CAD/PAD)1E Elderly (>65)
Sc Sex category (female modifier)1D Drugs (NSAID/antiplatelet) / alcohol
Pearl: The two scores overlap heavily — many stroke risk factors are also bleeding risk factors. Outside of active bleeding, do not withhold anticoagulation for a high HAS-BLED or for frequent falls; high-bleeding-risk patients often gain more absolute benefit. (An “average” elderly patient would need ≈295 falls/year for OAC harm to outweigh benefit — Man-Son-Hing 1999.) Anticoagulation can be started by any generalist — don't wait for cardiology.

segments.label: Objective 2 — Choosing an anticoagulant
segments.html:
ASK each, then reveal: "When do you anticoagulate? Does high bleeding risk change it? First-line agent? Does paroxysmal vs persistent matter? Options if they truly can't take OAC?"
QuestionAnswer
When to anticoagulate?Estimated stroke risk ≥2%/yr → OAC (Class 1) ≈ CHA₂DS₂-VASc ≥2 men/≥3 women; and everyone peri-cardioversion.
High bleeding risk?Generally still anticoagulate — OAC gives >60% relative stroke reduction and >25% mortality reduction; ischemic stroke is usually more disabling than the bleeds counted in risk scores.
First-line agent?A DOAC (Class 1). No DOAC is preferred over another.
Paroxysmal vs persistent?Same stroke risk — anticoagulate paroxysmal AF (and after successful cardioversion) if CHA₂DS₂-VASc qualifies.
Can't tolerate OAC?LAA occlusion (WATCHMAN; Class 2a) or surgical LAA ligation during other cardiac surgery (continue OAC).
Clinical scenarios that change the choice: Valvular AF = mechanical valve or moderate–severe MS → warfarin (everything else, incl. AS/MR/bioprosthetic, takes a DOAC). Obesity: apixaban/rivaroxaban OK to BMI ~50; shared decision above 50. Cardioversion: 3 weeks of OAC (or TEE) before if AF >48 h/unknown, then 4 weeks after regardless of score (atrial stunning). Frequent falls: not a reason to withhold. Stable CAD (>1 yr from MI/PCI): OAC alone — adding aspirin only adds bleeding. CrCl <15/ESRD: apixaban or warfarin.

segments.label: Objective 3 — Rate control
segments.html:
ASK: "What's the heart-rate target, and what three drug classes get you there?" Reveal: <110 bpm; beta-blocker, non-DHP CCB, digoxin.

Target HR <110 bpm for most (lenient control was non-inferior to strict in RACE II). Aim lower if symptomatic, declining LV function, or tachycardia-induced cardiomyopathy. All three classes slow the AV node:

ClassNotes
Beta-blockersMetoprolol, carvedilol, bisoprolol, atenolol. Prefer guideline BBs (metoprolol succinate, carvedilol, bisoprolol) in HFrEF; caution in decompensated HF and in asthma (COPD usually tolerates).
Non-DHP CCBDiltiazem most common. Contraindicated in HFrEF (strong negative inotrope).
DigoxinAdjunct, not first-line. Useful in decompensated HFrEF (positive inotrope). Renally cleared — toxicity risk in renal dysfunction.
ProceduralAV-node ablation ＋ pacemaker for patients refractory to rate and rhythm control (often tachycardia-induced cardiomyopathy).

segments.label: Objective 4 — Rhythm control
segments.html:
ASK: "What pushes you toward rhythm control rather than just rate control?"

Factors favoring rhythm control: younger age, fewer comorbidities, recent-onset AF, symptoms attributable to AF (strongest), inability to rate control, tachycardia-induced cardiomyopathy, and LV dysfunction of any cause. Methods: electrical cardioversion, antiarrhythmic drugs, and ablation.

Antiarrhythmic toxicities to know: Amiodarone — lung, liver, thyroid (monitor PFTs/LFTs/TFTs). Sotalol & dofetilide — QT prolongation/Torsades, so inpatient initiation on telemetry; renally dosed. Flecainide — contraindicated in structural/coronary heart disease. 2023 change: catheter ablation is now Class 1 first-line in selected symptomatic paroxysmal AF and in AF + HFrEF, so cardiology referral happens earlier than the older rate-first reflex.

segments.label: Cases
segments.html: Work the case set below in small groups (encourage sparctool.com for risk estimates); reveal answers one at a time. Close by hiding the board and having the group rebuild the four take-home points from memory.

cases.title: Case 1 — evolving over time (1a → 1b → 1c)
cases.stem: 1a. 59 M with HLD and OA on chronic NSAIDs presents with dyspnea on exertion; found in AF, HR 115, BMI 45. TTE: LVEF 58%, no valve disease.
cases.reveal: Rate: goal HR <110 — start a beta-blocker (e.g., metoprolol) or diltiazem (LVEF normal, so diltiazem is allowed). Anticoagulation: CHA₂DS₂-VASc 0, HAS-BLED 1 (NSAIDs) — not indicated.

cases.title: Case 1 (cardioversion branch)
cases.stem: The same patient is scheduled for cardioversion.
cases.reveal: Before: 3 weeks of OAC, or a TEE at the time of cardioversion, if AF >48 h/unknown. After: 4 weeks of OAC even if CHA₂DS₂-VASc is 0 (atrial stunning). Given BMI 45, use apixaban or rivaroxaban.

cases.title: Case 2 — stroke prevention across patients
cases.stem: Choose a stroke-prevention strategy for each: (a) 75 M, HTN, moderate–severe aortic stenosis; (b) 55 F, severe asthma, normal LV; (c) 82 F, HTN, HLD, CKD IV, baseline Cr 2.4.
cases.reveal:
(a) CHA₂DS₂-VASc 3 → anticoagulate with any DOAC; AS is NOT “valvular” AF (only mechanical valve or moderate–severe mitral stenosis is).
(b) For rate control here, avoid beta-blocker (severe airflow obstruction) → use diltiazem.
(c) CHA₂DS₂-VASc 4 → apixaban 2.5 mg BID (preferred at CrCl <15). Reduced apixaban dose if any 2 of: age ≥80, weight ≤60 kg, or Cr ≥1.5.

cases.title: Case 3 — rate control in HFrEF, and a high-risk anticoagulation call
cases.stem: (a) 75 M with HFrEF (EF 38%); (b) 62 M, severe obesity BMI 55, DM2, PAD; (c) 62 M with mildly decompensated HFrEF on metoprolol succinate 50 mg, HR >120, normal renal function — then later started on digoxin with up-titrated metoprolol, still HR uncontrolled, SBP 90–100; (d) 78 F with prior CVA (residual left-sided weakness) on aspirin, CAD, frequent falls, and prior NSAID-related GI bleed needing transfusion.
cases.reveal:
(a) Evidence-based beta-blocker (metoprolol succinate, carvedilol, or bisoprolol); avoid diltiazem in HFrEF.
(b) CHA₂DS₂-VASc 2 → anticoagulate. BMI >50: discuss warfarin vs apixaban/rivaroxaban; limited data but BMI is not an absolute DOAC contraindication — consult a pharmacist.
(c) Cautious BB up-titration vs add digoxin as adjunct (HFrEF); when hypotension limits BB and digoxin fails → cardiology referral for rhythm control or AV-node ablation + pacemaker.
(d) CHA₂DS₂-VASc 4 (≈4.8%/yr), HAS-BLED 4 (≈8.9%/yr). Still anticoagulate — disabling-stroke risk outweighs non-major bleeding; any DOAC. Falls are not a reason to withhold.

mcqs.stem: A 70-year-old woman with hypertension and diabetes has newly documented paroxysmal AF. She has never had a stroke or bleeding. Which is the most appropriate stroke-prevention strategy?
opts: No therapy because her AF is only paroxysmal
mcqs.exp: B. CHA₂DS₂-VASc = 4 (HTN 1, DM 1, age 65–74 1, female 1) → well above the ≥2%/yr threshold, so anticoagulate. A DOAC is first-line over warfarin (C is wrong — paroxysmal AF carries the same stroke risk). Aspirin is not recommended for stroke prevention in AF (A). Warfarin (D) is reserved for mechanical valves or moderate–severe mitral stenosis.

mcqs.stem: A 68-year-old man with AF and HFrEF (EF 30%) has a resting HR of 120 despite an attempt at rate control. Which agent is contraindicated for rate control?
mcqs.exp: B. Non-dihydropyridine CCBs such as diltiazem are contraindicated in HFrEF (strong negative inotropes). Guideline beta-blockers (A, C) are appropriate, and digoxin (D) is a useful adjunct in HFrEF because it is a positive inotrope.

mcqs.stem: A 60-year-old man with persistent AF for an unknown duration is scheduled for elective cardioversion. He is not currently anticoagulated. What is required?
opts: Cardiovert now; start a DOAC only if CHA₂DS₂-VASc ≥2
3 weeks of anticoagulation beforehand (or a TEE), then 4 weeks after regardless of score
Aspirin before and after the procedure
No anticoagulation because cardioversion restores sinus rhythm
mcqs.exp: B. With AF >48 h or unknown duration, give 3 weeks of OAC or perform a TEE to exclude LAA thrombus before cardioversion, then 4 weeks of OAC afterward regardless of CHA₂DS₂-VASc because of post-cardioversion atrial stunning. The other options under-treat a high-risk peri-cardioversion window.

mcqs.stem: A 55-year-old man with symptomatic paroxysmal AF, no structural heart disease, remains highly symptomatic despite a beta-blocker and wants to avoid lifelong antiarrhythmics. Per the 2023 ACC/AHA guideline, which is now a reasonable first-line rhythm-control option?
mcqs.exp: A. The 2023 guideline upgraded catheter ablation to Class 1 first-line rhythm control for selected younger, symptomatic paroxysmal-AF patients with few comorbidities (and for AF + HFrEF). Digoxin (B) is a rate-control adjunct; antiplatelets (C) don't prevent AF stroke; AV-node ablation + pacemaker (D) is a last-line rate-control strategy, not first-line rhythm control.

mcqs.stem: An 80-year-old woman with AF (CHA₂DS₂-VASc 5) has had two falls and a prior NSAID-related GI bleed. She and her family worry that anticoagulation is too dangerous. What is the best advice?
opts: Withhold anticoagulation given her falls and bleeding history
Use aspirin instead of an anticoagulant
Anticoagulate — stroke risk and disability outweigh fall/bleeding risk; address modifiable bleeding factors
Place a WATCHMAN device first-line before trying any anticoagulant
mcqs.exp: C. Falls and an elevated HAS-BLED are not reasons to withhold anticoagulation; the disability from an AF-related stroke generally outweighs the non-major bleeding counted in risk scores, and modifiable factors (stop NSAIDs, control BP) should be optimized. Aspirin (B) isn't effective stroke prevention in AF. LAA occlusion (D) is Class 2a only when there is a genuine long-term contraindication to anticoagulation, not as a reflexive first move.

anki: What score estimates stroke risk in AF, and what are its components?
CHA₂DS₂-VASc: CHF (1), Hypertension (1), Age 65–74 (1)/≥75 (2), Diabetes (1), Stroke/TIA/embolism (2), Vascular disease (1), Sex category–female (1).
2023 ACC/AHA threshold to start anticoagulation in AF?
Annual stroke risk ≥2%/yr → OAC (Class 1) ≈ CHA₂DS₂-VASc ≥2 in men / ≥3 in women. Score 1 (men)/2 (women): reasonable (Class 2a). Use the score to estimate risk, not as a rigid cutoff.
First-line anticoagulant for most AF, and why?
A DOAC — more effective and lower bleeding risk than warfarin (Class 1). Guidelines do not prefer one DOAC over another.
What counts as "valvular" AF (requires warfarin, not a DOAC)?
Only moderate–severe mitral stenosis or a mechanical heart valve. Everything else (AS, MR, bioprosthetic) is non-valvular and can take a DOAC.
Which DOAC for high BMI or ESRD/CrCl <15?
Apixaban (rivaroxaban also reasonable up to BMI ~50). Apixaban or warfarin for CrCl <15/ESRD.
Should high bleeding risk or frequent falls stop anticoagulation in AF?

No. Outside active bleeding, don't withhold OAC for HAS-BLED alone; high-bleeding-risk patients often gain more absolute benefit. A typical elderly patient would need ~295 falls/year for harm to outweigh benefit.
Bleeding risk tool in AF and its components?
HAS-BLED: Hypertension (uncontrolled, SBP >160), Abnormal renal/liver function, Stroke, Bleeding history/predisposition, Labile INR, Elderly (>65), Drugs/alcohol.
Anticoagulation around cardioversion?
If AF >48 h or unknown duration: 3 weeks of OAC OR a TEE before cardioversion; then 4 weeks of OAC after, regardless of CHA₂DS₂-VASc (atrial stunning).
Target heart rate for rate control in AF?
<110 bpm for most (lenient control; RACE II). Aim lower if symptomatic, declining LV function, or tachycardia-induced cardiomyopathy.
Three drug classes for rate control in AF?
Beta-blockers, non-dihydropyridine calcium channel blockers (diltiazem), and digoxin (adjunct). All slow the AV node.
Which rate-control drug is contraindicated in HFrEF?
Diltiazem (non-DHP CCB) — strong negative inotrope. Use a guideline beta-blocker (metoprolol succinate, carvedilol, bisoprolol); digoxin is a useful adjunct (positive inotrope).
Factors favoring a rhythm- over rate-control strategy?
Younger age, fewer comorbidities, recent-onset AF, symptoms attributable to AF (strongest), inability to rate control, tachycardia-induced cardiomyopathy, and LV dysfunction of any cause.
2023 guideline change for catheter ablation?
Upgraded to Class 1 first-line rhythm control in selected symptomatic paroxysmal AF (younger, few comorbidities) AND in AF with HFrEF.
Key toxicities of common antiarrhythmics?

Amiodarone: lung/liver/thyroid (monitor PFTs, LFTs, TFTs). Sotalol & dofetilide: QT prolongation/Torsades (inpatient initiation), renally dosed. Flecainide: contraindicated in structural/coronary heart disease.
New 2023 AF staging system?

Stage 1 (at risk) → 2 (pre-AF) → 3 (AF: 3A paroxysmal, 3B persistent, 3C long-standing persistent, 3D post-ablation) → 4 (permanent). Replaces duration-only labels and frames AF as a progressive disease.
When is left atrial appendage occlusion (e.g., WATCHMAN) reasonable?
Class 2a (2023) for patients with a long-term contraindication to oral anticoagulation and elevated stroke risk; clots in AF usually form in the LAA.
Lifestyle/risk-factor targets that improve AF control?
Weight loss to BMI <27 (>10% loss), reduce/eliminate alcohol, control BP and lipids, screen and treat OSA, smoking cessation, optimize activity.
lessons.dot:
#Atrial fibrillation (chronic), * [paroxysmal/persistent/long-standing/permanent; 2023 stage *]. Plan: stroke prevention + rate vs rhythm + risk-factor mod.
- STROKE RISK: CHA2DS2-VASc * [C/H/A2/D/S2/V/Sc] = * → anticoagulate if ≥2%/yr (≈≥2 men / ≥3 women; Class 1). Peri-cardioversion: anticoag x4wk regardless of score. ASA NOT for stroke prevention.
- BLEEDING: HAS-BLED * — do NOT withhold OAC for high score or falls alone; optimize modifiable factors (BP, stop NSAIDs).
- ANTICOAG: DOAC 1st-line * [apixaban / rivaroxaban / dabigatran / edoxaban]. apixaban if CrCl<15/ESRD or BMI>40-50. WARFARIN only if mechanical valve or mod-severe MS.
- CARDIOVERSION: if AF >48h/unknown → 3wk OAC or TEE first; then 4wk OAC after (atrial stunning).
- RATE CONTROL: goal HR <110 * → BB [metop succinate / carvedilol / bisoprolol] OR diltiazem (AVOID diltiazem in HFrEF); digoxin adjunct (renally cleared, watch tox). Refractory → AV-node ablation + PPM.
- RHYTHM CONTROL if *: symptoms attributable to AF / young / few comorbid / recent onset / tachy-cardiomyopathy / LV dysfunction → cardiology. Catheter ablation Class 1 1st-line in select parox AF & AF+HFrEF.
- AAD * [amiodarone (PFT/LFT/TFT q monitoring) / sotalol·dofetilide (QT, inpatient start, renal dosing) / flecainide (NO structural/CAD)].
- LAAO [WATCHMAN] 2a if long-term OAC contraindication.
- RISK-FACTOR MOD: wt loss → BMI <27, reduce/stop EtOH, BP & lipid control, OSA screen, tobacco cessation.
takehome: Use CHA₂DS₂-VASc to estimate annual stroke risk; anticoagulate at ≥2%/yr (≥2 men / ≥3 women) and in all patients peri-cardioversion regardless of score. A high HAS-BLED or falls is not a reason to withhold.
DOACs are first-line for most AF; apixaban for high BMI and ESRD/CrCl <15; warfarin only for a mechanical valve or moderate–severe mitral stenosis.
Rate-control to HR <110 for most (beta-blocker or non-DHP CCB; digoxin adjunct). Diltiazem is contraindicated in HFrEF.
Persistent symptoms most favor rhythm control; the 2023 guideline makes catheter ablation Class 1 first-line in selected patients and in AF + HFrEF.

extras.label: 2024 Update — 2023 ACC/AHA/ACCP/HRS AF Guideline NEW — teach with current framing
extras.html:
The original lesson draws on the 2019 ACC/AHA/HRS and 2020 ESC guidelines. The 2023 ACC/AHA/ACCP/HRS guideline (Joglar et al., Circulation 2024) keeps the core teaching intact but reframes several points. Use this when you teach; the board's content is still excellent for the physiology and the scoring.

TopicWhat changed in 2023
AF classificationNew stages 1–4 (1 at-risk → 2 pre-AF → 3 AF [3A paroxysmal, 3B persistent, 3C long-standing, 3D post-ablation] → 4 permanent). Frames AF as a progressive disease rather than just by episode duration.
When to anticoagulateDecide by absolute risk: ≥2%/yr stroke risk → anticoagulate (Class 1) ≈ CHA₂DS₂-VASc ≥2 men/≥3 women. Score 1 (men)/2 (women) is now “reasonable” (Class 2a). Use the score to estimate risk, not as a rigid on/off switch; aspirin is not recommended for stroke prevention.
DOAC vs warfarinDOAC over warfarin is Class 1; warfarin reserved for mechanical valve or moderate–severe MS. The label “nonvalvular AF” is de-emphasized in favor of naming the specific valve lesion.
Catheter ablationUpgraded to Class 1 first-line rhythm control in selected symptomatic paroxysmal AF (younger, few comorbidities) and in AF with HFrEF (mortality/HF-hospitalization benefit).
Lifestyle / risk factorsNow a named pillar: weight loss (target BMI <27), alcohol reduction, BP and lipid control, OSA screening — improves success of both rate and rhythm control.
LAA occlusionWATCHMAN-type devices upgraded to Class 2a for patients with a long-term contraindication to anticoagulation.

Currency checkOriginal drew on the 2019 ACC/AHA/HRS + 2020 ESC guidelines. Verified June 2026: the 2023 ACC/AHA/ACCP/HRS AF guideline (Joglar et al., Circulation 2024) updates — AF stages 1–4, absolute-risk anticoagulation (≥2%/yr = Class 1), DOAC Class 1, catheter ablation Class 1 first-line (selected paroxysmal AF and AF+HFrEF), LAAO upgraded to 2a, and a lifestyle/risk-factor pillar. Core teaching (scoring, rate target, DOAC-first) unchanged.
CorrectionsNone — no factual or numeric errors found in the original deck or facilitator guide.
Content added“2024 update” section + an appended deck slide summarizing the 2023 guideline; antiarrhythmic toxicities and cardioversion/CKD/obesity scenarios consolidated into the script.
DeckAppend-only edit of a copy: original interactive animations, graphics, and slide order preserved; three slides added (2023 update, take-home points, attribution/changelog).
PedagogyAdded 5-min pre-work; timed segments with ASK pause points keyed to the board's cursor prompts; the original cases converted to click-to-reveal; retrieval-based close.
AssessmentAdded 5 ABIM-style MCQs with explanations and 17 Anki cards.
Clinical toolAdded an attending EMR dotphrase (CHA₂DS₂-VASc/HAS-BLED, anticoag pick-list, cardioversion rule, rate/rhythm strategy, AAD monitoring, LAAO, risk-factor mod).
One-pagerAdded a dense single-page printable clinical reference (PDF).
Companion filesOriginal facilitator guide and AF summary graphic copied into this folder and linked (reviewed — no changes needed). The TeachIM “Learner's Summary Handout” is distributed on the website and was not among the source files.
Human review neededA clinician should confirm the 2023-guideline framing (staging, ablation Class 1, LAAO 2a) and local anticoagulation practice before teaching.

lessons.title: Chronic Heart Failure Management
lessons.subtitle: Optimized lesson package · Facilitator-led interactive chalk talk for IM residents · 30 min (＋10 min cases)
lessons.credit: Adapted from TeachIM.org — Chronic Heart Failure Management (Jonathan Lipsey, MD; Kelsey Flint, MD; sect. ed. Brandon Fainstad, MD; exec. ed. Yilin Zhang, MD; published June 2023), CC BY-NC 4.0. Optimized June 2026: original built on the 2022 AHA/ACC/HFSA guideline — HFrEF quadruple therapy verified unchanged; developments since publication (2023 ESC SGLT2i Class 1A in HFmrEF/HFpEF, finerenone FDA-approved for LVEF ≥40% in 2025, GLP-1/tirzepatide for HFpEF+obesity) added; pedagogy and assessments added. Companion deck: Chronic_HF_Management_Optimized.pptx (original interactive board, three slides appended).
objectives: Develop a systematic process for the evaluation and classification of a patient with chronic heart failure (HF).
Describe evidence-based therapies for HF with reduced ejection fraction (HFrEF) and their impact on patient outcomes.
Initiate and titrate guideline-directed medical therapy (GDMT) for HFrEF based on patient characteristics and side effects.
Determine guideline-directed therapies for HFpEF, HFmrEF, and HF with improved EF.

prework.q: Q1. Name the four EF-based HF categories with their cut-offs, and which one is defined by trajectory rather than a single value.
prework.a: HFrEF LVEF ≤40%; HFmrEF 41–49%; HFpEF ≥50%; HFimpEF — a history of LVEF ≤40% that has improved by ≥10 points to >40% (defined by trajectory, not a single value). HFimpEF patients should continue GDMT indefinitely.

prework.q: Q2. List the four GDMT pillars for HFrEF and the approximate combined mortality benefit at target doses.
prework.a: Beta-blocker (evidence-based: metoprolol succinate, carvedilol, bisoprolol) + ARNI (preferred) or ACEi/ARB + MRA (spironolactone/eplerenone) + SGLT2 inhibitor (dapagliflozin/empagliflozin). Combined ~73% relative mortality reduction at target doses — yet only ~1% of real-world patients reach all target doses (CHAMP-HF).

prework.q: Q3. A patient on GDMT has a 20% rise in creatinine after you start an ACEi and an SGLT2i. Stop them?
prework.a: No. A mild GFR decline (<30%) when starting ACEi/ARB/ARNI or SGLT2i is expected and does not warrant dose reduction. Reassess volume status and diuretic dose for true AKI before touching GDMT.

segments.label: Objective 1 — The HF one-liner
segments.html:
ASK: "You open a chart before a clinic visit. What three descriptors should be in your HF one-liner?" Elicit: NYHA class, ejection fraction, etiology. Click each button to build it.
ComponentCategories
NYHA class (symptom burden)I asymptomatic · II symptoms with normal activity · III symptoms with less-than-ordinary activity · IV symptoms at rest/ADLs. Predicts mortality but is not a strict treatment cut-off (don't withhold GDMT in an asymptomatic patient with LV dysfunction).
Ejection fractionHFrEF ≤40% · HFmrEF 41–49% · HFpEF ≥50% · HFimpEF (was ≤40%, improved ≥10 pts to >40%). The 2022 guideline consolidated HFrEF therapy indications to LVEF ≤40%.
EtiologyIschemic (most common, ~50–60%) vs non-ischemic (HTN, toxin/alcohol, tachycardia-induced, infectious, infiltrative, genetic/idiopathic). Distinction mainly guides revascularization evaluation; GDMT is largely the same regardless of cause.
Pearl: "NYHA class III HFrEF, likely ischemic" tells you the symptom severity, the therapy class, and whether to think about revascularization — all in one line.

segments.label: Objective 2 — Why GDMT matters & the four pillars
segments.html:
ASK each, then reveal the number: "How many Americans have HF? One-year mortality untreated? Mortality reduction with full GDMT? Percent of real patients on target doses?"
QuestionAnswer
HF prevalence (US)~6 million
1-yr mortality untreated~25% (up to 25–50% in original ACEi placebo arms)
Mortality reduction with target-dose GDMT~73% combined
Patients actually on target doses~1% (CHAMP-HF registry)

The four HFrEF pillars, each with mortality + hospitalization benefit:

ClassAgents · target doseLandmark trials · NNT (3-yr mortality)
Beta-blockerMetoprolol succinate 200 mg daily · carvedilol 25–50 mg BID · bisoprololMERIT-HF, US Carvedilol, COMET · NNT 9
ARNI (preferred over ACEi/ARB)Sacubitril-valsartan 97/103 mg BIDPARADIGM-HF (vs ACEi) · NNT 27
ACEi / ARBLisinopril 40 mg daily · losartan 150 mg dailySOLVD, CONSENSUS · NNT 26
MRASpironolactone 25–50 mg daily (eplerenone if gynecomastia)RALES, EMPHASIS-HF · NNT 6
SGLT2 inhibitorDapagliflozin 10 mg · empagliflozin 10 mg dailyDAPA-HF, EMPEROR-Reduced · NNT 22

segments.label: Objective 3 — Initiate, titrate & troubleshoot
segments.html:
ASK: "When can you safely start each pillar?" Reveal the conditions, then work the three common roadblocks.
PillarWhen to initiate / key contraindication
Beta-blockerOnly when compensated (euvolemic, adequately perfused) — negative inotropy worsens acute decompensation. Continuation through mild decompensation is generally safe.
ARNIPreferred over ACEi/ARB; can start de novo (no need to start ACEi first). Needs SBP >100; 36-h washout off an ACEi; transition from an ARB can be immediate. Can initiate inpatient (PIONEER-HF). Watch cost.
ACEi / ARBStable creatinine and potassium. ARB often chosen to facilitate later ARNI transition.
MRAHold if K⁺ >5, GFR <30, or Cr >2.5 (men)/>2 (women). Can start early alongside loops to spare potassium supplementation.
SGLT2 inhibitorAvoid if GFR <20 (empagliflozin; <30 dapagliflozin), type 1 DM, or DKA history. Caution with recurrent UTIs. Cost can be a barrier.
Titration philosophy: there is no fixed order — start low doses of all four and up-titrate every 1–2 weeks (faster is safe: STRONG-HF). The single most important rule is to add or titrate something at every visit. Monitor HR, BP, BMP, and volume status.
RoadblockHow to keep GDMT going
AKIExpect a mild GFR dip with ACEi/ARB/ARNI + SGLT2i — <30% drop is OK. For true AKI: reassess volume and adjust diuretics first; temporarily reduce ACEi/ARB/ARNI and re-trial after resolution; hydralazine + isosorbide is a renal-sparing afterload option.
HypotensionTreat symptoms, not the number — asymptomatic SBP 90–100 is an acceptable target. Avoid non-GDMT antihypertensives (CCB, thiazide), stagger dosing AM/PM, switch carvedilol → metoprolol succinate (less alpha-blockade).
HyperkalemiaSGLT2i and ARNI mitigate K⁺; counsel low-K diet; hydralazine + isosorbide as a last resort if ACEi/ARB/ARNI truly can't be tolerated.

segments.label: Other therapies — HFrEF (device & vasodilator)
segments.html:
After ≥3 months of maximally tolerated GDMT, re-image. If EF remains <35%:

TriggerTherapy · NNT (3-yr mortality)
Wide QRS (>120–150 ms, esp. LBBB)CRT (biventricular pacing) · NNT 8 (MIRACLE, COMPANION, MADIT-CRT)
Life expectancy >1 yr, EF <35%ICD (single-chamber or CRT-D) for sudden-death prevention · NNT 23 (MADIT-II, SCD-HeFT)
Self-identified African-American on background therapyHydralazine + isosorbide dinitrate (Class 1a) · NNT 8 (A-HeFT) — high pill burden; also useful when ACEi/ARB/ARNI can't be used. Note the inherent limitations of race-based recommendations.

segments.label: Objective 4 — Across the EF spectrum
segments.html:
ASK: "What do you actually offer a patient with HFpEF? With HFmrEF? Someone whose EF recovered?"
CategoryGuideline-recommended therapy
HFimpEFContinue full GDMT indefinitely — TRED-HF showed >40% of patients relapsed after withdrawal vs none who continued.
HFmrEFSGLT2i recommended. Consider HFrEF-style therapies depending on EF and trajectory (a declining 41% behaves like HFrEF; a stable 49% may not).
HFpEFSGLT2i reduce HF hospitalizations (EMPEROR-Preserved, DELIVER) — 2a in 2022 US guideline, 1A in 2023 ESC. MRA/ARNI 2b (TOPCAT, PARAGON-HF — negative primaries, hospitalization signal). Since publication: finerenone FDA-approved for LVEF ≥40% (2025); GLP-1/tirzepatide improve HFpEF + obesity — see the update section.

segments.label: Cases
segments.html: Work the three original cases below in small groups; reveal answers one at a time. Close by hiding the board and having the group rebuild the take-home points from memory.

cases.title: Case 1 — HFrEF with a wide QRS
cases.stem: 63 M with HTN, CAD s/p CABG, recurrent volume-overload admissions; cannot dress or walk to the kitchen without dyspnea. Meds: ASA 81, metoprolol tartrate 50 BID, lisinopril 40, furosemide 40 BID. BP 100/60, euvolemic. Cr 1.3, K 3.2. TTE: LVEF 30%, apical/anterior wall-motion abnormality. ECG: LBBB, QRS 150 ms.
cases.reveal: NYHA class IV HFrEF (EF <40%), likely ischemic cardiomyopathy (focal WMA, prior CABG).

cases.title: Case 2 — recovered EF (HFimpEF)
cases.stem: 27 M with HF, no other history; climbs several flights without limitation. TTE 3 yr ago LVEF 25% (global hypokinesis); TTE 3 mo ago LVEF 50%. Cr 0.8, K 4.2. BP 110/60, HR 88. Meds: metoprolol succinate 100, losartan 150.
cases.reveal: NYHA class I HFimpEF, likely non-ischemic cardiomyopathy (young, global hypokinesis).

cases.title: Case 3 — HFpEF
cases.stem: 67 with HTN, CKD, DM2, AF, OSA, HF; dyspnea walking to the bathroom, not at rest. BP 140/80, HR 90. Cr 1.3, K 4.1. Meds: metoprolol tartrate 50 BID, lisinopril 40, metformin 1 g BID, glipizide 5, furosemide 40 daily. TTE: LVEF 62%, LVH, LA enlargement, grade II diastolic dysfunction.
cases.reveal: NYHA class III HFpEF, likely from long-standing hypertension (LVH).

mcqs.stem: A 58-year-old man with newly diagnosed HFrEF (LVEF 28%), now euvolemic on a loop diuretic, has BP 118/72, HR 78, Cr 1.1, K⁺ 4.3. Which regimen best reflects guideline-directed therapy?
opts: Lisinopril alone, then add others only if symptoms persist
Begin low doses of a beta-blocker, an ARNI, an MRA, and an SGLT2 inhibitor, then up-titrate
Wait until a follow-up echo in 6 months before starting disease-modifying therapy
mcqs.exp: B. All four pillars provide additive mortality benefit, so the modern approach is to start low doses of all four in a compensated patient and titrate up — not a sequential "fail one, add the next" strategy (A). Digoxin (C) is symptomatic only, with no mortality benefit. Delaying therapy (D) forgoes early benefit; GDMT should start once the patient is compensated.

mcqs.stem: A 64-year-old woman with HFrEF on an ACE inhibitor is being switched to sacubitril-valsartan. What is required for a safe transition?
opts: Start the ARNI immediately after the last ACEi dose
A 36-hour washout after stopping the ACEi
Discontinue her beta-blocker during the switch
mcqs.exp: B. A 36-hour washout off the ACEi is required before starting an ARNI to avoid overlapping angioedema risk (no washout is needed when switching from an ARB). The ARNI is indicated in HFrEF, not preserved EF (C), and the beta-blocker should be continued (D).

mcqs.stem: A 70-year-old man with HFrEF (EF 32%) starts dapagliflozin and lisinopril. Two weeks later his creatinine has risen from 1.2 to 1.5 mg/dL (≈25%). He is asymptomatic and euvolemic. Best next step?
opts: Continue both; a mild GFR decline is expected and acceptable
mcqs.exp: B. A creatinine rise of <30% after starting an ACEi/ARB/ARNI or SGLT2 inhibitor is expected hemodynamic change, not true AKI, and does not warrant stopping or dose-reducing therapy in an asymptomatic euvolemic patient. Stopping (A, C) or admitting (D) would forgo proven benefit.

mcqs.stem: A 60-year-old man with persistent HFrEF (EF 30%) on maximally tolerated GDMT for 4 months has a QRS of 156 ms with LBBB morphology and a life expectancy >1 year. Which intervention has the strongest indication?
opts: Cardiac resynchronization therapy (often with a defibrillator)
Switch the ARNI back to an ACE inhibitor
mcqs.exp: A. Persistent EF <35% after an adequate GDMT trial, with a wide QRS in an LBBB pattern, is the classic indication for CRT (NNT 8), typically combined with an ICD given the EF. The other options do not address dyssynchrony and offer no comparable mortality benefit.

mcqs.stem: A 72-year-old woman with HFpEF (LVEF 58%), obesity (BMI 36), and class III symptoms is already on a loop diuretic. Which therapy now has guideline support to reduce heart-failure events in HFpEF?
mcqs.exp: A. SGLT2 inhibitors reduce HF hospitalizations in HFpEF (EMPEROR-Preserved, DELIVER) — 2a in the 2022 US guideline and Class 1A in the 2023 ESC update. Digoxin (B) and nitrates (C) have no HFpEF outcome benefit. MRAs have a 2b recommendation with a hospitalization signal but no proven mortality benefit in HFpEF, so "routine for mortality benefit" (D) overstates the evidence. (Newer options since publication: finerenone, FDA-approved for LVEF ≥40% in 2025, and GLP-1/tirzepatide for HFpEF + obesity.)

anki: Three descriptors in an HF "one-liner"?
NYHA class (symptom burden) + ejection fraction category + etiology (ischemic vs non-ischemic).
EF cut-offs: HFrEF / HFmrEF / HFpEF / HFimpEF?
HFrEF ≤40%; HFmrEF 41–49%; HFpEF ≥50%; HFimpEF = prior ≤40% improved ≥10 pts to >40%.
Four GDMT pillars for HFrEF?
Beta-blocker + ARNI (or ACEi/ARB) + MRA + SGLT2 inhibitor. ~73% combined mortality reduction at target doses.
Evidence-based beta-blockers in HFrEF?
Metoprolol succinate, carvedilol, bisoprolol. (Metoprolol tartrate is NOT evidence-based for HFrEF.)
ARNI vs ACEi: what washout is required?
36-h washout after stopping an ACEi before starting an ARNI; switching from an ARB can be immediate. ARNI is preferred over ACEi/ARB.
MRA contraindications?
K+ >5, GFR <30, or Cr >2.5 (men)/>2 (women).
SGLT2i contraindications in HF?
GFR <20 (empagliflozin; <30 dapagliflozin), type 1 DM, prior DKA; caution with recurrent UTIs.
Is a <30% creatinine rise on ACEi/ARNI + SGLT2i a reason to stop?
No — expected hemodynamic change. Reassess volume/diuretics for true AKI before reducing GDMT.
How often to titrate GDMT, and the core rule?
Every 1–2 weeks (faster is safe — STRONG-HF). Add or titrate something at every visit.
Managing hypotension on GDMT?
Treat symptoms not the number (asymptomatic SBP 90–100 OK); avoid CCB/thiazide; stagger dosing; carvedilol → metoprolol succinate.
Persistent EF <35% after ≥3 mo GDMT + wide QRS/LBBB?
Cardiac resynchronization therapy (CRT), NNT 8; add ICD given EF (CRT-D).
ICD indication in HFrEF?
EF <35% after adequate GDMT with life expectancy >1 yr — sudden-death prevention (MADIT-II, SCD-HeFT), NNT 23.
Hydralazine + isosorbide dinitrate — who?
Class 1a in self-identified African-Americans on background GDMT (A-HeFT, NNT 8); also when ACEi/ARB/ARNI can't be used.
HFimpEF management?
Continue full GDMT indefinitely — TRED-HF showed >40% relapse after withdrawal vs none who continued.
Guideline therapy for HFmrEF and HFpEF?
SGLT2 inhibitor (reduces HF hospitalizations; 2a US 2022, 1A ESC 2023). MRA/ARNI 2b in HFpEF.
Finerenone in HF — what changed (2024–25)?
FINEARTS-HF (NEJM 2024): cut CV death/worsening HF in LVEF ≥40%. FDA-approved July 2025 for HF with LVEF ≥40% — first MRA proven beneficial in HFmrEF/HFpEF.
Obesity drugs in HFpEF?
STEP-HFpEF (semaglutide, 2023) improved symptoms/function; SUMMIT (tirzepatide, 2025) cut CV death/worsening HF ~38% in HFpEF + obesity.
lessons.dot:
#Chronic heart failure: NYHA * [I/II/III/IV], * [HFrEF ≤40 / HFmrEF 41-49 / HFpEF ≥50 / HFimpEF], * [ischemic/non-ischemic]. LVEF *% ([date]). Euvolemic? *.
- IF HFrEF (≤40): 4-pillar GDMT, titrate q1-2wk, add/uptitrate SOMETHING each visit.
  * BB (compensated only) * [metoprolol SUCCINATE / carvedilol / bisoprolol] — NOT tartrate; goal target dose.
  * ARNI 1st-line * [sacubitril-valsartan] — SBP>100, 36h off ACEi; ARB→immediate. (ACEi/ARB if cost: lisinopril/losartan)
  * MRA * [spironolactone / eplerenone] — hold if K>5, GFR<30, Cr>2.5 M /> 2 F.
  * SGLT2i * [dapagliflozin / empagliflozin] — avoid GFR<20, T1DM/DKA.
- ROADBLOCKS: AKI → expect <30% GFR dip (OK); fix volume/diuretic 1st, hold ACEi/ARNI temp. Hypotension → tx symptoms not number (SBP 90-100 OK), drop non-GDMT antiHTN, carved→metop succ. HyperK → SGLT2i/ARNI help, low-K diet, hydral/isosorbide last resort.
- DEVICE (EF<35% after ≥3mo GDMT): CRT if QRS>120-150/LBBB; ICD if life expectancy>1yr (CRT-D).
- HYDRAL+ISOSORBIDE: self-ID Black pt on GDMT (1a) or if ACEi/ARB/ARNI not tolerated.
- IF HFmrEF/HFpEF: SGLT2i (1A ESC 2023). + finerenone (FDA 2025, LVEF≥40). Tx obesity → GLP-1/tirzepatide if HFpEF+obese. MRA/ARNI 2b. Diuretic for congestion.
- IF HFimpEF: continue GDMT INDEFINITELY (do not withdraw — TRED-HF).
- MONITOR: HR, BP, BMP, volume, daily weights; repeat TTE ~3-6 mo after GDMT optimized.
takehome: Classify every HF patient with a one-liner: NYHA class + EF category (HFrEF ≤40 / HFmrEF 41–49 / HFpEF ≥50 / HFimpEF) + etiology. It drives therapy.
HFrEF gets all four GDMT pillars — beta-blocker, ARNI (or ACEi/ARB), MRA, SGLT2i — for a ~73% mortality reduction at target doses, yet most patients are under-dosed.
Add or titrate something every visit; manage AKI, hypotension, and hyperkalemia rather than abandoning GDMT, and expect a mild GFR dip on ACEi/ARB/ARNI + SGLT2i.
Persistent EF <35% after ≥3 months of GDMT → CRT (wide QRS/LBBB) and/or ICD (life expectancy >1 yr); HFimpEF patients continue GDMT indefinitely.
SGLT2i benefit HFmrEF and HFpEF. Since publication: finerenone is FDA-approved for LVEF ≥40% (2025) and treating obesity with GLP-1/tirzepatide improves HFpEF outcomes.

extras.label: Update — HF Guidance Since 2022 (2023–2025) NEW — teach with current framing
extras.html:
The original board teaches the 2022 AHA/ACC/HFSA guideline. That guideline is still the US standard and the HFrEF quadruple-therapy teaching is unchanged and correct. Three developments since June 2023 are worth folding in, especially for the HFpEF/HFmrEF end of the spectrum, which the original (accurately, for 2022) described as therapy-poor.

TopicWhat's new since publication
SGLT2 inhibitors across the EF spectrumThe 2023 ESC focused update made empagliflozin/dapagliflozin Class 1A in HFmrEF and HFpEF (the 2022 US guideline lists 2a; the deck reflects 2a). SGLT2i now have the strongest recommendation across the whole EF range.
Finerenone in HFmrEF/HFpEFFINEARTS-HF (Solomon, NEJM 2024) showed the nonsteroidal MRA finerenone reduced CV death + worsening-HF events in LVEF ≥40%. FDA-approved July 14, 2025 (Kerendia) for HF with LVEF ≥40% — the first MRA proven to benefit HFmrEF/HFpEF, and beneficial on top of an SGLT2i. Emerging as a second pillar for preserved/mildly-reduced EF.
Obesity as a treatable HFpEF driverSTEP-HFpEF (semaglutide, NEJM 2023) and STEP-HFpEF DM (2024) improved symptoms and function in HFpEF + obesity. SUMMIT (tirzepatide, NEJM 2025) cut CV death or worsening HF by ~38% in HFpEF + obesity. Treat obesity directly rather than as a comorbidity to ignore.
HFrEF teachingUnchanged. The four pillars, target doses, NNTs, CRT/ICD criteria, and the titrate-something-every-visit philosophy in the original board all remain current.
Pearl: When you reach the HFpEF column of the EF-spectrum slide, name three things that now have outcome data: an SGLT2i (Class 1A per ESC 2023), finerenone (FDA-approved for LVEF ≥40% in 2025), and treating obesity with a GLP-1/GIP agent. HFpEF is no longer "diuretics only."

lessons.changelog:
Currency checkOriginal built on the 2022 AHA/ACC/HFSA guideline + 2021 ACC Expert Consensus Decision Pathway. Verified June 2026: HFrEF quadruple therapy, target doses, NNTs, and CRT/ICD criteria are unchanged and current. New since publication — surfaced in the update section and on an appended deck slide: 2023 ESC focused update (SGLT2i Class 1A in HFmrEF/HFpEF); finerenone FINEARTS-HF (NEJM 2024) → FDA approval for LVEF ≥40% (July 2025); STEP-HFpEF (semaglutide) and SUMMIT (tirzepatide) for HFpEF + obesity.
CorrectionsNone — no factual or numeric errors found in the original deck or teaching script.
Content added"2023–25 update" section + an appended deck slide; pre-work; consolidated roadblock and device tables in the script.
DeckAppend-only edit of a copy: original interactive animations, graphics, and slide order preserved; three slides added (2023–25 update, take-home points, attribution/changelog).
PedagogyAdded 5-min pre-work; timed segments with ASK pause points keyed to the board's cursor prompts; the three original cases converted to click-to-reveal; retrieval-based close.
AssessmentAdded 5 ABIM-style MCQs with explanations and 17 Anki cards.
Clinical toolAdded an attending EMR dotphrase (one-liner, four-pillar GDMT with conditionals, roadblock fixes, device triggers, EF-spectrum therapy, HFimpEF rule).
One-pagerAdded a dense single-page printable clinical reference (PDF).
Companion filesOriginal facilitator teaching script copied into this folder and linked (reviewed — no changes needed). The TeachIM Learner's Handout for board set-up is distributed on the website and was not among the source files.
Human review neededA clinician should confirm the framing of the newer agents (finerenone for LVEF ≥40%; GLP-1/tirzepatide in HFpEF; ESC vs US SGLT2i class) and local formulary/cost considerations before teaching.

lessons.title: Management of Chronic Hypertension
lessons.subtitle: Optimized lesson package · Facilitator-led interactive chalk talk for IM residents · 15–20 min (talk) / 30–35 min (with cases)
lessons.credit: Adapted from TeachIM.org — Management of Chronic Hypertension (Fainstad B, Guinn K, Melton M, Trefilek B; ed. Brett M; Sept 2021, rev. Oct 2022), CC BY-NC 4.0. Optimized June 2026: guideline currency verified, 2025 AHA/ACC update surfaced, pedagogy restructured, assessments added. Companion deck: HTN_Chronic_Optimized.pptx (original interactive board, append-only edit).
objectives: Identify the patients for whom starting antihypertensive therapy produces a clinically meaningful reduction in cardiovascular events and death.
Set the target blood pressure and select an appropriate first-line agent based on the patient's comorbidities.
Recognize the clinical features that should prompt evaluation for a secondary cause of hypertension.

prework.q: Q1. Name the four first-line drug classes for chronic hypertension, with one example of each.
prework.a: Thiazide-like diuretic (chlorthalidone, preferred over HCTZ), dihydropyridine calcium-channel blocker (amlodipine), ACE inhibitor (lisinopril), and ARB (losartan). ACEi and ARB are never combined.

prework.q: Q2. A 55-year-old with diabetes has a confirmed BP of 134/82. Do you start a medication, and to what target?
prework.a: Yes. Diabetes is a high-risk condition, so under the 2025 AHA/ACC guideline you treat at ≥130/80 to a target of <130/80. (Even under the older 2017 framing, the high-risk target was <130/80.)

prework.q: Q3. Give three features that should make you suspect a secondary cause of hypertension.
prework.a: Severe or resistant HTN; acute onset or new lability; onset before puberty or age <30 without obesity/family history; unexplained electrolyte abnormalities (especially hypokalemia → hyperaldosteronism). Any of these lowers your threshold to work it up.

segments.label: Hook (2 min)
segments.html: "Hypertension is the single most common condition you'll manage in clinic, and the number needed to treat to prevent a cardiovascular event is lower than for statins, aspirin, or diabetes drugs. So why do we still under-treat it?" Plant the tension between how much good treatment does and how often we stop short.

segments.label: Objective 1 — Why treat, and who benefits
segments.html:
ASK: "What fraction of US adults have hypertension, and what does treating it actually buy them?" — then click to reveal. ~45% of adults >18 by the ACC/AHA definition; lowering BP reduces stroke, MI, and heart failure.

Work the board's worked example: a 50-year-old smoker with sustained BP >150/90, treated to <140/85, gets an absolute 10-year CV-event risk reduction of ~10% (NNT 10) and an all-cause mortality reduction of ~6% (NNT 16). The teaching point is that benefit scales with baseline risk — higher-risk patients get more absolute benefit from the same BP drop.

segments.label: Objective 2 — Target & first-line agent
segments.html:
ASK: "What's the ideal BP if you could get there with lifestyle alone — and why isn't that the medication target for everyone?" — reveal <120/80 ideal, but drug-driven over-treatment carries its own risk (syncope, electrolytes, falls), as SPRINT showed (avg 2.7 meds for tight control, more adverse events).

Set the targets, then flag the 2025 simplification (see the update section): the board shows low-risk <140/90 vs high-risk <130/80; the 2025 AHA/ACC goal is <130/80 for most. High-risk = CKD, prior stroke/MI, diabetes, heart failure, or elevated 10-year ASCVD risk.

ASK: "Four first-line agents — talk me through the best candidate and the one catch for each."
Agent (example)Best candidateKey caution / monitoring
Dihydropyridine CCB — amlodipine 5–10 mgMost patients; well tolerated; good in late CKDDose-dependent pedal edema; avoid in HFrEF (amlodipine OK if needed)
ACE inhibitor — lisinopril 5–40 mgMost patients; preferred with CVD or proteinuriaCheck BMP in 1–2 wk; a GFR drop <30% is acceptable; angioedema; never with an ARB
ARB — losartan 25–100 mgACEi-intolerant (cough/angioedema)Same monitoring as ACEi; never combine with an ACEi
Thiazide-like — chlorthalidone 12.5–25 mg (preferred)Age <65; chlorthalidone > HCTZ (longer half-life, more potent)Check BMP at 2 wk; low Na/K; gout; loses efficacy at GFR <30
Pearls worth dropping in: ACE inhibitors lower BP somewhat less effectively in Black patients (but still use one if there's a compelling indication like diabetic nephropathy). Chlorthalidone is more potent and longer-acting than HCTZ — at the cost of more hypokalemia. SGLT2 inhibitors lower SBP by ~5 mmHg but are not FDA-approved for BP alone.

segments.label: Objective 3 — Secondary causes
segments.html:
ASK: "Four features that should make you stop and think 'this isn't just essential hypertension.'"

Triggers: severe or resistant HTN; acute rise or new lability; before puberty or age <30 without obesity/family history; associated electrolyte abnormalities. Then build the differential:

CauseClueTest
Hyperaldosteronism (most common — 20–30%)Unexplained hypokalemia (sometimes)Aldosterone:renin ratio >20 (off spironolactone)
Renovascular>50% GFR drop after starting an ACEiRenal US with Doppler, CTA, or MRA
Obstructive sleep apneaObesity, snoring, +STOP-BANG / EpworthPolysomnography
Other endocrineSigns of Cushing's, pheochromocytoma, thyroid disease; OCPsSalivary cortisol / dexamethasone suppression; TSH ± fT4; urine/serum metanephrines

segments.label: Close (2 min)
segments.html: Hide the board. Have the group rebuild: the <130/80 target, the four first-line agents and one catch each, the two-drug rule for stage 2, and three secondary-cause triggers.

cases.title: Case 1 — Not yet at goal on one drug
cases.stem: 55-year-old African-American woman, no heart or kidney disease, never-smoker. Diagnosed with HTN 3 months ago (home average 155/95), started on amlodipine 10 mg; now averaging 145/90, BMI 29, normal A1c and lipids.
cases.reveal: C. Start lisinopril 10 mg daily. She is not at goal on a maximal-dose CCB, so add a second first-line agent. (B) Amlodipine BID adds little. (D) Hydralazine is not first-line. (E) A statin may be reasonable later but her 10-year ASCVD risk is <10% (~6.6%). (A) No red flags for a secondary work-up.

cases.title: Case 2 — A single high reading
cases.stem: 45-year-old man with a single office BP of 145/90.
cases.reveal: Recheck after he sits calmly 5 min, arm relaxed, correct cuff size; confirm persistent elevation with 2+ readings in or out of the office.

cases.title: Case 3 — High-risk comorbidity
cases.stem: 60-year-old man with gout and peripheral arterial disease, BP 160/95 on several visits, doesn't check at home.
cases.reveal: <130/80 — high risk because of established ASCVD (PAD).

cases.title: Case 4 — Lifestyle-first and white-coat question
cases.stem: 60-year-old woman with obesity and pre-diabetes, 1–2 alcoholic drinks nightly, non-smoker. BP 150/90 on two consecutive visits; she prefers to try lifestyle first.
cases.reveal: Sodium <2 g/d (↓~5/2.5); high-potassium diet if no CKD (↓~5/3); weight loss (↓~0.5–2 mmHg per kg); exercise ~40 min, 3–4×/wk (↓~4–6/3); reduce alcohol (≥2 drinks/d in women, ≥3 in men significantly raise HTN risk).

mcqs.stem: A 58-year-old man with type 2 diabetes has an office BP of 136/84, confirmed by home readings averaging 134/82. His eGFR is 70 and he is on no antihypertensives. Under current AHA/ACC guidance, what is the most appropriate management?
opts: Lifestyle changes only; recheck in 6 months
Start a single first-line antihypertensive, targeting <130/80
No treatment; his BP is below 140/90
mcqs.exp: B. Diabetes is a high-risk condition, so pharmacologic therapy is indicated at ≥130/80 with a target of <130/80 — he is at stage 1 with a compelling indication, so lifestyle alone (A) is insufficient and "below 140/90" (D) is the outdated threshold. Two agents (C) are reserved for stage 2 (≥140/90). An ACEi or ARB is especially appropriate given diabetes.

mcqs.stem: A 49-year-old woman is found to have a BP of 162/98, confirmed on repeat. She has no other conditions. What is the most appropriate initial pharmacologic strategy?
opts: Start a single first-line agent and titrate
Start two first-line agents of different classes, ideally as a single-pill combination
Lifestyle changes for 6 months before any drug
mcqs.exp: B. She has stage 2 hypertension (≥140/90), for which the guideline recommends initiating two first-line agents, ideally as a single-pill combination, because most patients this far above goal need more than one drug. A single agent (A) under-treats stage 2; a beta-blocker (C) is not first-line for uncomplicated HTN; deferring all drugs (D) is appropriate only for lower-stage, lower-risk patients.

mcqs.stem: A 52-year-old man with hypertension remains at 150/92 despite adherence to lisinopril, amlodipine, and chlorthalidone at adequate doses. His eGFR is 60 and potassium is 4.1. What is the best next step?
mcqs.exp: A. This is resistant hypertension (uncontrolled on an ACEi/ARB + CCB + thiazide-like diuretic). The guideline-preferred fourth agent is a mineralocorticoid receptor antagonist — spironolactone — when the eGFR is ≥45 and potassium permits, both of which he meets. Two RAAS blockers (B) are contraindicated. Swapping to the less potent HCTZ (C) would likely worsen control. Hydralazine (D) is a later option if an MRA can't be used.

mcqs.stem: A 26-year-old woman with no obesity or family history of hypertension presents with a BP of 168/104 and an unprovoked serum potassium of 3.1. Which is the most appropriate next step?
opts: Start chlorthalidone and recheck in a month
mcqs.exp: C. Young age without typical risk factors plus unexplained hypokalemia is a classic trigger for a secondary work-up, and primary hyperaldosteronism is the most common secondary cause — screen with an aldosterone:renin ratio (off spironolactone). A thiazide (A) would worsen the hypokalemia and miss the diagnosis. Reassurance (B) ignores the red flags. Polysomnography (D) fits an obese, snoring patient, which she is not.

mcqs.stem: A 61-year-old man with peripheral arterial disease has a BP of 158/96 on two visits. He needs combination therapy. Which pairing has the strongest cardiovascular-outcome evidence?
mcqs.exp: B. In the ACCOMPLISH trial, an ACE inhibitor + amlodipine reduced cardiovascular events more than an ACE inhibitor + hydrochlorothiazide (A) in high-risk hypertensives. ACEi + ARB (C) is contraindicated (dual RAAS blockade). Beta-blocker + thiazide (D) is not a preferred first-line combination for uncomplicated HTN and carries metabolic downsides.

anki: ACC/AHA definition of hypertension?
Sustained BP >=130/80 (2017, reaffirmed 2025).
2025 AHA/ACC BP treatment target for most adults?
<130/80 (a single target; replaces the older split of low-risk <140/90 vs high-risk <130/80).
Which risk calculator does the 2025 guideline use, and what did it replace?
PREVENT (ages 30-79; includes eGFR, statin use, social factors) — replaces the Pooled Cohort Equations.
Whom do you treat with a drug at stage 1 (130-139/80-89)?
Known CVD, DM, CKD, or 10-yr risk >=7.5% -> start a drug; lower risk -> lifestyle 3-6 mo, then a drug if still >=130/80.
Initial drug strategy for stage 2 HTN (>=140/90)?
Start two first-line agents of different classes, ideally as a single-pill combination.
The four first-line antihypertensive classes (with examples)?
Thiazide-like (chlorthalidone), dihydropyridine CCB (amlodipine), ACE inhibitor (lisinopril), ARB (losartan).
Which two first-line classes must never be combined?
ACE inhibitor + ARB (dual RAAS blockade).
Why is chlorthalidone preferred over HCTZ?
Longer half-life and greater potency (likely more CV risk reduction), at the cost of more hypokalemia.
Monitoring after starting an ACEi/ARB?
Check a BMP in 1-2 weeks; a GFR drop <30% is expected and acceptable; watch K and Cr; angioedema risk.
Best first-line agent in diabetic nephropathy / proteinuria?
ACE inhibitor (or ARB) — reno-protective; preferred despite somewhat less BP-lowering in Black patients.
First-line agent to avoid (or use cautiously) in HFrEF?
Dihydropyridine CCB — avoid in HFrEF (amlodipine acceptable if needed).
Preferred combination by CV outcomes (ACCOMPLISH)?
ACE inhibitor + dihydropyridine CCB > ACE inhibitor + thiazide.
Drug to add for resistant HTN (on ACEi/ARB + CCB + thiazide-like)?
An MRA — spironolactone — if eGFR >=45; aprocitentan (endothelin antagonist) is a newer add-on.
Four triggers to evaluate for a secondary cause of HTN?
Severe/resistant HTN; acute rise or new lability; age <30 (or pre-puberty) without obesity/FHx; associated electrolyte abnormalities.
Most common secondary cause of HTN and its screen?
Primary hyperaldosteronism (20-30%); aldosterone:renin ratio >20 off spironolactone.
Highest-yield lifestyle changes and rough BP effect?
Sodium <2 g/d (~5/2.5), high-K diet (~5/3), weight loss (~0.5-2 mmHg/kg), exercise (~4-6/3), limit alcohol; DASH diet overall.
How should white-coat hypertension be handled?
Don't treat empirically; it's a higher-risk pre-hypertensive state — manage other CV risk factors and monitor for progression to true HTN.
lessons.dot:
#HTN (chronic): avg BP */* [confirm w/ 2+ readings, in or out of office; proper cuff/technique]. stage [elevated 120-129/<80 / stage 1 130-139 or 80-89 / stage 2 ≥140 or ≥90]. 10-yr CVD risk * % [PREVENT, age 30-79]. GOAL <130/80 (2025 AHA/ACC).
- start drug if: known CVD/DM/CKD OR PREVENT ≥7.5% → treat at ≥130/80; if <7.5% → lifestyle ×3-6mo then drug if still ≥130/80; stage 2 → START 2 agents (SPC preferred)
- 1st-line (pick by comorbidity): [thiazide-like: chlorthalidone* 12.5-25 (preferred >HCTZ; gout/↓Na/↓K, GFR<30 less eff) / DHP-CCB: amlodipine* 5-10 (pedal edema; avoid HFrEF) / ACEi: lisinopril* 5-40 (CVD, proteinuria/DM; BMP 1-2wk, GFR↓<30% ok, angioedema) / ARB: losartan* 25-100 (ACEi-intol)]. NEVER ACEi+ARB.
- preferred combo: ACEi + DHP-CCB (ACCOMPLISH > ACEi+thiazide)
- resistant (uncontrolled on ACEi/ARB + CCB + thiazide-like): add MRA spironolactone* if eGFR ≥45 & K ok; then [amiloride / BB / alpha / aprocitentan / central / vasodilator]
- lifestyle (foundational): DASH diet, Na <2g/d (↓~5/2.5), high-K diet if no CKD (↓~5/3), wt loss (↓~0.5-2/kg), aerobic 90-150min/wk (↓~4-6/3), limit EtOH; ± GLP-1 RA if obesity
- secondary w/u IF [severe/resistant, acute/labile, age <30 or pre-puberty w/o RF, electrolyte abnl]: aldo:renin >20 off spirono (hyperaldo, most common) / renal US-Doppler or CTA/MRA (renovascular, >50% GFR↓ post-ACEi) / polysomnography (OSA) / metanephrines, dex-suppression, TSH (endocrine)
- monitoring: home BP log, BMP 1-2wk after ACEi/ARB/thiazide, reassess/titrate q2-4wk to goal; ASCVD risk factors [statin, ASA per risk, A1c, smoking]
takehome: Treat to <130/80 for most adults (2025 AHA/ACC); the board's split low-risk/high-risk target reflects the older 2017 risk-based framing.
Decide WHO gets a drug by absolute risk: established CVD/DM/CKD or a 10-year risk ≥7.5% (PREVENT) means treat at ≥130/80; lower-risk stage-1 patients get a lifestyle trial first.
Four first-line agents — thiazide-like (chlorthalidone preferred), dihydropyridine CCB (amlodipine), ACE inhibitor (lisinopril), ARB (losartan); never ACEi + ARB; start two for stage 2.
Lifestyle is foundational and effective: DASH, sodium <2 g/d, high-potassium diet, weight loss, exercise, limit alcohol.
Work up a secondary cause when HTN is severe/resistant, acute/labile, very young, or paired with electrolyte abnormalities — hyperaldosteronism is the most common.

extras.label: 2025 AHA/ACC Guideline Update NEW — what changed since this talk was written
extras.html:
The talk was built on the 2017 ACC/AHA guideline (and JNC 8). In 2025 the AHA/ACC released a new hypertension guideline that replaces the 2017 document. The standing teaching here — the four first-line agents, the secondary-cause triggers, and the lifestyle measures — remains correct; what moved is the target, the risk tool, and the intensification strategy.

TopicWhat this talk teaches (2017 era)2025 AHA/ACC
BP goalLow-risk <140/90; high-risk <130/80<130/80 for most adults (single target); confirm the diagnosis with out-of-office readings
Risk toolPooled Cohort Equations (10-yr ASCVD)PREVENT equation (ages 30–79; adds eGFR, statin use, social factors)
Who to treat at stage 1 (130–139/80–89)High-risk diagnoses or ASCVD >10%Known CVD/DM/CKD or 10-yr risk ≥7.5% → drug; otherwise lifestyle 3–6 mo, then drug if still ≥130/80
Stage 2 (≥140/90)Often start one agentStart two first-line agents, ideally as a single-pill combination
Resistant HTNAdd agentsAdd an MRA (spironolactone) if eGFR ≥45; aprocitentan (endothelin antagonist) is a newer add-on
ObesityWeight lossGLP-1 receptor agonists endorsed for weight-driven BP reduction
Teaching tip: Leave the board's split target on screen as the historical framing, then add one line — "2025 simplified this to <130/80 for most, and now uses the PREVENT calculator to decide who needs a drug at stage 1." The appended deck slide carries this for the projected board.

1. Whelton PK, et al. 2017 ACC/AHA/AAPA/ABC/ACPM/AGS/APhA/ASH/ASPC/NMA/PCNA Guideline for the Prevention, Detection, Evaluation, and Management of High Blood Pressure in Adults. J Am Coll Cardiol. 2018;71(19):e127-e248.
  2. Jones DW, et al. 2025 AHA/ACC/AANP/AAPA/ABC/ACCP/ACPM/AGS/AMA/ASPC/NMA/PCNA/SGIM Guideline for the Prevention, Detection, Evaluation and Management of High Blood Pressure in Adults. J Am Coll Cardiol / Hypertension. 2025.
  3. Jamerson K, et al. Benazepril plus Amlodipine or Hydrochlorothiazide for Hypertension in High-Risk Patients (ACCOMPLISH). N Engl J Med. 2008;359(23):2417-2428.
  4. SPRINT Research Group. A Randomized Trial of Intensive versus Standard Blood-Pressure Control. N Engl J Med. 2015;373(22):2103-2116.
  5. US Preventive Services Task Force. Screening for Hypertension in Adults: Reaffirmation Recommendation Statement. JAMA. 2021;325(16):1650-1656.
  6. James PA, et al. 2014 Evidence-Based Guideline for the Management of High Blood Pressure in Adults (JNC 8). JAMA. 2014;311(5):507-520.
