lessons.title: Community Acquired Pneumonia
lessons.subtitle: Optimized lesson package · Facilitator-led interactive chalk talk for IM residents · 30 min (+15 min cases)
lessons.credit: Adapted from TeachIM.org — Community Acquired Pneumonia (Yilin Zhang MD; Sept 2021), CC BY-NC 4.0. Optimized June 2026: guideline currency verified, three deck errors corrected, pedagogy restructured, assessments added. Companion deck: Community_Acquired_Pneumonia_Optimized.pptx (original interactive board, surgically corrected).
objectives: Identify the bacterial pathogens commonly responsible for CAP and the risk factors for more resistant pathogens (MRSA, Pseudomonas).
Determine CAP severity using PSI, CURB-65, and the ATS criteria, and use it to choose the site of care (outpatient / floor / ICU).
Manage non-severe and severe inpatient CAP, including when to culture and when to broaden coverage.
Manage CAP in the ambulatory setting, stratified by comorbidities and MDR risk.

prework.q: Q1. What does each letter of CURB-65 stand for, and what score sends a patient inpatient?
prework.a: Confusion; Urea (BUN) >19–20 mg/dL; Respiratory rate ≥30; Blood pressure (SBP <90 or DBP ≤60); age ≥65. One point each. 0–1 = outpatient candidate; 2 = consider admission/observation; 3–5 = inpatient (often ICU). PSI is the better-validated/preferred tool; CURB-65 is the easy-to-remember alternative.

prework.q: Q2. Which two patient features actually justify empiric MRSA/Pseudomonas coverage now that HCAP is gone?
prework.a: (1) Prior respiratory isolation/colonization with MRSA or Pseudomonas, and (2) hospitalization with receipt of IV antibiotics within the prior 90 days — combined with locally validated risk and severity. Nursing-home residence, dialysis, and wound care alone are no longer indications.

prework.q: Q3. A patient has CAP and a procalcitonin of 0.08 µg/L. Do you withhold antibiotics?
prework.a: No. Procalcitonin is not used to decide whether to start antibiotics in CAP (sensitivity for bacterial CAP only ~38–91%). Treat based on the clinical and radiographic diagnosis. Procalcitonin can help guide early discontinuation, not initiation.

segments.label: Hook (2 min)
segments.html: "Three patients walk in with cough, fever, and an infiltrate. One goes home on a pill, one goes to the floor, one goes to the ICU on vanc and Zosyn. Same diagnosis — what are the two questions that sort them?" The whole talk answers that: how severe is it (site of care) and does this patient have MDR risk factors (how broad to cover).

segments.label: Objective 1 — Microbiology & MDR risk
segments.html:
ASK: "Most common bacterial cause of CAP?" Reveal: S. pneumoniae (still #1 despite the pneumococcal vaccine). Then GNRs (H. flu, Moraxella), atypicals (Legionella, Chlamydia, Mycoplasma), and viruses (influenza, COVID-19) — a major cause.
Pearl — HCAP is dead (2019). The old "healthcare-associated pneumonia" label (nursing home, dialysis, wound care) over-predicted resistance and drove overtreatment. It's retired. Add MRSA/Pseudomonas coverage only for (1) prior MRSA/Pseudomonas, or (2) hospitalization + IV antibiotics within 90 days — plus local epidemiology.

segments.label: Objective 2 — Severity & site of care
segments.html:
ASK: "PSI or CURB-65 — which decides ICU admission?" Reveal: neither directly — they triage outpatient vs inpatient. The ATS criteria (>1 major or ≥3 minor) decide ICU.
ToolWhat it decidesThresholds
PSI (preferred)Site of careClass I–II → outpatient; III → observe; IV–V → inpatient
CURB-65 (easy)Site of care0–1 → outpatient; 2 → admit/observe; 3–5 → inpatient
ATS criteriaICU admissionMajor: septic shock on pressors, mechanical ventilation. Minor (≥3): RR >30, P:F <250, multilobar, confusion, BUN >20, WBC <4k, plt <100k, T <36 °C, hypotension needing fluids
Pearl — scores assist, judgment decides. Always pair PSI/CURB-65 with clinical judgment (hypoxia, social factors, ability to take oral meds). A "low score" hypoxemic patient still gets admitted.

segments.label: Objective 3 — Inpatient management
segments.html:
ASK: "First two questions on every admitted CAP patient?" Reveal: (1) Is it severe? (2) Are there MDR risk factors? Those two answers drive cultures and coverage.
ScenarioCultures?Empiric coverage
Severe CAPYes — blood, sputum, Legionella + pneumococcal urinary AgStandard CAP + empiric MRSA and Pseudomonas
Non-severe, prior MRSA/PseudomonasYesStandard CAP + cover MRSA ± Pseudomonas per prior data
Non-severe, hospitalization + IV abx <90 dYes (cultures)Standard CAP treatment
Non-severe, no risk factorsNoStandard CAP treatment

Standard inpatient CAP: IV beta-lactam (ampicillin-sulbactam or ceftriaxone) + azithromycin or doxycycline; or a respiratory fluoroquinolone. MRSA: IV vancomycin (or linezolid). Pseudomonas: piperacillin-tazobactam, cefepime, ceftazidime, aztreonam, or meropenem — continue atypical coverage. De-escalate to standard CAP if cultures are negative.

ASK: "Does procalcitonin tell you whether to start antibiotics?" Reveal: no — start based on the clinical/radiographic diagnosis. Procalcitonin only helps with early stopping.

segments.label: Objective 4 — Outpatient management & duration
segments.html:
Outpatient groupRegimen
No comorbidities, no MDR riskAmoxicillin 1 g TID, or doxycycline (some add azithromycin for atypical coverage)
Comorbidities (chronic heart/lung/liver/renal disease, DM, alcoholism, malignancy, asplenia)Amoxicillin-clavulanate or a cephalosporin + azithromycin/doxycycline, or respiratory FQ monotherapy
Pearl — no macrolide monotherapy. US pneumococcal macrolide resistance is too high; azithromycin alone is not recommended. Amoxicillin is dosed 1 g TID (high-dose) for reliable pneumococcal coverage.
Pearl — duration is 5–7 days. Treat at least 5 days and until clinically stable (afebrile, stable vitals/oxygenation). Longer courses for extrapulmonary infection, Pseudomonas/Mycobacteria/Burkholderia, or necrotizing pneumonia / abscess / empyema.

segments.label: Retrieval close (3 min)
segments.html: Close the board. Have learners rebuild the algorithm from memory for an imaginary admit: name the likely bugs, score severity (which tool for site, which for ICU), decide cultures + coverage from the two questions, and state the duration. Whoever names a drug must say which organism it covers.

cases.title: Case 1 — The floor admit
cases.stem: 70-year-old, 4 days of productive cough, malaise, fevers. T 100.9 °F, HR 110, BP 99/53, RR 33, SpO₂ 90%. WBC 17k, lactate 2.1. CXR: focal RLL consolidation.
cases.reveal: Inpatient. CURB-65 = 2 (age ≥65, RR >30). PSI is preferred over CURB-65 for the site-of-care decision.

cases.title: Case 2 — The ICU admit
cases.stem: 54-year-old with DM2, 3 days of fever and cough, recently admitted for MRSA cellulitis. T 103 °F, HR 122, BP 80/53 after 2 L IVF, RR 33, SpO₂ 88% on 6 L NC. CXR: LUL cavitary lesion. COVID-19 and flu PCR negative.
cases.reveal: Inpatient ICU — septic shock not responsive to fluids (an ATS major criterion).

cases.title: Case 3 — The outpatient
cases.stem: 41-year-old, no PMH, fever and cough ×2 days. T 99 °F, HR 93, BP 110/53, RR 33, SpO₂ 94%. CXR: diffuse interstitial infiltrates. WBC 12k, BUN 12, Cr 1.1. COVID-19 and flu PCR negative.
cases.reveal: Outpatient. CURB-65 = 0, PSI class I.

mcqs.stem: A 72-year-old nursing-home resident is admitted with non-severe CAP. He has not been hospitalized or received IV antibiotics in over a year and has no prior MRSA or Pseudomonas. The team wants to start vancomycin and cefepime "because he's from a facility." What is the most appropriate empiric therapy?
mcqs.exp: B. The 2019 IDSA/ATS guideline retired HCAP; facility residence alone no longer justifies broad coverage. Empiric MRSA/Pseudomonas coverage (A, C) is reserved for prior MRSA/Pseudomonas or hospitalization + IV antibiotics within 90 days. Azithromycin monotherapy (D) is inappropriate due to high US pneumococcal resistance. Standard CAP therapy is a beta-lactam + macrolide.

mcqs.stem: A 60-year-old woman with CAP has RR 34, BUN 26, confusion, multilobar infiltrates, and WBC 3.5k, but no shock and is not intubated. Which disposition is most appropriate?
mcqs.exp: C. She meets ≥3 ATS minor criteria (RR >30, BUN >20, confusion, multilobar, WBC <4k) — ICU-level care, even without a major criterion (shock or mechanical ventilation). PSI/CURB-65 (A, B, D) decide outpatient vs inpatient, not ICU; here the ATS minor-criteria count drives the decision.

mcqs.stem: A 48-year-old man with CAP is being discharged. He has no comorbidities and no MDR risk factors. Which oral regimen is most appropriate?
mcqs.exp: B. For outpatient CAP without comorbidities, high-dose amoxicillin (1 g TID) or doxycycline is first-line. Macrolide monotherapy (A) is not recommended in the US due to pneumococcal resistance. Low-dose amoxicillin (C) under-doses for pneumococcus. Ciprofloxacin (D) is not a "respiratory" fluoroquinolone — it lacks reliable S. pneumoniae activity; levofloxacin or moxifloxacin would be the FQ options.

mcqs.stem: A 65-year-old admitted with severe CAP is started on vancomycin, piperacillin-tazobactam, and azithromycin. At 48 hours he is improving; blood and sputum cultures and the MRSA nasal PCR are all negative. What is the best next step?
opts: Continue all three agents for a full 14 days
De-escalate to standard CAP therapy (e.g., ceftriaxone + azithromycin)
mcqs.exp: B. Negative cultures and a negative MRSA nasal PCR (high negative predictive value) support de-escalation to standard CAP coverage — a core stewardship move. Prolonged broad therapy (A, D) and empiric antifungals (C) add toxicity and resistance without benefit. Total duration is 5–7 days guided by clinical stability, not 14.

mcqs.stem: A 58-year-old with severe CAP in the ICU has refractory hypoxemia and high inflammatory markers. Which adjunctive therapy is now recommended by the 2025 ATS guideline?
mcqs.exp: B. The 2025 ATS update recommends systemic corticosteroids for severe CAP (hydrocortisone <400 mg/day ×5–7 days), based on CAPE COD (NEJM 2023; mortality RR 0.62) — a change from the older "against steroids" teaching, which still holds for non-severe CAP. Procalcitonin (A) is not used to start antibiotics. Inhaled antibiotics (C) have no role in CAP. Oseltamivir (D) is given for confirmed/suspected influenza, not empirically for all.

anki: Most common bacterial cause of CAP?
S. pneumoniae (still #1 despite the pneumococcal vaccine). Then GNRs (H. flu, Moraxella) and atypicals; viruses are also a major cause.
Atypical CAP pathogens?
Legionella, Chlamydia (Chlamydophila), Mycoplasma.
Two risk factors that justify empiric MRSA/Pseudomonas coverage?
(1) Prior MRSA/Pseudomonas isolation; (2) hospitalization + IV antibiotics within 90 days. HCAP was retired in 2019.
What does CURB-65 stand for?
Confusion; Urea (BUN) >19-20 mg/dL; RR >=30; BP (SBP <90/DBP <=60); age >=65. One point each.
CURB-65 cut-offs for disposition?
0-1 outpatient; 2 admit/observe; 3-5 inpatient (often ICU).
PSI vs CURB-65 -- which is preferred?
PSI is better validated and preferred; CURB-65 is the easier-to-remember alternative.
Which criteria decide ICU admission, and the threshold?
ATS criteria: >1 major (septic shock on pressors, mechanical ventilation) OR >=3 minor (RR >30, P:F <250, multilobar, confusion, BUN >20, WBC <4k, plt <100k, T <36 C, hypotension needing fluids).
Standard inpatient CAP regimen?
IV beta-lactam (ampicillin-sulbactam or ceftriaxone) + azithromycin or doxycycline, OR a respiratory fluoroquinolone.
Empiric MRSA and Pseudomonas agents in CAP?
MRSA: IV vancomycin (or linezolid). Pseudomonas: piperacillin-tazobactam, cefepime, ceftazidime, aztreonam, or meropenem. Continue atypical coverage.
Who gets cultured in CAP?
Severe CAP and those with prior MRSA/Pseudomonas (or recent hospitalization + IV abx). Non-severe CAP without risk factors: do not culture.
Outpatient CAP, no comorbidities/MDR risk?
Amoxicillin 1 g TID or doxycycline (some add azithromycin for atypicals). No macrolide monotherapy (US resistance).
Outpatient CAP with comorbidities?
Amoxicillin-clavulanate or a cephalosporin + azithromycin/doxycycline, OR respiratory FQ monotherapy.
Duration of CAP therapy?
5-7 days, at least 5 and until clinically stable. Longer for extrapulmonary infection, Pseudomonas/Mycobacteria/Burkholderia, or necrotizing pneumonia/abscess/empyema.
Role of procalcitonin in CAP?
NOT used to decide whether to start antibiotics (sensitivity ~38-91%). May guide early discontinuation.
2025 ATS update on corticosteroids in CAP?
Recommend systemic steroids (hydrocortisone <400 mg/day x5-7 d) for SEVERE CAP (CAPE COD, NEJM 2023). NOT for non-severe CAP; dexamethasone if COVID-19.
Why isn't azithromycin used as CAP monotherapy in the US?
High rates of pneumococcal macrolide resistance. Azithromycin is dosed once daily (not TID).
lessons.dot:
#CAP: * [non-severe / severe] community-acquired pneumonia. dx = compatible sx (fever/cough/dyspnea) + infiltrate on * [CXR / CT / lung US]. likely 2/2 * [S.pneumo, H.flu/Moraxella, atypical (Legionella/Myco/Chlamydia), viral (flu/COVID)]. excl mimics [CHF, PE, aspiration pneumonitis, organizing PNA].
- severity: PSI class * [I-II outpt / III obs / IV-V inpt] (preferred) or CURB-65 = * [C/U>20/R≥30/B<90/65; 0-1 outpt, 2 admit, 3-5 inpt]. ICU if ATS: >1 major [septic shock on pressors, mech vent] or ≥3 minor [RR>30, P:F<250, multilobar, confusion, BUN>20, WBC<4k, plt<100k, T<36, hypotension→fluids].
- MDR risk: * [prior MRSA/PsA isolation; hosp + IV abx <90d]. severe OR prior MRSA/PsA → cx [blood, sputum, Legionella + pneumococcal urine Ag, MRSA nares] + empiric MRSA±PsA. non-severe + no RF → NO cx, standard tx.
- abx: standard = IV BL [ampicillin-sulbactam / ceftriaxone] + [azithromycin / doxycycline (if QTc)] OR resp FQ [levo/moxi]. +MRSA = vancomycin (or linezolid). +PsA = [zosyn/cefepime/ceftazidime/aztreonam/meropenem]; continue atypical cov. de-escalate to standard if cx neg / MRSA nares neg @48h.
- outpt: no comorbid/RF → amoxicillin 1g TID or doxycycline. comorbid [chronic heart/lung/liver/renal, DM, EtOH, malignancy, asplenia] → augmentin/cephalosporin + azithro/doxy OR resp FQ. *no macrolide monotherapy.*
- adjuncts: procalcitonin NOT for initiation (may guide d/c). steroids ONLY if SEVERE CAP [hydrocort <400mg/d x5-7d; dexamethasone if COVID]. O2/support * .
- duration: 5-7d, ≥5d AND clinically stable (afebrile, stable VS/O2). longer if [extrapulm infection, PsA/Mycobacteria/Burkholderia, necrotizing/abscess/empyema].

  3. File TM Jr. Treatment of community-acquired pneumonia in adults in the outpatient setting. UpToDate (accessed Sept 2021).

Newly cited (2023–2025 updates):

  1. American Thoracic Society. 2025 Clinical Practice Guideline Updates for the Diagnosis and Management of Community-Acquired Pneumonia. Am J Respir Crit Care Med. 2025;212(1):24.
  2. Dequin PF, et al. Hydrocortisone in Severe Community-Acquired Pneumonia (CAPE COD). N Engl J Med. 2023;388:1931–1941.

AreaChange

Content added2025 ATS CAP guideline: corticosteroids now recommended for severe CAP (hydrocortisone <400 mg/day ×5–7 d) per CAPE COD (NEJM 2023) — a change from the deck's "against steroid use," which still applies to non-severe CAP; reaffirmed shorter (3–5 d) courses for stable patients; more selective empiric broad-spectrum coverage; lung ultrasound as an alternative imaging tool. Added as an HTML section and an appended deck slide.
CorrectionsDeck slide 7: azithromycin "500 mg TID" → "500 mg daily" (azithromycin is once-daily; its long half-life is the rationale for a short once-daily course) — flagged for human review. Slide 7: "INTPATIENT" → "INPATIENT" (spelling). Slide 8 (case answer): outpatient amoxicillin "500 mg TID" → "1 g TID" to match the deck's own slide-6 standard and the IDSA high-dose recommendation — flagged for human review. All click-to-reveal animations and graphics preserved.
PedagogyRestructured into timed segments per objective; added a 2-min hook, the two-question framing (severity → MDR risk), 5 ASK pause-points, pearls in callout boxes, severity/management tables, and a retrieval-based close.

One-pagerAdded a dense single-page CAP reference (PDF + source HTML).

Human review needed(1) Azithromycin slide-7 dose: corrected TID → daily (the "*" footnote on the slide references the long half-life, supporting once-daily; confirm the authors' intended regimen — e.g., 500 mg daily ×3 d, or 500 mg ×1 then 250 mg daily). (2) Outpatient amoxicillin case dose harmonized 500 mg → 1 g TID to match the deck's slide-6 standard; confirm intended value. Both are reasonable corrections of internal inconsistencies but were inferred, not stated.

lessons.title: Contraception
lessons.subtitle: Optimized lesson package · Facilitator-led interactive chalk talk for IM residents · 15–20 min (talk) / +20–30 min (cases)
lessons.credit: Adapted from TeachIM.org — Contraception (deQuillfeldt, Saxon, Guinn, Ramaswami, Haynes, Cleeves, Soiseth, Fainstad, Do; Section Eds. Brett, McClintock; Exec. Ed. Zhang; April 2022), CC BY-NC 4.0. Optimized June 2026: guideline currency verified against CDC US MEC/US SPR 2024, two deck typos corrected, pedagogy restructured, assessments added. Companion deck: Contraception_Optimized.pptx (original interactive board, surgically corrected). Educational use only — clinician-reviewed before teaching.
objectives: Use patient-centered, non-judgmental language in history-taking and contraceptive counseling (the PATH framework).
Use evidence-based tools (US MEC, the Contraception app, Bedsider) to select a method that matches the patient's stated goals — not an efficacy-first script.
Recognize how specific conditions change the safety and efficacy of each method, and apply the US MEC categories 1–4.

prework.q: Q1. What are the four "PATH" domains you can use to open a contraception conversation?
prework.a: Pregnancy Attitudes, Timing, and How important is prevention. The PATH questions assess attitude toward pregnancy ("Do you think you might like to have (more) children at some point?"), timing ("When?"), and the importance of preventing pregnancy until then — in a way that does not assume the patient has a defined "plan."

prework.q: Q2. Which four history items most often rule OUT estrogen-containing (combined) methods?
prework.a: Smoking at age >35, migraine with aura, personal history of VTE/CVD/CVA, and current/personal history of breast cancer. Each pushes combined hormonal contraception to US MEC category 3 or 4. (Breast cancer affects all hormonal methods.)

prework.q: Q3. Rank these for emergency contraception efficacy: copper IUD, ulipristal (Ella), levonorgestrel (Plan B), combined OCPs (Yuzpe).
prework.a: Copper IUD is the most effective (>99% if ≤5 days). Ulipristal (Ella) outperforms levonorgestrel, especially at days 3–5 and higher BMI. Levonorgestrel (Plan B) is next and is least effective at higher BMI. Combined OCPs (Yuzpe method) are the least effective but the most widely available.

segments.label: Hook (2 min)
segments.html: "Half of the patients walking into your primary-care clinic today who could get pregnant want to talk about contraception — but most won't bring it up first." Ask the room: what's the first sentence you'd actually say? Park the answers; you'll replace efficacy-first habits with the PATH approach in the next five minutes.

segments.label: Objective 1 — Patient-centered language: PATH
segments.html:
ASK: "What phrasing do you use to bring up contraception?" Collect 2–3 openers, then click to reveal the PATH questions.

Walk the board's PATH build: Pregnancy Attitudes ("Do you think you might like to have (more) children at some point?"), Timing ("When do you think that might be?"), How important is prevention ("How important is it to you to prevent pregnancy until then?"). Add the method-preference openers ("What's the most important thing you'd like your birth control to do for you?", "What have you tried, and what worked or didn't?").

Pearl — PATH is inclusive of patients beyond cis, heterosexual couples and avoids framing pregnancy as a "plan," which can be inappropriate or triggering. Lead with open-ended questions; avoid assumptions about goals.

segments.label: Objective 2a — Match the method to what matters
segments.html:
ASK: Before showing the comparison grid, ask: "What attributes actually drive a patient's choice?" Steer away from leading with the efficacy chart.

Click through the Methods grid by attribute, not by device: hormone-free vs hormonal, efficacy, maintenance burden (per-day vs per-week vs per-year), and impact on bleeding. Key bleeding effects:

MethodTypical bleeding effectMaintenance
Copper IUDHeavier / crampier periods — reserve for those avoiding hormones, wanting EC, or with light periodsUp to 10–12 yr
LNG IUD & implantLighter, often amenorrhea; some prolonged spotting (progestin thins endometrium — treat with oral estrogen if no contraindication)3–8 yr
Depo-Provera (DMPA)Lighter to amenorrhea, esp. >1 yr; can have ongoing spottingEvery 3 months
Pill / patch / ringLighten or maintain regular monthly menses (predictability is a feature)Daily / weekly / monthly
Pearl — Bleeding change may not settle until 3–4 cycles. Estrogen-free methods are more prone to 6–12 months of unscheduled spotting; combined methods usually settle within ~3 months. Spotting is a leading reason patients quit early — counsel up front.

segments.label: Objective 2b — Choosing an OCP
segments.html:
Click "Estrogen" and "Progestin" to reveal mechanism. Estrogen (usually ethinyl estradiol) suppresses ovulation and stabilizes the endometrium; a common start is ~20 mcg. Lower estrogen → more breakthrough bleeding; higher estrogen → more thrombosis. Progestin thickens cervical mucus, slows tubal transport, thins the endometrium, and suppresses ovulation. Four generations: earlier = more androgenic; later (3rd/4th) = more anti-androgenic but higher VTE risk when combined with estrogen.

Pearl — Reasonable default: ~20 mcg EE + a progestin. Reserve 3rd/4th-gen progestins (e.g., drospirenone) for anti-androgenic benefit because of higher clot risk. Pick 2–3 "favorites" and titrate. Examples: Junel/Loestrin (1st-gen), Lutera (2nd-gen), Yaz (4th-gen — FDA VTE warning). Bonus: if a pill name lacks "Lo," it usually contains 35 mcg EE.

segments.label: Objective 3 — Conditions that change safety/efficacy
segments.html:
ASK: "What are the classic contraindications to estrogen?" Take answers, then click each contraindication icon.

Avoid combined (estrogen) methods with: smoking at age >35, migraine with aura, personal VTE/CVD/CVA. Breast cancer → avoid any hormonal method. Cervical/uterine cancer, anatomic abnormality, or active PID → avoid IUD placement. When in doubt, open the US MEC tab and read the category (1–4).

US MEC categoryMeaning
1Use without restriction
2Advantages generally outweigh risks
3Risks usually outweigh advantages — not recommended unless no better option
4Unacceptable health risk — do not use

segments.label: Emergency contraception
segments.html:
Use the STEPS lens (Safety, Tolerability, Efficacy, Price, Simplicity). Headlines: Plan B (levonorgestrel) and Ella (ulipristal) are the best efficacy/tolerability combo; copper IUD is the most effective overall (>99% if ≤5 days); combined OCPs (Yuzpe) are the most widely available backup (100 mcg EE + 0.5 mg LNG per dose, ~4–5 pills, q12h ×2). Ella and Plan B lose efficacy with higher BMI; ulipristal is preferred at days 3–5.

Pearl — The LNG IUD is non-inferior to the copper IUD for EC (Turok, NEJM 2021) though not FDA-labeled for that indication. If unprotected sex occurred within the prior 2 weeks, a negative urine pregnancy test can't fully exclude pregnancy before IUD placement.

segments.label: Retrieval close (2 min)
segments.html: Cover the board. Ask learners to rebuild from memory: (1) the four PATH domains, (2) the four estrogen red-flags, (3) the EC efficacy ranking, (4) the default OCP. Fill gaps from their answers, not your slides.

cases.title: Case 1 — Ella, 23, acne, wants a COCP
cases.stem: 23F, history of acne, establishing care, wants a combined OCP. Exam normal, BMI 35.
cases.reveal: Category 2 — advantages generally outweigh theoretical/proven risks; the only flag is BMI >30.

cases.title: Case 2 — Miranda, 35, HTN, bipolar on lamotrigine, menorrhagia
cases.stem: 35F, well-controlled HTN, bipolar depression on lamotrigine, menorrhagia; wants to switch off her COCP.
cases.reveal: Category 3 — estrogen increases lamotrigine clearance (lowers lamotrigine levels), risking loss of seizure/mood control. Not recommended unless no better option.

cases.title: Case 3 — David, 21, transgender man, T1DM, wants "the shot"
cases.stem: 21yo transgender man, obesity, type 1 diabetes (A1c 9%, microalbuminuria, mild retinopathy), one male partner, requesting DMPA.
cases.reveal: DM with complications: combined hormonal methods and DMPA are category 3; all others except the copper IUD are category 2.

cases.title: Case 4 — Kylee, 35, comorbidity vignettes
cases.stem: 35F, contraceptive counseling. Which combined hormonal methods fit each comorbidity?
cases.reveal: Compensated: all methods low-risk. Decompensated: COCPs contraindicated; all other methods except the copper IUD are category 3.

mcqs.stem: A 38-year-old woman who smokes 20 cigarettes/day requests contraception. She has regular menses and no other medical problems. Which method is US MEC category 4 (do not use)?
mcqs.exp: C — combined estrogen-progestin pill. Age ≥35 plus smoking ≥15 cigarettes/day is US MEC category 4 for any combined (estrogen-containing) method because of arterial/VTE risk. Progestin-only methods (A, B, D) and the copper IUD (E) are estrogen-free and remain category 1–2 — they're all acceptable.

mcqs.stem: A 29-year-old with bipolar disorder maintained on lamotrigine wants to start a combined oral contraceptive. What is the key concern?
opts: Lamotrigine raises estrogen levels, increasing VTE risk
Estrogen increases lamotrigine clearance, lowering its levels and risking breakthrough symptoms
mcqs.exp: B. Ethinyl estradiol induces glucuronidation of lamotrigine, increasing its clearance and lowering serum levels — risking loss of mood/seizure control (and levels rebound during the pill-free week). This makes combined methods US MEC category 3, not 4 (C is wrong). The interaction lowers lamotrigine, not contraceptive efficacy (D wrong); A reverses the direction.

mcqs.stem: A 26-year-old presents 4 days after unprotected intercourse requesting the most effective emergency contraception. BMI is 31. Which is most effective?
mcqs.exp: C — copper IUD, the single most effective EC (>99%, up to 5 days, and unaffected by BMI). Among oral options ulipristal (B) beats levonorgestrel (A), especially at days 3–5 and higher BMI, but neither matches the copper IUD. Yuzpe (D) is least effective. E is wrong: the LNG IUD is non-inferior to copper for EC in trials but is not FDA-labeled for that indication.

mcqs.stem: A 22-year-old transgender man on testosterone has receptive vaginal intercourse with a male partner and does not desire pregnancy. He believes testosterone prevents pregnancy. What is the best counseling?
opts: Testosterone is adequate contraception; no method needed
Testosterone is not reliable contraception; offer a method, and note estrogen-containing methods won't reverse his masculinization
Only the copper IUD is safe on testosterone
He must stop testosterone before starting any contraceptive
Combined hormonal contraception is first-line to "balance" testosterone
mcqs.exp: B. Per CDC US MEC/US SPR 2024, testosterone does not reliably suppress ovulation — a substantial fraction of users ovulate — so a person with a uterus who doesn't want pregnancy needs contraception. Progestin-only methods or the LNG IUD (which also reduces bleeding/dysphoria) are reasonable; testosterone need not be stopped (D wrong). C and E are fabricated rules.

mcqs.stem: A 24-year-old with no contraindications wants to start a combined OCP and asks you to pick a starting pill. Which is the most appropriate default?
opts: A 50 mcg ethinyl estradiol monophasic pill
A ~20 mcg ethinyl estradiol pill with a 1st/2nd-generation progestin
A multiphasic (triphasic) pill to minimize spotting
A progestin-only pill, because it has fewer contraindications
A 4th-generation drospirenone pill for everyone, given its anti-androgenic benefit
mcqs.exp: B. A reasonable default combined pill is ~20 mcg EE with an earlier-generation progestin — effective, lowest practical estrogen dose, lowest relative VTE risk. 50 mcg EE (A) is unnecessarily high-dose. Multiphasic pills (C) confer no clinical advantage and cause more unscheduled bleeding. A POP (D) is fine but not the "default" combined choice she's asking for. Drospirenone (E) carries higher VTE risk and should be reserved for an anti-androgenic indication, not used universally.

anki: What do the PATH questions assess?
Pregnancy Attitudes, Timing, and How important is prevention — patient-centered openers that don't assume a "plan."
Default starting combined OCP?
~20 mcg ethinyl estradiol + a progestin; pick 2-3 favorites and titrate.
Estrogen dose tradeoff in OCPs?
Lower estrogen = more breakthrough bleeding; higher estrogen = more thrombosis.
Which progestin generations are more anti-androgenic, and the catch?
3rd/4th generation; but higher VTE risk when combined with estrogen.
Four history items that exclude combined (estrogen) methods?
Smoking at age >35, migraine with aura, VTE/CVD/CVA, breast cancer (breast CA excludes all hormonal methods).
US MEC categories 1-4?
1 = use without restriction; 2 = advantages > risks; 3 = risks usually outweigh advantages; 4 = do not use.
Combined method + smoking + age >=35 + >=15 cigarettes/day = which MEC category?
Category 4.
Lamotrigine + combined hormonal contraception interaction and category?
Category 3 — estrogen increases lamotrigine clearance, lowering levels (risk of breakthrough symptoms).
Most effective emergency contraception?
Copper IUD (>99%, up to 5 days, BMI-independent).
Ulipristal vs levonorgestrel for emergency contraception?
Ulipristal (Ella) is more effective, especially days 3-5 and higher BMI; both lose efficacy as BMI rises.
Yuzpe method for emergency contraception?
Combined OCPs: 100 mcg ethinyl estradiol + 0.5 mg levonorgestrel per dose (~4-5 pills), q12h x2 — least effective but most available.
Bleeding effect: copper IUD vs LNG IUD/implant?
Copper -> heavier/crampier; LNG IUD & implant -> lighter/amenorrhea (may have prolonged spotting early).
DMPA (Depo-Provera) bleeding pattern over time?
Lighter to amenorrhea, especially after >1 year; can have ongoing spotting.
How long until a method's bleeding pattern settles?
3-4 cycles; estrogen-free methods may spot 6-12 months, combined methods usually settle by ~3 months.
Most effective reversible method by typical use?
The etonogestrel implant (~0.05 pregnancies per 100 users/yr), then LNG and copper IUDs.
Combined hormonal method after major surgery with immobilization?
US MEC category 4 — switch to a progestin-only method.
Does testosterone prevent pregnancy (CDC 2024)?
No — a substantial fraction of users ovulate; offer contraception to testosterone users with a uterus who don't want pregnancy.
First OTC daily oral contraceptive?
Opill (norgestrel 0.075 mg, progestin-only) — FDA-approved July 2023, on shelves 2024.
US SPR 2024 tip to reduce IUD-placement pain?
Lidocaine — paracervical block or topical.
lessons.dot:
#Contraceptive counseling (well-person / method start)
PATH: wants children *[yes/no/unsure]; timing *[<1y/1-3y/>3y/none]; prevention priority *[high/med/low]
goals: *[max efficacy / hormone-free / lighter menses / discreet / low-maintenance / STI dual-protection w/ condom]
hx for estrogen safety: smoker *[y/n] & age *__; migraine w/ aura *[y/n]; VTE/CVD/CVA *[y/n]; breast CA *[y/n]; HTN *[y/n]; postpartum/postop immob *[y/n]
 -> US MEC reviewed via app, category *__ for chosen method
method selected: *[LNG-IUD / Cu-IUD / implant / DMPA(±SC self-admin) / COC / patch / ring / POP / Opill-OTC / pH-modulator]
 COC default if chosen: ethinyl estradiol *20mcg + progestin *[1st/2nd gen]; reserve 3rd/4th-gen for anti-androgenic need (^VTE)
counsel: efficacy/typical-use, bleeding change x3-4 cycles (spotting 6-12mo if estrogen-free), no STI protection w/o condom
 -> continuous dosing option to skip menses; prolonged spotting on progestin -> oral estrogen if no contraindication
AVOID: combined/estrogen if MEC 3-4 [smoker>35, migraine+aura, VTE/CVD, breast CA, decompensated cirrhosis, postop immob]; IUD if cervical/uterine CA, anatomic abnl, active PID; any hormone if breast CA
if testosterone use (TGD pt, uterus): NOT contraception - offer method (CDC 2024)
emergency contraception (if asked): Cu-IUD most effective (<=5d, BMI-indep) > ulipristal(Ella) > LNG(PlanB, ^BMI lowers) > Yuzpe[100mcg EE+0.5mg LNG, ~4-5 pills q12h x2]
IUD placement: offer lidocaine [paracervical/topical] for pain (US SPR 2024)
f/u: *__wk for tolerability/bleeding; STI screen offered *[y/n]; UPT today *[neg/n/a]
RTC or message for: heavy/prolonged bleeding, s/sx VTE, new migraine aura, method dissatisfaction
takehome: Lead with the patient — open-ended PATH questions, not an efficacy-first script.
Match the method to what matters: hormone-free vs hormonal, efficacy, maintenance, and bleeding effect.
Take a focused history for estrogen red-flags (smoking at >35, migraine with aura, VTE/CVD/CVA, breast cancer); use the US MEC categories when unsure.
A sensible COC default is ~20 mcg EE + a progestin; reserve 3rd/4th-gen progestins for anti-androgenic benefit given higher VTE risk.
EC: copper IUD is most effective; ulipristal > levonorgestrel; combined OCPs (Yuzpe) are the available backup.
Know the 2024 updates: testosterone is not contraception; lidocaine for IUD-placement pain; self-administered DMPA-SC; and OTC Opill expand access.

extras.label: 2024 Practice Update — CDC US MEC & US SPR NEW — projected on the appended deck slide
extras.html:
The deck's framework (PATH, attribute-based selection, MEC categories) still holds. CDC updated both core documents in 2024 (MMWR, Aug 2024); the practice-relevant additions:

  • US MEC 2024: new recommendations for chronic kidney disease; revised guidance across DVT/PE ± anticoagulation, thrombophilia, SLE, cirrhosis/liver tumor, sickle cell disease, solid-organ transplant, obesity, surgery, and antiretroviral drug interactions; new formulations folded in (COCs, patch, ring, POPs, LNG-IUDs, vaginal pH modulator).
  • Transgender guidance (NEW): testosterone does not reliably prevent pregnancy — up to ~36% of users ovulate. Offer contraceptive counseling to testosterone users with a uterus who don't desire pregnancy. (Directly updates Case 3.)
  • US SPR 2024: lidocaine (paracervical block or topical) recommended to reduce pain at IUD placement; new support for self-administration of subcutaneous DMPA (DMPA-SC 104 mg).
  • OTC access: Opill (norgestrel 0.075 mg, progestin-only) — first FDA-approved nonprescription daily oral contraceptive (FDA July 2023; on shelves 2024, ~$20/month). No estrogen; same POP cautions (e.g., current breast cancer).
  • Newer non-hormonal method: vaginal pH modulator gel (Phexxi; lactic/citric acid + potassium bitartrate; FDA 2020) — typical-use efficacy ~86%, applied immediately before intercourse.

lessons.refs:
Original lesson references (Daniels & Abma CDC 2017–2019; Lee JGIM 2011; Steinberg/Holms Contraception app; Callegari AJOG 2017; Curtis US MEC/US SPR 2016; Turok NEJM 2021; and others as listed on the TeachIM page) are retained. Newly cited for the 2024 update:

  1. Nguyen AT, et al. U.S. Medical Eligibility Criteria for Contraceptive Use, 2024. MMWR Recomm Rep 2024;73(RR-4):1–126.
  2. Curtis KM, et al. U.S. Selected Practice Recommendations for Contraceptive Use, 2024. MMWR Recomm Rep 2024;73(RR-3):1–77.
  3. CDC. Testosterone Use and Risk for Pregnancy (US SPR 2024 companion guidance).
  4. FDA. FDA Approves First Nonprescription Daily Oral Contraceptive (Opill, norgestrel 0.075 mg). July 13, 2023.
  5. Phexxi (lactic acid/citric acid/potassium bitartrate) FDA prescribing information, 2020; AMPOWER phase 3 trial.
  6. Turok DK, et al. Levonorgestrel vs. Copper IUDs for Emergency Contraception. N Engl J Med 2021;384:335–344.

lessons.title: Management of Decompensated Cirrhosis
lessons.subtitle: Optimized lesson package · Facilitator-led interactive chalk talk for IM residents · 35–40 min
lessons.credit: Adapted from TeachIM.org — Management of Decompensated Cirrhosis (Zhang, Fainstad; Jan 2020), CC BY-NC 4.0. Optimized June 2026: guideline currency verified, one deck typo corrected, MELD 3.0 / terlipressin / Baveno VII updates added, pedagogy restructured, assessments added. Companion deck: Decompensated_Cirrhosis_Optimized.pptx (original interactive board, surgically corrected).
objectives: Differentiate compensated from decompensated cirrhosis.
Recognize the significance of the MELD score (now MELD 3.0) for prognosis and transplant allocation.
Evaluate a patient presenting with acute decompensation.
Evaluate and manage the common complications: hepatic encephalopathy, ascites, variceal bleeding, hepatorenal syndrome, and spontaneous bacterial peritonitis.

prework.q: 1. What single procedure should essentially every patient admitted with cirrhosis and ascites receive?
prework.a: A diagnostic paracentesis (SAAG, cell count with differential, culture) — to evaluate for SBP and characterize the ascites, even without classic symptoms.

prework.q: 2. What defines decompensation in a patient with known cirrhosis?
prework.a: A rise in the MELD score or development of a new complication: ascites, variceal bleeding, hepatic encephalopathy, SBP, or hepatorenal syndrome.

prework.q: 3. A cirrhotic with ascitic fluid neutrophil count (ANC) of 400 — what is the diagnosis and treatment?
prework.a: Spontaneous bacterial peritonitis (ANC ≥250). Treat with cefotaxime or ceftriaxone PLUS IV albumin (1.5 g/kg day 1, 1 g/kg day 3) to reduce HRS and mortality.

segments.label: Hook (0:00–0:02)
segments.html:
"Compensated cirrhosis has a median survival measured in years; the day a patient first develops ascites, a variceal bleed, or encephalopathy, that clock resets to a fraction of it. This talk is about recognizing that pivot — and reacting to each complication fast." Use the interactive board’s "cirrhosis one-liner" as the organizing spine.

ASK — "Give me the cirrhosis one-liner for any admitted patient: cause, compensated or not, MELD, and prior complications." Build it live.

segments.label: Objective 1 & 2 — Compensated vs decompensated; MELD (0:02–0:10)
segments.html:
The "one-liner" frames every cirrhosis patient: (1) cause, (2) compensated vs decompensated, (3) baseline MELD, (4) prior complications.

Cause of cirrhosisState & prognosis
Alcohol (60–70%), MASH (10%), HCV/HBV (10%), hemochromatosis, uncommon (AIH, PSC, PBC)Compensated (no complications): median survival >12 y. Decompensated (+complications): survival falls steeply.

MELD 3.0 (Cr, bilirubin, INR, Na, albumin, sex) predicts ~3-month mortality and drives US transplant allocation (replaced MELD-Na in 2023):

MELD~3-month mortality
203–4%
3027–32%
4065–66%
Pearl. Decompensation is a prognostic state change, not just a symptom. Once a patient decompensates, transplant evaluation belongs on the problem list.

segments.label: Objective 3 — Evaluating acute decompensation (0:10–0:16)
segments.html:
ASK — "Three precipitants drive most cirrhosis complications. What are they, and what’s your reflex work-up?"

Overlapping precipitants: infection/SBP, GI bleeding, and portal vein thrombosis. Reflex work-up: diagnostic paracentesis, evaluate for bleeding, and RUQ ultrasound with Doppler. Recheck MELD components (Cr, bilirubin, INR, Na).

Pearl. "Essentially everyone admitted with cirrhosis and ascites gets a diagnostic paracentesis." Missed SBP is a preventable death.

segments.label: Objective 4 — Managing complications (0:16–0:38)
segments.html:
ComplicationCause/precipitantEvaluationManagement
Ascites & hepatic hydrothoraxDiet, medications, PVTDx paracentesis (SAAG, cell count, culture); RUQ US w/ DopplerSodium restriction; diuretics (spironolactone ± furosemide); large-volume para/thora with albumin
AKI / hepatorenal syndrome↓ volume / GI bleed; infection/SBPUA, urine lytes; albumin challenge; diagnostic paracentesisHRS-AKI: albumin + terlipressin (first-line) or octreotide/midodrine + albumin; hold diuretics/nephrotoxins
Hepatic encephalopathyInfection/SBP, meds/drugs, GI bleed, PVTCultures, diagnostic paracentesis; ± RUQ US Doppler; ± ammoniaLactulose; rifaximin; PEG (HELP trial) as an alternative for overt HE
GI bleed (varices, PHG, hemorrhoids)Portal hypertensionCBC, coags, fibrinogen; urgent EGDPantoprazole, octreotide, ceftriaxone/cefotaxime (prophylaxis), urgent EGD; restrictive transfusion (Hgb ~7)
Spontaneous bacterial peritonitis—Diagnostic paracentesis (ANC ≥250 = SBP)IV cefotaxime or ceftriaxone PLUS albumin
ASK — "Which two SBP patients especially need albumin, and why?" (Renal dysfunction or bilirubin >4 — to prevent HRS.)
Pearl. For acute variceal bleed, the antibiotic (ceftriaxone) is as much a survival intervention as the octreotide and the scope — prophylaxis reduces rebleeding, infection, and mortality.

segments.label: Retrieval close (0:38–0:40)
segments.html: Board off: "Reconstruct the one-liner, the three precipitants, the SBP rule (ANC ≥250 → cefotaxime + albumin), and first-line HRS-AKI therapy." Fill gaps from what they rebuild.

cases.title: Case 1 — New ascites
cases.stem: 54-year-old with alcohol-related cirrhosis presents with abdominal distension and a 6-lb weight gain. Afebrile, BP 108/64. Moderate ascites on exam.
cases.reveal: Diagnostic paracentesis — SAAG (≥1.1 g/dL confirms portal hypertension), cell count with differential, and culture. Manage uncomplicated ascites with sodium restriction and diuretics (spironolactone ± furosemide); large-volume paracentesis with albumin for tense ascites.

cases.title: Case 2 — Fever and abdominal pain
cases.stem: The same patient returns febrile with diffuse abdominal pain. Ascitic fluid ANC is 480.
cases.reveal: Spontaneous bacterial peritonitis (ANC ≥250). Treat with cefotaxime or ceftriaxone PLUS IV albumin (1.5 g/kg day 1, 1 g/kg day 3) — albumin reduces HRS and mortality, especially with renal dysfunction or bilirubin >4.

cases.title: Case 3 — Rising creatinine
cases.stem: A cirrhotic with ascites develops creatinine rising from 0.9 to 2.3 despite holding diuretics; urine is bland, no proteinuria, FeNa low, and no improvement after 2 days of albumin.
cases.reveal: Hepatorenal syndrome (HRS-AKI) — a diagnosis of exclusion after volume expansion with albumin and stopping nephrotoxins/diuretics. Treat with terlipressin + albumin (first-line) or octreotide/midodrine + albumin; evaluate for transplant.

cases.title: Case 4 — Hematemesis
cases.stem: A cirrhotic presents with hematemesis and melena, HR 112, BP 96/58.
cases.reveal: Resuscitate with a restrictive transfusion strategy (Hgb ~7); start octreotide and pantoprazole; give ceftriaxone prophylaxis; arrange urgent EGD for band ligation. Antibiotics independently reduce rebleeding and mortality.

mcqs.stem: A 60-year-old with cirrhosis and ascites is admitted with worsening encephalopathy but no fever or abdominal pain. Which step is most important?
opts: Check an ammonia level to confirm HE
mcqs.exp: B. Essentially every admitted cirrhotic with ascites needs a diagnostic paracentesis — SBP is a common, often silent precipitant of HE. A/E wrong: treat HE but miss the precipitant. C wrong: not the first step. D wrong: ammonia neither confirms nor excludes HE and doesn’t change management.

mcqs.stem: A cirrhotic with ascitic ANC of 600 is diagnosed with SBP. Beyond cefotaxime, which intervention most reduces hepatorenal syndrome and mortality?
mcqs.exp: A — IV albumin (1.5 g/kg day 1, 1 g/kg day 3) reduces HRS and mortality in SBP. B wrong: FFP isn’t indicated without bleeding. C wrong: octreotide is for variceal bleeding/HRS regimens, not SBP. D/E wrong: neither addresses the SBP-related renal risk; diuretics may worsen it.

mcqs.stem: Which best describes the current US liver-transplant allocation score?
opts: MELD-Na, using Cr, bilirubin, INR, and sodium
MELD 3.0, which adds albumin and sex
mcqs.exp: B. MELD 3.0 (2023) replaced MELD-Na and adds serum albumin and female sex, improving equity. A/D wrong: superseded. C wrong: Child-Pugh is prognostic but not the allocation score. E wrong: FIB-4 is a fibrosis estimate, not an allocation tool.

mcqs.stem: A cirrhotic with ascites develops AKI (Cr 0.9 → 2.4). Urinalysis is bland, no proteinuria; creatinine does not improve after stopping diuretics and 2 days of albumin. What is the most appropriate treatment?
opts: Loop diuretics to increase urine output
mcqs.exp: B. This is HRS-AKI after excluding other causes and a failed albumin challenge — treat with terlipressin (first-line) + albumin, or octreotide/midodrine + albumin. A wrong: more crystalloid won’t help and may worsen ascites. C wrong: diuretics worsen HRS. D wrong: no infection is described. E wrong: dialysis is a bridge for refractory cases/transplant candidates, not first-line medical therapy.

mcqs.stem: A patient with acute variceal hemorrhage is being resuscitated. Which transfusion target and adjunct are most appropriate?
opts: Transfuse to Hgb ≥10; no antibiotics needed
Restrictive strategy plus prophylactic FFP to normalize INR
mcqs.exp: B. A restrictive transfusion threshold (~7 g/dL) improves survival in acute UGIB (Villanueva, NEJM 2013), and antibiotic prophylaxis (ceftriaxone) reduces rebleeding, infection, and death in cirrhotic variceal bleeding. A/C wrong: liberal transfusion worsens portal pressure/outcomes. D wrong: hemodynamically significant bleeding still needs resuscitation. E wrong: routine FFP to "correct" INR is not beneficial and may volume-overload.

anki: The "cirrhosis one-liner" — 4 elements?
Cause; compensated vs decompensated; baseline MELD; history of complications.
Define decompensation in cirrhosis?
A rise in MELD or a new complication (ascites, variceal bleed, HE, SBP, HRS).
Median survival: compensated vs decompensated cirrhosis?
Compensated >12 y; decompensated much shorter (~2 y by current data).
Current US liver-transplant allocation score?
MELD 3.0 (adds albumin + sex; replaced MELD-Na in 2023).
MELD 3-month mortality at 20 / 30 / 40?
Three overlapping precipitants of decompensation?
Infection/SBP, GI bleeding, portal vein thrombosis.
Reflex work-up of acute decompensation?
Diagnostic paracentesis, evaluate for bleeding, RUQ US with Doppler.
Who gets a diagnostic paracentesis?
Essentially everyone admitted with cirrhosis + ascites.
SBP diagnostic threshold?
Ascitic fluid ANC ≥250 cells/µL.
SBP treatment?
Cefotaxime or ceftriaxone PLUS IV albumin (1.5 g/kg day 1, 1 g/kg day 3).
Uncomplicated ascites management?
Sodium restriction + diuretics (spironolactone ± furosemide); LVP + albumin if tense.
HRS-AKI first-line treatment?
Albumin + terlipressin (first-line) or octreotide/midodrine + albumin; stop diuretics/nephrotoxins.
HRS nomenclature update?
Type 1 HRS → HRS-AKI (ICA 2015 / AASLD 2021); terlipressin FDA-approved 2022.
Hepatic encephalopathy treatment?
Lactulose + rifaximin; PEG is an alternative for overt HE; find/treat the precipitant.
Acute variceal bleed bundle?
Restrictive transfusion (Hgb ~7), octreotide, PPI, ceftriaxone prophylaxis, urgent EGD.
Baveno VII prevention pearl?
Carvedilol in compensated cirrhosis with CSPH reduces first decompensation and mortality.
NASH/NAFLD current names?
MASH / MASLD (2023 nomenclature).
lessons.dot:
#Cirrhosis 2/2 *[EtOH/MASH/HCV/HBV/hemochromatosis/AIH/PSC/PBC], *[compensated/DECOMPENSATED], MELD 3.0 *
 one-liner: cause * | compensated? * | MELD 3.0 * (Cr/Tbili/INR/Na/albumin/sex) | prior complications *[ascites/VB/HE/SBP/HRS]
 acute decompensation = ↑MELD or new complication; screen precipitants: infection/SBP, GI bleed, PVT
 reflex w/u: DIAGNOSTIC PARACENTESIS (SAAG, cell count, cx); assess bleeding; RUQ US w/ doppler
COMPLICATIONS:
 #ascites/hydrothorax: Na restrict <2g + diuretics [spironolactone *: furosemide ~5:2]; LVP + albumin 6-8 g/L removed >5L
 #SBP: ANC *≥250 → cefotaxime/ceftriaxone + ALBUMIN 1.5 g/kg d1, 1 g/kg d3 (esp Cr>1 or Tbili>4)
 #HRS-AKI: hold diuretics/nephrotoxins, albumin challenge 1 g/kg; if no response → terlipressin + albumin [1st-line] | octreotide+midodrine + albumin
 #HE: lactulose titrate to 3-4 BM/d + rifaximin 550 mg BID; treat precipitant; PEG alt for overt HE
 #variceal bleed: restrictive transfusion Hgb *~7; octreotide gtt; PPI; CEFTRIAXONE ppx; urgent EGD/banding
PROGNOSIS/dispo: MELD 3.0 * → 3-mo mortality; transplant eval if decompensated
 prevention (compensated + CSPH): carvedilol to prevent 1st decompensation (Baveno VII)
takehome: MELD 3.0 predicts 3-month mortality and drives US transplant allocation (replaced MELD-Na in 2023).
Decompensation = a rise in MELD or a new complication; precipitants overlap (infection/SBP, GI bleeding, portal vein thrombosis).
Initial work-up: diagnostic paracentesis, evaluate for bleeding, RUQ US with Doppler.
Essentially everyone admitted with cirrhosis and ascites gets a diagnostic paracentesis. SBP (ANC ≥250) = cefotaxime/ceftriaxone + albumin.
HRS-AKI: terlipressin (first-line) or octreotide/midodrine + albumin. Variceal bleed: restrictive transfusion, octreotide, PPI, ceftriaxone, urgent EGD.

extras.label: 2021–2023 Guideline Update
extras.html:
The board’s framework is current; these refinements are surfaced here and on the appended deck slide:

  • MELD 3.0 replaced MELD-Na for US liver allocation (OPTN, 2023) — adds serum albumin and female sex and re-weights the other variables, modestly lowering waitlist mortality and improving access for women.
  • Terlipressin is FDA-approved (Sept 2022) and AASLD-recommended first-line for HRS-AKI (with albumin); octreotide/midodrine + albumin is the alternative where terlipressin isn’t available. Type 1 HRS is now HRS-AKI (ICA 2015 / AASLD 2021).
  • Baveno VII (2022): in compensated cirrhosis with clinically significant portal hypertension (CSPH), carvedilol (NSBB of choice) reduces first decompensation — chiefly ascites — and mortality (PREDESCI).
  • Nomenclature (2023): NASH → MASH, NAFLD → MASLD; "MetALD" for metabolic dysfunction + moderate alcohol. The board’s "NASH (10%)" cause reads as MASH.
  • Prognosis context: the board cites compensated median survival >12 y and a low figure for decompensated disease (tied to its Salpeter reference); current data place median survival after decompensation closer to ~2 years, with the most advanced sub-phenotypes far shorter.

lessons.refs:
1. Ge PS, Runyon BA. Treatment of Patients with Cirrhosis. N Engl J Med. 2016;375:767–777.
  2. Kim WR, Mannalithara A, Heimbach JK, et al. MELD 3.0: The Model for End-Stage Liver Disease Updated for the Modern Era. Gastroenterology. 2021;161(6):1887–1895. (newly cited)
  3. de Franchis R, Bosch J, Garcia-Tsao G, et al. Baveno VII — Renewing consensus in portal hypertension. J Hepatol. 2022;76(4):959–974. (newly cited)
  4. Biggins SW, Angeli P, Garcia-Tsao G, et al. AASLD Practice Guidance: Diagnosis, Evaluation, and Management of Ascites, SBP, and Hepatorenal Syndrome. Hepatology. 2021;74(2):1014–1048. (newly cited)
  5. Villanueva C, Colomo A, Bosch A, et al. Transfusion Strategies for Acute Upper Gastrointestinal Bleeding. N Engl J Med. 2013;368:11–21.
  6. Sharma BC, et al. Rifaximin Plus Lactulose vs Lactulose Alone in Overt Hepatic Encephalopathy. Am J Gastroenterol. 2013;108:1458–1463.
  7. Rahimi RS, et al. Lactulose vs PEG 3350 for Overt Hepatic Encephalopathy (HELP trial). JAMA Intern Med. 2014;174(11):1727–1733.

lessons.title: Depression and Anxiety
lessons.subtitle: Optimized lesson package · Facilitator-led interactive chalk talk for IM residents · 10–15 min (talk) / +10–15 min (cases) · divisible into two talks
lessons.credit: Adapted from TeachIM.org — Depression and Anxiety (Trefilek, Melton, Guinn, Fainstad; Section Ed. Brett; Univ. of Colorado; published Dec 2021, updated Nov 2022), CC BY-NC 4.0. Optimized June 2026: guideline currency verified against USPSTF 2023; no deck errors found (append-only edit); pedagogy restructured, assessments added. Companion deck: Depression_and_Anxiety_Optimized.pptx (original interactive board, all animations preserved). Educational use only — clinician-reviewed before teaching.
objectives: Choose the appropriate screening and diagnostic tools for anxiety and depression (PHQ-2/PHQ-4 vs PHQ-9/GAD-7).
Differentiate generalized anxiety disorder (GAD) and unipolar major depression from behavioral-health mimics that are managed differently.
Construct a first-line treatment strategy, including the monitoring and follow-up timeline after starting pharmacologic and/or non-pharmacologic therapy.
Select the right pharmacologic agent based on key clinical features, comorbidities, and patient preference (side-effect–guided selection).

prework.q: Q1. Which screening tools diagnose and monitor depression and anxiety, and which are only quick screens?
prework.a: PHQ-9 (depression) and GAD-7 (anxiety) diagnose and track response. PHQ-2 and PHQ-4 are brief screens for general populations — don't rely on them when clinical suspicion is high.

prework.q: Q2. Name three features that should make you pause before starting an antidepressant because the patient may actually have bipolar disorder.
prework.a: Age <25, a family history of bipolar disorder, and multiple (≥3–5) recurrences of major depression. Antidepressant monotherapy can unmask mania/hypomania.

prework.q: Q3. A patient on many medications needs an SSRI. Which one minimizes drug–drug interactions, and what is its main caution?
prework.a: Citalopram — relatively little CYP P450 inhibition. Main caution: dose-dependent QTc prolongation (max 40 mg/day; 20 mg in older adults).

segments.label: Hook (2 min)
segments.html: "Half of clinical depression walks out of our clinics undiagnosed — not because it's subtle, but because it hides behind fatigue, pain, and poor sleep, and we don't ask." Ask the room: how does depression actually show up in your panel? Park the somatic answers — you'll use them again at Objective 2 and in every case.

segments.label: Objective 1 — Screening & diagnostic tools
segments.html:
ASK: "What do you actually order — PHQ-2, PHQ-9, GAD-7, PHQ-4 — and when?" Collect answers, then click to reveal the tool map.

Why screen: screening roughly doubles detection of depression (Mitchell, Lancet 2009), and treatment improves quality of life and reduces mortality and economic burden. Depression and anxiety co-occur in ~39–62% of cases. The USPSTF recommends screening all adults for depression (and, as of 2023, adults <65 for anxiety).

ToolUse
PHQ-2First 2 PHQ-9 items — quick screen for general populations
PHQ-4PHQ-2 + first 2 GAD-7 items — combined quick screen
PHQ-9Diagnoses severity AND monitors response for depression
GAD-7Diagnoses AND monitors response for anxiety
Special situationsEdinburgh (perinatal — drops somatic items); GDS-5 (geriatric)
Pearl — PHQ-9 score bands: 5–9 mild, 10–14 moderate, 15–19 moderately severe, ≥20 severe (≥10 = likely major depression). It measures and trends severity but doesn't, by itself, diagnose — diagnosis needs DSM-5 criteria plus functional impairment.

segments.label: Objective 2 — Diagnose & differentiate
segments.html:
ASK: "Which DSM-5 symptoms are required to call it major depression?" Take answers before revealing the criteria.

Unipolar major depression: ≥5 of 9 symptoms for ≥2 weeks nearly every day, and must include depressed mood or loss of interest/pleasure (SIG-E-CAPS: Sleep, Interest, Guilt, Energy, Concentration, Appetite, Psychomotor, Suicidality). GAD: excessive, hard-to-control worry more days than not for ≥6 months plus ≥3 of (restlessness, fatigue, poor concentration, irritability, muscle tension, sleep disturbance). Both require functional impairment and exclusion of substance/medical causes.

ASK: "Before you write for an SSRI — what would make you worry this is bipolar disorder?"
Pearl — Screen for bipolarity (age <25, family history of bipolar disorder, ≥3–5 recurrences of MDD) — antidepressant monotherapy can precipitate mania. Keep mimics on the differential: adjustment disorder, ADHD, borderline PD, complicated grief, delirium, hypothyroidism, schizophrenia/schizoaffective, substance use; for anxiety also panic, OCD, separation anxiety, PTSD.

No mandated lab panel, but for new-onset symptoms consider CBC (anemia), TSH (thyroid), HCG (pregnancy — also guides safe drug choice), and BMP (renal dosing).

segments.label: Objective 3 — Treatment strategy & follow-up
segments.html:
ASK: "After you start a drug, when does the patient come back — and why does the timing matter?"

GAD and MDD share a first-line algorithm: medication and/or psychotherapy have comparable response rates and are additive. Set a target (patient-defined goals; remission = absence of sad mood and reduced interest for ~3 weeks), then plan close follow-up:

CheckpointPurposeAction
1–2 weeksConfirm no worsening suicidality/mania; no intolerable side effectsIf intolerable → choose another agent
2–4 weeksAssess response and resolution of early side effectsNo response → switch; partial → increase dose, recheck in 2 wk; complete → maintain (consider discontinuation at 3–9 mo)
Pearl — The first couple of weeks are the danger zone for emergent suicidality/mania and for early drop-out from transient side effects. A brief touchpoint (prescriber, RN, or therapist) prevents both. When stopping, taper slowly — withdrawal can be prolonged, and per ANTLER (NEJM 2021) relapse is common whether patients continue or stop.

segments.label: Objective 4 — Pick the agent by side-effect profile
segments.html:
ASK: "Give me a patient feature, and I'll make you pick the drug." Run a few before revealing the medication grid.

SRIs (especially SSRIs) are first-line with the best tolerability. Choose by cost, prior response, preference, and side effects — and use side effects to your advantage:

Want…Reach for
Weight gain + sedation (poor appetite, insomnia, esp. elderly)Mirtazapine
Few drug–drug interactions (polypharmacy)Citalopram/escitalopram (watch QTc)
More activationSertraline, fluoxetine, bupropion
Comorbid chronic painSNRI (duloxetine, venlafaxine)
Avoid weight gain / shortest half-life issuesAvoid paroxetine; fluoxetine has the longest half-life (less withdrawal)
Pearl — "Start low and go slow," especially in older adults. A dramatic, "better-than-ever," high-energy response after a switch can be a good response — or unmasked bipolar disorder. Re-assess the diagnosis.

segments.label: Safety assessment (every visit)
segments.html: Suicide risk assessment is a critical step at each visit. Prediction is poor, but use clear, direct language ("Are you having thoughts of killing yourself right now?" — not "hurting yourself"). Asking does not increase risk. Know your site's safety-plan handout and immediately available mental-health resources; tools include the PHQ-9 item 9 and the Columbia Suicide Severity Rating Scale.

segments.label: Retrieval close (2 min)
segments.html: Cover the board. Have learners rebuild from memory: (1) which tools diagnose vs screen, (2) the two required MDD symptoms, (3) the 1–2-week and 2–4-week follow-up logic, (4) one "feature → drug" pairing. Fill gaps from their answers, not your slides.

cases.title: Case 1 — 25-year-old man, new-onset low mood
cases.stem: 25M establishing care, no PMH; over weeks since moving cities, increasing loneliness, sadness, and job stress.
cases.reveal: PHQ-9 (high clinical suspicion warrants the full tool, not PHQ-2/PHQ-4); also consider GAD-7. Note: screening tools alone don't diagnose.

cases.title: Case 2 — 79-year-old woman, fatigue & poor concentration
cases.stem: 79F (HTN, RA, CAD) with 6 months of fatigue and poor concentration since an MI; cardiac workup normal.
cases.reveal: Broaden the differential for vague geriatric symptoms: EKG, CBC, BMP, TSH, plus PHQ-9 or GDS-5 and a MOCA. Depression is especially common after MI and stroke.

cases.title: Case 3 — 28-year-old woman, somatic complaints & anxiety
cases.stem: 28F with years of arthralgias and low back pain, longstanding "restless/on edge" feelings, intermittent untreated depression; drinks 2 glasses of wine nightly for sleep.
cases.reveal: Substance use (the AUDIT/CAGE) as a contributor to anxiety and depression. She has mixed GAD + unipolar depression — a common presentation.

mcqs.stem: A 34-year-old woman has a PHQ-2 of 4 at a routine visit and reports 3 weeks of low mood and anhedonia. What is the most appropriate next step?
opts: Start an SSRI based on the PHQ-2
Administer the PHQ-9 to characterize severity and confirm criteria
Order a TSH and defer any mental-health assessment
Refer to psychiatry before any further evaluation
mcqs.exp: B. A positive PHQ-2 is a screen; it doesn't diagnose or grade severity. The next step is the PHQ-9 (plus DSM-5 criteria and functional impairment). A is premature (no severity/criteria yet); C ignores a positive screen with symptoms; D inappropriately defers; E is unnecessary for an uncomplicated presentation manageable in primary care.

mcqs.stem: A 22-year-old man with a first episode of major depression and a father with bipolar I disorder is about to start treatment. What most changes your plan?
opts: Nothing — start an SSRI as usual
His age and family history raise bipolar risk; screen carefully before antidepressant monotherapy
mcqs.exp: B. Age <25, a family history of bipolar disorder, and recurrent MDD are red flags for bipolarity; antidepressant monotherapy can unmask mania. Screen for prior (hypo)manic episodes first. A ignores the risk; C is not a treatment for the underlying disorder and carries dependence risk; D overstates (antidepressants aren't absolutely contraindicated, but use caution); E commits to a mood stabilizer without establishing bipolar diagnosis.

mcqs.stem: A 70-year-old woman on warfarin, amiodarone, and metoprolol develops major depression. Which SSRI best minimizes drug–drug interactions, and what must you monitor?
mcqs.exp: C. Citalopram (and escitalopram) have relatively little CYP P450 inhibition, useful in polypharmacy; the key caution is dose-dependent QTc prolongation (cap 40 mg/day; 20 mg in older adults) — especially relevant with amiodarone. Fluoxetine and fluvoxamine are strong CYP inhibitors (raising interaction risk with warfarin); paroxetine is a potent CYP2D6 inhibitor with anticholinergic effects. Hyponatremia is a class effect of all SSRIs, not sertraline-specific.

mcqs.stem: A patient started sertraline 10 days ago for depression and now reports markedly elevated energy, decreased need for sleep, rapid speech, and "feeling better than ever." What is the best next step?
opts: Congratulate the rapid response and continue
Reassess for a (hypo)manic episode / bipolar disorder
Add a benzodiazepine for the insomnia
mcqs.exp: C. A rapid surge in energy with reduced sleep need shortly after an antidepressant should prompt evaluation for antidepressant-unmasked (hypo)mania. A and B could worsen mania; D treats a symptom while missing the diagnosis; E continues antidepressant monotherapy without reassessing. Re-diagnose before adjusting therapy.

mcqs.stem: A 29-year-old woman with GAD and comorbid chronic low back pain wants pharmacotherapy. Which first-line choice best addresses both?
mcqs.exp: C — duloxetine, an SNRI that is first-line for GAD and also has evidence for chronic musculoskeletal/neuropathic pain. Paroxetine works for anxiety but adds weight gain/anticholinergic burden and offers no analgesic benefit. Bupropion is activating and can worsen anxiety and is not first-line for GAD. Mirtazapine helps sleep/appetite but isn't an analgesic. Scheduled daily benzodiazepines risk dependence and aren't first-line maintenance therapy.

anki: Why screen for depression in primary care?
~50% of clinical depression is missed without screening; untreated depression worsens quality of life, mortality, and economic burden. USPSTF 2023: screen all adults (Grade B).
PHQ-2 / PHQ-4 vs PHQ-9 / GAD-7 — when to use which?
PHQ-2 and PHQ-4 are brief SCREENS for general populations. PHQ-9 (depression) and GAD-7 (anxiety) DIAGNOSE and MONITOR response — use them when pretest probability is high.
PHQ-9 score interpretation?
5–9 mild, 10–14 moderate, 15–19 moderately severe, ≥20 severe; ≥10 = likely major depression. It measures severity/trends response but does not by itself diagnose.
DSM-5 criteria for unipolar major depression?
≥5 of 9 symptoms for ≥2 weeks nearly every day, and MUST include depressed mood or loss of interest/pleasure; symptoms cause functional impairment and aren't due to a substance/medical condition.
DSM-5 criteria for generalized anxiety disorder?

Excessive, hard-to-control anxiety more days than not for ≥6 months about multiple events, plus ≥3 symptoms (restlessness, fatigue, poor concentration, irritability, muscle tension, sleep disturbance), with functional impairment.
Which screening labs to consider for new depression/anxiety?
No routine panel is mandated. Consider CBC (anemia), TSH (thyroid), HCG (pregnancy — also informs drug safety), and BMP (renal dosing). Rule out medical/psychiatric mimics.
Clues that raise suspicion for bipolar disorder before starting an antidepressant?
Age <25, family history of bipolar disorder, and multiple (≥3–5) recurrences of major depression. Antidepressants can unmask mania/hypomania.
First-line treatment for both GAD and unipolar depression?
SRIs (especially SSRIs) — comparable response to psychotherapy, and combination is additive. Pick the agent by comorbidity, prior response, cost, preference, and side-effect profile.
Why is short-term follow-up after starting an antidepressant critical?
The first 1–2 weeks carry risk of emergent suicidality or mania, intolerable (often transient) side effects, and early discontinuation. Early contact improves safety and adherence.
Typical antidepressant treatment timeline / response check?

First follow-up at 1–2 weeks (confirm no worsening suicidality/mania or intolerable side effects); second at 2–4 weeks to assess response. Increase dose, switch, or maintain accordingly; consider discontinuation at 3–9 months.
Which SSRI is preferred in a patient on many medications, and why?
Citalopram — relatively few CYP P450 drug–drug interactions (note dose-dependent QTc prolongation; cap 40 mg, 20 mg in elderly).
When is mirtazapine a useful choice?
When weight gain and sedation are desirable (e.g., an older patient with poor appetite and insomnia).
Which antidepressant to avoid if a patient is concerned about weight gain, and which SSRI has the longest half-life?
Avoid paroxetine (weight gain, anticholinergic, short half-life → withdrawal). Fluoxetine has the longest half-life (less withdrawal, needs no taper).
When might an SNRI be first-line, and what's a hidden risk of dramatic improvement after a switch?
SNRI (e.g., duloxetine, venlafaxine) when comorbid chronic pain. A sudden "better than ever"/high-energy response can signal unmasked bipolar disorder — reassess the diagnosis.
How should antidepressants be stopped, and what does ANTLER (NEJM 2021) show?
Taper slowly (withdrawal can be prolonged). ANTLER: discontinuation after long-term use → ~56% relapse vs ~39% with maintenance — relapse is common either way.
How should you ask about suicide, and does asking increase risk?

Use clear, direct language ("Are you having thoughts of killing yourself right now?"). Asking does NOT increase suicide risk or rates. Assess at each visit; know your site's safety plan and resources.
USPSTF 2023 screening recommendations for depression and anxiety in adults?
Screen ALL adults for depression (Grade B) and adults <65 for anxiety disorders (Grade B), including pregnant/postpartum. Insufficient evidence for anxiety screening at ≥65 (I).
Newer oral antidepressant agents (2022–2023) and their niche?

Dextromethorphan–bupropion (Auvelity, FDA 2022) — rapid-acting oral NMDA antagonist for MDD. Zuranolone (Zurzuvae, FDA 2023) — first oral agent for postpartum depression (14-day course), not general MDD.
lessons.dot:
#Depression/Anxiety (unipolar MDD / GAD) - outpatient
dx: *[MDD / GAD / mixed MDD+GAD]; DSM-5 criteria met *[y/n]; functional impairment *[social/occupational/other]
severity (validated): PHQ-9 *__ [5-9 mild/10-14 mod/15-19 mod-severe/>=20 severe]; GAD-7 *__ [5-9 mild/10-14 mod/>=15 severe]
 -> tool diagnoses AND trends response; PHQ-2/PHQ-4 are screens only (USPSTF 2023: screen all adults dep, <65 anxiety)
r/o mimics/contributors: TSH *__, CBC *__, BMP *__, HCG *[neg/n-a]; substance use [AUDIT/CAGE *__]; bipolar screen [age<25 / fam hx / >=3-5 recurrences] *[neg]
SAFETY: SI *[denies/passive/active]; plan/intent *[no]; access to means *__; PHQ-9 item-9 *__; Columbia *[neg]; safety plan + crisis resources given *[y]
plan - 1st line SRI *[sertraline/escitalopram/citalopram(QTc, max 40mg/20mg elderly)/fluoxetine(long t1/2)/paroxetine(avoid if wt-gain)]
 +/- psychotherapy [CBT] (additive); treat somatic sx [PT/HA care]; +/- SNRI [duloxetine/venlafaxine] if comorbid pain
 side-effect-guided: mirtazapine if need wt-gain/sedation; bupropion if activating/avoid sexual SE (avoid if sz/eating d/o)
monitoring: f/u *1-2wk (emergent suicidality/mania, intolerable SE, adherence); *2-4wk (response)
 -> no response: switch SRI; partial: ^dose, recheck 2wk; complete + tolerating: maintain, consider d/c at 3-9mo
AVOID: antidepressant monotherapy if suspect bipolar; benzo as maintenance monotherapy; abrupt stop (taper - withdrawal prolonged; ANTLER NEJM 2021: relapse common either way)
RTC/message for: worsening mood/SI, new (hypo)mania (^energy, dec sleep need), intolerable SE
takehome: PHQ-9 and GAD-7 diagnose and monitor unipolar depression and GAD; PHQ-2/PHQ-4 are screens only — don't rely on them when suspicion is high.
Short-term follow-up (within ~1–2 weeks) of starting a drug is critical to catch emergent suicidality/mania, intolerable side effects, and early drop-out.
SRIs are first-line for both disorders; choose by comorbidity, prior response, cost, preference, and side-effect profile.
Treat somatic symptoms too (PT, headache care) and keep re-screening for bipolar disorder and substance use.
USPSTF 2023: screen all adults for depression and adults <65 for anxiety. Taper antidepressants slowly when stopping (ANTLER).

extras.label: 2022–2025 Practice Update NEW — projected on the appended deck slide
extras.html:
The deck's framework (PHQ-9/GAD-7, SRI first-line, close early follow-up, side-effect–guided selection) was verified current. Practice-relevant additions since the lesson's 2021–22 publication:

  • USPSTF 2023 (final): screen all adults for depression (Grade B) and adults <65 for anxiety disorders (Grade B), including pregnant and postpartum adults. Insufficient evidence to screen for anxiety at ≥65 (I statement). No recommendation for routine suicide-risk screening in asymptomatic adults (I).
  • Discontinuation evidence: the ANTLER trial (Lewis, NEJM 2021) — stopping long-term antidepressants led to ~56% relapse vs ~39% with maintenance; taper slowly, and counsel that relapse is common either way.
  • Newer oral agents (context — not first-line for routine GAD/MDD): dextromethorphan–bupropion (Auvelity, FDA Aug 2022) — first rapid-acting oral NMDA-antagonist for MDD, separating from placebo by week 1; zuranolone (Zurzuvae, FDA Aug 2023) — first oral agent for postpartum depression (14-day course; not approved for general MDD); intranasal esketamine (Spravato) for treatment-resistant MDD, expanded to monotherapy in 2025.

These do not change the chalk talk's first-line algorithm; they update the screening recommendation and add newer second-/third-line and postpartum-specific options for the appended slide.

  1. US Preventive Services Task Force. Screening for Depression and Suicide Risk in Adults: Recommendation Statement. JAMA 2023;329(23):2057–2067 (Grade B for depression).
  2. US Preventive Services Task Force. Screening for Anxiety Disorders in Adults: Recommendation Statement. JAMA 2023;329(24):2163–2170 (Grade B, adults <65; I statement ≥65).
  3. FDA. Auvelity (dextromethorphan–bupropion) approval for major depressive disorder, August 2022.
  4. FDA. FDA Approves First Oral Treatment for Postpartum Depression (zuranolone/Zurzuvae), August 4, 2023.
