lessons.title: Gender Affirming Care
lessons.subtitle: Optimized lesson package · Facilitator-led interactive chalk talk for IM residents · ~25–40 min total
lessons.credit: Adapted from TeachIM.org — Gender Affirming Care (Adams B, Hedian H, Lee R; updated Aug 2024), CC BY-NC 4.0. Optimized June 2026: guideline currency verified, one deck correction made, pedagogy restructured, assessments added. Companion deck: Gender_Affirming_Care_Optimized.pptx (original interactive board, surgically corrected).
objectives: Apply appropriate, patient-centered terminology in clinical interactions with gender-diverse patients.
Evaluate a patient for gender-affirming therapy using an informed-consent framework.
Describe the anticipated effects, timeline, side effects, and monitoring of gender-affirming hormone therapy (GAHT).
Apply additional ethical and medical considerations — fertility, perioperative/inpatient management, VTE on estrogen, cancer screening, and access.

prework.q: Q1. A patient's chart lists "male." How would you ask, in a patient-centered way, about their name, pronouns, gender identity, and the sex assigned at their birth — using four separate questions?
prework.a: "What name do you go by?" (avoid deadnaming) · "What pronouns do you use?" (he/she/they/ze) · "How would you describe your current gender identity?" · "What sex was assigned to you at birth — what was marked on your birth certificate?" Add a focused organ inventory when it changes the differential, using the patient's preferred terms.

prework.q: Q2. Name the two pillars of feminizing hormone therapy and the single pillar of masculinizing therapy, with target hormone levels.
prework.a: Feminizing = estrogen (17β-estradiol; goal estradiol 100–200 pg/mL) PLUS an anti-androgen (spironolactone, 5α-reductase inhibitor, or GnRH agonist) to suppress testosterone <50 ng/dL. Masculinizing = testosterone alone; goal trough total testosterone 400–700 ng/dL.

prework.q: Q3. Which single GAHT medication carries a meaningful thrombotic risk, and what is the lowest-risk way to deliver it?
prework.a: Estrogen → venous thromboembolism. Transdermal (patch) estradiol carries the lowest VTE risk; oral/ethinyl-estradiol formulations carry the highest. A DVT/PE is not an absolute reason to stop — manage patient-centered (switch to transdermal + anticoagulate if continuing).

segments.label: Hook (2 min)
segments.html: "Your next patient's chart says 'male,' but the person in front of you introduces herself as Maria. You have about 90 seconds to build trust. What are the first four things you ask — and what's the one thing that, if you get it wrong, ends the visit?" (Name, pronouns, gender identity, sex assigned at birth — and deadnaming/misgendering is the trust-ender.) This sets up Objective 1.

segments.label: Objective 1 — Terminology & the affirming intake
segments.html:
ASK: "Gender identity, gender expression, sex assigned at birth, and sexual orientation — these are four independent axes. Map a patient who is a trans woman, presents femininely, was AMAB, and is attracted to women." Then click the Gender-Unicorn build.

Key distinctions: gender identity (internal sense of self) vs gender expression (outward presentation) vs sex assigned at birth (AMAB/AFAB; DSD formerly "intersex") vs sexual orientation (attraction). Gender-diverse identities include transgender, nonbinary, and genderfluid. Epidemiology: roughly 1.0% of millennials and 2% of Gen Z identify as gender-diverse; >2.6 million Americans.

Ask about…A patient-centered phrasing
Name"What name do you go by?" — then use it. Avoid deadnaming (using a former name).
Pronouns"What pronouns do you use?" (he/him, she/her, they/them, or neopronouns such as ze/zir)
Gender identity"How would you describe your current gender identity?"
Sex assigned at birth"What sex was assigned to you at birth — what was marked on your birth certificate?"
Organ inventory"What organs do you have, or have had removed, as part of your transition? Is cervix a word you're comfortable with?" — drives screening, not curiosity.
Pearl — recover gracefully from a mistake: if you misname or misgender a patient, the move is Recognize → Apologize → Commit to change → Move on. Don't over-apologize; a brief correction and continuing the visit respectfully does more than a long mea culpa.

segments.label: Objective 2 — Evaluation for therapy
segments.html:
ASK: "Who is allowed to diagnose gender dysphoria and start hormones?" (A generalist — you don't need a specialist or a mental-health gatekeeper to initiate.)

Diagnosis of gender dysphoria uses DSM-5 criteria and can be made by a generalist. Gender incongruence itself is not a mental illness. Evaluation runs on an informed-consent model (WPATH SOC8): assess capacity, screen for contraindications, discuss expectations / anticipated changes / side effects (reversible and irreversible), and offer fertility counseling before starting — hormones can impair fertility.

Informed-consent checklistWhat it means
CapacityPatient understands risks, benefits, reversibility, and alternatives.
Contraindicationse.g., estrogen-sensitive cancer or significant unprovoked VTE for estrogen (see Objective 4).
ExpectationsRealistic changes and timeline; which effects are irreversible.
FertilityCounsel on impact and options (sperm/oocyte banking) before initiation.

Treatment options span more than hormones: GAHT, gender-affirming surgery, voice/vocal therapy, mental-health support — guided by the individual patient's goals, in an interdisciplinary medical home with appropriate monitoring.

segments.label: Objective 3 — The hormones, effects & monitoring
segments.html:
ASK: "Feminizing therapy has two jobs. Name them." (Add estrogen; lower/block testosterone.)

Feminizing

DrugRole / key pointsSide effects & monitoring
17β-estradiolEstrogen. PO, sublingual, transdermal patch, or IM. Goal estradiol 100–200 pg/mL.VTE (patch lowest risk); estrogen-dependent cancers a contraindication. Monitor estradiol, total testosterone, LFTs, lipids.
SpironolactoneMost common anti-androgen.↑K⁺, orthostasis, ↑urination, ↓libido. Monitor K⁺ and Cr.
5α-reductase inhibitorAnti-androgen; generally used with spironolactone.Generally none significant; occasional ↓libido.
GnRH agonistPotent androgen suppression.Cost/access; bone health with prolonged suppression.

Feminizing timeline: ↓spontaneous erections/libido and ↓muscle mass + fat redistribution by ~3 months; breast growth (max 2–5 y, often Tanner 1–2, not reversible); ↓facial/body hair by ~6–12 months (slow, often incomplete — many still need electrolysis).

Masculinizing

DrugRole / key pointsSide effects & monitoring
TestosteroneSingle agent. IM, SC, or topical. Goal trough total testosterone 400–700 ng/dL.Erythrocytosis (check Hct/CBC). Monitor total testosterone (± estradiol).

Masculinizing timeline: ↑skin oil/acne and ↑libido early; voice deepening (not reversible); cessation of menses (~70%; ~30% continue to menstruate); ↑muscle mass and fat redistribution; clitoral enlargement (not reversible); ↑facial/body hair and male-pattern scalp loss over months to years.

ASK: "Both regimens get labs on the same schedule. What is it?" (Levels + exam at 3 and 6 months, then annually.)

segments.label: Objective 4 — Ethics, safety & the hard FAQs
segments.html:
QuestionAnswer
CV risk on estradiol or testosterone?Slightly elevated CV risk; manage with USPSTF screening and risk-factor modification — not a reason to withhold therapy.
DVT/PE on estradiol?Risk-benefit and patient-centered. The patch is lowest-risk; consider anticoagulation rather than reflexively stopping.
Polycythemia on testosterone?Rule out other causes and OSA; decrease dose / increase frequency / switch injection → topical to reduce variability.
Cancer screening?Based on the organs present (not gender identity) — e.g., cervix → cervical screening; breast tissue → breast screening.
Hold hormones perioperatively / when admitted?Do not hold — with the exception of estradiol in a patient with VTE risk factors.
Cost & access?State Medicaid variable; Medicare should cover; surgery often self-pay. Know local legislation.
2025 access context: Since this talk was written (2024), the U.S. coverage and legal landscape has shifted — Executive Order 14187 (Jan 2025), proposed CMS Medicaid/Medicare rules (Dec 2025), and the ACA essential-health-benefit Final Rule (Jun 2025) primarily restrict care and coverage for patients under 19. The adult clinical standard of care is unchanged (WPATH SOC8, Endocrine Society), but coverage and access have become more variable. The deck's "know local legislation" point is now more important than ever — verify current local law and payer policy.

segments.label: Close (2 min)
segments.html: Hide the board. Ask the group to rebuild from memory: the four intake questions, the two feminizing pillars + the masculinizing single agent with target levels, the monitoring schedule, and the one drug-risk pair you must never forget (estrogen → VTE). Then show the take-home slide.

cases.title: Case 1 — The inpatient on masculinizing therapy
cases.stem: A 24-year-old person assigned female at birth (AFAB) on masculinizing therapy for 6 months presents with 2 months of fatigue and 2 weeks of fevers, chills, and dysuria.
cases.reveal: Ask preferred name and pronouns; discuss gender, gender identity, and sex assigned at birth in a patient-centered way (Terminology).

cases.title: Case 2 — Initiating feminizing therapy
cases.stem: A 38-year-old individual whose sex assigned at birth is male presents to clinic to discuss starting feminizing hormones.
cases.reveal: Estradiol, typically plus one or more anti-androgens. Expect ↓strength, fat redistribution, breast-bud development, and often erectile dysfunction by ~3 months; facial/body hair decreases around 6 months.

cases.title: Case 3 — DVT on estradiol (the patient-centered dilemma)
cases.stem: Rain, a 35-year-old trans female / nonbinary person (they/them) on oral estradiol for 2 years, presents with leg pain and swelling. Ultrasound shows a DVT extending through the right popliteal and femoral veins. No chest pain; hemodynamically stable.
cases.reveal: Discuss in a patient-centered way. The principal risk of continuing estradiol is extension of the DVT or conversion to a PE, which can be life-threatening. But the psychological and medical risks of stopping GAHT (depression, suicidality) can also be profound and must be weighed — this is shared decision-making, not an automatic stop.

mcqs.stem: A 29-year-old trans woman has been on oral estradiol and spironolactone for 8 months. She is satisfied with her progress. Labs: estradiol 130 pg/mL, total testosterone 35 ng/dL, K⁺ 5.2 mEq/L, Cr 1.0. What is the most appropriate next step?
opts: Increase estradiol to push the level higher
Stop spironolactone because testosterone is already suppressed
Continue current regimen; recheck potassium and counsel on dietary potassium
mcqs.exp: C. Her estradiol (target 100–200 pg/mL) and testosterone (<50 ng/dL) are both at goal — no dose change is warranted (A, D). The actionable finding is mild hyperkalemia from spironolactone; address it (recheck, dietary counseling, consider dose adjustment) rather than abandoning effective androgen blockade (B). Spironolactone monitoring includes K⁺ and Cr.

mcqs.stem: A 35-year-old trans woman on oral estradiol for 2 years presents with an acute proximal DVT and is hemodynamically stable with no chest symptoms. She strongly wishes to continue feminizing therapy. What is the most appropriate management?
opts: Continue oral estradiol unchanged and start anticoagulation
Switch to transdermal estradiol and start therapeutic anticoagulation
mcqs.exp: C. A DVT is not an absolute reason to stop GAHT — the psychosocial risks of abrupt cessation are real and decisions are shared. The lowest-VTE-risk delivery is transdermal, so switch from oral and add therapeutic anticoagulation (UCSF guidance). Continuing the higher-risk oral route (B) or escalating to ethinyl estradiol (D, the highest-risk estrogen) worsens thrombotic risk. Permanent discontinuation (A) ignores shared decision-making and is reserved for symptomatic PE / instability.

mcqs.stem: A 26-year-old trans man on intramuscular testosterone for 1 year is found to have a hematocrit of 54%. He feels well. What is the best next step?
opts: Evaluate for secondary causes (including OSA) and reduce dose / increase frequency or switch to topical to limit peaks
Begin therapeutic phlebotomy and continue the same regimen
Reassure and recheck in one year
mcqs.exp: B. Erythrocytosis is the characteristic hematologic effect of testosterone. Work up other causes (notably OSA) and reduce variability — lower the dose, increase frequency, or move from injection to topical, which smooths the peaks that drive erythrocytosis. Permanent cessation (A) is excessive. Phlebotomy without changing the driver (C) is incomplete, and ignoring an Hct of 54% (D) is unsafe.

mcqs.stem: A generalist sees a 22-year-old who reports persistent gender incongruence and requests masculinizing therapy. Which statement about initiating care is correct?
opts: A mental-health professional must formally approve hormones before a generalist can prescribe
The diagnosis of gender dysphoria can be made by a generalist, and therapy can start using an informed-consent model
Gender incongruence is itself a psychiatric disorder requiring treatment
Two referral letters are required before starting hormones
mcqs.exp: B. Under WPATH SOC8, a generalist can diagnose gender dysphoria (DSM-5) and initiate GAHT through an informed-consent process — capacity, contraindication screening, expectations, fertility counseling. A mandatory mental-health gatekeeper (A) is not required to start hormones, and SOC8 reduced the surgical requirement to a single letter, not two (D). Gender incongruence is explicitly not a mental illness (C).

mcqs.stem: A 40-year-old trans man (AFAB) on testosterone for 3 years, with a retained cervix, comes in for routine care. He has had no Pap testing. What is the most appropriate screening recommendation?
opts: No cervical cancer screening is needed because he identifies as male
Screen for cervical cancer per standard guidelines because a cervix is present
Screen only if he reports symptoms
Replace Pap testing with serum testosterone monitoring
mcqs.exp: B. Cancer screening follows the organs that are present, not gender identity. A patient with a retained cervix needs cervical cancer screening on the standard schedule (testosterone can cause atrophic changes that make cytology harder to interpret, reinforcing the need for guideline-based screening). Gender identity (A), symptom-only screening (C), and substituting hormone labs (D) all miss an organ-based screening indication.

anki: Four independent axes to distinguish in gender care?
Gender identity, gender expression, sex assigned at birth (AMAB/AFAB), and sexual orientation.
The four patient-centered intake questions in gender care?
What name do you go by? / What pronouns do you use? / How would you describe your current gender identity? / What sex was assigned to you at birth?
What to do after misnaming or misgendering a patient?
Recognize, apologize, commit to change, move on (briefly; don't over-apologize).
Who can diagnose gender dysphoria and start GAHT?
A generalist, using DSM-5 criteria and an informed-consent model. Gender incongruence is NOT a mental illness.
Informed-consent checklist before starting GAHT?
Capacity; screen contraindications; discuss expectations/changes/side effects; offer fertility counseling.
Two pillars of feminizing hormone therapy?
Estrogen (17-beta-estradiol) PLUS an anti-androgen (spironolactone, 5-alpha-reductase inhibitor, or GnRH agonist).
Feminizing hormone targets?
Estradiol 100-200 pg/mL; suppress total testosterone <50 ng/dL.
Masculinizing therapy and its target?
Testosterone (single agent); goal trough total testosterone 400-700 ng/dL.
GAHT monitoring schedule?
Levels plus exam at 3 and 6 months, then annually.
Feminizing labs to monitor?
Estradiol, total testosterone, LFTs, lipids; add potassium/creatinine if on spironolactone.
Masculinizing labs to monitor?
Total testosterone (plus or minus estradiol) and hematocrit/CBC (erythrocytosis).
Irreversible feminizing change? Irreversible masculinizing changes?
Feminizing: breast growth. Masculinizing: voice deepening and clitoral enlargement.
Key adverse effect of each: estrogen, spironolactone, testosterone?
Estrogen -> VTE; spironolactone -> hyperkalemia (and orthostasis); testosterone -> erythrocytosis.
Lowest-VTE-risk estrogen formulation? Highest?
Lowest: transdermal (patch) estradiol. Highest: oral, especially ethinyl estradiol.
DVT on estradiol - how to manage?
Patient-centered shared decision; if continuing, switch to transdermal plus therapeutic anticoagulation; stop only for symptomatic PE or instability.
Hold GAHT when admitted or perioperatively?
No - do not hold, EXCEPT estradiol in a patient with VTE risk factors.
What drives cancer screening in transgender patients?
The organs that are present, not gender identity (e.g., retained cervix -> cervical screening).
lessons.dot:
#Gender-affirming care (adult, outpt): * [tF/tM/nonbinary], AMAB/AFAB *, name *, pronouns *. Dx gender incongruence/dysphoria per DSM-5 (generalist OK); gender incongruence NOT a mental illness. Informed consent: capacity *, contraindications screened, expectations/changes/SE discussed, fertility counseling offered. Goals [GAHT / surgery / voice / MH / none].
- organ inventory: * [breast, uterus, cervix, ovaries, prostate, penis, neovagina] — cancer screening by organs PRESENT (not gender identity).
- feminizing: 17β-estradiol * [PO / SL / patch / IM] (PATCH lowest VTE risk) + anti-androgen [spironolactone * / 5α-reductase inh / GnRH agonist]. goal estradiol 100–200 pg/mL, total testosterone <50 ng/dL. SE: VTE (estrogen), ↑K/orthostasis (spiro). expected: ↓erections/libido + ↓muscle/fat redistrib (~3mo), breast growth (2–5y, IRREVERSIBLE), ↓body hair (6–12mo).
- masculinizing: testosterone * [IM / SC / topical]. goal trough total testosterone 400–700 ng/dL. SE: erythrocytosis (check Hct → r/o OSA, ↓dose/↑freq/→topical). expected: ↑acne/oil + ↑libido, voice deepening (IRREVERSIBLE), menses cessation (~70%; ~30% persist), ↑muscle + fat redistrib, clitoral enlargement (IRREVERSIBLE), ↑facial/body hair + scalp loss.
- monitoring: levels + exam at 3mo & 6mo, then q1yr. feminizing labs [estradiol, total T, LFTs, lipids, +K/Cr if spiro]; masculinizing labs [total T, Hct/CBC].
- safety/FAQ: do NOT hold hormones inpt/periop EXCEPT estradiol w/ VTE risk; VTE-on-estrogen → transdermal + therapeutic AC if continuing (UCSF), stop only if symptomatic PE/instability; mild ↑CV risk → USPSTF screening; cost — Medicaid variable / Medicare covers / surgery often self-pay; verify local law + payer (2025 access changes).
- standard: WPATH SOC8 (2022), Endocrine Society (2017), UCSF guidelines.
takehome: Lead with patient-centered language: ask name, pronouns, gender identity, sex assigned at birth, and a focused organ inventory; if you misgender, recognize–apologize–commit–move on.
A generalist can diagnose gender dysphoria and start GAHT through informed consent (capacity, contraindications, expectations, fertility counseling); gender incongruence is not a mental illness.
Feminizing = estrogen (17β-estradiol; goal 100–200 pg/mL) ± anti-androgen; masculinizing = testosterone (goal trough 400–700 ng/dL). Monitor levels + exam at 3 and 6 months, then annually.

Remember the drug-risk pairs — estrogen→VTE (transdermal lowest-risk), spironolactone→hyperkalemia, testosterone→erythrocytosis. Don't hold hormones when admitted (except estradiol with VTE risk); screen cancers by organs present.

  4. Getahun D, et al. Cross-sex Hormones and Acute Cardiovascular Events in Transgender Persons (STRONG cohort). Ann Intern Med. 2018;169(4):205-213.

lessons.title: Inpatient Management of Hyperkalemia
lessons.subtitle: Optimized lesson package · Facilitator-led interactive chalk talk for IM residents · 15 min (talk) / 20 min (with cases)
lessons.credit: Adapted from TeachIM.org — Inpatient Management of Hyperkalemia (Gardner T, Dietsche C, Zhang Y; Dec 2021), CC BY-NC 4.0. Optimized June 2026: guideline currency verified (UKKA 2023, KDIGO 2024), three dose-unit errors corrected on the board, pedagogy restructured, assessments added. Companion deck: Hyperkalemia_Optimized.pptx (original interactive board, surgically corrected).
objectives: Practice a 5-step framework for managing hyperkalemia in the hospital: confirm → ECG → stabilize → shift → eliminate.
Recognize the cardiac complications of hyperkalemia and the characteristic, progressive ECG changes.
Distinguish temporizing measures (intracellular shift) from interventions that actually eliminate potassium — and pair them correctly.

prework.q: Q1. The nurse calls: K is 6.7. Does IV calcium lower the potassium level?
prework.a: No. Calcium does nothing to the serum K — it stabilizes the myocardial membrane and buys time. Its effect is judged on the ECG (changes should normalize), not on the chemistry panel.

prework.q: Q2. You give insulin/D50 and albuterol and the K falls from 6.8 to 5.9. Is the problem solved?
prework.a: No. Shifting is temporizing — total body potassium is unchanged, and serum K rebounds in hours as the effect wears off. Definitive management requires elimination: diuresis, a GI binder, or dialysis.

prework.q: Q3. Which patients should NOT receive sodium bicarbonate as a potassium-shifting agent?
prework.a: Patients without metabolic acidosis, and patients with ESRD (trials show non-responders were on hemodialysis). It is the least effective shifting strategy even when used correctly.

segments.label: Hook (2 min)
segments.html: "You're pre-rounding and the nurse calls: 'Critical lab on Mrs. Smith — her potassium is 6.7.' You have sixty seconds before she asks what you want to do. What do you say?" Let learners commit out loud. Then frame the talk: "There are exactly five moves, in order, and two of them don't lower the potassium at all — they keep the patient alive while the others work."

segments.label: Step 1 — Confirm the hyperkalemia
segments.html:
ASK: "Before you treat a K of 6.7 — what would make you doubt the number?" — then click to reveal.

Was the result expected (AKI, ESRD, K-raising drugs)? Is there hemolysis flagged on the specimen? Fist clenching, traumatic draw, and processing delays all spuriously raise K. Confirmation must never delay treatment when the level is high or the patient is symptomatic (myalgias, weakness, palpitations).

segments.label: Step 2 — STAT ECG
segments.html:
ASK: "Draw the ECG progression as the potassium climbs." Have a learner sketch it before revealing.
StageECG findings
EarlyPeaked T waves (best in precordial leads), QT shortening
WorseningPR prolongation → P-wave flattening/loss; QRS widening
Pre-arrestSine wave, ventricular arrhythmia, asystole; also pseudo-RBBB and pseudo-STE patterns in V1–V2

Any K > 5.5 warrants an ECG; treat K > 6.5 as emergent regardless of ECG. Repeat the ECG every 30–60 min until abnormalities resolve; keep the patient on telemetry.

Pearl: ECG changes track outcomes better than the absolute K, but correlate poorly with the level itself. A rapid rise is far more arrhythmogenic than a chronically elevated K — CKD/ESRD patients tolerate levels that would be alarming in a previously normal patient.

segments.label: Step 3 — Stabilize the myocardium
segments.html:
ASK: "What's the trigger for IV calcium, which salt do you reach for, and how do you know it worked?"
Calcium gluconateCalcium chloride
Dose1–2 g IV — preferred, peripheral OK0.5–1 g IV — more potent, caustic; central line
Onset / duration3–5 min / 30–60 min
EffectNo effect on serum K. Should normalize the ECG — if changes persist at 5–10 min, re-dose.

Trigger: ECG changes or K > 6.5.

segments.label: Step 4 — Shift potassium intracellularly
segments.html:
ASK: for each agent — "What dose? How much will it drop the K? How long do you have before it wears off?" — then click each button.
AgentDoseOnset / durationΔKCaveats
Regular insulin + D5010U IV + 1 amp D50 (5U if renal impairment; no D50 if BG > 250)15–60 min / 4–6 h (longer in AKI)↓ 0.5–1.0 mEq/LHypoglycemia is the most common complication — monitor glucose (UKKA 2023: for ~6 h)
β2-agonist (albuterol)10–20 mg neb in 4 mL saline — far above the 2.5 mg bronchodilator dose15–30 min / 2–4 h↓ 0.5–1.5 mEq/LCaution in active CAD (tachycardia/angina); most effective with insulin
Sodium bicarbonate50–100 mEq IV15–30 min / 1–2 hleast effectiveOnly if metabolic acidosis; not in ESRD (HD patients were the non-responders)
Pearl: Pick the shifting cocktail by comorbidity: AKI/ESRD → halve the insulin; active CAD → go easy on albuterol; acidotic non-ESRD → bicarbonate earns a slot. Shifting buys hours, not a fix.

segments.label: Step 5 — Eliminate potassium
segments.html:
ASK: "Ninety percent of potassium leaves the body one way. What's the route, and what's the back door?" — then click the kidney and the gut.
RouteTherapyNotes
Kidney (~90%)Loop diuretic (furosemide 40–160 mg IV)1st-line if hypervolemic; onset ~30 min; ineffective in anuria; risk of volume depletion worsening AKI if used alone
Isotonic IVF (NS or isotonic bicarb)Pair with loops if euvolemic; isotonic bicarbonate also shifts K in acidosis
Hemodialysis / CRRTFor severe renal dysfunction or refractory hyperkalemia — most likely intervention to achieve normal K within 4 h
Gut (~10%)SZC (Lokelma) 10 g TID × 48 hOnset ~1 h — preferred binder; sodium load/edema, cost. UKKA 2023: an option alongside shifting agents in acute life-threatening hyperkalemia (K ≥ 6.5). (Patiromer: colon-only, onset ~7 h — chronic niche.)
Kayexalate (SPS) 15–60 g q6h PO (30–50 g PR)Bowel-necrosis risk — avoid post-op, ileus/SBO, IBD, infectious colitis; separate other meds by 3 h; skip if HD is planned (UKKA 2023: not for emergency treatment)

After interventions: recheck K in 1–4 h, repeat ECG q30–60 min, and repeat shifting + elimination until K normalizes or dialysis happens.

KDIGO 2024 update: when hyperkalemia is driven by an ACEi/ARB in CKD, prefer lowering the potassium (drug/diet review, diuretic, binder, bicarbonate if acidotic) over stopping the RASi — discontinuation is associated with higher mortality and CV events.

segments.label: Close — retrieval (2 min)
segments.html: Erase/hide the board and have learners rebuild the 5 steps from memory, with one drug + dose per step. Finish with: "Which two steps change the potassium not at all?" (confirm + calcium).

cases.title: Case 1
cases.stem: 55-year-old with ESRD on HD via an AV fistula presents with shortness of breath and pain at the fistula site after missing dialysis. Fistulogram shows stenosis of the AV fistula. They are anuric. BMP: K 6.7 (no hemolysis), HCO3 19, BUN 56, Cr 10.5.
cases.reveal: Obtain an ECG. (Board shows diffusely peaked T waves.)

cases.title: Case 2
cases.stem: 65-year-old with metastatic colon cancer presents for colectomy. On POD3, develops a new non-oliguric AKI. Euvolemic on exam. Labs: K 6.0, Cr 0.6 → 3.5, HCO3 13; ABG pH 7.2.
cases.reveal: Obtain an ECG.

mcqs.stem: A 58-year-old man with ESRD missed dialysis twice. He reports weakness. K is 7.1, no hemolysis. ECG shows peaked T waves and a widening QRS. Which is the best next step?
opts: IV regular insulin 10 units with D50
mcqs.exp: B — IV calcium gluconate. ECG changes mandate immediate membrane stabilization (onset 3–5 min) before anything else. A: insulin is the right second move but takes 15–60 min and does not protect the myocardium now. C: SZC takes ~1 h and removes K slowly — adjunct, not first. D: dialysis is the definitive therapy and should be arranged, but it cannot start within minutes; calcium first. E: bicarbonate is the least effective shifter and is generally ineffective in ESRD. Also: insulin should be dose-reduced to 5U in renal failure.

mcqs.stem: A 70-year-old woman with CKD has K 6.6 treated with calcium gluconate, insulin/D50, and albuterol. One hour later K is 5.8. Which statement best describes her situation?
opts: The potassium problem is resolved; recheck in 24 h
Total body potassium is unchanged and will rebound; elimination therapy is required
Calcium lowered the serum potassium and can be re-dosed for further effect
Albuterol provides definitive potassium removal over the next 6 h
She now requires emergent dialysis regardless of further labs
mcqs.exp: B. Insulin and albuterol only shift K into cells (duration 2–6 h); serum K rebounds as they wear off. A: rechecking at 24 h misses the rebound — recheck in 1–4 h. C: calcium has no effect on serum K. D: albuterol eliminates nothing. E: dialysis is for severe renal dysfunction or refractory hyperkalemia, not automatic here — diuretics/binders may suffice with residual renal function.

mcqs.stem: A 64-year-old man is POD2 from sigmoid colectomy with a postoperative ileus. K is 6.3 with mild AKI; ECG normal. Insulin/D50 and albuterol are given. Which elimination therapy is contraindicated?
mcqs.exp: C — Kayexalate. SPS carries a risk of bowel necrosis and is contraindicated post-operatively and with ileus/obstruction, IBD, or infectious colitis. A/B: loops + isotonic fluids are the first-line elimination strategy in a non-anuric patient. D: SZC acts in small and large intestine and lacks the same necrosis association (sodium load is its issue). E: dialysis is not contraindicated — just not yet indicated.

mcqs.stem: A 59-year-old with diabetic CKD G3b (eGFR 38) on lisinopril has a routine K of 5.7, repeatedly. He is asymptomatic; ECG unchanged. Per KDIGO 2024 guidance, the best initial approach is:
opts: Review K-raising drugs/diet and add a diuretic or potassium binder, continuing the ACE inhibitor
Admit for IV calcium and insulin
mcqs.exp: B. KDIGO 2024 recommends managing RASi-associated hyperkalemia with K-lowering measures rather than reflexively stopping the RASi, whose discontinuation is associated with higher mortality and CV events in CKD. A: last resort after other measures. C: chronic mild hyperkalemia without ECG changes is not an emergency. D: SPS is the least preferred binder for chronic use (GI toxicity); patiromer/SZC are better tolerated. E: nothing here suggests imminent ESRD.

mcqs.stem: Which patient is the best candidate for sodium bicarbonate as part of potassium-shifting therapy?
opts: CKD G3 with normal bicarbonate, K 6.0
Acute hyperkalemia from rhabdomyolysis with pH 7.44
mcqs.exp: B. Bicarbonate is reserved for metabolic acidosis in patients NOT on dialysis — responders in studies had K > 6, HCO3 < 17, pH < 7.35. A: ESRD/HD patients were the non-responders. C/D: no acidosis, no role. E: bicarbonate is not a default add-on; it is the least effective shifter and adds sodium load.

anki: You're called about a critical K of 6.7. What are the first two steps?
Confirm the lab (expected? hemolysis? concurrent AKI?) AND obtain a STAT ECG — any K > 5.5 warrants an ECG. Confirmation must not delay treatment.
What is the ECG progression of worsening hyperkalemia?
Early: peaked T waves + QT shortening → P-wave flattening/loss with PR prolongation → QRS widening → sine wave, ventricular arrhythmia, or asystole. (Also pseudo-RBBB / pseudo-STE in V1–V2.)
Do ECG changes correlate with the serum potassium level?
Poorly. ECG changes predict poor outcomes better than the absolute K, and a rapid rise is more arrhythmogenic than a slow one — chronic CKD/ESRD patients tolerate higher K.
What triggers IV calcium in hyperkalemia?
ECG changes OR K > 6.5 (treat K > 6.5 as emergent regardless of ECG).
Compare the two IV calcium formulations.
Calcium gluconate 1–2 g IV — preferred, peripheral OK. Calcium chloride 0.5–1 g IV — more potent but caustic; give via central line.
IV calcium: onset, duration, effect on K, and re-dosing rule?
Onset 3–5 min, duration 30–60 min. No effect on serum K — it stabilizes the myocardium and should normalize the ECG; re-dose every 5–10 min if ECG changes persist.
Insulin/dextrose for hyperkalemia: dose, renal adjustment, effect?

Regular insulin 10U IV + 1 amp D50 (5U if renal impairment; skip D50 if BG > 250). Onset 15–30 min, lasts 4–6 h (longer in AKI). Lowers K ~0.5–1.0 mEq/L. Monitor glucose afterward — hypoglycemia is the most common complication.
Albuterol for hyperkalemia: dose, effect, caveat?

10–20 mg nebulized in 4 mL saline — far above the 2.5 mg bronchodilator dose. Lowers K 0.5–1.5 mEq/L, onset 15–30 min; most effective combined with insulin. Use caution in active coronary disease (tachycardia/angina).
When is sodium bicarbonate a reasonable shifting agent?
Only with metabolic acidosis and NOT in ESRD (shown ineffective on HD). 50–100 mEq IV. It is the least effective shifting strategy.
Why is intracellular shifting only a temporizing measure?
It moves K into cells without removing it from the body — serum K rebounds as the effect wears off, so always pair shifting with elimination (diuresis, binder, or dialysis).
How is potassium normally eliminated from the body?
~90% renal, ~10% GI (the basis for diuretics/dialysis vs. gut binders).
Role of loop diuretics in hyperkalemia?
First-line if hypervolemic (furosemide 40–160 mg IV); add isotonic fluids if euvolemic to avoid volume depletion worsening AKI; ineffective in anuria.
When is urgent dialysis indicated for hyperkalemia?
Severe renal dysfunction (especially anuria/ESRD without access to K elimination) or hyperkalemia refractory to medical therapy — HD is the intervention most likely to achieve normal K within 4 h.
Sodium zirconium cyclosilicate (SZC/Lokelma): dosing, onset, downsides?

10 g TID for up to 48–72 h; onset ~1 h (fastest binder); works in small + large intestine. Downsides: cost/availability, sodium load → edema. UKKA 2023 now lists it as an option alongside shifting agents in acute life-threatening hyperkalemia (K ≥ 6.5).
Kayexalate (SPS): dosing and contraindications?
15–60 g PO q6h (or 30–50 g PR). Risk of bowel necrosis — avoid post-op, ileus/obstruction, IBD, infectious colitis; separate from other meds by 3 h; skip it if dialysis is planned.
Patient on an ACE inhibitor develops hyperkalemia — stop the drug?

Per KDIGO 2024: prefer lowering the K (review K-raising drugs/diet, diuretic, binder, bicarbonate if acidotic) over stopping the RASi — discontinuation is associated with higher mortality and CV events in CKD.
What monitoring follows treatment of hyperkalemia?
Recheck K in 1–4 h and repeat ECG every 30–60 min until abnormalities resolve; telemetry; repeat temporizing + elimination measures until K normalizes or dialysis occurs.
lessons.dot:
#Hyperkalemia: K *6.7 [confirmed/repeat sent] [no hemolysis] likely 2/2 * [AKI/CKD-ESRD/missed HD/RASi/K-sparing diuretic/TMP-SMX/NSAID/rhabdo/TLS/acidosis/adrenal insufficiency/dietary+binder gap]; sx [none/weakness/myalgias/palpitations]
ECG: * [no changes/peaked T/P-wave loss/QRS widening/sine wave] — STAT ECG for any K >5.5; tele; repeat ECG q30-60min until nl
stabilize (ECG ch or K >6.5): Ca gluconate 1-2g IV PIV [CaCl 0.5-1g via CVC]; onset 3-5min, no effect on K; redose q5-10min if ECG unchanged
shift: insulin reg 10U* IV + amp D50 [5U if renal impairment; no D50 if BG>250; POC glucose q1h x6h] (↓K 0.5-1.0) + albuterol neb 10-20mg [caution active CAD] (↓K 0.5-1.5) ± NaHCO3 50-100mEq IV ONLY if [metabolic acidosis + not ESRD]
eliminate: [hypervolemic → furosemide 40-160mg IV / euvolemic → loop + isotonic IVF (NS or isotonic bicarb) / anuric-severe renal failure → urgent HD-CRRT (normal K w/in 4h)]; binder: SZC 10g TID x48h [pref; Na load] > patiromer [onset 7h] > Kayexalate 15-60g q6h [AVOID: post-op/ileus/SBO/IBD/colitis; sep other meds 3h; not if HD planned]
recheck K 1-4h; repeat shift+eliminate until K nl or HD
RASi: per KDIGO 2024 lower K [diet/diuretic/binder/bicarb] rather than d/c ACEi-ARB
AVOID: NaHCO3 in ESRD (ineffective); Kayexalate w/ bowel risk; loops if anuric
takehome: Hyperkalemic crisis is a medical emergency — severe weakness and fatal arrhythmias. Any K > 5.5 → STAT ECG; treat K > 6.5 as emergent regardless of ECG.

ECG changes: peaked T waves → P-wave loss → QRS widening → sine wave/VF. ECG changes or K > 6.5 → IV calcium gluconate 1–2 g; re-dose in 5–10 min if the ECG hasn't normalized. Calcium never lowers the K.
Shift while you arrange elimination: insulin 10U + D50 (5U if renal impairment), albuterol 10–20 mg neb, ± bicarbonate only in non-ESRD metabolic acidosis.
Eliminate: loops ± IVF by volume status; binders (SZC 10 g TID preferred over Kayexalate) when kidneys can't do the job; urgent dialysis for severe renal dysfunction or refractory hyperkalemia.
Recheck K in 1–4 h and repeat the cycle — shifting buys time, it doesn't remove potassium.

  2. Palmer BF, et al. Clinical Management of Hyperkalemia. Mayo Clin Proc. 2021;96(3):744-762.
  3. Littmann L, Gibbs MA. Electrocardiographic manifestations of severe hyperkalemia. J Electrocardiol. 2018;51:814-817.
  4. Mount DB. Treatment and prevention of hyperkalemia in adults. UpToDate (accessed Dec 31, 2021 by original authors).
  5. Lexicomp drug information: albuterol; sodium bicarbonate; furosemide. UpToDate.
  6. Abuelo JG. Treatment of Severe Hyperkalemia: Confronting 4 Fallacies. Kidney Int Rep. 2018;3(1):47-55.

AreaWhat changed
Currency checkCore framework verified current against UKKA Management of Hyperkalaemia in Adults (Oct 2023) and KDIGO 2024 CKD guideline. Thresholds (ECG for K > 5.5; emergency at K ≥ 6.5), calcium, insulin/glucose, and β2-agonist teaching all unchanged.

KDIGO 2024: lower K rather than stop RASi. Surfaced in this package and on one appended deck slide.
CorrectionsDeck slide 7: Lokelma "10 mg TID" → "10 g TID"; Kayexalate "15-60 mg q6h" → "15-60 g q6h". Deck slide 8 (Case 1): albuterol "nebulizer 20 g" → "20 mg". All three were unit errors contradicted by the lesson's own text. Spelling "Kayexelate" → "Kayexalate" (8×).
PedagogyScript restructured into timed segments with a hook, 6 ASK pause-points, pearls, and a retrieval-based close; pre-work added.
Assessment5 board-style MCQs with distractor explanations; 17 spaced-repetition cards (+ Anki import file).
Clinical toolAttending dotphrase (acute-management template) added — HTML package only.
One-pagerSingle-page printable reference created (PDF + source HTML).
FormatDeck edits were surgical XML replacements on a copy; all 9 original slides, graphics, and click-to-reveal animations preserved; 3 slides appended (update / take-home / attribution).
Human review neededNone — every correction is stated in the lesson's own text or cited guideline. (Companion facilitator guide retains the original "Kayexelate"/"polystyreme" spellings; doses there are correct, so it was copied unchanged.)

lessons.title: Hypertensive Emergency
lessons.subtitle: Optimized lesson package · Facilitator-led interactive chalk talk for IM residents · 15–20 min (talk) / 30–35 min (with cases)
lessons.credit: Adapted from TeachIM.org — Hypertensive Emergency (Slaven S, Sailer C, Zhang Y; Sept 2021), CC BY-NC 4.0. Optimized June 2026: guideline currency verified, one drug-name error corrected, 2025 AHA/ACC terminology surfaced, pedagogy restructured, assessments added. Companion deck: HTN_Emergency_Optimized.pptx (original interactive board, surgically corrected).
objectives: Define hypertensive emergency and distinguish it from hypertensive urgency (now termed severe hypertension without acute target-organ damage).
Identify the signs and symptoms of end-organ failure across the CNS, cardiovascular, and renal systems.
Differentiate the treatment goals — and the speed of BP lowering — for emergency vs. severe-HTN-without-damage, and name the scenarios that break the general rule.
Match the common IV antihypertensives to the clinical scenario using their mechanism of action.

prework.q: Q1. A patient has a BP of 220/125. What single additional finding turns this from "severe hypertension" into a "hypertensive emergency"?
prework.a: Evidence of acute target-organ damage. The absolute number does not define an emergency — a previously normotensive patient can have damage below 180/120, and a chronically hypertensive patient can tolerate 220/125 without it. The rate of rise matters more than the number.

prework.q: Q2. Why is it dangerous to drop the blood pressure to normal quickly in a hypertensive emergency?
prework.a: Cerebral (and other vascular bed) autoregulation has reset to a higher range. A rapid drop falls below the autoregulatory floor and causes hypoperfusion/ischemia. The goal is to lower SBP by no more than ~25% in the first hour, not to normalize it.

prework.q: Q3. Name one IV antihypertensive you would AVOID in (a) acute LV failure with pulmonary edema and (b) pheochromocytoma crisis, and why.
prework.a: (a) Avoid a non-vasodilating β-blocker / labetalol before the EF is known — losing inotropy can precipitate cardiogenic shock. (b) Avoid a β-blocker as initial therapy — unopposed α-adrenergic vasoconstriction worsens the hypertension. Treat the α first (phentolamine).

segments.label: Hook (2 min)
segments.html: "Two patients, both with a BP of 230/130. One gets an ICU bed and an IV drip; the other goes home on their own pills. What's the one thing that decides which is which?" (Acute end-organ damage.) Then: "And here's the counterintuitive part — for the sick one, your job is NOT to get the pressure to normal. Fixing it too fast can stroke them out." That tension drives the whole talk.

segments.label: Objective 1 — Definition
segments.html:
ASK: "Define hypertensive emergency" — then click to reveal. Severe HTN (SBP >180 or DBP >120) PLUS acute end-organ dysfunction. The end-organ damage — not the number — is the dividing line.

Drive home two points: (1) rate of rise > absolute number — a chronic hypertensive tolerates 200/120 without damage; a previously normotensive patient (eclampsia, acute GN) can have damage well below 180/120. (2) The contrast term has been renamed (see the 2025 update box below): what the board calls "hypertensive urgency" is now severe hypertension without acute target-organ damage.

2025 AHA/ACC terminology update: The 2025 AHA/ACC hypertension guideline formally retires "hypertensive urgency" in favor of severe hypertension without acute target-organ damage — a more precise label meant to curb unnecessary ED visits, IV drips, and aggressive lowering in this group. Management is unchanged: reinstitute/intensify oral therapy as an outpatient with close follow-up. The definition of hypertensive emergency and the >180/120 threshold are unchanged. The board still says "HTN urgency"; flag the new wording when you teach.

segments.label: Objective 2 — End-organ damage
segments.html:
ASK: "You have a patient at 215/130. Go organ by organ — what are you looking for on history, exam, and labs that says 'damage'?" — then click each organ system to reveal.
SystemEmergenciesClues
NeurologicHypertensive encephalopathy; intraparenchymal/subarachnoid hemorrhage; acute ischemic strokeEncephalopathy: insidious headache, confusion, nausea, seizures (PRES = vasogenic edema, parieto-occipital); papilledema; focal deficits point to stroke/ICH; thunderclap headache to SAH
CardiovascularAcute LV failure + pulmonary edema; ACS; aortic dissectionChest pain, dyspnea; dissection: tearing pain to the back, inter-arm BP differential, diminished/asymmetric pulses, widened mediastinum
RenalRapidly progressive renal failureRapidly rising Cr, falling urine output, microscopic hematuria
OtherEclampsia; pheochromocytoma crisis; sympathomimetic ingestion; thrombotic microangiopathyPregnancy >20 wk + seizure; episodic headache/sweating/palpitations/tachycardia/hyperthermia; positive urine tox (cocaine, amphetamines); VEGF inhibitors can also drive it

segments.label: Objective 3 — Treatment goals
segments.html:
ASK: "Goal BP for a hypertensive emergency — is it normal? How fast?" — let them commit before revealing.

The goal is to prevent further irreversible organ damage, not to normalize BP. Manage in the ICU with IV titratable agents and remove exacerbating factors (pain, volume, drugs).

ScenarioBP target / pace
General principle↓ SBP by ≤25% in the first hour → ~160/100 by 2–6 h → normal over 24–48 h
Aortic dissectionHR to ~60 FIRST (β-blocker), then SBP <120 ASAP (within ~20 min)
ICH / SAHSBP <140 promptly (per the dedicated stroke/ICH protocol)
Eclampsia / pheo crisisLower SBP toward <140 in the first hour; magnesium for eclampsia
Acute ischemic strokePermissive HTN if ≤220/120; if >220/120, lower ~15% over 24 h; if tPA candidate, must be <185/110 before lytics (<180/105 for 24 h after)
Pearl — the dissection sequence: Rate before pressure. Give the β-blocker (esmolol/labetalol) to bring HR to ~60 before adding a pure vasodilator like nitroprusside. Vasodilating first triggers a reflex tachycardia that increases aortic wall shear stress (dP/dt) and can extend the dissection.

segments.label: Objective 4 — Medications by mechanism
segments.html:
ASK: "Don't memorize a list — reason from mechanism. If I want to drop afterload without touching preload, what class? If I need to kill a reflex tachycardia, what class?"

Two big buckets: vasodilators and adrenergic antagonists.

AgentClass / actionUse it forAvoid / caution
NicardipineArterial vasodilator (CCB)Preferred in acute ischemic stroke; safe in pregnancy; broadly useful—
HydralazineArterial vasodilatorPregnancyReflex sympathetic surge → ↑myocardial demand (caution in ACS); ↑ICP; unpredictable response — not first-line for most
NitroprussideMixed arterial + venousAlmost any scenario except pregnancyCyanide toxicity in AKI; may ↑ICP/affect platelets in stroke; tachyphylaxis
FenoldopamMixed (dopamine-1 agonist; renal vasodilation)Especially acute renal failureCan ↑ICP and intra-ocular pressure
NitroglycerinVenous vasodilator (↓preload)ACS and acute LV failure/pulmonary edemaWeak antihypertensive; tachyphylaxis
Labetalolα1/β antagonistEclampsia, hypertensive ACS, aortic dissection (↓shear stress); safe in pregnancyRV infarct (preload-dependent); acute LV failure until EF known
EsmololPure β1 antagonistAortic dissection (with a vasodilator) to prevent rebound tachycardiaDecompensated HF
Phentolamineα1 antagonistCatecholamine excess: pheochromocytoma crisis, sympathomimetics, clonidine withdrawal—

segments.label: Close (2 min)
segments.html: Hide the board. Ask the group to rebuild from memory: the one-line definition, the three organ systems, the general lowering rule + the three exceptions that break it, and the drug they'd pick for stroke, for pulmonary edema, for dissection, and for pheo.

cases.title: Case 1 — Weakness + very high BP
cases.stem: 67-year-old on chronic steroids presents with left leg > arm weakness for 18 h. BP 245/123.
cases.reveal: CTA head: acute right ACA infarct without hemorrhagic conversion.

cases.title: Case 2 — Chest pain + pulmonary edema
cases.stem: 59-year-old, no known PMHx, acute chest pain and shortness of breath. BP 220/130.
cases.reveal: Troponin 1.1 → 1.2 → 1.1 ng/mL; BNP 5000; ECG: ST depressions V4–V6; CXR: marked pulmonary edema.

cases.title: Case 3 — Tearing chest pain to the back
cases.stem: 48-year-old, severe chest pain radiating to the back. BP 225/134. On exam BP is 201/110 in the left arm with a 1+ left radial pulse vs 2+ elsewhere; no focal neuro deficit.
cases.reveal: ECG: no ischemic changes; troponin 0.01 ng/mL; Cr 1.0; CXR: widened mediastinum; CTA: Type B aortic dissection.

cases.title: Case 4 — Missed doses + nausea
cases.stem: 67-year-old with HTN, 3 days of nausea/vomiting, no meds for 3 days. BP 190/125. Home meds: lisinopril 40, amlodipine 10, HCTZ 12.5. Reports headache; denies blurry vision, chest pain, dyspnea, focal weakness, confusion. Exam: no focal deficits.
cases.reveal: ECG: no ischemic changes; troponin 0.01 ng/mL; Cr 1.0; CXR: no focal infiltrate. No evidence of acute organ damage.

cases.title: Case 5 — Episodic spells + fever + confusion
cases.stem: 47-year-old with episodic headaches, palpitations, diaphoresis now presents with confusion and fever. T 40°C, BP 243/143, HR 140s.
cases.reveal: ECG: sinus tachycardia; CT A/P: 1.4 cm right adrenal mass; elevated plasma and urine metanephrines.

cases.title: Case 6 — Oliguria + rising Cr
cases.stem: 51-year-old with malaise and decreased urine output. BP 197/103.
cases.reveal: BMP: K 5.6, HCO3 17, Cr 6.7; renal US: no hydronephrosis or obstruction.

mcqs.stem: A 54-year-old man with poorly controlled hypertension presents with a BP of 224/126. He is asymptomatic. ECG, troponin, creatinine, and urinalysis are normal, and his neurologic exam is intact. What is the most appropriate management?
opts: Admit to the ICU for an IV nicardipine drip
IV labetalol bolus in the ED, then reassess
Restart/intensify oral antihypertensives and arrange close outpatient follow-up
IV hydralazine to lower the BP below 180/120 before discharge
mcqs.exp: C. This is severe hypertension without acute target-organ damage (the 2025 AHA/ACC term replacing "hypertensive urgency"). With no evidence of organ injury, there is no benefit — and real harm — from rapid parenteral lowering (A, B, D); abrupt drops risk hypoperfusion. The standard of care is to reinstitute or intensify oral therapy and follow up closely as an outpatient.

mcqs.stem: A 62-year-old presents with tearing chest pain radiating to the back, BP 230/120, and a CT angiogram showing a Type B aortic dissection. Which initial pharmacologic step is most appropriate?
opts: Start nitroprusside to rapidly lower SBP, then add a β-blocker
Start esmolol to a target heart rate of ~60, then add nitroprusside for SBP <120
Start hydralazine for rapid afterload reduction
mcqs.exp: B. Control heart rate first. A pure vasodilator given before β-blockade (A, D) causes reflex tachycardia, raising aortic wall shear stress (dP/dt) and risking extension of the dissection. Esmolol (or labetalol) to a HR of ~60 first, then nitroprusside to drive SBP <120 ASAP, is the correct sequence. Nicardipine alone (C) does not address the heart rate.

mcqs.stem: A 70-year-old presents with right-sided weakness for 4 hours; BP is 215/118. Non-contrast head CT shows no hemorrhage and he is not a candidate for thrombolysis. What is the most appropriate blood pressure management?
opts: Lower SBP to <140 immediately with nicardipine
Lower BP to <185/110 before any further care
Permissive hypertension — do not lower the BP
Start nitroprusside to reduce BP by 50% over the first hour
mcqs.exp: C. In acute ischemic stroke without thrombolysis, BP is permitted up to 220/120; lowering it risks extending the infarct by reducing perfusion to the ischemic penumbra (A, D). The <185/110 target (B) applies only to tPA candidates. Above 220/120, the recommendation is a modest ~15% reduction over 24 h.

mcqs.stem: A 45-year-old with episodic headache, palpitations, and diaphoresis presents in crisis with BP 250/140 and elevated plasma metanephrines. A resident reaches for IV metoprolol. Why is this the wrong first move?
opts: β-blockers are contraindicated in any hypertensive emergency
Unopposed α-adrenergic vasoconstriction can worsen the hypertension
It will cause reflex bradycardia and asystole
mcqs.exp: B. In catecholamine excess, blocking β2-mediated vasodilation while leaving α1-mediated vasoconstriction unopposed can precipitate a paradoxical surge in BP. Establish α-blockade first (phentolamine). β-blockers are appropriate in other emergencies such as dissection (A is false). The issue is pharmacologic, not route (C) or reflex bradycardia (D).

mcqs.stem: A 58-year-old with acute pulmonary edema, chest pain, and BP 210/120 is found to have an LVEF that has not yet been assessed. Which is the best initial antihypertensive strategy?
opts: IV labetalol to reduce afterload and rate
Nitroglycerin (± nitroprusside) plus IV diuresis to reduce preload
mcqs.exp: B. Venodilation with nitroglycerin reduces preload and pulmonary venous hydrostatic pressure, directly relieving the edema; diuresis adds to this, and nitroprusside can be added for further BP control. A β-blocking agent like labetalol (A) is hazardous before the EF is known — loss of inotropy can precipitate cardiogenic shock. Oral amlodipine (C) is too slow for an emergency, and hydralazine (D) causes a reflex tachycardia that worsens ischemia.

anki: Definition of hypertensive emergency?
Severe HTN (SBP >180 or DBP >120) PLUS acute end-organ damage. The damage, not the number, defines it.
What distinguishes hypertensive emergency from "severe HTN without acute target-organ damage" (old "hypertensive urgency")?
The presence of acute end-organ damage. Without it, manage with oral meds as an outpatient.
What matters more — the absolute BP or the rate of rise?
Rate of rise. Chronic hypertensives tolerate very high BP; a previously normotensive patient can have damage below 180/120.
2025 AHA/ACC terminology change for "hypertensive urgency"?
Renamed "severe hypertension without acute target-organ damage"; management (oral, outpatient) is unchanged.
Three organ systems to screen for end-organ damage?
Neurologic (encephalopathy, ICH/SAH, ischemic stroke), cardiovascular (acute LV failure, ACS, aortic dissection), renal (rapidly rising Cr, low UOP, hematuria).
What is hypertensive encephalopathy and its classic imaging finding?
Diagnosis of exclusion; insidious headache/confusion/seizures from cerebral edema; MRI shows parieto-occipital vasogenic edema (PRES).
General BP-lowering target/pace in hypertensive emergency?
Lower SBP <=25% in hour 1 -> ~160/100 by 2-6 h -> normal over 24-48 h. Goal is to prevent further damage, not normalize BP.
Why not normalize BP quickly in a hypertensive emergency?
Vascular autoregulation has reset upward; a rapid drop causes hypoperfusion/ischemia (especially cerebral).
BP management of aortic dissection?
HR to ~60 FIRST with a beta-blocker (esmolol/labetalol), THEN nitroprusside for SBP <120 ASAP. Rate before pressure to limit shear stress.
BP management of acute ischemic stroke?
Permissive HTN if <=220/120; if higher, lower ~15% over 24 h. tPA candidate: must be <185/110 before lytics, <180/105 for 24 h after.
Preferred agent in acute ischemic stroke?
Nicardipine (also helps prevent vasospasm; safe in pregnancy).
Best agents for ACS / acute LV failure with pulmonary edema?
Nitroglycerin (venodilator, decreases preload) +/- nitroprusside; avoid beta-blockers until EF known.
Agent for catecholamine excess (pheo crisis, sympathomimetics, clonidine withdrawal)?
Phentolamine (alpha-1 antagonist). Avoid beta-blockers as initial therapy (unopposed alpha).
Cautions with nitroprusside?
Cyanide toxicity in AKI; may raise ICP / affect platelets in stroke; tachyphylaxis with prolonged use.
Which agent is particularly useful in hypertensive emergency with acute renal failure?
Fenoldopam — a dopamine-1 agonist causing renal vasodilation (can raise ICP and intra-ocular pressure).
Two antihypertensives safe in pregnancy/eclampsia?
Labetalol and nicardipine (and hydralazine). Add magnesium for eclampsia seizure prophylaxis.
lessons.dot:
#HTN crisis: BP */* . severe HTN (SBP >180 / DBP >120). acute target-organ damage? [YES=HTN EMERGENCY / NO=severe HTN w/o organ damage (old "urgency")]. rate of rise > absolute #. likely 2/2 * [nonadherence, undertreated essential HTN, renovascular, pheo, cocaine/sympathomimetic, eclampsia, AKI/GN, scleroderma renal].
- organ screen (defines emergency): CNS [HTN enceph/PRES, ICH/SAH, ischemic CVA] — exam + noncon head CT if sx; CV [acute LV failure/flash pulm edema, ACS, aortic dissection — inter-arm BP diff, widened mediastinum] — ECG, hs-trop, CXR; RENAL [rapidly ↑Cr, ↓UOP, micro-hematuria] — BMP, UA; OTHER [eclampsia >20wk, pheo crisis, TMA] — BHCG, metanephrines, smear/LDH/hapto
- TARGET (emergency): ↓SBP ≤25% in 1st h → ~160/100 by 2-6h → nl over 24-48h. ICU + IV titratable gtt. do NOT normalize fast (autoreg reset → hypoperfusion)
- scenario overrides: aortic dissection → HR ~60 w/ BB FIRST then SBP <120 ASAP; ICH/SAH → SBP <140; eclampsia/pheo → SBP <140 1st h (+MgSO4 for eclampsia); ischemic CVA → permissive ≤220/120, else ↓~15%/24h, tPA cand <185/110 pre & <180/105 ×24h
- agent by scenario: CVA/pregnancy=nicardipine; ACS/pulm edema=nitroglycerin ±nitroprusside +diuresis; dissection=esmolol/labetalol ±nitroprusside; pheo/sympathomimetic=phentolamine; renal failure=fenoldopam/nicardipine; broad=nitroprusside (not in pregnancy)
- AVOID: BB before EF known in acute LV failure (→ cardiogenic shock); BB as initial in pheo/catechol excess (unopposed α); nitroprusside in AKI (CN tox); hydralazine if ACS (reflex tachy) / ↑ICP
- severe HTN w/o organ damage: NO IV gtt, NO rapid drop → reinstitute/uptitrate ORAL meds, outpt close f/u (2025 AHA/ACC)
- monitoring: a-line if gtt, ICU, neuro checks, UOP, telemetry, q*h BP; remove exacerbators (pain, vol, drug)
- labs: BMP, hs-trop, UA, CBC ± BNP, ± metanephrines, ± BHCG, ECG, CXR, ± CTA [per organ screen]
takehome: Hypertensive emergency = severe HTN (>180/120) WITH acute end-organ damage; the damage — not the BP number — defines it, and the rate of rise matters more than the absolute value.
Screen three organ systems every time: CNS (encephalopathy, ICH/SAH, ischemic stroke), cardiac (acute LV failure, ACS, dissection), and renal (rising Cr, low UOP).

Goal is to prevent further damage, not normalize BP: ≤25% SBP drop in hour 1 → 160/100 by 2–6 h → normal over 24–48 h — with faster, deeper targets only for dissection, ICH/SAH, eclampsia/pheo, and special stroke rules.

Pick the drug from the mechanism: nicardipine (stroke, pregnancy), nitroglycerin (ACS/pulmonary edema), labetalol/esmolol (dissection), phentolamine (catecholamine excess); avoid β-blockers in pheo crisis and before the EF is known in acute LV failure.
Markedly high BP with NO organ damage is "severe hypertension without acute target-organ damage" (2025 AHA/ACC) — oral meds and outpatient follow-up, not an ICU drip.

lessons.title: Evaluation of Hyponatremia
lessons.subtitle: Optimized lesson package · Facilitator-led interactive chalk talk for IM residents · 20 min (talk) / 30 min (with cases)
lessons.credit: Adapted from TeachIM.org — Evaluation of Hyponatremia (Fainstad B, Zhang Y; May 2020, updated Oct 2021), CC BY-NC 4.0. Optimized June 2026: framework verified current (2024 Austrian Society for Nephrology consensus uses the same triad), two typos corrected on BOTH boards, pedagogy restructured, assessments added. Companion decks: Hyponatremia_Optimized.pptx (presenter) and Hyponatremia.pptx (learning board, corrected).
objectives: Learn the "3 lab approach" to the diagnosis of hyponatremia (this talk does not cover treatment).
Understand the roles of ADH and aldosterone in hyponatremia.

prework.q: Q1. A patient with multiple myeloma has Na 126 but a measured serum osmolality of 285. Is this true hyponatremia?
prework.a: No — true hyponatremia is hypo-osmolar (SOsm < 290). A normal SOsm with low measured Na is pseudohyponatremia: paraprotein (or lipid) dilutes whole plasma while the water-plasma [Na] is normal. Send a whole-blood Na.

prework.q: Q2. Why does the urine osmolality tell you whether ADH is "on"?
prework.a: ADH's only job is to make the collecting duct reclaim free water, which concentrates the urine. Dilute urine (<100) means ADH is off; concentrated urine (>100) means ADH is acting — appropriately or not.

prework.q: Q3. A hypovolemic patient on furosemide has a urine Na of 60. Does that rule out hypovolemia?
prework.a: No — diuretics force salt wasting, so the urine Na stays high despite RAAS activation. Diuretics are the one standard exception to "low urine Na = kidneys seeing low volume."

segments.label: Hook (2 min)
segments.html: "Three patients, all with a sodium of 125: a drinker in withdrawal, a diabetic with glucose of 500, and a woman with two days of vomiting and diarrhea. Same number — three completely different diseases. Three lab values will tell them apart, and none of them is your exam's guess at volume status." Acknowledge the elephant: we're all taught hyper/hypo/euvolemic — and we're all bad at assessing it.

segments.label: Step 1 — Is it real? (Serum osm)
segments.html:
ASK: "Sodium is most of your serum osm. If the Na is 125, what SHOULD the serum osm be — and what does it mean if it isn't low?"
Serum osmInterpretationCauses
< 290True (hypo-osmolar) hyponatremia → go to Step 2—
NormalPseudohyponatremia (artifact)↑Triglycerides, ↑protein (multiple myeloma) dilute whole plasma; water-plasma [Na] normal → send whole-blood Na
> 290Hyperosmolar hyponatremia — "something extra"Active osmoles (glucose, mannitol, sorbitol, glycerin) pull water from cells and dilute Na. Inactive osmoles (BUN, ethanol) raise osm but cross membranes — no hyponatremia
Pearl: corrected Na = measured Na + 1.6 × (glucose − 100)/100. A glucose of 500 "hides" about 6.5 mEq/L of sodium.

segments.label: Step 2 — Is ADH on or off? (Urine osm)
segments.html:
ASK: "What does ADH do to the urine, and who has hyponatremia with ADH OFF?"

ADH (pituitary; triggered by low volume via baroreceptors, or high serum osm) makes the kidney reclaim water → concentrated urine. Urine osm < 100 = ADH off: the kidney is doing its job, diluting maximally — the intake is the problem: primary polydipsia (water in > out) or low solute (beer potomania, tea-and-toast — not enough osmoles to excrete the water). Everything else is a high-ADH state → ask Step 3: is the ADH appropriate?

segments.label: Step 3 — Are the kidneys seeing enough fluid? (Urine Na)
segments.html:
ASK: "Low volume — or low EFFECTIVE volume — does what to renin, aldosterone, and the urine sodium?"
Urine NaPhysiologyDifferential
< 25RAAS on — kidneys reclaim Na (appropriate ADH)True hypovolemia (GI losses, bleeding); low effective circulating volume with high total volume: heart failure, cirrhosis
> 40Kidneys see enough fluid — ADH is inappropriateSIADH (drugs — antidepressants/antiepileptics/antipsychotics, surgery/pain, brain injury, lung disease, malignancy); hypothyroidism; adrenal insufficiency; diuretics (the exception — salt wasting despite hypovolemia)
Pearl: cerebral salt wasting (CNS injury, esp. SAH) mimics SIADH's labs but the patient is hypovolemic — the one place a careful volume exam still earns its keep. And always check TSH + cortisol before calling SIADH: hormone replacement fixes those two impostors.

segments.label: Close — retrieval (2 min)
segments.html: Hide the board. Learners rebuild the three questions, the cutoffs (290 / 100 / 25–40), and one diagnosis per branch from memory. Finish: "Which lab would change first if I started this SIADH patient on furosemide?" (urine Na — the diuretic exception.)

cases.title: Case 1
cases.stem: 68M with alcohol use disorder presents in withdrawal. Na 125. Serum osm 270, urine osm 60, urine Na 65.
cases.reveal: Low — true (hypo-osmolar) hyponatremia.

cases.title: Case 2
cases.stem: 21M with type 1 DM, nausea/vomiting and abdominal pain. Glucose 500, +ketones. Na 125. Serum osm 310, urine osm 800, urine Na 20.
cases.reveal: 310 — high: hyperosmolar hyponatremia. "Something else in the blood" — glucose is the active osmole. Corrected Na = 125 + 1.6 × (500−100)/100 ≈ 131.

cases.title: Case 3
cases.stem: 40F with nausea/vomiting and diarrhea. Na 125. Serum osm 265, urine osm 400, urine Na < 10.
cases.reveal: Low — true hyponatremia.

mcqs.stem: A 61-year-old woman with multiple myeloma has Na 124 on routine labs. She feels well. Serum osmolality is 287. What is the next best step?
mcqs.exp: B. Normal serum osm + low measured Na = pseudohyponatremia from paraprotein; a direct-ISE/whole-blood Na bypasses the dilution artifact. A/D: workup of true hyponatremia starts only after confirming hypo-osmolality. C: she has no true hyponatremia and no symptoms. E: desmopressin is part of overcorrection management, irrelevant here.

mcqs.stem: A 45-year-old marathon runner presents with Na 122, serum osm 252, urine osm 70, urine Na 45. Which mechanism explains the hyponatremia?
opts: Water intake exceeding renal free-water excretion with ADH appropriately suppressed
mcqs.exp: B. Urine osm 70 (<100) = ADH OFF — the kidney is maximally diluting but intake outran excretion (water intoxication/polydipsia physiology). A: SIADH requires concentrated urine (ADH on). C: RAAS activation gives LOW urine Na and ADH would be on. D: serum osm is low — this is true hyponatremia. E: CSW is a high-ADH, hypovolemic state with concentrated urine.

mcqs.stem: A 70-year-old man with small-cell lung cancer: Na 121, serum osm 250, urine osm 600, urine Na 78, euvolemic on exam, normal TSH and cortisol. Diagnosis?
mcqs.exp: A. True hyponatremia + ADH on (urine osm 600) + kidneys seeing enough fluid (urine Na 78) + euvolemia + impostors excluded (TSH/cortisol) = SIADH — classic with SCLC. B/D: both produce LOW urine Na via RAAS. C: requires dilute urine (ADH off). E: serum osm is low, not high.

mcqs.stem: A cirrhotic patient has Na 124, serum osm 262, urine osm 450, urine Na 8. Which best describes the physiology?
opts: ADH is inappropriately secreted; the kidneys see adequate volume
Total body volume is high, but low effective circulating volume activates RAAS and (appropriately) ADH
ADH is suppressed by chronic liver disease
Renal salt wasting from cirrhotic nephropathy
The labs indicate pseudohyponatremia from low albumin
mcqs.exp: B. Cirrhosis = vasodilation + third-spacing → low EFFECTIVE volume despite total-body overload: RAAS on (urine Na 8), ADH on (urine osm 450) — the same lab pattern as true hypovolemia. A: urine Na 8 says the kidneys see LOW volume. C: urine osm 450 proves ADH is on. D: salt wasting would raise urine Na. E: hypoalbuminemia does not cause pseudohyponatremia (lipids/paraproteins do), and the serum osm is genuinely low.

mcqs.stem: A 58-year-old on hydrochlorothiazide presents dizzy with dry mucous membranes: Na 123, serum osm 255, urine osm 500, urine Na 52. The intern concludes "high urine sodium rules out hypovolemia — this is SIADH." What's the flaw?
opts: Diuretics cause salt wasting, so urine Na stays high despite hypovolemia and RAAS activation
Urine osm must be >800 to diagnose SIADH
The serum osm excludes true hyponatremia
mcqs.exp: B. Diuretics are the standard exception: they force natriuresis, making urine Na uninterpretable for volume. With an exam suggesting hypovolemia, thiazide-associated hypovolemic hyponatremia is the lead diagnosis. A: ignores the diuretic exception and the exam. C: any urine osm >100 indicates ADH activity; no 800 threshold. D: thiazides are a classic hyponatremia culprit (and can coexist with SIADH-like physiology) — but the exam points to hypovolemia. E: 255 is low — true hyponatremia.

anki: What are the 3 labs (and 3 questions) for diagnosing hyponatremia?
Serum osm — "is it real?"; urine osm — "is ADH on or off?"; urine Na — "are the kidneys seeing enough fluid?"
Why not classify hyponatremia by volume status at the bedside?
Physician assessment of volume status is classically unreliable; the 3-lab approach interrogates the physiology (osmolality, ADH, renal perfusion) directly.
What defines TRUE hyponatremia?
Hypo-osmolar hyponatremia: serum osm < 290. Normal or high serum osm means something else is in the blood.
What is pseudohyponatremia?
Normal serum osm + low measured Na: excess lipids (hypertriglyceridemia) or protein (multiple myeloma) dilute whole plasma while water-plasma [Na] is normal. Confirm with a whole-blood Na.
What causes hyperosmolar hyponatremia, and how do you correct the Na?

An extra ACTIVE osmole (glucose, mannitol, sorbitol) pulls water out of cells, diluting Na. Corrected Na = measured Na + 1.6 × (glucose − 100)/100. (BUN and ethanol are INACTIVE osmoles — they raise measured osm but cross membranes and do not cause hyponatremia.)
What does a urine osm < 100 mean in hyponatremia?
ADH is OFF — the only causes are excess free-water intake (primary polydipsia) or inadequate solute (beer potomania, tea-and-toast diet).
What does a high urine osm (>100) mean in hyponatremia?
ADH is ON (appropriately or not) — the kidney is concentrating urine; next ask whether ADH secretion is appropriate using the urine Na.
What triggers ADH release physiologically?
Low volume/effective circulating volume (baroreceptors) and high serum osm — ADH makes the kidney reclaim free water, concentrating the urine.
ADH on + urine Na < 25: what does it mean?
RAAS is activated — the kidneys see low volume: true hypovolemia (GI losses) OR low effective circulating volume with high total body volume (heart failure, cirrhosis).
ADH on + urine Na > 40: what's the differential?
The kidneys see enough fluid → inappropriate ADH: SIADH, hypothyroidism, adrenal insufficiency — or diuretic use (salt wasting falsely raises urine Na).
What is the exception that breaks the "low urine Na = low volume" rule?
Diuretics (loop, thiazide) — they waste salt, so urine Na stays high even in a hypovolemic patient.
Common SIADH triggers?
Medications (antidepressants, antiepileptics, antipsychotics), surgery/pain, brain injury, lung disease, malignancy. SIADH patients are euvolemic.
How does cerebral salt wasting differ from SIADH?
Both follow CNS insults (esp. SAH) with high urine Na, but cerebral salt wasting patients are HYPOVOLEMIC; SIADH patients are euvolemic.
How do hypothyroidism and adrenal insufficiency cause hyponatremia?
Both drive excess ADH secretion; the hyponatremia corrects when the hormone deficiency is replaced — always check TSH and cortisol in "SIADH-pattern" labs.
A patient with alcohol use disorder has Na 125, SOsm 270, UOsm 60, UNa 65. Diagnosis?
Beer potomania — true (hypo-osmolar) hyponatremia with ADH OFF (UOsm < 100): solute intake too low to excrete the ingested water.
A patient with DM1 has glucose 500 and Na 125 with SOsm 310. Interpretation?
Hyperosmolar hyponatremia from glucose: corrected Na = 125 + 1.6 × 4 = ~131 — a mild true hyponatremia (here from hypovolemia) plus glucose effect.
Has the 3-lab diagnostic approach changed in recent guidelines?
No — the 2024 Austrian Society for Nephrology consensus confirms serum osm, urine osm, and urine Na identify most causes of hyponatremia. (This lesson covers diagnosis only, not treatment.)
lessons.dot:
#Hyponatremia: Na *125 [acute/chronic/unknown duration]; sx [none/HA/nausea/confusion/seizure]
is it real: SOsm * → [<290 true hypo-osmolar / nl → pseudohypoNa (TG/paraprotein — send whole-blood Na) / >290 hyperosmolar → corrected Na = Na + 1.6×(glc−100)/100 = *]
ADH on?: UOsm * → [<100 ADH OFF → excess water (polydipsia) vs low solute (beer potomania/tea+toast) / >100 ADH ON]
kidneys seeing fluid?: UNa * → [<25 low (E)CV: true hypovolemia vs HF vs cirrhosis / >40: SIADH vs hypothyroid vs AI vs diuretic effect]
excl: TSH *, AM cortisol *, [diuretic use/recent surgery/pain/CNS dz/lung dz/malignancy/drugs: SSRI-antiepileptic-antipsychotic]
impression: * likely 2/2 [hypovolemia (GI/renal loss)/low-ECV (HF/cirrhosis)/SIADH 2/2 */low solute intake/polydipsia/pseudohypoNa/hyperglycemia]
plan (dx talk — tx per separate pathway): [confirm w/ repeat lytes/whole-blood Na]; monitor Na q*h; AVOID rapid correction — chronic hypoNa overcorrection risks ODS
takehome: Skip the bedside volume guess — run the 3 labs in order: serum osm, urine osm, urine Na.
True hyponatremia is hypo-osmolar (<290). Normal osm = pseudohyponatremia (whole-blood Na); high osm = active osmole (corrected Na = +1.6 per 100 of glucose).
Urine osm < 100 = ADH off — only polydipsia, beer potomania, or tea-and-toast.
ADH on + urine Na < 25 = kidneys see low volume (hypovolemia, HF, cirrhosis); urine Na > 40 = inappropriate ADH (SIADH, hypothyroid, adrenal insufficiency) — or diuretics breaking the rule.
This is a diagnosis framework; treatment (rates, 3% saline, ODS risk) is a separate talk.
