lessons.title: Immune Checkpoint Inhibitors
lessons.subtitle: Optimized lesson package · Facilitator-led interactive chalk talk for IM residents · 10–25 min (modular, ± cases)
lessons.credit: Adapted from TeachIM.org — Immune Checkpoint Inhibitors (Longino, Geiger, Schenk; exec. ed. Fainstad; Nov 2021), CC BY-NC 4.0. Optimized June 2026: guideline currency verified, one deck typo corrected, LAG-3 class & irAE-grading updates added, pedagogy restructured, assessments added. Companion deck: Immune_Checkpoint_Inhibitors_Optimized.pptx (original interactive board, surgically corrected + 3 appended slides).
objectives: Build a visual model for how the immune system recognizes — and how tumors evade — destruction by T cells.
Name the major classes of immune checkpoint inhibitors and the cancers in which they are most useful.
Recognize the most common and the most dangerous immune-related adverse events (irAEs) and the first steps in managing them.
Use the NCCN/ASCO irAE guidelines to grade severity and initiate treatment.

prework.q: Q1. In one sentence, what does an immune checkpoint normally do, and how does a tumor exploit it?
prework.a: An immune checkpoint (e.g., PD-L1→PD-1, ligands→CTLA-4) is a normal "brake" that prevents the immune system from attacking healthy tissue (autoimmunity). Tumors hijack this by expressing checkpoint ligands so the T cell receives the "stand down" signal and fails to kill the cancer cell.

prework.q: Q2. Why are melanoma, lung, and bladder cancers especially responsive to ICIs?
prework.a: They carry a high mutational burden (UV light, tobacco), so they display many mutated "non-self" peptides on MHC — more targets for T cells to recognize once the brakes are off.

prework.q: Q3. A patient on pembrolizumab develops diarrhea. What is the first thing you must do before blaming the drug?
prework.a: Rule out other causes — especially infection (stool studies including C. diff). irAE management always starts with excluding mimics, then stopping the ICI, grading severity, and starting steroids.

segments.label: Hook (2 min) — "Why do we care?"
segments.html: Open with the stakes: ~40% of cancer patients now receive an ICI, you will see their complications on the wards and in the ICU, and those complications can appear at any point — even months after the drug is stopped. "If you remember one thing today: a new autoimmune problem in a cancer patient is an ICI side effect until proven otherwise."

segments.label: Objective 1 — How the immune system sees a tumor, and how tumors hide
segments.html:
ASK: "How does your adaptive immune system recognize an infected or cancerous cell?" Reveal: every cell displays peptide fragments on MHC; a T cell's TCR reads the MHC–peptide. Cancer cells carry mutated proteins → "non-self" peptides → the T cell sees them and kills via toxic granules.
ASK: "So how does a tumor escape an immune system that's constantly surveilling it?" Reveal the two checkpoint axes: tumor PD-L1 binds T-cell PD-1 → T-cell "exhaustion"; tumor ligands bind CTLA-4 → same effect. Both "put the brakes" on the T cell.
Pearl: Checkpoints are a normal, borrowed mechanism — PD-L1 and CTLA-4 ligands on healthy tissue prevent autoimmunity. Tumors merely hijack a system your body already uses to protect itself.

segments.label: Objective 2 — The drugs, and when to use them
segments.html:
ASK: "What would happen if you blocked these checkpoints?" Reveal: ICIs are monoclonal antibodies that release the brakes so T cells stay active. Build the class table:
TargetClassExample agents
PD-1Anti-PD-1nivolumab, pembrolizumab, cemiplimab, dostarlimab
PD-L1Anti-PD-L1atezolizumab, durvalumab, avelumab
CTLA-4Anti-CTLA-4ipilimumab, tremelimumab
LAG-3Anti-LAG-3 2022relatlimab (with nivolumab = Opdualag)
ASK: "Which cancers respond best, and why?" Reveal the mutational-burden dot plot: high-mutation cancers (melanoma, lung, bladder) display more neoantigens → more immunogenic → better ICI response. FDA approvals now span melanoma, NSCLC/SCLC, RCC, HCC, urothelial, head & neck, Merkel cell, MSI-high/dMMR tumors, triple-negative breast, classical Hodgkin lymphoma, and more.

segments.label: Objective 3 & 4 — Side effects and how to manage them
segments.html:
ASK: "Given the mechanism, what kind of side effects would you predict?" Reveal: autoimmune toxicity in essentially any organ (irAEs). Most common: GI/colitis, hepatitis, pneumonitis, dermatitis, thyroid, hypophysitis.
CategoryExamplesNote
Most commonColitis/diarrhea, hepatitis, pneumonitis, rash, thyroiditisUsually reversible with steroids
Often irreversibleMyocarditis, type 1 diabetes, primary adrenal insufficiencyHormone replacement / lifelong
Most deadlyMyocarditis (~40–50% fatal, rare); pneumonitis, colitis, hepatitisLow threshold to escalate
Pearl: Toxicity frequency rises with the agent and combination — severe irAEs ~16% with anti-PD-1, ~27% with anti-CTLA-4, up to ~55% with combination therapy. Most develop ~8–12 weeks in but can appear any time, including after stopping.
ASK: "Walk me through the five-step framework for any suspected irAE." Reveal:
StepAction
1Rule out other causes (infection, meds) — e.g., stool studies for diarrhea, cultures for cough/hypoxia
2Stop the checkpoint inhibitor
3Grade severity using the NCCN/ASCO guidelines (G1–G4)
4Start prednisone 1–2 mg/kg/day (G2+) to suppress the over-active immune response
5If not improving in a few days, add a second agent (e.g., infliximab for colitis/pneumonitis, vedolizumab, IVIG, mycophenolate)
Grade-based rule (ASCO/NCCN): G1 → continue ICI + monitor (exceptions: neuro/some heme). G2 → hold ICI, steroids, resume when ≤G1. G3 → hold + high-dose steroid, taper ≥4–6 wk, add 2nd agent if refractory. G4 → permanently discontinue (exception: endocrinopathies controlled on replacement). Check TSH/FT4 at baseline and q4–6 wk.

segments.label: Close (2 min)
segments.html: Hide the board. Have the group rebuild: the two checkpoint axes (PD-1/PD-L1, CTLA-4) + the newer LAG-3; why high-mutation cancers respond; the three irreversible irAEs; and the five-step management framework — then show the take-home slide.

cases.title: Case 1 — Diarrhea on combination therapy
cases.stem: 46-year-old woman with metastatic melanoma on nivolumab (anti-PD-1) + ipilimumab (anti-CTLA-4) presents with new diarrhea, 5–6 bowel movements per day, not interfering with her ADLs.
cases.reveal: Rule out other causes first: stool PCR (C. diff and other infectious causes), and stool lactoferrin/calprotectin. Don't assume irAE colitis until infection is excluded.

cases.title: Case 2 — Cough and hypoxia on pembrolizumab
cases.stem: 68-year-old man with stage IV NSCLC on single-agent pembrolizumab (anti-PD-1). On cycle 5 he develops worsening cough and exertional dyspnea; in clinic SpO₂ is 88% on room air with bilateral coarse crackles.
cases.reveal: Evaluate for infection (nasal viral swab, sputum culture) and image with CT chest with contrast; consider a pulmonary consult. The classic ICI pneumonitis pattern is multifocal peripheral and subpleural mid/lower-lung airspace consolidations.

mcqs.stem: A 60-year-old man with metastatic melanoma on ipilimumab develops 6 watery stools/day for 3 days, not limiting his activities. Stool studies including C. difficile are negative. What is the most appropriate next step?
opts: Continue ipilimumab and start loperamide alone
Hold ipilimumab and start prednisone 1–2 mg/kg/day
Permanently discontinue ipilimumab and start infliximab now
Continue ipilimumab and add empiric oral vancomycin
mcqs.exp: B. This is grade 2 immune-related colitis after excluding infection: hold the ICI and start prednisone 1–2 mg/kg/day. Symptomatic loperamide alone (A) undertreats an autoimmune process. Infliximab (C) is reserved for steroid-refractory disease (no improvement in 2–3 days), not first-line. Vancomycin (D) treats C. diff, which was already excluded.

mcqs.stem: Which immune checkpoint inhibitor mechanism was the first new target class approved after PD-1/PD-L1 and CTLA-4?
mcqs.exp: A. Relatlimab, an anti-LAG-3 antibody, is the third checkpoint class in clinical use (after PD-1/PD-L1 and CTLA-4); the fixed-dose nivolumab+relatlimab combination (Opdualag) was FDA-approved in March 2022 for unresectable/metastatic melanoma. Bevacizumab (B) is an anti-angiogenic, not a checkpoint inhibitor. Rituximab (C) and ibrutinib (D) are not checkpoint inhibitors.

mcqs.stem: A 55-year-old woman on pembrolizumab presents with fatigue, nausea, and hypotension. Labs show Na 128, K 5.4, and a low 8 AM cortisol. Which statement is correct?
opts: This toxicity is typically reversible after a short steroid course
Pembrolizumab can be safely continued without intervention
This is a potentially irreversible endocrinopathy requiring hormone replacement, and ICI may be continued once she is stabilized on replacement
High-dose immunosuppression with infliximab is first-line
mcqs.exp: C. This is ICI-induced primary adrenal insufficiency (or hypophysitis) — an endocrinopathy that is often permanent and managed with physiologic hormone replacement (stress-dose steroids in crisis). Per ASCO/NCCN, ICIs may be continued (or resumed) once the endocrinopathy is controlled on replacement — endocrinopathies are the key exception to the "permanently discontinue at grade 3–4" rule. It is not reversible with a short taper (A), should not be ignored (B), and infliximab (D) has no role here.

mcqs.stem: A 70-year-old man on nivolumab/ipilimumab develops chest pain, a troponin rise, and new heart block 5 weeks into therapy. Which irAE is most concerning, and what is the immediate step?
opts: Pericarditis; start NSAIDs and continue ICI
Myocarditis; stop the ICI and start high-dose corticosteroids urgently
Stable angina; refer to outpatient cardiology
mcqs.exp: B. ICI myocarditis is rare but the most lethal irAE (~40–50% mortality) and frequently presents with conduction disease and troponin elevation. Permanently discontinue the ICI and start high-dose corticosteroids immediately (with cardiology/ICU involvement); it is one of the irreversible/high-mortality toxicities. NSAIDs (A) are inadequate and risky; this is not stable angina (C) or thyroiditis (D).

mcqs.stem: A 64-year-old man with NSCLC on pembrolizumab has grade 1 maculopapular rash over <10% of his body surface, no systemic symptoms. What is the recommended management?
opts: Hold pembrolizumab and start systemic prednisone 1–2 mg/kg
Continue pembrolizumab with topical steroids/emollients and monitor
mcqs.exp: C. For grade 1 toxicities (with the exception of some neurologic/hematologic events), the ICI can be continued with close monitoring; a limited rash is managed with topical steroids, emollients, and antihistamines. Systemic steroids (B) and discontinuation (A) are reserved for higher grades; infliximab (D) is for steroid-refractory severe irAEs, not grade 1 rash.

anki: What is an immune checkpoint?
A normal "brake" (e.g., PD-L1->PD-1, ligands->CTLA-4) that prevents the immune system from attacking healthy tissue (autoimmunity).
How do tumors evade T cells?
They express checkpoint ligands (PD-L1, CTLA-4 ligands) that engage T-cell receptors -> T-cell "exhaustion" -> no kill.
How does a T cell recognize a cancer cell?
TCR reads mutated "non-self" peptides displayed on MHC; more mutations = more neoantigens = more immunogenic.
Anti-PD-1 agents?
Nivolumab, pembrolizumab, cemiplimab, dostarlimab.
Anti-PD-L1 agents?
Atezolizumab, durvalumab, avelumab.
Anti-CTLA-4 agents?
Ipilimumab, tremelimumab.
Newest checkpoint inhibitor class (2022)?
Anti-LAG-3: relatlimab (with nivolumab = Opdualag), FDA-approved Mar 2022 for unresectable/metastatic melanoma.
Which cancers respond best to ICIs?
High mutational burden: melanoma (UV), lung & bladder (tobacco); also RCC, HCC, head/neck, Merkel cell, MSI-high/dMMR, classical Hodgkin lymphoma.
What are irAEs?
Immune-related adverse events -- autoimmune toxicity in essentially any organ from releasing T-cell brakes.
Most common irAEs?
Colitis/diarrhea, hepatitis, pneumonitis, dermatitis, thyroiditis, hypophysitis.
Which irAEs are usually irreversible?
Myocarditis, type 1 diabetes, primary adrenal insufficiency (+/- hypophysitis/hypopituitarism).
Most deadly irAE?
Myocarditis -- rare but ~40-50% fatal; presents with troponin rise + conduction disease.
irAE frequency by agent?
Severe irAEs ~16% anti-PD-1, ~27% anti-CTLA-4, up to ~55% with combination therapy. Usually 8-12 wk in, but any time.
Five-step framework for a suspected irAE?
1) Rule out other causes (infection/meds) 2) Stop the ICI 3) Grade severity (NCCN/ASCO) 4) Prednisone 1-2 mg/kg 5) Add 2nd agent if no improvement in a few days.
ASCO/NCCN grade-based rule for irAEs?
G1: continue + monitor; G2: hold + steroids, resume when <=G1; G3: hold + high-dose steroid, taper >=4-6 wk; G4: permanently d/c (except controlled endocrinopathies).
First step before treating ICI diarrhea as colitis?
Exclude infection -- stool studies including C. difficile.
Steroid-refractory ICI colitis -- next agent?
Infliximab or vedolizumab if no improvement in 2-3 days of high-dose steroids.
lessons.dot:
#?irAE: * yo on ICI [anti-PD-1 (pembro/nivo/cemip/dostar) / anti-PD-L1 (atezo/durva/avelu) / anti-CTLA-4 (ipi/treme) / anti-LAG-3 (relatlimab) / combo], cycle *, presenting w/ * [diarrhea/colitis, dyspnea/pneumonitis, transaminitis/hepatitis, rash, thyroid, AI/hypophysitis, chest pain/myocarditis]. Sx onset * wks (typical 8-12 wk, can be any time incl after d/c). HDS [yes/no].
- STEP 1 r/o mimics: infection [stool PCR + C.diff for diarrhea; resp viral panel/sputum cx/CT chest for pulm; cultures], med/dz-related, progression. labs *
- STEP 2 hold ICI (G2+); STEP 3 grade severity per NCCN/ASCO [G1-G4]
- organ w/u: colitis [stool studies, lactoferrin/calprotectin, ?scope] / pneumonitis [CT chest contrast: multifocal peripheral subpleural consolidation; pulm c/s] / hepatitis [LFTs, r/o viral/EtOH/biliary] / endo [TSH/FT4, AM cortisol+ACTH, glucose] / cardiac [trop, ECG, echo, MRI - URGENT]
- STEP 4 tx by grade:
  - G1: continue ICI + monitor (EXCEPT neuro/some heme); topical/supportive
  - G2: hold ICI; prednisone 1-2 mg/kg/day; resume ICI when <=G1
  - G3: hold ICI; high-dose steroid (pred/methylpred 1-2 mg/kg) taper >=4-6 wk; if no response 48-72h -> 2nd agent [infliximab (colitis/pneumonitis), vedolizumab, IVIG, MMF]
  - G4: PERMANENTLY d/c ICI (EXCEPT endocrinopathy controlled on hormone replacement)
- irreversible/deadly watch: myocarditis (~40-50% fatal -> urgent steroids + cards/ICU), T1DM, primary AI
- monitoring: TSH/FT4 baseline + q4-6 wk; consult onc; NCCN irAE guideline at bedside
- counsel pt: report new sx promptly, irAE card, *
takehome: Tumors evade T cells by engaging immune checkpoints (PD-L1→PD-1, ligands→CTLA-4) that put the brakes on the immune response — a normal anti-autoimmunity mechanism the tumor hijacks.
ICIs — anti-PD-1/PD-L1, anti-CTLA-4, and now anti-LAG-3 — release those brakes; they work best in high-mutation-burden cancers (melanoma, lung, bladder).
irAEs are autoimmune, can hit any organ, and may appear during or months after therapy — keep ICIs on the differential for any new autoimmune problem.

Management framework: rule out other causes → stop the ICI → grade severity → prednisone 1–2 mg/kg → add a second agent if no improvement in a few days. Myocarditis, type 1 DM, and adrenal insufficiency are the irreversible ones.
The NCCN/ASCO irAE guidelines are the go-to bedside resource for grading and treatment.

  7. Tawbi HA, Schadendorf D, Lipson EJ, et al. Relatlimab and nivolumab versus nivolumab in untreated advanced melanoma (RELATIVITY-047). NEJM. 2022;386(1):24-34.
  8. NCCN Clinical Practice Guidelines in Oncology — Management of Immunotherapy-Related Toxicities. National Comprehensive Cancer Network (current version, nccn.org).
  9. TeachIM.org — Immune Checkpoint Inhibitors. Longino N, Geiger C, Schenk E; exec. ed. Fainstad B. Nov 2021. teachim.org/teaching_material/immune-checkpoint-inhibitors-2/

lessons.title: Inflammatory Arthritis
lessons.subtitle: Facilitator-led interactive chalk talk · ~45 min · IM attendings & senior residents teaching residents
objectives: Use acuity (acute vs chronic) and number of involved joints (mono-, oligo-, polyarticular) to generate a focused differential for inflammatory arthritis.

Determine when synovial fluid analysis, advanced microbiologic testing (Lyme PCR, GC cultures), or imaging (chondrocalcinosis, marginal erosions, SI-joint changes) is indicated to clarify the etiology.
Select initial management for common arthritides and recognize red-flag presentations requiring urgent antimicrobials, rheumatology involvement, or surgical evaluation.

prework.q: 1. A patient has one hot, swollen knee that started yesterday. What diagnosis must you exclude first, and what test does it?
prework.a: Septic arthritis — presume it until proven otherwise. Arthrocentesis with cell count + differential, Gram stain, culture, and crystal exam. Fluid is typically >50K WBC with PMN predominance (lower thresholds for prosthetic joints or partially treated infection).

prework.q: 2. Name the two crystal arthropathies and the crystal seen in each.
prework.a: Gout — monosodium urate (needle-shaped, negatively birefringent). CPPD — calcium pyrophosphate (rhomboid, weakly positively birefringent); x-rays may show chondrocalcinosis.

prework.q: 3. Which chronic polyarthritis spares the DIP joints, and which one targets them?
prework.a: RA spares the DIPs (and thoracolumbar spine); psoriatic arthritis can involve the DIPs — look for nail pitting, onycholysis, dactylitis, and enthesitis.

segments.label: Hook
segments.html: "You're called about a 58-year-old with a hot, swollen knee. The night intern wants to start steroids for a 'gout flare.' What single test changes everything you do next?" Let them argue, then frame the talk: every inflammatory joint complaint can be placed on a 2-axis grid — time (acute vs chronic) and space (1 joint, 2–4 joints, 5+ joints) — and that grid, plus synovial fluid, drives the whole work-up.

segments.label: Segment 1 — The time-and-space grid
segments.html:
ASK — "Before any labs: what two questions about the arthritis itself most narrow the differential?" (Answer: how fast, and how many joints.)

Build the board's grid: acute mono → septic, gout, CPPD; acute oligo/poly → GC, reactive, viral, sarcoid (Löfgren's); chronic mono/oligo → fungal/TB, Lyme; chronic poly → RA, SLE, psoriatic, ankylosing spondylitis, tophaceous gout.

Synovial fluid WBCThink
<2KNon-inflammatory (OA, internal derangement)
2–20KInflammatory — autoimmune, crystal, Lyme (>2K), indolent infection (fungal/TB 10–20K)
10–50KGonococcal; overlaps with crystal disease
>50K, PMN-predominantSeptic until proven otherwise (lower cutoff if prosthetic joint or prior antibiotics)
Pearl: crystals in the fluid do NOT exclude infection — septic arthritis and gout coexist. If the story says infection, culture and cover even when you see crystals.

segments.label: Segment 2 — Acute monoarthritis: the can't-miss three
segments.html:
ASK — "Gram stain shows gram-positive cocci in clusters. What's your empiric drug? What if it showed gram-negative rods?" (Vancomycin; ceftriaxone.)

Septic: hot, swollen, painful joint with limited passive ROM; fluid usually >50K PMN-predominant. Empiric therapy by Gram stain (vancomycin for GPC, ceftriaxone for GN); most need urgent surgical washout.

Acute gout: MSU crystals under polarized light; first MTP most common, lower-extremity predominant. Risk factors: obesity, HTN, CKD. Treat flares with NSAIDs, colchicine, or steroids. Start ULT (allopurinol first-line) for ≥2 flares/yr — or after one flare if CKD ≥3, urate >9, urolithiasis, erosions, or tophi — goal urate <6, and keep prophylaxis running while titrating (ACR 2020).

CPPD: CPP crystals; chondrocalcinosis on x-ray (knees, wrists, fingers). Phenotypes range from acute pseudogout to chronic RA-like arthritis to asymptomatic chondrocalcinosis. Screen for hyperparathyroidism and hemochromatosis. Flares treated like gout flares.

ASK — "Your gout patient starts allopurinol and flares two weeks later. Did the allopurinol fail?" (No — mobilization flare; this is why prophylaxis continues during titration.)

segments.label: Segment 3 — Acute oligo/polyarthritis
segments.html:
Gonococcal: young, sexually active; migratory polyarthralgia + tenosynovitis + pustular dermatitis; fluid 10–50K; isolate N. gonorrhoeae from joint, blood, or mucosal sites. Ceftriaxone first-line; cover chlamydia if not excluded (doxycycline preferred per CDC 2021). Excellent prognosis treated.

Reactive: 1–6 weeks after GI/GU infection; asymmetric lower-extremity oligoarthritis; HLA-B27; uveitis, urethritis, keratoderma blennorrhagicum; synovial cultures negative.

Viral: parvovirus B19 (most common — RA-like small-joint polyarthritis, +IgM), then HBV/HCV; travel → dengue, chikungunya, Zika. Supportive NSAIDs. Transient autoantibodies (ANA, RF) are common — don't anchor on serologies in acute arthritis.

Sarcoid: ankles/knees/wrists; Löfgren's = erythema nodosum + bilateral hilar adenopathy + acute arthritis; NSAIDs, then sarcoid DMARDs (MTX, TNFi) if needed.

segments.label: Segment 4 — Chronic arthritis
segments.html:
ASK — "A chronic knee monoarthritis with 15K WBC and negative cultures — what two indolent infections are still on the table, and what tests find them?" (Fungal/TB → synovial biopsy; Lyme → Borrelia PCR.)

Fungal/TB: indolent; immunosuppression or endemic travel; fluid 10–20K, may be culture-negative → synovial biopsy; surgical evaluation + ID consult.

Lyme: months after tick exposure; large joint (knee); fluid >2K; Borrelia PCR; 4-week doxycycline course (IDSA 2020); persistent post-infectious arthritis may need DMARDs.

Spondyloarthropathies (HLA-B27 family): ankylosing spondylitis (SI joints > rest of spine; marginal erosions, syndesmophytes, ankylosis; TNFi for axial disease, oral DMARDs like sulfasalazine for peripheral), psoriatic (asymmetric, DIPs, enthesitis, dactylitis, nail pitting/onycholysis — skin severity does NOT track joint severity; screen for uveitis and IBD), reactive (usually acute), and IBD-associated.

SLE: symmetric small-joint polyarthritis; ROS for photosensitive rash, oral ulcers, alopecia, Raynaud's, serositis; soft-tissue swelling > erosions; NSAIDs/low-dose prednisone acutely; HCQ, MTX, or azathioprine long-term.

RA: symmetric wrists/MCPs/PIPs/ankles/MTPs, spares DIPs and the spine; RF, CCP, ESR, CRP; periarticular osteopenia and marginal erosions; MTX is first-line DMARD; baseline and q1–2-yr hand/foot films.

Tophaceous gout: chronic urate deposition punctuated by acute flares; favors cool sites (fingertips, toes, Achilles, elbows); punched-out erosions with overhanging edges; ULT to urate <6 (<5 to shrink tophi) plus prophylaxis while titrating.

ASK — "Symmetric small-joint polyarthritis with DIP involvement and pitted nails — RA or something else?" (PsA — RA spares DIPs.)

segments.label: Retrieval close
segments.html: Erase the board. Have learners rebuild the time-and-space grid from memory, then state: the synovial fluid threshold for septic arthritis, the ULT start rules and urate goal, and three features that separate PsA from RA. Finish with the take-home points below.

mcqs.stem: A 61-year-old man with diabetes presents with 24 hours of a hot, swollen right knee and fever. Arthrocentesis: 68,000 WBC/µL, 93% PMNs; Gram stain shows gram-positive cocci in clusters; intracellular needle-shaped negatively birefringent crystals are also seen. Which is the best next step?
opts: Oral colchicine and follow-up in 1 week
IV vancomycin and urgent orthopedic consultation for washout
mcqs.exp: C. Crystals do not exclude infection: with >50K PMN-predominant fluid, fever, and organisms on Gram stain, this is septic arthritis (with coexistent gout) — treat empirically by Gram stain (GPC → vancomycin) plus urgent washout. A and B treat the gout while the joint is being destroyed and can worsen infection; D delays therapy in a closed-space emergency; E — ULT is never started for the acute event alone and does nothing for infection.

mcqs.stem: A 24-year-old sexually active woman has 4 days of migratory wrist and ankle pain, painful tendon sheaths of the dorsal hand, and scattered pustular lesions on the extremities. Knee fluid: 32,000 WBC/µL, Gram stain negative. Which treatment is most appropriate?
opts: Ceftriaxone, plus doxycycline if chlamydia is not excluded
mcqs.exp: A. Migratory polyarthralgia + tenosynovitis + pustular dermatitis = disseminated gonococcal infection; fluid is typically 10–50K and Gram stain is often negative — treat with ceftriaxone and cover chlamydia co-infection (doxycycline preferred, CDC 2021). B covers gram-positives, not N. gonorrhoeae; C is Lyme dosing; D and E miss a treatable bacteremic infection.

mcqs.stem: A 55-year-old man with CKD stage 3 has his third gout flare this year. Serum urate is 8.9 mg/dL. After the flare is treated, which long-term plan is most appropriate (ACR 2020)?
opts: No urate-lowering therapy; treat flares as they come
Start low-dose allopurinol, titrate to urate <6 mg/dL, with colchicine prophylaxis during titration
Start allopurinol only if tophi develop
mcqs.exp: C. ≥2 flares/year is a strong indication for ULT; allopurinol is first-line (including in CKD, started low and titrated), target urate <6, and prophylaxis continues while titrating to prevent mobilization flares. A ignores a strong ULT indication; B — febuxostat is second-line and high starting doses are not recommended; D waits for irreversible damage; E — diet alone rarely reaches target.

mcqs.stem: A 68-year-old woman has recurrent acute knee swelling. Radiographs show cartilage calcification in both knees and the wrist triangular fibrocartilage. Aspirate shows rhomboid, weakly positively birefringent crystals. Beyond treating the flare, which evaluation is most appropriate?
mcqs.exp: A. Chondrocalcinosis + CPP crystals = CPPD; screen for the associated metabolic diseases — hyperparathyroidism (Ca/PTH) and hemochromatosis (iron studies). B is for spondyloarthropathy; C is for Lyme (chronic large-joint mono/oligoarthritis after tick exposure); D is for RA; E has no role.

mcqs.stem: A 39-year-old Connecticut hiker has 3 months of a swollen, mildly painful left knee. Fluid: 24,000 WBC/µL, cultures negative. He recalls a febrile illness with a spreading rash last summer, untreated. Which is the best diagnostic and therapeutic approach?
opts: Synovial fluid Borrelia PCR; if supportive, doxycycline for 4 weeks
mcqs.exp: A. Chronic large-joint monoarthritis months after tick exposure in an endemic area = Lyme arthritis; fluid is inflammatory (>2K, often 10–25K), PCR supports the diagnosis, and a 4-week doxycycline course is standard (IDSA 2020). B — the indolent course and fluid count don't fit pyogenic infection; C before antibiotics can worsen outcomes; D is reserved for post-infectious arthritis after antibiotics fail; E doesn't address the leading diagnosis.

anki: What two features rapidly narrow the differential for inflammatory arthritis?
Time (acute vs chronic) and space (number of joints: mono = 1, oligo = 2–4, poly = 5+).
What is the default diagnosis for an acute hot, swollen, painful monoarthritis until proven otherwise?
Septic arthritis — a true emergency requiring synovial fluid analysis, empiric antibiotics, and usually urgent surgical washout.
Typical synovial fluid WBC count in septic arthritis?
Usually >50,000/µL with PMN predominance (use lower cutoffs in prosthetic joints or after partial antibiotic treatment).
Empiric antibiotics for septic arthritis by Gram stain?
Gram-positive cocci → vancomycin; gram-negative organisms → ceftriaxone.
Synovial fluid finding diagnostic of acute gout?
Monosodium urate crystals (needle-shaped, negatively birefringent) under polarized microscopy.
Most common joint in acute gout?
First MTP (podagra, ~90%); lower-extremity predominant including the mid-foot, but any joint is possible.
When should urate-lowering therapy be started in gout (ACR 2020)?
≥2 flares/year, tophi, or radiographic gouty erosions; consider after a single flare if CKD stage ≥3, serum urate >9 mg/dL, or urolithiasis. Allopurinol is first-line; goal urate <6 mg/dL.
Why continue colchicine when starting allopurinol?
Anti-inflammatory prophylaxis prevents mobilization flares while urate-lowering therapy is titrated (ACR 2020 — continue ~3–6 months).
Synovial fluid and imaging findings in CPPD?
Calcium pyrophosphate crystals (rhomboid, weakly positively birefringent); radiographs may show chondrocalcinosis of knees, wrists, and fingers.
Two metabolic diseases to screen for in CPPD?
Hyperparathyroidism and hemochromatosis.
What is Löfgren's syndrome?
Acute sarcoidosis triad: erythema nodosum + bilateral hilar adenopathy + acute arthritis (classically ankles).
Classic triad of disseminated gonococcal infection?
Migratory polyarthralgia, tenosynovitis, and pustular dermatitis; treat with ceftriaxone (add doxycycline if chlamydia not excluded — CDC 2021).
Key features of reactive arthritis?
Asymmetric lower-extremity oligoarthritis 1–6 weeks after a GI or GU infection, HLA-B27 association, negative synovial cultures; look for uveitis, urethritis, keratoderma blennorrhagicum.
Most common viral arthritis and its serology?
Parvovirus B19 — RA-like symmetric small-joint polyarthritis with positive IgM (IgG = prior exposure); HBV and HCV are next most common.
Presentation and treatment of Lyme arthritis?
Mono/oligoarthritis of a large joint (knee) months after tick exposure; synovial fluid >2K WBC, send Borrelia PCR; treat with a 4-week course of doxycycline (IDSA 2020).
Joint pattern that distinguishes RA from psoriatic arthritis?
RA: symmetric wrists/MCPs/PIPs/ankles/MTPs, SPARES DIPs and the spine. PsA: asymmetric, can involve DIPs and the axial skeleton, with enthesitis, dactylitis, and nail pitting/onycholysis.
Hallmark axial imaging findings of ankylosing spondylitis?
Sacroiliitis (SI involvement > rest of spine) with marginal erosions, syndesmophytes, and ankylosis; HLA-B27 associated.
X-ray hallmark of tophaceous gout?
Radiolucent tophi with punched-out erosions and overhanging edges, especially hands and feet; urate goal <6 mg/dL (may need <5 to shrink tophi).
lessons.dot:
#Acute inflammatory arthritis: * joint(s) x * days, [mono/oligo(2-4)/poly(5+)], [acute/chronic]
hx: [trauma/prior flares/tick exposure/GI-GU infxn 1-6wk prior/psoriasis/IBD/immunosuppression/sexual hx/travel]
likely 2/2 * [septic/gout/CPPD/GC/reactive/viral(parvo-HBV-HCV)/Lyme/RA/PsA/SLE/axSpA/sarcoid] — r/o septic joint [tap before steroids; crystals do NOT excl infection]
core w/u: arthrocentesis [WBC * /diff *, gram stain *, cx *, crystals *] + CBC/BMP/ESR/CRP/blood cx if febrile
  fluid: <2K non-inflamm | 2-20K autoimmune/crystal/Lyme/indolent | 10-50K GC | >50K PMN septic [lower if prosthetic/prior abx]
targeted only: [GC NAAT mucosal+fluid cx / Borrelia PCR / parvo IgM / HBV-HCV / RF-CCP / ANA / HLA-B27 / uric acid (may be nl in flare) / XR affected jt + SI if axial sx / synovial bx if chronic culture-neg]
AVOID: ANA/RF for acute arthritis [transient post-viral autoAb common]; serum urate alone to dx/excl gout
mgmt: septic → vanc [GPC] / CTX [GNR] + urgent ortho washout | gout/CPPD flare → NSAID/colchicine/steroid [renal dosing]
  gout ULT: allopurinol 1st-line start * mg if >=2 flares/yr, tophi, erosions [or 1 flare + CKD>=3 / urate>9 / urolithiasis]; goal <6* [<5 if tophi]; colchicine ppx while titrating (ACR 2020)
  GC → CTX + doxy if chlamydia not excl (CDC 2021) | Lyme → doxy x4wk (IDSA 2020) | chronic autoimmune → DMARD + rheum [RA: MTX 1st-line]
red flags → urgent eval: [fever + mono jt / prosthetic jt / IVDU / immunosupp / failed outpt tx]
f/u: repeat exam *; rheum referral if [chronic >6wk / erosions / multisystem sx]
takehome: Start with time and space — acuity and joint count are the fastest way to narrow the differential.
Never miss infection — acute monoarthritis is septic until proven otherwise; synovial fluid analysis and timely antibiotics/surgical consultation are critical, and crystals don't exclude infection.
Pattern recognition matters — symmetry, axial involvement, DIP disease, enthesitis, dactylitis, and extra-articular clues (skin, eye, GI, GU) separate RA, SLE, PsA, and the spondyloarthropathies.
Match treatment to etiology and trajectory — flares get anti-inflammatories; chronic autoimmune and indolent infectious arthritis need early disease-modifying therapy and specialty involvement.

extras.label: Currency Notes checked June 2026
extras.html:
The original is recent (Dec 2025) and its frameworks are fully current — no update slide was needed on the deck. Verified against:

  • ACR 2020 gout guideline — still the current ACR guidance (allopurinol first-line incl. CKD, start low and titrate, treat-to-target <6, prophylaxis during titration, ULT indications as taught). No newer ACR gout guideline has superseded it as of June 2026.
  • CDC 2021 STI Treatment Guidelines — ceftriaxone remains first-line for gonococcal infection; for presumptive chlamydia co-treatment, doxycycline is now preferred over azithromycin (azithromycin mainly in pregnancy). The deck's "doxycycline or azithromycin" remains acceptable wording; preference noted here.
  • IDSA/AAN/ACR 2020 Lyme guideline — 28-day oral doxycycline for Lyme arthritis, as taught.
  • IDSA/ASM 2024 microbiology utilization guide (cited by the original) — synovial fluid testing recommendations unchanged.

  3. Miller M, et al. IDSA/ASM Guide to Utilization of the Microbiology Laboratory for Diagnosis of Infectious Diseases: 2024 Update. Clin Infect Dis. 2024.
  4. Stamp LK, Dalbeth N. Critical appraisal of serum urate targets in the management of gout. Nat Rev Rheumatol. 2022;18(10):603–609.

lessons.title: Inpatient Evaluation of Hypoxemia
lessons.subtitle: Facilitator-led chalk talk · ~20 min (core) / ~35 min (with cases) · IM attendings & senior residents teaching residents
objectives: Develop a broad differential for acute dyspnea.
Construct a framework for evaluating acute dyspnea.
Identify the causes of acute hypoxemia and how to differentiate them.

prework.q: 1. List the four system categories of acute dyspnea.
prework.a: Respiratory (airways / alveoli / vessels), cardiac, musculoskeletal, and the "3 A's" — anemia, anxiety, acidosis. Respiratory and cardiac are most common.

prework.q: 2. Write the A-a gradient and the expected-value formulas.
prework.a: A-a = PAO₂ − PaO₂, where PAO₂ = FiO₂ × (Patm − PH₂O) − PaCO₂/0.8 (≈ 150 − 1.25 × PaCO₂ on room air). Expected A-a = age/4 + 4 (a 64-year-old expects ~20).

prework.q: 3. Which cause of hypoxemia fails to correct with 100% oxygen?
prework.a: Shunt — perfusion without ventilation (anatomic shunt or a large alveolar filling/collapse process). V/Q mismatch, by contrast, improves with supplemental O₂.

segments.label: Hook
segments.html: "Your patient just desatted to 88%. Before you reflexively turn up the oxygen, you need two things: a differential built by anatomy, and one number — the A-a gradient — that splits the causes of hypoxemia in half." Project the Causes board.

segments.label: Objective 1 — Differential for acute dyspnea
segments.html:
ASK — "Walk the airway from mouth to alveolus — what blocks or narrows each level?"
CategoryExamples
Respiratory — airwaysAnaphylaxis/angioedema, mucus plugging, airway stenosis, bronchoconstriction (COPD, asthma)
Respiratory — alveoliFilling: DAH (blood), pneumonia (pus), edema (water). Collapse: atelectasis. Pleural process (blood/pus/water/air)
Respiratory — vesselsPulmonary embolism
CardiacMI, arrhythmia, valvular disease, pericardial disease
MusculoskeletalDiaphragm dysfunction, abdominal pressure
The "3 A's"Anemia, anxiety, acidosis (metabolic and respiratory)
Pearl. "Blockage or narrowing of any part of the respiratory system causes dyspnea." Building the list anatomically (airway → alveolus → vessel) makes it reproducible at the bedside.

segments.label: Objective 2 — Evaluation toolbox
segments.html:
ASK — "For each category, what first-line test confirms it?"

Map tests to pathology: physical exam everywhere; CXR for alveolar/pleural processes; CT-PE for vascular; ECG + exam for cardiac; CBC for anemia; BMP + blood gas for acidosis. The four high-yield "toolbox" tests: CXR, blood gas, CBC, ECG — now joined by bedside POCUS.

Pearl — ABG vs VBG. Get an ABG when you need the PaO₂ — to confirm/grade hypoxemia or compute the A-a gradient. Otherwise a VBG suffices: ABG pH ≈ VBG pH + 0.04; ABG PaCO₂ ≈ VBG PvCO₂ − 4.

segments.label: Objective 3 — Causes of hypoxemia
segments.html:
ASK — "Dyspnea vs hypoxemia — are they the same?" Reveal: no. Hypoxemia = low PaO₂ (normal 75–100); many dyspneic patients are not hypoxemic.
CauseA-a gradientNotes
Low FiO₂NormalAltitude; not an acute inpatient cause
HypoventilationNormal↑ PaCO₂ displaces O₂; CNS depression, opioids/benzos, neuromuscular weakness
V/Q mismatchElevatedMost common; obstructive disease, vascular disease, PE, small filling/collapse
ShuntElevatedPerfusion without ventilation; doesn't correct with 100% O₂
Low diffusion capacityElevatedChronic (ILD, emphysema); not acute
Pearl — the A-a split. First decide normal vs elevated A-a. Normal → hypoventilation (check the PaCO₂). Elevated → V/Q mismatch vs shunt; the 100%-O₂ challenge separates them (shunt won't budge). Expected A-a rises with age (age/4 + 4).

segments.label: Retrieval close
segments.html: Board off. Ask a learner to rebuild the 5 causes sorted by A-a, and to compute the expected A-a for a 60-year-old. Ask a second learner: when do you reach for an ABG instead of a VBG?

cases.title: Case 1 — 25F, type 1 diabetes
cases.stem: 1 day of dyspnea. T 36, HR 110, BP 125/70, RR 32, SaO₂ 99% on RA. Tachypneic without increased work of breathing, clear lungs.
cases.reveal: CXR clear; BMP Na 128, K 4.5, HCO₃ 8, BUN 32, Cr 2.2; WBC 12.5k, Hgb 10.2; VBG 7.1/29/–/9 (no hypoxemia concern, so a VBG suffices). Diagnosis: dyspnea/tachypnea from respiratory compensation of severe metabolic acidosis — DKA. (A "3 A's" cause: acidosis, normal oxygenation.)

cases.title: Case 2 — 84M, HLD/CAD
cases.stem: 1 day of dyspnea. T 38, HR 110, BP 125/70, RR 24, SaO₂ 92% on RA. Crackles in the R lower lung fields.
cases.reveal: Focal lung findings → CXR (focal abnormality). ABG 7.48/32/65/22 (hypoxemic). Diagnosis: dyspnea and hypoxemia from RLL pneumonia.

cases.title: Case 3 — 74F, non-Hodgkin lymphoma
cases.stem: 1 day of dyspnea. T 36, HR 110, BP 98/60, RR 24, SaO₂ 94% on RA. Elevated JVP, clear lungs.
cases.reveal: CXR shows an enlarged cardiac silhouette; ECG shows electrical alternans. With hypotension, elevated JVP, and a pericardial effusion → cardiac tamponade. (See changelog: the board's CXR line says "cardiomegaly or pleural effusion" — likely intended as "pericardial effusion"; flagged for author review.)

cases.title: Case 4 — 64M, POD after R nephrectomy
cases.reveal: BMP/CXR/ECG largely unremarkable; ABG normal without hypoxemia; CBC shows a severe drop in hematocrit. Diagnosis: dyspnea from severe anemia (post-op bleeding) — a "3 A's" cause with normal PaO₂.

mcqs.stem: A 64-year-old has PaO₂ 50, PaCO₂ 32 on room air. PAO₂ ≈ 110. What is the A-a gradient and is it elevated for age?
mcqs.exp: B. A-a = PAO₂ − PaO₂ = 110 − 50 = 60. Expected = age/4 + 4 = 64/4 + 4 = 20. 60 ≫ 20 → elevated. D uses the miscalculated "16" (the original deck error — 64/4 + 4 is 20, not 16). E is wrong: room air FiO₂ 0.21 is assumed in PAO₂ ≈ 150 − 1.25×PaCO₂.

mcqs.stem: A patient is hypoxemic with a normal A-a gradient and PaCO₂ of 70. The most likely mechanism is:
mcqs.exp: C. A normal A-a gradient with hypercapnia = hypoventilation (the high CO₂ displaces alveolar O₂; the alveolar-capillary unit is intact). Shunt and V/Q mismatch raise the A-a gradient. Low diffusion is chronic and also raises the A-a. Anemia causes tissue hypoxia, not a low PaO₂.

mcqs.stem: A hypoxemic patient does not improve despite 100% oxygen by non-rebreather. This most strongly suggests:
mcqs.exp: C. Hypoxemia refractory to 100% O₂ is the hallmark of a shunt — blood bypasses ventilated alveoli, so added FiO₂ never reaches it. V/Q mismatch and hypoventilation correct with supplemental oxygen.

mcqs.stem: Which patient most clearly needs an ABG rather than a VBG?
opts: DKA where you only need the pH and bicarbonate
A hypoxemic patient in whom you must calculate the A-a gradient
mcqs.exp: B. The ABG is required when you need the PaO₂ — to confirm/grade hypoxemia or compute the A-a gradient. For pH/CO₂ trends (DKA, COPD) a VBG suffices (ABG pH ≈ VBG + 0.04; ABG PaCO₂ ≈ VBG − 4).

mcqs.stem: A 74-year-old with hypotension, elevated JVP, clear lungs, and electrical alternans on ECG is dyspneic. The unifying diagnosis is:
mcqs.exp: B. Hypotension + elevated JVP + electrical alternans + an enlarged cardiac silhouette point to a pericardial effusion causing tamponade (a cardiac cause of dyspnea, clear lungs). The others don't explain electrical alternans with elevated JVP.

anki: Four system categories of acute dyspnea
Respiratory (airways/alveoli/vessels), cardiac, musculoskeletal, and the "3 A's" (anemia, anxiety, acidosis).
Most common categories of dyspnea
Respiratory and cardiac.
Core diagnostic toolbox for dyspnea
CXR, blood gas (ABG/VBG), CBC, ECG — plus bedside POCUS.
Is dyspnea the same as hypoxemia?
No — dyspnea is subjective shortness of breath; hypoxemia is a low PaO2. Either can occur without the other.
Definition and normal range of PaO2
Hypoxemia = low PaO2 measured on ABG; normal PaO2 ~75–100 mmHg.
Five causes of hypoxemia
Low FiO2, low diffusion capacity (both normal A-a, not acute inpatient causes), hypoventilation (normal A-a), V/Q mismatch, and shunt (both elevated A-a).
A-a gradient formula
A-a = PAO2 − PaO2; PAO2 = FiO2 × (Patm − PH2O) − PaCO2/0.8 (≈150 − 1.25×PaCO2 on room air).
Expected (normal) A-a gradient
Age/4 + 4 (e.g., a 64-year-old ≈ 20).
Which hypoxemia causes have a NORMAL A-a gradient?
Low FiO2 and hypoventilation (and low diffusion is chronic).
Which hypoxemia causes have an ELEVATED A-a gradient?
V/Q mismatch and shunt (and chronically, low diffusion capacity).
How to distinguish shunt from V/Q mismatch at the bedside
Shunt does NOT correct with 100% O2; V/Q mismatch does.
Mechanism of hypoxemia in hypoventilation
↓ CO2 elimination → ↑ PaCO2 displaces alveolar O2 → ↓ PaO2 (normal A-a). Causes: CNS depression, opioids/benzos, neuromuscular weakness, diaphragm dysfunction.
What causes a shunt?
Perfusion without ventilation — anatomic shunt or a large alveolar filling/collapse process (blood, water, pus).
When should you get an ABG instead of a VBG?
When you need the PaO2 — to confirm/grade hypoxemia or calculate the A-a gradient.
VBG-to-ABG conversion estimates
ABG pH ≈ VBG pH + 0.04; ABG PaCO2 ≈ VBG PvCO2 − 4.
Causes of hypoxia that are NOT hypoxemia
Stagnant (low flow), anemic, histotoxic (cyanide), and methemoglobinemia — tissue-level hypoxia with a normal PaO2.
Drugs causing methemoglobinemia / cyanide in hospital
Methemoglobinemia: nitrates, local anesthetics, sulfonamides. Cyanide: sodium nitroprusside.
Role of POCUS in acute dyspnea
BLUE-protocol lung ultrasound rapidly distinguishes cardiogenic edema, pneumonia, pneumothorax, and effusion (high sensitivity), complementing CXR/ABG/ECG/CBC.
lessons.dot:
#Acute dyspnea: SpO2 *% on *; RR *; WOB *
dyspnea ddx by system:
  resp-airway [anaphylaxis/angioedema / mucus plug / stenosis / bronchospasm COPD-asthma]
  resp-alveoli [edema / PNA / DAH / atelectasis / pleural process]
  resp-vessel [PE]
  cardiac [MI / arrhythmia / valvular / pericardial-tamponade]
  MSK [diaphragm dysfunction / abd pressure]
  3 A's [anemia / anxiety / acidosis]
toolbox: CXR / blood gas / CBC / ECG / POCUS (BLUE)
hypoxemia? PaO2 *= [<75 = hypoxemic; normal 75-100] (ABG if need PaO2)
A-a gradient: PAO2 = 150 - 1.25*PaCO2 (RA); A-a = PAO2 - PaO2 *= ; expected = age/4 + 4 *=
  NORMAL A-a -> [low FiO2 / hypoventilation (check PaCO2)]
  ELEVATED A-a -> [V/Q mismatch / shunt]; shunt = no correction w/ 100% O2; low diffusion = chronic
ABG vs VBG: ABG for PaO2/A-a; else VBG (pH +0.04, PaCO2 -4)
non-hypoxemic hypoxia: [anemic / stagnant / cyanide-nitroprusside / methemoglobin-nitrates/LA/sulfa]
plan: target SpO2 *; treat underlying *; reassess *
* = bedside-editable value
takehome: Build the dyspnea differential by system: respiratory (airways/alveoli/vessels), cardiac, musculoskeletal, and the "3 A's" (anemia, anxiety, acidosis).
The core work-up toolbox: CXR, blood gas, CBC, ECG — add POCUS at the bedside.
Dyspnea ≠ hypoxemia. Hypoxemia = low PaO₂ (normal 75–100); confirm on an ABG.
Sort hypoxemia by the A-a gradient: normal (hypoventilation, low FiO₂) vs elevated (V/Q mismatch, shunt; low diffusion is chronic). Shunt doesn't correct with 100% O₂.
Get an ABG over a VBG whenever you need the PaO₂ — e.g., to calculate the A-a gradient.

extras.label: Modern Framing — Context currency check
extras.html:
The dyspnea differential, the evaluation toolbox, and the 5-cause / A-a-gradient framework are timeless physiology, verified current as of June 2026. There are no guideline changes to this diagnostic content; the notes below reinforce it.

Lung ultrasound at the bedside. POCUS (BLUE protocol; Lichtenstein) is now a routine adjunct in acute dyspnea — it rapidly distinguishes cardiogenic pulmonary edema (B-lines), consolidation/pneumonia, pneumothorax (absent lung sliding), and pleural effusion with high sensitivity, complementing the CXR/ABG/ECG/CBC toolbox.
Don't forget non-hypoxemic hypoxia. A normal PaO₂ doesn't rule out tissue hypoxia: anemic, stagnant (low-flow), histotoxic (cyanide — sodium nitroprusside), and methemoglobinemia (nitrates, local anesthetics, sulfonamides). Pulse oximetry can be falsely reassuring in CO poisoning and methemoglobinemia — confirm with co-oximetry.

  2. Sarkar M, Niranjan N, Banyal P. Mechanisms of hypoxemia. Lung India. 2017;34(1):47–60.
  3. Petersson J, Glenny RW. Gas exchange and ventilation–perfusion relationships in the lung. Eur Respir J. 2014;44:1023–1041.
  4. Lichtenstein DA, Mezière GA. Relevance of lung ultrasound in the diagnosis of acute respiratory failure: the BLUE protocol. Chest. 2008;134:117–125.
  5. Johannes J, Saggar R. Arterial Hypoxemia. In: Textbook of Critical Care. 7th ed. Elsevier; 2017.

lessons.title: Is It Lupus?
lessons.subtitle: Facilitator-led interactive chalk talk · ~20 min (core) / 35 min (with cases) · IM attendings & senior residents teaching residents
objectives: Describe the two major pathophysiologic mechanisms underlying SLE's clinical and immunologic manifestations.
Identify the most common clinical pathologies of SLE using a systems-based approach.
Distinguish signs/symptoms that should prompt an SLE workup from those that are less concerning.
Obtain the appropriate diagnostic workup for a presentation concerning for SLE.

prework.q: 1. A 30-year-old with fatigue and diffuse body aches but no objective joint swelling wants "a lupus test." Should you send an ANA?
prework.a: Generally no. Fatigue and diffuse pain without objective inflammation are "yellow-box" symptoms; the differential is fibromyalgia, thyroid disease, OA, or another condition. An ANA in this setting is more likely to mislead than help (a low-positive ANA is common in healthy people).

prework.q: 2. Name two autoantibodies that are specific for SLE.
prework.a: Anti-dsDNA (specific; titer tracks disease activity and nephritis) and anti-Smith (specific; associated with lupus nephritis). ANA must be positive to diagnose SLE but is not specific.

prework.q: 3. Why does a high CRP in a known-SLE patient point you toward infection?
prework.a: In lupus activity, ESR is typically elevated out of proportion to CRP (CRP often stays normal). A markedly high CRP suggests infection (or an RA overlap) rather than a pure lupus flare — and infection is a leading cause of mortality in SLE.

segments.label: Hook
segments.html: "How many ANAs did your team order last month? How many were positive, and how many of those patients actually had lupus?" Name the real problem this talk solves: not 'how to treat lupus,' but 'how to decide whether to test for it' — because a poorly-chosen ANA generates anxiety, referrals, and repeat testing. The board moves Autoimmunity → Clinical Features → SLE Workup → Cases.

segments.label: Segment 1 — Why SLE looks like everything (pathophysiology)
segments.html:
ASK — "Why can one disease hit skin, kidney, blood, joints, lung, and brain?" (One trigger — self-antigen exposure — feeding two pathways.)

UV light → apoptosis → spilled nuclear contents picked up by macrophages → two pathways: the antibody pathway (APC → T-helper → B cell → ANA → cellular destruction, immune-complex deposition, positive ANA test) and the inflammatory pathway (antigen on TLRs → IL-1, IL-6, IFN-α → inflammation anywhere). Autoantibodies can brew silently for years (up to ~9.4 yrs in the Arbuckle VA cohort) before clinical disease.

Pearl: the wide manifestation list isn't random — it's the same two pathways playing out in different organs. That's why a systems-based exam, not a reflex ANA, is the right first move.

segments.label: Segment 2 — Concerning vs "yellow-box" features
segments.html:
ASK — Go system by system: "Constitutional — which finding is concerning, which isn't?" (Unexplained documented fever ≥38.3 vs nonspecific fatigue/weight change.)

Every feature counts only if not better explained by another cause (EULAR/ACR 2019). Walk the systems:

SystemConcerning for SLELess concerning ("yellow box")
ConstitutionalDocumented fever ≥38.3°C, unexplainedFatigue, weight change
MucocutaneousMalar/photosensitive rash, alopecia, oral/nasal ulcers, Raynaud's, sicca"Hair loss in the shower"
MusculoskeletalSynovitis — red, hot, objectively swollen joints (often MCP/PIP, wrist); myositis (↑CK)Generalized aches, pain without swelling
RenalProteinuria >0.5 g, unexplained hematuriaPyuria (think infection/STI)
HemeCytopenias (lympho/neutropenia, hemolytic anemia, thrombocytopenia), VTEIron-deficiency anemia (replete iron first)
SerosalPleural/pericardial effusion, acute pericarditisAtypical chest pain
PulmonaryILD or PAH (→ also look for systemic sclerosis overlap)Chronic dyspnea without hypoxia or effusion
GIAutoimmune hepatitis, mesenteric vasculitis (rare)Abdominal pain/diarrhea (IBS), hematochezia (IBD)
GURecurrent (>1) or late (>10 wk) miscarriageSingle or early miscarriage
NeuropsychPsychosis, seizuresAnxiety, depression, headache, brain fog
ASK — "A patient reports 'joint pain.' What one exam finding decides whether you're worried?" (Objective synovitis — red, hot, swollen — vs pain alone.)

segments.label: Segment 3 — The workup, once you've decided to test
segments.html:
If suspicion is high enough for an ANA, send the full panel, not the ANA alone: CBC w/ diff (cytopenias), CMP (Cr, transaminases, bilirubin), C3/C4 (expect low), ESR/CRP (ESR ≫ CRP in lupus), ANA + anti-dsDNA/Smith/RNP/SSA/SSB, antiphospholipid antibodies, and UA (hematuria + proteinuria). Refer to rheumatology if abnormalities accompany unexplained SLE-specific features.

AntibodyMeaning
ANAMust be positive to diagnose SLE; not specific
Anti-dsDNASpecific; titer tracks activity and nephritis
Anti-SmithSpecific; associated with lupus nephritis
Anti-RNPMCTD + Raynaud's
Anti-SSA/SSBSjögren's, neonatal lupus
Antiphospholipid (anticardiolipin, β2-GP1, lupus anticoagulant)Common in SLE (30–40%); a clot is not required to diagnose APS
ANA stewardship: a positive ANA is found in ~32% of healthy people at 1:40, ~13% at 1:80, ~5% at 1:160. Order it for pre-test probability, not reassurance.

segments.label: Retrieval close
segments.html: Run the board's seven yes/no vignettes as a vote, then have learners state from memory: the two pathways, three "concerning" features, three "yellow-box" features, and the full lab panel.

cases.title: "Get an ANA?" rapid-fire (7 vignettes)
cases.reveal: NO — most likely viral pericarditis; symptoms are explained by another cause.

cases.title: Longer cases
cases.reveal: Most concerning: photosensitive symptom flares and objective joint swelling/warmth (not "aches"). Workup returns ANA 1:320 fine speckled, anti-dsDNA 1:40, low C4, lymphopenia, bland UA, negative antiphospholipid → refer to rheumatology for likely SLE. Counsel on SPF 30+, NSAIDs for joints, pregnancy prevention until a plan is set; urgent referral if end-organ damage, severe cytopenia, extensive rash, bullae/infection, or pregnancy.

mcqs.stem: A 29-year-old woman has 3 months of fatigue, "joint pain," and a positive ANA at 1:80 sent by her PCP. Exam shows no synovitis, normal skin, normal labs otherwise. Which is the most appropriate next step?
opts: Reassure; a low-titer ANA is common and there is no objective evidence of SLE
Order anti-dsDNA, anti-Smith, C3/C4, and refer to rheumatology
mcqs.exp: B. A 1:80 ANA occurs in ~13% of healthy people; with no objective SLE features, this does not warrant treatment, cascade testing, or referral. A and E treat a disease that isn't established; C escalates a workup the pre-test probability doesn't support; D — serial ANA titers are not used to track or diagnose.

mcqs.stem: Which constellation most warrants ordering an ANA and a full lupus panel?
opts: Fatigue and a positive "hair loss in the shower" history
Chronic widespread pain without joint swelling and a normal exam
Objective wrist synovitis, an unexplained documented fever, and new lymphopenia
Atypical chest pain with a normal cardiac workup
mcqs.exp: C. Objective synovitis + unexplained fever + cytopenia are SLE-specific, unexplained features that raise pre-test probability. A, B, D, and E are all "yellow-box" presentations (fibromyalgia, normal hair shedding, fibromyalgia-type chest pain, nonspecific dyspnea) that shouldn't by themselves trigger an ANA.

mcqs.stem: A 24-year-old woman with established SLE on mycophenolate and prednisone presents with fever, cough, a lobar infiltrate, CRP 90, and ESR 110. What is the best initial approach?
opts: Increase prednisone for a presumed flare
Treat empirically for infection while evaluating; in SLE with high CRP, think infection first
Order an ANA titer to assess activity
Send anti-dsDNA and wait for results before acting
mcqs.exp: B. In lupus, ESR is usually elevated out of proportion to CRP; a markedly high CRP and a lobar infiltrate point to infection, the leading cause of SLE mortality. A could worsen an untreated infection; C — ANA titers don't track activity; D risks adrenal crisis and isn't indicated reflexively; E dangerously delays therapy.

mcqs.stem: A patient with new SLE features asks which autoantibody is both specific for SLE and tends to rise with disease activity and nephritis. Which is it?
mcqs.exp: C. Anti-dsDNA is specific for SLE and its titer fluctuates with activity (and nephritis). Anti-RNP suggests MCTD; anti-SSA relates to Sjögren's/neonatal lupus; RF is nonspecific; ANA is sensitive but not specific (positive in many healthy people).

mcqs.stem: A 33-year-old woman is being worked up for possible SLE. Beyond the ANA, which panel best screens for the major organ manifestations?
opts: ANA titer alone, repeated in 3 months
CBC w/ diff, CMP, C3/C4, ESR/CRP, dsDNA/Smith/RNP/SSA-SSB, antiphospholipid antibodies, and UA
mcqs.exp: B. Once you've decided to test, send the broad panel that screens cytopenias, renal/hepatic involvement, complement consumption, the SLE-specific antibodies, antiphospholipid antibodies, and urinary findings. A and C are insufficient and ANA titers aren't tracked serially; D misses organ screening; E isn't a screening test.

anki: What are the two major pathophysiologic pathways driving SLE manifestations?

(1) Antibody pathway — APC presents self-antigen → T-helper activates B cells → antinuclear antibodies (ANA) cause direct cellular destruction, immune-complex/tissue deposition, and a positive ANA test. (2) Inflammatory pathway — antigen binds TLRs on macrophages → cytokines (IL-1, IL-6, IFN-alpha) → inflammation.
Why are SLE patients especially susceptible to autoantibody formation after UV light?
UV light increases apoptosis; nuclear contents spilled by dying cells are taken up by antigen-presenting cells (macrophages) that then mount immune responses against self-antigens.
How long can autoantibodies precede clinical SLE?
Years — in a VA cohort, 115/130 SLE patients had autoantibodies up to ~9.4 years before diagnosis, accumulating in a predictable order (benign → pathogenic autoimmunity → clinical illness).
Which classification criteria underlie the SLE clinical-manifestation framework?
The EULAR/ACR 2019 Classification Criteria for SLE; each manifestation counts only if not better explained by another, more probable cause.
Describe the SLE malar rash.
Sun-sensitive facial rash that SPARES the nasolabial folds; usually rough and raised (vs rosacea, which is smooth and flat). Photosensitive rashes can occur elsewhere and notably spare the knuckles.
Which mucocutaneous findings are concerning for SLE?
Malar/photosensitive rashes, alopecia (scarring or non-scarring), painless or painful oral/nasal ulcers (active disease), Raynaud's, and sicca.
Which hematologic findings prompt an SLE workup?
Cytopenias (anemia — watch for hemolysis; leukopenia WBC <4, especially lympho/neutropenia, always get a diff; thrombocytopenia, though Plt <20 is more likely ITP) and arterial/venous VTE.
What proportion of SLE patients have antiphospholipid antibodies?
30–40%; they raise the risk of vascular events and death. Revisit "provoked" VTEs; a clot is not required to diagnose antiphospholipid syndrome.
What renal findings suggest lupus nephritis, and how common is it?
Proteinuria (>0.5 g) and/or hematuria; creatinine is often normal but a rising Cr demands urgent workup. Up to 50% of SLE patients develop lupus nephritis.
Most common musculoskeletal manifestation of SLE?
Synovitis — red, hot, objectively swollen joints (often MCP/PIP, wrists, knees); joints are the first sign in ~50%. Myositis presents as quadriceps weakness (dx with CK + EMG).
Which serosal manifestations are common in SLE?
Pleural effusion (40–60%, often asymptomatic), pericardial effusion (>40%, can cause tamponade), and acute pericarditis (>50% of echos). Usually benign and atypical as an initial presentation.
Which "yellow-box" symptoms should NOT, by themselves, trigger an ANA?

Fatigue/weight change, hair loss in the shower, abdominal pain/chronic diarrhea/hematochezia (IBS/IBD), single/early miscarriage, iron-deficiency anemia, anxiety/depression/brain fog/headache, atypical chest pain, isolated dyspnea, pyuria, and generalized aches or pain without joint swelling.
For diffuse pain without objective inflammation, what is the differential instead of SLE?
Fibromyalgia, thyroid disease, osteoarthritis, or another autoimmune disease (e.g., spondyloarthritis) — absence of a red/hot/swollen joint or elevated inflammatory markers argues against lupus.
What is the recommended laboratory workup once SLE suspicion is high enough to send an ANA?

CBC w/ diff, CMP, C3/C4 (expect low), ESR (often elevated, CRP often normal — high CRP suggests RA overlap or infection), ANA, anti-dsDNA, anti-Smith, anti-RNP, anti-SSA/SSB, antiphospholipid antibodies, and UA.
Specificity/associations of the SLE autoantibodies?

Anti-dsDNA — specific, titer tracks activity (and nephritis); anti-Smith — specific, associated with lupus nephritis; anti-RNP — MCTD + Raynaud's; anti-SSA/SSB — Sjögren's and neonatal lupus. ANA must be positive to diagnose SLE.
Prevalence of a positive ANA in people WITHOUT SLE?
~32% at 1:40, ~13% at 1:80, ~5% at 1:160 — a low-positive ANA is common and non-specific.
In a patient with known SLE and a high CRP, what should you think of first?

Infection — infections are a leading cause of mortality in SLE; both the disease and immunosuppressants raise risk, and ESR is usually elevated out of proportion to CRP in lupus activity (a high CRP points away from pure flare).
Beyond this diagnostic talk, what is the 2024 ACR treatment shift for active class III/IV lupus nephritis?

Early triple/combination therapy: hydroxychloroquine for all, plus pulse + oral glucocorticoids and one of — MPAA + belimumab, MPAA + a calcineurin inhibitor (voclosporin), or low-dose Euro-Lupus cyclophosphamide → MPAA + belimumab; lower-dose steroid taper to ≤5 mg/day by 6 months.
lessons.dot:
#?SLE: * presenting w/ * — assess pre-test prob by SYSTEM before sending ANA (each feature counts only if unexplained, EULAR/ACR 2019)
CONCERNING (warrants testing): constitutional [fever >=38.3 unexplained] / mucocutaneous [malar* (spares nasolabial), photosensitive rash, alopecia, oral-nasal ulcers, Raynaud, sicca] / MSK [objective synovitis red-hot-swollen MCP-PIP-wrist; myositis ↑CK] / renal [proteinuria >0.5g, hematuria] / heme [cytopenias - lympho-neutropenia, hemolytic anemia, thrombocytopenia; VTE] / serosal [pleural-pericardial effusion, pericarditis] / pulm [ILD, PAH → also r/o scleroderma] / GI [AIH, mesenteric vasculitis] / GU [>1 or >10wk miscarriage] / neuropsych [psychosis, seizures]
YELLOW-BOX (do NOT reflex ANA): fatigue/wt change, shower hair loss, IBS/IBD sx, single/early miscarriage, Fe-def anemia [replete first], anxiety/depression/brain fog/HA, atypical chest pain, isolated dyspnea, pyuria, generalized aches/pain w/o swelling → consider fibromyalgia/thyroid/OA/spondyloarthritis
IF testing indicated, send PANEL (not ANA alone):
  CBC w/ diff [cytopenias] · CMP [↑Cr=LN, transaminitis=AIH, ↑bili=hemolysis] · C3/C4 [low expected] · ESR/CRP [ESR >> CRP; high CRP → infection or RA overlap]
  ANA [must be + to dx; +in healthy ~32%@1:40, 13%@1:80, 5%@1:160] · anti-dsDNA [specific, tracks activity/nephritis] · anti-Smith [specific, nephritis] · anti-RNP [MCTD/Raynaud] · anti-SSA/SSB [Sjogren/neonatal]
  antiphospholipid [anticardiolipin/β2-GP1/LA — clot not required] · UA [hematuria + proteinuria = LN]
DISPO: refer rheumatology if abnl labs + unexplained SLE-specific features; urgent if end-organ damage/severe cytopenia/extensive rash/bullae/pregnancy
PEARL: known SLE + high CRP → infection FIRST (leading cause of mortality); ESR≫CRP in pure flare
takehome: SLE's many complications trace to a few immune pathways — autoantibody production and TLR-driven inflammatory cytokines.
Before ordering an ANA, use a systems-based exam to look for SLE-specific manifestations and gauge pre-test probability.
If suspicion is high enough to order an ANA, add the broader metabolic and immunologic workup to screen the major manifestations.
A low-positive ANA is common in healthy people, and fibromyalgia frequently coexists with (and mimics) lupus — test for probability, not reassurance.

extras.label: Currency Notes checked June 2026
extras.html:
This is a diagnosis / ANA-stewardship talk; its content was verified current and not changed. Scope and currency:

  • EULAR/ACR 2019 Classification Criteria for SLE (Aringer, Arthritis Rheumatol 2019) — the basis for the deck's clinical-manifestation framework; still the current classification standard as of June 2026.
  • ANA prevalence figures (Tan, Arthritis Rheum 1997: ~32%/13%/5% at 1:40/1:80/1:160) remain the canonical reference and are accurately quoted.
  • Out of scope — treatment update: the 2024 ACR Lupus Nephritis guideline (Sammaritano, Arthritis Care Res 2025) marks a conceptual shift to early COMBINATION therapy — hydroxychloroquine for all, plus for active class III/IV(±V) LN, triple therapy = pulse + oral glucocorticoids and one of (MPAA + belimumab), (MPAA + a calcineurin inhibitor, e.g. voclosporin), or low-dose Euro-Lupus cyclophosphamide → MPAA + belimumab; lower-dose steroid taper to ≤5 mg/day by 6 months, add a RAAS inhibitor for proteinuria; KDIGO 2024 aligns. This is surfaced as context only — the diagnostic teaching is unchanged.

  6. Shin JI, et al. Systemic Lupus Erythematosus and Lung Involvement: A Comprehensive Review. J Clin Med. 2022;11(22):6714.
  7. Tan EM, et al. Range of antinuclear antibodies in "healthy" individuals. Arthritis Rheum. 1997;40(9):1601–1611.
  8. Wolfe F, et al. Fibromyalgia, SLE, and evaluation of SLE activity. J Rheumatol. 2009;36(1):82–88.
  9. Sammaritano LR, et al. 2024 American College of Rheumatology Guideline for the Screening, Treatment, and Management of Lupus Nephritis. Arthritis Care Res. 2025. (added — treatment context, out of scope)
