lessons.title: Ischemic CVA
lessons.subtitle: Optimized lesson package · Facilitator-led interactive chalk talk for IM residents · 35–45 min total
lessons.credit: Adapted from TeachIM.org — Ischemic CVA (Brandon Fainstad MD; expert review pending; 2020), CC BY-NC 4.0. Optimized June 2026: currency verified against 2021 secondary-prevention & 2024 acute-stroke AHA/ASA guidelines, three deck errors corrected, pedagogy restructured, assessments added. Companion deck: Ischemic_CVA_Optimized.pptx (original 5-step interactive board, surgically corrected).
objectives: Apply a 5-step approach to evaluating and managing acute ischemic CVA: exclude hemorrhage → acute reperfusion → determine etiology → secondary prophylaxis → risk modification.
Decide which strokes are eligible for IV thrombolysis, endovascular therapy (EVT), or neither — based on timing, clinical assessment, and initial imaging.
Determine CVA etiology (atherosclerotic vs cardioembolic vs other) using targeted diagnostics.
Match secondary prophylaxis (antiplatelet vs anticoagulation, single vs dual) to the etiology and timing.

prework.q: Q1. What is the single first branch point in every acute stroke algorithm, and what test answers it?
prework.a: Hemorrhagic vs ischemic — answered by an emergent noncontrast head CT. Everything downstream (thrombolysis, antithrombotics) depends on first excluding intracranial hemorrhage.

prework.q: Q2. A patient is 3 hours from last-known-well with a disabling deficit and no hemorrhage on CT. What two reperfusion questions are you now asking?
prework.a: (1) Is the patient eligible for IV thrombolysis (within 4.5 h, no contraindications)? (2) Is there a large-vessel occlusion amenable to thrombectomy? The two are complementary, not either/or — give IV lytic if eligible and pursue EVT for LVO.

prework.q: Q3. Why does stroke etiology change your antithrombotic choice?
prework.a: Atherosclerotic strokes are prevented mainly with antiplatelet therapy (± short-course DAPT); cardioembolic strokes (e.g., AF, LV thrombus) need anticoagulation. Picking the wrong class either under-treats the embolic source or needlessly raises bleeding risk.

segments.label: Hook (2 min)
segments.html: "68-year-old, last seen normal 2 hours ago, now with dense left hemiparesis and gaze deviation. The clock is running — what happens in the next 30 minutes decides whether she walks out of the hospital." Set the door-to-needle/door-to-groin frame, then build the 5 steps.

segments.label: Step 1 — Exclude hemorrhage
segments.html: ASK: "First test, before anything else?" Reveal: noncontrast head CT. ~85% of strokes are ischemic, ~15% hemorrhagic; you cannot give an antithrombotic or lytic until hemorrhage is off the table.

segments.label: Step 2 — Acute reperfusion without causing ICH
segments.html:
The acute-management sub-board runs two parallel tracks off "acute focal deficit, no hemorrhage on CT":

TrackTriggerAction
IV thrombolysisLKW < 4.5 h, no contraindication, BP < 185/110tPA — or tenecteplase 0.25 mg/kg (2024 Class 1 alternative). Door-to-needle < 30 min.
Endovascular therapy (EVT)CTA shows LVO; salvageable brain (ASPECTS ≥6, or CT perfusion mismatch); LKW < 6 h, selected to 24 hMechanical thrombectomy ± stent. Door-to-groin < 90 min.
ASK: "Name three absolute contraindications to IV thrombolysis." Reveal the board's checklist: prior ICH, surgery < 3 mo, active bleeding, coagulopathy, BP > 185/110 (and more on the linked full list).
Pearl — 2023–2024 expansion: Thrombectomy now benefits large-core infarcts too — anterior LVO, 6–24 h, NIHSS ≥6, ASPECTS 3–5, age < 80 (SELECT2, ANGEL-ASPECT, NEJM 2023; 2024 AHA/ASA Class 1). Don't write off a big established infarct as "too late" without imaging-based selection. Basilar-occlusion EVT is also now supported (ATTENTION/BAOCHE 2022).
Pearl — acute BP: Permissive hypertension < 220/120 ×48 h if not reperfused; < 185/110 if thrombolysed/EVT. Avoid tubes and lines; DVT prophylaxis with SCDs (pharmacologic held < 24 h post-tPA); dysphagia screen before anything PO; PT/OT/SLP; intensive statin before discharge if atherosclerotic.

segments.label: Step 3 — Determine etiology
segments.html:
Etiology guides secondary prevention. The sub-board sorts into atherosclerotic (large- vs small-artery), cardioembolic (atrial/LV thrombus, valvular, VTE-with-shunt), or other (dissection, vasculitis, thrombophilia). Standard diagnostics on everyone: brain MRI, H&N CTA/MRA, carotid/vertebral/transcranial Doppler, telemetry, TTE with bubble study.

Pattern on imagingSuggested etiology
Large and/or multiple-vessel territoryCardioembolic
Lacunar (small, deep)HTN or amyloid vasculopathy
Small/single vessel + vascular risk factorsAtherosclerosis
ASK: "When does a patient need a TEE instead of a TTE?" Reveal: only with moderate-high suspicion of a cardioembolic source, no identified intracardiac source on TTE, or a confirmed right-to-left shunt — TEE better visualizes the LA appendage, mitral valve, and ascending aorta.
Pearl: Thrombophilia testing is low-yield in the acute phase — defer to hematology/neurology after the acute thrombotic state resolves and only with moderate-high pretest probability.

segments.label: Steps 4 & 5 — Secondary prophylaxis + risk modification
segments.html:
The prophylaxis sub-board contrasts embolic (antiplatelet bridge 1–2 weeks → anticoagulation after the hemorrhagic-conversion window) vs atherosclerotic (antiplatelet, with short-course DAPT for high-risk patients then step-up monotherapy).

ASK: "Minor stroke, NIHSS 2 — single or dual antiplatelet, and for how long?" Reveal: short-course DAPT (clopidogrel + aspirin) then narrow to one.
Pearl — 2021 AHA/ASA secondary prevention: DAPT (clopidogrel 300–600 mg load → 75 mg daily + aspirin) for minor stroke NIHSS ≤3 or high-risk TIA ABCD2 ≥4, started < 24 h and continued 21 days (then single agent) — the CHANCE/POINT window that maximizes benefit while limiting bleeding. Ticagrelor + aspirin ×30 d is an alternative (THALES). Long-term: high-intensity statin to LDL < 70, BP < 130/80, A1c < 7, tobacco cessation; acute inpatient glucose < 180 mg/dL. PFO closure is reasonable for cryptogenic embolic stroke in selected patients < 60 (RESPECT/CLOSE/REDUCE).

segments.label: Retrieval close (3 min)
segments.html: Close the laptop. Have learners reconstruct the 5 steps in order and, for a given stroke, state the reperfusion decision and the etiology-matched antithrombotic. Then have them recite the take-home points before you reveal them.

cases.title: Case 1 — Hyperacute LVO
cases.stem: 72-year-old with AF (not anticoagulated), last-known-well 90 minutes ago, NIHSS 16 with right gaze preference and left hemiplegia. CT: no hemorrhage. CTA: right M1 occlusion. BP 178/96.
cases.reveal: Within 4.5 h and no contraindication → IV thrombolysis (alteplase 0.9 mg/kg or tenecteplase 0.25 mg/kg). LVO with salvageable brain → activate thrombectomy. Give the lytic and go to EVT — they are complementary. Keep BP < 185/110 around lysis.

cases.title: Case 2 — Minor stroke / high-risk TIA
cases.stem: 59-year-old with HTN, DM, smoking; 40 minutes of right-hand weakness and dysarthria, now resolved. NIHSS 0, ABCD2 = 5. CT without hemorrhage; MRI shows a small left-corona-radiata infarct.
cases.reveal: No — deficit resolved/minor and non-disabling; no lytic/EVT. Focus shifts to rapid secondary prevention.

cases.title: Case 3 — Young stroke, cryptogenic
cases.stem: 41-year-old, no vascular risk factors, wakes with aphasia and right facial droop. MRI: left MCA cortical infarct. CTA/carotids normal; telemetry without AF; TTE-bubble shows a right-to-left shunt.
cases.reveal: Pursue cardioembolic/shunt work-up: TEE if moderate-high suspicion with no identified source; prolonged rhythm monitoring for occult AF; consider hypercoagulable evaluation (deferred, hematology-guided). LE duplex to assess for paradoxical-embolism source.

mcqs.stem: A 64-year-old presents 2 hours after sudden right-sided weakness; NIHSS 12. Noncontrast CT shows no hemorrhage and an ASPECTS of 9. BP 170/92. CTA shows a left M1 occlusion. What is the best immediate management?
opts: IV thrombolysis (alteplase or tenecteplase) AND activate thrombectomy
Lower BP to < 140/90 before any therapy
mcqs.exp: B. Within 4.5 h, no hemorrhage, no contraindication → give IV lytic (alteplase 0.9 mg/kg or TNK 0.25 mg/kg, 2024 Class 1) and, for the M1 LVO with good ASPECTS, pursue thrombectomy — they are complementary. A under-treats a disabling LVO. C needlessly withholds an eligible lytic. D — target is < 185/110 for lysis, not < 140/90 (over-lowering risks hypoperfusion). E acute full-dose anticoagulation is not indicated and raises ICH risk.

mcqs.stem: An 71-year-old presents 10 hours after last-known-well with an anterior LVO, NIHSS 14, and ASPECTS 4 (large core). Which statement is correct?
opts: The infarct is too large; thrombectomy is contraindicated
Thrombectomy is reasonable — large-core EVT now has Class 1 support in this window
Only IV thrombolysis should be offered
Defer all therapy and treat with aspirin
mcqs.exp: B. SELECT2 and ANGEL-ASPECT (NEJM 2023) extended thrombectomy benefit to large-core infarcts (ASPECTS 3–5) for anterior LVO, 6–24 h, NIHSS ≥6, age < 80 — now a 2024 AHA/ASA Class 1 recommendation. A/E reflect the older ASPECTS ≥6 threshold. C/D withhold a beneficial therapy. He is past the 4.5-h lytic window, so EVT is the reperfusion option.

mcqs.stem: A 58-year-old has a minor ischemic stroke (NIHSS 2) from small-vessel atherosclerosis, presenting at 6 hours. What antithrombotic regimen best reduces 90-day recurrence?
opts: Clopidogrel + aspirin (DAPT) for 21 days, then single-agent
mcqs.exp: B. For minor noncardioembolic stroke (NIHSS ≤3) or high-risk TIA (ABCD2 ≥4), short-course DAPT started < 24 h and continued ~21 days then narrowed to monotherapy reduces early recurrence (CHANCE/POINT; 2021 AHA/ASA). A misses the early high-risk DAPT window. C/D anticoagulants are for cardioembolic sources, not atherosclerotic. E prolonged DAPT increases bleeding without added benefit.

mcqs.stem: A patient with a large cardioembolic MCA infarct from newly diagnosed AF is ready for secondary prevention. What is the most appropriate timing of anticoagulation?
opts: Start full-dose anticoagulation within the first 24 hours
Bridge with an antiplatelet, then start anticoagulation after the hemorrhagic-conversion window (often ~1–2 weeks for large infarcts)
Anticoagulation is not indicated for AF-related stroke
Start anticoagulation only if a repeat CT at 6 hours is clear
mcqs.exp: B. AF stroke needs anticoagulation for prevention, but starting too early after a large infarct risks hemorrhagic conversion — bridge with an antiplatelet and delay full anticoagulation (timing scaled to infarct size, often ~1–2 weeks for large strokes). A is too early for a large infarct. C/D under-treat the embolic source. E 6 hours is too short to exclude conversion.

mcqs.stem: Which acute blood-pressure approach is correct for an ischemic stroke patient who did NOT receive thrombolysis or thrombectomy?
opts: Permissive hypertension, treating only if > 220/120, for ~48 h
Lower to < 120/80 to protect the brain
Start a nitroprusside drip for any BP > 160/90
mcqs.exp: B. In the non-reperfused acute ischemic stroke, permissive hypertension preserves penumbral perfusion — treat only if > 220/120 (or for another indication) for about 48 h. E < 185/110 applies specifically to patients who received thrombolysis/EVT. A/C over-lowering worsens ischemia. D is overly aggressive and not threshold-based.

anki: The 5 steps of the ischemic-CVA framework?
(1) Exclude hemorrhage, (2) acute reperfusion without causing ICH, (3) determine etiology, (4) secondary prophylaxis, (5) risk-factor modification.
First test in any acute stroke?
Noncontrast head CT to exclude hemorrhage (~85% ischemic, ~15% hemorrhagic).
IV thrombolysis time window and agents?
Within 4.5 h of last-known-well; alteplase 0.9 mg/kg or tenecteplase 0.25 mg/kg (TNK now a 2024 Class 1 alternative).
Door-to-needle and door-to-groin targets?
Door-to-CT <25 min, door-to-needle <30 min, door-to-CTA <40 min, door-to-groin <90 min.
Name 5 contraindications to IV thrombolysis.
Prior ICH, surgery <3 mo, active bleeding, coagulopathy, BP >185/110 (plus full eligibility checklist).
Classic thrombectomy selection?
LVO with salvageable brain: ASPECTS >=6 or CT-perfusion mismatch; LKW <6 h, selected to 24 h (DAWN/DEFUSE-3).
2023-24 expansion of thrombectomy?
Large-core infarcts: anterior LVO, 6-24 h, NIHSS >=6, ASPECTS 3-5, age <80 (SELECT2/ANGEL-ASPECT; 2024 Class 1). Basilar occlusion supported (ATTENTION/BAOCHE).
Acute BP target if NOT reperfused?
Permissive HTN: treat only if >220/120, for ~48 h.
Acute BP target if thrombolysed/EVT?
Imaging pattern -> etiology: large/multi-vessel?
Cardioembolic.
Imaging pattern -> etiology: lacunar?
HTN or amyloid vasculopathy.
Imaging pattern -> etiology: small/single vessel + risk factors?
Atherosclerosis.
When is TEE indicated over TTE?
Mod-high cardioembolic suspicion with no source on TTE, or a confirmed R-to-L shunt; TEE better sees LA appendage, MV, ascending aorta.
Antithrombotic by etiology?
Atherosclerotic -> antiplatelet (+/- short DAPT); cardioembolic -> anticoagulation after the hemorrhagic-conversion window.
DAPT indication and duration (2021 AHA/ASA)?
Minor stroke NIHSS <=3 or high-risk TIA ABCD2 >=4: clopidogrel + aspirin started <24 h, continued 21 days, then single-agent. Ticagrelor+aspirin x30 d an alternative (THALES).
Long-term secondary-prevention targets?
High-intensity statin to LDL <70, BP <130/80, A1c <7, tobacco cessation; acute inpatient glucose <180 mg/dL.
PFO closure - who benefits?
Cryptogenic embolic stroke, selected patients <60 with otherwise negative work-up (RESPECT/CLOSE/REDUCE).
Why defer thrombophilia testing acutely?
Low yield during the acute thrombotic state; defer to heme/neuro after it resolves and only with mod-high pretest probability.
lessons.dot:
#Acute ischemic CVA: focal deficit, NIHSS *, LKW * [time], territory * [ant/post-circ]. 5-step: r/o bleed -> reperfuse -> etiology -> 2ndary ppx -> risk mod.
- STEP 1 r/o hemorrhage: noncontrast HCT [no ICH] -> ischemic
- STEP 2 reperfuse w/o ICH:
  - IV lysis if LKW <4.5h & no CI [no prior ICH, no surg <3mo, no active bleed, no coagulopathy, plt/INR ok, BP <185/110]: *tPA 0.9mg/kg or TNK 0.25mg/kg. door-to-needle <30min
  - EVT if LVO on CTA + salvageable brain [ASPECTS ≥6 or CTP mismatch]; LKW <6h, sel to 24h [DAWN/DEFUSE-3]; large-core ASPECTS 3-5 NIHSS≥6 age<80 6-24h [SELECT2/ANGEL-ASPECT]. door-to-groin <90min
  - BP: permissive <220/120 x48h; <185/110 if lysed/EVT
  - supportive: NPO til dysphagia screen, DVT ppx [SCD; pharm held <24h post-tPA], avoid lines/tubes, PT/OT/SLP, hi-intensity statin if athero
- STEP 3 etiology: MRI brain + H&N CTA/MRA + carotid/vert/TCD + tele + TTE-bubble. pattern * [large/multi=cardioembolic / lacunar=HTN-amyloid / small+RF=athero]. TEE/prolonged-rhythm/heme-thrombophilia only if indicated
- STEP 4 2ndary ppx by etiology:
  - ATHERO: antiplt; if NIHSS ≤3 or ABCD2 ≥4 -> DAPT [clopidogrel load + ASA] x21d then mono [2021 AHA/ASA]; ticagrelor+ASA x30d alt
  - CARDIOEMBOLIC: anticoag [DOAC/warfarin] AFTER hemorrhagic-conversion window (antiplt bridge ~1-2wk for large infarct)
  - cryptogenic + PFO & age<60 -> consider closure [RESPECT/CLOSE/REDUCE]
- STEP 5 risk mod: LDL <70 (hi-intensity statin ± ezetimibe/PCSK9), BP <130/80, A1c <7, glu <180 acute, tobacco cessation
- AVOID: early full anticoag in large infarct; over-lowering BP if not reperfused; routine acute thrombophilia panel
takehome: Run every ischemic stroke through the same 5 steps: exclude hemorrhage → reperfuse → etiology → secondary prophylaxis → risk modification.

Reperfusion is time-critical and complementary: IV lytic (alteplase or tenecteplase) if LKW < 4.5 h, plus thrombectomy for LVO with salvageable brain — now including large-core infarcts in extended windows.
Etiology picks the antithrombotic: atherosclerotic → antiplatelet (DAPT 21 d for minor stroke/high-risk TIA, then mono); cardioembolic → anticoagulation after the hemorrhagic-conversion window.
Acute BP is permissive (< 220/120 ×48 h; < 185/110 if reperfused); long-term targets are LDL < 70, BP < 130/80, A1c < 7.
Don't dismiss a big or "late" stroke without imaging-based selection — and don't start full anticoagulation too early in a large infarct.
lessons.refs:
The original TeachIM page listed References as "coming soon." Sources supporting this optimized package:

  1. Powers WJ, Rabinstein AA, et al. 2019 Guidelines for the Early Management of Patients With Acute Ischemic Stroke: A Guideline for Healthcare Professionals From the AHA/ASA. Stroke. 2019;50(12):e344–e418.
  2. 2024 Guideline for the Primary & Acute Management of Patients With Acute Ischemic Stroke (AHA/ASA comprehensive acute ischemic stroke guideline) — tenecteplase 0.25 mg/kg Class 1; large-core thrombectomy Class 1. Stroke. 2024.
  3. Kleindorfer DO, Towfighi A, et al. 2021 Guideline for the Prevention of Stroke in Patients With Stroke and Transient Ischemic Attack: AHA/ASA. Stroke. 2021;52(7):e364–e467.
  4. Yoshimura S, et al. (SELECT2) and Huo X, et al. (ANGEL-ASPECT). Endovascular therapy for large-core infarcts. N Engl J Med. 2023.
  5. Nogueira RG, et al. (DAWN) and Albers GW, et al. (DEFUSE-3). Thrombectomy 6–24 h with imaging mismatch. N Engl J Med. 2018.
  6. Wang Y, et al. (CHANCE) and Johnston SC, et al. (POINT). Short-course clopidogrel + aspirin after minor stroke/TIA. N Engl J Med. 2013/2018.
  7. Johnston SC, et al. (THALES). Ticagrelor + aspirin in acute stroke/TIA. N Engl J Med. 2020.

lessons.title: Knee Arthrocentesis & Injection
lessons.subtitle: Optimized procedure package · Facilitator-led simulation-based training for IM residents · Knee exam + procedure ~45–70 min
lessons.credit: Adapted from TeachIM.org — Knee Arthrocentesis (Brandon Fainstad MD, Molly Brett MD, Lisa Thompson MD, Meara Melton MD; published Feb 2021, reviewed Jan 2023, materials rev. Mar 2025), CC BY-NC 4.0. Optimized June 2026: guideline currency verified, teaching design upgraded, assessments and clinical tools added. Procedure-type lesson — TeachIM publishes no slide deck for procedures; the package is built around the original instructional guide, assessment checklist, clinical assessment tool, practice cases, and quiz (all included below).
objectives: Determine the appropriate indications and contraindications for an intra-articular knee corticosteroid injection (and for diagnostic aspiration).
Perform all critical steps of a knee injection (except needle insertion) using high-fidelity simulation under direct preceptor observation, to a Minimum Passing Score.
Perform all critical steps of a steroid injection of the knee in the clinical environment under direct preceptor observation, to the Unsupervised Practice Standard.

prework.q: Q1. Which injection approach is most accurate, and how should the patient be positioned for it?
prework.a: The superolateral approach — ~90% accuracy for intra-articular placement vs ~70% for anteromedial/anterolateral (Hermans, Semin Arthritis Rheum 2011), and the most reliable site for aspiration. Position the patient supine with the leg fully extended (or slightly hyperextended) to relax the quadriceps/patellar tendons and open the space superolateral to the patella.

prework.q: Q2. Which knee-exam finding most strongly suggests osteoarthritis?
prework.a: Palpable bony enlargement (+LR ≈ 12). Crepitus on passive motion (+LR ≈ 2) and varus deformity (+LR ≈ 3.4) are also associated with OA but are far less predictive (McGee, Evidence-Based Physical Diagnosis).

prework.q: Q3. What's the single absolute contraindication to an intra-articular knee steroid — and what's the most important thing to tell a diabetic patient?
prework.a: Hardware/prosthetic joint in the knee is the only absolute contraindication on the list. Anticoagulation, well-controlled diabetes, and surgery >3 months away are not contraindications. Counsel diabetic patients that blood glucose may rise on average >100 mg/dL for ~24–48 h (up to several days), greatest in those with poorer baseline control.

segments.label: Hook & framing (2 min)
segments.html: "A 65-year-old with chronic knee pain asks for 'the shot that fixed my neighbor.' Before you reach for the triamcinolone — what's the one intervention that beats the injection for long-term pain and function, and what one exam finding would most change your diagnosis?" Hold the answers. Establish that the two highest-yield skills here are making the diagnosis on exam and setting expectations (steroids = modest, short-term relief; PT/exercise = durable benefit).

segments.label: LEARN — Knee exam by likelihood ratio
segments.html:
ASK: "Sort these by how much they move you toward OA: crepitus, bony enlargement, varus deformity."
Finding / maneuverPoints toward+LR
Palpable bony enlargementKnee OA≈ 12
Varus (genu varum) deformityKnee OA≈ 3.4
Morning stiffness <30 minOA (vs inflammatory)≈ 3
Crepitus on passive ROMKnee OA≈ 2
Positive McMurray'sMeniscal tear≈ 4
Inability to fully extend (locking)Meniscal tear w/ mechanical sx≈ 3.2
TTP anteromedial tibia (below joint line)Pes anserine bursitis—
Pearl — localize the tenderness: Pes anserine bursitis is point tenderness at the anteromedial tibia ~2–5 cm below the joint line (common insertion of sartorius/gracilis/semitendinosus) — anterior and inferior to the medial joint line, which distinguishes it from a medial meniscus tear.

segments.label: LEARN — Indications, contraindications & expectations
segments.html:
ASK: "Which of these blocks the injection: anticoagulation, T2DM on insulin, a knee prosthesis, or surgery in 6 months?"
IndicationsAbsolute contraindicationNot contraindications (counsel/caution)
Symptomatic knee OA; aseptic inflammatory arthritis flare; (diagnostic aspiration to evaluate an effusion / r/o septic joint or crystals)Hardware / prosthetic joint in the knee; overlying skin infection; suspected septic joint (aspirate & treat — do not inject steroid)Anticoagulation; well-controlled diabetes; surgery >3 months out. (Defer steroid if surgery is imminent, e.g. <3 months.)
Pearl — efficacy & expectation setting (ACR 2019 + Cochrane 2015): Of injectables, corticosteroids have the best evidence — but only a small-to-moderate pain benefit lasting ~6 weeks. Physical therapy/exercise and weight loss are more effective long-term and are core therapy; topical NSAIDs (diclofenac) are preferred first-line pharmacotherapy. ACR conditionally recommends against intra-articular hyaluronic acid, PRP, and stem cells (limited/uncertain efficacy, high cost).
Safety — repeated steroids & joint structure: Repeated intra-articular steroids may accelerate cartilage/structural loss (McAlindon RCT, JAMA 2017; OAI cohort, Osteoarthritis Cartilage 2019). Use steroids for flares/short-term relief, don't over-inject, and lean on PT/weight loss for durable benefit.

segments.label: SEE — Supply prep (no-touch technique)
segments.html:
1. Identify supplies with non-sterile gloves: marker, chlorhexidine swab, 1% lidocaine (5 cc), triamcinolone 40 mg/mL (1 cc), an 18 g needle to draw up, a 21–25 g 1.5" needle to inject, 5 cc syringe, gauze, band-aid. (Add a 2nd 18 g needle + 20–30 cc syringe if aspirating an effusion.)
  2. Draw-up tips: clean vial tops with alcohol; use a separate needle for drawing up vs injecting; inject air into a vial when withdrawing >3 cc; draw up lidocaine first so a little can be injected into the small steroid vial to ease aspiration.

segments.label: SEE — Marking, prep & injection
segments.html:
1. Mark the superior and lateral edges of the patella; draw two straight intersecting lines to mark the insertion point superolateral to the patella.
  2. Sterilize with a chlorhexidine scrub ×30 s.
  3. Position: supine, leg fully extended; use the non-dominant hand to gently displace the patella laterally.
  4. Insert the needle at the marked point, advancing parallel to the floor, directed slightly under the patella (perpendicular to the leg axis or just slightly toward the toes), to ~3 cm.
  5. Aspirate first (no blood return), then inject the lidocaine/triamcinolone mix without resistance (resistance = not in the joint space — reposition). Remove needle, apply band-aid.
ASK: "You meet firm resistance when you push the plunger. What does that tell you, and what do you do?"

Resistance means the needle tip is not in the joint space (it's in soft tissue, cartilage, or against bone). Stop, withdraw slightly and redirect; injecting against resistance risks intra-substance/tendon injection. Free flow = intra-articular.

segments.label: PRACTICE → PROVE — Mastery learning
segments.html: Each learner takes a turn as proceduralist with a peer/standardized patient as model. In the simulation, the learner lists indications/contraindications and verbalizes needle insertion (insertion is not physically performed in sim). In the clinical setting, they must identify the specific indication for this patient and demonstrate the supporting history/exam finding. Repeat until the learner meets the passing standard on the clinical assessment tool (error-counting checklist + Global Skills + Entrustment).

segments.label: Close (2 min)
segments.html: Hide the guide. Ask the learner to rebuild: the most accurate approach and position, the exam finding with the highest +LR for OA, the one absolute contraindication, what to tell a diabetic patient, and why PT beats the injection long-term.

cases.title: Case 1 — Chronic knee OA, stepwise management
cases.stem: A 65-year-old man has 6 months of worsening left knee pain, <15 min of morning stiffness, worse with activity; no inciting event, swelling, or locking. Exam: bony enlargement (+LR 12), crepitus (+LR 2), varus deformity (+LR 3.4); negative McMurray's/Lachman's. He's tried acetaminophen and ibuprofen with partial relief but no PT.
cases.reveal: Knee osteoarthritis. First-line: topical diclofenac (preferred), PT referral, and weight management if appropriate — these give durable benefit. X-ray is optional (poor correlation with symptoms); MRI is not indicated. If he returns without adequate relief, a corticosteroid injection may give small-to-moderate short-term relief (less effective than PT). If he returns months later with only ~1 month of relief from the injection, refer to orthopedics for consideration of total knee replacement rather than repeating injections indefinitely.

cases.title: Case 2 — Degenerative meniscal tear vs surgery
cases.stem: A 70-year-old man has 2 months of worsening right medial knee pain, no trauma, no swelling/locking/instability. Exam: medial joint-line tenderness (+LR 2) and pain/clicking on McMurray's (+LR 4).
cases.reveal: Physical therapy referral. For degenerative meniscal tears (older adults, often with concomitant OA), PT is as effective as arthroscopic surgery for pain and function (Khan, CMAJ 2014). MRI isn't recommended when conservative management is appropriate and wouldn't change management. Surgery is favored for younger patients with acute tears and mechanical symptoms (true locking / inability to fully extend).

mcqs.stem: Which approach is most accurate for intra-articular knee needle placement?
mcqs.exp: B. The superolateral approach has the highest accuracy (~90%) for intra-articular placement vs ~70% for anteromedial/anterolateral, and is the most reliable for aspiration (Hermans 2011; Wind 2004). Position supine with the leg fully extended.

mcqs.stem: Which exam finding has the highest positive likelihood ratio for knee osteoarthritis?
mcqs.exp: B. Palpable bony enlargement carries a +LR ≈ 12 for knee OA. Crepitus (+LR ≈ 2) and varus deformity (+LR ≈ 3.4) are associated but far less predictive; normal temperature simply argues against an inflammatory/septic process.

mcqs.stem: Which injectable has the best evidence for symptomatic knee OA?
mcqs.exp: B. Of the options, corticosteroids are best studied — a small-to-moderate pain benefit for ~6 weeks (Cochrane 2015). Physical therapy is more effective long-term. ACR 2019 conditionally recommends against hyaluronic acid, PRP, and stem cells (limited/uncertain efficacy, high cost).

mcqs.stem: A patient with T2DM asks what an intra-articular steroid will do to their blood sugar. The most accurate counseling is:
opts: A mean rise >100 mg/dL lasting ~24–48 hours (up to several days), greatest with poorer baseline control
A drop in blood glucose for 1–2 days
mcqs.exp: C. Studies show a mean glucose elevation >100 mg/dL for roughly 24–48 hours (sometimes several days), most pronounced in patients with worse baseline control (Waterbrook 2017; Shin 2020). Counsel monitoring; it is not a contraindication in well-controlled diabetes.

mcqs.stem: Which is an absolute contraindication to an intra-articular knee corticosteroid injection?
opts: Presence of hardware/prosthesis in the joint
mcqs.exp: C. Injecting into a prosthetic joint risks seeding hardware and is an absolute contraindication (as is overlying infection or a suspected septic joint — aspirate and treat, don't inject steroid). Anticoagulation (A), well-controlled diabetes (B), and surgery >3 months away (D) are not contraindications.

anki: Most accurate approach for intra-articular knee injection?
Superolateral (~90% accuracy vs ~70% for anteromedial/anterolateral; Hermans 2011). Also most reliable for aspiration.
Patient position for the superolateral knee injection?
Supine with the leg fully extended (or slightly hyperextended), relaxes quad/patellar tendons and opens the superolateral space.
Exam finding with the highest +LR for knee OA?
Palpable bony enlargement (+LR ~12). Varus deformity +LR ~3.4; morning stiffness <30 min ~3; crepitus ~2.
+LR of McMurray's and inability to fully extend for meniscal tear?
McMurray's +LR ~4; inability to fully extend (locking) +LR ~3.2.
Where is pes anserine bursitis tenderness?
Anteromedial tibia ~2-5 cm BELOW the joint line (insertion of sartorius/gracilis/semitendinosus), anterior & inferior to the medial joint line (distinguishes from medial meniscus tear).
Best-evidence injectable for knee OA?
Corticosteroids, small-to-moderate pain relief ~6 weeks (Cochrane 2015). Hyaluronic acid, PRP, stem cells: limited/uncertain efficacy (ACR conditionally recommends against).
What beats the steroid injection for long-term knee OA?
Physical therapy / exercise and weight loss (core therapy); topical NSAIDs (diclofenac) preferred first-line drug. Set expectations: steroid = modest, short-term.
Risk of REPEATED intra-articular knee steroids?
May accelerate cartilage/structural loss (McAlindon JAMA 2017 RCT; OAI cohort 2019). Use for flares/short-term; don't over-inject.
Absolute contraindication to intra-articular knee steroid?
Hardware/prosthetic joint; overlying skin infection; suspected septic joint (aspirate & treat, do not inject steroid).
Are anticoagulation or diabetes contraindications to knee steroid injection?
No, generally safe on anticoagulants and in well-controlled diabetes. Defer if joint surgery is imminent (<3 months).
Effect of intra-articular steroid on blood glucose in T2DM?
Mean rise >100 mg/dL for ~24-48 h (up to several days), greatest with poorer baseline control (Waterbrook 2017). Counsel monitoring.
Degenerative meniscal tear (older adult + OA), surgery or PT?
PT is as effective as arthroscopic surgery for pain/function (Khan CMAJ 2014). MRI usually won't change management.
Which meniscal-tear features favor surgery?
Younger patient, acute tear, true mechanical symptoms (locking / inability to fully extend). Concomitant OA, gradual swelling, or pain only on deep flexion favor conservative management.
Supplies for a knee steroid injection?

Marker, chlorhexidine, 1% lidocaine (5 cc), triamcinolone 40 mg/mL (1 cc), 18 g needle to draw up, 21-25 g 1.5" needle to inject, 5 cc syringe, gauze, band-aid (+ 18 g needle & 20-30 cc syringe if aspirating).
"No-touch" draw-up tips?
Clean vial tops with alcohol; separate needles for drawing up vs injecting; inject air when withdrawing >3 cc; draw lidocaine first and add a little to the steroid vial to ease aspiration.
Needle trajectory for the superolateral approach?
Insert superolateral to the patella, advance parallel to the floor, perpendicular to the leg axis or slightly toward the toes, ~3 cm; displace patella laterally with the non-dominant hand.
What does resistance on injection mean?
The tip is NOT in the joint space (soft tissue/cartilage/bone). Stop, withdraw slightly, redirect. Free flow = intra-articular.
First-line pharmacotherapy for knee OA per ACR 2019?
Topical NSAIDs (e.g., diclofenac) and oral NSAIDs, alongside exercise/PT and weight loss as core nonpharmacologic therapy.
lessons.dot:
PROCEDURE: Intra-articular knee corticosteroid injection *(+/- aspiration), *R/L
Indication: *[symptomatic knee OA / aseptic inflammatory arthritis flare] — supporting hx/exam: *[bony enlargement, crepitus, varus; stiffness <30 min]
Pre-proc checklist:
 - contraindic reviewed: NO joint hardware/prosthesis, NO overlying skin infection, NOT a suspected septic joint. Not contraindic: anticoagulation, well-controlled DM, surgery >3 mo out.
 - expectations set: steroid = small-to-moderate relief ~6 wks; PT/exercise + weight loss = durable benefit & core therapy; *diabetic counseled glucose may rise >100 mg/dL x24-48h.
 - consent obtained; risks (pain flare, infection, bleeding, skin/fat atrophy or depigmentation, transient hyperglycemia, repeated-injection cartilage concern) & alternatives discussed
 - TIME-OUT: correct patient (2 identifiers), procedure, and SIDE/site confirmed
Technique: supine, leg fully extended. Superior + lateral patellar edges marked → insertion point SUPEROLATERAL to patella. Chlorhexidine scrub x30 s. Patella displaced laterally; *21g needle advanced parallel to floor, slightly under patella, ~3 cm. Aspiration *[no return / *__ mL *[straw/cloudy/bloody] fluid sent for *cell count/crystals/cx]. Then *__ mL 1% lidocaine + 1 mL triamcinolone 40 mg/mL injected WITHOUT resistance. Needle removed, band-aid applied.
Post: tolerated well, no immediate complication. PT referral placed; pt encouraged to begin PT within ~4 wks for durable benefit. Return precautions (signs of infection) given.
Performing provider: *  | Supervising: *
takehome: Make the diagnosis on exam first: palpable bony enlargement (+LR ≈ 12) for OA; McMurray's (+LR ≈ 4) / locking for meniscal tear; anteromedial tibial tenderness below the joint line for pes anserine bursitis.
Superolateral approach, supine with the leg fully extended — ~90% accuracy and the most reliable site for aspiration.

Set expectations: intra-articular steroids give modest relief for ~6 weeks; PT/exercise and weight loss are more effective long-term and are core therapy; topical NSAIDs are preferred first-line. ACR recommends against HA/PRP/stem cells.

One absolute contraindication — hardware/prosthesis (plus overlying infection / suspected septic joint, where you aspirate and treat rather than inject steroid). Anticoagulation and controlled diabetes are not contraindications.
Counsel diabetics that glucose may rise >100 mg/dL for ~24–48 h; and don't over-inject — repeated steroids may accelerate structural progression.
Inject only without resistance — resistance means you're not in the joint space.

Currency checkVerified against current standards (June 2026): the ACR/Arthritis Foundation 2019 OA guideline (Kolasinski, Arthritis Rheumatol 2020) remains current and is not superseded — exercise/PT, weight loss, topical & oral NSAIDs, and intra-articular steroids are recommended; hyaluronic acid, PRP, and stem cells are conditionally recommended against. The superolateral-accuracy and exam likelihood-ratio data are unchanged. No standing-content change required.
Content addedAdded the repeated-steroid structural-progression caution (McAlindon JAMA 2017 RCT; OAI cohort, Osteoarthritis Cartilage 2019) and the explicit "PT > injection for durable benefit / topical NSAIDs first-line" framing from ACR 2019; consolidated the knee-exam likelihood ratios into a table; added suspected septic joint as an explicit contraindication to steroid (aspirate & treat instead).

lessons.title: Large Volume Paracentesis
lessons.subtitle: Optimized procedure package · Facilitator-led simulation-based mastery learning for IM residents · Pre-work ~20 min + simulation 60–90 min
lessons.credit: Adapted from TeachIM.org — Large Volume Paracentesis (Brandon Fainstad MD, Josephine Cool MD; published July 2022, materials rev. Mar–Apr 2025), CC BY-NC 4.0. Optimized June 2026: guideline currency verified, teaching design upgraded, assessments and clinical tools added. Procedure-type lesson — TeachIM publishes no slide deck for procedures; the package is built around the original instructional guide, clinical assessment tool (CAT), and quiz (all included below).
objectives: Determine the appropriate indications and contraindications to performing a large volume paracentesis.
Achieve a Minimum Passing Score (MPS) on the validated competency assessment tool in a simulation setting.
Perform a large volume paracentesis under full or partial supervision in the clinical setting until demonstrating the Unsupervised Practice Standard (UPS) on the validated assessment tool.

prework.q: Q1. What's the minimum "safe pocket" on ultrasound — and what additional depth makes a therapeutic tap worthwhile?
prework.a: A safe pocket = >2–3 cm of hypoechoic (black) fluid from peritoneum to viscera in all three dimensions; that's enough for a diagnostic tap. For a therapeutic (large-volume) tap, you generally want a maximal fluid depth >5 cm in three dimensions — less than that won't yield enough fluid to relieve symptoms and the catheter will occlude early. Scan with the low-frequency curvilinear/phased-array probe; note the probe-to-skin angle to guide your needle angle.

prework.q: Q2. Where do you insert, and which two structures must you screen for with the linear probe + Doppler before sticking?
prework.a: Insert in the LLQ (or RLQ) ~2 cm superior and ~2 cm medial to the anterior superior iliac spine — this avoids the flank musculature and stays lateral to the rectus sheath. Switch to the high-frequency linear probe with color/power Doppler to screen for (1) superficial abdominal-wall veins at the site (use minimal pressure — they compress easily) and (2) the inferior epigastric vessels (slide medially until the rectus abdominis comes into view). The inferior epigastrics run just lateral to the rectus — stay lateral to them.

prework.q: Q3. How much albumin do you give, and after what volume?
prework.a: Post-paracentesis albumin is generally not needed for taps <4–5 L. For large-volume taps, give 6–8 g of 25% albumin per liter removed — this reduces post-paracentesis circulatory dysfunction and improves survival (AASLD 2021). For a 10 L tap that's ≈60–80 g (e.g., 75 g of 25% albumin).

segments.label: Hook & framing (2 min)
segments.html: "Your cirrhotic patient with a tense, painful belly needs a tap. The intern wants to drain 'until it's dry' and skip the albumin to save time. What two decisions in that sentence could hurt the patient?" Hold the answer. Establish that the two highest-yield safety levers are ultrasound site selection (avoiding the inferior epigastric and abdominal-wall vessels) and replacing albumin for large-volume removal — not needle technique.

segments.label: LEARN — Indications & contraindications
segments.html:
ASK: "A patient has large ascites but feels fine — do you need another indication to do a therapeutic tap? And which of these counts: early satiety, leg edema, AKI?"
Indications for therapeutic (large-volume) tapDiagnostic tap (separate)
Symptomatic ascites: abdominal distention/pain, early satiety, dyspnea from increased intra-abdominal pressure. Large ascites alone, without symptoms, is not by itself an indication for a large-volume tap.New-onset ascites; every cirrhotic admitted to hospital with ascites (rule out SBP) — even if asymptomatic.
ASK: "Which is the real contraindication to a large-volume tap: sepsis/hemodynamic instability, intra-abdominal hypertension, INR >2, or platelets <50K?"
Weigh before large-volume removalNOT contraindications
Hemodynamic instability / sepsis — large-volume removal can precipitate or worsen circulatory dysfunction and hypotension (a diagnostic tap is still indicated to evaluate for SBP); also a clear surgical abdomen, or DIC / clinically evident fibrinolysis.INR >2, platelets <50K — coagulopathy alone does not increase clinically significant bleeding and routine correction/transfusion is not recommended. Intra-abdominal hypertension is relieved by the tap. Pregnancy and prior abdominal surgery are cautions (use ultrasound, avoid scars).
Pearl — coags don't block the tap: Paracentesis is safe across a wide range of INR/platelets; AASLD does not recommend routine FFP or platelet transfusion before the procedure. Hemodynamic instability is what makes you defer large-volume drainage, not the coags.

segments.label: LEARN — Positioning & pre-procedure ultrasound
segments.html:
ASK: "How do you position the patient to pool the most fluid in a dependent quadrant — and why does it matter more at the end of the drain than the start?"
  • Position: head of bed 15–45°, rotate the patient ~15° toward the side of the tap so air-filled bowel floats up and fluid pools in the dependent lower quadrant. Often unnecessary to find a pocket at the start, but as ascites drains the space shrinks and the catheter occludes early if the patient isn't optimally positioned.
  • Pre-procedure scan (low-freq curvilinear/phased-array): start in the RLQ/LLQ ~2 cm superior and ~2 cm medial to the ASIS. Find hypoechoic fluid >2–3 cm from peritoneum to viscera in all dimensions (≥5 cm for a worthwhile therapeutic tap). Note the probe angle — it guides needle angle.
  • Vascular screen (high-freq linear + color/power Doppler): look for superficial abdominal-wall veins at the site (compress easily — use light pressure; fan tangential to flow), then slide medially to identify the inferior epigastric vessels just lateral to the rectus abdominis. Stay lateral to them.
Safety anchor — the inferior epigastric arteries: they run just lateral to the rectus sheath; a tap placed too medially can lacerate one and cause a rectus-sheath hematoma. Go lateral (≥2 cm medial to ASIS, lateral to rectus), and Doppler the site first.

segments.label: SEE — Sterile prep & anesthesia
segments.html:
1. Time-out with the nurse (and patient) before opening sterile supplies; confirm consent (or the emergent indication).
  2. Sterile gear: mask, eye protection, sterile gloves (cap and gown optional for paracentesis — less than the maximal barrier required for a CVC).
  3. Skin prep: chlorhexidine scrub ×2, 30 s each, over an area larger than the drape aperture (chlorhexidine is scrubbed; iodine is applied and left to dry); apply the fenestrated drape.
  4. Anesthetize: dermal wheal with a 25 g needle, then advance a 21 g needle on the same syringe under negative pressure, anesthetizing the tract until straw-colored fluid is aspirated; withdraw slightly and deposit ~2 cc at the peritoneum.
  5. Prepare supplies: draw up lidocaine with the largest needle; identify the simple IV tubing (NO one-way valves) for draining ascites (different from the thoracentesis valved set).

segments.label: SEE — Catheter insertion, drainage & closure
segments.html:
1. #11 scalpel skin nick at the marked site.
  2. Advance the catheter-over-needle slowly under negative pressure (a "Z-track" — pull skin taut/offset before entry to reduce post-procedure leak) until straw-colored fluid returns; advance the unit another 0.5 cm, then thread the flexible catheter over the needle to the hub and remove the needle.
  3. Drain: connect simple IV tubing to the stopcock and to a vacuum bottle/vacutainer; exchange bottles until flow stops and symptoms improve.
  4. Closure: withdraw the catheter, clean, and dress with gauze. Document volume, color, lidocaine, and albumin given.
ASK: "Flow slows to a trickle after 4 L but the patient still feels tense — run me through your troubleshooting before you give up."

In order: (1) turn off suction and reposition — raise the HOB or roll the patient toward the side; (2) flush the catheter with 5–10 cc sterile saline (bowel may be plugging it); (3) rotate the catheter; (4) turn off suction and withdraw the catheter to ~2 cm + skin-to-peritoneum depth (often ~4 cm total). If there's too little ascites (<2 cm), switch to dynamic ultrasound guidance or refer to IR.

Pearl — give the albumin: for >5 L removed, infuse 6–8 g of 25% albumin per liter (≈75 g for 10 L). This prevents post-paracentesis circulatory dysfunction (a fall in effective volume → renin/AKI) and improves survival (AASLD 2021).

segments.label: PRACTICE → PROVE — Mastery learning
segments.html:
The learner rehearses each step, then signals readiness. With the checklist out of view, quiz the non-technical steps; then run the full simulation with it visible, marking each mistake or required prompt, and assign Global Skills + Entrustment scores. Repeat until the learner reaches the MPS. For steps the simulator can't reproduce, have the learner verbalize the intended step.

ComponentMPS (graduate simulation)UPS (unsupervised clinical practice)
Error-counting checklist (41 items, Angoff-set)<30 mistakes<8 mistakes
Global Skills Assessment>2≥ "proficient"
Entrustment score>2"without direct supervision" or better

segments.label: Close (2 min)
segments.html: Hide the guide. Ask the learner to rebuild: the symptom indications for a therapeutic tap, the insertion landmark and the two vessels to Doppler, the minimum safe pocket vs the therapeutic-worthwhile depth, the albumin dose and threshold, and the four troubleshooting steps when flow stops.

cases.title: Case 1 — High-SAAG, high-protein ascites
cases.stem: A patient presents with subacute dyspnea and painful abdominal distention. Ultrasound shows ascites; 2 L of clear straw-colored fluid is removed. Fluid: WBC 420 (40% PMN), protein 3.3 g/dL, albumin 2.1 g/dL. Serum: creatinine 1.9, total bilirubin 1.5, AST 70, ALT 100, albumin 3.3 g/dL.
cases.reveal: Obtain a transthoracic echocardiogram. The PMN count is 168 (40% of 420), well below 250 — so this is not SBP, and ceftriaxone is not indicated. The SAAG = 3.3 − 2.1 = 1.2 (≥1.1 → portal hypertension), but the high ascitic protein (>2.5 g/dL) points to a post-hepatic/cardiac cause rather than cirrhosis (cirrhotic ascites is high-SAAG but low-protein). High SAAG + high protein → evaluate the heart with an echocardiogram.

cases.title: Case 2 — Which fluid studies on a routine therapeutic tap?
cases.stem: A patient with known alcohol-related cirrhosis, whose ascites is usually controlled with diuretics, presents with a tense, painful abdomen and no other new symptoms. Large ascites is confirmed and a therapeutic tap is performed.
cases.reveal: Cell count + differential, Gram stain and culture (and, on a first/diagnostic tap, albumin and total protein for the SAAG). Even when the indication is symptom relief, send a cell count and culture every admission to exclude SBP — it is frequently asymptomatic in cirrhotics. Cytology (A) is for suspected malignancy, not routine; "just remove fluid" (D) misses an occult SBP.

mcqs.stem: Which finding is an appropriate indication for a therapeutic large-volume paracentesis in a patient with large ascites?
opts: No additional indication is needed — large ascites alone justifies it
mcqs.exp: B. Therapeutic taps are for symptoms of increased intra-abdominal pressure — distention/pain, early satiety, or dyspnea. Lower-extremity edema (A) and AKI (C) are not relieved by (and may be worsened by) large-volume removal, and asymptomatic large ascites (D) does not by itself warrant a therapeutic tap (though a diagnostic tap is indicated on admission to exclude SBP).

mcqs.stem: A cirrhotic patient needs a tap. INR is 2.4 and platelets are 45K. What is the most appropriate approach?
opts: Transfuse FFP and platelets to correct the coagulopathy first
Proceed with ultrasound guidance — coagulopathy alone is not a contraindication
Cancel the procedure; INR >2 is an absolute contraindication
Place a central line for access first
mcqs.exp: B. Paracentesis is safe across a wide range of INR and platelet counts; AASLD does not recommend routine FFP or platelet transfusion beforehand, and abnormal coags alone don't increase clinically significant bleeding (A, C wrong). The real reason to defer a large-volume tap is hemodynamic instability/sepsis, not the coags.

mcqs.stem: When mapping a site with ultrasound, which structure must you identify and stay lateral to, to avoid a rectus-sheath hematoma?
opts: The inferior epigastric vessels (just lateral to the rectus abdominis)
mcqs.exp: A. The inferior epigastric arteries run just lateral to the rectus sheath; a too-medial puncture can lacerate one and cause a rectus-sheath hematoma. Insert in the LLQ/RLQ ~2 cm medial to the ASIS and lateral to the rectus, after a Doppler screen for these and superficial abdominal-wall veins.

mcqs.stem: After removing 10 L of ascites, what albumin replacement is most appropriate?
opts: ~75 g of 25% IV albumin (6–8 g per liter removed)
mcqs.exp: C. For large-volume taps (>5 L), give 6–8 g of 25% albumin per liter removed — ≈60–80 g for 10 L — to prevent post-paracentesis circulatory dysfunction and improve survival (AASLD 2021). 5% albumin (A) is too dilute (large volume needed), no replacement (B) risks circulatory dysfunction after a large tap, and FFP (D) is not volume-expansion therapy here.

mcqs.stem: During a therapeutic tap, fluid flow slows and stops after 3 L though the abdomen is still tense. What is the best first troubleshooting step?
opts: Remove the catheter and abort the procedure
Turn off suction and reposition the patient (raise HOB / roll toward the side), then flush the catheter with saline
Advance the catheter deeper toward the bowel
mcqs.exp: B. When flow slows, turn off negative pressure and reposition to move fluid toward the catheter, then flush with 5–10 cc saline (bowel may be occluding the port); next rotate or withdraw the catheter slightly. Higher suction (C) and advancing toward bowel (D) increase the risk of perforation/occlusion, and aborting (A) is premature.

anki: Indication for a therapeutic (large-volume) paracentesis?
Symptomatic ascites: abdominal distention/pain, early satiety, or dyspnea from increased intra-abdominal pressure. (Large ascites alone, asymptomatic, is not by itself an indication.)
When is a diagnostic paracentesis indicated?
New-onset ascites; and EVERY cirrhotic admitted to hospital with ascites (to exclude SBP), even if asymptomatic.
What defers a LARGE-VOLUME tap (vs a diagnostic tap)?

Hemodynamic instability / sepsis (large-volume removal worsens circulatory dysfunction); surgical abdomen; DIC/clinically evident fibrinolysis. A diagnostic tap is still indicated in sepsis to evaluate for SBP.
Are INR >2 or platelets <50K contraindications to paracentesis?
No, coagulopathy alone does not increase clinically significant bleeding; AASLD does not recommend routine FFP/platelet transfusion before the tap.
Is intra-abdominal hypertension a contraindication to paracentesis?
No, it is relieved BY the paracentesis.
Minimum "safe pocket" on ultrasound for a tap?
More than 2-3 cm of hypoechoic fluid (peritoneum to viscera) in all 3 dimensions. For a worthwhile therapeutic tap, want maximal depth >5 cm.
Which ultrasound transducer to evaluate ascites depth?
Low-frequency CURVILINEAR (or phased-array). Use the high-frequency LINEAR probe + Doppler for the vascular screen.
Insertion landmark for paracentesis?
LLQ (or RLQ) ~2 cm superior and ~2 cm medial to the anterior superior iliac spine, avoids flank musculature, stays lateral to the rectus sheath.
Two vessels to screen for with Doppler before the tap?
Superficial abdominal-wall veins at the site (compress with light pressure) and the inferior epigastric vessels (just lateral to the rectus abdominis, stay lateral to them).
Why avoid a too-medial puncture in paracentesis?
The inferior epigastric arteries run just lateral to the rectus sheath; laceration causes a rectus-sheath hematoma.
Patient positioning for paracentesis?
HOB 15-45 deg, rotate ~15 deg toward the side of the tap so bowel floats up and fluid pools dependently. Matters most late in the drain as the space shrinks.
Sterile gear for paracentesis vs CVC?
Mask, eye protection, sterile gloves (cap/gown optional), less than the maximal sterile barrier required for a CVC. Chlorhexidine scrub x2, 30 s each.
Which drainage tubing for paracentesis?
Simple IV tubing with NO one-way valves, to a vacuum bottle/vacutainer (different from the valved thoracentesis set).
Albumin replacement after large-volume paracentesis?
6-8 g of 25% albumin per liter removed for taps >5 L (~75 g for 10 L). Prevents post-paracentesis circulatory dysfunction; improves survival (AASLD 2021). Not needed for <4-5 L.
Troubleshooting when ascites flow slows/stops?

(1) Turn off suction + reposition (raise HOB/roll to side); (2) flush catheter 5-10 cc saline (bowel may be plugging it); (3) rotate catheter; (4) withdraw to ~2 cm + skin-to-peritoneum depth (~4 cm). Too little fluid (<2 cm), use dynamic US or IR.
High SAAG (>=1.1) + HIGH ascitic protein (>2.5) suggests?
Cardiac / post-hepatic cause (e.g., heart failure), get an echocardiogram. Cirrhotic ascites is high-SAAG but LOW-protein.
SBP diagnosis on fluid cell count?
Ascitic PMN >=250 cells/mm3, treat (e.g., ceftriaxone). 40% of 420 = 168, NOT SBP.
MPS vs UPS thresholds on the paracentesis CAT?
MPS (graduate sim): <30/41 mistakes, Global >2, Entrustment >2 (Angoff-set). UPS (unsupervised practice): <8/41 mistakes.
lessons.dot:
PROCEDURE: Large volume paracentesis, *R/L lower quadrant
Indication: symptomatic large-volume ascites — *[abdominal distention/pain / early satiety / dyspnea] *(+/- diagnostic: r/o SBP on admission)
Pre-proc checklist:
 - contraindic reviewed: hemodynamically stable (NOT septic/unstable for large-volume removal); no surgical abdomen; no DIC/clinical fibrinolysis. Coags (INR *__/plt *__) NOT corrected — coagulopathy alone is not a contraindication.
 - consent obtained from *[pt/surrogate] *(or emergent indication); risks (bleeding/rectus-sheath hematoma, infection, bowel/bladder injury, persistent ascites leak, post-paracentesis circulatory dysfunction/AKI, failure) & alternatives discussed
 - TIME-OUT w/ RN: name/DOB/procedure/SIDE/site/allergies confirmed BEFORE opening sterile supplies
US (low-freq curvilinear): pocket *>2-3 cm (>5 cm for therapeutic) in 3 dimensions, *R/L LQ ~2 cm sup + ~2 cm medial to ASIS, lateral to rectus; skin-to-peritoneum *__ cm. High-freq linear + Doppler — *no superficial abd-wall vein, inferior epigastric vessels identified and avoided.
Technique: position HOB *30°, rotated *toward side. Chlorhexidine scrub x2 (30 s each), fenestrated drape. 1% lidocaine *5-10 mL (wheal → tract under negative pressure to fluid → ~2 cc at peritoneum). #11 skin nick. Catheter-over-needle advanced (Z-track) under negative pressure to straw-colored fluid; advanced 0.5 cm more, catheter threaded to hub, needle removed. Simple IV tubing (NO valves) to vacuum bottle.
Drainage: * L *[clear straw / cloudy / bloody / chylous] removed; vacuum bottles exchanged until flow stopped + symptoms improved. *Troubleshooting [reposition / flush / rotate / withdraw] *as needed.
Studies sent: *[cell count+diff, Gram stain+cx; albumin + total protein for SAAG (first tap); cytology if malignancy suspected].
Albumin: *__ g of 25% IV albumin given (6-8 g/L for >5 L removed; ~75 g for 10 L).
Closure: catheter removed, site cleaned + dressed. Pt tolerated well, *EBL minimal, no immediate complication.
Lidocaine used (mL): *__   Performing provider: *  | Supervising: *
takehome: Indication is symptoms, not just fluid: a therapeutic tap is for distention/pain, early satiety, or dyspnea — but every cirrhotic admitted with ascites still gets a diagnostic tap to exclude SBP.

Coagulopathy doesn't block the tap: INR >2 and platelets <50K are not contraindications and don't warrant routine FFP/platelets. Hemodynamic instability/sepsis is what makes you defer large-volume removal.

Ultrasound site selection is the core safety skill: LLQ/RLQ ~2 cm medial to the ASIS, lateral to the rectus; Doppler to avoid the inferior epigastric vessels and abdominal-wall veins; safe pocket >2–3 cm (≥5 cm to be worth a therapeutic tap).
Replace albumin for large volumes: 6–8 g of 25% albumin per liter removed for taps >5 L prevents post-paracentesis circulatory dysfunction and improves survival.
Know the troubleshooting ladder when flow stops: reposition → flush → rotate → withdraw the catheter.
Prove competence by mastery learning: reach the MPS (<30/41 mistakes) in simulation, then the UPS (<8/41) under graded observation before unsupervised practice.

lessons.title: Lumbar Puncture
lessons.subtitle: Optimized procedure package · Facilitator-led simulation-based mastery learning for IM residents · Pre-work ~20 min + simulation 60–90 min
lessons.credit: Adapted from TeachIM.org — Lumbar Puncture (Fainstad, Ortiz-Lopez, Kramer, Peterson, Meliagros, Ma; sec. ed. Cool; Dec 2023), CC BY-NC 4.0. Optimized June 2026: guideline currency verified, teaching design upgraded, assessments and clinical tools added. Procedure-type lesson — TeachIM publishes no slide deck for procedures; the package is built around the original instructional guide, clinical assessment tool, and MCQ pre-quiz (all included below).
objectives: Describe the appropriate indications and contraindications to performing a bedside lumbar puncture.
Achieve a minimum passing score (MPS) on the clinical assessment checklist in a simulation setting.
Perform a lumbar puncture under full or partial supervision in the clinical setting until demonstrating the Unsupervised Practice Standard (UPS).

prework.q: Q1. Name the one absolute contraindication to LP, and three relative ones.
prework.a: Absolute: overlying skin infection or suspected epidural abscess at the puncture site. Relative: space-occupying lesion / Arnold-Chiari malformation (get CT ± neurosurgery first), platelets <50K, INR ≥1.5, therapeutic anticoagulation, and prior lumbar surgery at the target level. A space-occupying lesion is relative, not absolute — but get a head CT first when there are red flags (see Q2).

prework.q: Q2. Which clinical findings should prompt a head CT before LP in suspected meningitis?
prework.a: Altered mentation, focal neurologic signs, papilledema, seizure within the past week, or impaired cellular immunity (Hasbun, NEJM 2001). Absent all of these, imaging is not required before LP — and giving antibiotics must never wait for the CT or the LP in suspected bacterial meningitis.

prework.q: Q3. Why must opening pressure be measured in the lateral decubitus position, and what one maneuver lowers a falsely elevated reading?
prework.a: In the upright (sitting) position the hydrostatic column falsely elevates the measured pressure, so a true opening pressure can only be obtained lying laterally with legs and neck relaxed. Normal OP is 10–25 cm H₂O. If OP is elevated, straightening the patient's flexed legs can drop it by ~5 cm H₂O (tense flexion and Valsalva artificially raise it).

segments.label: Hook & framing (2 min)
segments.html: "Your intern is about to do their first LP on a febrile, confused 59-year-old. Before they touch a needle, what are the two questions that decide whether the procedure happens at all — and in what order?" (Indication confirmed? Any contraindication / need for CT first? Then consent.) Establish that most procedural failures are cognitive, not manual — landmarks and patient selection drive success more than needle skill.

segments.label: LEARN — Indications & contraindications
segments.html:
ASK: "Sort these into urgent-diagnostic, non-urgent-diagnostic, and therapeutic: suspected meningitis, suspected SAH with negative CT, NPH, MS, idiopathic intracranial hypertension, intrathecal chemo."
Diagnostic — urgentDiagnostic — non-urgentTherapeutic
Suspected CNS infection / meningitis; suspected SAH with negative imagingNon-obstructive malignancy (cytology); normal-pressure hydrocephalus; autoimmune/inflammatory (CNS vasculitis, MS, Guillain-Barré, paraneoplastic)Pseudotumor cerebri / idiopathic intracranial hypertension (also diagnostic); intrathecal medication or contrast injection
ASK: "Which contraindication is absolute, and which are relative?"
AbsoluteRelative (weigh urgency & mitigate)
Overlying skin infection or suspected epidural abscess at the siteSpace-occupying lesion / Arnold-Chiari (CT ± neurosurgery first) · platelets <50K · INR ≥1.5 · therapeutic anticoagulation (hold heparin gtt <4 h, LMWH <12 h, DOAC <2.5 half-lives, or per local guideline) · prior lumbar surgery
Pearl — herniation fear vs reality: Not every space-occupying lesion raises ICP, and not every raised ICP herniates after LP. Get a head CT first only with altered mentation, focal signs, papilledema, recent seizure, or immunocompromise (Hasbun, NEJM 2001). In suspected bacterial meningitis, never delay empiric antibiotics for the CT or the LP.

segments.label: LEARN — Consent, positioning & landmarks
segments.html:
Consent (confirm understanding of indication, benefits, risks & mitigation, and the alternative of not doing it). Position to maximize spinal flexion and minimize R/L rotation:

Upright (sitting)Lateral decubitus
Edge of bed, leaning over a side table, feet on a stool so knees are above hips. Easier landmarking in larger patients — but no opening pressure possible.Provider-handed side down (left lateral if right-handed). Stack ankles–knees–shoulders (pillow between knees), pillow under head, chin tucked, knees toward abdomen. Required for opening pressure.
ASK: "Show me how you find the interspace — by palpation and by ultrasound. Which interspaces are safe?"

Palpation: palpate the iliac crest → corresponds to ~L4; palpate spinous processes to find midline; mark L3–L4 and L4–L5 (or L2–L3). Ultrasound (recommended): low-frequency transducer from the sacrum cephalad — transverse view marks midline across 3 spinous processes; longitudinal view marks the interspinous spaces. Stay at or below L2–L5 to remain below the conus medullaris.

Pearl — ultrasound earns its place: Pre-procedure US marking improves first-pass success and reduces needle passes, especially in obesity and difficult anatomy (SHM position statement, Soni, J Hosp Med 2019; Millington, Chest 2018). It does not need to be used in real time — mark, then proceed sterile.

segments.label: SEE — Sterile prep, anesthesia & needle insertion
segments.html:
1. Supplies & time-out: gather kit; confirm name, DOB, procedure, site, allergies with the nurse before opening sterile supplies; include the patient.
  2. Sterile prep: iodine-povidone ×3, let dry (preferred over chlorhexidine given theoretical meningeal-irritation risk); non-fenestrated drape on the bed, then the fenestrated drape in landscape orientation.
  3. Draw-up lidocaine: aspirate 5–10 cc of 1% lidocaine without epinephrine through the filter/large needle, then switch to a 25 g needle.
  4. Anesthetize the tract: raise a superficial dermal wheal using only ~0.5 cc so you don't obscure your skin marks, then anesthetize deeper along the planned path.
  5. Insert the spinal needle: stylet in, at the midline, aimed toward the umbilicus, along the anesthetized tract. Advance slowly, removing the stylet frequently to check for CSF return. If you strike bone, withdraw to the subcutaneous tissue and redirect — each pass should go deeper if you're staying midline.
Safety stop: Always advance with the stylet in place and never inject through the spinal needle. Use an atraumatic (pencil-point) needle — strong recommendation for all patients (see Pearl). If you cannot stay midline, stop, re-mark (ultrasound), and reposition rather than fanning blindly.
Pearl — needle choice is the single biggest modifiable risk: Atraumatic pencil-point needles cut PDPH roughly in half (≈11% → ≈4%) with equal success, a strong recommendation for all ages and indications (Rochwerg/Nath, BMJ 2018; Lancet 2018). A 22 g atraumatic needle balances PDPH reduction, flow, and OP accuracy. Replacing the stylet before withdrawal also reduces PDPH.

segments.label: SEE — Opening pressure, collection & closure
segments.html:
• Opening pressure (lateral decubitus only): prepare the manometer/column and extension tubing beforehand; once CSF returns, attach with tubing horizontal, open the stopcock to the column, read when the meniscus stalls. Normal 10–25 cm H₂O. If elevated, straighten the legs (can drop OP ~5 cm H₂O).
  • Collection: close the stopcock to the patient, then collect into tubes 1–4. If OP was not obtained, remove the stylet and collect directly. Slow flow → rotate the needle in 30° increments. Send the minimum necessary volume per study (see ASK below).
  • Closure: replace the stylet, then withdraw the needle (stylet in); ensure hemostasis; apply dressing.
  • Post-procedure: dispose of sharps; reassess vitals/clinical status; label and send all tubes; update nursing; document the procedure note.
ASK: "Standard studies need ~8–12 mL across four tubes. Which studies need much more — and how much?"

Routine chemistries, cell counts, viral studies, and typical bacterial cultures: ~8–12 mL total over 4 tubes. Cytology and slow-growing cultures (fungal, AFB): a minimum of ~30 mL distributed over the tubes — under-collecting is a common reason these come back non-diagnostic.

segments.label: PRACTICE → PROVE — Mastery learning
segments.html:
The learner familiarizes with the kit and rehearses each step, then signals readiness for a graded run. With the checklist out of view, quiz the non-technical steps, then return the checklist to view and run the full simulation. Mark each mistake or required prompt. Repeat until the learner reaches the minimum passing standard.

ComponentMPS (graduate simulation)UPS (unsupervised clinical practice)
Error-counting checklist<12 mistakes<3 mistakes
Global Skills Assessment (1–5)≥ "beginner" (>2)≥ "proficient"
Entrustment score"full supervision" acceptable"indirect supervision" or better

segments.label: Close (2 min)
segments.html: Hide the guide. Ask the learner to rebuild from memory: the one absolute contraindication, the five clinical triggers for pre-LP CT, the safe interspace levels, why OP needs lateral decubitus, and the single most effective way to prevent PDPH.

cases.title: Case 1 — Morning headache + pulsatile tinnitus
cases.stem: 35-year-old woman with headache worse in the morning, faint blurred vision, and a buzzing in her ears when lying down. No fever, confusion, or URI symptoms. PMH: obesity, dysfunctional uterine bleeding on an OCP. Neuro exam non-focal. MRI with gadolinium shows faint optic-nerve enhancement, otherwise normal. An LP is planned.
cases.reveal: Opening pressure. The syndrome is highly consistent with idiopathic intracranial hypertension (IIH); a clearly elevated OP (>25 cm H₂O) is the classic confirmatory finding. Send routine studies too (cell counts, protein, glucose, bacteriology). Cytology, fungal culture, and oligoclonal bands are not indicated here.

cases.title: Case 2 — Headache, confusion, subjective fever
cases.stem: 59-year-old man with 2 days of mild headache, then morning confusion and feeling warm. PMH: OA, HTN, HLD, remote L4/5 discectomy. Exam: T 100.4 °F, disoriented, mildly agitated; can sit up and extend his arms on command. An LP is planned for possible meningoencephalitis.
cases.reveal: HSV 1 & 2 PCR — send for all patients with possible meningoencephalitis. Also obtain an opening pressure. Fungal cultures and oligoclonal bands are not indicated without immunosuppression or another specific concern.

mcqs.stem: A 48-year-old woman needs a diagnostic LP for suspected MS. Which choice most reduces her risk of post-dural puncture headache?
opts: 1 L of IV fluid before the procedure
mcqs.exp: C. Atraumatic pencil-point needles roughly halve PDPH (≈11% → ≈4%) with equal success and are a strong recommendation for all patients (BMJ/Lancet 2018). Post-procedure bed rest (A) and IV fluids (D) do not prevent PDPH (Cochrane). A larger cutting needle (B) increases PDPH. Replacing the stylet before withdrawal also helps.

mcqs.stem: A 24-year-old with suspected bacterial meningitis is febrile and confused with new papilledema. What is the correct sequence?
opts: Blood cultures + empiric antibiotics now, head CT, then LP if safe
Head CT, then LP, then antibiotics after CSF results
mcqs.exp: B. Papilledema (plus altered mentation/focal signs/recent seizure/immunocompromise) mandates a head CT before LP (Hasbun, NEJM 2001). But antibiotics must never wait for imaging or LP in suspected bacterial meningitis — draw cultures and treat empirically first (A and C dangerously delay therapy; D delays both imaging interpretation and treatment).

mcqs.stem: During an LP you obtain CSF and want an accurate opening pressure. Which condition must be met?
opts: Patient in lateral decubitus with legs relaxed
Manometer held above the level of the heart
mcqs.exp: B. Opening pressure is valid only in lateral decubitus; the upright column (A) falsely elevates it. Sustained Valsalva (D) and tense leg flexion artificially raise OP — straightening the legs can lower a high reading ~5 cm H₂O. Normal OP is 10–25 cm H₂O.

mcqs.stem: A 62-year-old needs CSF cytology and fungal cultures to evaluate possible leptomeningeal disease. What volume should you collect?
opts: A minimum of ~30 mL distributed over the tubes
mcqs.exp: C. Cytology and slow-growing cultures (fungal, AFB) need substantially more CSF (~30 mL) than routine chemistries, cell counts, viral studies, or typical bacterial cultures (~8–12 mL total). Under-collection (A, B) is a frequent cause of non-diagnostic cytology. 100 mL (D) far exceeds what's needed or safe.

mcqs.stem: A 70-year-old on apixaban for AF needs a non-urgent diagnostic LP. Which is the most appropriate approach?
opts: Proceed now; DOACs are not a contraindication
Hold apixaban for ~2.5 half-lives (or per local protocol) and confirm timing/renal function before proceeding
Give andexanet alfa, then proceed immediately
mcqs.exp: B. Therapeutic anticoagulation is a relative contraindication. For an elective LP, hold the DOAC for ≥2.5 half-lives (longer with renal impairment), or follow local/ABN periprocedural guidance, and confirm timing before the needle (A is unsafe for an elective procedure). Reversal agents (C) are reserved for emergent, not elective, situations. Permanent cancellation (D) is unnecessary once anticoagulation is safely managed.

anki: The one absolute contraindication to LP?
Overlying skin infection or suspected epidural abscess at the puncture site.
Relative contraindications to LP?
Space-occupying lesion / Arnold-Chiari (CT ± neurosurgery first); platelets <50K; INR ≥1.5; therapeutic anticoagulation; prior lumbar surgery at the target level.
Five findings that require a head CT before LP in suspected meningitis?
Altered mentation, focal neurologic signs, papilledema, seizure within the past week, impaired cellular immunity (Hasbun NEJM 2001).
In suspected bacterial meningitis, what must never wait for CT or LP?
Empiric antibiotics (draw blood cultures, then treat immediately).
Safe interspace levels for LP and why?
L2–L5 (mark L3–L4, L4–L5, or L2–L3) — below the conus medullaris.
How to find the L4 level by palpation?
Palpate the iliac crest — it corresponds to roughly the L4 spinous process / L4–L5 interspace.
Why use pre-procedure ultrasound for LP?
Improves first-pass success and reduces needle passes, especially in obesity/difficult anatomy (SHM 2019; Millington Chest 2018). Mark midline + interspaces, then proceed sterile.
Which needle type is preferred for LP and why?
Atraumatic (pencil-point) needle — strong rec for all ages/indications; halves PDPH (~11% → ~4%) with equal success (BMJ/Lancet 2018). A 22 g balances PDPH, flow, OP accuracy.
Interventions that reduce PDPH vs those that do NOT?
DO reduce: atraumatic needle, smaller gauge, replacing the stylet before withdrawal. Do NOT: post-procedure bed rest, extra IV fluids (Cochrane).
Preferred skin antiseptic for LP and why?
Iodine-povidone ×3 (let dry), preferred over chlorhexidine due to theoretical meningeal-irritation/neurotoxicity risk.
How is the spinal needle advanced?
Stylet in, midline, aimed at the umbilicus along the anesthetized tract; advance slowly, removing the stylet frequently to check for CSF; if bone, withdraw to subcutaneous tissue and redirect.
Why must opening pressure be measured in lateral decubitus?
Upright positioning creates a hydrostatic column that falsely elevates the reading; only lateral decubitus gives a true OP.
Normal CSF opening pressure, and how to lower a falsely high reading?
10–25 cm H₂O; straightening the patient's flexed legs can drop it ~5 cm H₂O.
CSF volume for routine studies vs cytology/fungal cultures?
Routine chemistries/cell counts/viral/bacterial: ~8–12 mL over 4 tubes. Cytology + slow-growing (fungal, AFB): minimum ~30 mL.
Classic LP finding in idiopathic intracranial hypertension?
Clearly elevated opening pressure (>25 cm H₂O) with otherwise normal CSF; obtain OP whenever IIH is suspected.
Closure sequence at the end of an LP?
Replace the stylet, then withdraw the needle (stylet in); ensure hemostasis; dressing; reassess vitals; label/send tubes; document.
MPS vs UPS thresholds on the LP clinical assessment tool?
MPS (graduate sim): <12 mistakes, Global >2, "full supervision." UPS (unsupervised practice): <3 mistakes, "proficient," "indirect supervision."
lessons.dot:
PROCEDURE: Diagnostic/therapeutic lumbar puncture
Indication: * [meningitis/encephalitis, SAH w/ neg CT, IIH/pseudotumor, NPH, CNS malignancy/cytology, MS/autoimmune, intrathecal med]
Pre-proc checklist:
 - contraindic reviewed: NO overlying skin infxn/epidural abscess (absolute); plt *>50K, INR *<1.5, anticoag held [hep gtt <4h / LMWH <12h / DOAC <2.5 t1/2], no prior lumbar fusion at target
 - head CT before LP? [indicated if: AMS, focal deficit, papilledema, szr <1wk, immunocompromised] → CT *[done/not indicated]; *abx NOT delayed for CT/LP in susp bact meningitis
 - consent obtained from *[pt/surrogate]; risks (PDPH, bleed, infxn, herniation-rare, failure) & alternatives discussed
 - TIME-OUT performed w/ RN: name/DOB/procedure/site/allergies confirmed
Technique: position *[L/R lateral decubitus / sitting]; site marked by palpation *and US at *[L3-L4 / L4-L5 / L2-L3] (below conus, L2-L5). Sterile prep iodine-povidone x3, fenestrated drape. 1% lidocaine *5 mL w/o epi (dermal wheal + tract). *22g ATRAUMATIC (pencil-point) spinal needle inserted midline toward umbilicus, stylet in, advanced w/ frequent stylet removal to check CSF return. SAFETY: never inject thru needle; if bone → withdraw to subQ & redirect midline.
Opening pressure (lateral decubitus only): * cm H2O [nl 10-25; straighten legs if elevated] — *[obtained / not obtained]
CSF collected: * mL into tubes 1-4 [routine ~8-12 mL; cytology/fungal/AFB ≥30 mL]; *studies sent [cell count+diff, protein, glucose, gram stain+cx, *HSV PCR, *cytology, *oligoclonal bands, *fungal/AFB, *OP]
Closure: stylet replaced, needle withdrawn, hemostasis achieved, sterile dressing applied.
Post: sharps disposed; vitals/neuro reassessed — pt tolerated well, *EBL minimal, no immediate complication; tubes labeled & sent; RN updated.
Color of fluid: *[clear/xanthochromic/bloody]  | Atraumatic vs traumatic: *  | Provider: *  | Supervising: *
takehome: The only absolute contraindication is overlying skin infection / epidural abscess; the rest are relative — weigh urgency and mitigate (platelets, INR, anticoagulation, prior surgery).
Get a head CT before LP only for altered mentation, focal signs, papilledema, recent seizure, or immunocompromise — and never delay antibiotics for it in suspected bacterial meningitis.
Stay at L2–L5; use ultrasound to mark midline and interspace — it improves success and cuts passes.
Use an atraumatic (pencil-point) needle — the single most effective way to prevent PDPH; bed rest and IV fluids do not help.
Opening pressure is valid only in lateral decubitus (normal 10–25 cm H₂O); collect ~30 mL when cytology or fungal/AFB cultures are needed.
Competence is proven by mastery learning: reach the MPS in simulation, then the UPS under graded clinical observation before unsupervised practice.

  4. Rochwerg B, Almenawer SA, Siemieniuk RAC, et al. Atraumatic (pencil-point) versus conventional needles for lumbar puncture: a clinical practice guideline. BMJ. 2018;361:k1920.
  5. Nath S, Koziarz A, Badhiwala JH, et al. Atraumatic versus conventional lumbar puncture needles: a systematic review and meta-analysis. Lancet. 2018;391(10126):1197-1204.
