lessons.changelog:
AreaWhat changed
Currency checkVerified against current standards (June 2026): atraumatic-needle strong recommendation (Rochwerg/Nath, BMJ/Lancet 2018) unchanged and still standard; SHM ultrasound-guidance position statement (Soni, J Hosp Med 2019) current; ABN periprocedural antithrombotic guidance (Dodd, Pract Neurol 2018) current; Hasbun pre-LP CT criteria (NEJM 2001) current. No standing-content change required.
Content addedSurfaced atraumatic-needle PDPH evidence and the "bed rest/fluids do not prevent PDPH" point explicitly; added DOAC/anticoagulation hold guidance; added the ~8–12 mL vs ~30 mL collection-volume teaching to the script.
CorrectionsNo errors found in the instructional guide or clinical assessment tool. See "Human review needed."
PedagogyRestructured into the Learn–See–Practice–Prove arc with a 2-min hook, ≥4 ASK pause-points, pearls/safety-stop callouts, HTML tables for indications/contraindications/positioning/MPS-UPS, and a retrieval close.
AssessmentAdded 3 pre-work retrieval questions, 5 ABIM-style board MCQs with full distractor explanations, 2 case vignettes (converted from the pre-quiz), and 17 spaced-repetition cards.
Clinical toolAdded an EMR-ready LP procedure-note / time-out dotphrase (procedure-type structure).
One-pagerBuilt a dense single-page procedure reference (separate print-CSS HTML → PDF, one page).
FormatSelf-contained HTML launcher linking all original companion files (instructional guide, clinical assessment tool, both pre-quiz versions) + generated files; matches the Syncope/Chronic AF design system. No _Optimized.pptx — TeachIM publishes no slide deck for procedures; per the no-rebuild rule none was created.
Human review neededPre-quiz answer key (Lumbar-Puncture-MCQ-quiz-answer-sheet.pdf), Q7 explanation: the final distractor explanation is lettered "C) Is incorrect. Fungal cultures…" but should read "D)" (C is the correct answer, HSV PCR). Cosmetic lettering typo in the source PDF — left unedited (flattened PDF; not surgically editable) and flagged for the authors.

lessons.title: Lung Cancer Screening
lessons.subtitle: Optimized lesson package · Facilitator-led interactive chalk talk for IM residents · 30–45 min
lessons.credit: Adapted from TeachIM.org — Lung Cancer Screening (Nie; ed. Geiger; exec. eds. Zhang, Fainstad; Dec 2023), CC BY-NC 4.0. Optimized June 2026: guideline currency verified, ACS-2023 & perioperative-therapy updates added, pedagogy restructured, assessments added. No factual errors found — deck edit is append-only. Companion deck: Lung_Cancer_Screening_Optimized.pptx (original interactive board, unchanged + 3 appended slides).
objectives: Select the appropriate screening or diagnostic imaging based on age, tobacco use, and symptoms.
Identify the preferred biopsy site and procedural service that establish accurate staging while minimizing risk.
Once cancer is confirmed, obtain the studies needed to fully stage disease and determine eligibility for surgery and targeted therapy.
Compare the indications and side effects of the major oncologic treatment categories: chemotherapy, immunotherapy, and targeted therapy.

prework.q: Q1. Who qualifies for annual low-dose CT lung cancer screening?
prework.a: USPSTF 2021 (the basis for most US coverage): adults 50–80 years old with a ≥20 pack-year history who currently smoke or quit within the past 15 years ("A-50-80-20-15"). The ACS updated its guideline in 2023 to drop the years-since-quitting limit entirely — age 50–80, ≥20 pack-year, current or former smoker.

prework.q: Q2. A patient has several lesions on imaging. Which one do you biopsy, and why?
prework.a: The most advanced (farthest) lesion that can be sampled safely. A single distant lesion gives you both the diagnosis and the stage at once — a distant metastasis automatically makes it stage IV, which changes everything downstream.

prework.q: Q3. Why does molecular testing matter so much in NSCLC?
prework.a: EGFR, ALK, and ROS1 alterations (and PD-L1 expression) determine eligibility for targeted therapy and immunotherapy, which dramatically change both treatment and prognosis. About 50% of never-smokers with lung cancer have a targetable mutation — and adjuvant osimertinib is now standard for EGFR-mutated resected disease.

segments.label: Hook (2 min)
segments.html: "Where does lung cancer fall in the spectrum of cancer deaths?" Let them guess, then reveal: #1 cause of cancer death worldwide, #1 cancer in men, #2 in women. Follow with the stat that does the teaching work — stage I 5-year survival is ~73–90%, stage IV is ~0–10%. Everything in this talk exists to move patients leftward on that curve.

segments.label: Objective 1 — Screening & the diagnostic pathway
segments.html:
ASK: "What signs and symptoms make you worry about lung cancer?" — click "Symptoms?" to reveal. Early disease is usually asymptomatic; advanced disease brings weight loss, malaise, hemoptysis, and metastatic symptoms (bone pain, headache/seizure).

The board splits on a single question: symptomatic or not?

Asymptomatic → screening. Run the risk assessment. High risk = annual low-dose CT; low risk = reassess annually.

GuidelineWho gets annual LDCT
USPSTF 2021 (coverage basis)Age 50–80, ≥20 pack-year, current smoker OR quit ≤15 years ago
ACS 2023 (expanded)Age 50–80, ≥20 pack-year, current OR former smoker — no years-since-quitting limit

Other risk modifiers: radon, occupational/chemical exposures, secondhand smoke, family history (1st-degree relative), COPD/pulmonary fibrosis, prior cancer/treatment.

Symptomatic → CXR first. Not concerning → risk-assess (persistent symptoms or red flags like hemoptysis still warrant CT). Concerning → CT chest ± abd/pelvis (contrast preferred for nodal/structural detail).

ASK: "What on the CT makes you think malignancy?" Reveal: size >15 mm, irregular/spiculated margins, thick-walled cavitation, a solid component within ground glass, extrapulmonary/satellite lesions, and interval growth.
Pearl — tissue is the issue: imaging raises suspicion; only biopsy diagnoses. Prefer core or excisional biopsy over FNA when feasible — it preserves architecture and yields enough tissue for the molecular testing that now drives treatment.

segments.label: Objective 2 — Where & how to biopsy
segments.html:
ASK: "You see a lung mass and a distant lesion. Which do you biopsy?" — click "Which lesion would you biopsy?"

Biopsy the most advanced lesion that is safe to reach: one sample yields diagnosis and staging (a distant met = stage IV automatically). The procedural service follows the location:

SiteServiceApproach
Peripheral lungIR / SurgeryCT-guided biopsy
Endobronchial / near airwayPulmonologyBronchoscopy
Hepatic lesionIRCT/US-guided
Skin / soft tissue / lymph nodeGen surgery (excisional) or IR (core)Excisional or CT/US-guided core
Bone lesionOrtho / IRImage-guided or open

segments.label: Objective 3 — Staging the confirmed cancer
segments.html:
ASK: "Biopsy says cancer — what do you order before you talk treatment?" Build the staging list together.
DomainTestWhy
Distant diseaseCT chest/abd/pelvis with contrast or PET-CTIntrathoracic & extrathoracic (non-CNS) mets
CNSMRI brain with & without contrastPET is poor for brain (it always lights up)
NodesBronchoscopy ± mediastinoscopy*Clarifies the "N" in TNM
Surgical candidacyPFTs*Predicts tolerance of lobectomy/pneumonectomy
Labs / molecularCBC, BMP+Ca, LFTs; PD-L1; molecular testingPD-L1 → immunotherapy candidacy; EGFR/ALK/ROS1 → targeted therapy

*Typically when surgical resection is on the table; varies with initial staging. Metastatic disease usually isn't surgical, so PFTs are often unnecessary.

Pearl: ~50% of never-smokers with lung cancer carry a targetable mutation — molecular testing isn't optional, and it now changes early-stage management too (adjuvant osimertinib for resected EGFR-mutated NSCLC).

segments.label: SCLC vs NSCLC
segments.html:
SCLC (10–15%)NSCLC (80–85%)
CharacterSmoking-associated, aggressive, brain mets, paraneoplastic syndromesSubtypes (adeno, squamous, other); molecular markers direct therapy
StagingLimited (ipsilateral chest, single radiation field) vs ExtensiveStage I–IV by tumor / nodes / mets
TreatmentChemo + radiation; limited stage can occasionally be resected; PCI in limited stage w/o brain metsEarly: resection or definitive radiation; advanced: systemic therapy
Prognosis (5-yr)Limited ~10–13% (median 15–20 mo); extensive ~1–2% (median 8–13 mo)Stage I ~73–90% → Stage IV ~0–10%

segments.label: Objective 4 — Systemic therapy
segments.html:
ASK: "Neoadjuvant, adjuvant, palliative — what's the difference?" Reveal: neoadjuvant = before surgery (shrink/ease resection); adjuvant = after surgery (clear residual/micrometastatic disease); palliative = disease control without curative intent (≠ palliative care service).
ClassExamplesHallmark toxicity
ChemotherapyCarboplatin/cisplatin, pemetrexed, etoposide, taxanes, lurbinectedinN/V/diarrhea, cytopenias, neuropathy, nephropathy, mucositis
ImmunotherapyPembrolizumab, nivolumab ± ipilimumab, atezolizumab, durvalumabThe "-itises" — any organ, may appear late or after stopping
Targeted (TKIs)Osimertinib, erlotinib (EGFR); alectinib, crizotinib (ALK); crizotinib, entrectinib, lorlatinib (ROS1)Rash (EGFR), diarrhea, anemia/fatigue; rarely pneumonitis, QTc prolongation
2023–25 update: Immunotherapy has moved around surgery for resectable NSCLC — neoadjuvant nivolumab+chemo (CheckMate 816), perioperative pembrolizumab (KEYNOTE-671), adjuvant atezolizumab (IMpower010), perioperative durvalumab (AEGEAN). And adjuvant osimertinib (ADAURA) is standard for EGFR-mutated resected stage IB–IIIA disease, with an overall-survival benefit.

segments.label: Close (2 min)
segments.html: Hide the board. Ask the group to rebuild: who to screen, the symptomatic vs asymptomatic split, the biopsy rule, the staging set, and the three treatment classes — then show the take-home slide.

cases.title: Case 1 — Screening the asymptomatic patient
cases.stem: 47-year-old with HLD, HTN, CAD presents to establish care, no complaints. 20 pack-year history; strong family history of lung cancer (mother, father, paternal grandparent, all smokers).
cases.reveal: Smoking history / pack-years and a strong family history of lung cancer.

cases.title: Case 2 — Newly metastatic adenocarcinoma
cases.stem: The same patient returns 5 years later (age 52) with 3 months of cough, 2 weeks of hemoptysis, fatigue, 20-lb weight loss over 6 months, and persistent RUQ pain. Still smoking 1 ppd.
cases.reveal: For symptomatic patients, a CXR is the first step; add labs (CBC, BMP, LFTs) to evaluate for other causes. (CXR shows a 2.5 cm ill-defined nodule.)

mcqs.stem: A 58-year-old man quit smoking 12 years ago after a 30 pack-year history. He is asymptomatic. Which is the most appropriate recommendation?
opts: No screening — he quit more than 10 years ago
Low-dose CT only if he develops symptoms
mcqs.exp: B. He meets USPSTF 2021 criteria: age 50–80, ≥20 pack-years, and quit within the past 15 years (12 < 15). Annual low-dose CT is indicated (A is wrong — the cutoff is 15, not 10 years; the ACS 2023 guideline removes the quit-time limit altogether). Chest radiograph (C) is not a screening tool — the NLST showed LDCT, not CXR, reduces lung-cancer mortality. Waiting for symptoms (D) defeats the purpose of screening, which is to catch disease while it's curable.

mcqs.stem: A 64-year-old woman with biopsy-proven lung adenocarcinoma has a 3 cm lung mass and a solitary adrenal lesion that is the only possible site of distant disease. Which approach best establishes diagnosis and stage with one procedure?
opts: Bronchoscopic biopsy of the lung mass
Repeat the lung biopsy for more tissue
mcqs.exp: B. Biopsy the most advanced lesion that is safe to sample. If the adrenal lesion is malignant, the patient is stage IV — diagnosis and stage in a single procedure. Sampling the lung mass (A, D) confirms the diagnosis but leaves the critical staging question (is the adrenal a met?) unanswered. Sputum cytology (C) is insensitive and yields no staging or tissue for molecular testing.

mcqs.stem: After diagnosing NSCLC, which staging study is most important specifically for detecting central nervous system metastases?
opts: MRI brain with and without contrast
mcqs.exp: C. MRI brain with and without contrast is the test for CNS staging. PET-CT (A) is excellent for body staging but unreliable intracranially — the brain's high glucose uptake always lights up, masking metastases. CT C/A/P (B) assesses thoracic and extrathoracic non-CNS disease. EBUS/bronchoscopy (D) clarifies nodal (N) status, not brain involvement.

mcqs.stem: A never-smoker is diagnosed with metastatic lung adenocarcinoma. Which test most directly determines whether she is a candidate for targeted therapy?
opts: Molecular testing for EGFR, ALK, and ROS1
mcqs.exp: B. Targeted therapy is driven by molecular alterations — EGFR, ALK, ROS1 (and others). Roughly 50% of never-smokers harbor a targetable driver, so molecular testing is mandatory. PD-L1 (A) guides immunotherapy, a different class. Serum tumor markers (C) have no role in selecting lung-cancer therapy. PFTs (D) assess surgical candidacy, not drug selection.

mcqs.stem: A 60-year-old man has a fully resected stage II EGFR exon 19–deleted NSCLC. Which adjuvant therapy is now standard of care?
opts: No adjuvant therapy — surgery is sufficient
mcqs.exp: A. The ADAURA trial established adjuvant osimertinib for resected stage IB–IIIA EGFR-mutated NSCLC (FDA-approved Dec 2020; overall-survival benefit reported 2023). Pembrolizumab (B) has perioperative roles but isn't the answer for a driver-mutated tumor, where targeted therapy takes precedence. "Surgery alone" (C) is no longer standard for this group. Erlotinib (D) is an older EGFR TKI not used in this adjuvant setting; osimertinib is preferred (better CNS penetration and efficacy).

anki: Where does lung cancer rank as a cause of cancer death?
#1 cause of cancer-related death worldwide; #1 cancer in men, #2 in women. Most internists will diagnose or co-manage it.
USPSTF 2021 lung cancer screening criteria?

Annual low-dose CT for adults 50-80 yo with a >=20 pack-year history who currently smoke OR quit within the past 15 years (mnemonic A-50-80-20-15). Stop after 15 years smoke-free or once life expectancy/surgical candidacy is limited.
How did the ACS 2023 guideline change screening eligibility?

Age 50-80, >=20 pack-year, current OR former smoker -- it REMOVED the "quit within 15 years" requirement entirely (years since quitting no longer limits starting or stopping). Broadens eligibility to ~5 million more US adults.
Other (non-pack-year) risk factors for lung cancer?
Radon, occupational/chemical exposures, secondhand smoke; family history of lung cancer (1st-degree relative); personal history of COPD or pulmonary fibrosis; prior cancer/cancer treatment.
First imaging step for a SYMPTOMATIC patient with possible lung cancer?

A chest X-ray. If concerning -> CT chest +/- abd/pelvis (contrast preferred for nodes). If not concerning -> risk assessment; persistent symptoms or high risk (e.g., hemoptysis) still warrant CT despite a clear CXR.
CT features concerning for malignancy?
Size >15 mm, irregular/spiculated margins, thick-walled cavitation, solid component within ground glass, extrapulmonary/satellite lesions, and growth over time.
Which lesion should you biopsy, and why?
The most advanced (farthest) lesion that is safe to sample -- it gives BOTH diagnosis and staging at once (a distant metastasis automatically = stage IV).
Why prefer core/excisional biopsy over FNA?
Preserves tissue architecture and yields enough tissue for molecular/genetic testing (especially important for lymphoma; also enables EGFR/ALK/PD-L1 testing in lung cancer).
Staging workup after a lung cancer diagnosis?

CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast OR PET-CT; MRI brain with and without contrast (PET is poor for brain -- it always lights up); bronchoscopy +/- mediastinoscopy for nodes; PFTs if a surgical candidate; CBC, BMP+Ca, LFTs; PD-L1 and molecular testing.
Why are PFTs part of lung cancer staging?

To assess baseline lung function and predict tolerance of lobectomy/pneumonectomy in potential surgical candidates. Metastatic patients are usually not surgical candidates, so PFTs are often unnecessary.
SCLC -- key features?

10-15% of lung cancers; strongly smoking-associated; rapidly progressive/aggressive; high rate of brain metastases; paraneoplastic syndromes; initially chemo/radiation-sensitive but frequently recurs.
SCLC staging and treatment?

Limited stage = confined to ipsilateral chest, treatable in a single radiation field (limited stage can occasionally be resected). Extensive stage = beyond ipsilateral chest/distant mets. Mainstays: chemotherapy + radiation; prophylactic cranial irradiation in limited stage without brain mets.
SCLC prognosis (5-yr survival, median survival)?
Limited stage ~10-13% 5-yr survival (median 15-20 mo); extensive stage ~1-2% (median 8-13 mo).
NSCLC -- key features?
Most common lung cancer (80-85%); histologic subtypes (adenocarcinoma, squamous, other) direct treatment; molecular testing changes therapy. Staged I-IV by tumor size, nodes, metastases.
NSCLC treatment by stage?

Early stage: resection or definitive radiation (curative intent). Advancing stages: systemic therapy becomes standard. Incurable disease -> "palliative" intent (distinct from palliative care service).
NSCLC prognosis by stage (5-yr survival)?
Stage I ~73-90% vs Stage IV ~0-10% -- survival drops sharply with each stage, which is the rationale for screening.
Neoadjuvant vs adjuvant vs palliative therapy?
Neoadjuvant = before surgery to shrink the tumor/ease resection. Adjuvant = after resection to kill residual/micrometastatic disease. Palliative = control/shrink disease without curative intent.
Three major systemic therapy classes for lung cancer + a toxicity hallmark of each?

Chemotherapy (cisplatin/carboplatin, etoposide, pemetrexed, taxanes, lurbinectedin) -- N/V, cytopenias, neuropathy, nephropathy. Immunotherapy (pembrolizumab, nivolumab+/-ipilimumab, atezolizumab, durvalumab) -- the "-itises" (any organ, can be delayed). Targeted/TKIs (-tinib: osimertinib, alectinib, lorlatinib, entrectinib) -- rash (EGFR), diarrhea, rare pneumonitis/QTc prolongation.
Common targetable molecular alterations in NSCLC?

EGFR (osimertinib, erlotinib), ALK (alectinib, crizotinib), ROS1 (crizotinib, entrectinib, lorlatinib). ~50% of never-smokers with lung cancer harbor a targetable mutation -- it dramatically changes treatment and prognosis.
What changed in resectable NSCLC treatment since 2022-2023?

Immunotherapy moved around surgery: neoadjuvant nivolumab+chemo (CheckMate 816), perioperative pembrolizumab (KEYNOTE-671), adjuvant atezolizumab (IMpower010), perioperative durvalumab (AEGEAN). Adjuvant osimertinib is SOC for EGFR-mutated resected stage IB-IIIA (ADAURA).
lessons.dot:
#Lung nodule / suspected lung CA: * yo, * pack-yr, [current/former/never] smoker, quit * yr ago. presentation [screen-detected / incidental / symptomatic: cough/hemoptysis/wt loss/bone pain/HA]. lesion: * cm, [RUL/RML/RLL/LUL/LLL], features [spiculated, >15mm, thick-wall cavity, solid-in-GGO, growth, extrapulm] -- [LOW / HIGH] concern.
- screen elig: USPSTF [50-80 yo + >=20 py + current or quit <=15 yr] OR ACS 2023 [50-80 + >=20 py + current/former, no quit limit] -> annual LDCT *; if not elig -> reassess annually + cessation
- dx (symptomatic): CXR -> if concerning CT chest +/- A/P w/ contrast; *tissue is the issue* -> bx MOST ADVANCED safe lesion (dx + stage in 1); core/excisional > FNA (preserve tissue for molecular)
- bx route by site: peripheral lung [IR/surg, CT-guided] / endobronchial [pulm, bronch] / liver [IR] / node-soft tissue [gen surg excisional or IR core] / bone [ortho/IR]
- staging: CT C/A/P w/ contrast OR PET-CT + MRI brain w/wo + node eval [bronch +/- mediastinoscopy] + PFTs* if surgical candidate; labs CBC/CMP+Ca/LFTs
- molecular (NSCLC): PD-L1 % [immunoRx] + EGFR/ALK/ROS1 [targeted Rx]; ~50% never-smokers w/ driver -- DO NOT skip
- type/stage: [SCLC: limited vs extensive] / [NSCLC: I-IV]; tx intent [curative: resect/definitive RT] vs [systemic] vs [palliative]
- systemic [chemo / IO (-itises) / TKI -tinib]; resectable NSCLC consider periop IO (CheckMate816/KEYNOTE671); EGFR-mut resected -> adjuvant osimertinib (ADAURA)
- counseling: SMOKING CESSATION (highest-yield), shared decision re: screening, goals of care if advanced
- f/u: tumor board, [onc / IR / pulm / CT surg / rad onc] referrals, *
takehome: Screen the right people: annual low-dose CT for 50–80 yo with ≥20 pack-years (USPSTF requires current or quit ≤15 yr; ACS 2023 drops that limit). Asymptomatic → risk assessment; symptomatic → CXR first.
Tissue is the issue, and biopsy the most advanced lesion when safe — one sample gives diagnosis and staging (distant met = stage IV).
Stage fully before treating: CT C/A/P or PET-CT, MRI brain, node sampling, PFTs if a surgical candidate, plus PD-L1 and molecular testing.

Molecular markers and PD-L1 steer targeted therapy and immunotherapy — and now change early-stage care (adjuvant osimertinib). Early stage can be cured; survival falls sharply with each stage, which is why screening matters.

lessons.title: Management of Gout
lessons.subtitle: Facilitator-led interactive chalk talk · ~30 min (core) / 45 min (with cases) · IM attendings & senior residents teaching residents
objectives: Describe the presentation of acute gout and contrast it with acute septic arthritis.
Identify first-line treatments for an acute gout flare and pick the preferred agent by comorbidity.
Counsel patients on modifiable risk factors that reduce future flares.
State the indications to start urate-lowering therapy (ULT) plus prophylaxis in a patient presenting with acute gout.

prework.q: 1. A patient with a hot, swollen first toe has crystals on aspiration. Does that rule out septic arthritis?
prework.a: No. Crystals confirm crystal arthritis but do not exclude infection — gout and septic arthritis can coexist. If infection is suspected, send Gram stain/culture and treat empirically while awaiting results.

prework.q: 2. Name the four indications to start urate-lowering therapy (ACR 2020).
prework.a: ≥2 flares/year, tophi, or 1 flare + radiographic erosions; also consider after a single flare if CKD stage ≥3, serum urate >9 mg/dL, or urolithiasis.

prework.q: 3. Your patient has diabetes and a flare. Which flare drug do you avoid, and why?
prework.a: Systemic corticosteroids worsen glycemic control; prefer an NSAID or colchicine (if <48 h, renally dosed) — or an intra-articular steroid for monoarticular disease.

segments.label: Hook
segments.html: "Your 72-year-old diabetic comes in with a hot, swollen ankle overnight. The resident says 'classic gout, start prednisone.' Name one thing that could go very wrong if you skip a step — and one thing that could go wrong with the prednisone itself." Surface septic arthritis and hyperglycemia, then frame: the board walks Diagnosis → Acute Rx → Modifiable RFs → Urate-Lowering Rx → Takeaways → Cases.

segments.label: Segment 1 — Diagnosis & mimics
segments.html:
ASK — "What single bedside procedure separates gout from septic arthritis, and what do you send?" (Arthrocentesis: cell count, Gram stain/culture, crystals.)

Acute gout: abrupt warm, red, exquisitely painful joint, often the first MTP (~73% of flares); ±fever, ↑inflammatory markers, leukocytosis. Mimics: pseudogout (rhomboid CPP crystals, larger joints, knee >50%), hemarthrosis (sanguineous fluid), and septic arthritis (the surgical emergency).

Synovial WBCInterpretation
<2,000Non-inflammatory
2,000–75,000Inflammatory (gout typical, with MSU crystals)
>75,000High risk for septic arthritis
Pearl: the curves overlap — septic arthritis can be <75K after antibiotics, and gout can exceed 100K. Cover infection when the story fits even if crystals are present.

segments.label: Segment 2 — Acute flare therapy by comorbidity
segments.html:
ASK — "Same flare in three patients: a GI bleeder, a brittle diabetic, and someone on a statin with CKD. One drug each?" (Avoid NSAID in the bleeder, steroids in the diabetic, full-dose colchicine in the statin/CKD patient → pick the one that fits.)
AgentTypical doseAvoid / caution
NSAIDNaproxen 500 BID / indomethacin 50 TID ×5–7 d (all equivalent)GI bleed, renal disease, ischemic cardiac disease
SteroidPrednisone 40 mg ×5 d (extend 7–10 d if severe/tophaceous); triamcinolone IA 40/20/10 mg by joint sizeDiabetes (hyperglycemia), concurrent infection
Colchicine1.2 mg → 0.6 mg in 1 h → 0.6 mg QD/BID until resolvedOnly if <48 h; renally dose in CKD; statin myotoxicity, CYP3A4 inhibitors; diarrhea
IL-1 (anakinra)100 mg SQ daily ×3–5 d (off-label, cheaper than canakinumab)Active infection; use for polyarticular flare when others contraindicated; with rheumatology

segments.label: Segment 3 — Modifiable risk factors
segments.html:
Diet ↑risk: red meat, shellfish, beer & spirits, fructose drinks; dairy is protective. Meds ↑risk: loop/thiazide diuretics, aspirin, ACE-I, ARBs (except losartan), some TB drugs (ethambutol, pyrazinamide), niacin. Metabolic: HTN, diabetes, hyperlipidemia — treating them lowers risk; losartan and amlodipine are preferred BP agents (mild uricosuric effect).

Pearl: do NOT stop indicated low-dose aspirin to manage gout (ACR 2020) — the cardiovascular benefit outweighs the small flare risk.

segments.label: Segment 4 — Urate-lowering therapy
segments.html:
ASK — "Who actually needs a urate-lowering drug, and what's the urate number you're chasing?" (≥2 flares/yr, tophi, or 1 flare + erosions; target <6.)

Start ULT for ≥2 flares/yr, tophi, or 1 flare + erosions — also consider after one flare if CKD ≥3, urate >9, or urolithiasis. Allopurinol first-line (start 100 mg/day, ≤50 if CKD; titrate q2–4 wks; max ~800 mg). Treat-to-target urate <6 (<5 for tophi). Always pair with prophylaxis (low-dose colchicine, NSAID, or prednisone) for 3–6 months until the target dose is reached. Starting ULT during a flare does not worsen it; continue ULT through flares.

HLA-B*5801: allopurinol hypersensitivity syndrome (rash, fever, eosinophilia, ↑LFTs, renal failure) is far more likely with this allele — screen patients of Han Chinese, Korean, Thai, or African-American ancestry and avoid allopurinol if positive.
ASK — "Why not just start febuxostat for everyone?" (CARES boxed warning — increased CV and all-cause death; it's second-line.)

segments.label: Retrieval close
segments.html: Erase the board. Have learners rebuild from memory: the synovial WBC cutoff that worries you for septic arthritis, the flare drug to avoid in a diabetic, the four ULT indications, and the urate target. Finish with the take-home points.

lessons.casesIntro: Case 1. 72M with insulin-dependent DM2, HTN (on an ACE-I), HLD (on a statin); 1 day of a hot, swollen, painful right ankle, no trauma, no fever.

cases.title: Cases 1
cases.stem: Next diagnostic step — and why?
cases.reveal: X-ray the joint (fracture, erosions) and arthrocentesis to rule out septic arthritis — the can't-miss emergency. Aspirate: negatively birefringent needle-shaped crystals (gout); Gram stain/culture negative.

cases.title: Cases 2
cases.stem: Best way to manage his flare?
cases.reveal: Avoid steroids (diabetes) → short course of NSAID or colchicine. Review meds: switch the ACE-I to losartan (uricosuric, also treats his HTN).

cases.title: Cases 3
cases.stem: His gout risk factors?
cases.reveal: Non-modifiable: age, male sex. Modifiable: diabetes, HTN, hyperlipidemia, ACE-I use.

cases.title: Cases 4
cases.stem: Classify the presentation.
cases.reveal: Tophaceous gout with an acute polyarticular flare.

cases.title: Cases 5
cases.stem: Management approach?
cases.reveal: Flare: prednisone preferred (renal disease + HF). Start allopurinol (≥3 flares; if GFR <60, start 50 mg/day). Prophylaxis: renally-dosed colchicine or low-dose prednisone, continued through up-titration.

cases.title: Cases 6
cases.stem: Lab to check before allopurinol?
cases.reveal: HLA-B*5801 — elevated allopurinol-hypersensitivity risk in Han Chinese ancestry; if positive, don't use allopurinol.

cases.title: Cases 7
cases.stem: Goal urate?
cases.reveal: <6 mg/dL (consider <5 to dissolve tophi).

cases.title: Cases 8
cases.stem: Initial labs?
cases.reveal: CBC (WBC 20), CMP, ESR/CRP, ankle X-ray, blood cultures; serum urate (may be falsely low/normal/high in a flare — don't anchor on it).

cases.title: Cases 9
cases.stem: Next step?
cases.reveal: Arthrocentesis: synovial WBC 100,000, culture pending; polarized microscopy shows MSU crystals. The high count + fever + recent skin infection means you must treat for possible septic arthritis even with crystals present.

cases.title: Cases 10
cases.stem: Management?
cases.reveal: Consider ortho consult for washout/source control, start antibiotics, and avoid steroids during active infection — treat gout with NSAIDs/colchicine (or after washout) once infection is controlled.

mcqs.stem: A 66-year-old man with CKD stage 3 and a prior GI bleed presents with an acutely hot, swollen first MTP that began 12 hours ago. Aspirate: MSU crystals, Gram stain negative, WBC 22,000. Which is the best treatment for this flare?
opts: Colchicine 1.2 mg then 0.6 mg, renally dosed
mcqs.exp: B. Within 48 h, colchicine (renally dosed) is appropriate and avoids both the NSAID (GI bleed, CKD) and steroids. A is contraindicated by the bleed and CKD; C and E are urate-lowering drugs that do nothing for the acute flare and can trigger flares if started without prophylaxis; D — aspirin raises urate and isn't a flare treatment. (An intra-articular steroid would be another reasonable choice.)

mcqs.stem: A 58-year-old woman has had three gout flares this year. Serum urate is 8.2 mg/dL; renal function normal. After treating the current flare, which long-term plan best follows ACR 2020?
opts: No ULT; manage flares as they occur
Start allopurinol 100 mg daily, titrate q2–4 weeks to urate <6, with colchicine prophylaxis
mcqs.exp: B. ≥2 flares/year is a strong ULT indication; allopurinol is first-line, started low and titrated to target <6, with prophylaxis during titration. A ignores the indication; C — febuxostat is second-line (CARES boxed warning); D — high starting doses raise hypersensitivity risk and are not recommended; E rarely reaches target alone.

mcqs.stem: Before prescribing allopurinol to a 45-year-old man of Han Chinese ancestry, which test most reduces the risk of a life-threatening reaction?
mcqs.exp: A. HLA-B*5801 strongly predicts allopurinol hypersensitivity syndrome and is recommended before allopurinol in higher-prevalence ancestries (Han Chinese, Korean, Thai, African-American); avoid the drug if positive. G6PD relates to rasburicase/oxidant drugs; TPMT to thiopurines; D and E don't address the reaction risk.

mcqs.stem: A 70-year-old man with gout, hypertension, and known coronary disease needs urate-lowering therapy. Which choice is most appropriate?
opts: Allopurinol, titrated to target, as first-line
Febuxostat as first-line because it is more potent
No ULT because of his cardiac disease
mcqs.exp: A. Allopurinol is first-line for all patients including those with cardiac disease. B — febuxostat carries an FDA boxed warning for CV/all-cause death (CARES) and is second-line; C — cardiac disease is not a reason to withhold needed ULT; D — pegloticase is reserved for refractory tophaceous gout; E — probenecid is uricosuric and ineffective/contraindicated with reduced renal function and stones.

mcqs.stem: A 62-year-old man on chronic allopurinol presents with a single acutely inflamed knee, fever to 38.6°C, and a recent overlying skin infection. Aspirate: WBC 90,000 with MSU crystals; culture pending. What is the best next step?
opts: Treat empirically for septic arthritis (antibiotics ± washout) and avoid systemic steroids
Diagnose a gout flare based on the crystals and give prednisone
mcqs.exp: A. Crystals don't exclude infection; with WBC 90K, fever, and adjacent cellulitis, treat as septic arthritis (cover and consider washout) and avoid steroids during active infection. B anchors on crystals and risks steroids in infection; C — ULT should be continued through flares, not stopped; D — a urate-lowering drug doesn't treat the acute problem; E misses a joint-space emergency.

anki: What is the classic presentation of an acute gout flare?
Abrupt onset of a warm, red, excruciatingly painful, swollen peripheral joint, sometimes with fever; up to 90% of gout patients involve the first MTP at some point (~73% of flares are at the MTP).
Why must arthrocentesis be done when gout is suspected with signs of infection?
Septic arthritis is a surgical emergency (rapid joint destruction) and can mimic — or coexist with — gout; crystals do NOT exclude infection.
Synovial fluid cell count ranges for non-inflammatory, inflammatory, and septic?
<2,000 = non-inflammatory; 2,000–75,000 = inflammatory; >75,000 = high risk for septic (overlaps exist — gout can exceed 100K; septic can be lower after antibiotics).
Crystal appearance of gout vs pseudogout?
Gout = needle-shaped, negatively birefringent monosodium urate (yellow when parallel to the red compensator). Pseudogout (CPPD) = rhomboid, weakly positively birefringent calcium pyrophosphate.
Three first-line options for an acute gout flare?
NSAIDs, corticosteroids (systemic or intra-articular), or colchicine; choose by comorbidities.
When are NSAIDs a poor choice for a gout flare?
History of GI bleeding, renal disease, or ischemic cardiac disease.
When should colchicine be used for a flare, and key cautions?
Only effective within ~48 h of onset; renally dose in CKD; main side effect is diarrhea; caution with statins (myotoxicity) and CYP3A4 inhibitors.
What agent treats a gout flare when NSAIDs, steroids, and colchicine are all contraindicated?
Anakinra (IL-1 receptor antagonist), off-label but far cheaper than canakinumab; with rheumatology referral; avoid in active infection.
Common medication precipitants of gout?
Loop and thiazide diuretics, aspirin, ACE inhibitors, ARBs (except losartan), ethambutol, pyrazinamide, niacin.
Which antihypertensives lower gout risk?
Losartan (uricosuric via URAT1 inhibition) and amlodipine — reasonable preferential BP agents in gout patients.
Dietary contributors to gout vs protective?
Increase risk: red meat, shellfish, alcohol (beer & spirits), fructose-rich drinks. Protective: higher dairy intake.
Indications to start urate-lowering therapy (ACR 2020)?
>=2 flares/year, tophi, or 1 flare + radiographic erosions; also consider after 1 flare if CKD stage >=3, serum urate >9 mg/dL, or urolithiasis.
First-line urate-lowering therapy and the serum urate target?
Allopurinol (a xanthine oxidase inhibitor), treat-to-target serum urate <6 mg/dL (often <5 to dissolve tophi).
How is allopurinol started and titrated?
Start 100 mg daily (≤50 mg/day if CKD), up-titrate q2–4 weeks to target urate <6; max ~800 mg/day.
Why is prophylaxis given when starting ULT, and for how long?
Starting ULT without prophylaxis precipitates flares as urate falls; give low-dose colchicine, NSAID, or prednisone for 3–6 months until the target ULT dose is reached.
What is allopurinol hypersensitivity syndrome and who is at risk?

Cutaneous reaction, fever, eosinophilia, abnormal LFTs, and renal failure; risk markedly higher with HLA-B*5801 (highest in Han Chinese, Korean, Thai ~7.4%, and African American ~3.8%) — screen these groups and avoid allopurinol if positive.
Why is febuxostat second-line?
FDA boxed warning for increased cardiovascular and all-cause death (CARES, NEJM 2018); reserve for allopurinol intolerance or failure.
Does starting ULT during an acute flare worsen it, and should chronic ULT be stopped during a flare?
No — starting ULT during a flare does not worsen or prolong it; patients already on ULT should continue it through flares.
lessons.dot:
#Gout: acute flare * joint(s) [mono/poly], onset * [<48h?], [1st MTP/midfoot/ankle/knee/MCP], h/o gout [y/n], on ULT [y/n]
r/o septic joint — arthrocentesis BEFORE steroids [crystals do NOT excl infection]
  fluid: WBC * [<2K non-infl | 2-75K infl/gout | >75K septic risk], gram stain *, cx *, crystals * [MSU neg-biref needle / CPP rhomboid wk-pos]
  also: XR joint [fx/erosions/chondrocalc], CBC/BMP/ESR/CRP, blood cx if febrile; urate * [unreliable in flare]
acute Rx (by comorbidity):
  NSAID [naproxen 500 BID / indomethacin 50 TID x5-7d] — AVOID GI bleed/CKD/ischemic CAD
  steroid [prednisone 40 mg x5d; IA triamcinolone 40/20/10 by joint] — AVOID DM, active infxn
  colchicine [1.2 then 0.6 mg x1, then 0.6 QD-BID] — only if <48h; renal dose; statin/CYP3A4 caution
  anakinra 100 mg SQ daily x3-5d — if all above contraindicated, w/ rheum; AVOID active infxn
modifiable RF: d/c offending med if able [thiazide/loop, ACE-I → losartan*, niacin, ethambutol/PZA]; do NOT stop indicated ASA; diet [↓red meat/shellfish/beer-spirits/fructose, ↑dairy]; treat HTN/DM/HLD
ULT (start if): >=2 flares/yr | tophi | 1 flare + erosions | [consider: 1 flare + CKD>=3 / urate>9 / urolithiasis]
  allopurinol 1st-line: start 100 mg QD [50 if CKD], titrate q2-4wk; goal urate <6* [<5 if tophi]; max ~800
  screen HLA-B*5801 if [Han Chinese/Korean/Thai/African-American] → if + do NOT use allopurinol
  febuxostat 2nd-line ONLY [CARES boxed warning CV/all-cause death]
  ppx while titrating x3-6mo: low-dose colchicine 0.6 QD-BID / NSAID / prednisone
  starting ULT during flare OK; continue chronic ULT through flares
f/u: recheck urate * wk; rheum referral if [refractory / IL-1 needed / dx unclear]
takehome: Tap the joint — arthrocentesis rules out septic arthritis whenever infection is possible or the picture is ambiguous; crystals don't exclude infection.
Treat flares with NSAIDs, colchicine (if <48 h), or steroids — matched to comorbidities; anakinra (with rheumatology) if all are contraindicated.
Start ULT (allopurinol first-line) for ≥2 flares/yr, 1 flare + erosions, or tophi — also consider after 1 flare with CKD ≥3, urate >9, or urolithiasis.
Treat-to-target urate <6, up-titrating q2–4 weeks, and keep flare prophylaxis running until the target dose is reached. Screen HLA-B*5801 before allopurinol in high-risk ancestry.

extras.label: Currency Notes checked June 2026
extras.html:
The original is recent (Oct 2024) and built directly on the 2020 ACR gout guideline, which remains the current standard — no update slide was needed for standing content. Verified:

  • ACR 2020 gout guideline (FitzGerald, Arthritis Care Res 2020) — allopurinol first-line for all including CKD, start-low-and-titrate, treat-to-target urate <6, prophylaxis during titration, the four ULT indications, HLA-B*5801 screening, and "don't stop indicated low-dose aspirin." No newer ACR gout guideline supersedes it as of June 2026.
  • Febuxostat CV safety — the deck's boxed-warning teaching reflects CARES (White, NEJM 2018): similar overall MACE but higher CV and all-cause death vs allopurinol; febuxostat stays second-line.
  • Newer agents — dotinurad, a selective URAT1 reabsorption inhibitor, is approved in Japan and remains investigational in the US (Phase 1 US data 2024–25); not yet a US prescribing option. Pegloticase remains the choice for refractory tophaceous gout.

lessons.title: Medical Abortion in the First Trimester
objectives: Identify relative and absolute contraindications to medication abortion.
Counsel patients on options for medication abortion and correct administration of the medications.
Identify adverse effects of medication abortion and when patients should be referred for emergency care or procedural abortion.
Locate your local laws and institutional guidelines regarding prescription of medication abortion.

prework.q: 1. What are the two medications used for first-trimester medication abortion, and what does each one do?
prework.a: Mifepristone — a progesterone-receptor modulator that promotes placental separation, cervical dilation, and sensitizes the myometrium to prostaglandins. Misoprostol — a prostaglandin E1 analogue that softens the cervix and causes uterine contractions. The combination is 95–98% effective up to 11 weeks.

prework.q: 2. Up to what gestational age is medication abortion used, and how is gestational age usually established?
prework.a: Up to 11 weeks (77 days); use beyond 10 weeks is off-label. Gestational age is estimated from the first day of the last menstrual period (LMP). Ultrasound is only needed if the LMP is unknown, the patient conceived on hormonal contraception, or ectopic pregnancy is suspected.

prework.q: 3. Name two absolute contraindications to any medication abortion.
prework.a: Ectopic pregnancy, IUD in place, known coagulopathy, or anticoagulant therapy (any two). Medication-specific: chronic corticosteroids/adrenal failure (mifepristone), porphyria (misoprostol).

segments.label: 0:00–2:00 — Hook (Impact board)
segments.html:
Open on the Impact slide. Since Dobbs (June 2022), hundreds of thousands of patients per year lost local access to abortion care; lack of access disproportionately affects people of color and lower-income patients. Adults with uteruses are half of an internist's panel — and competent prescribing here requires exactly two medications. Reveal the three numbers on the board: 250k patients/year without access, 27 states with mandatory waiting periods, 11 gestational weeks of safety/efficacy.

ASK: "Before we start — who do you think can legally prescribe medication abortion where we practice, and where would you look that up?" (Answer: depends on state; Guttmacher Institute keeps the current map. Surface this up front because every later decision sits inside it.)

segments.label: 2:00–10:00 — Objective 1: Stepwise approach & contraindications (Approach board)
segments.html:
Walk the four steps on the board, clicking to reveal each:

StepContentKey details
1. Confirm pregnancy & EGAUrine hCG + EGA from first day of LMPConsidered up to 77 d (11 wk); >10 wk is off-label. Ultrasound only if LMP unknown, conceived on hormonal contraception, or ectopic risk (prior ectopic, tubal surgery/pathology, IUD in place, IVF conception)
2. Choose regimenMifepristone + misoprostol preferredCombination 95–98% effective; misoprostol alone 76–88% (use when mifepristone unavailable or contraindicated)
3. Screen contraindicationsSee table belowHgb is not routinely needed — transfusion rate <0.1%
4. Obtain consentBenefits, risks, alternatives; prescriber/patient agreements; after-care instructionsReview referral pathway for aspiration or transfusion before prescribing
ASK: "Your patient is sure of her LMP and conceived off contraception. Does she need an ultrasound before medication abortion?" (No — ultrasound is not required when the patient is certain of LMP and was not on hormonal contraception; reserve it for unknown dates or ectopic risk.)
Contraindications — any regimenMifepristone-specificMisoprostol-specific
Ectopic pregnancy · IUD in place (remove first) · known coagulopathy · anticoagulant therapy · anemia Hgb <9.5 g/dL (relative — closer follow-up)Chronic corticosteroids or adrenal failure (mifepristone is an antiglucocorticoid — can precipitate adrenal crisis)Porphyria; avoid simultaneous magnesium-containing supplements (worse GI effects)
Pearl: Counsel at consent: no effect on future fertility or pregnancy outcomes; >90% of patients are confident in their decision; there is no reversal agent, and because misoprostol is teratogenic, an incomplete abortion should be completed by uterine aspiration. Rh testing is no longer recommended <12 weeks — even for known Rh-negative patients (Society of Family Planning 2022).
ASK: "A patient with uncontrolled SLE wants to continue her pregnancy. What do you offer?" (Referral to maternal-fetal medicine — conditions like heart failure, cancer, and uncontrolled lupus raise pregnancy morbidity/mortality; the CDC US-MEC site lists conditions associated with adverse pregnancy outcomes.)

segments.label: 10:00–15:00 — Objective 2: Medications (Medications board)
segments.html:
ScenarioRegimenEfficacy
Mifepristone availableMifepristone 200 mg PO ×1, then misoprostol 800 mcg buccal or vaginal 24–48 h later. If 9–11 wk: second misoprostol dose 3–6 h after the first. Vaginal route can be given as early as 6 h after mifepristone.95–98% up to 11 wk
Mifepristone unavailable / contraindicatedMisoprostol 800 mcg q3h × 3 doses (vaginal route has fewer GI effects)~76–88% (reported up to 93% in meta-analysis)
Pearl — two timing rules patients must hear: (1) wait at least 24 h after mifepristone before misoprostol for optimal efficacy; (2) if the patient vomits <30 min after mifepristone, the dose must be repeated.

segments.label: 15:00–20:00 — Objective 3: Follow-up, side effects, complications (Follow-up & Side Effects boards)
segments.html:
ASK: "What follow-up is needed at 1–2 weeks? At 4 weeks?" (click to reveal)

1–2 wk: all patients must be offered phone or in-person follow-up within 14 days (NAF 2020) — they may opt for self-assessment. Screen for ongoing pregnancy symptoms (breast tenderness, fatigue, morning sickness), no passage of clot/POC, ongoing bleeding. Optional pelvic US — an empty gestational sac confirms success; there is no endometrial-thickness cutoff, so don't let an "RPOC" read trigger reflex re-intervention — route concerns to family planning. 4 wk: urine pregnancy test (home or clinic) — not before 4 weeks (false positives from residual hCG). Still symptomatic and <11 wk EGA? One more vaginal misoprostol dose is an option before aspiration.

ASK: "What should your patient expect — and what should send her to the ED?" (click each reveal)
Expected effectsComplications (rare)
Cramping + bleeding heavier than menses — starts ~2.5–4 h after misoprostol, lasts ~1 h (up to 24 h)
GI: nausea 43–46%, vomiting 23–40%, diarrhea 23–35%
Fever/flushing/chills 32–69%, malaise		Prolonged/heavy bleeding (~1%): >2 pads/h × 2 consecutive h → ED; transfusion <0.1%
Incomplete/failed abortion (5–7% combo): risk ↑ >8 wk (57 d); misoprostol-alone >7%, up to 22% → uterine aspiration
Intrauterine infection (1.2%): pelvic pain >24 h, abnormal discharge, fevers/chills → immediate care

segments.label: 20:00–45:00 — Cases (below), then retrieval close
segments.html: Retrieval close: board markers down — have learners rebuild from memory: the 4 steps, the two regimens with doses, three absolute contraindications, the bleeding threshold for the ED, and when a urine pregnancy test is interpretable. Then run the post-test QR quiz and compare with pre-test.

lessons.casesLabel: Cases (from the original board — answers click-to-reveal)

cases.title: Case 1 — 30-year-old transgender man (they/them) with a uterus, missed period, LMP 6 weeks ago, no hormonal contraception
cases.reveal: Confirm pregnancy with urine hCG. (It returns positive.)

cases.title: Case 2 — 21-year-old woman with SLE on prednisone 5 mg daily; urine hCG positive; wants medication abortion; unclear LMP (9–11 wk ago, irregular periods)
cases.reveal: SLE, insulin-dependent diabetes with complications, uncontrolled hypertension, heart disease (valvular/ischemic/peripartum), bariatric surgery within 2 years, solid-organ transplant within 2 years (see CDC US-MEC for the full list). Offer MFM referral if she chooses to continue a pregnancy.

mcqs.stem: A 27-year-old healthy woman requests medication abortion. She is certain her LMP was 7 weeks ago and conceived off contraception. Urine hCG is positive. Which management is most appropriate?
opts: Mifepristone 200 mg PO, then misoprostol 800 mcg buccally 24–48 h later
Mifepristone 200 mg PO and misoprostol 800 mcg taken simultaneously
mcqs.exp: B. Combination mifepristone → misoprostol 24–48 h later is the recommended regimen (95–98% effective ≤11 wk). A — ultrasound is unnecessary with a certain LMP, no hormonal contraception, and no ectopic risk factors. C — misoprostol-alone is reserved for mifepristone contraindication/unavailability (less effective). D — efficacy requires ≥24 h between the drugs. E — medication abortion is appropriate at 7 wk; procedural abortion is an alternative, not the only option.

mcqs.stem: A 24-year-old woman with adrenal insufficiency on chronic hydrocortisone requests medication abortion at 8 weeks EGA. Why is the standard combination regimen contraindicated?
opts: Misoprostol induces acute porphyria attacks in adrenal insufficiency
Mifepristone blocks glucocorticoid receptors and can precipitate adrenal crisis
Misoprostol is ineffective beyond 6 weeks gestation
Mifepristone requires intact adrenal function for hepatic activation
The combination causes refractory hypertension with steroid use
mcqs.exp: B. Mifepristone is a synthetic steroid that at higher doses antagonizes cortisol at the glucocorticoid receptor — it can negate exogenous steroids and precipitate adrenal insufficiency (monitor for hypokalemia and hypotension if ever used). She should receive misoprostol-only or procedural abortion. A — porphyria is a separate misoprostol-specific caution, unrelated to adrenal disease. C — misoprostol works through 11 weeks. D — invented mechanism. E — hypotension, not hypertension, is the adrenal-crisis concern.

mcqs.stem: Ten days after mifepristone/misoprostol at 6 weeks EGA, a patient reports persistent breast tenderness and morning sickness. Which next step is most appropriate?
opts: Serum quantitative hCG today, repeat in 48 h
One additional dose of vaginal misoprostol
Immediate uterine aspiration without further evaluation
mcqs.exp: C. With ongoing pregnancy symptoms and EGA still <11 wk, an additional vaginal misoprostol dose is appropriate (alternatively pelvic US — an empty sac confirms success — or referral for aspiration). A — urine hCG is uninterpretable before 4 weeks post-abortion (residual hCG). B — serial quantitative hCG is not the recommended follow-up pathway here. D — aspiration is an option but not obligatory before a repeat misoprostol dose at <11 wk. E — persistent pregnancy symptoms at 1–2 wk suggest possible ongoing pregnancy and need action.

mcqs.stem: Six hours after misoprostol, a patient reports heavy bleeding soaking 3 pads per hour for the past 2 hours and feels lightheaded. What do you advise?
opts: Take a second dose of misoprostol now
Oral iron and outpatient follow-up in 1 week
Present to the emergency department now
Urine pregnancy test to assess completion
mcqs.exp: D. Bleeding >2 pads/h for 2 consecutive hours defines prolonged/heavy bleeding (~1% of patients) — ED evaluation, possible aspiration; <0.1% need transfusion. Lightheadedness raises concern for hemodynamically significant loss. A — expected bleeding is heavier than menses but below this threshold. B — more misoprostol doesn't treat hemorrhage. C — too slow for active heavy bleeding. E — irrelevant in an emergency and uninterpretable this early.

mcqs.stem: A 31-year-old Rh-negative woman undergoes medication abortion at 8 weeks EGA. Regarding Rh testing and anti-D immune globulin, which is correct?
opts: Rh testing and anti-D are required for all abortions
Anti-D is needed only if bleeding lasts >24 hours
Neither Rh testing nor anti-D is recommended for abortion under 12 weeks
Rh testing is needed, but anti-D can be deferred to 4-week follow-up
Anti-D should be given only with misoprostol-alone regimens
mcqs.exp: C. Per the Society of Family Planning (2022) consensus, Rh testing is no longer recommended before abortion <12 weeks — even for known Rh-negative patients — because maternal-fetal blood mixing at this gestational age is insufficient to cause alloimmunization. A — outdated practice. B/D/E — invented conditions; the recommendation is independent of bleeding duration, follow-up timing, and regimen.

anki: Up to what gestational age is medication abortion used?
11 weeks (77 days); beyond 10 weeks is off-label
Standard combination regimen for medication abortion?
Mifepristone 200 mg PO x1, then misoprostol 800 mcg buccal or vaginal 24-48 h later
Efficacy: mifepristone+misoprostol vs misoprostol alone?
Combination 95-98%; misoprostol alone ~76-88% (up to 93% in meta-analysis)
Misoprostol-only regimen when mifepristone is unavailable?
Misoprostol 800 mcg q3h x 3 doses (vaginal route has fewer GI side effects)
When is a second misoprostol dose required in the combination regimen?
EGA 9-11 weeks - second 800 mcg dose 3-6 h after the first
Mechanism of mifepristone?
Progesterone-receptor modulator: placental separation, cervical dilation, sensitizes myometrium to prostaglandins
Mechanism of misoprostol?
Prostaglandin E1 analogue: cervical softening + uterine contractions
Absolute contraindications to any medication abortion?
Ectopic pregnancy, IUD in place, known coagulopathy, anticoagulant therapy
Why is mifepristone contraindicated with chronic corticosteroids or adrenal failure?
It is an antiglucocorticoid - blocks cortisol receptors, can negate exogenous steroids and precipitate adrenal insufficiency
Misoprostol-specific cautions?
Porphyria; avoid simultaneous magnesium-containing supplements (worse GI effects)
If the patient vomits within 30 min of mifepristone?
Repeat the dose
When is ultrasound needed before medication abortion?
Unknown LMP, conception on hormonal contraception, or ectopic risk (prior ectopic, tubal surgery/pathology, IUD, IVF)
Definition of prolonged/heavy bleeding after medication abortion requiring ED care?
>2 pads/h for 2 consecutive hours (~1% incidence; <0.1% transfused)
Earliest interpretable urine pregnancy test after medication abortion?
4 weeks post-abortion (residual hCG causes false positives earlier)
Is Rh testing recommended before abortion <12 weeks?
No - not even for known Rh-negative patients (Society of Family Planning 2022)
Ultrasound finding that confirms completed medication abortion?
Absent gestational sac (no endometrial-thickness cutoff; do not reflexively re-intervene for "RPOC" reads)
lessons.dot:
#Medication abortion (first trimester): desired termination of intrauterine pregnancy, EGA ** wk by [LMP * / US *] — UCG + confirmed
state law/waiting period: [reviewed — * / waiting period * h] (Guttmacher checked **date); consent + prescriber/pt agreements signed, after-care instructions given
US only if: [LMP unknown / conceived on hormonal contraception / ectopic risk: prior ectopic, tubal surg/path, IUD, IVF]
contraindications screened: [ectopic / IUD in place / coagulopathy / on AC] = none; mife-specific [chronic steroids / adrenal failure] = *; miso-specific [porphyria] = *; Hgb <9.5 (relative) = *
regimen: [mifepristone 200 mg PO x1 → misoprostol 800 mcg buccal/vaginal 24-48 h later (95-98% eff; 2nd miso dose 3-6 h after 1st if EGA 9-11 wk) / miso-alone 800 mcg q3h x3 if mife CI or unavailable (76-88% eff)]
counseled: re-dose mife if vomits <30 min; expected cramping + bleeding > menses ~2.5-4 h after miso lasting ~1 h (up to 24 h), N/V/D, fever/chills <24 h; no Mg-containing supplements w/ miso
seek care: bleeding >2 pads/h x2 h [ED *] / pelvic pain >24 h, abnl discharge, fever >24 h → eval for infection (1.2%)
f/u: offered w/in 14 d [phone/in-person *]; ongoing preg sx at 1-2 wk + <11 wk EGA → [repeat vaginal miso x1 / pelvic US (empty sac = success) / refer aspiration]; UCG no earlier than 4 wk
no Rh testing <12 wk (SFP 2022); no effect on future fertility; incomplete abortion (5-7%) → aspiration (miso teratogenic); contraception: all methods day 1 except IUD/sterilization (ACOG)
takehome: Before prescribing: confirm pregnancy, estimate gestational age from LMP (± US), screen contraindications, and check whether your state has a mandatory waiting period.
State and federal rules around abortion remain in active flux (see the 2023–26 update above) — always confirm your local law and the current federal status before prescribing.

Medication abortion can be considered up to 11 weeks. Mifepristone + misoprostol combination therapy is more effective than misoprostol alone; misoprostol-alone (800 mcg q3h ×3) is the fallback when mifepristone is contraindicated or unavailable.

extras.label: Access & Regulatory Update (2023–2026) added
extras.html:
The clinical teaching above is unchanged. The access landscape has moved since the June 2023 original:

  • Jan 2023 — FDA REMS modification: the in-person dispensing requirement was removed; certified retail and mail-order pharmacies may dispense mifepristone with a prescription from a certified prescriber. CVS and Walgreens completed certification and began dispensing in select states in March 2024.
  • Jun 13, 2024 — FDA v. Alliance for Hippocratic Medicine: the U.S. Supreme Court unanimously held the challengers lacked standing — FDA approval and the REMS changes (2016, 2021–23) remain in effect.
  • May 2026 — Louisiana v. FDA: a Fifth Circuit panel (May 1, 2026) ordered a return to in-person-only dispensing; on May 14, 2026 the Supreme Court stayed that order (Danco Laboratories v. Louisiana), preserving telehealth prescribing and mail/pharmacy dispensing while the litigation continues. A merits ruling is still pending — check the current status and your state law before prescribing.
Teaching tie-in: this is exactly why Objective 4 ("locate your local laws") is a standing objective, not a footnote. The Guttmacher Institute tracker and your institution's policy are the two sources to check the week you give this talk.

lessons.title: Menopause
objectives: Diagnose menopause and the menopausal transition from characteristic clinical features, and recognize when additional evaluation is warranted.
Identify evidence-based hormonal and nonhormonal treatments for menopausal symptoms.
Counsel patients on the risks and benefits of hormone therapy and on appropriate treatment duration.

prework.q: 1. A 52-year-old whose last period was 14 months ago has hot flashes. What lab work-up is needed to "confirm" menopause?
prework.a: None — menopause is a clinical diagnosis (≥12 months of amenorrhea at a typical age). FSH is a poor single marker. Testing is reserved for women <45 or with atypical features.

prework.q: 2. Who is an appropriate candidate for systemic menopausal hormone therapy?
prework.a: A woman with moderate-to-severe hot flashes who is <60 years old OR within 10 years of menopause, without contraindications (breast cancer, prior VTE/stroke/CHD, active liver disease, unexplained bleeding, high-risk endometrial cancer). Add progesterone whenever the uterus is intact.

prework.q: 3. Name two nonhormonal medication options for hot flashes.
prework.a: SSRIs/SNRIs (venlafaxine and escitalopram best studied; paroxetine is the only FDA-approved one), gabapentin/pregabalin, and the newer NK-receptor antagonists fezolinetant and elinzanetant. (Avoid fluoxetine and sertraline for this indication.)

segments.label: Hook (2 min)
segments.html: "A 50-year-old asks for 'the menopause blood test' and wants to know if hormones will give her breast cancer. What do you tell her about the test — and where does the breast-cancer fear come from?" (Surfaces by the end: usually no test needed, and the fear traces to the WHI's older population.)

segments.label: Objective 1 — Definitions & Diagnosis (5 min)
segments.html:
ASK: "Define perimenopause vs. menopause." Reveal each definition.
TermDefinitionTiming
Menopausal transition (perimenopause)Irregular menses (changed interval) + hormonal symptoms (hot flashes, mood)~4 yr before LMP
Menopause12 consecutive months of amenorrhea (retrospective)Mean age ~51
ASK: "When is testing indicated?" Reveal the clinical-diagnosis button.

No testing in women >45 with classic symptoms. For symptoms <45, work up (hCG, TSH, prolactin ± FSH); <40 = full amenorrhea/primary-ovarian-insufficiency work-up (see the AUB talk).

Pearl: A single FSH is unreliable — it swings during the transition. In the SWAN study, irregular menses predicted menopause better than FSH.

segments.label: Objective 2 — Symptom Management (8 min)
segments.html:
ASK: "A perimenopausal patient has heavy bleeding — what must you do first?" (Rule out malignancy: hCG, pelvic exam, pelvic ultrasound ± endometrial biopsy.)
SymptomFirst-line management
Hot flashes (mild)Lifestyle: avoid heat/EtOH/caffeine/cigarettes; fan; layers; weight loss; exercise
Hot flashes (mod–severe)MHT (most effective); nonhormonal SSRI/SNRI, gabapentin; NK antagonists (fezolinetant, elinzanetant)
Mood / sleepAs for hot flashes; SSRI preferred if mood predominates
GSM (dryness, dyspareunia, recurrent UTIs)Moisturizers/lubricants; vaginal estrogen (most effective, minimal systemic absorption)
Menstrual irregularityMHT; IUD or combined OCP until menopause
Pearl: Avoid fluoxetine and sertraline for hot flashes (no demonstrated benefit). Don't forget prevention — estrogen loss raises atherosclerosis and osteoporosis risk (lifestyle ± statin by ASCVD risk; calcium/vitamin D/exercise).

segments.label: Objective 3 — Hormone Therapy & Counseling (8 min)
segments.html:
ASK: "Who is a candidate for systemic MHT, and for how long?" Reveal the candidate and duration buttons.

Candidate: moderate-to-severe hot flashes, <60 yr OR <10 yr post-menopause. Duration: ~5–10 yr, re-evaluate yearly, stop by ~60. Add progesterone whenever the uterus is intact.

ASK: "What formulation would you pick for a patient with migraine or moderate ASCVD risk?" (Transdermal estrogen — lower VTE/stroke risk than oral.)

The WHI (2002) showed excess CHD, stroke, VTE, and breast cancer — but the mean age was 63, so it overstated risk for the younger, symptomatic women we typically treat. Use of the risk/benefit chart (per 1000 women, ages 50–59, 5 years): estrogen-alone is more favorable than estrogen+progesterone; average absolute risks are small (~3 additional cases/1000 over 5 years).

Pearl (newer context): The 2022 Menopause Society (NAMS) statement reaffirms that HT benefits outweigh risks for healthy symptomatic women <60 / <10 yr post-menopause. New nonhormonal NK-receptor antagonists — fezolinetant (Veozah, FDA 2023; monitor LFTs) and elinzanetant (Lynkuet, FDA 2025) — expand options for those who can't or won't take hormones.

segments.label: Retrieval Close (2 min)
segments.html: Close the board and have learners rebuild the three take-home points and the MHT candidacy rule from memory.

cases.title: Cases (from the original board) 1
cases.stem: Case 1 — 45-year-old, 3 months of amenorrhea, fatigue, hot flashes.
cases.reveal: First steps: pregnancy test (women in their 40s are a high-risk group for unintended pregnancy); consider TSH and prolactin (see AUB talk). If perimenopause is suspected, the most effective hot-flash medications are SSRI/SNRI (venlafaxine, escitalopram) and gabapentin/pregabalin.

cases.title: Cases (from the original board) 2
cases.stem: Case 2 — 50-year-old, heavy prolonged bleeding (10–18 days, soaking pads q1–2h).
cases.reveal: Evaluate menorrhagia: pelvic exam, transvaginal ultrasound ± endometrial biopsy to rule out endometrial cancer; consider FSH/prolactin/TSH. If other causes are negative, manage with a progesterone IUD or OCPs. If sexually active, counsel on contraception (IUD ok to age 55, OCPs to age 51).

cases.title: Cases (from the original board) 3
cases.stem: Case 3 — 49-year-old with DM, HTN, PAD, tobacco use, asking about MHT for hot flashes.
cases.reveal: MHT is contraindicated (PAD/established vascular disease; HTN + DM + tobacco together are high-risk). Combined OCP contraindications in perimenopause include CVD risk factors, prior VTE, migraine with aura, and breast cancer. Use nonhormonal options.

cases.title: Cases (from the original board) 4
cases.stem: Case 4 — 48-year-old with migraine with aura, severe hot flashes.
cases.reveal: If MHT is used, choose transdermal estrogen (lower stroke risk than oral) + progesterone (uterus intact). Counsel: slight ↑ breast cancer risk; slight ↓ fracture and all-cause mortality.

cases.title: Cases (from the original board) 5
cases.stem: Case 5 — 50-year-old post-hysterectomy (TAH), refractory hot flashes, ASCVD risk 7.5%.
cases.reveal: Transdermal estrogen (moderate ASCVD risk); no progesterone needed (no uterus). Estrogen-alone has a favorable profile: slight ↓ CAD, CVA, breast cancer, fracture, and mortality.

cases.title: Cases (from the original board) 6
cases.stem: Case 6 — 52-year-old with breast cancer history, recurrent UTIs and dyspareunia unresponsive to nonhormonal therapy.
cases.reveal: Systemic MHT is contraindicated. Discuss low-dose vaginal estrogen with her oncologist: limited data do not show increased recurrence, but it may interfere with aromatase-inhibitor efficacy, so shared decision-making with oncology is essential.

mcqs.stem: A 53-year-old woman whose last menstrual period was 13 months ago reports hot flashes and disturbed sleep. Exam is normal. What is the most appropriate next step to confirm the diagnosis of menopause?
opts: No testing — the diagnosis is clinical
mcqs.exp: B. Menopause is a clinical diagnosis in a woman >45 with ≥12 months of amenorrhea and typical symptoms; no labs are needed. FSH (A) is unreliable as a single test. C/E are indicated only for abnormal bleeding, not for confirming menopause. AMH (D) has no role here.

mcqs.stem: A 51-year-old with severe hot flashes, an intact uterus, and no contraindications wants the most effective therapy. Which regimen is appropriate?
mcqs.exp: B. MHT is most effective for moderate-to-severe hot flashes, and progesterone must be added whenever the uterus is intact to prevent endometrial hyperplasia/cancer. Estrogen alone (A) is reserved for women without a uterus. Vaginal estrogen (D) treats GSM, not systemic VMS. Gabapentin (E) is nonhormonal second-line.

mcqs.stem: A 49-year-old with migraine with aura and an intact uterus has severe hot flashes and elects hormone therapy. Which formulation best reduces her thrombotic risk?
mcqs.exp: B. Transdermal estrogen has lower VTE and stroke risk than oral and is preferred in migraine and moderate ASCVD risk; progesterone is added for the intact uterus. Oral routes (A, C) carry higher thrombotic risk. Femring (D) delivers systemic-level doses. Combined OCPs (E) are contraindicated in migraine with aura.

mcqs.stem: Which statement best explains why the 2002 WHI results overstated the risk of hormone therapy for typical symptomatic patients?
opts: The mean participant age was 63, older than most symptomatic women starting therapy
It enrolled only women with prior breast cancer
mcqs.exp: B. WHI's mean age was ~63 — well beyond the typical <60 / <10-yr-post-menopause window — so absolute risks were higher than in younger symptomatic women, for whom benefits generally outweigh risks (2022 Menopause Society statement). A, C, D, E are factually wrong.

mcqs.stem: A 55-year-old with a history of breast cancer on an aromatase inhibitor has bothersome vasomotor symptoms and wants a nonhormonal prescription option. Which newer agent is an NK3-receptor antagonist FDA-approved for moderate-to-severe hot flashes, requiring LFT monitoring?
mcqs.exp: B. Fezolinetant (Veozah), an NK3-receptor antagonist (FDA May 2023), treats moderate-to-severe VMS and carries a warning for rare serious liver injury → monitor LFTs. Paroxetine (A) is an SSRI option but not an NK antagonist. Clonidine (C) is weakly effective. Estradiol (D) is hormonal and contraindicated here. Raloxifene (E) does not treat hot flashes (may worsen them).

anki: How is the menopausal transition (perimenopause) defined?
By irregular menses (a change in intermenstrual interval) plus hormonal symptoms (hot flashes, mood changes). Begins on average ~4 years before the last menstrual period.
How is menopause defined?
Retrospectively, after 12 consecutive months of amenorrhea. Mean age ~51.
Is menopause a clinical or laboratory diagnosis, and when is testing indicated?

Clinical diagnosis. In women >45 with irregular menses and classic symptoms, no testing is needed. Test (hCG, TSH, prolactin ± FSH) in women <45, and a full amenorrhea work-up if <40 (primary ovarian insufficiency).
Why is a single FSH a poor test for menopause?
FSH varies widely during the transition; irregular menses predict menopause better than FSH (SWAN study). FSH + estradiol can confirm completed menopause but is rarely needed.
What is the most effective treatment for moderate-to-severe hot flashes?
Menopausal hormone therapy (MHT).
Who is a candidate for systemic MHT?
Women with moderate-to-severe vasomotor symptoms who are <60 years old OR within 10 years of menopause; absolute risks are low in this group.
What are the contraindications to systemic MHT?
History of breast cancer, prior VTE or stroke/CHD, active liver disease, unexplained vaginal bleeding, and high-risk endometrial cancer.
When must progesterone be added to estrogen MHT?
In all patients with an intact uterus, to prevent endometrial hyperplasia/cancer.
Why is transdermal estrogen preferred over oral for some patients?
It confers lower risk of VTE and stroke; preferred for moderate ASCVD risk (5–10%) and migraine. Main downside is cost.
What did the Women's Health Initiative (2002) show, and what was its key limitation?
Excess CHD, stroke, VTE, and breast cancer with MHT — but the mean age was 63, far older than typical symptomatic patients, so results overstated risk for younger women.
How does the risk-benefit profile differ between estrogen-only and estrogen+progesterone MHT (ages 50–59, 5 yr)?

Estrogen alone is more favorable (e.g., fewer CHD and breast cancer cases); E+P carries more additional cases of CHD and breast cancer. Average absolute risks are small (~3 extra cases/1000 over 5 yr).
What is the recommended duration of MHT?

Generally 5–10 years, re-evaluating risk/benefit yearly and ideally stopping by age 60; longer use can be considered for severe symptoms after weighing risks. Rebound hot flashes are common on stopping.
What nonhormonal medications are effective for hot flashes?

SSRIs/SNRIs (venlafaxine and escitalopram best studied; paroxetine is the only FDA-approved one but is anticholinergic), and gabapentin/pregabalin. Avoid fluoxetine and sertraline for this indication.
What is the preferred treatment for isolated genitourinary syndrome of menopause (vaginal dryness, dyspareunia, recurrent UTIs)?
Vaginal moisturizers/lubricants for mild symptoms; vaginal (topical) estrogen is most effective with limited systemic absorption.
What is fezolinetant and its key safety monitoring?
Veozah, an NK3-receptor antagonist (FDA May 2023) for moderate-to-severe vasomotor symptoms; monitor LFTs (rare serious liver injury warning).
What is elinzanetant?
Lynkuet, a dual NK1/NK3-receptor antagonist (FDA Oct 2025) for moderate-to-severe vasomotor symptoms; ~73% VMS reduction and improves sleep.
What did the 2022 Menopause Society (NAMS) hormone therapy position statement conclude?

Benefits of hormone therapy outweigh risks for most healthy symptomatic women <60 years or within 10 years of menopause; HT remains most effective for VMS and genitourinary syndrome and prevents bone loss/fracture.
What are the menopause-related preventive priorities besides symptom control?
Estrogen deficiency raises atherosclerosis and osteoporosis risk: lifestyle ± statin by ASCVD risk; calcium, vitamin D, exercise, smoking cessation, and reduced alcohol for bone health.
A perimenopausal patient has heavy menstrual bleeding — what work-up is required?
Rule out malignancy: pregnancy test, pelvic exam, pelvic/transvaginal ultrasound ± endometrial biopsy (based on ultrasound findings and endometrial-cancer risk factors); consider TSH/prolactin.
lessons.dot:
#Menopause / menopausal transition (clinical dx) — *__ yo, LMP *__ ; pattern [perimenopause: irregular menses+VMS | menopause: ≥12mo amenorrhea]
  testing: NONE if >45 + classic sx ; if <45 → hCG/TSH/prolactin *±FSH ; <40 → POI work-up
SYMPTOMS / plan:
  VMS (hot flashes) *[mild→lifestyle | mod-severe→tx]: 1st-line MHT (most effective)
    nonhormonal: SSRI/SNRI *[venlafaxine/escitalopram/paroxetine] | gabapentin | NK-antagonist [fezolinetant*(LFT monitor)/elinzanetant]  (AVOID fluoxetine/sertraline)
  mood/sleep: as VMS; SSRI if mood predominant
  GSM (dryness/dyspareunia/recurrent UTI): vaginal estrogen (min systemic absorption) ± moisturizer/lubricant
  menstrual irregularity (peri): IUD or COC until menopause
MHT (if chosen):
  candidate? *[<60 yo OR <10 yr post-menopause] + mod-severe VMS
  regimen: estrogen *[transdermal pref if migraine/mod-ASCVD/VTE-concern | oral] + progesterone IF INTACT UTERUS
  duration *5–10 yr, reassess yearly, stop ~60
  CONTRAINDICATED: breast ca, prior VTE/stroke/CHD, active liver dz, unexplained vaginal bleeding, high-risk endometrial ca
PREVENTION: ASCVD — lifestyle ± statin by risk ; bone — Ca/vit D/exercise, stop tobacco, ↓EtOH ; DEXA per screening
RED FLAGS: heavy/postmenopausal bleeding → r/o malignancy (pelvic US ± endometrial bx)
COUNSEL: contraception still needed in peri (IUD→55, COC→51); rebound VMS common on stopping MHT
takehome: Menopause is primarily a clinical diagnosis from age and symptoms; lab testing is only for younger women or atypical features, and FSH is a poor single marker.
Clinical features: menstrual irregularity, vasomotor symptoms, mood/sleep changes, and genitourinary symptoms (dryness, dyspareunia, recurrent UTIs).
MHT is the most effective treatment for moderate-to-severe hot flashes; risk is low with good selection (<60 yr or <10 yr post-menopause). Add progesterone whenever the uterus is intact.
Transdermal estrogen lowers VTE/stroke risk vs. oral; vaginal estrogen is preferred for isolated GSM.
Nonhormonal options include SSRI/SNRI, gabapentin, and the NK-receptor antagonists fezolinetant and elinzanetant.
