lessons.title: Myeloproliferative Neoplasms
lessons.subtitle: Optimized lesson package · Facilitator-led interactive chalk talk for IM residents · 30–45 min
lessons.credit: Adapted from TeachIM.org — Myeloproliferative Neoplasms (Lakritz, Geiger, McMahon; ed. Geiger; exec. ed. Zhang; May 2022), CC BY-NC 4.0. Optimized June 2026: guideline currency verified, one deck typo corrected, WHO-2022 & new-therapy updates added, pedagogy restructured, assessments added. Companion deck: Myeloproliferative_Neoplasms_Optimized.pptx (original interactive board, surgically corrected + 3 appended slides).
objectives: Diagnose the four most common myeloproliferative neoplasms.
Build a differential for erythrocytosis, leukocytosis, and thrombocytosis.
Describe the basic principles of treating MPNs.

prework.q: Q1. What distinguishes an MPN from a myelodysplastic syndrome?
prework.a: Both are clonal stem-cell diseases, but MPNs overproduce mature myeloid cells, while MDS produces cells with abnormal/ineffective maturation (cytopenias with dysplasia). The four classic MPNs are ET, PV, PMF, and CML.

prework.q: Q2. How does the EPO level help you work up erythrocytosis?
prework.a: Low/suppressed EPO points to polycythemia vera (the marrow is autonomously making RBCs, suppressing EPO). Elevated EPO points to a secondary cause (hypoxia or an EPO-secreting tumor). Normal EPO fits relative (hemoconcentration) erythrocytosis. EPO isn't perfectly sensitive/specific (normal in ~15% of PV), but it orients you.

prework.q: Q3. Does a very high platelet count mean you should start aspirin?
prework.a: No. The magnitude of thrombocytosis does not predict thrombosis (or bleeding) risk, and reactive thrombocytosis (e.g., iron-deficiency anemia) does not warrant aspirin. Treatment decisions in ET/PV are driven by thrombosis risk stratification (age >60, prior thrombosis), not the count.

segments.label: Hook (2 min)
segments.html: Put a CBC on the board: Hgb 18, platelets 600k, WBC 14k. "Three lines up — what's going on in this marrow?" Use it to introduce the MPN concept: a clonal stem-cell disease that overproduces mature cells (unlike MDS, where maturation is abnormal).

segments.label: Overview
segments.html: ASK: "What is an MPN, and what are the four most common?" Reveal: overproduction of mature myeloid cells → ET (platelets), PV (RBCs), CML (granulocytes), PMF (marrow fibrosis). ET/PV/PMF share the JAK2/CALR/MPL drivers; CML is BCR-ABL1. Any MPN can transform to acute leukemia or marrow failure.

segments.label: Objective 1 & 2 — Diagnose each MPN with its differential
segments.html:
Essential thrombocythemia

Clonal platelet overproduction; JAK2/CALR/MPL (10–20% triple-negative → marrow biopsy). Symptoms: often asymptomatic; vasomotor symptoms, TIAs, ocular migraines, 1st-trimester loss.

ASK: "Differential for thrombocytosis?" Reactive (iron-deficiency anemia — commonly missed; infection/inflammation; post-splenectomy) vs primary (the 4 MPNs).
Pearl: Platelet count magnitude does NOT predict thrombosis — don't reflexively start aspirin for reactive thrombocytosis.

Polycythemia vera

Most common MPN; erythrocytosis (± leukocytosis/thrombocytosis); >95% JAK2 V617F. Suspect with Hgb >16.5 (M)/>16.0 (F), 25% rise in RBC mass, or thrombosis. Symptoms: aquagenic pruritus, headache, thrombosis, bleeding (acquired vWD), TIA/ocular migraine.

Pearl: Splanchnic-vein thrombosis (Budd-Chiari, portal/splenic/mesenteric) should trigger a PV hunt — portal hypertension can mask the lab signs.
ASK: "Walk the erythrocytosis differential by EPO level." Build the EPO physiology (hypoxia → renal EPO → marrow RBCs), then:
TypePrimary problemExamplesEPO
RelativeHemoconcentrationDiuretics, vomiting, diarrhea (dehydration)Normal
SecondaryHypoxia or ↑EPOCOPD/OSA, altitude, renal artery stenosis; EPO-secreting tumor (HCC, RCC, pheo)High
PVMarrow (autonomous)JAK2 V617FLow (normal in ~15%)

Primary myelofibrosis

Marrow replaced by fibrosis → extramedullary hematopoiesis; JAK2/CALR/MPL; can evolve from PV or ET. Hepatosplenomegaly, bone pain, cytopenias. Classic findings: teardrop cells (dacrocytes) on smear, "dry tap" with megakaryocyte hyperplasia on biopsy.

Chronic myeloid leukemia

Mature granulocyte excess; BCR-ABL1 (Philadelphia chromosome, t(9;22)) → ↑tyrosine kinase activity; low LAP score. Chronic phase (85% at dx) → accelerated → blast crisis.

ASK: "Differential for leukocytosis?" Leukemoid reaction (infection, stress, meds, tobacco) vs malignancy. Suspicious for CML: neutrophilia with left shift, basophilia, eosinophilia, constitutional symptoms, splenomegaly; WBC can exceed 100k but leukostasis is rare (vs acute leukemia).
ASK: "Sort the four leukemias on smear and marrow." Use the board's table:
AMLCMLALLCLL
SmearMyeloblasts, Auer rodsEosinophilia/basophilia, median WBC 100kLymphoblastsLymphocytosis, smudge cells
Marrow>20% myeloblastsGranulocytic hyperplasia, <20% blasts>20% lymphoblastsLymphocytic hyperplasia
DemographicAdults > childrenMedian age 50Children > adultsMedian age 70
UniqueDIC (esp. APL)t(9;22) BCR-ABL1LN/CNS infiltration (LP before tx)Richter transformation, autoimmune cytopenias

segments.label: Objective 3 — Treatment principles
segments.html:
MPNDriving principleTreatment
ETPrevent thrombosis; risk-stratify (age >60, prior clot)Low risk: observe + low-dose ASA. High risk: cytoreduction (hydroxyurea/interferon); anticoagulate if prior venous, ASA if arterial
PVPrevent thrombosis; risk-stratifyPhlebotomy (goal Hct <45%) + low-dose ASA; add cytoreduction if high risk
PMFPrognosis-drivenGood prognosis: surveillance + supportive care. Poor prognosis + transplant candidate: HSCT (only cure); otherwise JAK inhibitor (ruxolitinib)
CMLSuppress BCR-ABL1; highly effectiveTyrosine kinase inhibitor (imatinib first); switch TKI for resistance; HSCT now rare; blast phase → induction + TKI
2022–25 update: PV — ropeginterferon alfa-2b (Besremi) is now an NCCN-preferred first-line cytoreductive. Myelofibrosis — four JAK inhibitors chosen by counts: ruxolitinib, fedratinib, pacritinib (Vonjo; severe thrombocytopenia, plt <50k), momelotinib (Ojjaara; MF with anemia). CML — asciminib (Scemblix), a STAMP inhibitor binding the ABL myristoyl pocket, for ≥2 prior TKIs or T315I.

segments.label: Close (2 min)
segments.html: Hide the board. Have the group rebuild the four MPNs with their defining cell line and mutation, the EPO-based erythrocytosis differential, and the one treatment principle for each — then show the take-home slide.

cases.title: Case 1 — Erythrocytosis
cases.stem: 53-year-old woman with fatigue. T 37.6°C, HR 76, BP 118/77, SaO₂ 99%. CBC shows erythrocytosis (Hgb 17.4, Hct 51.6).
cases.reveal: Evaluate for PV symptoms (headache, aquagenic pruritus, thrombosis) and secondary risk factors (smoking, cancer). She reports headache and itching after showering; denies smoking/cancer.

cases.title: Case 2 — Leukocytosis
cases.stem: 45-year-old establishing care; routine CBC shows leukocytosis (WBC 29.3, with basophilia/eosinophilia on differential).
cases.reveal: Infection, chronic inflammation, medication-induced (steroids, drug reaction), leukemoid reaction (surgery, GI bleed), tobacco, hematologic malignancy.

cases.title: Case 3 — Cytopenias and a dry tap
cases.stem: 67-year-old with 1 month of malaise and weakness. Labs: anemia and thrombocytopenia; smear shows teardrop cells.
cases.reveal: Teardrop cells (dacrocytes) suggest myelofibrosis → bone marrow biopsy. Initial attempt is a "dry tap"; pathology shows reticular fibrosis and megakaryocyte hyperplasia.

mcqs.stem: A 58-year-old man has Hgb 18.9, Hct 56%, platelets 520k, and WBC 13k. He reports itching after hot showers. SaO₂ is 99%. Which test best distinguishes polycythemia vera from a secondary cause?
mcqs.exp: B. A suppressed EPO points to PV (autonomous marrow production suppresses EPO), whereas an elevated EPO indicates a secondary cause (hypoxia or EPO-secreting tumor). Ferritin (A) doesn't separate these. ABG (C) helps only if hypoxia is suspected and his SaO₂ is normal. Teardrop cells (D) suggest myelofibrosis, not PV. Confirm with JAK2 testing (>95% V617F-positive in PV).

mcqs.stem: A 40-year-old woman has an isolated platelet count of 850k. She is asymptomatic. Iron studies show iron-deficiency anemia. What is the most appropriate next step?
opts: Start low-dose aspirin for thrombosis prophylaxis
Treat the iron deficiency and recheck the platelet count
mcqs.exp: C. This is reactive thrombocytosis from iron-deficiency anemia (a commonly missed cause) — treat the iron deficiency and the count should normalize. Aspirin (A) and cytoreduction (B) are not indicated for reactive thrombocytosis; platelet magnitude does not drive thrombosis risk. Marrow biopsy (D) is premature; it's reserved for suspected primary thrombocytosis, e.g., a triple-negative ET workup.

mcqs.stem: A 50-year-old man has WBC 145k with neutrophilia, basophilia, and eosinophilia, plus splenomegaly. A low leukocyte alkaline phosphatase score is noted. Which finding confirms the diagnosis?
mcqs.exp: B. The picture is chronic myeloid leukemia, defined by the BCR-ABL1 fusion (Philadelphia chromosome, t(9;22)); a low LAP score is a classic supportive clue. JAK2 (A) drives the BCR-ABL1–negative MPNs (PV/ET/PMF), not CML. >20% marrow blasts (C) would indicate acute leukemia / blast crisis, not chronic-phase CML. Smudge cells (D) are seen in CLL.

mcqs.stem: A 72-year-old woman with primary myelofibrosis has symptomatic splenomegaly and platelets of 38k. Which JAK inhibitor is best suited given her thrombocytopenia?
mcqs.exp: B. Pacritinib (Vonjo) is specifically approved for myelofibrosis with severe thrombocytopenia (platelets <50k), where full-dose ruxolitinib (A) is limited by dose-dependent cytopenias. Hydroxyurea (C) helps proliferative symptoms but isn't the targeted answer here and can worsen cytopenias. Imatinib (D) targets BCR-ABL1 (CML), not myelofibrosis. (Momelotinib is the analogous choice when anemia dominates.)

mcqs.stem: A patient newly diagnosed with polycythemia vera is low-risk (age 45, no prior thrombosis). Which is the cornerstone of initial management?
opts: Phlebotomy to a hematocrit goal <45% plus low-dose aspirin
mcqs.exp: A. For PV, phlebotomy to a hematocrit <45% (CYTO-PV target) plus low-dose aspirin reduces thrombotic events and is the foundation for low-risk patients. Anticoagulation (B) is reserved for an established thrombosis. Transplant (C) has no role in PV. Cytoreduction such as hydroxyurea or ropeginterferon (D) is added for high-risk patients (age >60 or prior thrombosis), not universally.

anki: What is a myeloproliferative neoplasm (MPN)?
A clonal disease of the hematopoietic stem cell causing overproduction of MATURE myeloid cells. (Contrast with MDS = abnormal/ineffective maturation.)
The four most common MPNs?
Essential thrombocythemia (ET), polycythemia vera (PV), primary myelofibrosis (PMF), and chronic myeloid leukemia (CML).
Which MPNs share driver mutations, and which?
ET, PV, and PMF are grouped by three common driver mutations: JAK2, CALR, MPL. CML is defined separately by BCR-ABL1.
Essential thrombocythemia — definition and mutations?
Clonal overproduction of platelets; associated with JAK2, CALR, or MPL. 10–20% are "triple negative" (no identifiable mutation) and may need a marrow biopsy.
ET — symptoms?
Often asymptomatic; vasomotor symptoms, TIAs, ocular migraines, 1st-trimester pregnancy loss; thrombosis or bleeding less common.
Differential for thrombocytosis?
Reactive (iron-deficiency anemia — commonly missed; infection/inflammation; post-splenectomy) vs primary (the 4 MPNs).
ET/PV treatment principle and risk stratification?

Goal is to prevent thrombosis; platelet/Hgb magnitude does NOT drive thrombosis risk (don't treat reactive thrombocytosis). Low risk (<60 yo, no prior thrombosis): observation + low-dose aspirin (PV: phlebotomy to Hct <45%). High risk (>60 yo OR prior thrombosis): add cytoreduction; anticoagulate if prior venous clot, aspirin if arterial.
Polycythemia vera — hallmark and key mutation?
Erythrocytosis (often with leukocytosis/thrombocytosis too); >95% carry JAK2 V617F. Suspect with Hgb >16.5 (M) / >16.0 (F), a 25% rise in RBC mass, or arterial/venous thrombosis.
WHO 2022 PV diagnostic thresholds?
Hgb >16.5 (M) / >16.0 (F) OR Hct >49% (M) / >48% (F), plus marrow panmyelosis and a JAK2 mutation; subnormal EPO is the minor criterion.
Classic thromboses that should raise suspicion for PV?
Budd-Chiari, portal/splenic/mesenteric vein thrombosis (splanchnic) — portal hypertension can mask the lab signs of PV.
EPO level across causes of erythrocytosis?
Relative (hemoconcentration/dehydration): normal EPO. Secondary (hypoxia — COPD/OSA/altitude/RAS; or EPO-secreting tumor — HCC/RCC/pheo): elevated EPO. PV: suppressed EPO (but normal in ~15%).
PV treatment?
Phlebotomy (goal Hct <45%) + low-dose aspirin; add a cytoreductive agent (hydroxyurea or interferon) if high risk. Ropeginterferon alfa-2b (Besremi) is now an NCCN-preferred first-line cytoreductive.
Primary myelofibrosis — pathology and findings?
Marrow replaced by fibrosis (→ extramedullary hematopoiesis); JAK2/CALR/MPL; can evolve from PV or ET. Classic: teardrop cells (dacrocytes) on smear and a "dry tap" with megakaryocyte hyperplasia.
PMF — symptoms and treatment?

Hepatosplenomegaly, bone pain, cytopenias (anemia, bleeding, infections). Only cure = allogeneic HSCT (for poor-prognosis transplant candidates); otherwise active surveillance/supportive care or JAK inhibitors (ruxolitinib).
Newer JAK inhibitors for myelofibrosis?
Four total: ruxolitinib, fedratinib, pacritinib (Vonjo — for severe thrombocytopenia, plt <50k), and momelotinib (Ojjaara — for MF with anemia). Chosen by blood counts.
Chronic myeloid leukemia — definition and genetics?

Malignancy of mature granulocytes; BCR-ABL1 fusion (Philadelphia chromosome, t(9;22)) → ↑tyrosine kinase activity. Granulocytes are morphologically normal but cytochemically abnormal with a LOW leukocyte alkaline phosphatase (LAP) score.
Differential for leukocytosis?

Leukemoid reaction (infection, stress, medications, tobacco) vs malignancy. Suspicious for CML: neutrophilia with left shift, basophilia, eosinophilia, constitutional symptoms, abdominal fullness; WBC can exceed 100k.
Natural history and treatment of CML?

Chronic phase (85% at diagnosis) → accelerated phase → blast crisis. First-line = a tyrosine kinase inhibitor (imatinib was the first); switch TKIs for resistance (BCR-ABL1 testing); blast phase may need induction chemo + TKI. Asciminib (Scemblix, a STAMP inhibitor) is approved for ≥2 prior TKIs or T315I.
AML vs CML vs ALL vs CLL — smear/marrow snapshot?

AML: myeloblasts/Auer rods, >20% marrow blasts (DIC esp. APL). CML: eosinophilia/basophilia, median WBC 100k, granulocytic hyperplasia <20% blasts, t(9;22). ALL: lymphoblasts, >20% marrow lymphoblasts, children, CNS infiltration (LP before tx). CLL: lymphocytosis + smudge cells, median age 70, Richter transformation, autoimmune cytopenias.
Why is leukostasis rare in CML despite high WBC?
CML (and CLL) tolerate higher WBC counts than acute leukemias; leukostasis/hyperleukocytosis is a hematologic emergency far more typical of AML/ALL.
lessons.dot:
#?MPN: * yo, abnl CBC [erythrocytosis / thrombocytosis / leukocytosis / cytopenias + splenomegaly]. WBC *, Hgb *, Hct *, plt *. MPN = clonal overproduction of MATURE myeloid cells (vs MDS = abnl maturation). HDS.
- erythrocytosis w/u: confirm true (RBC mass) vs relative (hemoconcentration); EPO level [low->PV / high->2ndary]; JAK2 V617F; if Hgb >16.5(M)/>16.0(F) or Hct >49/48% + low EPO -> susp PV; r/o 2ndary [hypoxia COPD/OSA/altitude/RAS, EPO tumor HCC/RCC/pheo]; *splanchnic clot (Budd-Chiari/portal) -> hunt PV*
- thrombocytosis w/u: reactive [Fe-def anemia (check ferritin!), infection/inflam, post-splenectomy] vs primary (MPN). DON'T treat reactive; magnitude != thrombosis risk. JAK2/CALR/MPL; marrow bx if triple-neg susp ET
- leukocytosis w/u: leukemoid [infection, steroids, stress, tobacco] vs malignant; smear (left shift, basophilia/eosinophilia, blasts); if CML susp -> BCR-ABL1/t(9;22), low LAP; WBC >100k but leukostasis rare in CML
- cytopenias + teardrop cells / dry tap -> myelofibrosis -> marrow bx (fibrosis, megakaryocyte hyperplasia)
- dx confirm: heme/onc referral, marrow bx + cytogenetics + NGS [JAK2, CALR, MPL, BCR-ABL1]
- treatment by dx:
  - ET/PV: prevent thrombosis, risk-stratify [age >60 OR prior clot]. low risk -> ASA (+ phlebotomy Hct <45% for PV); high risk -> + cytoreduction [hydroxyurea / ropeginterferon]; anticoag if prior venous, ASA if arterial
  - PMF: prognosis-driven [MIPSS70]; HSCT only cure; JAK inhibitor [ruxolitinib / pacritinib if plt<50k / momelotinib if anemia]; supportive transfusion
  - CML: TKI [imatinib / 2nd-gen / asciminib if >=2 prior TKI or T315I]; monitor BCR-ABL1; blast phase -> induction + TKI
- counsel: thrombosis/bleeding precautions, smoking cessation, hydration; *
takehome: Four MPNs — ET, PV, PMF, CML — are clonal overproductions of MATURE myeloid cells (vs MDS = abnormal maturation). ET/PV/PMF share JAK2/CALR/MPL; CML is defined by BCR-ABL1.
A low EPO helps separate polycythemia vera from secondary (high EPO) and relative (normal EPO) erythrocytosis.
ET/PV treatment is risk-stratified by age >60 and prior thrombosis — aspirin/phlebotomy for low risk, add cytoreduction for high risk — and platelet magnitude does not drive that decision.
PMF is prognosis-driven (HSCT the only cure; JAK inhibitors for symptoms); CML is treated with TKIs and is now highly effective.

lessons.title: Osteoporosis
lessons.subtitle: Optimized lesson package · Facilitator-led interactive chalk talk for IM residents · 20–25 min core, 35–40 min with cases
lessons.credit: Adapted from TeachIM.org — Management of Osteoporosis (Saxon, deQuillfeldt, Jankousky, Guinn, Wagoner; eds. Brett, Fainstad, Zhang; Feb 2022, rev. Mar 2023), CC BY-NC 4.0. Optimized June 2026: guideline currency verified against the 2023 ACP living guideline and ASBMR/BHOF 2024, one deck terminology error corrected, pedagogy restructured, assessments added. Companion deck: Osteoporosis_Optimized.pptx (original interactive board, surgically corrected).
objectives: Apply osteoporosis screening guidelines based on age and risk factors.
Diagnose osteoporosis by any of three criteria (T-score, fragility fracture, or FRAX).
Initiate treatment based on severity and comorbidities, and know when a secondary work-up is needed.
Determine appropriate DXA monitoring intervals and drug-holiday timing.

prework.q: Q1. What are the three ways to diagnose osteoporosis?
prework.a: (1) T-score ≤ −2.5 on DXA; (2) a fragility fracture (low-trauma fracture of hip, vertebra, wrist, pelvis, ribs, or proximal humerus) regardless of T-score; (3) a high FRAX 10-year risk (≥20% major osteoporotic or ≥3% hip fracture).

prework.q: Q2. Who should be screened with DXA, and why does T-score alone miss patients?
prework.a: All women ≥65; postmenopausal women <65 with ≥1 risk factor; men with major risk factors. Most fragility fractures actually occur in people with T-scores better than −2.5, so screening on BMD alone misses many at-risk patients — clinical risk factors and FRAX matter.

prework.q: Q3. A patient on long-term denosumab wants to stop. What is the one thing you must not do?
prework.a: Do not simply discontinue it. Stopping denosumab causes a rapid rebound in bone turnover and a surge of vertebral fractures; you must transition to a bisphosphonate. (And do not follow denosumab with a PTH analog.)

segments.label: Hook (2 min)
segments.html: "A hip fracture in an older adult carries a one-year mortality around 25% — comparable to many cancers — and only half of survivors return to independent living. Yet osteoporosis is asymptomatic until it breaks a bone, and we have treatments that cut fracture risk by ~40%." Frame the talk as finding the silent disease before the fracture.

segments.label: Objective 1 — Burden & screening
segments.html:
ASK: "Describe osteoporosis at the bone level." — low bone mass + microarchitectural deterioration → skeletal fragility. ~20% of women and ~5% of men over 50 have it.

Who to screen with DXA: all women ≥65; postmenopausal women <65 with ≥1 risk factor (use FRAX); men with major risk factors.

ASK: "Name risk factors by category." — then click each.
CategoryExamples
SubstancesTobacco; alcohol >2 drinks/day
MedicationsGlucocorticoids (≥20 mg/day ×3 mo), aromatase inhibitors, androgen-deprivation therapy, antiepileptics
EndocrinopathiesPremature menopause, hypogonadism, hyperparathyroidism, T1DM, Cushing's
Chronic disease / malignancyMalabsorption/celiac/IBD, RA, chronic liver disease, CKD, HIV, multiple myeloma
OtherLow BMI/anorexia, family history of hip fracture, prior fracture

segments.label: Objective 2 — Diagnosis
segments.html:
ASK: "What are the three ways to diagnose osteoporosis?" — then reveal.
CriterionDetail
T-score ≤ −2.5 on DXANormal > −1.0; osteopenia −1.0 to −2.5; osteoporosis < −2.5. (T-score, not Z-score, in postmenopausal women/older men.)
Fragility fractureLow-trauma fracture (ground-level fall) of hip, vertebra, wrist, pelvis, ribs, proximal humerus — diagnostic regardless of T-score. Start treatment without waiting for DXA (can begin ~2 wk post-fracture).
High FRAX10-year risk ≥20% major osteoporotic OR ≥3% hip fracture.
Pearl — BMD underestimates risk: Most fragility fractures occur in patients with T-scores better than −2.5. Vertebral fractures are the most common manifestation and two-thirds are asymptomatic/incidental. Treat by clinical risk, not the DXA number alone.

Who needs a secondary work-up? All men; premenopausal women; and anyone with osteoporosis unexplained by comorbidities or under 50. Initial labs: CBC, CMP, Mg, Phos, 25-OH vitamin D, testosterone (men). Subsequent (directed): TSH, intact PTH, Cushing's screen, celiac panel, ESR/ferritin, SPEP/UPEP, FSH/LH/estradiol.

segments.label: Objective 3 — Treatment
segments.html:
Primary interventions for all: calcium ~1200 mg/day (ideally dietary), vitamin D 800 IU/day (50,000 IU weekly if level <40), weight-bearing high-impact exercise (brisk walking, jogging, dancing, stairs — not cycling/swimming), smoking cessation, limit alcohol.

Pearl — the VITAL trial: VITAL (NEJM 2022) found no fracture reduction from vitamin D in community-dwelling adults with a normal baseline level. But still replete to "normal" when starting a bisphosphonate (efficacy concern at low levels) — and never delay the bisphosphonate while repleting; start them together.
AgentRoleKey points
Bisphosphonate (alendronate PO weekly; zoledronic acid IV yearly)First-line for most (eGFR ≥35)Take oral on empty stomach with 8 oz water, sit upright ≥30 min, nothing by mouth ≥30 min. Harms: esophagitis, hypocalcemia, rare ONJ and atypical femur fracture. Drug holiday after 5 yr PO / 3 yr IV.
Denosumab (SQ q6 months)Second-line (intolerance/contraindication, eGFR <35)Anti-RANKL. Effectively lifelong — stopping causes rebound vertebral fractures, so always transition to a bisphosphonate.
Anabolics — teriparatide (SQ daily), romosozumab (SQ monthly)Very-high-risk (T ≤ −3.5, or T ≤ −2.5 + fragility fracture) or failure/intolerance of antiresorptivesMost effective; endocrinology-led. Avoid romosozumab with recent MI/stroke. Follow the anabolic course with an antiresorptive.
SERM — raloxifene (PO daily)Rarely; selected postmenopausal womenUseful if also high breast-cancer risk; harms: ↑VTE, hot flashes.
ASK: "How should a patient take an oral bisphosphonate, and who needs IV instead?" — empty stomach, full glass of water, stay upright 30 min; IV for esophageal disease, GI intolerance, Roux-en-Y, or inability to sit upright.

segments.label: Objective 4 — Monitoring & drug holidays
segments.html:
SituationRepeat DXA
Low risk (normal DXA)~15 years
Moderate risk (osteopenia, e.g., T −2.2)1–2 years
On IV bisphosphonate~3 years
On oral bisphosphonate~5 years
During a drug holidayEvery 2–4 years

After a treatment course, if BMD is stable/improved and risk has fallen → consider a drug holiday (lasting bisphosphonate benefit; cumulative atypical-fracture risk rises with duration). If BMD worsens or risk stays high → continue or refer to endocrine for denosumab/anabolic. (Intervals are largely expert opinion, not firm data.)

segments.label: Close (2 min)
segments.html: Hide the board. Ask the group to rebuild: who to screen, the three diagnostic criteria, first-line therapy and its counseling points, and when to consider a drug holiday.

cases.title: Case 1 — Compression fracture in an older man
cases.stem: 72 yo man, 2 wk of low back pain; 50-pack-year tobacco + alcohol use, CKD stage 3. 70 kg, 160 cm. Lumbar XR: L4 compression fracture. DXA T-score −2.2 (LS spine), −1.7 (left hip).
cases.reveal: FRAX 10-yr: major fracture ~15%, hip ~6.5%. He has osteoporosis by fragility fracture and high FRAX (hip >3%) — note the DXA (−1.7 to −2.2) is only osteopenic, illustrating that BMD alone misses fractures.

cases.title: Case 2 — Hip fracture after a fall
cases.stem: 64 yo woman with HTN, rheumatoid arthritis, and T2DM admitted after slipping on ice with a left femoral-neck fracture. Femoral-neck T-score −2.7.
cases.reveal: Osteoporosis (fragility fracture + T ≤ −2.5). Start calcium, vitamin D, and an antiresorptive; check baseline labs (vitamin D, BMP, calcium). This is a high-risk patient — a recent hip fracture marks imminent risk, so consider whether an anabolic-first strategy (endocrine input) is warranted.

cases.title: Case 3 — Worsening on treatment
cases.stem: 74 yo woman with severe COPD and osteoporosis after an atraumatic vertebral compression fracture from a coughing spell. Femoral-neck T was −2.8 five years ago; on alendronate since; repeat DXA now −3.1.
cases.reveal: She is failing an antiresorptive and is now very-high-risk (worsening BMD + new fragility fracture on therapy). Consider an anabolic agent — teriparatide or romosozumab — when T < −3.0, or T ≤ −2.5 with a vertebral fracture. (The original board said "anabolic steroids" — corrected to "anabolic agents"; teriparatide and romosozumab are not steroids.)

cases.title: Case 4 — The unifying secondary cause
cases.stem: 65 yo man, never-smoker, no chronic conditions, acute atraumatic low back pain + fatigue. Lumbar XR: L1 compression fracture. Work-up: Hgb 13.1, Cr 2.4, Ca 10, albumin 2.7, total protein 10.8, ALP 200; TSH/PTH/vitamin D/testosterone normal; femoral-neck T −2.7.
cases.reveal: The combination of age, renal insufficiency, and a high total-protein/low-albumin gap (with a fragility fracture) points to multiple myeloma. Next step: SPEP/UPEP (± serum free light chains).

mcqs.stem: A 68-year-old woman with no prior fractures has a DXA showing a femoral-neck T-score of −2.6 and normal labs. eGFR is 70. Which is the most appropriate initial pharmacologic therapy?
mcqs.exp: B. Bisphosphonates are first-line for most patients with osteoporosis and a preserved eGFR (ACP 2023, strong recommendation in postmenopausal women). Denosumab (A) is second-line for bisphosphonate intolerance/contraindication. Romosozumab (C) is reserved for very-high-risk patients. Raloxifene (D) is rarely first-line and adds VTE risk.

mcqs.stem: A 70-year-old woman on denosumab for 4 years wants to stop because of the injections. What is the most important next step?
opts: Transition to a bisphosphonate after the last denosumab dose
Reduce the dosing interval to once yearly
mcqs.exp: C. Stopping denosumab abruptly triggers a rebound in bone turnover with a surge of multiple vertebral fractures; transition to a bisphosphonate to prevent it. Simple discontinuation (A) is the classic error. Following denosumab with a PTH analog (B) can amplify the rebound and is not recommended. Stretching the interval (D) precipitates the same rebound.

mcqs.stem: A 67-year-old woman sustains a vertebral compression fracture; her DXA T-score is −3.6 and she has had a prior wrist fracture. Which strategy gives the greatest fracture-risk reduction?
opts: An anabolic agent (e.g., romosozumab or teriparatide) followed by a bisphosphonate
mcqs.exp: C. She is very-high-risk (very low T-score plus fragility fractures, imminent risk). An anabolic first, then an antiresorptive, produces greater BMD gain and earlier fracture reduction than starting with a bisphosphonate (ACP 2023; sequential-therapy data). Bisphosphonate alone (A) or a SERM (B) is less effective here; supplements alone (D) are inadequate.

mcqs.stem: A 66-year-old man presents with an atraumatic vertebral fracture, fatigue, Cr 2.3, calcium 10.6, total protein 10.5, and albumin 2.6. DXA shows osteoporosis. What is the best next diagnostic test?
mcqs.exp: B. Renal insufficiency, hypercalcemia, and a wide protein–albumin gap with a fragility fracture suggest multiple myeloma; SPEP/UPEP (± free light chains) is the next step. Osteoporosis in a younger man warrants a secondary work-up. Deferring evaluation (A, C) risks missing the diagnosis; a Cushing's screen (D) does not fit this picture.

mcqs.stem: A 72-year-old woman completes 5 years of oral alendronate. Her femoral-neck T-score has improved to −2.0, she has had no fractures, and FRAX risk is now modest. What is the most appropriate management?
opts: Consider a bisphosphonate drug holiday, with DXA every 2–4 years
mcqs.exp: C. After 5 years of oral bisphosphonate with improved BMD, no fractures, and lower risk, a drug holiday is appropriate — benefit persists after stopping, and cumulative use raises atypical-femur-fracture risk. Re-screen every 2–4 years during the holiday. Escalation (A, B) is not indicated for a patient whose risk has fallen; indefinite therapy without monitoring (D) ignores the holiday rationale.

anki: Definition of osteoporosis at the bone level?
Low bone mass + microarchitectural deterioration → skeletal fragility and increased fracture risk.
Three diagnostic criteria for osteoporosis?
T-score ≤ −2.5 on DXA; a fragility fracture (any T-score); or high FRAX (≥20% 10-yr major OR ≥3% hip).
DXA T-score classification?
Normal > −1.0; osteopenia −1.0 to −2.5; osteoporosis < −2.5.
Who should be screened with DXA?
All women ≥65; postmenopausal women <65 with ≥1 risk factor; men with major risk factors.
Why does T-score alone underestimate fracture risk?
Most fragility fractures occur in patients with T-scores better than −2.5; use clinical risk factors and FRAX, not BMD alone.
What is a fragility fracture, and where do they occur?
A fracture from minor trauma (ground-level fall): hip, vertebra, wrist, pelvis, ribs, proximal humerus. Diagnostic of osteoporosis regardless of T-score.
Most common clinical manifestation of osteoporosis?
Vertebral fracture — two-thirds are asymptomatic/incidental.
1-year mortality and return-to-independence after hip fracture?
~25% one-year mortality; only ~50% return to independent living.
Who needs a secondary work-up for osteoporosis?
All men; premenopausal women; osteoporosis that is unexplained or under age 50. Initial labs: CBC, CMP, Mg, Phos, 25-OH vitamin D, testosterone (men).
Primary interventions for everyone with/at risk for osteoporosis?
Calcium ~1200 mg/day (dietary preferred), vitamin D 800 IU/day, weight-bearing high-impact exercise, smoking cessation, limit alcohol.
What did the VITAL trial show about vitamin D?
No fracture reduction from vitamin D in community-dwelling adults with normal baseline levels (NEJM 2022). Still replete to normal when starting a bisphosphonate, but don't delay the bisphosphonate.
First-line drug for most osteoporosis, and the eGFR limit?
Bisphosphonate (alendronate PO weekly or zoledronic acid IV yearly); use if eGFR ≥35.
How to take an oral bisphosphonate?
First thing AM, empty stomach, with 8 oz water; sit upright ≥30 min; nothing by mouth ≥30 min. Stop if esophagitis symptoms.
Bisphosphonate adverse effects?
Esophagitis, hypocalcemia; rare osteonecrosis of the jaw (~1/10,000-1/100,000 pt-yr) and atypical femur fracture (~0.2% after 5 yr).
Denosumab — mechanism, line, and the critical caution?
Anti-RANKL antibody, SQ q6 months, second-line. Effectively lifelong — stopping causes rebound vertebral fractures, so always transition to a bisphosphonate (never to a PTH analog).
When are anabolic agents indicated, and which sequence?

Very-high-risk (T ≤ −3.5, or ≤ −2.5 + fragility fracture) or antiresorptive failure. Use the anabolic (teriparatide/romosozumab) FIRST, then follow with a bisphosphonate. Avoid romosozumab with recent MI/stroke.
DXA monitoring intervals by risk?
Low risk ~15 yr; moderate (osteopenia) 1-2 yr; on IV bisphosphonate ~3 yr; on oral ~5 yr; during drug holiday every 2-4 yr.
When to consider a bisphosphonate drug holiday?
After ~5 yr oral or ~3 yr IV if BMD is stable/improved and risk has fallen — benefit persists, and cumulative use raises atypical-fracture risk.
lessons.dot:
#Osteoporosis (chronic): dx by [DXA T <=-2.5 / fragility fx (any T) / FRAX 10y major >=20% or hip >=3%]. femoral-neck T *, LS T *. risk tier * [low / mod / high(OP) / very-high (T<=-3.5 or <=-2.5 + fx, or recent hip/vert fx)]. pillars: 1)risk-factor mod 2)Ca/VitD 3)targeted rx by risk 4)2ndary w/u if indicated 5)DXA monitoring.
- risk factors: meds (steroids >=20mg x3mo, AI, ADT, AED); endo (premature menopause, hypogonadism, HPT, T1DM, Cushing); GI/malabs (celiac, IBD, CLD); tobacco/EtOH >2/d; low BMI; FHx hip fx; prior fx.
- primary (ALL): Ca *1200mg/d (diet preferred), VitD *800 IU/d (50k qwk if <40), wt-bearing high-impact exercise, d/c tobacco, limit EtOH.
- targeted rx: 1st-line BISPHOSPHONATE *(alendronate PO qwk / zoledronic IV qyr) if eGFR>=35 [PO: empty stomach, 8oz water, upright >=30min]; 2nd-line DENOSUMAB SQ q6mo if intol/eGFR<35 (*NEVER stop w/o bridge to bisphosphonate -> rebound vert fx; do NOT follow w/ PTH analog); VERY-HIGH-RISK -> ANABOLIC FIRST *(teriparatide SQ qd / romosozumab SQ qmo; avoid romo if recent MI/CVA) THEN antiresorptive; SERM (raloxifene) rare.
- 2ndary w/u (all men, premenopausal, unexplained/<50): CBC, CMP, Mg, Phos, 25-OH-VitD, testosterone(M); then directed TSH, iPTH, celiac, SPEP/UPEP, AM cortisol/Cushing, FSH/LH/estradiol.
- monitoring DXA: low ~15y; mod (osteopenia) 1-2y; IV bisphos ~3y; PO bisphos ~5y; drug holiday q2-4y. drug holiday after 5y PO / 3y IV if BMD stable + risk down.
- harms to counsel: esophagitis, hypoCa, rare ONJ + atypical femur fx.
- f/u: *; RTC *.
takehome: Screen all women ≥65 (and younger postmenopausal women or men with major risk factors) with DXA. Diagnose by T-score ≤ −2.5, a fragility fracture, or a high FRAX — and remember most fractures occur above a −2.5 T-score.

Bisphosphonates are first-line for most (eGFR ≥35); counsel on rare harms (esophagitis, ONJ, atypical femur fracture). Denosumab is second-line — never stop it without bridging to a bisphosphonate. Anabolics (anabolic-first, then antiresorptive) for very-high-risk.
Consider a drug holiday after 5 years oral / 3 years IV bisphosphonate, re-screening every 2–4 years. Work up secondary causes in men, premenopausal women, and unexplained disease.

extras.label: Guideline Update ACP 2023 living guideline & ASBMR/BHOF 2024
extras.html:
The talk (2022, rev. 2023) is built on the 2019 Endocrine Society guideline. The 2023 ACP living guideline (Ann Intern Med) confirms and sharpens the same framework:

TopicCurrent guidance
First-lineBisphosphonates — strong recommendation in postmenopausal women, conditional in men with primary osteoporosis.
Second-lineDenosumab when bisphosphonates are contraindicated or not tolerated.
Very-high-risk womenAn anabolic first (romosozumab or teriparatide), then a bisphosphonate. Anabolic-first-then-antiresorptive yields greater BMD and earlier fracture reduction than the reverse sequence — important for imminent risk (recent hip/vertebral fracture).
Denosumab discontinuationNever stop without transitioning to a bisphosphonate (rebound vertebral fractures). Do not follow denosumab with a PTH analog.
Estrogen / raloxifeneNot recommended as first-line for most; reserve SERMs for selected patients.
Treat-to-targetASBMR/BHOF 2024 goal-directed position: aim for a target BMD/T-score, not a fixed duration.

Sources: Qaseem A, et al. ACP living clinical guideline, Ann Intern Med 2023 (M22-1034); ASBMR/BHOF goal-directed therapy position statement, JBMR 2024. Clinician-reviewed before teaching.

1. Qaseem A, et al. Pharmacologic Treatment of Primary Osteoporosis or Low Bone Mass to Prevent Fractures in Adults: A Living Clinical Guideline From the American College of Physicians. Ann Intern Med. 2023;176(2):224-238 (M22-1034).
  2. Eastell R, et al. Pharmacological Management of Osteoporosis in Postmenopausal Women: An Endocrine Society Clinical Practice Guideline. J Clin Endocrinol Metab. 2019;104(5):1595-1622.
  3. ASBMR/BHOF Goal-Directed Osteoporosis Treatment Task Force position statement. J Bone Miner Res. 2024;39(10):1393.
  4. Barrionuevo P, et al. Efficacy of Pharmacological Therapies for the Prevention of Fractures in Postmenopausal Women: A Network Meta-Analysis. J Clin Endocrinol Metab. 2019;104(5):1623-1630.
  5. LeBoff MS, et al. Supplemental Vitamin D and Incident Fractures in Midlife and Older Adults (VITAL). N Engl J Med. 2022;387:299-309.
  6. Wells GA, et al. Alendronate for primary and secondary prevention of osteoporotic fractures in postmenopausal women. Cochrane Database Syst Rev. 2008;(1):CD001155.

lessons.title: Outpatient T2DM
lessons.subtitle: Optimized lesson package · Facilitator-led interactive chalk talk for IM residents · 20 min core, 35–40 min with cases
lessons.credit: Adapted from TeachIM.org — Outpatient Management of Type II Diabetes Mellitus (Brett, Guinn, Melton, Trefilek, Fainstad; exec. ed. Zhang; Mar 2022, rev. Jun 2023), CC BY-NC 4.0. Optimized June 2026: guideline currency verified against ADA Standards of Care 2025–2026, three deck errors corrected, pedagogy restructured, assessments added. Companion deck: T2DM_Optimized.pptx (original interactive board, surgically corrected).
objectives: Identify which patients should be screened for type 2 diabetes (T2DM), and how to make the diagnosis.
Recognize and screen for the microvascular and macrovascular complications of T2DM.
Choose pharmacologic therapy driven first by comorbidities (ASCVD, HF, CKD) and only then by the A1c.

prework.q: Q1. List the four ways to diagnose T2DM and the threshold for each.
prework.a: (1) Random glucose >200 mg/dL with symptoms of hyperglycemia or hyperglycemic crisis; (2) A1c ≥6.5%; (3) fasting glucose ≥126 mg/dL (≥8 h fast); (4) 2-h glucose ≥200 mg/dL on a 75-g OGTT. In an asymptomatic patient, confirm with a repeat or a second test type.

prework.q: Q2. Name the three microvascular and three macrovascular complications of diabetes, and how each microvascular one is screened.
prework.a: Microvascular: retinopathy (annual dilated retinal exam), nephropathy (annual spot urine albumin:creatinine ratio, abnormal >30 mg/g), neuropathy (annual foot exam + monofilament). Macrovascular: coronary (CAD/MI), cerebrovascular (stroke), peripheral arterial disease — no routine screening test; evaluate by symptoms and treat risk factors.

prework.q: Q3. A patient has an A1c of 6.6% (at goal) plus HFrEF and diabetic CKD with albuminuria. Should you change their diabetes meds?
prework.a: Yes. Comorbidity drives therapy independent of A1c: this patient warrants an SGLT2 inhibitor (HF + CKD benefit) regardless of being at glycemic goal — the modern central teaching point of the talk.

segments.label: Hook (2 min)
segments.html: "Your patient's A1c is 6.7% — at goal. They also have heart failure, diabetic kidney disease, and obesity. Do you change their diabetes regimen?" Most learners say no. The whole talk argues yes — and reframes diabetes as a cardiovascular-renal disease, not just a glucose number.

segments.label: Objective 1 — Diagnosis & screening
segments.html:
ASK: "What are the classic symptoms of diabetes?" — then click. Polyuria, polydipsia, weight loss, blurry vision; acanthosis nigricans on exam.

Diagnostic criteria:

TestDiagnostic threshold
Random glucose + symptoms / crisis>200 mg/dL
Fasting glucose (≥8 h)≥126 mg/dL
HbA1c≥6.5%
2-h glucose, 75-g OGTT≥200 mg/dL (mainly pregnancy)

In asymptomatic patients, confirm with a repeat value or a second method (e.g., fasting glucose ×2, or A1c + fasting glucose).

Pearl — when the A1c lies: A1c tracks RBC age. Low RBC turnover (iron/B12/folate deficiency, asplenia) → too many old glycated cells → A1c falsely high. High RBC turnover (hemolysis, recent EPO or B12/folate repletion, pregnancy, CKD) → A1c falsely low. Use fasting glucose or a CGM/GMI when A1c is unreliable.
ASK: "Who gets screened, and how often?" — then click.

Screen at age ≥35 for everyone; screen earlier if BMI ≥25 (≥23 in Asian Americans, who develop metabolic disease at lower BMI) plus a risk factor (high-risk ethnicity, first-degree relative, HTN, dyslipidemia, ASCVD, PCOS, physical inactivity); also screen patients on diabetogenic drugs (steroids, atypical antipsychotics, some HIV meds) and those with prior gestational DM. Frequency: every 3 years, or yearly with prediabetes (A1c 5.7–6.4%), or in 3–6 months for marginal results.

segments.label: Objective 2 — Complications
segments.html:
ASK: "Name the microvascular and macrovascular complications." — then click each.
Microvascular (small vessel)ScreeningPrevention
Retinopathy → blindnessAnnual dilated retinal examTight glycemic control (A1c ~7%) reduces microvascular disease. Benefit takes ~10 yr — relax the target in limited life expectancy.
Nephropathy → CKD, albuminuriaAnnual spot urine albumin:Cr (abnl >30 mg/g)
Neuropathy (periph + autonomic)Annual foot exam + monofilament
Macrovascular (large vessel)ScreeningPrevention
CAD/MI, stroke, PADNone routine — evaluate by symptomsTight A1c does NOT reduce events in longstanding diabetes. Aggressively treat BP, lipids, tobacco: moderate-intensity statin; BP <130/80 if higher CV risk (10-yr risk >15% or ASCVD), else <140/90; smoking cessation.
Pearl — the legacy effect: Early intensive control in newly diagnosed diabetes may reduce later macrovascular events (UKPDS legacy effect), but starting tight control late does not. Glucose is a weak lever on the heart; the statin and the BP med do the heavy lifting.

segments.label: Objective 3 — Comorbidity-driven management
segments.html:
ASK: "Before you look at the A1c, what four patient factors should steer therapy?" — comorbidities (ASCVD/HF/CKD), desire for weight loss, cost, and hypoglycemia risk.

Step 1 — lifestyle for all (≥5% weight loss, ≥150 min/wk moderate-vigorous activity). Metformin (eGFR ≥30) remains a reasonable, cheap, safe anchor for most — though the original ADA algorithm no longer mandates it first for everyone.

Step 2 — comorbidity first. If the patient has ASCVD (or high risk), HF, or CKD, start an SGLT2 inhibitor and/or GLP-1 RA with proven benefit regardless of the A1c — these reduce mortality and renal events; insulin has no such benefit.

Step 3 — only now consider the A1c. If no high-risk comorbidity and A1c is above goal, prioritize by: weight loss (GLP-1 RA, then SGLT2i), cost (formulary), and avoiding hypoglycemia (liberalize to 8–8.5% in frail/elderly). If still above goal → add sulfonylurea or insulin. Severe hyperglycemia (glucose >300, A1c >10%, or symptoms) often warrants starting insulin up front, then simplifying.

ASK: "Pair the side effect to the drug class." — metformin (GI upset, avoid eGFR<30); GLP-1 RA (GI upset, pancreatitis, medullary thyroid ca contraindication); SGLT2i (genital/UTI infection, euglycemic DKA, volume depletion, ↑amputation with canagliflozin); sulfonylurea/insulin (hypoglycemia, weight gain).

segments.label: Close (2 min)
segments.html: Hide the board. Ask the group to rebuild from memory: the four diagnostic criteria, the micro- vs macrovascular complications and how each is screened, and the one rule that changed practice — comorbidity before A1c.

cases.title: Case 1 — Would you screen? (asymptomatic)
cases.stem: (a) 42 yo with SLE, ESRD on HD, HTN, hyperlipidemia, on prednisone, EPO, amlodipine, atorvastatin. BMI 22, BP 115/74. Fasting glucose 160, A1c 5.6%.  (b) 33 yo Asian woman, BMI 23, PCOS.  (c) 28 yo on olanzapine, BMI 22.
cases.reveal: Very likely — confirm with a second fasting glucose ≥126. The A1c (5.6%) is discordant and underestimates average glucose here because of high RBC turnover (EPO therapy). Trust the glucose, not the A1c.

cases.title: Case 2 — New diagnosis, severe
cases.stem: 55 yo establishing care, 2–3 months of polyuria/polydipsia, BMI 42.
cases.reveal: BMP, urine albumin:creatinine, lipid panel, plus a baseline retinal exam and foot exam to screen for complications already present at diagnosis.

cases.title: Case 3 — Comorbidity drives therapy
cases.stem: (a) 45 yo, T2DM + tobacco, on metformin 2000 mg ER + simvastatin. BP 152/83, normal foot exam, BMI 24. A1c 7.4%, Cr 1.2, urine albumin:Cr 303 mg/g.  (b) 45 yo on metformin, PAD s/p amputation, A1c 6.5%.  (c) 72 yo on metformin, prior DKA, HFrEF, A1c 8.6%.
cases.reveal: ACE inhibitor or ARB (albuminuria + HTN) plus lifestyle. Add an SGLT2 inhibitor for diabetic kidney disease with albuminuria to slow CKD progression — independent of the near-goal A1c. Tobacco cessation is the single biggest ASCVD lever.

cases.title: Case 4 — Glycemic targets & the frail patient
cases.stem: 82 yo with T2DM, COPD, HTN, CAD, diabetic CKD stage 2, BPH, cognitive impairment, presenting with recurrent falls. A1c 7.5% on metformin + glargine 10 U qHS, lisinopril, tamsulosin, atorvastatin.
cases.reveal: Liberalize to <8% (even up to 8.5%) given age, comorbidity, cognitive impairment, and fall risk. The glargine is a hypoglycemia and falls hazard — consider reducing or stopping it. The priority in this patient is avoiding hypoglycemia, not chasing a lower number.

cases.title: Case 5 — Cost & access
cases.stem: 53 yo, homeless and uninsured, T2DM on metformin, CKD stage 3, A1c 8.5%; later loses job and A1c rises.
cases.reveal: Glipizide (a sulfonylurea — not glyburide, which accumulates in CKD and causes hypoglycemia). Insulin is hard to manage without stable housing/refrigeration. Equally important: assess social determinants of health and mental health — access and adherence, not drug choice, are the rate-limiting step.

mcqs.stem: A 58-year-old woman with T2DM (A1c 6.8% on metformin), HFrEF (EF 30%), and stage 3 CKD with a urine albumin:creatinine ratio of 250 mg/g returns for follow-up. Her glucose logs are at goal. What is the most appropriate next step?
opts: Make no changes — she is at her A1c goal
mcqs.exp: B. Therapy is driven by comorbidity, not the A1c. An SGLT2 inhibitor reduces HF hospitalizations and slows CKD progression and is indicated here despite a glucose already at goal — the central teaching point. Doing nothing (A) misses a mortality/renal benefit. Insulin (C) and sulfonylureas (D) add hypoglycemia risk with no cardiorenal benefit.

mcqs.stem: A 47-year-old man with iron-deficiency anemia has a fasting glucose of 138 mg/dL and an A1c of 5.9%. How should you interpret the discordance?
opts: The A1c is most reliable; he does not have diabetes
Iron deficiency falsely lowers the A1c
Iron deficiency falsely raises the A1c; the fasting glucose is the more reliable diagnostic test here
Repeat the A1c in one year
mcqs.exp: C. Low RBC turnover states (iron, B12, or folate deficiency; asplenia) leave a disproportionate share of old, heavily glycated red cells, falsely elevating the A1c — yet here the A1c is paradoxically the lower number, so it is unreliable and the diabetic-range fasting glucose should drive diagnosis (confirm with a repeat). Conversely, hemolysis or recent EPO/iron repletion falsely lowers A1c (B describes the wrong direction for iron deficiency). When A1c and glucose disagree, trust glucose (or a CGM).

mcqs.stem: A 60-year-old man with T2DM and established peripheral arterial disease (prior toe amputation), A1c 7.1% on metformin, asks to intensify therapy for cardiovascular benefit. Which agent is the best choice?
mcqs.exp: B. A GLP-1 RA provides cardiovascular benefit in established ASCVD and is preferred here. Canagliflozin (A) — and SGLT2 inhibitors broadly — carry an amputation signal and are best avoided after amputation/severe PAD. Sulfonylureas (C) and insulin (D) offer no cardiovascular benefit and add hypoglycemia.

mcqs.stem: An 84-year-old woman with T2DM, moderate dementia, and recurrent falls has an A1c of 6.7% on metformin and glargine 14 U nightly. She has had two episodes of morning shakiness. What is the most appropriate management?
opts: Continue current regimen; her A1c is excellent
Increase glargine to lower the A1c further
Liberalize the A1c target and reduce or stop the insulin
mcqs.exp: C. An A1c of 6.7% on insulin in a frail elder with dementia and falls is too tight — the morning shakiness signals hypoglycemia. Liberalize to <8% (up to 8.5%) and de-intensify, starting with the insulin. Tightening (A, B) or adding a hypoglycemic agent (D) increases harm; in this population hypoglycemia carries more risk than modest hyperglycemia.

mcqs.stem: A 52-year-old man with T2DM and CKD (eGFR 40, urine albumin:creatinine 400 mg/g) is on a maximally tolerated ACE inhibitor, an SGLT2 inhibitor, metformin, and a statin. Albuminuria persists and potassium is 4.6. Per current ADA guidance, what should be added to further reduce kidney and cardiovascular risk?
mcqs.exp: A. Finerenone, a nonsteroidal MRA, reduces CKD progression and CV events in diabetic kidney disease with residual albuminuria despite ACEi/ARB + SGLT2i (FIDELIO-/FIGARO-DKD), and is the 2025–2026 ADA recommendation; normokalemia makes it appropriate to start. Steroidal MRAs (B) carry more hyperkalemia/gynecomastia and lack the same DKD outcome data. Dual RAAS blockade (C) is harmful. Insulin (D) does not slow CKD.

anki: Four diagnostic criteria for T2DM?
Random glucose >200 + symptoms; fasting glucose ≥126; A1c ≥6.5%; 2-h OGTT glucose ≥200. Confirm asymptomatic cases with a repeat or second test.
At what age do you screen everyone for T2DM?
Age 35. Earlier if BMI ≥25 (≥23 in Asian Americans) plus a risk factor, or on diabetogenic drugs, or prior gestational DM.
Screening frequency for T2DM?
Every 3 years; yearly if prediabetes (A1c 5.7-6.4%); in 3-6 months for marginal results.
When is the A1c falsely HIGH?
Low RBC turnover — iron, B12, or folate deficiency; asplenia (old, heavily glycated cells dominate).
When is the A1c falsely LOW?
High RBC turnover — hemolysis, recent EPO or B12/folate repletion, pregnancy, advanced CKD.
Three microvascular complications of T2DM and how each is screened?
Retinopathy (annual dilated retinal exam); nephropathy (annual urine albumin:Cr, abnl >30 mg/g); neuropathy (annual foot exam + monofilament).
Three macrovascular complications, and how are they screened?
CAD/MI, stroke, PAD. No routine screening test — evaluate by symptoms; prevent by treating BP, lipids, and tobacco.
Does tight glycemic control prevent microvascular or macrovascular disease?

Microvascular yes (A1c ~7%, benefit takes ~10 yr). Macrovascular — not in longstanding disease; aggressive BP/lipid/tobacco control matters more. Early control in new diabetes may help later (legacy effect).
BP and lipid targets in T2DM?
Moderate-intensity statin for primary prevention; BP <130/80 if higher CV risk (10-yr risk >15% or ASCVD), else <140/90.
The single most important rule of modern T2DM pharmacotherapy?
Choose the drug by comorbidity (ASCVD/HF/CKD) FIRST, then by A1c — even if the A1c is already at goal.
Which agents have a mortality/cardiorenal benefit in T2DM, and which does not?
SGLT2 inhibitors and GLP-1 RAs do; insulin does not.
Preferred agent in T2DM + HF (HFrEF or HFpEF)?
SGLT2 inhibitor — fewer HF hospitalizations; recommended in both reduced and preserved EF (2025-26 ADA).
Preferred agent in T2DM + ASCVD or high CV risk?
GLP-1 RA and/or SGLT2 inhibitor with proven benefit, regardless of A1c.
T2DM + CKD with persistent albuminuria despite ACEi/ARB + SGLT2i — next drug?
Finerenone (nonsteroidal MRA) — slows CKD progression and reduces CV events (2025-26 ADA).
Key side effects: metformin, GLP-1 RA, SGLT2i?

Metformin — GI upset, avoid eGFR<30. GLP-1 RA — GI upset, pancreatitis, medullary thyroid ca contraindication. SGLT2i — genital/UTI infection, euglycemic DKA, volume depletion, ↑amputation (canagliflozin).
Which sulfonylurea to avoid in CKD, and why?
Glyburide — renally cleared active metabolites accumulate and cause prolonged hypoglycemia. Use glipizide instead.
A1c target in a frail elderly patient with dementia and falls?
Liberalize to <8% (up to 8.5%); prioritize avoiding hypoglycemia and de-intensify insulin/sulfonylureas.
lessons.dot:
#T2DM (chronic): dx by [A1c >=6.5 / FBG >=126 / RBG >200+sx / OGTT >=200]. duration *yr. last A1c *% (goal * [<7 most / <8 frail-elderly/limited-life-exp / 6.5 select young]). pillars: 1)comorbidity-driven rx 2)CV risk-factor control 3)complication screening 4)glycemic control.
- comorbidity FIRST (independent of A1c): ASCVD/high-risk -> [GLP1-RA *(semaglutide/dulaglutide/liraglutide) +/- SGLT2i]; HF (HFrEF or HFpEF) -> SGLT2i *(empagliflozin/dapagliflozin); CKD (eGFR * / UACR *mg/g) -> SGLT2i if eGFR>=20 + ACEi/ARB; if UACR persists despite both -> add finerenone (K *<5.0). *SGLT2i + GLP1-RA give mortality/renal benefit; insulin does NOT.
- glycemic ladder (only after comorbidity): lifestyle (>=5% wt loss, >=150min/wk) + metformin *(eGFR>=30) -> if A1c >goal prioritize [wt loss: GLP1-RA>SGLT2i] [cost: formulary] [avoid hypoG: frail] -> SU(glipizide, NOT glyburide if CKD)/insulin last. start insulin up front if BG>300 / A1c>10 / sx; consider initial combo if A1c 1.5-2% over goal.
- CV risk factors (do the heavy lifting): statin [moderate-int primary prev / high-int if ASCVD]; BP * goal [<130/80 if 10yr-risk>15% or ASCVD, else <140/90] w/ ACEi/ARB if albuminuria; tobacco d/c *; ASA only if established ASCVD.
- complication screen (annual): retina (dilated exam); nephropathy (UACR, abnl >30mg/g); neuropathy (foot exam + monofilament); macrovascular -> Sx-based, no routine test.
- A1c caveats: falsely HIGH (low RBC turnover: Fe/B12/folate defic, asplenia); falsely LOW (high turnover: hemolysis, EPO, preg, CKD) -> use FBG/CGM-GMI.
- f/u: A1c q3mo if not at goal else q6mo; titrate *; RTC *.
takehome: Screen at age 35, or earlier with BMI ≥25 (≥23 in Asian Americans) plus a risk factor; diagnose with A1c ≥6.5%, fasting glucose ≥126, random >200 + symptoms, or OGTT ≥200 (confirm asymptomatic cases).

Diabetes causes microvascular (retinopathy, nephropathy, neuropathy — screen yearly) and macrovascular (ASCVD) disease. Tight A1c prevents microvascular complications; controlling BP, lipids, and tobacco prevents the macrovascular ones.

Choose therapy by comorbidity FIRST: ASCVD/HF/CKD → SGLT2 inhibitor and/or GLP-1 RA regardless of the A1c (mortality and renal benefit). Add finerenone for residual albuminuria. Liberalize the A1c and avoid hypoglycemia in frail elders.

extras.label: Guideline Update ADA Standards of Care 2025–2026
extras.html:
The original talk (2022, rev. 2023) is built on the 2022–2023 ADA Standards. The core framework — comorbidity-driven therapy — has only been reinforced since. Key changes to surface:

TopicCurrent ADA guidance (2025–2026)
Organ-protection drugsSGLT2i and/or GLP-1 RA with proven benefit are a core, foundational strategy for ASCVD/HF/CKD — used for cardiorenal protection independent of A1c and independent of metformin.
First-line therapyMetformin is no longer mandated first for all; therapy is chosen by comorbidity. Metformin remains an appropriate, low-cost option (eGFR ≥30).
Initial combinationConsider starting two agents at once when A1c is 1.5–2.0% above goal, or with established/high-risk CVD.
Weight + glycemiaPrefer a GLP-1 RA or dual GIP/GLP-1 RA (semaglutide, tirzepatide) for greater weight loss; relevant in obesity, MASLD/MASH, HFpEF.
Heart failureSGLT2 inhibitor recommended in both HFrEF and HFpEF for glycemic control and fewer HF hospitalizations.
CKD with residual albuminuriaAdd finerenone (nonsteroidal MRA) when albuminuria persists despite a maximally tolerated ACEi/ARB plus SGLT2i — reduces CKD progression and CV events (FIDELIO-/FIGARO-DKD).

Sources: ADA, Standards of Care in Diabetes — 2025 and 2026, Diabetes Care Suppl. 1 (Sections 2, 9, 10, 11). Medical content should be clinician-reviewed before teaching.

lessons.refs:
1. American Diabetes Association. Standards of Care in Diabetes—2026. Diabetes Care. 2026;49(Suppl 1). (Sections 2 Diagnosis/Classification; 9 Pharmacologic Approaches; 10 Cardiovascular Disease & Risk Management; 11 Chronic Kidney Disease.)
  2. American Diabetes Association. Standards of Care in Diabetes—2025. Diabetes Care. 2025;48(Suppl 1).
  3. American Diabetes Association. Standards of Care in Diabetes—2023, abridged for primary care providers. Clin Diabetes. 2023;41(1):4-31.
  4. American Diabetes Association. Pharmacologic Approaches to Glycemic Treatment: Standards of Medical Care in Diabetes—2022. Diabetes Care. 2022;45(Suppl 1):S125-S143.
  5. Bakris GL, et al. Effect of finerenone on chronic kidney disease outcomes in type 2 diabetes (FIDELIO-DKD). N Engl J Med. 2020;383:2219-2229. Pitt B, et al. (FIGARO-DKD). N Engl J Med. 2021;385:2252-2263.
  6. Wexler DJ. Initial management of hyperglycemia in adults with type 2 diabetes mellitus. UpToDate (accessed June 2023).

lessons.title: Oxygen Delivery Systems
lessons.subtitle: Facilitator-led chalk talk · 20–25 min (core) / 35–40 min (with cases) · IM attendings & senior residents teaching residents
objectives: Name the factors that affect oxygenation and ventilation.
Identify common oxygen delivery devices and differentiate them.
Differentiate CPAP vs BiPAP and their respiratory and cardiac physiology.
Name the indications (and contraindications) for non-invasive positive-pressure ventilation.

prework.q: 1. Which two settings improve oxygenation, and which two determine ventilation?
prework.a: Oxygenation: FiO₂ and PEEP. Ventilation: tidal volume and respiratory rate (minute ventilation = Vt × RR).

prework.q: 2. Why does a patient in respiratory distress on 6 L nasal cannula get a lower effective FiO₂ than the same flow in a comfortable patient?
prework.a: Distress raises inspiratory flow (30–120 L/min), which far exceeds the cannula's flow, so each breath entrains more ambient air (21% O₂), diluting the delivered oxygen.

prework.q: 3. A COPD patient is hypoxemic AND hypercapnic. Which non-invasive mode, and why?
prework.a: BiPAP. The EPAP (= PEEP) treats hypoxemia; the IPAP−EPAP difference (ΔPAP) augments tidal volume to treat hypercapnia. CPAP only adds PEEP and would not fix ventilation.

segments.label: Hook
segments.html: "Your patient is satting 86%. You can turn two knobs — FiO₂ and PEEP — and you have a menu of devices that each deliver them differently. Today we'll match the right device to the right physiology, and know when to jump to non-invasive ventilation." Project The Basics slide.

segments.label: Objective 1 — Oxygenation vs ventilation
segments.html:
ASK — "What raises oxygenation? What changes ventilation?" Reveal: oxygenation = ↑FiO₂ and ↑PEEP (recruits alveoli); ventilation = Vt and RR.
Pearl. Most ward devices only adjust FiO₂. PEEP requires HFNC (a little), CPAP/BiPAP, or the ventilator. Keep "FiO₂ vs PEEP" in mind as the organizing axis for the whole talk.

segments.label: Objective 2 — Oxygen delivery devices
segments.html:
ASK — "If a patient's inspiratory flow exceeds the device's flow, what happens to delivered FiO₂?" Reveal: ambient air is entrained → effective FiO₂ falls.
DeviceTypical flowMax effective FiO₂Notes
Nasal cannula2–6 L/min~44% at 6 L~3–4% FiO₂ per L; FiO₂ falls with mouth-breathing / high work of breathing
Simple face mask5–10 L/min~60–70%Mask reservoir; needs >6 L to wash out CO₂; precludes eating
Venturi maskset by adapter~60%Delivers a precise, fixed FiO₂ — best for variable breathing patterns
Oxymizerup to ~12–15 L/min>80% (reported)~20 mL pendant/mustache reservoir → higher FiO₂ per flow than NC
Non-rebreather12–15 L/min~75–90%Rescue only — inflate the bag; de-escalate once stable
High-flow nasal cannulaup to ~60 L/min~100%Matched flow, titratable FiO₂, small PEEP ~3–5 cm H₂O; improves WOB & minute ventilation
Pearl — the reservoir concept. The bigger the 100%-O₂ reservoir, the less ambient air is entrained: nasopharynx (~50 mL, plain NC) < oxymizer pendant (~20 mL added) < face-mask cup (100–250 mL) < NRB bag (600–1500 mL). HFNC instead floods the upper airway with high flow so the airway itself becomes the reservoir.
Pearl — HFNC does more than oxygen. Beyond high titratable FiO₂, HFNC washes out anatomic dead space (lowers CO₂ rebreathing), reduces work of breathing, and adds a small PEEP. It's the bridge between simple devices and non-invasive ventilation.

segments.label: Objective 3 — CPAP vs BiPAP
segments.html:
CPAPBiPAP
PressureContinuous (= PEEP)IPAP (inspiration) + EPAP (= PEEP)
AffectsOxygenation onlyOxygenation (EPAP) + ventilation (ΔPAP raises Vt)
Use forHypoxemic failureHypoxemic and hypercapnic failure
ASK — "Name the three hemodynamic effects of positive intrathoracic pressure and why they help in flash pulmonary edema." Reveal: ↓ RV preload, ↓ LV preload, ↓ LV afterload — unloads the failing heart.
Pearl — titrating BiPAP. To improve oxygenation, raise FiO₂ or raise both pressures together (↑EPAP keeping ΔPAP). To improve ventilation (lower CO₂), widen the gap — raise IPAP while holding EPAP, increasing ΔPAP and thus Vt.

segments.label: Objective 4 — Indications & contraindications for NIPPV
segments.html:
ASK — "Where is the evidence for NIPPV strongest, and when is it contraindicated?"
Strong indications (acute)Contraindications
Acute cardiogenic pulmonary edema (CPAP or BiPAP); COPD exacerbation with hypercapnia (BiPAP); immunocompromised AHRF; post-op/post-extubation; palliationCan't protect/remove the mask (AMS, paralysis); active vomiting / copious secretions; facial trauma; recent esophageal or tracheal surgery; hemodynamic instability
Pearl. NIPPV is not recommended for ARDS hypoxemia and is weak in pneumonia unless there's concurrent COPD. For most non-cardiogenic, non-COPD hypoxemic failure, HFNC is now the preferred first step (see Modern Framing).

segments.label: Retrieval close
segments.html: Board off. Ask a learner to rank devices by max FiO₂ and to state which add PEEP. Ask a second learner: CPAP vs BiPAP — what each affects and one indication for each.

cases.title: Case 1 — 56-year-old, HFrEF exacerbation
cases.stem: Diffuse bilateral alveolar infiltrates. T 36.5, HR 110, BP 160/80, RR 32, SaO₂ 75% on RA.
cases.reveal: Given severe hypoxemia, a non-rebreather quickly improves oxygenation as a rescue. On NRB, SaO₂ improves to 89%, RR 28, ABG 7.46/29/59/24.

cases.title: Case 2 — 74-year-old, COPD exacerbation
cases.stem: Sleepy but arousable, diffuse wheeze, clear CXR. T 37.1, HR 120, BP 160/60, RR 32, SaO₂ 82% on RA. ABG 7.21/72/45/28.
cases.reveal: BiPAP — this is hypoxemic and hypercapnic failure; BiPAP treats both. Started on 10/5, 35% FiO₂; repeat ABG 7.30/60/79/28.

cases.title: Case 3 — 64-year-old, POD5 after R nephrectomy
cases.stem: Acute SOB. T 37.1, HR 140, BP 90/60, RR 28, SaO₂ 82% on RA.
cases.reveal: Trial nasal cannula for mild–moderate hypoxemia; escalate as needed. They desaturate to 89% on 6 L NC → step up to oxymizer, Venturi, or HFNC.

mcqs.stem: A patient in respiratory distress (RR 35, accessory muscle use) on a 6 L/min simple face mask has SaO₂ 86%. The team is puzzled the mask "isn't delivering enough oxygen." Best explanation?
opts: Their high inspiratory flow entrains ambient air, lowering effective FiO₂
They have a shunt unresponsive to any oxygen
The wall oxygen is set to 21%
mcqs.exp: B. When inspiratory flow (30–120 L/min in distress) exceeds device flow, each breath pulls in 21%-O₂ ambient air and dilutes the delivered oxygen — a device-flow/inspiratory-flow mismatch. The fix is a device that matches flow and titrates FiO₂ (HFNC) or a Venturi for a fixed FiO₂. C is unlikely to be absolute. A/D/E are not the physiology described.

mcqs.stem: Which device most reliably delivers a precise, set FiO₂ regardless of a patient's variable breathing pattern?
mcqs.exp: C. The Venturi mask entrains a fixed ratio of ambient air per jet adapter, delivering a precise FiO₂ independent of breathing pattern. The others deliver an FiO₂ that varies with flow and inspiratory effort.

mcqs.stem: A COPD patient with pH 7.22, PaCO₂ 70 and SaO₂ 84% is alert. Best initial support?
mcqs.exp: B. Hypercapnic respiratory failure in a COPD exacerbation is the strongest evidence-based indication for BiPAP — IPAP augments tidal volume to clear CO₂ while EPAP supports oxygenation. A only adds PEEP (no ventilation). C/E add FiO₂ but not ventilation. D is premature in an alert, protectable patient.

mcqs.stem: A non-intubated patient with non-cardiogenic acute hypoxemic respiratory failure (P/F 150) is on a non-rebreather. According to current guidance, the best next step to reduce intubation risk is:
mcqs.exp: A. For non-cardiogenic, non-COPD AHRF, HFNC is preferred over conventional oxygen and reduces intubation (FLORALI; ESICM ARDS 2023), and is at least as good as NIV. Track the ROX index and add awake proning. B/E are inferior. C misapplies a COPD indication. D is not routine if non-invasive support can be trialed safely.

mcqs.stem: A patient on HFNC (FiO₂ 0.8, flow 60) has SpO₂ 90%, RR 34. The ROX index is trending down over 6 hours. Best action?
opts: Recognize impending HFNC failure and escalate / prepare for intubation
Add a non-rebreather over the HFNC
mcqs.exp: B. The ROX index = (SpO₂/FiO₂) ÷ RR; values ≥4.88 predict success, and a low or falling ROX predicts HFNC failure — escalate support and don't delay intubation (Roca, AJRCCM 2019). The other options ignore or worsen a deteriorating trajectory.

anki: Two levers that improve oxygenation
FiO2 (more O2 molecules for gas exchange) and PEEP (recruits alveoli).
Two determinants of ventilation
Tidal volume (Vt) and respiratory rate; minute ventilation = Vt × RR.
Why does effective FiO2 fall with respiratory distress?
Inspiratory flow can reach 30–120 L/min, far exceeding device flow, so delivered O2 is diluted by entrained ambient air (21%).
Nasal cannula — practical range and max FiO2
Ideal ~2–4 L/min; ~3–4% FiO2 per L; max ~44% at 6 L/min.
Simple face mask — FiO2 range and a caveat
~40–60% at 5–8 L/min (max ~60–70%); needs >6 L/min to wash out CO2 and prevent rebreathing.
What is a Venturi mask best for?
Delivering a precise, fixed FiO2 to a patient with a variable breathing pattern (max ~60%).
Non-rebreather mask — key teaching point
Rescue device only (effective FiO2 ~75–90% at 12–15 L/min); de-escalate once stable; bag must be inflated.
Oxymizer — how it helps
A small (~20 mL) pendant/mustache reservoir raises effective FiO2 and allows higher flows more comfortably than plain nasal cannula.
High flow nasal cannula — what makes it unique

Heated/humidified high flow (up to ~60 L/min) matches inspiratory demand, finely titratable FiO2 up to ~100%, and provides a small PEEP (~3–5 cm H2O); improves work of breathing and minute ventilation.
CPAP — what it affects
Oxygenation only (continuous pressure = PEEP); use for hypoxemic respiratory failure.
BiPAP — what it affects and how
IPAP and EPAP; EPAP = PEEP (oxygenation); IPAP−EPAP (ΔPAP) increases Vt (ventilation) → use for hypercapnic failure.
Hemodynamic effects of positive pressure ventilation
↓ RV preload, ↓ LV preload, ↓ LV afterload — beneficial in acute cardiogenic pulmonary edema.
Strongest indications for NIPPV in acute respiratory failure
Acute cardiogenic pulmonary edema (CPAP or BiPAP) and COPD exacerbation with hypercapnia (BiPAP).
Contraindications to NIPPV
Can't protect/remove mask (AMS, paralysis), active vomiting/copious secretions, facial trauma, recent esophageal/tracheal surgery, hemodynamic instability.
FLORALI trial (Frat NEJM 2015) take-away
In AHRF, HFNC vs standard O2 vs NIPPV: no difference in 28-day intubation overall, but lower 90-day mortality and more ventilator-free days with HFNC; fewer intubations with HFNC if P/F <200.
2023 ESICM recommendation for non-intubated AHRF
HFNC over conventional oxygen to reduce intubation — except cardiogenic pulmonary edema and COPD exacerbation (use NIPPV).
ROX index — formula and use
(SpO2/FiO2) ÷ RR; ≥4.88 predicts HFNC success; low/falling ROX flags failure — escalate, don't delay intubation.
Awake prone positioning — role
Adjunct for non-intubated COVID-related hypoxemic AHRF on HFNC/NIPPV; reduces intubation (strong rec, 2023 guideline).
lessons.dot:
#Acute hypoxemia: SpO2 *% on *; RR *; ABG *= [pH/pCO2/pO2/HCO3]
type: [hypoxemic / hypercapnic / mixed]
2 levers: FiO2 (most ward devices) + PEEP (HFNC small / CPAP / BiPAP / vent)
device ladder (max effective FiO2):
  NC 2-6L [~44%] -> oxymizer/simple mask [~60-70%] -> Venturi [set FiO2, ~60%]
  -> NRB 12-15L [RESCUE only, ~75-90%] -> HFNC <=60L [~100%, PEEP ~3-5 cm H2O]
  -> NIPPV [CPAP=PEEP only; BiPAP IPAP/EPAP, dPAP raises Vt]
escalation logic:
  hypoxemic non-cardiogenic/non-COPD -> HFNC first (ESICM 2023, FLORALI); + awake proning
  cardiogenic pulm edema -> CPAP/BiPAP (unloads heart: down RV/LV preload, LV afterload)
  COPD + hypercapnia -> BiPAP; titrate IPAP up to lower CO2, EPAP for O2
monitor HFNC: ROX = (SpO2/FiO2)/RR *= [>=4.88 reassuring; falling -> escalate]
NIPPV contraindic: AMS/can't protect airway, vomiting/secretions, facial trauma, recent esoph/tracheal sx, instability
plan: target SpO2 [88-92% if CO2-retainer / 92-96% otherwise]; reassess *; intubate if failing
* = bedside-editable value
takehome: Hypoxemia is corrected by raising FiO₂ or adding PEEP; most ward devices adjust only FiO₂, and effective FiO₂ falls as work of breathing rises.
HFNC delivers high matched flow, titratable FiO₂ to ~100%, and a small PEEP (~3–5 cm H₂O); the non-rebreather is a rescue device only; the Venturi gives a precise set FiO₂.
CPAP supports oxygenation (PEEP); BiPAP adds ventilation via ΔPAP — use it for hypercapnic failure. Both unload the heart in acute cardiogenic pulmonary edema.
Strongest NIPPV indications: acute cardiogenic pulmonary edema and COPD hypercapnic failure. Mind the contraindications.
For non-cardiogenic/non-COPD hypoxemic failure, HFNC is first-line; add awake proning, track the ROX index, and escalate early.

extras.label: Modern Framing — 2022–25 Context currency check
extras.html:
Device physiology, NIPPV mechanics, and indications are timeless and verified current as of June 2026. The items below add modern evidence; none change the lesson's standing teaching.

HFNC is first-line for AHRF. ESICM ARDS 2023 recommends HFNC over conventional oxygen in non-intubated acute hypoxemic respiratory failure to reduce intubation — except cardiogenic pulmonary edema and COPD exacerbation, where NIPPV is preferred. Consistent with FLORALI (Frat, NEJM 2015).
Track the ROX index. ROX = (SpO₂/FiO₂) ÷ RR; ≥4.88 at 2/6/12 h predicts HFNC success, and a low or falling ROX predicts failure — escalate and don't delay intubation (Roca, AJRCCM 2019).
Awake prone positioning. A recommended adjunct for non-intubated patients with COVID-related hypoxemic AHRF on HFNC/NIPPV — reduces intubation (strong recommendation, 2023 rapid practice guideline). Trial ~15 min and continue if tolerated.
CPAP in COVID AHRF (RECOVERY-RS, JAMA 2022). CPAP reduced the intubation/death composite vs conventional oxygen; HFNC did not differ in that trial. Across AHRF generally, HFNC and NIV are similarly effective at avoiding intubation (2023 meta-analysis) — choose based on the failure type and local resources.

  2. Frat JP, et al. High-flow oxygen through nasal cannula in acute hypoxemic respiratory failure (FLORALI). N Engl J Med. 2015;372:2185–2196.
  3. Hernández G, et al. Postextubation high-flow nasal cannula vs conventional oxygen on reintubation. JAMA. 2016;315:1354–1361.
  4. Roca O, et al. An index combining respiratory rate and oxygenation to predict outcome of nasal high-flow therapy (ROX). Am J Respir Crit Care Med. 2019;199:1368–1376.
  5. Grasselli G, et al. ESICM guidelines on acute respiratory distress syndrome. Intensive Care Med. 2023.
  6. Perkins GD, et al. Effect of noninvasive respiratory strategies on intubation or mortality in COVID-19 (RECOVERY-RS). JAMA. 2022;327:546–558.
  7. Rochwerg B, et al. ERS/ATS guidelines: noninvasive ventilation for acute respiratory failure. Eur Respir J. 2017;50:1602426.
