lessons.title: Polycystic Ovary Syndrome
objectives: Identify symptoms and clinical signs concerning for PCOS.
Develop a differential and initial evaluation for patients with symptoms concerning for PCOS.
Identify gynecologic and non-gynecologic comorbidities commonly associated with PCOS.

prework.q: 1. What are the three Rotterdam criteria, and how many are needed to diagnose PCOS?
prework.a: (1) Hyperandrogenism — clinical (acne, hirsutism, androgenic alopecia) or biochemical (↑ androgens); (2) menstrual irregularity / ovulatory dysfunction (oligomenorrhea, amenorrhea); (3) polycystic ovaries on ultrasound (>12 follicles and/or volume >10 cc). Two of three — AND exclusion of mimics.

prework.q: 2. Why is PCOS called a "diagnosis of exclusion," and what is the single best test for hyperandrogenism?
prework.a: Many conditions (CAH, thyroid disease, hyperprolactinemia, Cushing's, androgen-secreting tumors, pregnancy) mimic its features, so secondary causes must be excluded first. The single best test for hyperandrogenism is total testosterone (free testosterone is unreliable; can calculate the free androgen index from total T and SHBG).

prework.q: 3. Name three comorbidities every PCOS patient should be screened for.
prework.a: Insulin resistance/T2DM (A1c, fasting glucose, or 2-h OGTT — 7.5–10× higher undiagnosed T2DM); cardiovascular risk (BP, fasting lipids); mood/eating disorders (PHQ-9, GAD-7, binge eating). Also endometrial hyperplasia (3× cancer risk), infertility, OSA, NAFLD.

segments.label: 0:00–2:00 — Hook
segments.html:
PCOS affects 8–13% of people with ovaries of reproductive age — one of the most common endocrine disorders you'll see. Its two core features are hyperandrogenism and menstrual dysfunction, but the teaching trap is that it's a diagnosis of exclusion wrapped in a positive-criteria set.

ASK: "A 24-year-old has irregular periods and acne. Can you diagnose PCOS today?" (Not yet — you need 2 of 3 Rotterdam criteria AND exclusion of mimics. Acne alone is an unreliable sign of hyperandrogenism.)

segments.label: 2:00–9:00 — Objective 1: Diagnosis (Rotterdam) (Diagnosis board)
segments.html:
Rotterdam criterion (need 2 of 3)Definition / teaching point
HyperandrogenismClinical (hirsutism by Ferriman-Gallwey, acne, androgenic alopecia) OR biochemical (↑ androgens). Serum testing misses 20–40% of biochemical hyperandrogenism. Voice deepening or clitoromegaly → think androgen-secreting tumor, not PCOS
Menstrual irregularityChronic anovulation: oligomenorrhea (<8 cycles/yr or cycle >35 d) or amenorrhea (no menses ×3 mo). Regular cycles don't fully exclude anovulation (unopposed estrogen → DUB)
Polycystic ovaries>12 follicles and/or ovarian volume >10 cc on ultrasound. Non-specific in isolation — common in normal young cycling patients
Pearl: Hirsutism is androgen-pattern terminal hair (upper lip, chin, chest, linea alba, inner thighs, lower back). Acne and alopecia in isolation are unreliable signs of hyperandrogenism — anchor on hirsutism and labs.

segments.label: 9:00–15:00 — Objective 2: Differential & evaluation (DDx and Eval board)
segments.html:
Because PCOS is a diagnosis of exclusion, drive the workup off each criterion:

FeatureMimics to excludeInitial evaluation
HyperandrogenismNon-classic CAH (21-hydroxylase deficiency), acromegaly, androgen-secreting tumor, Cushing'sTotal testosterone; ± 17-OH progesterone (CAH); ± SHBG, DHEAS (tumor if very high T / rapid virilization); midnight salivary cortisol if Cushingoid
Menstrual irregularityPregnancy, thyroid disease, hyperprolactinemia/prolactinoma, hypothalamic amenorrhea, POI/perimenopause, structural (polyp, fibroid, hyperplasia/malignancy)TSH ± free T4, β-hCG, prolactin; ± pelvic US (structural); ± LH/FSH (hypothalamic dysfunction, POI)
Polycystic ovariesNormal ovulatory cycles, exogenous testosterone useInterpret in context — non-specific alone
Pearl: Total testosterone is the single best androgen test (free T assays are unreliable). An increased LH/FSH ratio supports PCOS but its absence doesn't rule it out (LH secretion is pulsatile). Reserve DHEAS / midnight salivary cortisol for clinical red flags (very high T, rapid virilization, Cushingoid features).

segments.label: 15:00–20:00 — Objective 3: Comorbidities (Co-Morbidities boards)
segments.html:
Non-gynecologicScreen / manage
Mood disorders (depression, anxiety, binge eating) — independent of weight/hirsutism/infertilityPHQ-9, GAD-7; ask about disordered eating; refer
Hyperandrogenism (hirsutism)Combined OCPs (less-androgenic 3rd/4th-gen progestin) ± spironolactone (50 mg → up to 100 mg BID; teratogenic — need contraception); cosmetic (laser)
Cardiovascular risk (↑ LDL, ↓ HDL)Screen BP + fasting lipids; statin by calculated CV risk
Insulin resistance / T2DM (7.5–10× higher undiagnosed T2DM)2-h OGTT (or fasting glucose / A1c) q1–2y; lifestyle ± metformin (also helps cycles, androgens, fertility)
Also: OSA, NAFLDScreen per symptoms
GynecologicScreen / manage
Endometrial hyperplasia (3× endometrial cancer risk from chronic unopposed estrogen)Ensure a withdrawal bleed ≥ every 90 days: OCPs, progestin IUD, or cyclic progesterone. Routine US screening NOT recommended
Abnormal uterine bleedingCombined OCPs (less-androgenic progestin) — also helps hirsutism
Infertility (most common cause of anovulatory infertility; ~5–10%/cycle vs 10–15%)Discuss fertility goals early; 5–10% weight loss; refer OB/Gyn or REI; clomiphene/letrozole for ovulation induction
Pregnancy complications (early loss, GDM, PIH/pre-eclampsia, preterm labor — GDM risk independent of BMI)Close monitoring; treat as they arise
Pearl: The 90-day rule is the single most actionable gynecologic point — chronic anovulation means no progesterone to oppose estrogen, so the endometrium needs a scheduled withdrawal bleed at least quarterly to prevent hyperplasia.

Retrieval close: board down — have learners rebuild the 3 Rotterdam criteria, the "all-patients" labs (testosterone, TSH, prolactin, β-hCG), and the comorbidity screening panel (OGTT/A1c, lipids, PHQ-9/GAD-7, 90-day endometrial protection).

lessons.casesLabel: Cases (from the original board — answers click-to-reveal)

cases.title: Case 1 — Olga, 24-year-old woman, irregular periods since menarche (every 45–60 days); FHx type 2 DM
cases.reveal: Screen for the causes/associated signs of oligomenorrhea: hirsutism (PCOS/hyperandrogenism), breast discharge (prolactinoma), hot flashes (premature ovarian failure), weight gain (Cushing's, hypothyroidism), weight loss (stress, malnutrition, hyperthyroidism), sexual activity (pregnancy, STIs), abnormal pelvic exam (structural cause — polyp, fibroid). She has acne, some chin hair growth, slow weight gain, and is not sexually active.

mcqs.stem: A 26-year-old woman has hirsutism and cycles every 50 days. Total testosterone is mildly elevated; TSH, prolactin, and β-hCG are normal. Pelvic ultrasound is not yet done. What is the most accurate statement about her diagnosis?
opts: She cannot be diagnosed without ultrasound
She already meets criteria for PCOS (hyperandrogenism + ovulatory dysfunction), with mimics excluded
She needs an LH/FSH ratio >2 to confirm PCOS
A normal DHEAS rules out PCOS
Acne alone would have been sufficient
mcqs.exp: B. With hyperandrogenism plus ovulatory dysfunction and mimics excluded, she meets 2 of 3 Rotterdam criteria — ultrasound (or AMH) is not required (this is ~70% of cases per the 2023 guideline). A — US is unnecessary when 2 criteria are already met. C — an elevated LH/FSH supports but isn't required; its absence doesn't exclude PCOS. D — DHEAS is for tumor workup, not a rule-out. E — acne in isolation is an unreliable sign of hyperandrogenism.

mcqs.stem: A 28-year-old woman is being evaluated for hyperandrogenism. Which single test is the best initial measure of androgen excess?
mcqs.exp: B. Total testosterone is the single best test; free testosterone immunoassays are unreliable (the free androgen index can be calculated from total T and SHBG). C — DHEAS is reserved for suspected adrenal/androgen-secreting tumor (very high T, rapid virilization). D — 17-OHP screens for non-classic CAH, not the primary androgen measure. E — LH/FSH supports but doesn't quantify androgen excess.

mcqs.stem: A 30-year-old woman with PCOS has chronic anovulation and does not desire pregnancy. Which intervention most directly reduces her risk of endometrial hyperplasia?
opts: Ensuring a withdrawal bleed at least every 90 days (OCP, progestin IUD, or cyclic progesterone)
mcqs.exp: C. Chronic unopposed estrogen drives a 3× endometrial cancer risk; a progestin-based regimen producing a withdrawal bleed ≥ every 90 days promotes endometrial turnover. A — routine US screening for endometrial cancer is not recommended. B — spironolactone treats hirsutism, not the endometrium (and is teratogenic). D/E — metformin and statins address metabolic/CV risk, not endometrial protection.

mcqs.stem: Which comorbidity screening is recommended for ALL patients with PCOS?
opts: Glucose testing (A1c, fasting glucose, or 2-h OGTT), fasting lipids, and mood/eating-disorder screening
mcqs.exp: B. All PCOS patients should be screened for impaired glucose tolerance/T2DM (q1–2y), cardiovascular risk (BP + fasting lipids), and psychological comorbidity (PHQ-9, GAD-7, disordered eating). A/C/D/E — these are directed tests for specific clinical concerns (e.g., OSA symptoms, Cushingoid features), not universal screens.

mcqs.stem: A 17-year-old, 2 years post-menarche, has hirsutism and irregular cycles. Per the 2023 PCOS guideline, which is correct for adolescents?
opts: Pelvic ultrasound is required to diagnose PCOS
AMH should be used in place of ultrasound
Both hyperandrogenism and ovulatory dysfunction are required; ultrasound and AMH are not recommended
One criterion is sufficient at this age
Irregular cycles are always pathologic at this age
mcqs.exp: C. In adolescents the 2023 guideline requires BOTH hyperandrogenism AND ovulatory dysfunction, and does not recommend ultrasound or AMH (poor specificity <8 years post-menarche). A/B — neither US nor AMH is used in adolescents. D — one criterion is insufficient. E — irregular cycles can be normal for 2–3 years after menarche.

anki: Prevalence of PCOS in reproductive-age people with ovaries?
Three Rotterdam criteria (need 2 of 3)?
(1) Hyperandrogenism (clinical or biochemical); (2) menstrual irregularity/ovulatory dysfunction; (3) polycystic ovaries on US
Definition of polycystic ovaries on ultrasound?
>12 follicles and/or ovarian volume >10 cc
Oligomenorrhea vs amenorrhea?
Oligomenorrhea: <8 cycles/yr or cycle >35 d; amenorrhea: no menses x3 months
Single best test for hyperandrogenism?
Total testosterone (free testosterone assays are unreliable)
Mimics of hyperandrogenism to exclude?
Non-classic CAH (21-OH deficiency), acromegaly, androgen-secreting tumor, Cushing's
"All-patients" labs for menstrual irregularity?
TSH +/- free T4, beta-hCG, prolactin (rule out pregnancy, thyroid, hyperprolactinemia)
Red flags suggesting androgen-secreting tumor over PCOS?
Very high testosterone, rapid virilization, voice deepening, clitoromegaly -> check DHEAS
First-line treatment for hirsutism in PCOS?
Combined OCPs (less-androgenic progestin) +/- spironolactone (teratogenic - needs contraception)
Why screen PCOS patients for diabetes, and how often?
7.5-10x higher undiagnosed T2DM; screen with A1c/fasting glucose/2-h OGTT every 1-2 years
Endometrial cancer risk in PCOS and why?
3x higher - chronic anovulation -> unopposed estrogen
The "90-day rule" for PCOS?
Ensure a withdrawal bleed at least every 90 days (OCP, progestin IUD, or cyclic progesterone) to prevent hyperplasia
Most common cause of anovulatory infertility in the US?
PCOS (~5-10% conception/cycle vs 10-15% normal)
First-line for ovulation induction in PCOS?
Letrozole or clomiphene (after 5-10% weight loss in overweight/obese patients)
Mood comorbidity screening in PCOS?
PHQ-9 and GAD-7; ask about disordered/binge eating (risk independent of weight)
Pregnancy complications associated with PCOS?
Early pregnancy loss, GDM (independent of BMI), pregnancy-induced HTN/pre-eclampsia, preterm labor
2023 guideline: alternative to ultrasound for PCOM in adults?
Anti-Mullerian hormone (AMH) - adults only, not adolescents
2023 guideline: adolescent diagnostic requirement?
Both hyperandrogenism AND ovulatory dysfunction; no US or AMH (<8 y post-menarche)
lessons.dot:
#PCOS (chronic): Rotterdam ≥2/3 + diagnosis of EXCLUSION
criteria met: [hyperandrogenism (clinical hirsutism/acne/alopecia OR biochem ↑T) * / ovulatory dysfx (oligo <8 cyc/yr or >35d / amenorrhea >3mo) * / PCOM on US >12 follicles or vol >10cc — or AMH in adults (2023) *]
excluded mimics: total testosterone [*] ; TSH±fT4 [*] ; prolactin [*] ; β-hCG [*] ; ±17-OHP (CAH) ; ±DHEAS/midnight salivary cortisol if very-high T/rapid virilization/Cushingoid ; ±LH/FSH/pelvic US per picture
comorbidity screen (ALL pts):
  metabolic → A1c / fasting glucose / 2h-OGTT q1-2y [*] ; lifestyle ± metformin
  CV → BP + fasting lipid panel [*] ; statin per calculated risk
  psych → PHQ-9 + GAD-7 + disordered-eating screen [*]
  ± OSA, NAFLD per sx
gyn management:
  hyperandrogenism/hirsutism → combined OCP (less-androgenic progestin: norgestimate/desogestrel/drospirenone) ± spironolactone 50→100 BID (teratogenic→contraception)
  endometrial protection (3× CA risk) → withdrawal bleed ≥ q90d via OCP / progestin IUD / cyclic progesterone ; NO routine US screening
  fertility → discuss goals; 5-10% wt loss; refer OB-Gyn/REI; letrozole or clomiphene for ovulation induction
  pregnancy → ↑ early loss, GDM (BMI-independent), pre-eclampsia, preterm — monitor
2023 guideline: AMH may replace US in ADULTS; adolescents need BOTH hyperandrogenism + ovulatory dysfx (no US/AMH); GLP-1 RA may be considered for weight (limited evidence)
takehome: PCOS is defined by the Rotterdam criteria — hyperandrogenism, menstrual irregularity, and polycystic ovaries on ultrasound (or AMH in adults per the 2023 guideline). You need two of three.

It is a diagnosis of exclusion — rule out other causes of menstrual irregularity and/or hyperandrogenism (pregnancy, thyroid, prolactin, CAH, Cushing's, androgen-secreting tumor) before assigning the diagnosis.

Screen for the many associated comorbidities: abnormal uterine bleeding, endometrial hyperplasia/cancer, insulin resistance/T2DM, cardiovascular risk, mood/eating disorders, infertility, OSA/NAFLD, and pregnancy complications.

extras.label: 2023 Guideline Update added
extras.html:
The deck builds on the 2018 international guideline. The 2023 International Evidence-Based Guideline for PCOS (Teede et al.) keeps the Rotterdam framework and the "diagnosis of exclusion" approach, with these refinements:

  • AMH as an alternative to ultrasound: in adults, anti-Müllerian hormone may establish polycystic ovarian morphology in place of pelvic ultrasound. For patients with irregular cycles + hyperandrogenism (~70% of cases), neither US nor AMH is required — those two criteria already make the diagnosis.
  • Adolescents: require BOTH hyperandrogenism AND ovulatory dysfunction; ultrasound and AMH are NOT recommended (<8 years post-menarche) due to poor specificity.
  • Broader burden emphasized: metabolic and cardiovascular risk, obstructive sleep apnea, and very high prevalence of psychological features — screen for depression, anxiety, and disordered/binge eating.
  • Therapy: lifestyle first; metformin for metabolic/weight/cycle benefit; combined OCPs ± anti-androgen for hyperandrogenism and cycle control. Anti-obesity pharmacotherapy, including GLP-1 receptor agonists, may be considered for weight in adults — evidence in PCOS is still limited and flagged as a research priority.

  6. ACOG Practice Bulletin No. 194: Polycystic Ovary Syndrome. Obstet Gynecol. 2018;131(6):e157-e171.

lessons.title: Pericardial Effusion Without Tamponade (POCUS)
objectives: Identify a pericardial effusion in the parasternal long-axis (PLAX) view and distinguish it from a pleural effusion.
Build a focused differential for pericardial effusion based on the clinical setting.
Describe the key clinical features that suggest tamponade physiology.

prework.q: 1. On the PLAX view, how do you tell a pericardial from a pleural effusion?
prework.a: A pericardial effusion lies anterior to the descending thoracic aorta; a pleural effusion lies posterior to it. Confirm in a second view.

prework.q: 2. Name the three key clinical features of cardiac tamponade.
prework.a: Elevated JVP, tachycardia, and pulsus paradoxus >10 mmHg. (Beck's triad is rarely present in medical tamponade.)

prework.q: 3. What is the single most common cause of pericardial effusion in the developed world? In the developing world?
prework.a: Developed world: viral. Developing world: tuberculosis (>70%).

segments.label: Hook (2 min)
segments.html: "Your 78-year-old patient with ESKD comes in with a viral-sounding illness and fatigue. HR 110, BP 100/60, SpO2 82% on 3 L. You grab the probe and put it on the chest. In 90 seconds, POCUS will change what you're worried about — and what you do next. Let's read this clip together."

segments.label: Segment 1 — Identify the view & finding (Objective 1)
segments.html:
ASK: "What cardiac view is this, and what's your one-line interpretation?"

Reveal: Parasternal long axis (PLAX). Grossly normal chamber sizes and LV function with a small circumferential pericardial effusion.

ASK: "How do you know that dark stripe is pericardial and not pleural fluid?"
FeaturePericardial effusionPleural effusion
Relation to descending thoracic aorta (PLAX)Anterior to the aortaPosterior to the aorta
Most sensitive windowSubxiphoid (subcostal) view
Pearl: Always confirm any cardiac POCUS finding in a second view before you act on it. The aorta is your landmark: fluid in front of it is in the pericardium, behind it is in the pleura.

segments.label: Segment 2 — Differential by setting (Objective 2)
segments.html:
ASK: "Given an ESKD patient with a viral prodrome — what's on your differential for the effusion?"
Infectious (more common)Non-infectious (less common)
Viral — most common in developed world (Coxsackie, echovirus, HHV6, parvovirus B19, COVID-19)
Tuberculosis — >70% in developing world		Autoimmune · malignancy · uremia · post-traumatic/post-MI · radiation · hypothyroidism
Pearl: Match the differential to the patient. Here, both a viral pericarditis and uremic effusion (ESKD) are in play — the clinical course and labs sort it out.

segments.label: Segment 3 — Assess for tamponade (Objective 3)
segments.html:
ASK: "Does this patient need a pericardiocentesis right now? What would tell you the effusion is hemodynamically significant?"

Reveal: No — likely viral, no signs of hemodynamic compromise. Tamponade is a clinical diagnosis supported by imaging, not a number on the screen.

Physical findingFrequency in tamponade (%)
Elevated JVP100
Tachycardia81–100
Pulsus paradoxus >10 mmHg98
Beck's triad (↑JVP, hypotension, muffled tones)0–19
ASK: "How is pulsus paradoxus measured, and how do higher cutoffs change the test?"

Inspiratory fall in systolic BP >10 mmHg. Raising the cutoff increases specificity and decreases sensitivity: >10 mmHg LR+ 3.3 (LR− 0.03); >12 mmHg LR+ 5.9 (LR− 0.05).

Pearl — 2025 ESC framing: Tamponade is a dynamic process driven by the rate of fluid accumulation, not absolute size. A small, rapidly accumulating effusion can tamponade; a large chronic one may not.

segments.label: Retrieval close (1 min)
segments.html: Have a learner rebuild the take-home points from memory: the aorta rule, the three tamponade signs, and "treat the cause when there's no compromise."

lessons.casesIntro: 78-year-old with ESKD, URI symptoms and fatigue. HR 110, RR 18, BP 100/60, SpO2 82% on 3 L NC. PLAX clip shows a small circumferential pericardial effusion with normal chamber sizes and LV function.

cases.title: The Case (click to reveal) 1
cases.stem: What view is this and what is the finding?
cases.reveal: PLAX; small circumferential pericardial effusion, normal LV function.

cases.title: The Case (click to reveal) 2
cases.stem: Pericardial or pleural — how do you prove it?
cases.reveal: The fluid lies anterior to the descending aorta → pericardial. Confirm in a subxiphoid view.

cases.title: The Case (click to reveal) 3
cases.stem: Most likely causes here?
cases.reveal: Viral pericarditis and/or uremic effusion (ESKD). Less likely: autoimmune, malignancy, hypothyroidism.

cases.title: The Case (click to reveal) 4
cases.stem: Does the patient need pericardiocentesis?
cases.reveal: No — no tamponade signs (no elevated JVP/pulsus/hemodynamic compromise). Supportive care; treat the underlying cause. He improves clinically.

mcqs.stem: A 64-year-old man has a PLAX clip showing an anechoic stripe. To distinguish a pericardial from a pleural effusion in this view, you should note the fluid's relationship to which structure?
mcqs.exp: C. In PLAX, a pericardial effusion tracks anterior to the descending thoracic aorta, whereas a pleural effusion lies posterior to it. The aorta is the dividing landmark. The MV, LA, RVOT, and AV do not reliably separate the two fluid spaces.

mcqs.stem: You see a moderate pericardial effusion on PLAX. Which single view is MOST sensitive for confirming and sizing a pericardial effusion?
mcqs.exp: B. The subxiphoid view is the most sensitive cardiac window for detecting a pericardial effusion and is the standard confirmatory/second view. Always corroborate a POCUS finding in a second window.

mcqs.stem: A 40-year-old woman with a large pericardial effusion has BP 96/70, HR 118, JVP elevated, and an inspiratory systolic BP drop of 14 mmHg. Which finding most strongly raises the likelihood of tamponade?
mcqs.exp: C. Pulsus paradoxus >12 mmHg carries an LR+ of 5.9 for tamponade (>10 mmHg LR+ 3.3); higher cutoffs increase specificity. Beck's triad is present in only 0–19% of medical tamponade and is unreliable. Effusion size alone does not establish tamponade — physiology does.

mcqs.stem: A patient from a TB-endemic region has a large pericardial effusion. Which is the single most likely cause in that setting?
mcqs.exp: C. Tuberculosis causes >70% of pericardial effusions in the developing world. Viral is the most common cause in the developed world. The differential should always be weighted by the clinical setting.

mcqs.stem: A 55-year-old develops hypotension 30 minutes after a cardiac procedure. Echo shows only a SMALL pericardial effusion, yet there is RA/RV collapse and pulsus paradoxus. Best explanation per current (2025 ESC) understanding?
opts: Tamponade depends on the rate of accumulation, not absolute size
Pericardiocentesis is contraindicated for small effusions
The finding must be a pleural effusion
mcqs.exp: B. Tamponade is a dynamic process governed by the rate of fluid accumulation and pericardial compliance, not absolute volume — a rapidly accumulating small effusion (e.g., post-procedure) can tamponade. Management is urgent pericardiocentesis for hemodynamically significant effusion (2025 ESC). Beck's triad is unreliable; the RA/RV collapse and pulsus confirm physiology.

anki: In the PLAX view, where does a pericardial effusion lie relative to the descending thoracic aorta?
Anterior to the descending aorta (a pleural effusion lies posterior to it).
In the PLAX view, where does a pleural effusion lie relative to the descending thoracic aorta?
Posterior to the descending aorta (a pericardial effusion lies anterior to it).
Which cardiac window is the MOST sensitive for detecting a pericardial effusion?
The subxiphoid (subcostal) view.
What is a core POCUS habit when you see any cardiac ultrasound finding?
Always confirm it in a second view before acting on it.
What three factors determine the clinical significance of a pericardial effusion?
Size, chronicity (compare to prior echo), and clinical evidence of tamponade.
What are the three key clinical features of cardiac tamponade?
Elevated JVP, tachycardia, and pulsus paradoxus >10 mmHg.
How does raising the pulsus paradoxus cutoff change its test characteristics for tamponade?
Higher cutoffs increase specificity and decrease sensitivity.
What is the LR+ of pulsus paradoxus >10 mmHg for tamponade? Of >12 mmHg?
What is Beck's triad and how reliable is it in medical tamponade?
Elevated JVP, hypotension, and muffled heart tones; present in only 0–19% of medical tamponade — NOT a reliable predictor.
What is the most common cause of pericardial effusion in the developed world?
Viral (Coxsackie, echovirus, HHV6, parvovirus B19, COVID-19).
What is the most common cause of pericardial effusion in the developing world?
Tuberculosis (>70% of cases).
List the major non-infectious causes of pericardial effusion.
Autoimmunity, malignancy, uremia, post-traumatic/post-MI, radiation, and hypothyroidism.
Per the 2025 ESC guideline, tamponade is best understood as a function of what — not absolute effusion size?
The RATE of fluid accumulation (a dynamic process); a small rapid effusion can tamponade, a large chronic one may not.
What is first-line management of a hemodynamically significant pericardial effusion or tamponade?
Pericardiocentesis (surgical pericardial window for recurrent/loculated/purulent effusion or when drainage is unsafe/ineffective). (2025 ESC)
When are CMR and cardiac CT each Class I in pericardial disease (2025 ESC)?
CMR when diagnosis is unclear or myocarditis is suspected; cardiac CT for loculated/complex effusion, calcification, or masses.
What unifying clinical concept did the 2025 ESC guideline introduce?
Inflammatory Myopericardial Syndrome (IMPS) — merging myocarditis and pericardial disease along the myocardium–pericardium continuum.
A trace pericardial effusion in an asymptomatic patient — how should it be interpreted?
May be normal; interpret in clinical context. Determine chronicity by reviewing prior echocardiograms.
lessons.dot:
#POCUS cardiac — pericardial effusion:
PLAX *: anechoic stripe ANTERIOR to descending aorta = pericardial (vs posterior = pleural)
confirmed 2nd view [subxiphoid*/apical] · size [trace/small/mod/large*] · [circumferential*/loculated] · LVEF [grossly nl*/reduced]
likely 2/2 * [viral/uremic-ESKD/autoimmune/malignant/post-MI/post-procedure/TB/hypothyroid]
tamponade screen: JVP [nl*/↑] · HR [* ] · pulsus paradoxus [<10*/>10/>12] mmHg (>10 LR+3.3, >12 LR+5.9)
  echo signs [none*/RA systolic collapse/RV diastolic collapse/IVC plethora/respirophasic inflow]
  NB tamponade = dynamic, driven by RATE not size (2025 ESC)
hemodynamics: [stable, no compromise*  /  compromised → urgent]
plan:
 - [no pericardiocentesis* — treat cause / pericardiocentesis if hemodynamically significant or tamponade]
 - tx underlying: *
 - monitor: serial exam, repeat POCUS, [tele*], BP/pulsus trend
 - imaging if needed: CMR [dx unclear/myocarditis], cardiac CT [loculated/complex/calcified*]
AVOID: acting on size alone; relying on Beck's triad (0–19%); single-view dx
takehome: On PLAX, a pericardial effusion lies anterior to the descending aorta; a pleural effusion lies posterior. Confirm in a second view (subxiphoid is most sensitive).

Most pericardial effusions do not cause tamponade. The key clinical features are elevated JVP, tachycardia, and pulsus paradoxus >10 mmHg — and tamponade is driven by the rate of accumulation, not size.
Weight the differential by setting (viral vs TB vs uremic, etc.), and treat the cause when there is no hemodynamic compromise.

DeckOriginal 4-slide board preserved (animations + POCUS video clips intact); 3 slides appended (2025 ESC update / take-home / attribution) → 7 total.
Human review neededNone. Oncology/guideline content evolves — clinician-review before teaching; the deck's tamponade frequency figures (JVP 100%, pulsus 98%, Beck's 0–19%) are the authors' sourced values (Stolz/Roy/McGee) and were left unchanged.

lessons.title: Plasma Cell Dyscrasias
lessons.subtitle: Optimized lesson package · Facilitator-led interactive chalk talk for IM residents · 40–60 min total
lessons.credit: Adapted from TeachIM.org — Plasma Cell Dyscrasias (Colton, Forsberg; sec. ed. Geiger; exec. ed. Zhang; Jan 2023), CC BY-NC 4.0. Optimized June 2026: guideline currency verified, three deck errors corrected surgically, pedagogy restructured, assessments added. Companion deck: Plasma_Cell_Dyscrasias_Optimized.pptx (original interactive board, surgically corrected, animations preserved).
objectives: Review the pathogenesis and etiology of the most common complications of multiple myeloma (MM) — the CRABI features.
Describe the basics of testing for plasma cell dyscrasias: SPEP with immunofixation, serum free light chains (FLC), and the role (and limits) of UPEP.

Differentiate monoclonal gammopathy of undetermined significance (MGUS), smoldering myeloma (SMM), and MM by M-protein size, marrow plasma-cell burden, and end-organ damage / myeloma-defining biomarkers.
Bonus: Understand prognosis and natural course of MM (R-ISS / R2-ISS; induction → autoSCT → maintenance).
Bonus: Recognize complications of MM and the side effects of commonly used therapies.

prework.q: Q1. What does the mnemonic CRABI stand for, and what is the underlying mechanism for each?
prework.a: Calcium (hyperCalcemia, from osteoclast activation / RANK-L), Renal dysfunction (paraprotein/light-chain cast nephropathy), Anemia (marrow infiltration), Boney lytic lesions (osteoclast activation), Immunodeficiency (suppression of normal plasma cells → hypogammaglobulinemia, infection). These are the end-organ-damage criteria that define MM.

prework.q: Q2. A urine dipstick reads "negative protein," yet you suspect myeloma. Why can the dipstick be falsely reassuring?
prework.a: Urine dipstick detects albumin only. It misses monoclonal free light chains ("Bence Jones proteins"), which are what spill into urine in many plasma cell dyscrasias. You need UPEP with immunofixation or — far more conveniently — a serum free light chain (FLC) assay to detect them.

prework.q: Q3. Name the three screening tests you would order together to work up a suspected plasma cell dyscrasia, and why FLC matters.
prework.a: SPEP + serum immunofixation + serum FLC. SPEP+IFX alone is ~80% sensitive; adding FLC raises sensitivity to ~99% and captures the ~16% of patients with light-chain-only myeloma that SPEP misses — and it replaces the cumbersome 24-hour UPEP.

segments.label: Hook (2 min)
segments.html: "A 55-year-old is admitted for AKI and gets a routine UA — dipstick protein is negative. The hospitalist almost stops there. Two weeks later their SPEP shows a 3.5 g/dL IgA-lambda M-spike." Ask: how did a 'negative' urine protein nearly miss myeloma? (Dipstick reads albumin, not Bence Jones light chains.) You're planting Case 1.

segments.label: Objective 1 — Pathogenesis & the CRABI complications
segments.html:
Briefly name the family of plasma cell disorders (MGUS, SMM, MM, solitary plasmacytoma, Waldenström, AL amyloid, POEMS, cryoglobulinemia), then narrow to the three this talk covers: MGUS, SMM, MM. Click the lineage/structure buttons: lymphoid progenitor → B cell → plasma cell; each immunoglobulin = heavy chain + light chain (κ or λ). A clone overproduces one → an M protein (paraprotein) above the polyclonal background.

ASK: "What clinical findings come from (a) marrow infiltration, (b) RANK-L / osteoclast activation, (c) paraprotein deposition?" Reveal each: marrow infiltration → anemia + immunodeficiency; RANK-L → hypercalcemia + lytic bone lesions; paraprotein → renal disease (cast nephropathy in MM; nephrotic-range in AL amyloid).
PEARL: The complications aren't a random list — they map 1:1 to three mechanisms. CRABI = the marrow (A, I), the bone axis (C, B), and the paraprotein (R). Teach the mechanism and the mnemonic writes itself.

segments.label: Objective 2 — Testing: SPEP, UPEP, FLC
segments.html:
Build the SPEP: electrophoresis separates serum protein by charge into albumin / α1 / α2 / β / γ. A monoclonal M-spike is usually in the γ region (sometimes β or α2). SPEP tells you the size (concentration) of the spike. Immunofixation (IFX) is more sensitive and types the heavy + light chain (e.g., IgG-κ) but gives no size — always pair it with SPEP. SPEP+IFX ≈ 80% sensitive.

TestWhat it addsSensitivity (cumulative)
SPEP + immunofixationDetects & sizes M-protein; types heavy/light chain~80%
+ UPEP (24-h urine)Catches Bence Jones light chains in urine~95%
+ serum FLC (κ/λ)Catches light-chain-only disease; no 24-h collection~99%
ASK: "Urine dipstick protein is negative — can you stop?" No. Dipstick = albumin only; it misses Bence Jones free light chains. UPEP or (better) serum FLC is needed.

FLC ratio (κ/λ): normal 0.26–1.65. Ratio >1.65 → κ clone; <0.26 → λ clone. Renal failure raises the ratio (reduced clearance, up to ~3) — so use stricter thresholds (>4 or <0.25) to flag a true clone in ESRD. A markedly skewed ratio (>100 or <0.01) with a high involved FLC can itself be myeloma-defining.

PEARL: Up to 16% of myeloma is light-chain-only and can be invisible on SPEP. Order SPEP+IFX and serum FLC together — the FLC both rescues those cases and lets you skip the 24-h UPEP.

segments.label: Objective 3 — MGUS vs SMM vs MM
segments.html:
Three axes separate them: M-protein size, % clonal marrow plasma cells, and end-organ damage (CRABI) or a myeloma-defining biomarker.

MGUSSmoldering MMMultiple Myeloma
Serum M-protein<3 g/dL≥3 g/dL≥3 g/dL*
Urine M-protein<500 mg/24h≥500 mg/24h≥500 mg/24h*
Clonal marrow plasma cells<10%10–60%≥10% (≥60% is diagnostic)
End-organ damageNoneNoneCRABI or a myeloma-defining event
Progression risk~1%/yr~10%/yr (first 5 yr)—

*Oligo-secretory (M-protein <1 g/dL) and non-secretory MM will not meet the M-protein criterion — diagnose on marrow / biomarkers / FLC.

Myeloma-defining events (IMWG "SLiM" biomarkers), any one of which = MM even without CRABI: clonal marrow plasma cells ≥60%; involved:uninvolved serum FLC ratio ≥100 (involved FLC ≥100 mg/L); >1 focal lesion on MRI.

ASK: "Why bother separating MGUS from smoldering myeloma if neither is treated?" Because the slopes differ a lot — MGUS ~1%/yr vs SMM ~10%/yr for the first 5 years — which drives how closely you monitor (and, increasingly, whether to intervene early in high-risk SMM).
PEARL: SMM staging needs sensitive imaging to be sure you're not missing a lytic lesion (which would reclassify as MM). Whole-body X-ray is easy but insensitive; whole-body low-dose CT or MRI is preferred, and PET/CT is the most sensitive.

segments.label: Bonus — Prognosis (R-ISS / R2-ISS)
segments.html:
At diagnosis, send β2-microglobulin (mg/L), LDH, albumin, and FISH — these drive staging. R-ISS combines ISS (β2M, albumin) with LDH and high-risk cytogenetics [del(17p), t(4;14), t(14;16)].

R-ISSDefinition (abbreviated)5-yr OSMedian OS
Iβ2M <3.5 mg/L + normal LDH + standard-risk FISH + albumin ≥3.5 g/dL82%Not reached
IINeither I nor III62%83 months
IIIβ2M ≥5.5 mg/L AND (elevated LDH OR high-risk FISH)40%43 months

Note the units: β2-microglobulin is reported in mg/L (a frequent slide/chart error fixed in the optimized deck), albumin in g/dL. See the 2023–26 update for R2-ISS, which adds gain/amp 1q21.

segments.label: Bonus — Treatment overview & toxicities
segments.html:
Natural history: induction → (autoSCT if eligible) → maintenance, with serial M-protein monitoring; relapse/remission cycles recur. Induction is now a quadruplet — an anti-CD38 monoclonal (daratumumab) added to a proteasome inhibitor + immunomodulatory agent + steroid (e.g., D-VRd; see update). autoSCT eligibility: roughly age <75, ECOG <3, no cirrhosis/severe HF — renal disease is not a contraindication.

Class (examples)Signature toxicitiesProphylaxis pearl
Proteasome inhibitors (bortezomib, carfilzomib)Myelosuppression, GI, neuropathy (bortezomib), cardiovascular (carfilzomib)Shingles (VZV) prophylaxis — high HSV/VZV reactivation risk
Immunomodulatory agents (lenalidomide, pomalidomide)Myelosuppression, GI, infection, VTE / arterial thrombosisThromboprophylaxis — aspirin or full anticoagulation per risk
Steroids (dexamethasone)Hyperglycemia, infection, bone lossConsider PJP prophylaxis, PPI, Ca/vit-D ± bisphosphonate
Anti-CD38 / anti-SLAMF7 mAbs (daratumumab, isatuximab, elotuzumab)Infusion reactions, myelosuppression, infectionPremedicate; daratumumab interferes with blood-bank crossmatch (type & screen early)
ASK: "A myeloma patient on bortezomib + lenalidomide + dex — what three prophylactic meds should already be on the chart?" Acyclovir (VZV), aspirin/anticoagulation (VTE), and — if steroids continue — PJP prophylaxis ± PPI and bone support.

segments.label: Retrieval close (3 min)
segments.html: Boards closed: have learners rebuild (1) the three screening tests and the ~80→95→99% sensitivity ladder, (2) the MGUS/SMM/MM table from memory, and (3) the CRABI mechanisms. Then have one learner state the one-liner for a new MM diagnosis (see dotphrase).

cases.title: Case 1 — "negative" urine protein
cases.stem: 55-year-old recently admitted for AKI and proteinuria, now in clinic for follow-up (AKI resolved at discharge). SPEP + FLC ordered for the proteinuria: M-protein 2 g/dL; IFX "IgG-κ monoclonal protein"; κ 6.3, λ 2.1, κ/λ ratio 3.0.
cases.reveal: There is an M-spike <3 g/dL and an elevated κ/λ ratio (κ clone). Labs: Hgb 13, Ca 9, Cr stable at 1. PET-CT: no lytic lesions. Marrow: 5% clonal plasma cells. M-protein <3 g/dL, <10% marrow involvement, no CRABI → MGUS.

cases.title: Case 2a → 2b → 2c — the SMM→MM trajectory
cases.stem: 64-year-old, follow-up of abnormal labs. SPEP/IFX/FLC: M-protein 3.5 g/dL; IFX "IgA-λ monoclonal protein"; κ 1, λ 5, κ/λ ratio 0.2. Labs: Hgb 14, Cr 1.0, Ca 9.5. PET/CT: no lytic lesions. Marrow: 12% involvement.
cases.reveal: Smoldering myeloma — M-spike ≥3 g/dL and ≥10% marrow involvement, but no CRABI/end-organ damage. Progression risk ~10%/yr for the first 5 years (then ~5%/yr).

mcqs.stem: A 68-year-old man is found to have total protein elevation. SPEP shows a 1.8 g/dL IgG-κ M-spike; marrow biopsy 6% clonal plasma cells; Hgb 13.4, Ca 9.4, Cr 0.9, normal skeletal survey. Which is the most appropriate next step?
opts: Periodic monitoring with SPEP, FLC, CBC, Ca, Cr
mcqs.exp: C. This is MGUS (M-protein <3 g/dL, <10% marrow plasma cells, no CRABI) — managed with observation, not treatment. A/D (induction) and B (transplant) are for MM, not MGUS/SMM. E bisphosphonates are indicated for myeloma bone disease or hypercalcemia, neither present here. Monitor: progression risk ~1%/yr.

mcqs.stem: A 72-year-old woman has fatigue and back pain. Hgb 8.9, Ca 11.6, Cr 2.4. SPEP M-spike 4.2 g/dL IgG-κ; marrow 40% clonal plasma cells; spine MRI shows multiple focal lesions. Which feature alone is sufficient to diagnose multiple myeloma here?
opts: Hypercalcemia (Ca 11.6) with anemia and renal failure
mcqs.exp: C. CRABI end-organ damage (hyperCalcemia ≥11, Anemia, Renal dysfunction) defines MM. A 40% marrow involvement meets the ≥10% threshold but isn't diagnostic on its own (≥60% would be a myeloma-defining biomarker). B M-spike size doesn't establish MM without CRABI or a biomarker. D/E are not diagnostic criteria. (Here she also has >1 focal MRI lesion — independently myeloma-defining.)

mcqs.stem: A 60-year-old with suspected myeloma has a normal SPEP and normal immunofixation, but anemia and renal insufficiency. Which test is most likely to reveal the diagnosis?
mcqs.exp: B. Up to ~16% of myeloma is light-chain-only and can be missed by SPEP/IFX; the serum FLC assay detects the involved light chain and an abnormal ratio. A delays diagnosis. C viscosity is for hyperviscosity (more Waldenström/IgM). D flow of blood rarely shows marrow plasma cells. E technetium bone scans miss myeloma's purely lytic lesions — use skeletal survey/CT/MRI/PET.

mcqs.stem: A newly diagnosed MM patient is about to start bortezomib, lenalidomide, and dexamethasone. Which prophylaxis set best matches the regimen?
opts: Acyclovir + aspirin (or anticoagulation) + consider PJP prophylaxis
mcqs.exp: A. Bortezomib (proteasome inhibitor) → VZV reactivation risk → acyclovir. Lenalidomide (IMiD) → VTE/arterial thrombosis → aspirin or anticoagulation. High-dose steroids → consider PJP prophylaxis (± PPI, bone support). The other combinations mix unrelated or contraindicated agents.

mcqs.stem: At diagnosis of MM, which set of labs is required to assign the R-ISS (and feeds the newer R2-ISS) stage?
opts: β2-microglobulin, albumin, LDH, and FISH cytogenetics
mcqs.exp: B. R-ISS = ISS (β2-microglobulin + albumin) plus LDH and high-risk FISH [del(17p), t(4;14), t(14;16)]. R2-ISS adds gain/amp 1q21 to these. β2-microglobulin is reported in mg/L. The other panels are unrelated to myeloma staging.

anki: What does CRABI stand for in plasma cell dyscrasias?
hyperCalcemia, Renal dysfunction, Anemia, Boney lytic lesions, Immunodeficiency — the end-organ damage that defines MM.
Three screening tests for a plasma cell dyscrasia?
SPEP + serum immunofixation + serum free light chains (FLC).
Sensitivity: SPEP+IFX alone vs +UPEP vs +FLC?
~80% → ~95% (add UPEP) → ~99% (add serum FLC).
Why can a urine dipstick miss myeloma?
Dipstick detects albumin only; it misses monoclonal free light chains (Bence Jones protein).
What % of myeloma is light-chain-only (missed by SPEP)?
Up to ~16% — caught by serum FLC.
Normal serum κ/λ free light chain ratio?
Why is the FLC ratio unreliable in renal failure, and the workaround?
Reduced light-chain clearance raises the ratio (up to ~3); use stricter cutoffs (>4 or <0.25) to flag a true clone.
MGUS diagnostic criteria?
M-protein <3 g/dL, <10% clonal marrow plasma cells, NO CRABI/end-organ damage.
Smoldering myeloma criteria?
M-protein ≥3 g/dL (or ≥500 mg/24h) OR 10–60% clonal marrow plasma cells, but NO CRABI.
What turns SMM into MM?
CRABI end-organ damage OR a myeloma-defining biomarker (≥60% marrow, FLC ratio ≥100, >1 focal MRI lesion).
Progression risk: MGUS vs SMM?
MGUS ~1%/yr; SMM ~10%/yr for the first 5 years (then ~5%/yr).
Most sensitive imaging for myeloma bone disease?
PET/CT (gold standard); whole-body low-dose CT or MRI > plain whole-body X-ray, which misses small lytic lesions.
Labs to order at MM diagnosis for R-ISS staging?
β2-microglobulin (mg/L), albumin, LDH, and FISH cytogenetics.
R-ISS stage III definition & median OS?
β2M ≥5.5 mg/L AND (elevated LDH OR high-risk FISH); median OS ~43 months.
What does R2-ISS add to R-ISS (2022)?
Gain/amplification of 1q21 — a 4-tier additive score (D'Agostino, JCO 2022).
Current standard front-line induction for transplant-eligible MM?
Quadruplet: anti-CD38 mAb (daratumumab) + VRd (D-VRd) — PERSEUS, NEJM 2024.
autoSCT eligibility cutoffs in MM?
~age <75, ECOG <3, no cirrhosis/severe HF. Renal disease is NOT a contraindication.
Prophylaxis: bortezomib vs lenalidomide?
Bortezomib (proteasome inhibitor) → VZV/acyclovir prophylaxis; lenalidomide (IMiD) → VTE prophylaxis (ASA or anticoagulation).
lessons.dot:
#PlasmaCellDyscrasia: monoclonal plasma-cell proliferation; classify MGUS vs SMM vs MM
M-protein *IgG/IgA/IgM* *kappa/lambda*, serum spike *__ g/dL, marrow clonal PC *__%
screen: SPEP+IFX + serum FLC [kappa/lambda ratio *__; nl 0.26-1.65; >1.65=kappa,<0.26=lambda]
  (UPEP only if FLC unavail; dipstick misses Bence Jones; SPEP+IFX 80%->+FLC ~99%)
dx tier:
  MGUS = M<3 g/dL + marrow<10% + NO CRABI -> monitor q3-6mo (prog ~1%/yr)
  SMM  = M>=3 g/dL or marrow 10-60% + NO CRABI -> monitor (prog ~10%/yr x5y); *consider early tx if high-risk
  MM   = marrow>=10% + CRABI or myeloma-defining biomarker
CRABI (end-organ): Ca *>=11; Cr *>=2 (cast nephropathy); Hgb *<12; *lytic lesions/fx; *recurrent infxn (hypogamma)
myeloma-defining biomarkers (SLiM): marrow *>=60% / inv:uninv FLC ratio *>=100 / *>1 focal MRI lesion
w/u new MM: marrow bx + FISH, skeletal imaging *[whole-body CT/MRI/PET], beta2-microglobulin *(mg/L), LDH, albumin
stage: R-ISS *I/II/III  [I 5y-OS 82%; II 62%/median 83mo; III 40%/median 43mo]; R2-ISS adds 1q21+
tx (heme-onc): quadruplet induction *anti-CD38(dara)+VRd -> autoSCT if eligible *(age<75,ECOG<3,no cirrhosis/sev HF; renal NOT a contraindic) -> maintenance
ppx on tx: VZV acyclovir (bortezomib) / VTE ppx ASA or AC (lenalidomide) / PJP+PPI+Ca-vitD (steroids)
complications: hyperCa -> IVF+bisphosphonate(+calcitonin); cast nephropathy -> aggressive IVF UOP ~3L/d + urgent systemic tx; IVIG if IgG<500 + recurrent infxn
abbrev: 2/2, w/u, r/o, abnl, susp, d/c, RTC
takehome: MGUS, smoldering myeloma, and MM are a spectrum of clonal plasma-cell disorders; complications follow CRABI — hyperCalcemia, Renal dysfunction, Anemia, Boney lytic lesions, Immunodeficiency.

Screen with SPEP + serum immunofixation AND serum FLC. FLC catches the ~16% of light-chain-only myeloma that SPEP misses, brings sensitivity to ~99%, and replaces the 24-h UPEP. A "negative" urine dipstick does not rule out myeloma.
Distinguish the three diagnoses by M-protein size, % clonal marrow plasma cells, and end-organ damage (CRABI) or a myeloma-defining biomarker (≥60% marrow, FLC ratio ≥100, >1 focal MRI lesion).
Progression risk: MGUS ~1%/yr; SMM ~10%/yr for the first 5 years. At MM diagnosis, send β2-microglobulin (mg/L), LDH, albumin, and FISH for R-ISS / R2-ISS staging.

Front-line therapy is now quadruplet (anti-CD38 mAb + VRd) ± autoSCT then maintenance; manage hypercalcemia, cast nephropathy (aggressive IVF + urgent systemic therapy), and class-specific infection/VTE prophylaxis.

extras.label: 2023–2026 Guideline & Practice Update NEW — surfaced here & on appended deck slide
extras.html:
The original lesson's diagnostic framework is unchanged and remains correct (SPEP/IFX + serum FLC; CRABI; MGUS/SMM/MM thresholds; IMWG SLiM biomarkers). The standing teaching slides were left untouched; movement since Jan 2023 is in therapy and prognosis:

  • Quadruplet induction is now standard. Adding an anti-CD38 monoclonal (daratumumab) to VRd (D-VRd) reduced progression/death ~60% vs VRd in transplant-eligible newly diagnosed MM (PERSEUS, NEJM 2024); FDA-approved July 2024. Isatuximab-VRd was approved for transplant-ineligible patients (IMROZ, 2024). The deck's "3 or 4 drugs ± monoclonal antibody" is now "quadruplet with the mAb routinely included."
  • R2-ISS refines prognosis. The Second Revision of the ISS (D'Agostino, JCO 2022) adds gain/amplification of 1q21 to ISS, LDH, and del(17p)/t(4;14) as an additive 4-tier score — better separating the large, heterogeneous R-ISS stage II group.
  • Earlier intervention in high-risk smoldering myeloma. Daratumumab monotherapy delayed progression to active MM (5-yr PFS 63% vs 41% with active monitoring) in high-risk SMM (AQUILA, NEJM 2024) — shifting practice toward treating select high-risk SMM rather than observing.
  • New relapsed/refractory classes. BCMA-directed therapies are now mainstream: CAR-T cilta-cel (CARTITUDE-4; FDA April 2024 for ≥1 prior line, lenalidomide-refractory) and ide-cel; bispecific T-cell engagers teclistamab (BCMA, FDA Oct 2022), elranatamab (BCMA), and talquetamab (GPRC5D).
  • Monitoring. Mass spectrometry (MALDI-TOF) is emerging as a more sensitive alternative to immunofixation for detecting/monitoring M-protein (not yet routine).

Oncology therapy evolves quickly — confirm regimens with current NCCN/heme-onc guidance and your treating oncologist before teaching specifics.

lessons.title: Pleural Effusions
lessons.subtitle: Facilitator-led chalk talk · ~15 min (core) / 25–30 min (with cases) · IM attendings & senior residents teaching residents
objectives: Apply the criteria that separate pleural effusions into transudative and exudative.
Generate a differential for each, and tie each diagnosis to its characteristic pleural-fluid studies.
Build a framework for the management of parapneumonic effusions (uncomplicated vs complicated vs empyema).

prework.q: 1. State Light's criteria and how many must be met to call an effusion exudative.
prework.a: Exudate if any one of: pleural/serum protein >0.5; pleural/serum LDH >0.6; pleural LDH >2/3 the serum upper limit of normal. Sensitivity ~98%, specificity ~83%.

prework.q: 2. A patient on diuretics has fluid that looks exudative by Light's — what's the catch and the fix?
prework.a: Diuretics concentrate protein/LDH and misclassify ~25% of transudates as exudates. Use the serum − pleural albumin gradient >1.2 g/dL to reclassify it as a transudate.

prework.q: 3. What three findings flip a parapneumonic effusion from "antibiotics alone" to "must drain"?
prework.a: pH <7.2, low glucose, or loculations/septations (complicated); frank pus or a positive Gram stain/culture defines empyema. All need drainage in addition to antibiotics.

segments.label: Hook
segments.html:
"You see a blunted costophrenic angle and a meniscus on the CXR. Before you order a single send-out test, you need to answer two questions in order: is this fluid transudate or exudate, and if it's a parapneumonic exudate, does it need a drain? Get those two right and the rest of the work-up falls into place." Project the title slide.

ASK — "Which effusions warrant a tap?" Reveal: nearly all effusions >1 cm on ultrasound or lateral decubitus film should get an ultrasound-guided diagnostic thoracentesis.

segments.label: Objective 1 — Transudate vs exudate (Light's)
segments.html:
ASK — "Recite Light's criteria. How many must be met for exudate?" Reveal: any one.
Light's criterionExudate if…
Protein ratioPleural/serum protein > 0.5
LDH ratioPleural/serum LDH > 0.6
LDH absolutePleural LDH > 2/3 the serum ULN
Pearl. Light's is sensitive (~98%) but only ~83% specific and over-calls exudate in diuretic-treated patients. When the clinical picture screams transudate (HF, cirrhosis) but Light's says exudate, check the serum − pleural albumin gradient: >1.2 g/dL = transudate. High pleural RBCs (>10k) also falsely raise LDH.

segments.label: Objective 2 — Differential + fluid studies
segments.html:
ASK — "Name transudative causes, then exudative causes, before I reveal the slide."
Transudate (non-inflammatory)Exudate (inflammatory)
Heart failure, renal failure / nephrotic syndrome, cirrhosis (hepatic hydrothorax), hypoalbuminemia, peritoneal dialysis, atelectasis, trapped lungParapneumonic / pleural infection, malignancy, PE, autoimmune (RA, SLE, GPA, Sjögren's, sarcoid), chylothorax, hemothorax, GI (esophageal perforation, pancreatitis)
Targeted testPoints to…
PMN-predominantBacterial parapneumonic / empyema
Lymphocyte-predominantMalignancy, TB, autoimmune, chylothorax, post-CABG, transplant rejection, any chronic effusion
ADA >40TB pleurisy (sens/spec >90%; beats AFB/culture/biopsy)
Triglycerides >110Chylothorax
Pleural:blood Hct >0.5Hemothorax
Amylase ↑Esophageal rupture, pancreatitis (occasionally malignancy)
Cytology / flowMalignancy (flow only if leukemia/lymphoma suspected)
Pearl — yield grows with repeat taps. Initial cytology is ~60–65% sensitive for malignant effusion; a second large-volume thoracentesis raises it to ~75–92%, a third to ~97%. Send cytology on the initial work-up of any effusion of uncertain cause (BTS).

segments.label: Objective 3 — Parapneumonic management
segments.html:
ASK — "Sort a parapneumonic effusion into uncomplicated, complicated, or empyema — what features and what management for each?"
CategoryDefining featuresManagement
UncomplicatedNo loculations, pH >7.2, normal glucoseAntibiotics alone
ComplicatedLoculations/septations, pH <7.2, or low glucoseAntibiotics + drainage
EmpyemaFrank pus or positive Gram stain/cultureAntibiotics + drainage; often surgical (VATS/decortication)
Pearl — drain choice and adjuncts. A small-bore (≤14F) pigtail is first-line for most pleural infection (BTS 2023; less pain, equal efficacy); reserve larger bore (24–32F) for heavy loculation or pus, and VATS/decortication for empyema. Intrapleural tPA + DNase (MIST-2) improves drainage and cuts surgical referral but does not lower mortality.

segments.label: Retrieval close
segments.html: Board off. Have one learner rebuild the algorithm: tap any effusion >1 cm → Light's → if exudate, targeted studies → if parapneumonic, pH/glucose/loculation decide drain. Have a second learner state the three Light's criteria and the albumin-gradient rescue.

cases.title: Case 1 — 46-year-old, fevers/cough/SOB
cases.stem: L-sided consolidation + moderate effusion. Pleural: protein 3.5, LDH 76, pH 7.26, Gram stain +PMNs, cell count 350 (88% PMN). Serum: protein 4.0, LDH 278.
cases.reveal: Exudate — pleural/serum protein 3.5/4.0 = 0.88 (>0.5). (LDH ratio 0.27 and absolute LDH are not met, so the protein criterion alone makes it an exudate.)

cases.title: Case 2 — 72-year-old, prior CVA + chronic aspiration
cases.stem: CT: loculated R effusion with septations + RLL consolidation. Pleural: protein 3.5, LDH 356, pH 7.16, Gram stain +PMNs, cell count 980 (90% PMN). Serum: protein 3.5, LDH 278.
cases.reveal: Exudate — meets all three (protein ratio 1.0, LDH ratio 1.28, pleural LDH 356 > 2/3 of serum ULN).

cases.title: Case 3 — 82-year-old, metastatic breast cancer
cases.stem: Near-opacified L hemithorax; therapeutic tap. Pleural: protein 2.1, LDH 156, pH 7.46, Gram stain none, cell count 580 (80% lymphocytes). Serum: protein 2.5, LDH 278.
cases.reveal: Exudate — pleural/serum protein 2.1/2.5 = 0.84 (>0.5). (Note: the LDH ratio here is 156/278 = 0.56, which is below the 0.6 threshold — see the changelog "human review" item.)

mcqs.stem: A patient with decompensated heart failure on high-dose furosemide has a pleural tap: pleural/serum protein 0.55, pleural/serum LDH 0.62. Serum albumin 3.6, pleural albumin 1.9. What best explains the discordance, and how should you classify the effusion?
mcqs.exp: B. Diuretics concentrate pleural protein and LDH, pushing the ratios just over Light's thresholds (~25% of transudates misclassified). The serum − pleural albumin gradient = 3.6 − 1.9 = 1.7 (>1.2) reclassifies this as a transudate consistent with HF. A over-tests. C/D/E have specific findings absent here.

mcqs.stem: A parapneumonic effusion shows pH 7.10, glucose 35, and loculations on ultrasound; no frank pus, Gram stain negative. Best management?
opts: Observation with repeat imaging in 48 h
mcqs.exp: C. pH <7.2, low glucose, and loculations define a complicated parapneumonic effusion → antibiotics PLUS drainage. A is for uncomplicated effusions. B delays needed drainage. D treats malignant effusions, not infection. E treats transudates.

mcqs.stem: A lymphocyte-predominant exudative effusion with negative cytology and an ADA of 78 U/L in a patient from a TB-endemic region. Most likely diagnosis and next best test?
opts: TB pleurisy — pleural biopsy for tissue stain/culture
mcqs.exp: B. A markedly elevated ADA (>40) is highly sensitive/specific (>90%) for TB pleurisy; because pleural-fluid AFB stain/culture are insensitive, pleural biopsy for tissue stain and culture is the diagnostic confirmatory test. The other options don't fit a lymphocytic, high-ADA effusion.

mcqs.stem: Which statement about intrapleural tPA/DNase for pleural infection is correct?
opts: It reduces mortality compared with chest tube alone
It improves fluid drainage and reduces surgical referral but has no proven mortality benefit
It is contraindicated in all loculated effusions
It is first-line for transudative effusions
mcqs.exp: B. MIST-2 (Rahman, NEJM 2011) showed combined tPA + DNase improved radiographic drainage and reduced surgical referral/length of stay, without a mortality benefit. It supplements, not replaces, antibiotics and drainage; it has no role in transudates.

mcqs.stem: An 82-year-old with metastatic cancer has a large recurrent symptomatic effusion; first cytology is negative. Best next step?
opts: Repeat a large-volume thoracentesis (and consider cytology again)
mcqs.exp: B. A single negative cytology does not exclude malignancy (initial sensitivity ~60–65%); a second large-volume tap raises yield substantially (~75–92%). A is wrong. C treats infection. D is over-aggressive. E applies to parapneumonic, not malignant, effusions.

anki: Light's criteria — exudate if ANY one of…
Pleural/serum protein >0.5; pleural/serum LDH >0.6; pleural LDH >2/3 serum ULN.
Sensitivity/specificity of Light's criteria
~98% sensitive, ~83% specific.
When do Light's criteria misclassify, and the workaround?
Diuretic use (~25% of transudates called exudates) and high pleural RBCs (falsely raise LDH); use serum–pleural albumin gradient >1.2 g/dL to identify a transudate.
First step in evaluating a pleural effusion >1 cm
Ultrasound-guided diagnostic thoracentesis.
Causes of transudative effusion
Heart failure, renal failure/nephrotic syndrome, cirrhosis (hepatic hydrothorax), hypoalbuminemia, peritoneal dialysis, atelectasis, trapped lung.
Causes of exudative effusion
Infection/parapneumonic, malignancy, PE, autoimmune (RA, SLE, GPA, Sjögren's, sarcoid), chylothorax, hemothorax, GI (esophageal perforation, pancreatitis).
PMN-predominant exudate suggests…
Bacterial parapneumonic effusion or empyema.
Lymphocyte-predominant exudate suggests…
Malignancy, TB, autoimmune, chylothorax, post-CABG, lung-transplant rejection; any chronic effusion.
Pleural fluid finding diagnostic of chylothorax
Triglycerides >110 mg/dL.
Pleural fluid finding diagnostic of hemothorax
Pleural:blood hematocrit ratio >0.5.
Pleural fluid finding for esophageal rupture/pancreatitis
Elevated amylase.
Best pleural test for TB pleurisy
Adenosine deaminase (ADA) >40 — sens/spec >90%, better than AFB/culture/biopsy.
Definition of an uncomplicated parapneumonic effusion + treatment
No loculations/septations, pH >7.2, normal glucose; treat with antibiotics alone.
Definition of a complicated parapneumonic effusion + treatment
Loculations/septations, pH <7.2, or low glucose; antibiotics PLUS drainage.
Definition of empyema
Frank pus or positive pleural Gram stain/culture; antibiotics plus drainage (often surgical: VATS/decortication).
Does intrapleural tPA/DNase reduce mortality?
No — it improves drainage and reduces surgical referral (MIST-2, Rahman NEJM 2011) but no mortality benefit.
BTS 2023 first-line chest tube for pleural infection
Small-bore (≤14F) — equal efficacy, less pain; larger bore for heavy loculation/pus.
How does serial thoracentesis affect malignant effusion yield?
Cytology sensitivity rises ~65% (1st) → ~92% (2nd) → ~97% (3rd); large-volume taps increase yield.
lessons.dot:
#Pleural effusion: * side, * size (US/CXR >1cm -> tap)
US-guided diagnostic +/- therapeutic thoracentesis
Light's (exudate if ANY): pl/se protein *= [>0.5] / pl/se LDH *= [>0.6] / pl LDH *= [>2/3 se ULN]
  -> [TRANSUDATE / EXUDATE]
  if diuretic & looks transudative: se-pl albumin gradient *= [>1.2 -> transudate]
transudate 2/2 [HF / cirrhosis-hepatic hydrothorax / renal-nephrotic / hypoalbuminemia / atelectasis]
exudate 2/2 [parapneumonic-infection / malignancy / PE / autoimmune / chylo / hemothorax / GI]
fluid studies: cell ct/diff *= [PMN->bacterial parapneumonic; lymph->malignancy/TB/autoimmune]
  pH *= / glucose *= / Gram stain+cx / cytology (send if uncertain) / ADA [>40 TB] / TG [>110 chylo] / amylase / pl:bl Hct [>0.5 hemothorax]
parapneumonic class:
  uncomplicated [no loc, pH>7.2, nl glu] -> abx alone
  complicated [loc/septation OR pH<7.2 OR low glu] -> abx + DRAIN
  empyema [pus OR +Gram/cx] -> abx + drain, surgical (VATS/decort) if needed
  drain: small-bore <=14F first-line (BTS 2023); +/- intrapleural tPA+DNase (improves drainage, no mortality benefit)
malignant: serial large-volume taps raise yield; recurrent -> [IPC / talc pleurodesis]
* = bedside-editable value
takehome: Tap any effusion >1 cm on US/lateral decubitus with an ultrasound-guided diagnostic thoracentesis.

Light's criteria separate transudate (HF, renal/liver failure, hypoalbuminemia) from exudate (infection, malignancy, autoimmune, PE, chylothorax). Any one criterion = exudate; use the albumin gradient when diuretics confound.
Targeted fluid studies localize the cause — cell count/differential, pH/glucose, Gram stain/culture, cytology, ADA, amylase, triglycerides, hematocrit.
Parapneumonic: uncomplicated → antibiotics; complicated (pH <7.2, low glucose, loculations) and empyema (pus/+culture) → antibiotics + drainage, often multidisciplinary.
tPA/DNase augments drainage without a mortality benefit; small-bore tubes are first-line (BTS 2023).

extras.label: Modern Framing — 2023 Context currency check
extras.html:
Light's criteria, the differential, and the parapneumonic framework are timeless and verified current as of June 2026. The items below add modern context; none change the lesson's standing teaching.

BTS 2023 pleural disease guideline. Pleural fluid pH ≤7.2 (or pus / positive Gram stain or culture) defines a complicated effusion/empyema → drain. Small-bore (≤14F) chest tubes are first-line for pleural infection (equal efficacy, less pain). RAPID score risk-stratifies pleural-infection prognosis.
tPA + DNase (MIST-2, Rahman NEJM 2011). Improves radiographic drainage and reduces surgical referral and length of stay — but no mortality benefit. The deck's caveat remains accurate.
Malignant pleural effusion (ATS/STS/STR 2018). For recurrent symptomatic MPE, indwelling pleural catheter and talc pleurodesis are both first-line; IPC + talc (IPC-PLUS) increases pleurodesis success. Send cytology on initial work-up; serial large-volume taps raise yield.

  3. Feller-Kopman D, Light R. Pleural disease. N Engl J Med. 2018;378:740–751.
  4. Roberts ME, et al. British Thoracic Society Guideline for pleural disease. Thorax. 2023;78(Suppl 3):s1–s42.
  5. Rahman NM, et al. Intrapleural use of tissue plasminogen activator and DNase in pleural infection (MIST-2). N Engl J Med. 2011;365:518–526.
  6. Feller-Kopman DJ, et al. Management of Malignant Pleural Effusions: ATS/STS/STR Clinical Practice Guideline. Am J Respir Crit Care Med. 2018;198:839–849.

Diagnosis and management are correct, so the wording was left unchanged pending author confirmation rather than guessing the intended value.

lessons.title: Supraventricular Tachycardia (SVT)
lessons.subtitle: Optimized lesson package · Facilitator-led interactive chalk talk for IM residents · 30–45 min (talk + practice ECGs)
lessons.credit: Adapted from TeachIM.org — Supraventricular Tachycardia (SVT) (Fainstad B; expert review Goldberger Z; 2020), CC BY-NC 4.0. Optimized June 2026: guideline framework verified current, two deck errors corrected, modified-Valsalva and pre-excitation safety pearls added, pedagogy restructured, assessments added. Companion deck: SVT_Optimized.pptx (original interactive presenter board, surgically corrected).
objectives: Characterize the electrophysiology of the seven supraventricular tachycardias (automaticity vs. re-entry).
Differentiate the SVTs using two features: regular vs. irregular rhythm and the presence/morphology of P waves.
Identify the best acute management for each rhythm — and the AV-nodal-blocker pitfalls to avoid.

prework.q: Q1. Every tachycardia comes from one of two mechanisms. Name them, and say which tends to be faster.
prework.a: Increased automaticity (a node or ectopic focus firing too fast) or a re-entry circuit (a self-sustaining loop). Re-entry circuits are generally faster. This single split organizes the whole differential.

prework.q: Q2. You have a narrow-complex tachycardia. What two ECG features let you narrow the diagnosis fastest?
prework.a: Rhythm regularity (regular vs. irregular) and the P waves (present or absent, and their morphology — sinus, retrograde "retro-P," saw-tooth, multiple morphologies, or none). Those two axes separate the seven SVTs.

prework.q: Q3. What does adenosine do to (a) AVNRT/AVRT, (b) atrial flutter, and (c) VT?
prework.a: (a) Terminates AVNRT/AVRT by breaking the re-entry circuit at the AV node. (b) Transiently blocks the AV node to unmask the flutter waves (diagnostic), but usually does not convert flutter. (c) Has no effect on true VT. So adenosine is both therapeutic and a diagnostic probe.

segments.label: Hook (2 min)
segments.html: "A narrow-complex tachycardia at 170 just hit your pager. Instead of memorizing a list, I'll give you two questions that sort all seven SVTs in about ten seconds — and tell you what to do about each." Then the framing: every tachycardia is either automaticity or re-entry, and two ECG features (regularity + P waves) carve the rest.

segments.label: Objective 1 — Two mechanisms
segments.html:
ASK: "What are the only two ways a heart can go too fast?" — then introduce the key on the board (increased automaticity vs. re-entry circuit).

Automaticity = a pacemaker (the sinus node or an ectopic atrial focus) firing too fast, usually driven by an underlying process (fever, hypoxia, catecholamines, theophylline). Re-entry = a circular self-perpetuating loop (within the AV node, or using an accessory pathway), which tends to be faster and starts/stops abruptly. This mechanism split predicts the treatment: fix the driver for automaticity, break the loop for re-entry.

segments.label: Objective 2 — Sort the seven by regularity + P waves
segments.html:
ASK: "Regular or irregular? Now look at the P waves — present, absent, retrograde, saw-tooth, or multiple? Place this rhythm." Click each rhythm to reveal.
RhythmMechanismRhythm + P wavesAcute management
Sinus tachycardiaAutomaticityRegular; normal sinus P wavesIdentify/address the underlying process
Atrial tachycardiaAutomaticity (ectopic focus)Regular; non-sinus P waves (often inverted)AV-nodal blocker; treat the driver
AVNRTRe-entry (within AV node)Regular; no visible sinus P (often buried/retro-P)Interrupt the circuit — vagal maneuvers / adenosine
AVRT (orthodromic)Re-entry (accessory pathway)Regular, narrow; retro-P with short R-PInterrupt the circuit — vagal / adenosine
AVRT (antidromic)Re-entry (accessory pathway)Regular, wide-complex; sinus ECG shows delta wave + short PRProcainamide or cardioversion; avoid AV-nodal blockers if pre-excited
Atrial flutterRe-entry (macro, right atrium)Regular or regularly-irregular; saw-tooth flutter waves (atrial rate ~300)Rate control; adenosine unmasks flutter waves
Atrial fibrillationMultiple waveletsIrregularly irregular; no discrete P wavesRate control (anticoagulate per CHA₂DS₂-VASc)
Multifocal atrial tachycardia (MAT)Automaticity (multiple foci)Irregular; ≥3 distinct P-wave morphologiesAddress the underlying process (often COPD); avoid β-blockers in bronchospasm

(The board groups these as "7 SVTs"; AVRT is shown as orthodromic and antidromic variants of one re-entry mechanism.)

Pearl — slow it down to see it: when the rate hides the P waves, a vagal maneuver or adenosine transiently slows AV conduction so you can read the atrial activity — saw-tooth flutter, retrograde P, or organized atrial tach — turning a diagnostic puzzle into an answer.

segments.label: Objective 3 — Acute management & the three buckets
segments.html:
ASK: "Stable SVT — there are only three management strategies. What are they, and which rhythms go in each bucket?"

Stable SVT collapses into three actions: (1) address the underlying process (sinus tach, atrial tach, MAT); (2) interrupt the re-entry circuit with vagal maneuvers or adenosine (AVNRT, AVRT); (3) rate-control with AV-nodal blockers (AF, atrial flutter). Unstable at any point → synchronized cardioversion.

Pearl — the modified (REVERT) Valsalva: have the patient strain into a 10-mL syringe while semi-recumbent, then immediately lay them flat with a passive 45° leg raise. It converts roughly 43% of re-entrant SVT versus ~17% for the standard Valsalva — try it before adenosine.
Safety — pre-excited atrial fibrillation: an irregular, wide, very fast rhythm in a WPW patient is AF conducting down the accessory pathway. Avoid AV-nodal blockers (adenosine, calcium-channel blockers, β-blockers, digoxin): blocking the node forces conduction down the accessory pathway and can precipitate ventricular fibrillation. Use procainamide or synchronized cardioversion.

segments.label: Close (2 min)
segments.html: Hide the board. Ask the group to rebuild the 2×2 (regular/irregular × P-wave pattern), place all seven rhythms, and state the management bucket for each. Then the safety one-liner: "Irregular + wide + fast = don't touch the node."

lessons.casesIntro: After the framework, work the Practice ECGs deck one rhythm at a time. For each strip, have learners apply the two-question sort before revealing the answer. Sample teaching prompts:

cases.title: Practice ECGs 1
cases.stem: A regular narrow-complex tachycardia at 180 with no visible P waves, abrupt onset/offset. Most likely rhythm and first move?
cases.reveal: AVNRT (the most common paroxysmal SVT). First move: vagal maneuvers (modified Valsalva or carotid sinus massage), then adenosine if that fails. Both are diagnostic and therapeutic.

cases.title: Practice ECGs 2
cases.stem: A regular narrow tachycardia converts briefly with adenosine, revealing saw-tooth waves at an atrial rate of ~300 with 2:1 conduction. Diagnosis?
cases.reveal: Atrial flutter. Adenosine transiently blocks the AV node and unmasks the flutter waves; flutter is managed with rate control (and anticoagulation/rhythm strategy like AF), not adenosine conversion.

cases.title: Practice ECGs 3
cases.stem: An irregularly irregular narrow-complex tachycardia with no discrete P waves. Diagnosis and management?
cases.reveal: Atrial fibrillation. Rate-control with AV-nodal blockers and assess stroke risk (CHA₂DS₂-VASc) for anticoagulation.

cases.title: Practice ECGs 4
cases.stem: An irregular wide-complex tachycardia at 250 in a young patient with a delta wave on a prior ECG. What must you NOT give?
cases.reveal: Pre-excited AF (WPW). Do NOT give AV-nodal blockers (adenosine, CCB, β-blocker, digoxin) — they can accelerate accessory-pathway conduction and cause VF. Use procainamide or synchronized cardioversion.

cases.title: Practice ECGs 5
cases.stem: An irregular narrow tachycardia with at least three different P-wave morphologies in a patient with a COPD exacerbation. Diagnosis?
cases.reveal: Multifocal atrial tachycardia (MAT). Treat the underlying pulmonary process; avoid non-selective β-blockers if there is active bronchospasm.

mcqs.stem: A 24-year-old woman has sudden palpitations. ECG: regular narrow-complex tachycardia at 190 with no visible P waves. She is alert with a BP of 118/72. What is the most appropriate first step?
mcqs.exp: B. This is a stable, regular SVT (most likely AVNRT). Guidelines make vagal maneuvers the Class I first step; the modified (REVERT) Valsalva roughly doubles conversion over the standard technique. Cardioversion (A) is for instability. Metoprolol (C) is not first-line acute therapy here. Procainamide (D) is reserved for pre-excited/wide-complex scenarios.

mcqs.stem: A regular narrow-complex tachycardia transiently slows with adenosine, revealing saw-tooth deflections at an atrial rate near 300 with 2:1 block, then resumes. What is the diagnosis?
mcqs.exp: B. Adenosine briefly increases AV block and unmasks the saw-tooth flutter waves (~300 bpm atrial rate). It usually does not terminate flutter (a macro-re-entrant atrial circuit), which separates it from AVNRT (A), where adenosine breaks the circuit and converts the rhythm. Sinus tach (C) has normal upright P waves; MAT (D) is irregular with multiple P morphologies.

mcqs.stem: A 30-year-old with known WPW presents with an irregular, wide-complex tachycardia at rates up to 260. Which agent is contraindicated?
opts: None — any of these is fine
mcqs.exp: C. This is pre-excited atrial fibrillation. AV-nodal blockers — diltiazem, and also adenosine, β-blockers, and digoxin — can enhance conduction down the accessory pathway and precipitate VF, so they are contraindicated. Procainamide (A) and synchronized cardioversion (B) are the appropriate treatments.

mcqs.stem: An 80-year-old with a COPD exacerbation has an irregular narrow-complex tachycardia; the ECG shows at least three distinct P-wave morphologies. What is the best initial management?
mcqs.exp: C. This is multifocal atrial tachycardia, an automaticity rhythm usually driven by pulmonary disease/hypoxia. It does not respond to adenosine (A) or cardioversion (B), and the cornerstone is treating the underlying process (and correcting electrolytes). Non-dihydropyridine CCBs can help rate; avoid non-selective β-blockers with active bronchospasm. Digoxin (D) is not first-line.

mcqs.stem: Which statement about adenosine in the diagnosis of a regular tachycardia is most accurate?
opts: It reliably converts ventricular tachycardia to sinus rhythm
It terminates AVNRT/AVRT and can unmask atrial flutter, but does not affect VT
It is contraindicated in all narrow-complex tachycardias
mcqs.exp: B. Adenosine breaks AV-nodal-dependent re-entry (AVNRT/AVRT), transiently blocks the node to reveal flutter/atrial-tachycardia P waves, and has essentially no effect on true VT — making it both therapeutic and a diagnostic probe. A and D misstate its effects; C is false (it is first-line for regular SVT after vagal maneuvers). Caution remains in pre-excited AF.

anki: Two mechanisms of any tachycardia, and which is faster?
Increased automaticity (nodal/ectopic pacemaker) or a re-entry circuit. Re-entry circuits are generally faster.
Two ECG features that sort the seven SVTs?
Rhythm regularity (regular vs. irregular) and P-wave presence/morphology.
Three management strategies for stable SVT?
(1) Address the underlying process; (2) interrupt the re-entry circuit (vagal maneuvers/adenosine); (3) rate-control with AV-nodal blockers.
Sinus tachycardia - rhythm, P waves, management?
Regular; normal sinus P waves; identify and address the underlying process.
Atrial tachycardia - rhythm, P waves, management?
Regular; non-sinus (often inverted) P waves; AV-nodal blocker plus treat the driver.
AVNRT - mechanism, ECG, management?
Re-entry within the AV node; regular, no visible sinus P (buried/retro-P); vagal maneuvers / adenosine.
Orthodromic AVRT - ECG and management?
Re-entry via accessory pathway; regular narrow complex with retro-P and short R-P; vagal / adenosine. Resting ECG may show a delta wave.
Antidromic AVRT - ECG and caution?
Regular WIDE-complex tachycardia; resting ECG shows delta wave + short PR. Treat with procainamide/cardioversion; avoid AV-nodal blockers if pre-excited.
Atrial flutter - ECG and management?
Saw-tooth flutter waves, atrial rate ~300, often 2:1 conduction; rate control (+/- anticoagulation). Adenosine unmasks but rarely converts it.
Atrial fibrillation - ECG and management?
Irregularly irregular, no discrete P waves; rate control and anticoagulation per CHA2DS2-VASc.
Multifocal atrial tachycardia (MAT) - ECG, association, management?
Irregular with at least 3 distinct P-wave morphologies; associated with COPD/hypoxia; treat the underlying process, avoid non-selective beta-blockers in bronchospasm.
What does adenosine do to AVNRT/AVRT, flutter, and VT?
Terminates AVNRT/AVRT; transiently unmasks (rarely converts) flutter/atrial tach; no effect on VT. Therapeutic and diagnostic.
First-line maneuver before adenosine in stable regular SVT?
Vagal maneuvers - the modified (REVERT) Valsalva (strain into a 10-mL syringe, then supine with passive leg raise), ~43% vs ~17% conversion.
Which agents are contraindicated in pre-excited (WPW) atrial fibrillation?
AV-nodal blockers - adenosine, calcium-channel blockers, beta-blockers, digoxin. They can accelerate accessory-pathway conduction and cause VF. Use procainamide or cardioversion.
Quick ECG clue that favors VT over SVT in a wide-complex tachycardia?
Extreme right-axis deviation (positive QRS in aVR).
First action once you identify an unstable tachycardia of any kind?
Synchronized cardioversion.
Why can slowing AV conduction be diagnostic in a fast narrow tachycardia?
It exposes the underlying atrial activity (saw-tooth flutter, retro-P, or organized atrial tach) that the rate was hiding.
lessons.dot:
#SVT: narrow-complex tachycardia, rate * , [STABLE / UNSTABLE]. mechanism [automaticity vs re-entry]; sort by regularity + P waves.
- UNSTABLE (hypotension/ischemia/AMS/shock) → synchronized cardioversion NOW (* J), sedate if able
- STABLE → place defib pads, 12-lead, continuous tele, IV access; read the atrial activity (slow w/ vagal or adenosine if P waves hidden)
- regular + sort:
   sinus tach (nl P) → treat driver [fever, hypovolemia, pain, hypoxia, anemia, PE, thyrotoxicosis, withdrawal]
   atrial tach (non-sinus/inverted P) → AV-nodal blocker [diltiazem/metoprolol] + treat driver
   AVNRT / orthodromic AVRT (no sinus P / retro-P, short R-P) → vagal (modified REVERT Valsalva) → adenosine 6 mg→12 mg→12 mg rapid push + flush
   atrial flutter (saw-tooth, atrial ~300, 2:1) → rate control [diltiazem/metoprolol]; adenosine unmasks not converts; anticoag per CHA2DS2-VASc; consider DCCV/rhythm
- irregular + sort:
   atrial fibrillation (no P) → rate control [diltiazem/metoprolol]; CHA2DS2-VASc → anticoag *
   MAT (≥3 P morphologies, COPD/hypoxia) → treat pulm process, correct K/Mg; non-DHP CCB for rate; AVOID nonselective BB if bronchospasm
- WIDE + irregular + very fast + known WPW/delta = pre-excited AF → *AVOID AV-nodal blockers (adenosine, CCB, BB, digoxin → VF risk); give procainamide or synchronized cardioversion
- VT clue if wide: extreme RAD / positive aVR favors VT; when in doubt treat as VT
- adenosine = diagnostic (unmasks flutter/atrial tach) AND therapeutic (terminates AVNRT/AVRT); no effect on VT
- workup: electrolytes (K/Mg/Ca), TSH, trop if ischemic sx, prior ECGs for delta wave; EP/cards referral for recurrent re-entry (ablation)
takehome: Every tachycardia is either increased automaticity (a node/ectopic focus firing too fast) or a re-entry circuit — re-entry circuits are generally faster.
There are only seven SVTs; sort them with two questions — regular vs. irregular, and what the P waves look like.

Stable SVT has three management buckets: address the underlying process (sinus tach, atrial tach, MAT), interrupt the re-entry circuit with vagal maneuvers/adenosine (AVNRT, AVRT), or rate-control with AV-nodal blockers (AF, flutter).
Adenosine reveals the rhythm — it resolves AVNRT/AVRT, unmasks atrial flutter/tach, and does not affect VT; the modified (REVERT) Valsalva is the best first vagal maneuver.

Don't touch the node in pre-excited AF: an irregular, wide, very fast rhythm in WPW needs procainamide or cardioversion, not adenosine/CCB/β-blocker/digoxin. Extreme right-axis (positive aVR) is a quick clue for VT.

1. Page RL, Joglar JA, et al. 2015 ACC/AHA/HRS Guideline for the Management of Adult Patients With Supraventricular Tachycardia. Circulation. 2016;133(14):e506-e574.
  2. Brugada J, Katritsis DG, et al. 2019 ESC Guidelines for the management of patients with supraventricular tachycardia. Eur Heart J. 2020;41(5):655-720.
  3. Appelboam A, et al. Postural modification to the standard Valsalva manoeuvre for emergency treatment of supraventricular tachycardias (REVERT). Lancet. 2015;386(10005):1747-1753.
  4. Goldberger AL, Goldberger ZD, Shvilkin A. Goldberger's Clinical Electrocardiography: A Simplified Approach. Philadelphia: Elsevier, 2018.
  5. Nelson WP, Marriott HJL, Schocken DD. Concepts and Cautions in Electrocardiography. Northglenn, CO: MedInfo Inc., 2007.
