lessons.title: Shock and Pressors
lessons.subtitle: Optimized lesson package · Facilitator-led interactive chalk talk for IM residents · 25–30 min (talk) / +15 min (3 cases)
lessons.credit: Adapted from TeachIM.org — Shock and Pressors (Zhang Y, Fainstad B; 2020), CC BY-NC 4.0. Optimized June 2026: guideline currency verified (no content errors found in the original board), 2021 Surviving Sepsis Campaign updates surfaced, pedagogy restructured, assessments and clinical tools added. Companion deck: Shock_and_Pressors_Optimized.pptx (original interactive presenter board, append-only edit).
objectives: Differentiate the four categories of shock by their hemodynamic profiles (preload/CVP, cardiac output, afterload/SVR).
Match each shock category to its initial treatment lever — volume, inotrope, or vasopressor.
Recognize the commonly used vasopressors and inotropes, their receptor effects, and the indication for each.

prework.q: Q1. A patient is hypotensive with warm extremities and a wide pulse pressure. Which category of shock does that hemodynamic picture point to, and why?
prework.a: Distributive (most often septic). Warm extremities and a wide pulse pressure reflect a low systemic vascular resistance (vasodilation) with a preserved or high cardiac output — the hallmark of distributive shock. Cold, clammy extremities with a narrow pulse pressure instead suggest a low-output state (cardiogenic or hypovolemic).

prework.q: Q2. Why is there "no role for vasopressors if the tank is not full"?
prework.a: Vasopressors raise SVR by constricting an already under-filled vascular space; without adequate preload, squeezing empty vessels does little to restore cardiac output and risks end-organ and digital ischemia. Hypovolemic shock is corrected with volume (blood for hemorrhage) first; pressors are a bridge, not a substitute, for filling.

prework.q: Q3. Name the first-line vasopressor for septic shock and the first-line agent for cardiogenic shock — and the single reason they differ.
prework.a: Norepinephrine for septic (distributive) shock; dobutamine (an inotrope) for cardiogenic shock. They differ because the broken lever differs: septic shock is primarily a vasodilation/low-SVR problem (so you vasoconstrict), whereas cardiogenic shock is primarily a pump/contractility problem (so you augment contractility).

segments.label: Hook (2 min)
segments.html: "Three patients, all with a MAP of 50. One needs a liter of blood, one needs a pump drug, one needs a squeeze drug — and giving the wrong one can kill them. How do you tell them apart at the bedside in 30 seconds?" The answer — warm vs. cold, full vs. empty — is the entire framework, and it sets up the hemodynamic table.

segments.label: Objective 1 — The four categories & their hemodynamics
segments.html:
ASK: "Before any numbers — name the four broad categories of shock." Then build the table column by column (preload, CO, afterload) as you click each cause.
CategoryPreload (CVP)Cardiac outputAfterload (SVR)First treatment lever
Hypovolemic
hemorrhage, GI/renal losses		↓↓↓↑Volume (blood for hemorrhage)
Cardiogenic
HF, valvular, arrhythmogenic		↑↓↓↑Inotrope ± diuresis
Obstructive
PE, tension PTX, tamponade		↑↓↑Relieve obstruction; inotrope as bridge
Distributive
sepsis, neurogenic, anaphylactic, adrenal crisis		↓–nmlnml–↑↓IVF + norepinephrine (or vasopressin)
Pearl — read the extremities and the pulse pressure first: warm + wide pulse pressure = low SVR = distributive; cold + narrow pulse pressure = low output = cardiogenic or hypovolemic. The exam sorts the patient before any catheter does.
ASK: "Your septic patient has underlying cardiomyopathy — how do you tell if there's a cardiogenic component dragging the output down?"
Pearl — SvO2 sorts mixed pictures: central/mixed venous O2 saturation is low in cardiogenic shock (tissues extract more from a low output) and typically high in established septic shock (impaired extraction), though it can be low in early sepsis. Interpret with caution — SvO2 is also affected by cardiac output, SaO2, and hemoglobin, so it can read falsely low with hypoxemia or anemia.

segments.label: Objective 2 — Receptor physiology
segments.html:
ASK: "Map the receptors before the drugs: what does α1 do? β1? β2? V1?" — click each receptor type on the board.

Anchor the pharmacology in four receptors so learners can predict any agent's effect rather than memorize a list: α1 → vasoconstriction (↑SVR); β1 → ↑heart rate and ↑contractility (↑CO); β2 → vasodilation; V1 (vasopressin) → vasoconstriction through a non-adrenergic pathway (useful when acidosis blunts catecholamine response).

segments.label: Objective 3 — The pressors and their uses
segments.html:
ASK: "Predict the profile before I reveal it — for norepinephrine, what happens to SVR, to CO, to heart rate? Now pick its lead indication." Click each pressor.
AgentReceptorsHeart rateContractility / COSVRLead indication
Norepinephrineα1 > β1– / mild ↑mild ↑↑↑Septic / distributive shock (first-line)
VasopressinV1––↑Add-on in distributive shock (NE-sparing)
Epinephrineα1, β1, β2↑↑↑ (β2 at low dose can ↓)Anaphylaxis; refractory shock
Phenylephrineα1↓ (reflex)–↑Distributive shock when tachyarrhythmia limits NE
Dobutamineβ1 > β2↑↑↑mild ↓Cardiogenic shock (inotrope)
Dopaminedose-dependent↑↑↓→↑ (dose)Largely supplanted by NE; more arrhythmia
Pearl — dobutamine in cardiogenic shock: its mild β2-mediated drop in SVR (afterload reduction) can paradoxically improve blood pressure by raising cardiac output. A little norepinephrine can be layered on to keep the MAP up while you diurese.

segments.label: Close (2 min)
segments.html: Hide the board. Ask the group to rebuild from memory: the four categories with their CVP/CO/SVR arrows, the first treatment lever for each, and the lead agent for septic vs. cardiogenic shock. Then the one-liner: "Fill the tank before you squeeze it; fix the pump when the pump is the problem."

cases.title: Case 1 — Sepsis on a failing heart
cases.stem: 45-year-old man with ischemic cardiomyopathy (EF 40%) admitted with pneumonia and hypotension. T 39.2°C, RR 26, SpO2 90% on 5L NC, BP 87/32 (MAP 50). Exam: warm extremities, JVP ~10 cm, trace BLE edema. Labs: Cr 2.2 (baseline 1), WBC 18k, lactate 3.2. ECG: NSR, no new ischemia. BNP 500 (baseline 200–600).
cases.reveal: Septic (distributive) shock — warm extremities, wide pulse pressure, and a clear source of infection. In a patient with underlying cardiac dysfunction it can be hard to know whether a cardiogenic component is also present; an SvO2 helps (normal 65–75%; low suggests a cardiogenic contribution, high is typical of septic shock).

cases.title: Case 2 — Pump failure
cases.stem: 45-year-old man with dilated cardiomyopathy (EF 20%) admitted with hypoxia and altered mental status. Afebrile, RR 26, BP 72/45 (MAP 54), SpO2 90% on 5L NC. Exam: cold and clammy, JVP 12 cm, 2+ BLE edema. Cr 2.2 (baseline 1). ECG: sinus tachycardia, no ischemia. Troponin minimally elevated (0.05). BNP >1500 (baseline 200–500). CXR: cephalization, Kerley B lines, bilateral effusions.
cases.reveal: Cardiogenic shock — cardiomyopathy history plus exam signs of volume overload. SvO2 and TTE confirm: his SvO2 was 42% (low, consistent with cardiogenic shock even given hypoxemia) and TTE showed EF 15–20% with elevated RA pressure and no new wall-motion abnormality.

cases.title: Case 3 — The empty tank
cases.stem: 45-year-old man with cirrhosis, 1 day of hematochezia and orthostasis. Afebrile, RR 18, HR 140s, BP 72/45 (MAP 54), SpO2 92% on RA. Exam: dry mucous membranes, cold/clammy, JVP not appreciable. Cr 2.2 (baseline 1), plts 110k, Hct 24% (baseline 38%), INR 2.4 (baseline).
cases.reveal: Hypovolemic shock from a GI bleed. Hematochezia with hemodynamic instability is concerning for a brisk upper GI source.

mcqs.stem: A 60-year-old man with pneumonia has BP 80/40 (MAP 53) despite 30 mL/kg of crystalloid. He has warm extremities and a lactate of 4. Which is the most appropriate next vasoactive agent?
mcqs.exp: C. Norepinephrine is the first-line vasopressor for septic (distributive) shock — it raises SVR (α1) with modest inotropy and less tachyarrhythmia than dopamine. Dopamine (A) causes more arrhythmias and is no longer preferred. Phenylephrine (B) is a pure α1 agent reserved for when tachyarrhythmia precludes NE. Dobutamine (D) is an inotrope for cardiogenic shock and would worsen the vasodilatory hypotension here.

mcqs.stem: A 70-year-old with an EF of 15% presents cold, clammy, and hypotensive (BP 78/50) with pulmonary edema. After cautious management of his airway, which strategy best addresses his shock?
opts: 1–2 L crystalloid bolus to improve preload
Dobutamine, with diuresis as BP allows
mcqs.exp: B. This is cardiogenic shock — a pump problem. Dobutamine augments contractility and its mild afterload reduction can improve output; diuresis (when BP permits) relieves the over-stretched ventricle. A fluid bolus (A) is harmful in cardiogenic shock with pulmonary edema. Pure vasoconstrictors (C, phenylephrine; D, vasopressin) raise afterload against a failing ventricle and do not address the low output.

mcqs.stem: A 45-year-old with cirrhosis and brisk hematochezia has HR 140, BP 72/45, flat neck veins, and Hct 24%. A trainee asks to start norepinephrine for the low MAP. What is the best response?
opts: Start norepinephrine and titrate to MAP >65
Resuscitate with blood products and fluid first — pressors have no role until preload is restored
Start vasopressin to spare splanchnic flow
mcqs.exp: B. This is hypovolemic (hemorrhagic) shock — "no role for vasopressors if the tank is not full." Volume and blood are the cornerstone; squeezing an empty vascular space (A, C) risks end-organ/digital ischemia without restoring output. Dobutamine (D) addresses contractility, which is not the problem.

mcqs.stem: A patient in septic shock requires escalating norepinephrine (now 0.4 mcg/kg/min) with a MAP still of 58. According to the 2021 Surviving Sepsis Campaign, what is the preferred next step?
opts: Continue increasing the norepinephrine dose without limit
mcqs.exp: B. The 2021 guideline suggests adding vasopressin (typically once NE is ~0.25–0.5 mcg/kg/min) rather than escalating norepinephrine indefinitely; the combination was associated with lower mortality. Unlimited NE escalation (A) increases ischemic risk. Phenylephrine (C) is not a preferred substitute for NE. Dobutamine (D) is added for a cardiogenic component or persistent hypoperfusion despite adequate MAP, not for refractory vasodilation per se.

mcqs.stem: A patient with septic shock-induced hypotension is enrolled in a discussion of fluid strategy. Based on the CLOVERS trial (2023), which statement is correct?
opts: A liberal fluid strategy significantly reduced 90-day mortality
A restrictive-fluid/early-vasopressor strategy significantly reduced mortality
There was no significant difference in mortality between restrictive and liberal strategies
mcqs.exp: C. CLOVERS found no significant difference in 90-day mortality (14.0% restrictive vs 14.9% liberal). Both an early-vasopressor/restrictive approach and a fluid-liberal approach are acceptable; neither is clearly superior, so the choice can be individualized. A, B, and D overstate the findings.

anki: Four broad categories of shock?
Hypovolemic, cardiogenic, obstructive, distributive.
Hemodynamic profile of hypovolemic shock (CVP / CO / SVR)?
CVP down-down, CO down, SVR up. Treat with volume (blood for hemorrhage).
Hemodynamic profile of cardiogenic shock (CVP / CO / SVR)?
CVP up, CO down-down, SVR up. Treat with an inotrope +/- diuresis; do NOT give IVF.
Hemodynamic profile of obstructive shock (CVP / CO / SVR)?
CVP up, CO down, SVR up. Relieve the obstruction (PE, tension PTX, tamponade); inotrope as a bridge.
Hemodynamic profile of distributive shock (CVP / CO / SVR)?
CVP down-to-normal, CO normal-to-up, SVR down. Treat with IVF + norepinephrine (+/- vasopressin).
Bedside clues that point to distributive vs low-output shock?
Distributive: warm extremities + wide pulse pressure (low SVR). Low-output (cardiogenic/hypovolemic): cold, clammy, narrow pulse pressure.
First-line vasopressor for septic shock?
Norepinephrine.
First-line agent for cardiogenic shock and why?
Dobutamine (an inotrope); its mild SVR drop (afterload reduction) can paradoxically improve BP by raising cardiac output.
"No role for vasopressors if ___"?
"...the tank is not full." Hypovolemic shock needs volume/blood first; pressors squeeze an empty space and risk ischemia.
What does SvO2 tell you in shock?
Low in cardiogenic shock; typically high in established septic shock (low in early sepsis). Affected by CO, SaO2, and hemoglobin, so it can read falsely low with hypoxemia/anemia.
Receptor effects: alpha1, beta1, beta2, V1?
alpha1 = vasoconstriction (up SVR); beta1 = up HR + up contractility; beta2 = vasodilation; V1 = non-adrenergic vasoconstriction (vasopressin).
Receptor profile and use of norepinephrine?
alpha1 > beta1; up-up SVR, mild up CO; first-line for distributive/septic shock.
Vasopressor of choice in anaphylaxis?
Epinephrine (alpha1, beta1, beta2).
2021 Surviving Sepsis Campaign - three practice changes since this talk?

Balanced crystalloids over 0.9% saline; 30 mL/kg bolus downgraded to a weak recommendation; add vasopressin early (NE ~0.25-0.5 mcg/kg/min) rather than escalating NE; start NE peripherally to avoid delay.
CLOVERS trial (2023) bottom line?
No difference in 90-day mortality between restrictive-fluid/early-vasopressor and liberal-fluid strategies for sepsis-induced hypotension.
Add-on agent for catecholamine-refractory vasodilatory shock?
Angiotensin II (ATHOS-3; FDA-approved) when norepinephrine + vasopressin are insufficient.
lessons.dot:
#Shock: MAP */* , lactate * . category most c/w [HYPOVOLEMIC / CARDIOGENIC / OBSTRUCTIVE / DISTRIBUTIVE] 2/2 * [hemorrhage·GI loss / HF·valve·arrhythmia / PE·tension PTX·tamponade / sepsis·anaphylaxis·neurogenic·adrenal].
- bedside read: extremities [warm=low SVR/distributive | cold=low output]; pulse pressure [wide=distributive | narrow=cardiogenic/hypovolemic]; JVP/CVP *; UOP *
- hemodynamic profile (CVP / CO / SVR): hypovolemic ↓↓/↓/↑ · cardiogenic ↑/↓↓/↑ · obstructive ↑/↓/↑ · distributive ↓-nml/nml-↑/↓
- confirm/sort: lactate, ScvO2 [↓ cardiogenic | ↑ septic; falsely ↓ if anemic/hypoxic], POCUS/TTE [EF, IVC, effusion, RV], CVP if line
- TANK first: hypovolemic/hemorrhagic → blood + IVF, 2 large-bore IV/introducer; NO pressors until preload repleted. sepsis → balanced crystalloid (LR/Plasma-Lyte) *mL/kg, reassess (CLOVERS: restrictive vs liberal = no mortality diff)
- PUMP: cardiogenic → dobutamine *mcg/kg/min (mild ↓SVR can ↑BP via ↑CO) ± diurese when BP allows; NO bolus IVF. obstructive → relieve cause [thrombolyse/anticoag, needle/chest tube, pericardiocentesis]
- SQUEEZE: distributive/septic → norepinephrine *mcg/kg/min to MAP ≥65 (1st-line); start PERIPHERAL to avoid delay; ADD vasopressin 0.03 U/min when NE ~0.25-0.5 mcg/kg/min (not max-NE); epinephrine for anaphylaxis/refractory; phenylephrine if tachyarrhythmia limits NE; angiotensin II if catechol+vaso refractory
- AVOID: pressors in unfilled hypovolemia; IVF bolus in cardiogenic pulm edema; dopamine (arrhythmia) as routine 1st-line
- source-driven: sepsis → cultures + abx within 1h, source control; anaphylaxis → epi IM/IV + steroids/antihistamine; adrenal → hydrocortisone; neurogenic → vasopressor + ? atropine
- monitoring: MAP goal ≥65, a-line if gtt, ScvO2/lactate clearance, UOP, telemetry; central access for vasopressin/escalating NE
takehome: Four categories of shock — hypovolemic, cardiogenic, obstructive, distributive — each with a distinct CVP/CO/SVR signature that names the first treatment lever.
Sort at the bedside first: warm + wide pulse pressure = distributive (low SVR); cold + narrow pulse pressure = low-output (cardiogenic or hypovolemic).

Norepinephrine is the vasopressor of choice for septic/distributive shock; dobutamine is the inotrope for cardiogenic shock; "no pressors if the tank is not full" — volume and blood come first in hypovolemia.
SvO2 helps sort mixed pictures (low in cardiogenic, high in established sepsis) but is confounded by CO, SaO2, and hemoglobin.

2021 Surviving Sepsis Campaign updates: balanced crystalloids over saline, add vasopressin early rather than maxing norepinephrine, and start norepinephrine peripherally to avoid delay; CLOVERS showed no mortality difference between restrictive and liberal fluid strategies.

lessons.title: Shoulder (Subacromial) Injection
lessons.subtitle: Optimized procedure package · Facilitator-led simulation-based training for IM residents · Shoulder exam + procedure ~45–70 min
lessons.credit: Adapted from TeachIM.org — Shoulder (subacromial space) Injection (Brandon Fainstad MD, Molly Brett MD, Lisa Thompson MD, Meara Melton MD; published Feb 2021, reviewed Jan 2023, materials rev. Mar 2025), CC BY-NC 4.0. Optimized June 2026: guideline currency verified, teaching design upgraded, assessments and clinical tools added. Procedure-type lesson — TeachIM publishes no slide deck for procedures; the package is built around the original clinical checklist, clinical assessment tool, practice cases, and quiz (all included below).
objectives: Determine the appropriate indications and contraindications for a steroid injection of the subacromial space.
Perform all critical steps of a subacromial steroid injection (except needle insertion) using high-fidelity simulation under direct preceptor observation.
Perform all critical steps of a subacromial steroid injection in the clinical environment under direct preceptor observation.

prework.q: Q1. Which shoulder problems belong in the subacromial space — and which need an intra-articular (glenohumeral) injection instead?
prework.a: Subacromial: rotator cuff tendinopathy, subacromial bursitis, impingement. Intra-articular (glenohumeral): glenohumeral OA, adhesive capsulitis (a GH injection is more efficacious than subacromial here). Acromioclavicular (AC) OA needs an AC-joint injection. Matching the space to the diagnosis is the key teaching point.

prework.q: Q2. Where do you mark the landmark and insert for the posterolateral subacromial approach?
prework.a: Mark the scapular spine and the lateral margin of the acromion; draw two intersecting lines to find the posterolateral point of the acromion, then insert ~1–2 cm below it in a soft recess. Aim the needle under the acromion, angled slightly cephalad toward the coracoid. The posterolateral and lateral approaches have similar outcomes.

prework.q: Q3. What should you tell a patient about how much a subacromial steroid injection actually helps?
prework.a: It gives a short-term improvement in pain and function (better SPADI at ~8 weeks) but is no better than no injection at 6–12 months (GRASP, Lancet 2021). Progressive exercise is core therapy; a single PT session plus an injection was the best-value strategy, and patients did their exercises more after an injection. Subacromial decompression surgery has little benefit over conservative care for impingement (CSAW, Lancet 2018).

segments.label: Hook & framing (2 min)
segments.html: "A 70-year-old comes in 'for the shoulder shot.' Her empty-can and impingement tests are negative, but her biceps reflex is down and Spurling's is positive. Before you reach for the needle — where's the lesion, and would a subacromial injection do anything?" Hold the answer. Establish the two highest-yield skills: localizing the problem on exam (intrinsic shoulder vs cervical spine vs glenohumeral vs AC) and setting realistic expectations (injection = short-term; exercise = durable).

segments.label: LEARN — Shoulder exam & the mimics
segments.html:
ASK: "Your impingement tests are negative but the arm still hurts. Name two diagnoses you'd be missing if you stopped at the rotator cuff."
Finding / testPoints toward+LR
Positive Neer's / Hawkin's; painful arc; pain on empty-canSubacromial impingement / RC tendinopathy—
Drop-arm test positiveFull-thickness rotator cuff tearhigh specificity
Speed's & Yergason's positive + biceps-groove tendernessBiceps tendonitis—
Global loss of active and passive ROMAdhesive capsulitis (or GH OA — get an x-ray)—
Spurling's positiveCervical radiculopathy≈ 4.5
Decreased biceps reflexCervical radiculopathy (C5–6)≈ 9.1
Neck rotation to involved side <60°Cervical radiculopathy+LR 1.7 (absent: −LR 0.2)
Pearl — don't inject a neck: When intrinsic shoulder maneuvers are negative, think cervical radiculopathy — a positive Spurling's (+LR 4.5) and especially a decreased biceps reflex (+LR 9.1) point to the C-spine, which needs an MRI of the cervical spine, not a shoulder injection.

segments.label: LEARN — Match the injection to the diagnosis
segments.html:
ASK: "Subacromial, glenohumeral, or AC injection — sort these: RC tendinopathy, glenohumeral OA, adhesive capsulitis, AC arthritis."
DiagnosisWhere to inject
Rotator cuff tendinopathy / subacromial bursitis / impingementSubacromial space (this lesson)
Glenohumeral OA · adhesive capsulitisIntra-articular glenohumeral (more efficacious than subacromial for adhesive capsulitis)
Acromioclavicular OAAC joint

segments.label: LEARN — Indications, contraindications & expectations
segments.html:
Indications (subacromial)Contraindications / deferNot contraindications
RC tendinopathy, subacromial bursitis, impingement (after/with conservative care)Overlying skin infection; anticipated shoulder surgery within ~3 months (peri-op infection risk — esp. CSI within 4 weeks of arthroscopy); a CSI in the same space within the last 3 monthsWell-controlled T2DM (relative — counsel re: glucose); prior shoulder surgery without intra-articular hardware
Safety — injections and upcoming surgery: Corticosteroid injection within ~4 weeks before shoulder arthroscopy increases infection risk; defer the injection if surgery is anticipated within ~3 months.
Pearl — expectation setting (GRASP / CSAW): Subacromial CSI helps pain/function short-term (~8 wk) but not at 6–12 months; progressive exercise is core therapy, and subacromial decompression surgery offers little over conservative care for impingement. For a full-thickness RC tear with persistent symptoms after 3 months of PT, surgery beats conservative care (Cederqvist 2021); partial-thickness tears do equally well with PT.

segments.label: SEE — Supply prep (no-touch) & injection
segments.html:
1. Identify supplies (non-sterile gloves): 1% lidocaine (5 cc), triamcinolone 40 mg/mL (1 cc), alcohol swabs, 5–10 cc syringe, 18 g draw-up needle, 21–25 g 1.5" injection needle, marker, chlorhexidine, gauze, band-aid. Draw-up tips: clean vial tops; separate needles for drawing up vs injecting; inject air when withdrawing >3 cc; draw lidocaine first and add a little to the steroid vial.
  2. Mark the scapular spine and lateral acromion → posterolateral point of the acromion; insertion ~1–2 cm below it in a soft recess.
  3. Sterilize with a chlorhexidine scrub ×30 s.
  4. Position/stabilize the shoulder with the non-dominant hand; insert the needle under the acromion, angled slightly cephalad toward the coracoid, to ~3 cm.
  5. Aspirate (no blood), then inject the lidocaine/triamcinolone mix without resistance. Remove needle, band-aid.
ASK: "You feel firm resistance when you push the plunger. What does that mean and what do you do?"

Resistance = the tip is in tendon/soft tissue, not the bursa/subacromial space — injecting into the tendon risks rupture. Withdraw slightly and redirect under the acromion until the injection flows freely.

segments.label: PRACTICE → PROVE — Mastery learning
segments.html: Each learner takes a turn as proceduralist with a peer/standardized patient. In the simulation, the learner lists indications/contraindications and verbalizes needle insertion (insertion is not physically performed in sim). In the clinical setting, they must identify the specific indication for this patient and demonstrate the supporting history/exam finding. Repeat until the learner meets the passing standard on the clinical assessment tool (error-counting checklist + Global Skills + Entrustment).

segments.label: Close (2 min)
segments.html: Hide the guide. Ask the learner to rebuild: the exam findings that send you to the C-spine instead of the shoulder, which diagnoses get a subacromial vs glenohumeral vs AC injection, the posterolateral landmark and trajectory, what to tell the patient about durability, and when surgery beats PT.

cases.title: Case 1 — The shoulder pain that isn't the shoulder
cases.stem: A 70-year-old woman has 2 months of left shoulder pain. Empty-can and external-rotation strength are 4/5 without pain; Neer's and Hawkin's are negative. Her biceps reflex is decreased on the left, Spurling's is positive, and neck rotation to the left is reduced. Six weeks of PT haven't helped.
cases.reveal: MRI of the cervical spine (without contrast). Negative intrinsic shoulder maneuvers plus a positive Spurling's (+LR 4.5) and a decreased biceps reflex (+LR 9.1) point to cervical radiculopathy, not a subacromial problem. A subacromial injection would not help; image the neck.

cases.title: Case 2 — Frozen shoulder, what next
cases.stem: A 60-year-old woman with T2DM has 7 months of left shoulder pain after a fall and limited active and passive ROM in all planes. You refer her to PT and order a shoulder x-ray. What's the best next treatment step?
cases.reveal: This is adhesive capsulitis (global loss of active and passive ROM; diabetes is a risk factor). Best next step is an intra-articular glenohumeral injection (more efficacious than subacromial for frozen shoulder) alongside PT. The UK FROST trial found no clinically important difference between PT+injection, manipulation under anesthesia+injection, and capsular release — and the surgical arms had more serious adverse events, so start conservatively. The x-ray screens for glenohumeral OA, which can also limit passive ROM.

mcqs.stem: A patient with shoulder pain has negative Neer's/Hawkin's, a decreased biceps reflex, and a positive Spurling's. What is the best next diagnostic step?
mcqs.exp: C. The picture is cervical radiculopathy (Spurling's +LR 4.5; decreased biceps reflex +LR 9.1), not intrinsic shoulder disease — image the C-spine. A shoulder MRI/x-ray or subacromial injection would miss the diagnosis.

mcqs.stem: Which diagnosis is an appropriate indication for a subacromial (rather than intra-articular) injection?
mcqs.exp: C. Rotator cuff tendinopathy/impingement and subacromial bursitis are subacromial-space targets. Glenohumeral OA and adhesive capsulitis are treated with intra-articular glenohumeral injection (more efficacious for frozen shoulder), and AC OA with an AC-joint injection.

mcqs.stem: Which is the strongest reason to defer a subacromial corticosteroid injection?
opts: Prior shoulder surgery without intra-articular hardware
Anticipated shoulder surgery within the next 3 months
mcqs.exp: C. Corticosteroid injection close to surgery (especially within ~4 weeks of arthroscopy) increases infection risk, so defer if surgery is anticipated within ~3 months. Well-controlled diabetes (A) is a relative consideration (counsel re: glucose), prior surgery without hardware (B) is not a contraindication, and an injection 4 months ago (D) is outside the "avoid within 3 months" window.

mcqs.stem: A 45-year-old with 4 months of impingement-pattern shoulder pain asks about a "quick fix." What is accurate counseling?
opts: A minimum of 6 weeks of PT is required before anything else
A subacromial steroid injection can improve pain and function at ~8 weeks
Arthroscopic subacromial decompression gives better long-term relief than conservative care
An injection makes patients less likely to do their PT
mcqs.exp: B. In GRASP (Lancet 2021), subacromial CSI improved SPADI at 8 weeks (no difference by 6–12 months), 6 PT sessions were not superior to 1, and patients actually did more of their exercises after an injection. Subacromial decompression has little benefit over conservative care (CSAW, Lancet 2018), making C wrong; A and D are contradicted by the evidence.

mcqs.stem: A 50-year-old has 3 months of difficulty lifting the arm overhead despite PT and NSAIDs; exam shows a positive drop-arm test. What is the next best step?
opts: Refer to orthopedic surgery for rotator cuff repair
Refer for an intra-articular glenohumeral injection
mcqs.exp: B. A positive drop-arm test plus persistent symptoms after 3 months of PT suggests a full-thickness rotator cuff tear, for which surgery is significantly better than conservative care for pain and function at 2 years (Cederqvist, Ann Rheum Dis 2021). (Partial-thickness tears do equally well with PT.) An injection wouldn't address a full-thickness tear.

anki: Which diagnoses get a SUBACROMIAL injection?
Rotator cuff tendinopathy, subacromial bursitis, impingement.
Which shoulder diagnoses need INTRA-ARTICULAR (glenohumeral) injection instead?
Glenohumeral OA and adhesive capsulitis (GH injection more efficacious than subacromial for frozen shoulder). AC OA, AC-joint injection.
Landmark & insertion for the posterolateral subacromial approach?
Mark scapular spine + lateral acromion, find posterolateral point of the acromion; insert ~1-2 cm below it in a soft recess, angled under the acromion slightly cephalad toward the coracoid.
Posterolateral vs lateral subacromial approach, outcome difference?
No significant difference in night pain, function, or SPADI (Ogbeivor 2019). US guidance has mixed data vs landmark.
Exam findings pointing to cervical radiculopathy (a shoulder-pain mimic)?
Spurling's +LR 4.5; decreased biceps reflex +LR 9.1; neck rotation to involved side <60 deg +LR 1.7 (if rotation NOT limited, -LR 0.2). Image the C-spine, not the shoulder.
Positive drop-arm test suggests?
Full-thickness rotator cuff tear.
Speed's + Yergason's + biceps-groove tenderness suggests?
Biceps tendonitis (these tests can also be positive in RC tendinopathy; localized biceps tenderness + negative RC tests favor biceps).
Global loss of active AND passive ROM suggests?
Adhesive capsulitis (or glenohumeral OA, order an x-ray). Diabetes is a risk factor for adhesive capsulitis.
Does subacromial CSI work long-term for rotator cuff disease?
Short-term only: better SPADI at ~8 wk, no difference at 6-12 mo (GRASP Lancet 2021). Progressive exercise is core; injection helps adherence.
Subacromial decompression surgery for impingement, benefit?
Little/no benefit over conservative care (CSAW Lancet 2018).
Full-thickness vs partial-thickness RC tear, surgery or PT?
Full-thickness with persistent symptoms after ~3 mo PT, surgery better at 2 yr (Cederqvist 2021). Partial-thickness, PT = surgery.
Best-evidence management of adhesive capsulitis?

PT +/- intra-articular glenohumeral CSI. UK FROST: no clinically important difference between PT+CSI, MUA+CSI, and capsular release; surgical arms had more serious adverse events, so start conservative.
Strongest reason to defer a subacromial CSI?
Anticipated shoulder surgery within ~3 months (CSI within ~4 wk of arthroscopy raises infection risk); overlying skin infection; CSI in same space within last 3 months.
Is well-controlled diabetes a contraindication to shoulder CSI?
No, relative; counsel that glucose may rise transiently. Prior shoulder surgery without intra-articular hardware is also not a contraindication.
Glenohumeral OA without trauma/RC injury, what to consider?
The shoulder is non-weight-bearing, so primary GH OA is uncommon without trauma, consider a metabolic cause (e.g., hemochromatosis: check iron/TIBC/ferritin); RA is also in the differential.
Supplies for a subacromial injection?
1% lidocaine (5 cc), triamcinolone 40 mg/mL (1 cc), alcohol swabs, 5-10 cc syringe, 18 g draw-up needle, 21-25 g 1.5" injection needle, marker, chlorhexidine, gauze, band-aid.
What does resistance on injection mean?
The tip is in tendon/soft tissue, NOT the bursa, injecting into tendon risks rupture. Withdraw and redirect under the acromion until it flows freely.
"No-touch" draw-up tips?
Clean vial tops with alcohol; separate needles for drawing up vs injecting; inject air when withdrawing >3 cc; draw lidocaine first, add a little to the steroid vial to ease aspiration.
lessons.dot:
PROCEDURE: Shoulder (subacromial space) corticosteroid injection, *R/L
Indication: *[rotator cuff tendinopathy / subacromial bursitis / impingement] — supporting hx/exam: *[+Neer/Hawkin, painful arc, pain on empty-can]; mimics excluded (NO cervical radiculopathy — *Spurling neg/biceps reflex intact; not glenohumeral OA / adhesive capsulitis / AC arthritis requiring a different target)
Pre-proc checklist:
 - contraindic reviewed: NO overlying skin infection; NO anticipated shoulder surgery within ~3 mo; no CSI to same space within 3 mo. Relative: *well-controlled DM (counseled re: transient glucose rise); prior surgery w/o intra-articular hardware = OK.
 - expectations set: injection = short-term relief (~8 wk), no different at 6-12 mo; progressive exercise/PT = core durable therapy.
 - consent obtained; risks (pain flare, infection, bleeding, skin/fat atrophy or depigmentation, transient hyperglycemia, tendon injury) & alternatives discussed
 - TIME-OUT: correct patient (2 identifiers), procedure, and SIDE/site confirmed
Technique: scapular spine + lateral acromion marked → posterolateral point of acromion; insertion ~1-2 cm below in soft recess. Chlorhexidine scrub x30 s. Shoulder stabilized w/ non-dominant hand; *21g needle advanced UNDER the acromion, slightly cephalad toward coracoid, ~3 cm. Aspiration *[no return]. Then *__ mL 1% lidocaine + 1 mL triamcinolone 40 mg/mL injected WITHOUT resistance. Needle removed, band-aid applied.
Post: tolerated well, no immediate complication. PT referral placed; pt encouraged to begin PT within ~4 wks for durable benefit. Return precautions (signs of infection) given.
Performing provider: *  | Supervising: *
takehome: Localize before you inject: negative intrinsic shoulder maneuvers + a positive Spurling's / decreased biceps reflex = cervical radiculopathy (image the C-spine, don't inject the shoulder).
Match the space to the diagnosis: subacromial for RC tendinopathy/bursitis/impingement; glenohumeral for GH OA and adhesive capsulitis; AC joint for AC OA.
Posterolateral landmark: ~1–2 cm below the posterolateral acromion, needle under the acromion angled slightly toward the coracoid; inject only without resistance (resistance = tendon, risks rupture).
Set expectations: subacromial CSI helps short-term (~8 wk) but not at 6–12 months; progressive exercise is core therapy, and decompression surgery adds little for impingement.
Know when surgery wins: a full-thickness RC tear (positive drop-arm) with persistent symptoms after ~3 months of PT does better with repair; partial-thickness tears do equally well with PT.
Defer near surgery: avoid CSI if shoulder surgery is anticipated within ~3 months (infection risk). Well-controlled diabetes is only a relative caution.

1. Hopewell S, Keene DJ, Marian IR, et al.   2. Beard DJ, Rees JL, Cook JA, et al. Arthroscopic subacromial decompression for subacromial shoulder pain (CSAW): a multicentre, pragmatic, placebo-controlled, randomised surgical trial. Lancet. 2018;391(10118):329-338.
  3. Rangan A, Brealey SD, Keding A, et al. Management of adults with primary frozen shoulder in secondary care (UK FROST): a multicentre, pragmatic, three-arm, superiority randomised clinical trial. Lancet. 2020;396(10256):977-989.
  4. Cederqvist S, Flinkkilä T, Sormaala M, et al. Non-surgical and surgical treatments for rotator cuff disease: a pragmatic randomised clinical trial with 2-year follow-up. Ann Rheum Dis. 2021;80(6):796-802.
  5. Ogbeivor C, Bandaru S, Milton C. A comparison of the effectiveness of lateral versus posterior approach to shoulder injection in patients with subacromial impingement syndrome. Musculoskeletal Care. 2019;17(3):257-268.
  6. Tallia AF, Cardone DA. Diagnostic and therapeutic injection of the shoulder region. Am Fam Physician. 2003;67(6):1271-1278.
  7. McGee S. Evidence-Based Physical Diagnosis. 3rd ed. Elsevier/Saunders; 2012.

lessons.changelog:
AreaWhat changed
Currency checkVerified against current standards (June 2026): the AAOS 2019 rotator cuff guideline remains current; the landmark RCTs the quiz is built on — GRASP (Lancet 2021), CSAW (Lancet 2018), UK FROST (Lancet 2020), Cederqvist (Ann Rheum Dis 2021) — remain practice-defining and are not superseded. No standing-content change required.
Content addedConsolidated the shoulder-exam likelihood ratios (including the cervical-radiculopathy mimic) into a table; made explicit the "match the injection to the diagnosis" (subacromial vs glenohumeral vs AC) framing; added the peri-operative infection caution (CSI within ~4 weeks of shoulder arthroscopy → increased infection risk) supporting the defer-near-surgery rule.
CorrectionsNo factual errors found in the clinical checklist, clinical assessment tool, practice cases, or quiz. (A page-header typo "Jnauary" appears on TeachIM's hosted page, not in the bundled files; not edited.)
PedagogyRestructured into the Learn–See–Practice–Prove arc with a 2-min hook, ≥4 ASK pause-points, a likelihood-ratio exam table, a diagnosis→injection-site table, pearls/safety callouts, the resistance-on-injection teaching point, and a retrieval close. Integrated the shoulder-exam practice cases.
AssessmentAdded 3 pre-work retrieval questions, 5 ABIM-style board MCQs with full distractor explanations, 2 case vignettes (from the quiz/practice cases), and 18 spaced-repetition cards.
Clinical toolAdded an EMR-ready subacromial-injection procedure-note / time-out dotphrase (procedure-type structure) with the mimic-exclusion, expectation-setting, and defer-near-surgery lines.
One-pagerBuilt a dense single-page procedure + shoulder-exam reference (separate print-CSS HTML → PDF, one page).
FormatSelf-contained HTML launcher linking all original companion files (clinical checklist, clinical assessment tool, practice cases, quiz with and without answers) + generated files; matches the Syncope/Thoracentesis design system. No _Optimized.pptx — TeachIM publishes no slide deck for procedures; per the no-rebuild rule none was created.
Human review neededNone. (Quiz answers/explanations and citations are embedded in the source "with answers" PDF; answers used here match the keyed answers and current evidence.)

lessons.title: Stress Testing — Outpatient Evaluation for CAD
lessons.subtitle: Optimized lesson package · Facilitator-led interactive chalk talk for IM residents · 40–50 min total
lessons.credit: Adapted from TeachIM.org — Stress Testing / Outpatient Evaluation for CAD (Brandon Fainstad, MD; expert review Kelsey Flint, MD; published Dec 2020), CC BY-NC 4.0. Optimized June 2026: guideline currency verified and layered (2021 Chest Pain + 2023 Chronic Coronary Disease), one deck typo corrected, pedagogy restructured, assessments added. Companion deck: Stress_Testing_CAD_Optimized.pptx (original interactive board, surgically corrected).
objectives: Frame the meaningful clinical question in a patient with chest pain — for the outpatient with non-acute symptoms, "is this obstructive CAD?" (not "is this ACS?").
Use a validated pretest-probability tool (CAD Consortium Clinical Score) to risk-stratify the likelihood of clinically significant CAD — as a check on gestalt, not a replacement for it.
Build a framework for selecting the appropriate outpatient stress test and initial medical management based on risk category and the patient's ability to exercise.
Apply the framework to six clinical cases.

prework.q: Q1. For an outpatient with weeks of exertional chest pressure, what is the clinical question you are trying to answer — and how is it different from the question in the ED?
prework.a: Outpatient, non-acute: "Is this symptom due to a flow-limiting (obstructive, >50%) coronary lesion?" — a diagnosis-of-CAD question answered with pretest probability and stress/anatomic testing. The ED question is "Is this acute coronary syndrome, and will aggressive inpatient management reduce a short-term major adverse cardiac event (MACE)?" — answered with serial ECGs, troponin, and tools like the HEART score.

prework.q: Q2. Name the three pretest-probability risk bands the talk uses for obstructive CAD and the action each triggers.
prework.a: Low (<5%) → look for an alternative cause, no stress test. Intermediate (5–70%) → stress test to confirm or exclude obstructive CAD. High (>70%) → obtain ECG + imaging to exclude high-grade disease (left main, proximal LAD, 3-vessel) and start empiric medical therapy.

prework.q: Q3. When can a patient do an exercise treadmill ECG (ETT) instead of an imaging stress test — and what disqualifies the ECG portion?
prework.a: ETT requires that the patient can exercise AND has an interpretable baseline ECG. Disqualifying ECG findings (reduce sensitivity → need imaging): LBBB, paced rhythm, pre-excitation (WPW), LVH with repolarization changes, or >1 mm resting ST depression. If the patient can exercise but the ECG is uninterpretable, use exercise imaging (echo or MPI); if they cannot exercise, use pharmacologic stress.

segments.label: Hook (2 min)
segments.html: "Three patients, all with chest pain that comes on when they exert themselves and goes away with rest. One needs nothing, one needs a stress test, one needs to skip the stress test entirely. How do you decide before you order anything?" Tell them the whole talk is one decision tree — and that the tree starts not with a test but with a question.

segments.label: Objective 1 — Frame the question
segments.html:
ASK: "Same symptom — chest pain — in clinic vs. in the ED. Are you asking the same question? What is each question really about?" Click Clinical Question after they answer.

Outpatient, non-acute chest pain asks: is this obstructive (>50%) CAD? The ED asks: is this ACS, and will aggressive inpatient care reduce short-term MACE? The two questions use different tools (pretest probability + stress/anatomic testing vs. serial troponin/ECG + HEART score) and this talk is exclusively the outpatient question. Naming the question keeps the workup from sprawling.

Pearl — describe the pain precisely: The 2021 AHA/ACC Chest Pain Guideline asks clinicians to retire "typical / atypical" and instead describe pain as cardiac, possibly cardiac, or noncardiac. "Atypical" was vague and tended to falsely reassure. The substance of the history (exertional, relieved by rest/nitroglycerin, reproducible) is unchanged — only the label.

segments.label: Objective 2 — Pretest probability
segments.html:
ASK: "Before any test, what raises or lowers your suspicion that this chest pain is obstructive CAD?" Collect features, then click CAD Consortium Score.

The CAD Consortium Clinical Score estimates the pretest probability of obstructive CAD from age, sex, chest-pain character, and risk factors (diabetes, hypertension, dyslipidemia, smoking). It outperformed the older Diamond–Forrester model and predicts both obstructive disease and events (Bittencourt, Circulation 2016). The talk sorts patients into three bands:

Pretest probabilityInterpretationAction
Low (<5%)Obstructive CAD unlikelyLook for an alternative cause; no stress test
Intermediate (5–70%)Diagnostic uncertainty — testing changes managementStress test to confirm or exclude obstructive CAD
High (>70%)Obstructive CAD likely; the question is severityECG + imaging to exclude left main / proximal LAD / 3-vessel disease; start empiric medical therapy
ASK: "Did the score match your gut?" The teaching point: scores are a check on System-1 intuition, not a replacement. They will misclassify individual patients — use them to catch your own over- and under-calling, then decide.

segments.label: Objective 3 — Choose the test and the management
segments.html:
Click Diagnosis and Management, then the flow diagram. "Risk" on the diagram is the pretest probability of obstructive CAD.

Low risk: evaluate for an alternative cause (MSK, GI, anxiety, pulmonary). High risk: obtain an ECG and an imaging stress test — the goal here is not to diagnose CAD (you already believe it's there) but to rule out high-grade disease (left main, proximal LAD, significant cardiomyopathy) that carries a sudden-death risk; if none, treat medically and defer cath/CCTA until medical therapy fails.

ASK: "Intermediate-risk patient in front of you — what's the very first branch point in choosing the stress test?" (Can the patient exercise?)

Intermediate risk walks the branch points:

Branch pointIf yesIf no
Can the patient exercise?Exercise test (functional info + reproduces the symptom)Pharmacologic stress (vasodilator MPI or dobutamine echo)
Is the baseline ECG interpretable? (no LBBB, paced rhythm, pre-excitation, LVH w/ strain, >1 mm ST-dep)Exercise treadmill ECG (ETT) — cheapest, preferredExercise imaging (stress echo or MPI)
Echo vs. nuclear (MPI/SPECT)?Institution-dependent; both give EF and ischemia. Echo adds valve assessment; nuclear may be easier with body habitus / poor windows.
ASK: "The stress test comes back positive. Cath now?" — not necessarily. Start medical therapy; reserve coronary anatomy (CCTA or left heart cath) for markedly positive tests or failure of medical management.

"Markedly positive" — go define the anatomy: ECG/exercise — significant ST-depression at low workload, ST-elevation, or exertional hypotension. Imaging — resting EF <35%, EF drop or LV dilation with stress, or ischemia in >2 vascular territories.

Empiric medical management of stable CAD (build it on the board):

BucketTherapy
Risk-factor modificationDiet/exercise, smoking cessation, A1c <7.0, BP <130/80
CardioprotectiveASA 81 mg daily; high-intensity statin; ACE-I/ARB if DM, CKD, or EF <40%
Anti-anginalSublingual nitroglycerin PRN; beta-blocker; add long-acting nitrate or CCB; consider ranolazine
Pearl — what's changed since 2020 (2023 Chronic Coronary Disease guideline): a beta-blocker is no longer required as the default anti-anginal in someone without prior MI or LVEF ≤50% and no other indication — pick the anti-anginal that fits the patient. High-intensity statin remains first-line; add an SGLT2 inhibitor or GLP-1 RA when there is concomitant diabetes (SGLT2i if HF), and low-dose colchicine 0.5 mg daily is now a reasonable secondary-prevention add-on (Class 2a, LoDoCo2).

segments.label: Objective 4 — Cases
segments.html: Break into small teams. For each vignette: decide whether the pain is cardiac / possibly cardiac / noncardiac, assign a risk band, compare it to the CAD Consortium score, then choose a test and a disposition. The author built the stems deliberately incomplete — push the discussion, there is no single right answer. Full worked cases are in the Cases section below.

segments.label: Close (2 min)
segments.html: Hide the board. Ask the group to rebuild the tree from memory: the question, the three risk bands and their actions, the exercise-vs-pharmacologic and ECG-vs-imaging branch points, and the rule that medical therapy comes before cath. Then show the take-home slide.

cases.title: Case 1 — Can't exercise
cases.stem: 45-year-old woman, remote smoker with well-controlled DM, with 2 months of sharp, intermittent central chest pain every few days while doing housework or grocery shopping, resolving on its own with rest. She cannot climb stairs or walk more than a block because of bilateral knee OA. Total chol 180, HDL 38, LDL 142; A1c 7.2.
cases.reveal: Possibly cardiac — non-exertional quality but real risk factors (DM, prior smoking, LDL 142). The CAD Consortium score lands her in the intermediate range, where a stress test changes management. Note her score may differ from your gestalt — that discordance is the teaching point.

cases.title: Case 2 — Score vs. gestalt
cases.stem: 73-year-old man, never-smoker with HTN, with one week of an ache and pressure just inferior to the sternum that comes on with minimal activity, usually in the evening or early morning, self-resolving after ~10 minutes. Still able to walk a few blocks with his dog. A1c 5.6.
cases.reveal: The story sounds anginal (exertional, relieved by rest), so gestalt says cardiac. This is the deliberate gestalt-vs-score case: run the CAD Consortium score and discuss where it lands and why it might diverge from your gut. Whatever band you land on, an interpretable ECG and the ability to exercise keep your options open.

cases.title: Case 3 — High pretest, uninterpretable ECG
cases.stem: 75-year-old man, active smoker with HTN and COPD (GOLD 3), with one week of chest tightness when gardening or carrying in groceries that resolves after a few minutes of rest. Total chol 180, HDL 38, LDL 142 (on a statin); A1c 5.6. ECG: LVH with 1+ mm ST depression in V4–6.
cases.reveal: Typical exertional angina in a 75-year-old active smoker = high pretest probability. The job now is severity, not diagnosis: obtain an ECG and an imaging stress test to exclude left main / proximal LAD / 3-vessel disease.

cases.title: Case 4 — Known CAD, new symptoms
cases.stem: 62-year-old man, active smoker with COPD (GOLD 3), DM, and a prior drug-eluting stent in the RCA 3 years ago, now with shortness of breath and chest tightness every few days, usually with exertion but sometimes at rest, self-resolving. He cannot climb stairs due to hip pain. On ASA, beta-blocker, ACE-I, statin; A1c 7.9. Exam: expiratory wheezing. ECG: sinus with Q waves in II/III/aVF and incomplete RBBB.
cases.reveal: This is symptomatic known CAD despite guideline-directed medical therapy — at least intermediate risk. Stress imaging (MPI, echo, or CMR) is recommended to localize and quantify ischemia; he can't exercise (hip pain) so use pharmacologic stress. Some patients go straight to coronary anatomy (CCTA or LHC) given the symptom burden.

cases.title: Case 5 — The straightforward exerciser
cases.stem: 65-year-old man, active smoker with HTN, with 2 days of chest pain whenever he climbs stairs or walks a few blocks with the dog. He feels diaphoretic and dizzy when it happens and needs to sit down; symptoms self-resolve after 10 minutes. Total chol 180, HDL 38, LDL 142 (on a statin); A1c 5.6. ECG: normal sinus rhythm.
cases.reveal: Exertional pain with diaphoresis in a 65-year-old smoker = intermediate-to-high pretest. He can exercise and the baseline ECG is interpretable → exercise treadmill ECG (ETT) first; it reproduces the symptom and adds functional/prognostic data (METs achieved).

cases.title: Case 6 — When it's actually ACS
cases.stem: 55-year-old woman, active smoker with well-controlled DM, with 2–3 weeks of chest tightness and shortness of breath when hiking, resolving with rest after 10–15 minutes. Total chol 180, HDL 38, LDL 142; A1c 7.2. ECG: LVH with 1+ mm ST depression and T-wave inversions V1–3; troponin negative. (Board pretest tag: "High — concerning for ACS.")
cases.reveal: New dynamic-appearing ECG changes (ST depression, anterior TWI) with a concerning, accelerating story push this toward an acute evaluation — admit for ACS rule-out with serial ECGs and troponins, telemetry, and inpatient TTE rather than scheduling an outpatient stress test. The outpatient pretest framework applies to stable, non-acute chest pain; this is the boundary case that teaches when to switch questions.

mcqs.stem: A 58-year-old woman has 3 weeks of substernal pressure when climbing stairs, relieved by rest within 5 minutes. She has hypertension and hyperlipidemia and can walk on a treadmill. Resting ECG is normal. Pretest probability of obstructive CAD is intermediate. What is the most appropriate initial test?
mcqs.exp: B. She can exercise and her baseline ECG is interpretable, so the exercise treadmill ECG is the preferred first test — inexpensive, provides functional capacity and symptom reproduction. Pharmacologic MPI (C) is for patients who cannot exercise. CCTA (A) is a reasonable Class 1 alternative in the 2021 guideline, especially if <65 and less obstructive disease is suspected, but among the options the functional ETT is the standard first step when exercise and ECG allow. Invasive angiography (D) is not a first-line diagnostic test in stable, intermediate-risk patients.

mcqs.stem: A 70-year-old man with diabetes and a left bundle branch block on his resting ECG has exertional angina and cannot walk more than half a block because of severe peripheral arterial disease. Which stress test is most appropriate?
mcqs.exp: C. Two barriers: he cannot exercise (so any exercise-based test, A and B, is out) and he has LBBB (which makes the ECG uninterpretable and also causes false septal defects with exercise MPI — vasodilator stress is preferred over exercise or dobutamine in LBBB). Vasodilator MPI solves both. Empiric treatment alone (D) is reasonable in very high pretest probability but he still warrants assessment for high-grade disease given symptoms.

mcqs.stem: A 64-year-old man with stable exertional angina has a stress MPI showing a small, single-territory area of inducible ischemia with normal LV ejection fraction. He is started on a statin, aspirin, and an anti-anginal. Based on the ISCHEMIA trial, what is the expected benefit of proceeding directly to coronary angiography and revascularization rather than continuing optimal medical therapy?
opts: A significant reduction in death or myocardial infarction
No reduction in death or MI, though angina may improve
Elimination of the need for statin therapy
mcqs.exp: B. ISCHEMIA (NEJM 2020) randomized stable patients with moderate-to-severe ischemia to an initial invasive strategy vs. optimal medical therapy and found no reduction in death or MI; an invasive approach improved angina-related quality of life in symptomatic patients. So in stable CAD without high-risk anatomy, medical therapy first is appropriate; revascularization is driven by refractory symptoms or high-risk features — not by the presence of moderate ischemia alone (A, C). Statins remain essential regardless (D).

mcqs.stem: According to the 2021 AHA/ACC Chest Pain Guideline, how should a patient's chest pain be characterized in documentation?
mcqs.exp: B. The 2021 guideline recommends describing chest pain as cardiac, possibly cardiac, or noncardiac, and explicitly discourages "atypical," which was vague and falsely reassuring (A). "Stable vs. unstable" (C) describes the temporal pattern, not the likelihood of an ischemic cause. Diamond–Forrester (D) is an older pretest-probability model now superseded by CAD Consortium-type scores.

mcqs.stem: A 48-year-old woman with atypical chest pain has a low pretest probability of obstructive CAD. Per the 2021 Chest Pain Guideline, which test can be used as a first-line option to help exclude calcified atherosclerotic plaque and support deferring further testing?
mcqs.exp: A. In low-risk stable patients, a coronary artery calcium score of zero identifies a population at low risk of obstructive CAD and favorable prognosis, supporting deferral of further testing; the guideline endorses CAC as a useful first-line gatekeeper. Invasive angiography (B) and right heart cath (D) are inappropriate in this low-risk setting; stress CMR (C) is a functional imaging test reserved for intermediate-high risk or specific indications, not a first-line low-risk screen.

anki: Outpatient non-acute chest pain — what is the clinical question?
Is this symptom due to obstructive (>50%) CAD? (The ED question is instead: is this ACS, and will aggressive inpatient care reduce short-term MACE?)
What does the CAD Consortium Clinical Score estimate, and from what inputs?
Pretest probability of obstructive CAD, from age, sex, chest-pain character, and risk factors (DM, HTN, dyslipidemia, smoking). Outperforms Diamond–Forrester.
The three pretest-probability bands and the action for each?
Low <5% → seek alternative cause (no stress test). Intermediate 5–70% → stress test. High >70% → ECG + imaging to exclude high-grade disease, start empiric medical therapy.
For a high-pretest patient, what is the purpose of testing?
Not to diagnose CAD (already likely) but to exclude high-grade disease — left main, proximal LAD, or 3-vessel disease / significant cardiomyopathy — that carries sudden-death risk.
First branch point in choosing a stress test?
Can the patient exercise? Yes → exercise test (adds functional info, reproduces symptom). No → pharmacologic stress.
When can you use exercise treadmill ECG (ETT) vs. needing imaging?
ETT requires the ability to exercise AND an interpretable baseline ECG. Uninterpretable ECG (LBBB, paced, pre-excitation, LVH w/ strain, >1 mm ST-dep) → use imaging stress.
Why is ETT the preferred first functional test when feasible?
Cheap, gives functional capacity (METs) and prognostic data, and reproduces the symptom under observation.
Stress echo vs. nuclear MPI — how to choose?
Institution-dependent; both give EF and detect ischemia. Echo adds valve assessment; nuclear may be preferred with poor echo windows/body habitus.
"Markedly positive" exercise/ECG findings?
Significant ST depression at low workload, ST elevation, or exertional hypotension.
"Markedly positive" imaging findings?
Resting EF <35%, EF reduction or LV dilation with stress, or ischemia in >2 vascular territories.
After a positive (not markedly positive) stress test, next step?
Start medical management of stable CAD; defer coronary anatomy (CCTA or LHC) unless markedly positive or medical therapy fails.
Empiric medical therapy buckets for stable CAD?

Risk-factor modification (diet/exercise, smoking cessation, A1c <7, BP <130/80); cardioprotective (ASA 81, high-intensity statin, ACE-I/ARB if DM/CKD/EF<40%); anti-anginal (SL nitro PRN, beta-blocker, long-acting nitrate or CCB, consider ranolazine).
2021 Chest Pain Guideline — how should chest pain be described?
Cardiac / possibly cardiac / noncardiac — "typical/atypical" is discouraged.
2021 guideline — role of coronary artery calcium (CAC) in low-risk stable patients?
Useful first-line gatekeeper; CAC = 0 supports a low risk of obstructive CAD and deferral of further testing.
2021 guideline — status of CCTA for intermediate-high risk stable chest pain?
Class 1 first-line anatomic test (alongside stress imaging). CCTA favored if <65 y or less obstructive CAD suspected; stress favored if >65 y or more obstructive CAD suspected.
ISCHEMIA trial bottom line for stable CAD with moderate–severe ischemia?

An initial invasive strategy did not reduce death/MI vs. optimal medical therapy; revascularization improves angina-related quality of life. Treat medically first; revascularize for refractory symptoms or high-risk anatomy.
lessons.dot:
#Chest pain (stable, outpatient): * [cardiac / possibly cardiac / noncardiac] per 2021 AHA/ACC. Q: obstructive (>50%) CAD? (NOT ACS — non-acute, trop-neg, no dynamic ECG). exertional/rest, relieved by rest/SL-NTG y/n, reproducible y/n.
- pretest prob (CAD Consortium: age/sex/pain character + DM/HTN/dyslip/smoking) = * [low <5% / intermediate 5–70% / high >70%]; gestalt = * (use score to check, not replace, judgment)
- LOW <5%: no stress test; w/u alt cause [MSK, GERD, anxiety, pulm]; +/- CAC score (=0 supports deferral)
- INTERMEDIATE 5–70%: stress test. branch: can exercise? Y→exercise / N→pharm. ECG interpretable? (no LBBB/paced/WPW/LVH-strain/>1mm STd) Y→ETT ECG / N→imaging. echo (adds valves) vs MPI/SPECT (windows/habitus) = institutional
- HIGH >70%: ECG + IMAGING stress to r/o high-grade dz [L-main, prox LAD, 3v, EF<35]; start empiric med rx; defer cath unless markedly+ or fails med rx
- 2021 alt: CCTA = Class 1 first-line anatomic (favored if <65yo or less obstructive dz susp); stress imaging favored if >65yo or more obstructive dz susp
- markedly+ → define anatomy [CCTA vs LHC]: ECG/ex = STd low-workload / ST-elev / exertional hypoTN; imaging = rest EF<35 / EF↓ or dilation w/ stress / ischemia >2 territories
- med mgmt (stable CAD): ASA 81; high-intensity statin (+ ezetimibe/PCSK9 if LDL not at goal); ACE-I/ARB if DM/CKD/EF<40; anti-anginal [SL NTG prn, BB (not mandatory if no prior MI/EF≤50), long-acting nitrate, CCB, ranolazine]; SGLT2i or GLP-1 RA if DM (SGLT2i if HF); colchicine 0.5 mg daily (2a, LoDoCo2)
- risk-factor targets: A1c <7.0, BP <130/80, smoking cessation, diet/exercise/wt
- ISCHEMIA: stable + moderate–severe ischemia → invasive does NOT ↓ death/MI vs OMT; revascularize for refractory sx or high-risk anatomy
- f/u: RTC * wks for test results & titration; return precautions for rest pain / crescendo / dyspnea → ED for ACS eval
takehome: Frame the question first: outpatient non-acute chest pain asks "is this obstructive CAD?" — a different question, and a different workup, than the ED's "is this ACS?"

Risk-stratify by pretest probability (CAD Consortium): low <5% → seek an alternative cause; intermediate 5–70% → stress test; high >70% → ECG + imaging to exclude left main/proximal LAD/3-vessel disease, then empiric medical therapy.

Choose the stress test by what the patient can do: exercise treadmill ECG when they can exercise and the baseline ECG is interpretable; otherwise imaging stress (echo or MPI), exercise or pharmacologic.
Treat medically first; reserve CCTA/left heart cath for markedly positive testing or failure of medical management (ISCHEMIA).
Scores support — never supersede — clinical judgment.

extras.label: Guideline Update layered context — 2021 Chest Pain & 2023 Chronic Coronary Disease
extras.html:
The deck's standing diagnostic framework reflects the 2019 ESC chronic coronary syndromes guideline and remains a valid teaching structure. Two newer documents update the framing; surface them as context rather than replacing the original board.

2021 AHA/ACC/ASE/CHEST/SAEM/SCCT/SCMR Chest Pain Guideline (Gulati et al.)

  • Describe chest pain as cardiac / possibly cardiac / noncardiac; retire "typical/atypical."
  • Stable pain, no known CAD, low pretest probability → defer testing; coronary artery calcium (CAC) score is a useful first-line gatekeeper (CAC = 0 → low risk).
  • Intermediate–high risk → CCTA is Class 1 first-line (anatomic) alongside stress imaging (functional). CCTA favored if <65 y or less obstructive CAD suspected; stress favored if >65 y or more obstructive CAD suspected.
  • High-sensitivity troponin is preferred for the acute pathway.

2023 AHA/ACC/ACCP/ASPC/NLA/PCNA Chronic Coronary Disease Guideline (Virani et al.)

  • Beta-blockers are not required as anti-anginal therapy in patients without prior MI or LVEF ≤50% and no other indication — individualize the anti-anginal.
  • High-intensity statin first-line; add SGLT2 inhibitor or GLP-1 RA when there is concomitant diabetes (SGLT2i if HF), regardless of glycemic control.
  • Colchicine 0.5 mg daily is a reasonable secondary-prevention add-on (Class 2a; LoDoCo2).
  • ISCHEMIA (2020): in stable CAD with moderate–severe ischemia, an initial invasive strategy did not reduce death/MI vs. optimal medical therapy — supports the board's "treat medically first, defer cath" teaching.

  2. Knuuti J, Wijns W, Saraste A, et al. 2019 ESC Guidelines for the diagnosis and management of chronic coronary syndromes. Eur Heart J. 2020;41(3):407-477.
  3. Virani SS, Newby LK, Arnold SV, et al. 2023 AHA/ACC/ACCP/ASPC/NLA/PCNA Guideline for the Management of Patients With Chronic Coronary Disease. Circulation. 2023;148(9):e9-e119.
  4. Bittencourt MS, Hulten E, Polonsky TS, et al. ESC-Recommended Coronary Artery Disease Consortium Pretest Probability Scores More Accurately Predict Obstructive Coronary Disease and Cardiovascular Events Than the Diamond and Forrester Score: The Partners Registry. Circulation. 2016;134(3):201-211.
  5. Six AJ, Backus BE, Kelder JC. Chest pain in the emergency room: value of the HEART score. Neth Heart J. 2008;16(6):191-196.
  6. Maron DJ, Hochman JS, Reynolds HR, et al. Initial Invasive or Conservative Strategy for Stable Coronary Disease (ISCHEMIA). NEJM. 2020;382(15):1395-1407.
  7. Nidorf SM, Fiolet ATL, Mosterd A, et al. Colchicine in Patients with Chronic Coronary Disease (LoDoCo2). NEJM. 2020;383(19):1838-1847.
