lessons.title: Syncope
lessons.subtitle: Optimized lesson package · Facilitator-led interactive chalk talk for IM residents · 30–40 min total
lessons.credit: Adapted from TeachIM.org — Syncope (Hibshman, Dietsche; eds. Zhang, Fainstad; Oct 2023), CC BY-NC 4.0. Optimized June 2026: guideline currency verified, two errors corrected, pedagogy restructured, assessments added. Companion deck: Syncope_Optimized.pptx (original interactive board, surgically corrected).
objectives: Define syncope and distinguish it from other causes of transient loss of consciousness (TLOC).
Describe the pathophysiology differentiating the 3 classifications of syncope: orthostatic hypotension, reflex, and cardiac.
Select appropriate diagnostic tests based on history and exam — and know which tests are NOT routinely useful.
Identify high-risk features that warrant inpatient evaluation.
Bonus: Initiate first- and second-line management and know when to consult.

prework.q: Q1. Define syncope. What separates it from the broader category of "transient loss of consciousness"?
prework.a: Syncope is an abrupt, transient loss of consciousness due to global cerebral hypoperfusion, with rapid spontaneous full recovery. TLOC is the umbrella (short duration, abnormal motor control, loss of responsiveness, amnesia for the event); syncope is the subset caused by cerebral hypoperfusion. A 6–8 second drop in cerebral blood flow is enough.

prework.q: Q2. Name the three great mimics of syncope and one feature that distinguishes each.
prework.a: Seizure — prolonged convulsions and marked post-ictal confusion (convulsive syncope has brief myoclonus and no post-ictal state); tongue biting and incontinence also point to seizure. Vertebrobasilar TIA/stroke — LOC accompanied by focal findings (vertigo, dysarthria); posterior events don't cause isolated LOC. Hypoglycemia — impaired rather than abruptly lost consciousness, longer duration, and no spontaneous recovery without intervention.

prework.q: Q3. What four things does EVERY syncope patient get, regardless of suspected cause?
prework.a: History, physical exam (cardiac focus), orthostatic vital signs, and a 12-lead ECG. Everything else is individualized.

segments.label: Hook (2 min)
segments.html: "65-year-old watching TV goes unresponsive for 20 seconds with whole-body jerking, then wakes up clear-headed." Ask: syncope or seizure — and what one historical detail decides it? (No post-ictal confusion. Convulsive syncope is a thing.) This is Case 3; you're planting it early.

segments.label: Objective 1 — Definition & mimics
segments.html:
ASK: "Define syncope" — then click to reveal. The definition is mechanistic: TLOC due to global cerebral hypoperfusion with spontaneous full recovery. ~60% of TLOC cases can be sorted by history and exam alone.

Mimics (click each mini-case): seizure (LOC + shaking + post-ictal state), vertebrobasilar TIA/CVA (LOC + vertigo/dysarthria — never isolated LOC), hypoglycemia (preceded by diaphoresis/tremulousness; recovery requires treating the glucose).

segments.label: Pathophysiology review — optional
segments.html: Build the cascade on the board; the rest of the talk reuses it: CPP = MAP − ICP; MAP = CO × SVR; CO = HR × SV; SV depends on preload, afterload, contractility. Then the baroreceptor reflex with standing: blood pools → ↓preload → ↓CO → ↓MAP → baroreceptors fire → ↑sympathetic tone → ↑CO and SVR → CPP preserved. Every syncope mechanism is a lesion somewhere in this loop.

segments.label: Objective 2 — Three classifications
segments.html:
ASK: "Standing up drops your preload every time you do it. Why don't you faint every time — and what are the ways the rescue system can fail?"

Orthostatic hypotension — an insufficient autonomic response. Defined as a drop in SBP ≥20 or DBP ≥10 with postural change (ESC also accepts SBP <90 with symptoms — not in the ACC/AHA definition). Four failure points:

Failure pointMechanismDifferential
Sympathetic tone↓CO, HR, SVR → ↓MAP"Neurogenic OH": primary autonomic failure, Parkinson's/MSA; secondary — diabetes, amyloid, paraneoplastic, cord injury
SVR response↓SVR → ↓MAPDrugs (vasodilators, antidepressants) — drug-induced OH is the MOST common cause; adrenal insufficiency
HR response↓HR → ↓CO → ↓MAPBeta-blockers, calcium channel blockers
Preload↓SV → ↓CO → ↓MAPVolume loss, hemorrhage, diuretics; worse with exercise, meals, prolonged bedrest

Reflex (neurally mediated) — an inappropriate autonomic response to a trigger. Causes: vasovagal (orthostatic or emotional), situational (micturition, defecation, cough, post-exercise), carotid sinus hypersensitivity. Two hemodynamic subtypes — cardioinhibitory (vagal surge → bradycardia) and vasodepressive (sympathetic withdrawal → ↓SVR) — and most real patients are mixed.

Cardiac — low cardiac output, the classification that kills. Four mechanisms: ↑afterload (aortic stenosis, HOCM), ↓contractility (MI, ventricular arrhythmias), ↓HR (AV block, sinus node dysfunction, pacemaker malfunction), ↓preload (PE, pulmonary HTN, tamponade, dissection).

Pearl — the PE question: Prandoni (NEJM 2016) found PE in 17% of patients hospitalized for first syncope, but a 2018 study of >1.5 million patients across 4 countries found PE in <1% of all-comers with syncope and <3% of those hospitalized. PE is a real but uncommon cause — don't CT every fainter.

segments.label: Objective 3 — Evaluation
segments.html:
ASK: "Three patients, three stories. Build the table with me: who faints, where, and with what warning?" — work the Who / Where-How / Prodrome grid for OH vs reflex vs cardiac before revealing.
OHReflexCardiac
WhoOlder, comorbid; autonomic neuropathy or parkinsonism; new/changed BP meds or diureticsAll ages, generally healthy; recurrent syncope often starting <40Structural heart disease or CAD; family history of sudden death at young age
Where / howWhile or just after standing; post-prandialProlonged standing, hot/crowded places; micturition/defecation/cough; emotional triggers; head turn, tight collar, shavingSupine, sitting, or at rest (arrhythmia); during exertion (structural)
ProdromeLightheadedness with postural changePallor, diaphoresis, nauseaNone ("unheralded"), or sudden palpitations; ± chest pain, dyspnea

Everyone gets: history, exam, orthostatic vitals, ECG.

ASK: "What testing is NOT routinely useful?" — head CT/MRI (unless head injury or new neuro findings), EEG (unless the story suggests seizure), carotid imaging (bilateral disease would be needed to cause syncope; essentially never occurs without other neuro symptoms).

Targeted tests by suspected type: OH — BMP/CBC (volume, occult bleed), ± autonomic evaluation (Valsalva without HR/BP response suggests autonomic failure; refer). Reflex — usually a history diagnosis; tilt-table if uncertain (a cardioinhibitory response predicts asystolic spontaneous syncope — treatment implications); carotid sinus massage in >40s with a suggestive story (contraindicated with bruit, known stenosis >70%, or TIA/stroke within 3 months). Cardiac — telemetry, TTE, ± troponin/BNP, stress test if exertional, then ambulatory monitoring matched to event frequency:

MonitorDurationBest for
Holter24–72 hSymptoms likely to recur within days
Event (loop) monitor2–6 weeksModerate frequency + prodrome (patient-activated)
Patch monitor~2 weeksModerate frequency; comfortable, single lead
Implantable loop recorderYearsInfrequent, unexplained syncope

segments.label: Objective 4 — Disposition
segments.html:
ASK: "Which fainter goes home and which gets a bed?" — collect candidate features, then reveal the high-risk list.
Low risk → homeHigh risk → admit
PMHLong history of recurrent low-risk syncope; no structural heart diseaseSevere structural heart disease or CAD; family history of sudden cardiac death
EventTypical reflex prodrome; clear trigger; after prolonged standing or hot/crowded placesNew chest pain or dyspnea; exertional or supine onset; sudden palpitations; short/absent prodrome (<10 s) with structural disease
ExamNormalSBP <90; HR <40 awake; signs of GI bleed; undiagnosed systolic murmur
ECGNormalAcute ischemia; Mobitz II or 3rd-degree AV block; HR <40; pauses >3 s awake; BBB, Q waves, hypertrophy; VT (sustained or not); pre-excitation; device malfunction; QTc <340 or >460 ms; Brugada pattern; ARVC

Neither clearly high nor low risk → observe in the ED or hospital.

segments.label: Objective 5 (bonus) — Management
segments.html:
OH: fluids, stop/adjust culprit meds, behavioral measures (slow position changes, salt, compression garments, counter-pressure maneuvers); midodrine or fludrocortisone for autonomic dysfunction. Reflex: reassurance and trigger avoidance; counter-pressure maneuvers if there's a usable prodrome; consider midodrine/fludrocortisone/salt; pacemaker in selected patients >40 with documented cardioinhibitory syncope that is frequent and unpredictable. Cardiac: consult cardiology — treatment is etiology-specific.

2026 context: Since this talk was written, the case for pacing in cardioinhibitory reflex syncope has strengthened — the 2021 ESC pacing guideline upgraded dual-chamber pacing (closed-loop stimulation) for patients >40 with severe, recurrent, unpredictable syncope and documented asystolic pauses, on the strength of the BIOSync RCT (77% relative risk reduction at 2 years). Cardioneuroablation is an emerging device-free alternative still under study. The teaching point stands: these patients go to cardiology/EP, not to empiric therapy.

segments.label: Close (2 min)
segments.html: Hide the board. Ask the group to rebuild: the definition, the three classifications, the four universal tests, and three high-risk features — then show the take-home slide.

cases.title: Case 1 — The healthy fainter
cases.stem: 21-year-old presents after syncope. VS: 36.5°C, HR 82, BP 112/87. Exam: no murmurs, otherwise normal.
cases.reveal: Who: history of depression on escitalopram. Where/how: standing in line at the grocery store. Prodrome: clamminess, tunnel vision.

cases.title: Case 2 — The dry fainter
cases.stem: 56-year-old with HTN and chronic venous stasis presents with syncope. VS: HR 112, BP 112/87. Exam: no murmurs, dry mucous membranes, poor skin turgor.
cases.reveal: Who: HTN on lisinopril; recent GI illness. Where/how: occurred on standing up. Prodrome: lightheadedness and dizziness.

cases.title: Case 3 — The trap (convulsions ≠ seizure)
cases.stem: 65-year-old with CAD s/p PCI, HFrEF (ischemic), HTN, T2DM presents with LOC + convulsions while watching TV; unresponsive ~20 s with witnessed whole-body jerks, then immediately lucid — no confusion. VS: HR 72, BP 112/87. Exam: occasional irregular beats, no murmurs, euvolemic.
cases.reveal: Who: significant cardiac history on furosemide, carvedilol, sacubitril-valsartan, spironolactone, empagliflozin, aspirin, metformin; 2 weeks of episodic palpitations and lightheadedness at rest. Where/how: at rest, supine/sitting. Prodrome: none. At-rest onset + no prodrome + cardiac history = high risk.

mcqs.stem: A 24-year-old woman loses consciousness for 30 seconds while having blood drawn. Witnesses describe pallor, then a few seconds of limb jerking. She is immediately oriented on waking. Exam and ECG are normal. What is the most appropriate next step?
opts: Reassurance and counseling on counter-pressure maneuvers
mcqs.exp: C. Classic emotional vasovagal syncope with a textbook trigger and prodrome. Brief myoclonic jerks occur in convulsive syncope and do not indicate seizure when there's no post-ictal confusion (A). Imaging (B) has no role without head injury or focal findings. Monitoring (D) is for suspected arrhythmic syncope — nothing here suggests it. Reflex syncope is a history diagnosis; treatment starts with reassurance and counter-pressure maneuvers.

mcqs.stem: A 78-year-old man with Parkinson's disease faints two minutes after standing from dinner. Supine BP 142/84, HR 76; standing BP 108/70, HR 79. Which feature most specifically points to a neurogenic cause of his orthostatic hypotension?
opts: The near-absent compensatory heart rate increase
mcqs.exp: B. The BP drop (A) defines OH (SBP ≥20 or DBP ≥10) but not its cause. In volume depletion or drug-induced OH, an intact autonomic system mounts a compensatory tachycardia; a blunted HR response despite significant hypotension indicates the sympathetic limb itself is failing — neurogenic OH (here, Parkinson's-related autonomic failure). Age (C) and post-prandial venous pooling (D) are nonspecific contributors. Valsalva without HR/BP response supports the same conclusion.

mcqs.stem: A 71-year-old man passes out while climbing stairs, without warning. He has a harsh late-peaking systolic murmur radiating to the carotids, not previously documented. ECG shows LVH. What is the most appropriate management?
opts: Discharge with an event monitor and outpatient TTE
Admit; obtain TTE before any exertion or vasodilators
Carotid sinus massage to evaluate for carotid hypersensitivity
mcqs.exp: B. Exertional syncope + undiagnosed systolic murmur + LVH = presumed severe aortic stenosis until proven otherwise — two independent high-risk features mandating admission. Outpatient workup (A) risks sudden death. Tilt-table (C) evaluates reflex syncope. Carotid sinus massage (D) is for suspected carotid hypersensitivity in the right story and would add nothing; vasodilators and volume shifts are dangerous in critical AS.

mcqs.stem: A 58-year-old woman is admitted after syncope. Workup reveals a normal neuro exam and no head trauma. The admitting resident orders head CT, carotid Dopplers, and EEG "to be complete." Which statement is correct?
opts: All three are appropriate in unexplained syncope
Head CT is indicated; the others are not
None is routinely indicated without neurologic findings, head injury, or a seizure-suggestive history
Carotid Doppler is the highest-yield test for syncope
mcqs.exp: C. Routine neuroimaging, EEG, and carotid imaging are explicitly low-utility in syncope (ACC/AHA 2017). Syncope requires global hypoperfusion — unilateral carotid disease doesn't produce it, and bilateral critical disease essentially never presents as isolated syncope (D). EEG needs a seizure-suggestive story; CT needs trauma or focal findings. These three tests are among the most common sources of low-value spending in syncope evaluation.

mcqs.stem: A 66-year-old man has had three unexplained syncopal episodes over 18 months, each without prodrome. Holter ×48 h and a 2-week patch were unrevealing; TTE and ECG are normal. What is the most appropriate next step?
opts: Tilt-table testing to rule in reflex syncope
mcqs.exp: B. Match the monitor to event frequency: episodes months apart will never be caught on short-duration devices (A) — an implantable loop recorder monitors for years and has the highest diagnostic yield in infrequent, unexplained syncope. Pacing (C) requires a documented bradyarrhythmic correlate. Tilt-table (D) may be considered, but unheralded syncope in an older man is suspicious for arrhythmia, and capturing the rhythm during a real event is the priority.

anki: Definition of syncope?
Abrupt transient LOC from global cerebral hypoperfusion, with rapid spontaneous full recovery. 6–8 s of interrupted cerebral flow suffices.
Three great mimics of syncope and the distinguishing feature of each?

Seizure (prolonged convulsions, post-ictal confusion, tongue biting/incontinence); vertebrobasilar TIA/CVA (LOC only WITH focal signs like vertigo/dysarthria); hypoglycemia (impaired rather than lost consciousness; no recovery without treatment).
Can syncope cause convulsions?
Yes — brief myoclonic jerks ("convulsive syncope"). Distinguish from seizure by the absence of a post-ictal state.
The perfusion cascade used to classify all syncope?
CPP = MAP − ICP; MAP = CO × SVR; CO = HR × SV; SV ← preload, afterload, contractility.
Diagnostic criteria for orthostatic hypotension?
Postural drop in SBP ≥20 or DBP ≥10; ESC also accepts SBP <90 with symptoms (not in ACC/AHA).
Most common cause of orthostatic hypotension?
Drug-induced (vasodilators, antidepressants, diuretics, AV-nodal blockers).
OH vs reflex syncope — the autonomic response in one word each?
OH: insufficient. Reflex: inappropriate (to a physiologic trigger).
Two hemodynamic subtypes of reflex syncope?
Cardioinhibitory (vagal surge → bradycardia) and vasodepressive (sympathetic withdrawal → ↓SVR); mixed is common.
Four mechanisms of cardiac syncope with one example each?
↑Afterload (aortic stenosis, HOCM); ↓contractility (MI, VT); ↓HR (AV block, sinus node dysfunction); ↓preload (PE, tamponade, pulmonary HTN, dissection).
How common is PE among patients with syncope?
<1% of all-comers, <3% of those hospitalized (2018 multinational study) — the 17% figure (Prandoni 2016) applied to a selected hospitalized cohort. Uncommon cause overall.
The four things every syncope patient gets?
History, physical exam, orthostatic vital signs, 12-lead ECG.
Three tests NOT routinely useful in syncope?
Head CT/MRI (without trauma/focal findings), EEG (without seizure-suggestive history), carotid imaging.
Historical profile of cardiac syncope?
Structural heart disease/CAD or family history of sudden death; occurs supine/at rest (arrhythmia) or with exertion (structural); no prodrome or sudden palpitations.
High-risk exam and ECG features mandating admission (name 5)?
SBP <90; awake HR <40; undiagnosed systolic murmur; GI bleed signs; ECG — acute ischemia, Mobitz II/3rd-degree block, pauses >3 s, VT, pre-excitation, QTc <340 or >460 ms, Brugada, BBB/Q waves.
Cardiac monitor choice by syncope frequency?
Daily–weekly: Holter 24–72 h. Weekly–monthly with prodrome: event/loop monitor 2–6 wk or 2-wk patch. Infrequent/unexplained: implantable loop recorder (years).
First-line treatment of vasovagal syncope, and who gets a pacemaker?

Reassurance, trigger avoidance, counter-pressure maneuvers (± midodrine/fludrocortisone/salt). Pacing: selected patients >40 with documented cardioinhibitory (asystolic) syncope, frequent and unpredictable — strengthened by BIOSync/ESC 2021.
lessons.dot:
#Syncope: transient LOC. witnessed/unwitnessed, w/wo prodrome, exertional/at-rest/postural, w/wo injury. likely 2/2 * [reflex/vasovagal, orthostatic, cardiac (arrhythmic/structural), neuro-mimic, med/vol]. r/o serious cause [arrhythmia, ACS, AS/HOCM, PE, GIB, AAA, SAH]; excl mimics [szr, hypoglycemia, intox, TIA, pseudosyncope]. CSRS = * [vlow ≤−2 / low −1–0 / med 1–3 / high 4–5 / vhigh ≥6]. HDS/HDUS.
- core w/u: H&P [prodrome, trigger, EXERTIONAL, palpitations pre-event, FHx SCD/<50yo, structural HD, QT/AVN/diuretic/antiHTN meds, lat tongue bite/post-ictal] + 12-lead ECG [ischemia, AVB, BBB, QTc, Brugada, WPW, epsilon/ARVC, LVH] + orthostatics [SBP ↓≥20 / DBP ↓≥10 w/in 3min]
- CSRS pts: vasovagal predispo −1 / hx heart dz (CAD/AF/CHF/valv) +1 / ED SBP <90 or >180 +2 / trop >99%ile +2 / abnl QRS axis (<−30 or >100) +1 / QRS >130ms +1 / QTc >480ms +2 / ED dx vasovagal −2 / cardiac +2 → 30d SAE: vlow 0.4% / low 0.7% / med ~8% / high ~20% / vhigh ~51% (low → d/c w/ RTC; high/vhigh → tele/admit)
- targeted only: hs-trop + ECG if cardiac susp; TTE only if structural HD susp; inpt tele if arrhythmic; outpt monitor [patch/ELR/ILR] if recurrent unexplained; stress if exertional + CAD risk; CTPA only if PE susp; tilt select
- AVOID in simple syncope (nl neuro, no trauma, no szr features): head CT/MRI, EEG, carotid US (yield ~0.2–1.5%); routine TTE/CTPA/broad labs
- red flags → admit: exertional/supine, palpitations pre-event, FHx SCD, structural HD/↓EF, abnl ECG, recurrent, injury w/o prodrome, anemia/GIB, new murmur
- monitoring: tele, orthostatics q*h, hold QT-prolong/AVN/antiHTN, fall precautions
- labs: CBC, CMP, hs-trop, +/- BNP, +/- BHCG, EKG, +/- TTE, +/- ambulatory monitor [per CSRS + red flags]
takehome: Syncope is defined by its mechanism — global cerebral hypoperfusion — which is what separates it from seizure, hypoglycemia, and TIA.
Three classifications: orthostatic (insufficient autonomic response), reflex (inappropriate response to a trigger), cardiac (low output — the dangerous one).
Who + where/how + prodrome + the med list sorts most patients; ~60% of TLOC is diagnosable on history and exam alone.
Everyone gets history, exam, orthostatic vitals, and an ECG; further testing is individualized, and high-risk features buy a hospital bed.

lessons.title: Thoracentesis
lessons.subtitle: Optimized procedure package · Facilitator-led simulation-based mastery learning for IM residents · Pre-work ~20 min + simulation 60–90 min
lessons.credit: Adapted from TeachIM.org — Thoracentesis (Fainstad, Kramer, Cool, Ortiz-Lopez, Northcut, Cunningham, Zheng, Neumeier, Ma; sec. ed. Cool; Aug 2022, guide rev. 2025), CC BY-NC 4.0. Optimized June 2026: guideline currency verified, teaching design upgraded, assessments and clinical tools added. Procedure-type lesson — TeachIM publishes no slide deck for procedures; the package is built around the original instructional guide, clinical assessment tool, and quiz (all included below).
objectives: Describe the appropriate indications and contraindications to performing a thoracentesis.
Achieve a minimum passing score (MPS) on the validated clinical assessment checklist in a simulation setting.
Perform a thoracentesis under full or partial supervision in the clinical setting until demonstrating the Unsupervised Practice Standard (UPS).

prework.q: Q1. What is the minimum safe fluid depth on ultrasound, and where should you insert relative to the rib, spine, and diaphragm?
prework.a: ≥2 cm of fluid (parietal-to-visceral pleura) in all dimensions. Insert over the upper margin of the rib (the neurovascular bundle runs under the rib's lower margin), as laterally as possible (>9–10 cm from the spine, where intercostal vessels are least tortuous), and at least one interspace above the diaphragm. Use the low-frequency transducer to map the pocket and the high-frequency linear probe with color Doppler to screen for an aberrant subcostal vessel.

prework.q: Q2. Which is an absolute contraindication, and which findings are "consider increased risk" cautions?
prework.a: Absolute: overlying skin infection (and <2 cm fluid depth, or a multiloculated effusion needing a chest tube — diagnostic tap may still be indicated). Cautions (not absolute): full anticoagulation / INR >2 / platelets <50K (bleeding); mechanical ventilation (pneumothorax); signs of lung entrapment (re-expansion pulmonary edema). Notably, abnormal INR or platelet count alone has not been associated with more bleeding after ultrasound-guided thoracentesis (Patel, AJR 2011).

prework.q: Q3. When do you stop draining fluid?
prework.a: Stop for chest pain, dyspnea, or severe coughing; for excessive resistance / pleural pressure more negative than about −20 cm H₂O (manually or by manometry); or when flow stops. Drain to symptoms and pleural pressure, not to a fixed volume (see the 2026 context pearl).

segments.label: Hook & framing (2 min)
segments.html: "A 65-year-old with a large left effusion and worsening dyspnea needs a tap tonight. Your intern asks: how much fluid is safe to take off — a liter? a liter and a half? until it's dry?" Hold the answer; you'll return to it. Establish that the highest-yield safety levers are ultrasound site selection and knowing when to stop, not needle dexterity.

segments.label: LEARN — Indications & contraindications
segments.html:
ASK: "Diagnostic vs therapeutic — sort these: undiagnosed effusion, large effusion with hypoxemia, an effusion at risk of becoming a complicated parapneumonic, an asymptomatic reaccumulated malignant effusion."
DiagnosticTherapeutic
Pleural effusion of unknown etiologyLarge effusion with dyspnea/hypoxemia; effusion at risk of complications if not drained (e.g., parapneumonic)
ASK: "Which contraindication is absolute, and which are 'increased-risk' cautions you weigh against urgency?"
Absolute / hard stopsIncreased-risk cautions (weigh & mitigate)
Overlying skin infection · <2 cm fluid in any dimension · multiloculated effusion needing a chest tube (diagnostic tap may still be reasonable)Full anticoagulation, INR >2, platelets <50K (bleeding) · mechanical ventilation (pneumothorax) · suspected lung entrapment (re-expansion edema)
Pearl — coags rarely block the tap: Ultrasound-guided thoracentesis is low-risk for bleeding, and abnormal INR/platelets alone have not been linked to more bleeding complications (Patel, AJR 2011; Ault, Thorax 2015). Correct only when clearly excessive, and never delay a needed diagnostic tap in sepsis to chase a mildly abnormal INR.

segments.label: LEARN — Positioning & ultrasound site selection
segments.html:
Position for the largest accessible pocket: usually upright leaning forward over a table with a pillow; lateral decubitus or semi-recumbent if the patient can't sit up.

ASK: "Walk me through the scan. Which probe, what depth, and what are the four landmarks you must identify before you mark?"
  • Low-frequency curvilinear/phased-array probe, indicator cranial, depth >8 cm: scan mid-scapular to mid-axillary. Identify chest wall, parietal pleura, lung, diaphragm, and liver/spleen.
  • Choose the largest hypoechoic pocket ≥2 cm deep, >9–10 cm lateral to the spine (intercostal vessels are most tortuous medially), and ≥1 interspace above the diaphragm.
  • High-frequency linear probe + color/power Doppler: confirm a supracostal approach (insert over the upper margin of the rib) and screen for an aberrant subcostal vessel; measure skin-to-parietal-pleura depth.
  • Mark the site over the upper margin of the rib with a pen or skin indentation.
Safety anchor: The neurovascular bundle runs along the lower margin of each rib — always go over the top of the rib below the fluid. Don't mark and walk away — mark and proceed; patient position shifts move the pocket.

segments.label: SEE — Sterile prep, anesthesia & catheter insertion
segments.html:
1. Time-out with the nurse (name, DOB, procedure, site, allergies) before opening sterile supplies; include the patient.
  2. Sterile prep: chlorhexidine scrub with friction ×30 s over an area larger than the drape aperture (chlorhexidine is scrubbed, unlike iodine which is applied and left to dry); apply the fenestrated drape.
  3. Anesthetize: dermal wheal with a 25 g needle, then advance a longer 21–22 g needle under continuous negative pressure, anesthetizing the tract until pleural fluid is aspirated; withdraw slightly and deposit ~2 cc just superficial to the pleura.
  4. Nick the skin with an #11 scalpel to ~½ the blade width.
  5. Insert catheter-over-needle: advance firmly through the nick just above the rib (if you hit rib, lift over it — don't pivot), applying negative pressure with the syringe hand and guiding with the other. When fluid returns, advance another 0.5–1 cm so the catheter (not just the needle) is in the pleural space, then anchor the needle hand and advance the catheter to the hub; remove the needle.
Pearl — catheter, not needle, in the space: Pneumothorax most often happens when the operator drains through a needle still in the chest. Once fluid returns, advance the soft catheter and pull the needle — and select the largest pocket you can, since a bigger fluid window reduces pneumothorax risk.

segments.label: SEE — Drainage, sampling & closure
segments.html:
• Drainage: connect IV tubing with two one-way valves; 60 cc syringe to the short arm, collection bag to the long arm. Pull to fill, push to empty; if no one-way valves, toggle the stopcock between pull (open to port) and push (closed). Stop for excess resistance / pressure < −20 cm H₂O, or for chest pain, dyspnea, or severe coughing.
  • Sampling (per clinical concern): all → cell count + differential, LDH, protein (≥10 cc); microbiology → Gram stain + reflex culture (≥20 cc); cytology → ≥30–60 cc; chylothorax → cholesterol + triglycerides; infection → consider pH (on ice, in a blood-gas syringe). Apply Light's criteria with paired serum LDH/protein.
  • Catheter extraction: turn the stopcock toward the patient, briskly remove the catheter, hold gauze pressure >1 min, then dress. If any concern for pneumothorax, rule it out with bedside lung ultrasound (anterior chest, supine) or CXR.
ASK: "pH 7.15, glucose 10, and Gram-positive cocci in the fluid of a parapneumonic effusion — antibiotics alone or something more?"

That's a complicated parapneumonic effusion / early empyema (pH <7.2, low glucose, positive Gram stain) — it needs drainage with a chest tube, not antibiotics alone. This is the single most important "fluid result changes management" teaching point.

segments.label: PRACTICE → PROVE — Mastery learning
segments.html:
The learner rehearses each step, then signals readiness for a graded run. Quiz the non-technical steps with the checklist out of view, then run the full simulation with it visible, marking each mistake or required prompt. Repeat until the learner reaches the minimum passing standard.

ComponentMPS (graduate simulation)UPS (unsupervised clinical practice)
Error-counting checklist (Angoff-set)<24 mistakes<7 mistakes
Global Skills Assessment (1–5)>2 (beyond verbal-guided)≥ "proficient"
Entrustment score>2"indirect supervision" or better

segments.label: Close (2 min)
segments.html: Hide the guide. Ask the learner to rebuild: the minimum safe fluid depth, the three rules of site selection (above the rib, lateral to the spine, above the diaphragm), three reasons to stop draining, and which pleural-fluid pattern mandates a chest tube.

cases.title: Case 1 — Effusion after lung-cancer surgery
cases.stem: 60-year-old with stage I NSCLC s/p local excision + mediastinal node dissection 1 month ago presents with dyspnea and cough. POCUS shows a moderate right effusion. Afebrile, HR 90, RR 22, SpO₂ 90% RA. A diagnostic/therapeutic thoracentesis is planned.
cases.reveal: Cell count + differential, Gram stain + culture, LDH, total protein — plus triglycerides and cytology. The cancer history raises concern for malignant effusion (cytology), and mediastinal node dissection can injure the thoracic duct → chylothorax (triglycerides/cholesterol). Don't drain "to relieve symptoms" without sending diagnostics — the result changes management.

cases.title: Case 2 — Persistent fever despite antibiotics
cases.stem: 50-year-old admitted with sepsis from suspected CAP. After 2 days of broad-spectrum IV antibiotics the fever persists; repeat CXR shows a moderate effusion. Ultrasound-guided thoracentesis yields clear yellow fluid: total protein 6, LDH 1100, pH 7.15, glucose 10, Gram stain GPCs in pairs/chains.
cases.reveal: Continue antibiotics and place a chest tube for drainage. pH <7.2, low glucose, and a positive Gram stain define a complicated parapneumonic effusion / empyema, which will not resolve on antibiotics alone — it requires source control with tube drainage (BTS 2023). Monitoring, repeat imaging, or chasing other fever sources all delay definitive therapy.

mcqs.stem: While selecting a thoracentesis site you want to avoid the intercostal neurovascular bundle and minimize bleeding. Which approach is best?
opts: Insert just below the rib at a medial (paraspinal) site
Insert over the upper margin of the rib, as lateral as safely possible, after Doppler screening
Choose the smallest accessible fluid pocket to limit lung injury
Transfuse platelets to >100K before any tap
mcqs.exp: B. The neurovascular bundle runs along the lower rib margin, so insert over the top of the rib; intercostal vessels are most tortuous medially, so go lateral (>9–10 cm from spine); Doppler screens for an aberrant subcostal vessel (A is exactly backwards). A small pocket (C) raises pneumothorax risk. Routine platelet transfusion (D) isn't required — abnormal coags alone don't increase bleeding after US-guided taps (Patel 2011).

mcqs.stem: What is the minimum safe fluid depth (parietal-to-visceral pleura) for a thoracentesis?
mcqs.exp: B. A pocket ≥2 cm in all dimensions is the standard minimum safe depth; less than that markedly raises the risk of hitting lung. Larger pockets are safer but 2 cm is the accepted threshold.

mcqs.stem: A patient becomes dyspneic and coughs forcefully after ~1.2 L is removed during a therapeutic tap; you also feel increasing resistance on aspiration. What is the most appropriate action?
opts: Switch to wall suction to finish quickly
Continue to a target of exactly 1.5 L
Push the fluid back to relieve symptoms
mcqs.exp: B. Stop for chest pain, dyspnea, severe coughing, or excessive resistance / pleural pressure < −20 cm H₂O — these signal lung non-expansion and risk of re-expansion edema. Drain to symptoms and pressure, not to a fixed volume; the traditional 1.5 L "limit" (C) is arbitrary and not evidence-based. Wall suction (A) does not make continued drainage safe here.

mcqs.stem: Which pleural-fluid pattern from a parapneumonic effusion most clearly mandates chest-tube drainage rather than antibiotics alone?
opts: pH 7.40, glucose 90, LDH 150, negative Gram stain
mcqs.exp: B. pH <7.2, low glucose, and/or a positive Gram stain or frank pus define a complicated parapneumonic effusion/empyema needing drainage (BTS 2023). Option A is an uncomplicated parapneumonic effusion (antibiotics, observe). A lymphocytic exudate with low ADA (C) points elsewhere (e.g., malignancy), and a transudate (D) is managed by treating the underlying cause.

mcqs.stem: A 72-year-old on apixaban with an undiagnosed unilateral exudative effusion needs a diagnostic thoracentesis to evaluate for malignancy. INR 1.3, platelets 120K. What is the most appropriate approach?
opts: Proceed with ultrasound guidance; current evidence does not show increased bleeding from these parameters
Refuse until apixaban is reversed with andexanet alfa
Cancel — anticoagulation is an absolute contraindication
mcqs.exp: A. US-guided thoracentesis is low-risk; INR 1.3 and platelets 120K are well within safe ranges, and abnormal coags alone haven't increased bleeding in large series (Patel 2011; Ault 2015). For a non-urgent elective tap, holding a DOAC dose per local protocol is reasonable, but reversal (B), unnecessary transfusion (C), and cancellation (D) are not — anticoagulation is a relative caution, not an absolute contraindication.

anki: Indications for therapeutic thoracentesis?
Large effusion with dyspnea/hypoxemia; effusion at risk of complications if not drained (e.g., parapneumonic). Diagnostic: effusion of unknown etiology.
Absolute / hard-stop contraindications to thoracentesis?
Overlying skin infection; <2 cm fluid depth; multiloculated effusion needing a chest tube (a diagnostic tap may still be reasonable).
"Increased-risk" cautions (not absolute) for thoracentesis?
Full anticoagulation / INR >2 / platelets <50K (bleeding); mechanical ventilation (pneumothorax); suspected lung entrapment (re-expansion edema).
Do abnormal INR/platelets increase bleeding after US-guided thoracentesis?
No — abnormal INR or platelet count alone has not been associated with increased bleeding (Patel AJR 2011; Ault Thorax 2015). Don't delay a needed tap for mild coagulopathy.
Minimum safe fluid depth for thoracentesis?
≥2 cm (parietal-to-visceral pleura) in all dimensions.
Three rules of thoracentesis site selection?
Over the UPPER margin of the rib (NV bundle runs under the rib); as lateral as possible (>9–10 cm from spine, vessels most tortuous medially); ≥1 interspace above the diaphragm.
Which ultrasound transducers are used and for what?

Low-frequency curvilinear/phased-array (depth >8 cm) to map the pocket and landmarks; high-frequency linear with color Doppler to screen for an aberrant subcostal vessel and measure skin-to-pleura depth.
Why advance the catheter (not just the needle) into the pleural space?
Draining through a rigid needle risks lacerating lung → pneumothorax. After fluid returns, advance 0.5–1 cm more, then thread the soft catheter to the hub and remove the needle.
Chlorhexidine vs iodine prep technique?
Chlorhexidine: scrub with friction ×30 s. Iodine: apply and let dry. (Thoracentesis uses chlorhexidine.)
When do you stop draining pleural fluid?
Chest pain, dyspnea, or severe coughing; excessive resistance or pleural pressure < −20 cm H₂O; or when flow stops. Drain to symptoms/pressure, not a fixed volume.
Is the 1.5 L drainage limit evidence-based?
No — the traditional <1.5 L cap is arbitrary. Large volumes can be removed safely if guided by symptoms/pleural pressure; RPE risk is <1% (manometry doesn't clearly reduce RPE — Lentz 2019).
Fluid volumes for studies?
Chemistries (cell count, LDH, protein) ≥10 cc; microbiology (Gram stain + culture) ≥20 cc; cytology +30–60 cc. pH on ice in a blood-gas syringe.
Pleural-fluid pattern that mandates a chest tube in a parapneumonic effusion?
pH <7.2, low glucose, positive Gram stain/culture, or frank pus = complicated parapneumonic effusion/empyema → tube drainage, not antibiotics alone (BTS 2023).
Workup for suspected chylothorax?
Pleural triglycerides and cholesterol (e.g., after mediastinal/thoracic-duct surgery).
How is pneumothorax risk reduced during thoracentesis?
Select the largest fluid pocket; keep the catheter (not needle) in the space; pre-procedure/real-time ultrasound. Rule out PTX afterward with lung US (anterior chest, supine) or CXR if concern.
Light's criteria — exudate if any one is met?
Pleural/serum protein >0.5; pleural/serum LDH >0.6; pleural LDH >⅔ the upper limit of normal serum LDH. Otherwise transudate.
MPS vs UPS thresholds on the thoracentesis CAT?
MPS (graduate sim): <24 mistakes, Global >2, Entrustment >2 (Angoff-set). UPS (unsupervised practice): <7 mistakes, "proficient," "indirect supervision."
lessons.dot:
PROCEDURE: Thoracentesis (*diagnostic / *therapeutic), *R/L
Indication: pleural effusion * [unknown etiology / dyspnea-hypoxemia / parapneumonic-at-risk]
Pre-proc checklist:
 - contraindic reviewed: NO overlying skin infection; fluid depth *≥2 cm; not isolated multiloculated requiring chest tube. Cautions weighed: anticoag/INR *<2/plt *>50K, not on mech vent, no entrapment signs.
 - consent obtained from *[pt/surrogate]; risks (pneumothorax, bleeding, infection, re-expansion edema, failure) & alternatives discussed
 - TIME-OUT w/ RN: name/DOB/procedure/SIDE/site/allergies confirmed
US (low-freq curvilinear, depth >8 cm): largest pocket *≥2 cm, *>9-10 cm lateral to spine, *≥1 interspace above diaphragm; landmarks (chest wall, parietal pleura, lung, diaphragm, *liver/spleen) identified; skin-to-pleura *__ cm; high-freq linear + color Doppler — *no aberrant subcostal vessel. Site marked over UPPER margin of rib.
Technique: position *[upright leaning fwd / lateral decub / semi-recumbent]. Chlorhexidine scrub x30 s, fenestrated drape. 1% lidocaine *5-10 mL (dermal wheal → tract → ~2 cc superficial to pleura). #11 scalpel skin nick. Catheter-over-needle advanced OVER the rib w/ negative pressure; on fluid return advanced 0.5-1 cm more, catheter threaded to hub, needle removed. SAFETY: go over top of rib; keep CATHETER (not needle) in space.
Drainage: IV tubing w/ two one-way valves; * mL *[clear/yellow/serosanguinous/bloody/purulent] removed. STOPPED for *[symptom resolution / cough/CP/SOB / resistance / pressure < -20 cmH2O] — drained to symptoms/pressure, not fixed volume.
Studies sent: *[cell count+diff, LDH, protein (≥10 cc); Gram stain+cx (≥20 cc); cytology (≥30-60 cc); triglycerides/cholesterol; pH on ice]; Light's criteria applied w/ paired serum.
Closure: stopcock to patient, catheter removed briskly, pressure >1 min, dressing applied.
Post: pt tolerated well, *EBL minimal, no immediate complication. Pneumothorax *[ruled out by lung US / CXR / not clinically indicated].
Performing provider: *  | Supervising: *
takehome: Ultrasound site selection is the core safety skill: ≥2 cm pocket, over the upper rib margin, >9–10 cm lateral to the spine, above the diaphragm; Doppler to dodge an aberrant subcostal vessel.

The only true hard stops are overlying skin infection, <2 cm fluid, and an isolated multiloculated effusion; anticoagulation/INR/platelets are cautions, not absolutes — abnormal coags alone don't increase bleeding.
Get the catheter, not just the needle, into the pleural space to avoid pneumothorax.
Drain to symptoms and pleural pressure (stop at CP/SOB/cough, resistance, or < −20 cm H₂O) — not to an arbitrary 1.5 L limit.
Send fluid for the studies your differential demands; a pH <7.2 / low glucose / positive Gram stain parapneumonic effusion needs a chest tube, not antibiotics alone.
Prove competence by mastery learning: reach the MPS (<24 mistakes) in simulation, then the UPS (<7) under graded observation before unsupervised practice.

lessons.title: Transplant Medicine
objectives: Describe the constant balance of weighing the value of immunosuppression against the risks of rejection, infection, malignancy, and drug toxicity.
Identify the common induction and maintenance immunosuppression regimens and how they change over time after solid organ transplant.
Build a framework for the common post-transplant complications organized by time from transplant.

prework.q: 1. Which solid organ transplant is most likely to reject, and which is least?
prework.a: Lung is most likely (~50% experience rejection within 5 years); liver is least likely. Rejection risk (allogenicity) drives how much maintenance immunosuppression a patient needs long-term: lung > heart/kidney > liver.

prework.q: 2. What are the three core classes of maintenance immunosuppression?
prework.a: Calcineurin inhibitors (tacrolimus, cyclosporine), antimetabolites (mycophenolate, azathioprine), and steroids (prednisone). mTOR inhibitors (sirolimus, everolimus) are an additional class; belatacept is a costimulation-blocker alternative.

prework.q: 3. A transplant patient develops a reactivation infection such as CMV. How many months out from transplant is this most likely?
prework.a: 1–6 months. Nosocomial/donor-derived infections dominate <1 month; reactivation and opportunistic infections (CMV, BK, PJP) cluster at 1–6 months; community-acquired infections dominate >6 months.

segments.label: Hook (2 min)
segments.html: "Your patient is 4 months out from a kidney transplant and now has a fever and rising creatinine. Before you order a single test — what are the two competing diagnoses you're most worried about, and why does the timing matter?" (Answer surfaces by the end: infection vs. rejection, and 1–6 months is the reactivation/opportunistic window when both are plausible.)

segments.label: Objective 1 — The Balance & Allogenicity (10 min)
segments.html:
ASK: "What is the price we pay for suppressing rejection?" Reveal the oversuppression (infection, cancer) and undersuppression (rejection) buttons.

Frame all of transplant immunology as a single dial: turn it up and rejection falls but infection, malignancy, and drug toxicity rise; turn it down and the reverse happens.

ASK: "Which transplant is most likely to reject? Least likely?" Then reveal the allogenicity order.

Allogenicity (rejection risk): lung > heart/kidney > liver. Lung transplants reject ~50% within 5 years and stay on all three drug classes for life; liver may be maintained on a calcineurin inhibitor alone.

Pearl: Allogenicity is highest immediately after transplant and falls over time. This single fact explains both the drug timeline (Objective 2) and the complication timeline (Objective 3).

segments.label: Objective 2 — Induction vs. Maintenance (12 min)
segments.html:
ASK: "Why would a regimen on day 1 look completely different from the regimen at year 1?" Reveal the induction and maintenance buttons.
PhaseTimingAgentsRationale
InductionAt transplantAnti-lymphocyte antibody (rabbit ATG, alemtuzumab) or anti–IL-2 receptor antibody (basiliximab) + high-dose steroidsAllogenicity is at its peak; profound, brief suppression to get the graft through the highest-risk window
MaintenanceLong-termCNI + antimetabolite ± prednisoneLower-intensity therapy, de-escalated as rejection risk falls

Maintenance classes (know an example of each): CNI = tacrolimus, cyclosporine; antimetabolite = mycophenolate, azathioprine; steroid = prednisone. Additional: mTOR inhibitors (sirolimus, everolimus).

Pearl (newer context): Belatacept (CD28 costimulation blocker, FDA 2011) is a CNI-sparing maintenance option with better long-term GFR and fewer donor-specific antibodies — but is contraindicated in EBV-seronegative recipients (PTLD/PML risk). OKT3/muromonab was discontinued in 2010; the current depleting induction agents are rabbit ATG and alemtuzumab.

segments.label: Objective 3 — Complications by Time from Transplant (15 min)
segments.html:
ASK: "Name the five buckets of post-transplant complications." Reveal each on the board.

Categories: rejection, medication effects, infection, malignancy, surgical. The most teachable axis is time.

TimeInfectionRejection / Malignancy / Surgical
<1 monthNosocomial (surgical site, line, catheter, C. diff); donor- or recipient-derivedSurgical complications (leaks, kinks, fistulas, stenoses); hyperacute/early acute rejection
1–6 monthsReactivation & opportunistic: CMV, BK, HSV/VZV, TB, HBV; PJP, Cryptococcus, Toxoplasma (reduced by prophylaxis)Peak acute rejection window; PTLD (EBV) may begin
>6 monthsCommunity-acquired » opportunistic; late viral (HSV, HBV, HCV, CMV)Chronic rejection; late solid-organ malignancies
ASK: "If a well-adherent patient has a first episode of acute rejection at 14 months, what should that prompt?" (Reconsider the diagnosis or check adherence/drug levels — a first acute rejection beyond 6 months is highly unlikely.)

Rejection types: acute cellular (T-cell; biopsy lymphocytic infiltrate; treat with pulse methylprednisolone 1 g ×3 d ± anti-lymphocyte antibody) and acute humoral (antibody-mediated; biopsy C4d or donor-specific antibodies; treat with increased immunosuppression, IVIg, plasmapheresis, rituximab, bortezomib, eculizumab). Chronic rejection: lung = bronchiolitis obliterans; kidney = chronic allograft dysfunction.

Pearl: Malignancy risk is ~2× the general population, skewed toward virally driven cancers. PTLD (EBV) is the exception that occurs early (often first year); most others appear late.

segments.label: Retrieval Close (3 min)
segments.html: Close the boards and have learners rebuild the three take-home points from memory, then the complication timeline (<1 / 1–6 / >6 months).

lessons.casesIntro: The original board is framework-based; these short application cases reinforce it. Reveal answers only after discussion.

cases.title: Cases (apply the framework) 1
cases.stem: Case 1 — Kidney transplant, 3 months out: fever, malaise, leukopenia, rising creatinine.
cases.reveal: Timing (1–6 months) puts CMV reactivation at the top of the infectious differential — but acute rejection must be excluded in parallel (it shares graft dysfunction and malaise). Check CMV PCR and a calcineurin-inhibitor trough; transplant nephrology will often biopsy. This is exactly the early-fever pitfall: infection AND rejection, not one or the other.

cases.title: Cases (apply the framework) 2
cases.stem: Case 2 — Lung transplant, 3 years out: progressive dyspnea, dry cough, obstructive PFTs with falling FEV1, normal CXR.
cases.reveal: Chronic rejection = bronchiolitis obliterans syndrome. Bronchoscopy to rule out stenosis/infection; biopsy shows small-airway fibrous scarring. Optimize immunosuppression; azithromycin 3×/week ± montelukast/sirolimus/photopheresis. Lung's high allogenicity is why these patients stay maximally immunosuppressed.

cases.title: Cases (apply the framework) 3
cases.stem: Case 3 — Heart transplant, 8 months out: a new, rapidly enlarging lymph node; EBV viremia.
cases.reveal: PTLD — EBV-driven, occurs early (typically first year), risk tied to EBV status and immunosuppression intensity. First step in management is reduction of immunosuppression; tissue diagnosis guides further therapy (rituximab-based).

mcqs.stem: A 52-year-old man is 2 weeks status post deceased-donor kidney transplant. He develops fever and a productive cough. Which infection category is MOST likely at this time point?
mcqs.exp: B. In the first month, nosocomial (surgical-site, line, catheter, hospital-acquired pneumonia, C. diff) and donor/recipient-derived infections dominate. A & E (reactivation/opportunistic) cluster at 1–6 months. C dominates >6 months. D (PTLD) is an early malignancy but presents as lymphoproliferation, not acute febrile pneumonia.

mcqs.stem: A patient is being counseled before kidney transplant about a CNI-sparing maintenance regimen with belatacept. Which finding is an absolute contraindication?
mcqs.exp: B. Belatacept is contraindicated in EBV-seronegative recipients because of markedly increased risk of PTLD (including CNS) and PML. A, C, D, E are not contraindications; indeed a CNI-sparing strategy may be chosen because of CNI intolerance (C).

mcqs.stem: A well-adherent liver transplant recipient with therapeutic tacrolimus levels presents with a first episode of biopsy-proven acute cellular rejection 18 months post-transplant. What does the timing most appropriately prompt?
opts: This is expected and requires no change
Re-examination of the diagnosis and adherence/drug levels, since a first acute rejection beyond 6 months is unusual
Switch to induction-dose anti-thymocyte globulin as maintenance
mcqs.exp: B. Assuming adherence, a first acute rejection beyond 6 months is highly unlikely, so confirm the diagnosis and verify adherence/levels. Treatment of true acute cellular rejection is pulse steroids ± anti-lymphocyte antibody (not maintenance ATG, E). C and D are inappropriate.

mcqs.stem: Which maintenance immunosuppressant class is most associated with nephrotoxicity, neurotoxicity, hyperkalemia, and hyperuricemia?
mcqs.exp: B. Calcineurin inhibitors (tacrolimus, cyclosporine) cause nephrotoxicity, neurotoxicity, hyperkalemia, hyperuricemia, and metabolic syndrome. Antimetabolites (A) → marrow suppression and GI/colitis; steroids (C) → metabolic syndrome; D is an induction agent; mTOR inhibitors (E) cause proteinuria, poor wound healing, and cytopenias rather than this cluster.

mcqs.stem: A CMV-seronegative recipient receives a kidney from a CMV-seropositive donor (D+/R−). Which agent was FDA-approved in 2023 specifically for CMV prophylaxis in high-risk kidney transplant recipients, offering less myelosuppression than the standard?
mcqs.exp: B. Letermovir (Prevymis) gained FDA approval in June 2023 for prophylaxis in high-risk (D+/R−) kidney transplant recipients, with less myelosuppression than valganciclovir (A, the long-standing standard). Maribavir (D, FDA 2021) is for refractory/resistant CMV, not first-line prophylaxis. Acyclovir (C) is inadequate for CMV; foscarnet (E) is reserved for resistant disease and is nephrotoxic.

anki: What is the central trade-off in managing immunosuppression after solid organ transplant?
More immunosuppression lowers rejection risk but raises the risk of infection, malignancy, and drug toxicity — and vice versa.
Rank lung, liver, and heart/kidney transplants by the degree of immunosuppression required (allogenicity).
Lung > heart/kidney > liver. Lung transplants are most likely to reject (~50% at 5 yr); liver is least likely.
What is the difference between induction and maintenance immunosuppression?

Induction = intense, short-term immunosuppression given at the time of transplant (anti-lymphocyte/anti–IL-2 antibodies + high-dose steroids). Maintenance = lower-intensity long-term therapy, typically CNI + antimetabolite ± prednisone.
Name the three core maintenance immunosuppressant classes and an example of each.
Calcineurin inhibitors (tacrolimus, cyclosporine); antimetabolites (mycophenolate, azathioprine); steroids (prednisone). mTOR inhibitors (sirolimus, everolimus) are an additional class.
How does allogenicity (rejection risk) change over time after transplant?
It is highest immediately/within the first month and decreases over time, allowing immunosuppression to be reduced accordingly.
When is a FIRST episode of acute rejection highly unlikely?
Beyond 6 months from transplant, assuming good medication adherence.
What are the two main types of acute rejection and how is each diagnosed?
Acute cellular (T-cell mediated; biopsy shows lymphocytic/inflammatory infiltrate) and acute humoral/antibody-mediated (biopsy shows C4d deposition, or detection of donor-specific antibodies).
What is the first-line treatment of acute cellular rejection?
Pulse-dose methylprednisolone (e.g., 1 g IV × 3 days), ± anti-lymphocyte/anti–IL-2 antibody for severe or steroid-refractory cases.
What infections predominate in the FIRST month after transplant?
Nosocomial infections (surgical-site, line, catheter, C. diff) and donor- or recipient-derived infections; opportunistic infections are uncommon this early.
What infections predominate 1–6 months after transplant?
Reactivation and opportunistic infections — CMV, BK, HSV/VZV, TB, HBV, plus PJP, Cryptococcus, Toxoplasma. Incidence is reduced by prophylaxis.
What infections predominate beyond 6 months after transplant?
Community-acquired infections (and late viral infections such as HSV, HBV, HCV, CMV); opportunistic infections become less common with reduced immunosuppression.
How much higher is malignancy risk in transplant recipients, and which cancers predominate?
About 2× the general population, especially virally mediated cancers — lymphoma/PTLD (EBV), skin and anogenital cancers (HPV), liver cancer.
What is PTLD and when does it typically occur?
Post-transplant lymphoproliferative disorder — an EBV-driven lymphoid proliferation. It occurs early (typically the first year); EBV positivity and degree of immunosuppression are key risk factors.
What are the major categories of post-transplant complications?
Rejection, medication side effects, infection, malignancy, and surgical complications (leaks, kinks, fistulas, stenoses).
What are the key toxicities of calcineurin inhibitors?
Nephrotoxicity, neurotoxicity, hyperkalemia, hyperuricemia, and metabolic syndrome (hypertension, hyperlipidemia, hyperglycemia).
What is belatacept and a key contraindication?

A CD28/CD80-86 costimulation blocker used as CNI-sparing maintenance immunosuppression (FDA 2011); associated with better long-term GFR and fewer donor-specific antibodies. CONTRAINDICATED in EBV-seronegative recipients due to PTLD/PML risk.
Which CMV prophylaxis agent was FDA-approved in 2023 for high-risk kidney transplant, and why use it?

Letermovir (Prevymis), for high-risk (donor-positive/recipient-negative) kidney transplant recipients — an alternative to valganciclovir with less myelosuppression. Maribavir (2021) is used for refractory/resistant CMV.
What is the status of OKT3 (muromonab-CD3)?
Discontinued in 2010. Current T-cell–depleting induction agents are rabbit anti-thymocyte globulin (rATG) and alemtuzumab; basiliximab (anti–IL-2 receptor) is the non-depleting option.
lessons.dot:
#s/p *[kidney/liver/heart/lung] transplant *MM/YYYY (*__ mo out) — immunosuppression = balance rejection vs infxn/malignancy/tox
allogenicity (reject risk): lung > heart/kidney > liver; intensity de-escalates over time
IMMUNOSUPPRESSION:
  induction (at txp): [rATG/alemtuzumab (depleting) | basiliximab (anti–IL-2R)] + pulse steroids
  maintenance: CNI *[tacro/cyclo] (goal trough *__) + antimetabolite *[MMF/aza] ± pred *__mg
    alt: belatacept (CNI-sparing) — ONLY if EBV+ ; mTOR-i [siro/evero]
COMPLICATIONS by time:
  <1 mo: nosocomial/donor-derived infxn; surgical (leak/kink/fistula/stenosis); early rejection
  1–6 mo: reactivation/OI [CMV/BK/HSV-VZV/TB/HBV/PJP/Crypto/Toxo] — on ppx; PEAK acute rejection; PTLD may start
  >6 mo: community-acq infxn; chronic rejection; late solid malignancy
REJECTION:
  acute cellular (T-cell, bx infiltrate) → methylpred 1g IV *x3d ± anti-lymphocyte Ab
  acute humoral (C4d / DSA) → ↑IS, IVIg, PLEX, rituximab, bortezomib, eculizumab
  *r/o w/ graft dysfxn: first acute rejection >6mo unlikely if adherent → recheck levels/adherence
PROPHYLAXIS: TMP-SMX (PJP); CMV [valganciclovir | letermovir if D+/R− high-risk]; antifungal/antiviral per protocol
MONITORING: CNI troughs, *Cr/GFR, *CMV PCR, *BK PCR, CBC, metabolic panel; med interactions (azoles/macrolides/diltiazem ↑CNI)
AVOID: live vaccines; abrupt IS changes w/o txp team; nephrotoxins w/ CNI
red flags → call txp: graft dysfxn, fever, new mass/LAD (PTLD), CMV/BK viremia
takehome: Immunosuppression is a constant balance: lowering rejection risk costs you infection, malignancy, and drug toxicity.
Allogenicity (rejection risk) is highest in the first month and falls over time; so does immunosuppression intensity. Lung > heart/kidney > liver.
Assuming adherence, a first episode of acute rejection beyond 6 months is highly unlikely.
Reactivation infections (CMV, BK, HSV/VZV, TB, HBV) cluster at 1–6 months; nosocomial/donor-derived <1 month; community-acquired >6 months.
PTLD (EBV) occurs early; most other malignancies appear late. Overall cancer risk ~2× the general population.

lessons.title: Management of VTE
lessons.subtitle: Optimized lesson package · Facilitator-led case-based chalk talk for IM residents · 40–50 min (4 modular case segments)
lessons.credit: Adapted from TeachIM.org — Management of VTE (Wong, Fainstad; expert review Garcia; 2020), CC BY-NC 4.0. Optimized June 2026: framework verified current, agent/duration updates added, pedagogy restructured, assessments added. Companion deck: VTE_Management_Optimized.pptx (original board-set-up images + 3 appended slides). The original is intentionally a non-interactive set of whiteboard images — best reproduced on a whiteboard.
objectives: IF — determine whether a VTE warrants anticoagulation vs. further evaluation/surveillance.
WHERE — decide whether to initiate anticoagulation inpatient or outpatient.
WHICH — select the anticoagulant agent.
HOW LONG — build a framework for the duration of anticoagulation.

prework.q: Q1. Does every venous clot get anticoagulated?
prework.a: No. An isolated distal (below-knee) DVT that is asymptomatic/low-risk can be surveilled with a repeat duplex in 1–2 weeks (treat only if it extends), and an isolated subsegmental PE without a proximal DVT in a low-risk patient may be surveilled. Proximal DVT and main/segmental PE are anticoagulated.

prework.q: Q2. What score decides whether a PE patient can go home?
prework.a: The PESI (or simplified sPESI). An uncomplicated PE with PESI <85 (class I–II) or sPESI 0, without high bleeding risk, hemodynamic compromise, or social barriers, can be managed as an outpatient.

prework.q: Q3. First-line agent for most stable VTE, including cancer-associated VTE?
prework.a: A DOAC. DOACs are first-line for stable VTE and are now preferred over LMWH for most cancer-associated VTE; LMWH or apixaban is favored for luminal GI cancers, and warfarin is used for antiphospholipid syndrome and severe renal impairment.

segments.label: Hook (2 min)
segments.html: "Four patients, four clots — a calf DVT, a subsegmental PE, a big saddle PE, and a cancer patient with a proximal DVT. Not all four get the same treatment, and one might get none. Today we answer four questions for any clot: IF, WHERE, WHICH, HOW LONG."

segments.label: Objective 1 — IF: should this clot be treated?
segments.html:
ASK: "Map the clot onto the venous tree — which locations are 'definitely treat' vs. 'potentially treat'?" Reveal: main/segmental PE and proximal LE DVT (popliteal and above) → definitely anticoagulate.
ScenarioDecision
Proximal DVT (popliteal/femoral/iliac), main or segmental PEAnticoagulate
Isolated distal (below-knee) DVTIf severe symptoms or high risk → treat; otherwise surveil with repeat duplex in 1–2 weeks, treat only if it extends
Isolated subsegmental PE, no proximal DVT, low recurrence riskLE duplex; surveillance is an option over anticoagulation (CHEST 2021)
Pearl: The decision to treat is about the clot's threat (location, burden) balanced against bleeding risk — not the mere presence of a clot. "Not everything that clots needs a blood thinner."

segments.label: Objective 2 — WHERE: inpatient vs. outpatient?
segments.html:
ASK: "Your PE patient is stable — who still needs admission?" Reveal: risk-stratify with PESI. PESI <85 (class I–II) → consider outpatient; otherwise inpatient. Override to inpatient for high bleeding risk, unreliable disposition, or poor compliance (also hemodynamic instability/RV strain).
Pearl: Outpatient PE management is safe in carefully selected low-risk patients (HESTIA/PESI-based pathways) and avoids unnecessary admissions — but the social/logistic screen (reliable follow-up, ability to get and afford the drug) is as important as the score.

segments.label: Objective 3 — WHICH: agent selection
segments.html:
AgentBridge?Role
LMWHNoBridge / pregnancy; option for luminal GI cancer; poor oral absorption
DOACNo (apixaban/rivaroxaban) — dabigatran & edoxaban need ~5 d lead-in heparinFirst-line for stable VTE, including most cancer VTE
WarfarinYes (overlap with heparin until INR therapeutic)Antiphospholipid syndrome, mechanical valves, CrCl <25–30, cost/access
2020–25 update: For cancer-associated VTE, DOACs (apixaban, edoxaban, rivaroxaban) are now first-line over LMWH (CARAVAGGIO 2020; ASH 2021/CHEST 2021) — but apixaban or LMWH is preferred for luminal GI malignancies because rivaroxaban/edoxaban raised GI bleeding. Avoid DOACs in antiphospholipid syndrome (use warfarin — TRAPS).

segments.label: Objective 4 — HOW LONG: duration
segments.html:
ASK: "Plot thrombosis risk over time for four patients — who stops at 3 months and who goes indefinite?" Reveal the framework:
ScenarioDuration
Provoked by a reversible/transient cause (surgery, immobilization, estrogen)~3 months, then stop
Unprovoked but high bleeding risk~3 months, then reassess (risk/benefit may favor stopping)
Unprovoked, not high bleeding riskIndefinite anticoagulation
Provoked by an irreversible/persistent cause (active cancer, antiphospholipid syndrome)Indefinite (as long as the cause persists)
2020–25 update: For the extended phase (after the first 3–6 months), a reduced-dose DOAC — apixaban 2.5 mg BID (AMPLIFY-EXT) or rivaroxaban 10 mg daily (EINSTEIN-CHOICE) — maintains protection with less bleeding.

segments.label: Close (3 min)
segments.html: Hide the board. Have each group state their case's answer to all four questions (IF/WHERE/WHICH/HOW LONG) in one sentence, then show the take-home slide.

cases.title: Case A — IF: isolated distal DVT
cases.stem: A patient has an isolated below-knee (calf/muscular vein) DVT found on duplex, minimal symptoms, no major risk factors, low bleeding risk.
cases.reveal: For a low-risk, minimally symptomatic isolated distal DVT, surveillance with a repeat duplex in 1–2 weeks is appropriate — anticoagulate if it extends toward the popliteal vein. Treat up front if severely symptomatic or high-risk for extension.

cases.title: Case B — WHERE: segmental PE, stable
cases.stem: A hemodynamically stable patient with a segmental PE, normal vitals, no RV strain, PESI calculated at 70.
cases.reveal: PESI <85 (class I–II) without high bleeding risk or social barriers → outpatient management on a DOAC is reasonable, with close follow-up. Admit if PESI ≥85, bleeding risk, instability, or unreliable disposition.

cases.title: Case C — WHICH: cancer-associated proximal DVT
cases.stem: A patient with active malignancy develops a proximal lower-extremity DVT.
cases.reveal: A DOAC (apixaban, edoxaban, or rivaroxaban) is now first-line over LMWH for cancer-associated VTE (CARAVAGGIO; ASH/CHEST). If the cancer is a luminal GI malignancy, prefer apixaban or LMWH because of GI bleeding risk with rivaroxaban/edoxaban.

cases.title: Case D — HOW LONG: unprovoked proximal DVT
cases.stem: A patient with a first unprovoked proximal DVT, no identifiable transient trigger, average bleeding risk.
cases.reveal: Indefinite anticoagulation (unprovoked, not high bleed risk), reassessing bleeding risk periodically. A reduced-dose DOAC is an option for the extended phase to lower bleeding.

mcqs.stem: A 62-year-old woman with metastatic colon cancer develops a proximal femoral DVT. CrCl is normal; no active bleeding. Which is the most appropriate anticoagulant?
mcqs.exp: B. DOACs are first-line for cancer-associated VTE, but for a luminal GI malignancy (colon), apixaban or LMWH is preferred because rivaroxaban (A) and edoxaban carry higher GI bleeding in this group. Warfarin (C) is inferior to DOAC/LMWH for cancer VTE. Aspirin (D) is inadequate for acute VTE.

mcqs.stem: A 48-year-old man has a stable segmental PE, normal vitals, no RV dysfunction, PESI 65, reliable follow-up. What is the best disposition?
opts: Admit to the ICU for thrombolysis
Outpatient anticoagulation with a DOAC and close follow-up
mcqs.exp: C. A PESI <85 (class I–II) uncomplicated PE without bleeding risk or social barriers can be managed as an outpatient on a DOAC. Thrombolysis (A) is for hemodynamically unstable (massive) PE. Routine admission (B) is unnecessary for low-risk PE. An IVC filter (D) is reserved for when anticoagulation is contraindicated.

mcqs.stem: A 30-year-old woman with triple-positive antiphospholipid syndrome has a recurrent unprovoked DVT. Which anticoagulation strategy is preferred long-term?
mcqs.exp: C. In antiphospholipid syndrome, especially triple-positive disease, warfarin is preferred over DOACs — the TRAPS trial showed excess arterial thrombotic events with rivaroxaban. DOACs (A, B) are therefore avoided; aspirin alone (D) is inadequate for established VTE in APS.

mcqs.stem: A patient with a first unprovoked proximal DVT has completed 6 months of full-dose apixaban with no bleeding and average bleeding risk. What is the best next step?
opts: Continue extended anticoagulation, e.g., apixaban 2.5 mg twice daily
Place an IVC filter and stop the DOAC
mcqs.exp: B. An unprovoked VTE without high bleeding risk warrants indefinite anticoagulation; for the extended phase a reduced-dose DOAC (apixaban 2.5 mg BID per AMPLIFY-EXT, or rivaroxaban 10 mg daily per EINSTEIN-CHOICE) maintains protection with less bleeding. Stopping (A) leaves high recurrence risk; warfarin (C) adds monitoring burden without benefit; an IVC filter (D) is not a substitute for anticoagulation here.

mcqs.stem: A healthy 55-year-old has an isolated subsegmental PE found incidentally on a staging CT; bilateral leg duplex shows no DVT and he has low recurrence risk. What is a guideline-supported option?
mcqs.exp: B. For isolated subsegmental PE without a proximal DVT and with low recurrence risk, CHEST 2021 supports clinical surveillance over anticoagulation. Lifelong anticoagulation (A) is not warranted for this low-risk finding; thrombolysis (C) and IVC filter (D) have no role here.

anki: The four VTE-management questions?
IF (treat?), WHERE (inpatient vs outpatient), WHICH (agent), HOW LONG (duration).
Which clots are "definitely treat"?
Proximal LE DVT (popliteal and above) and main/segmental PE.
Isolated distal (below-knee) DVT -- approach?
If low-risk/asymptomatic, surveil with repeat duplex in 1-2 weeks; treat if it extends or is severely symptomatic/high-risk.
Isolated subsegmental PE without proximal DVT -- approach?
LE duplex; if low recurrence risk, surveillance is an option over anticoagulation (CHEST 2021).
What decides inpatient vs outpatient PE?
PESI/sPESI: PESI <85 (class I-II) -> outpatient if no high bleed risk, instability, or social barriers.
Overrides to admit a low-PESI PE?
High bleeding risk, unreliable disposition, poor compliance, hemodynamic instability/RV strain.
First-line agent for stable VTE?
A DOAC (apixaban, rivaroxaban, dabigatran, edoxaban).
Which DOACs need a heparin lead-in?
Dabigatran and edoxaban need ~5 days of parenteral heparin first; apixaban and rivaroxaban do not.
Cancer-associated VTE -- preferred agent?
A DOAC (apixaban/edoxaban/rivaroxaban) is now first-line over LMWH (CARAVAGGIO; ASH/CHEST 2021).
Cancer VTE caveat for luminal GI malignancy?
Prefer apixaban or LMWH (rivaroxaban/edoxaban raise GI bleeding).
Antiphospholipid syndrome -- agent?
Warfarin, NOT a DOAC (TRAPS: excess arterial events with rivaroxaban in triple-positive APS).
When is warfarin preferred over a DOAC?
Antiphospholipid syndrome, mechanical valves, CrCl <25-30, cost/access; needs heparin bridge until INR therapeutic.
Provoked VTE (reversible cause) -- duration?
~3 months, then stop.
Unprovoked VTE without high bleed risk -- duration?
Indefinite anticoagulation.
Provoked by an irreversible cause (active cancer, APS) -- duration?
Indefinite, as long as the cause persists.
Extended-phase DOAC dosing to reduce bleeding?
Apixaban 2.5 mg BID (AMPLIFY-EXT) or rivaroxaban 10 mg daily (EINSTEIN-CHOICE).
lessons.dot:
#VTE: * yo w/ [proximal DVT / distal DVT / segmental PE / subsegmental PE / saddle PE], [provoked by * (transient/persistent) / unprovoked]. HDS [yes/no], RV strain [yes/no], bleeding risk [low/high]. CrCl *.
- IF treat: proximal DVT or main/segmental PE -> ANTICOAGULATE. isolated distal DVT low-risk -> *duplex in 1-2 wk, tx if extends*. isolated SSPE no proximal DVT + low risk -> LE duplex, surveillance option (CHEST 2021)
- WHERE: PE -> PESI/sPESI [PESI *]. PESI <85 (class I-II) + no high bleed risk/instability/social barrier -> OUTPATIENT; else INPATIENT. unstable/massive PE -> thrombolysis + ICU
- WHICH agent:
  - DOAC first-line (stable VTE incl most cancer VTE): apixaban [10 BID x7d -> 5 BID] or rivaroxaban [15 BID x21d -> 20 daily] (no lead-in); dabigatran/edoxaban need ~5d heparin lead-in
  - cancer VTE: DOAC > LMWH (CARAVAGGIO); *luminal GI cancer -> apixaban or LMWH (GI bleed)*
  - warfarin (bridge to INR 2-3): antiphospholipid syndrome (NOT DOAC - TRAPS), mech valve, CrCl <25-30
  - LMWH: pregnancy, poor PO absorption, periprocedural bridging
- HOW LONG: provoked/reversible -> ~3 mo. unprovoked + high bleed risk -> ~3 mo then reassess. unprovoked not-high-bleed -> indefinite. irreversible cause (active cancer, APS) -> indefinite
  - extended phase: reduced-dose DOAC [apixaban 2.5 BID / rivaroxaban 10 daily] to cut bleeding
- w/u as indicated: provoking factors, age-appropriate cancer screening if unprovoked, thrombophilia eval only if it changes mgmt; baseline CBC/renal/LFTs; counsel bleeding precautions, adherence, f/u *
takehome: IF: not every clot is treated — an isolated distal DVT (low-risk) can be surveilled with a repeat duplex in 1–2 weeks, and an isolated subsegmental PE without proximal DVT may be surveilled; proximal DVT and main/segmental PE are anticoagulated.
WHERE: an uncomplicated PE with PESI <85 can be managed as an outpatient if there's no high bleeding risk, instability, or social barrier.
WHICH: DOACs are first-line for stable VTE, including most cancer-associated VTE (apixaban/LMWH preferred for luminal GI cancers; warfarin for antiphospholipid syndrome and severe renal impairment).
HOW LONG: provoked by a reversible cause → ~3 months; unprovoked or an irreversible cause without high bleeding risk → indefinite (consider a reduced-dose DOAC for the extended phase).
