lessons.title: Vaginitis
objectives: Outline the pathophysiology of common causes of vaginitis.
Differentiate causes of vaginitis based on history, physical examination, and diagnostic testing.
Discuss risk reduction and treatment options for the causes of vaginitis.

prework.q: 1. Normal vaginal flora is dominated by which organism, and what pH does it maintain?
prework.a: Lactobacillus (70–90% of flora) produces lactic acid → normal pH 3.8–4.5. Estrogen promotes lactobacilli.

prework.q: 2. Which of the three common infectious vaginitides does NOT raise vaginal pH?
prework.a: Yeast (candidal vulvovaginitis) — Candida and lactobacilli coexist, so pH stays normal (4.0–4.5). BV and trichomonas both raise pH >4.5.

prework.q: 3. Why can't you diagnose the cause of vaginitis from symptoms alone?
prework.a: Symptoms (itching, burning, discharge, odor) are nonspecific and overlap across causes. All symptomatic patients should be offered an exam with pH + microscopy (KOH/wet prep) and/or DNA assay/NAAT, and STI cervicitis (GC/chlamydia) should be excluded.

segments.label: 0:00–2:00 — Hook (Overview board)
segments.html:
Open with the four cardinal symptoms — itching, burning, abnormal discharge, abnormal odor — and the key framing: vaginitis splits into infectious (BV, yeast, trichomonas) and non-infectious (GSM/atrophic, irritant, allergic, inflammatory). The talk focuses on infectious causes, but the mimic that must not be missed is STI cervicitis (gonorrhea ± chlamydia).

ASK: "A patient says she 'has a yeast infection again.' Before you treat — what's your differential, and what one category can't you afford to miss?" (All three infectious causes plus non-infectious; don't miss GC/chlamydia cervicitis.)

segments.label: 2:00–9:00 — Objective 1: Pathophysiology (Causes boards)
segments.html:
Anchor everything on lactobacilli → lactic acid → pH. Click through each cause and have learners predict the pH:

CauseMechanismEffect on pH
BV (most common, 15–50%)Shift in flora: ↑ anaerobes (Gardnerella vaginalis, Mycoplasma hominis), ↓ lactobacilli → ↓ lactic acidpH >4.5
Yeast (75% lifetime)Overgrowth of Candida albicans (80–92%), krusei, or glabrata; lactobacilli unchangedNormal (4.0–4.5)
Trichomonas (5–50% acute, STI)Motile protozoan Trichomonas vaginalis; ↓ lactobacillipH >4.5 (often >5)
GSM/atrophic↓ estrogen (menopause) → ↓ lactobacillipH >5.0–5.5
Pearl: The single highest-yield discriminator is pH — yeast is the one that keeps a normal pH. That's the fastest bedside split between yeast and "everything else."

segments.label: 9:00–15:00 — Objective 2: Evaluation (Evaluation boards)
segments.html:
Stress that symptoms alone cannot determine the cause — offer exam + diagnostics to every symptomatic patient. Click through the H&P grid, then the lab grid.

BVCandidaTrichomonas
Risk factorsNew/multiple or same-sex partners; douching; smokingAntibiotics; diabetes; luteal phase; immunosuppressionNew/multiple partners; smoking; no barrier; other STIs
DischargeThin, homogeneous; clear/white/grayThick, "curd-like," white, non-odorousCopious, frothy, green-yellow
ExamNo inflammation; ± fishy odorErythematous labia/vulva, excoriations, itchingVulvar erythema/edema; "strawberry" cervix
pH>4.5 (Sn 97%)Normal 4.0–4.5>4.5, often >5
Whiff (KOH)Positive (Sp 91%)Negative± positive
Wet prepClue cells (≥20% epithelial cells)Hyphae/budding on KOHMotile trichomonads + ↑ WBC (only 50–60% Sn)
Best testDNA assay / gram stain (Nugent); Amsel ≥3/4DNA assay (Sn/Sp high); cultureNAAT/PCR (most Sn & Sp)
ASK: "Wet prep is negative but the patient clearly has symptoms and risk factors for trichomonas. Now what?" (Send NAAT/PCR — wet prep is only ~50–60% sensitive for trich; a negative microscopy does not rule it out.)
Pearl — Amsel criteria for BV (3 of 4): (1) thin homogeneous grayish-white discharge coating the walls, (2) pH >4.5, (3) positive whiff test, (4) clue cells (≥20% of epithelial cells) on saline wet mount. Highly sensitive; less specific than gram stain/DNA assay.
Pearl — co-existing etiologies: BV + yeast can coexist (elevated pH with both clue cells and hyphae). Incidental flora shifts, hyphae, or trichomonads on a Pap can be treated if symptomatic, monitored if not.

segments.label: 15:00–20:00 — Objective 3: Management (Management board)
segments.html:
Both topical and oral regimens are equally effective when used as directed — choose by patient preference, allergies, and pregnancy status.

CauseTopicalOralPregnancy
BVMetronidazole 0.75% gel 5 g intravaginal daily ×5 d, or clindamycin 2% cream qHS ×7 dMetronidazole 500 mg PO BID ×7 d, or clindamycin 300 mg PO BID ×7 dTopical option or oral metronidazole
YeastMiconazole or clotrimazole (1–7 d courses); miconazole 1200 mg suppository ×1Fluconazole 150 mg PO ×1Topical azole ×7 d (preferred)
TrichomonasNoneMetronidazole 500 mg PO BID ×7 d, or tinidazole 2 g PO ×1; treat partner(s)Metronidazole 500 mg PO BID ×7 d
Pearl: Trichomonas is an STI — treat the partner(s) (single 2 g metronidazole for men), avoid sex until both complete treatment, and re-test for reinfection at 3 months. Topical agents do not treat trich.

Risk reduction (emphasize for recurrent symptoms): fewer partners and barrier protection, avoid douching, properly clean sex devices, smoking cessation (BV/trich); glycemic control and avoid unnecessary antibiotics (yeast).

Retrieval close: board down — have learners rebuild the pH-by-cause table, name the test that confirms each, and give the first-line drug for each.

lessons.casesLabel: Cases (from the original board — answers click-to-reveal)

cases.title: Case 1 — 23-year-old woman, watery malodorous discharge; combined OCPs; LMP ~5 wk ago; 1 male partner; no douching/barrier/devices
cases.reveal: Discharge consistency/color/odor; associated symptoms (pruritus, burning, dyspareunia, abdominal pain, nausea); sexual history; menstrual history. No barrier contraception ↑ STI risk; combined OCPs ↑ physiologic discharge.

cases.title: Case 2 — 24-year-old woman, 2 wk discharge; 1 new male partner ×1 mo, intermittent condoms; recently started douching
cases.reveal: Additional STI testing plus DNA assay/PCR for trichomonas (wet prep only 50–60% sensitive). Consider empiric treatment of BV and trichomonas with metronidazole 500 mg PO BID ×7 d — note topical agents will not treat trich.

cases.title: Case 3 — 36-year-old woman, thick white discharge + vulvar itching/irritation; 1 female partner
cases.reveal: Candidal vulvovaginitis. Normal pH (4) with hyphae/budding yeast on wet prep is characteristic.

mcqs.stem: A 28-year-old woman has a thin gray discharge with a fishy odor. Vaginal pH is 5.0, whiff test is positive, and clue cells are seen on wet mount. Which is the most appropriate first-line treatment?
mcqs.exp: B. She meets Amsel criteria for BV (thin homogeneous discharge, pH >4.5, positive whiff, clue cells) — first-line is oral or topical metronidazole (or clindamycin). A/D — antifungals treat yeast (normal pH, hyphae). C — tinidazole is for trichomonas. E — ceftriaxone treats gonorrhea, not BV.

mcqs.stem: A 32-year-old woman has thick "cottage-cheese" discharge and vulvar itching. Vaginal pH is 4.2, whiff test is negative, and KOH prep shows hyphae. What is the diagnosis and pH explanation?
opts: Candidal vulvovaginitis; Candida and lactobacilli coexist, so pH is normal
mcqs.exp: C. Normal pH (4.0–4.5) with hyphae on KOH and a negative whiff is candidal vulvovaginitis — yeast and lactobacilli coexist, so pH is unchanged. A/B — both raise pH >4.5. D — GSM raises pH (>5). E — cervicitis does not characteristically lower pH and the KOH finding indicates yeast.

mcqs.stem: A 26-year-old woman with a new partner has frothy green-yellow discharge and a "strawberry cervix." Wet prep is negative for motile organisms. What is the best next step?
mcqs.exp: B. Wet prep is only ~50–60% sensitive for trichomonas — a negative microscopy does not exclude it, and the clinical picture (frothy green-yellow discharge, strawberry cervix, new partner) is classic. NAAT/PCR is the most sensitive/specific test. A/E — a negative wet prep can't rule out trich. C — wrong organism. D — topical metronidazole does not treat trichomonas (needs oral).

mcqs.stem: A patient is diagnosed with trichomoniasis. Which statement about management is correct?
opts: Topical metronidazole gel is adequate treatment
Her male partner does not require treatment
Preferred treatment is oral metronidazole 500 mg BID ×7 d, and partners should be treated
Single-dose 2 g metronidazole is preferred over the 7-day course in women
mcqs.exp: C. Per 2021 CDC, multi-dose metronidazole 500 mg BID ×7 d is preferred for women, and sexual partners must be treated (men: 2 g ×1). A — topical agents don't treat trich. B — partner treatment is required (STI). D — the 7-day course is preferred over single-dose in women. E — re-test for reinfection at 3 months.

mcqs.stem: A 58-year-old postmenopausal woman reports vaginal dryness, dyspareunia, and recurrent UTIs. Exam shows thin, pale vaginal mucosa with scant discharge; pH is 5.5. Which is the most likely diagnosis and first-line treatment?
opts: Genitourinary syndrome of menopause; vaginal estrogen and moisturizers/lubricants
mcqs.exp: B. Dryness, dyspareunia, recurrent UTIs, atrophic mucosa, and elevated pH in a postmenopausal woman are GSM (atrophic vaginitis) from estrogen deficiency — first-line is vaginal estrogen plus moisturizers/lubricants. A/C/D — infectious causes don't fit the atrophic exam and scant discharge. E — allergic vaginitis follows an exposure and isn't the best fit here.

anki: Normal vaginal flora and pH?
70-90% Lactobacillus produces lactic acid -> pH 3.8-4.5; estrogen promotes lactobacilli
Most common cause of vaginitis?
Bacterial vaginosis (15-50% of cases)
BV pathophysiology and pH?
Increased anaerobes (Gardnerella, Mycoplasma hominis), decreased lactobacilli -> pH >4.5
Candidal vulvovaginitis pH?
NORMAL (4.0-4.5) - Candida and lactobacilli coexist
Most common Candida species in VVC?
C. albicans (80-92%); also krusei, glabrata
Trichomonas organism and pH?
Motile protozoan Trichomonas vaginalis (STI); decreased lactobacilli -> pH >4.5 (often >5)
GSM/atrophic vaginitis pH?
Elevated (>5.0-5.5) from decreased estrogen -> decreased lactobacilli
Amsel criteria for BV (3 of 4)?
Thin homogeneous grayish-white discharge; pH >4.5; positive whiff; clue cells (>=20% epithelial cells)
Wet prep finding in BV?
Clue cells (epithelial cells with adherent coccobacilli, "shaggy" borders)
Wet prep / KOH finding in yeast?
Hyphae and budding yeast on KOH (specific, not highly sensitive)
Most sensitive/specific test for trichomonas?
NAAT/PCR (wet prep only 50-60% sensitive)
First-line treatment for BV?
Metronidazole (PO 500 mg BID x7 d or 0.75% gel x5 d) or clindamycin
First-line treatment for candidal vulvovaginitis?
Fluconazole 150 mg PO x1, or topical azole (miconazole/clotrimazole)
First-line treatment for trichomonas (women)?
Metronidazole 500 mg PO BID x7 d (or tinidazole 2 g x1); treat partner(s); topical agents do not work
Vaginitis treatment preferred in pregnancy?
Yeast -> topical azole x7 d; BV -> topical or oral metronidazole; trich -> metronidazole 500 mg BID x7 d
Key mimic of vaginitis to exclude?
STI cervicitis (gonorrhea +/- chlamydia)
Single-dose oral agent covering both BV and trichomonas?
Secnidazole (Solosec) 2 g granules x1 (FDA: BV 2017, T. vaginalis 2021)
Newer agents for recurrent VVC?
Ibrexafungerp (Brexafemme) and oteseconazole (Vivjoa, first RVVC-specific drug, FDA 2022)
lessons.dot:
#Vaginitis: vulvovaginal [itching/burning/discharge/odor *] x ** ; pregnant [Y/N *]
exam: discharge [thin homogeneous gray / thick curd-like white / frothy green-yellow / scant-atrophic *]; vulva [normal / erythema-edema / strawberry cervix *]
POC: vaginal pH * [BV/trich >4.5 (often >5) | yeast 4.0-4.5 normal | GSM >5]; whiff [+/− *]; wet prep [clue cells / hyphae-budding (KOH) / motile trichomonads + ↑WBC *]
dx: [BV (Amsel ≥3/4) / candidal VVC / trichomonas (STI) / GSM-atrophic / non-infectious irritant-allergic *]; r/o cervicitis [GC/chlamydia NAAT *]; confirm if microscopy neg → DNA assay/NAAT (wet prep trich Sn only 50-60%)
tx:
  BV → metronidazole 500 mg PO BID x7d [or 0.75% gel 5g intravag qD x5d / clinda 2% qHS x7d]; pregnancy: topical or PO metronidazole
  yeast → fluconazole 150 mg PO x1 [or topical azole]; pregnancy: topical azole x7d; recurrent → consider oteseconazole/ibrexafungerp
  trich → metronidazole 500 mg PO BID x7d [or tinidazole 2g x1]; TREAT partner(s) (men 2g x1); no sex until both done; re-test 3 mo; topical agents do NOT treat
  GSM → vaginal estrogen + moisturizer/lubricant
single-dose option (adherence): secnidazole 2g granules x1 (BV or trich)
risk-reduction (recurrent): avoid douching, clean sex devices, barrier protection, glycemic control, smoking cessation
takehome: The common causes disrupt vaginal flora differently → predictable pH: BV and trichomonas raise pH >4.5; yeast keeps a normal pH. GSM raises it further (>5).
Symptoms alone cannot determine the cause — offer exam with pH + microscopy (KOH/wet prep) and/or DNA assay/NAAT to all symptomatic patients, and exclude STI cervicitis.

Treat by etiology and address risk reduction (especially for recurrence): BV → metronidazole/clindamycin; yeast → fluconazole or topical azole; trichomonas → oral metronidazole ×7 d AND treat partner(s).

extras.label: Newer Context (2021–2025) added
extras.html:
The deck's diagnosis and first-line treatment match the 2021 CDC STI Treatment Guidelines, which remain current. Worth knowing:

  • Trichomoniasis in women: multi-dose metronidazole 500 mg BID ×7 d is preferred over single 2 g (more effective; Kissinger RCT, reflected in 2021 CDC) — already on the deck. Single 2 g still used for partners/men.
  • Secnidazole (Solosec) 2 g oral granules ×1 — single-dose option, FDA-approved for BV (2017) and T. vaginalis (2021); the only single-dose oral agent covering both. Useful when adherence to a 7-day course is a concern.
  • Recurrent vulvovaginal candidiasis (RVVC): two newer oral agents — ibrexafungerp (Brexafemme, first-in-class triterpenoid; VVC 2021, RVVC 2022) and oteseconazole (Vivjoa, FDA 2022, first drug approved specifically for RVVC).
  • GSM/atrophic vaginitis: vaginal estrogen, moisturizers/lubricants; non-hormonal options for those avoiding estrogen.

lessons.refs:
1. Workowski KA, et al. Sexually Transmitted Infections Treatment Guidelines, 2021. MMWR Recomm Rep. 2021;70(4):1-187.
  2. Vaginitis in Nonpregnant Patients: ACOG Practice Bulletin No. 215. Obstet Gynecol. 2020;135(1):e1-e17 (summary 243-245).
  3. Marnach ML, et al. Evaluation and Management of Vaginitis. Mayo Clin Proc. 2022;97(2):347-358.
  4. Paavonen J, Brunham RC. Bacterial Vaginosis and Desquamative Inflammatory Vaginitis. NEJM. 2018;379:2246-54.
  5. Eckert LO. Acute vulvovaginitis. NEJM. 2006;355:1244-52.

  10. new FDA. Brexafemme (ibrexafungerp) — VVC 2021, RVVC 2022; Vivjoa (oteseconazole) — RVVC 2022.

AreaWhat was done
Currency checkDiagnostic framework and first-line treatments verified against the 2021 CDC STI Guidelines (current) and ACOG PB 215. Newer context added: trich multi-dose preference (already on deck), secnidazole single-dose for BV/trich, ibrexafungerp + oteseconazole for recurrent VVC, GSM management.

All BioRender graphics and click-to-reveal animations preserved; 3 slides appended (newer context / take-home / attribution).

lessons.title: Ventilators Part 2: Pulmonary Mechanics
lessons.subtitle: Facilitator-led chalk talk · ~20 min (core) / ~45 min (with cases) · IM attendings & senior residents teaching residents · builds on Ventilators on the Fly
objectives: Name and define the key outputs in pressure-targeted vs volume-targeted modes (what you set vs what you must monitor).
Define resistance and compliance and predict how changes in each move peak, plateau, and driving pressure.
Apply a structured diagnostic approach to a high peak-pressure alarm in a mechanically ventilated patient.

prework.q: 1. In a volume-targeted mode, which two pressures must you watch? In a pressure-targeted mode, which two volumes?
prework.a: Targeting volume → monitor pressures (peak and plateau / driving). Targeting pressure → monitor volumes (tidal volume VT and minute ventilation VE = RR × VT). Simple principle: you watch the thing you don't control.

prework.q: 2. Write the bedside formulas for resistance and compliance and their normal cut-offs.
prework.a: Resistance ∝ Ppeak − Pplateau (normal <10 cm H₂O). Compliance = VT / (Pplateau − PEEP) = VT / driving pressure; driving pressure for a 4–6 mL/kg breath should be <10 cm H₂O. The single maneuver that gives you the plateau is an inspiratory hold.

prework.q: 3. A vent alarms for high peak pressure. What is the very first thing you do — before any calculation?
prework.a: Go to the bedside and establish urgency. Check vitals, VT/VE on the vent, and SpO₂. A high peak pressure plus falling SpO₂, falling VT/VE, rising PCO₂, or dropping BP = emergency → call for help and turn FiO₂ to 100% while you work through the steps.

segments.label: Hook
segments.html: "It's 2 a.m. RT pages you: 'Room 365's vent keeps alarming high peak pressures.' You don't need to know the patient's whole story to start — you need a plateau pressure and two subtractions. By the end of this talk you'll walk into that room with a three-step algorithm that works on any ventilator." Project the Key Outputs slide.

segments.label: Objective 1 — Key outputs: set vs monitor
segments.html:
ASK — "In a pressure-targeted mode, what are the key outputs? In a volume-targeted mode?" Reveal: pressure-targeted → VT and VE; volume-targeted → Ppeak and Pdriving.
TermDefinitionHow measured
Tidal volume (VT)Volume delivered each breathRead off vent (target it in VC)
Minute ventilation (VE)VT × RR (L/min)Read off vent; anchor adequacy to pH/PCO₂
Peak pressure (Ppeak)Total pressure to deliver VT during inspirationContinuously displayed
Plateau pressure (Pplateau)Pressure holding air in the lung at end-inspiration (no flow)Inspiratory hold maneuver
Driving pressure (Pdriving)Pressure difference to achieve VT = Pplateau − PEEPCompute after an inspiratory hold
Pearl. When you target volume, monitor pressures; when you target pressure, monitor volumes. The body of this talk is about volume-targeted modes, where peak and plateau are your dashboard.

segments.label: Objective 2 — Resistance and compliance
segments.html:
ASK — "Define resistance. Define compliance. Which one does the airway control, and which one does the lung/chest wall control?"

Resistance (R) = opposition to airflow through the whole circuit (ventilator tubing + ETT + airways). R ∝ Ppeak − Pplateau; normal <10 cm H₂O.

Compliance (C) = "stretchiness" of the respiratory system (lungs, pleura, chest wall, diaphragm, intra-abdominal pressure). C = ΔV/ΔP = VT / (Pplateau − PEEP) = VT / Pdriving. A stiffer system = lower compliance = higher driving pressure for the same breath.

OutputWhat moves it
PeakResistance and compliance, plus inspiratory flow rate/pattern and total PEEP (incl. auto-PEEP). It is the "everything" pressure.
PlateauMeasured with no airflow → resistance drops out. Reflects lung/chest-wall mechanics only. Keep <30 cm H₂O (barotrauma risk above).
DrivingSame drivers as plateau (compliance). Keep ≤14–15 cm H₂O in ARDS.
Pearl — the two subtractions. Ppeak − Pplateau isolates resistance. Pplateau − PEEP (driving pressure) isolates compliance. Almost everything in this talk reduces to those two subtractions.

segments.label: Objective 3 — The high peak-pressure algorithm
segments.html:
Step 1 — Establish urgency. At the bedside: full vitals + key vent outputs. High peak plus low SpO₂, low VT, VE down ~>50%, rising PCO₂, or dropping BP = emergency → call for help, FiO₂ to 100%, don't wait for an ABG.

Step 2 — Assess resistance. Inspiratory hold → plateau. Ppeak − Pplateau > 10 = resistance problem.

ASK — "Name the resistance differential and the matching action for each." Reveal: kinked tubing → inspect/uncompress; bitten ETT → bite block + analgesia/sedation; mucus plug / large-airway obstruction → deep suction (rarely bronchoscopy); bronchospasm → bronchodilators (esp. obstructive history); refractory kink/obstruction → tube exchange by airway-trained clinician.

Step 3 — Assess compliance. Pplateau > 30 or driving pressure > 10 = compliance problem.

ASK — "Sort the compliance differential into alveolar filling vs alveolar collapse vs extrapulmonary." Reveal: filling = cardiogenic edema, ARDS, pneumonia, diffuse alveolar hemorrhage; collapse = atelectasis, lobar collapse; extrapulmonary = pneumothorax, large pleural effusion (intrathoracic) or ascites, abdominal distention/compartment syndrome, pregnancy, stiff chest wall/burns (extrathoracic).
Pearl — tempo and exam localize the cause. A pneumothorax spikes peak over minutes; ARDS/fibrosis climbs over hours-to-days; severe COPD air-traps. Targeted exam + CXR/POCUS + ABG (and a bladder pressure if you suspect abdominal compartment syndrome) close the loop. Remember resistance and compliance problems can co-exist.

segments.label: Retrieval close
segments.html: Board off. Have a learner rebuild the algorithm from memory: Step 1 urgency → Step 2 peak−plateau for resistance → Step 3 plateau-30 / driving-10 for compliance → differential + action for each. Then have a second learner state the two subtractions and their normal cut-offs.

cases.title: Case 1 — Gradual rise, COVID ARDS
cases.stem: 65-year-old intubated in the ICU for COVID pneumonia; gradually rising Ppeak from ~35 to consistently 40. AC/volume control, FiO₂ 70%, PEEP 12, RR 20, VT 5 mL/kg (450 mL), deeply sedated and paralyzed. Vitals: T 37, BP 110/57, HR 93, RR 24, SaO₂ 97%, VT 450 mL.
cases.reveal: Stable: oxygenation fine, VE acceptable, hemodynamics intact. Proceed methodically.

cases.title: Case 2 — Acute decompensation, status asthmaticus
cases.stem: 25-year-old intubated for an asthma exacerbation; volume-targeted, FiO₂ 70%, PEEP 5, VT 350 mL (6 mL/kg), RR 24. One hour later: progressively hypotensive and hypoxemic — BP 100s/60s, RR 30, SaO₂ 92%, HR 120s; high peak pressures ~38, VT delivered 300 mL.
cases.reveal: Oxygenation borderline-stable; proceed but stay alert — a falling BP can flip this to an emergency. Get an ABG.

mcqs.stem: A patient on AC/volume control alarms with peak 42, and an inspiratory hold shows plateau 22 (PEEP 8). Which is the best next step?
opts: Suction the ETT and check for kinking/biting
mcqs.exp: B. Peak − plateau = 42 − 22 = 20 (>10) → a resistance problem. The first, reversible bedside causes are mucus plugging, a kinked or bitten tube — suction and inspect the circuit. A raises PEEP without addressing airway resistance. C targets compliance (edema), which is normal here (driving pressure 22 − 8 = 14). D is premature and moves an unstable patient. E doesn't fix resistance and may worsen ventilation.

mcqs.stem: Volume control, peak 40, plateau 36, PEEP 6. Which single value most specifically reflects a compliance problem?
mcqs.exp: C. Driving pressure = plateau − PEEP = 36 − 6 = 30 — markedly elevated (>10) and the most specific marker of low compliance. A (peak − plateau = 4) reflects resistance, which is normal. B and E are lumped pressures influenced by both resistance and compliance. D is a set parameter, not a derived mechanic.

mcqs.stem: A ventilated COPD patient becomes acutely hypotensive with rising peak pressures shortly after the RR was increased. Plateau − PEEP is normal; peak − plateau is high. What is the most appropriate immediate action?
opts: Give a fluid bolus and continue current settings
Disconnect from the ventilator to allow full exhalation, then treat bronchospasm
mcqs.exp: C. The picture is dynamic hyperinflation / auto-PEEP (breath-stacking) causing obstruction physiology and hemodynamic collapse. Disconnecting the circuit lets the patient exhale and immediately relieves intrathoracic pressure; then treat the underlying bronchospasm (bronchodilators, slow the rate to lengthen expiration). A worsens stacking. B alone won't fix the mechanics. D can help matching once stabilized but is not the immediate life-saving move. E treats the wrong diagnosis.

mcqs.stem: Which finding accompanying a high peak-pressure alarm should most prompt you to call for help before completing your stepwise assessment?
opts: A drop in blood pressure with falling SpO₂
mcqs.exp: C. Hemodynamic instability with desaturation marks an emergency (tension PTX, severe auto-PEEP, mucus plug occluding the airway). Call for backup, FiO₂ 100%, and proceed in parallel. The other options describe a stable patient in whom a methodical work-up is appropriate.

mcqs.stem: In a patient with ARDS on lung-protective settings (VT 6 mL/kg, plateau 28), which target is most strongly associated with improved survival and should guide further adjustment?
mcqs.exp: A. Driving pressure (plateau − PEEP) is the ventilatory variable most tightly linked to ARDS mortality; keeping ΔP ≤ 14–15 is associated with better survival even when VT and plateau are already protective (Amato, NEJM 2015). B peak pressure isn't the protective target. C/D are not survival targets and may be harmful (low VE may be acceptable with permissive hypercapnia). E low PEEP can worsen oxygenation/derecruitment.

anki: In a volume-targeted mode, which outputs do you monitor?
Pressures — peak and plateau (and driving pressure).
In a pressure-targeted mode, which outputs do you monitor?
Volumes — tidal volume (VT) and minute ventilation (VE).
Formula for minute ventilation
VE = VT × RR (L/min).
How is plateau pressure measured?
Inspiratory hold (no airflow at end-inspiration).
Resistance is proportional to…
Ppeak − Pplateau; normal <10 cm H2O.
Driving pressure formula
Pdriving = Pplateau − PEEP; compliance = VT/Pdriving.
Upper limit for plateau pressure
<30 cm H2O (barotrauma risk above).
Driving-pressure target in ARDS
≤14–15 cm H2O (Amato NEJM 2015).
Why does plateau pressure exclude resistance?
It is measured with no airflow, so airway resistance contributes nothing.
Step 1 of the high peak-pressure algorithm
Establish urgency at bedside (vitals, SpO2, VT/VE); call for help + FiO2 100% if unstable.
Peak − plateau > 10 indicates…
A resistance problem.
Plateau > 30 or driving > 10 indicates…
A compliance problem.
Causes of high resistance
Kinked tube, bitten ETT, mucus plug / large-airway obstruction, bronchospasm.
Compliance differential — two buckets
Alveolar filling (edema, ARDS, pneumonia, DAH) and alveolar collapse (atelectasis, lobar collapse), ± extrapulmonary (PTX, effusion, ascites/abdominal distention).
Actions for a resistance problem
Inspect/uncompress tubing, deep suction, bite block + sedation/analgesia, bronchodilators, exchange tube if refractory.
Acute hypotension + high peak in a ventilated asthmatic — first move
Suspect auto-PEEP/tension physiology; disconnect from vent to allow full exhalation, rule out PTX, slow the rate.
2023–24 ARDS framing for who you will troubleshoot
ESICM 2023 / Global ARDS definition 2024: SpO2/FiO2 ≤315 may define ARDS, lung US accepted, HFNO a recognized support category.
lessons.dot:
#Vent mechanics — high P*peak alarm w/u (volume-targeted)
mode: AC/VC  set: VT *mL/kg IBW (4-6) / RR * / PEEP * / FiO2 *
outputs: Ppeak * / Pplat * (insp hold) / VT del * / VE * / SpO2 *
STEP1 urgency: [stable / EMERGENT -> call help + FiO2 100%]
  emergent if: SpO2 down / VT or VE down >50% / PCO2 up / BP down
STEP2 resistance = Ppeak-Pplat *= [<10 nl / >10 RESISTANCE]
  ddx [kinked tube / bitten ETT / mucus plug / lg-airway obstr / bronchospasm]
  rx [inspect-uncompress tubing / deep suction / bite block + sedation-analgesia / bronchodilators / exchange ETT if refractory]
STEP3 compliance = Pplat-PEEP (driving) *= [<10 nl / >10 LOW COMPLIANCE]; Pplat goal <30; dP goal <=14-15 (ARDS)
  ddx filling [cardiogenic edema / ARDS / PNA / DAH]
      collapse [atelectasis / lobar collapse]
      extrapulm [PTX / pleural effusion / ascites-abd distention / abd compartment]
  tempo: PTX min-hrs / ARDS-fibrosis hrs-days / COPD air-trap (auto-PEEP)
  w/u [targeted exam / CXR / POCUS / ABG / bladder pressure if ACS]
note: R and C problems can coexist; auto-PEEP -> disconnect circuit to exhale
* = bedside-editable value
takehome: Peak and plateau are the key outputs in volume-targeted modes. Peak reflects resistance + compliance + PEEP + inspiratory flow; plateau (inspiratory hold) should be <30 cm H₂O.
For a high peak-pressure alarm: (1) establish urgency, then (2) get a plateau and decide resistance vs compliance vs both.
Resistance ∝ Ppeak − Pplateau; >10 = resistance problem (kink, bite, mucus plug, bronchospasm).
Compliance ∝ 1/driving pressure (Pplateau − PEEP); driving >10 (or plateau >30) = compliance problem (alveolar filling/collapse, extrapulmonary). Keep ΔP ≤ 14–15.
They can co-exist — and falling BP, SpO₂, or VE turns troubleshooting into an emergency: call for help and FiO₂ 100% first.

extras.label: Modern Framing — 2023–25 Context currency check
extras.html:
The core mechanics (resistance, compliance, peak/plateau/driving pressure, and the high peak-pressure algorithm) are timeless physiology and verified current as of June 2026. The items below add modern context; none change the lesson's standing teaching.

Driving pressure as the survival target. Driving pressure (ΔP = Pplateau − PEEP) is the ventilatory variable most tightly associated with ARDS mortality. Keep ΔP ≤ 14–15 cm H₂O — risk rises stepwise above that even when VT ≤6 mL/kg and plateau ≤30 (Amato, NEJM 2015; LUNG SAFE).

Lung-protective targets unchanged. VT 4–6 mL/kg ideal body weight, plateau <30, PEEP >5 (ARDSNet, NEJM 2000; ATS/ESICM/SCCM 2017; ESICM ARDS 2023).
ESICM ARDS 2023 / Global (new) ARDS definition 2024. SpO₂/FiO₂ ≤315 (when SpO₂ ≤97%) may define ARDS; lung ultrasound is an accepted imaging modality; high-flow nasal oxygen is recognized as a support category. Net effect: a broader population you'll be troubleshooting at the vent — the mechanics approach is unchanged.

  2. Amato MBP, et al. Driving pressure and survival in the acute respiratory distress syndrome. N Engl J Med. 2015;372:747–755.
  3. ARDS Network. Ventilation with lower tidal volumes for ARDS. N Engl J Med. 2000;342:1301–1308.
  4. Fan E, et al. ATS/ESICM/SCCM Clinical Practice Guideline: Mechanical Ventilation in Adult Patients with ARDS. Am J Respir Crit Care Med. 2017;195:1253–1263.

  6. Matthay MA, et al. A New Global Definition of ARDS. Am J Respir Crit Care Med. 2024.

lessons.title: Ventilators on the Fly
lessons.subtitle: Facilitator-led chalk talk · 20–25 min (core) / 30–35 min (with cases) · ~15 min intro option (modes + O₂) · IM attendings & senior residents teaching residents
objectives: Determine the mode of mechanical ventilation from the settings and waveforms.
Determine the relative level of oxygen support a ventilator is providing.
Determine whether minute ventilation is appropriate.
Identify problems with respiratory mechanics.

prework.q: 1. You glance at a vent: the pressure waveform is a square box, and there's a set RR of 18. What family of mode is this, and is it control or support?
prework.a: Square wave in pressure → a pressure-targeted mode (PC, PRVC, or PS). A set/mandatory RR → a control mode (so PC or PRVC, not PS). Pressure support has no mandatory rate.

prework.q: 2. Write the minute-ventilation equation and the normal range.
prework.a: VE = VT × RR. Normal 4–6 L/min (often higher with disease/metabolic acidosis).

prework.q: 3. In a volume-targeted mode the peak pressure alarms high. What single maneuver separates a resistance problem from a compliance problem?
prework.a: An inspiratory hold to get the plateau pressure. Peak − Plateau > 3–5 = resistance (mucus plug, kinked tube, bronchospasm); elevated Plateau − PEEP (>10) = compliance (pneumothorax, edema, fibrosis, mainstem intubation, distended abdomen).

segments.label: Hook
segments.html: "You walk into a room and the vent is alarming. You don't need to memorize every brand's screen — you need four questions that work on any ventilator, any brand. Let's build them." Project the Orientation slide and point out: dials on the right = what the clinician set; numbers on the left = what the ventilator measured. They aren't always equal (a spontaneously breathing patient can exceed the set rate).

segments.label: Orientation — reading the screen
segments.html:
ASK — "On the pressure waveform, what are PEEP, peak pressure, and ΔP?" Reveal: PEEP = pressure left in the circuit after exhalation; peak = max pressure during inspiration; ΔP = peak − PEEP (units cm H₂O). On the flow waveform, up = inspiratory flow, down = expiratory flow.
Pearl. Expiration on a ventilator is passive — expiratory flow depends only on patient mechanics (recoil + resistance), so it looks the same across all modes. Inspiration is where the mode shows itself.

segments.label: Objective 1 — What is the mode?
segments.html:
ASK — Before revealing each mode card: "Where's the square wave? Is there a mandatory RR? What's targeted, and what must you monitor?"
ModeSquare wave in…Mandatory RR?TargetMonitorWhen to use
Pressure Control (AC-PC)PressureYesInspiratory pressure (Pcontrol/ΔP)VT & minute ventilationComfort, precise airway-pressure control
Volume Control (AC-VC)FlowYesTidal volume (VT)Peak & plateau pressureARDS (lung-protective VT)
PRVC (hybrid)PressureYesTidal volume (VT)Peak/plateau + achieved VTLung-protective VT with PC comfort
Pressure Support (PS)PressureNoPressure support (Psupport)VT, RR, minute ventilationSBTs; patient providing adequate RR
Pearl — caveats. On modern vents the inspiratory-flow shape in volume control can be changed, so the absence of a square flow wave does NOT exclude volume control. A strong patient effort in PS blunts the pressure square wave. PRVC carries brand names like APV-CMV and VC+. Both PC and VC are forms of Assist Control — every breath (vent- or patient-triggered) gets the set pressure or volume.

segments.label: Objective 2 — Level of O₂ support
segments.html:
ASK — "Which two knobs set oxygenation in every mode?" → PEEP and FiO₂.
FiO₂PEEP
30%5
50%8
70%10
100%18–24

Reading the FiO₂/PEEP pair against the ARDSNet PEEP ladder tells you roughly how much oxygen support the patient needs — higher pair = sicker lungs.

segments.label: Objective 3 — Is ventilation appropriate?
segments.html:
Three steps: (1) check minute ventilation VE = VT × RR; (2) is it appropriate? — anchor to the pH (general rule keep pH 7.2–7.5), not the absolute VE; (3) vent- or patient-driven?

ASK — "Patient's measured RR exceeds the set rate and the pH is 7.5 — is the ventilator or the patient driving this?" If patient-triggered, look for patient causes of tachypnea: CNS injury, pain, salicylates, metabolic acidosis. If vent-driven, adjust settings.

segments.label: Objective 4 — Respiratory mechanics
segments.html:
Pressure-targeted modes: watch ΔP — good lungs need ΔP <10 cm H₂O to deliver ~6 mL/kg; a falling VT signals a mechanics problem. Volume-targeted modes: watch peak & plateau; low VT/VE points to a mechanics problem.

ASK — "Volume mode, ΔP > 10. What's your next move, and how do you localize the problem?"
Pearl — the inspiratory hold split. Do an inspiratory hold to get the plateau. Peak − Plateau > 3–5 = a resistance issue (mucus plug, kinked tube, bronchospasm). Elevated Plateau − PEEP (>10) = a compliance issue (pneumothorax, alveolar filling/edema, fibrosis, mainstem intubation, distended abdomen). (The deck's compliance box label was corrected from "Plat − Peak > 10" to "Plat − PEEP > 10" to match its own waveform annotation.)

segments.label: Retrieval close
segments.html: ASK — Cover the board. "List the four questions and, for each mode, the one square-wave clue and what you monitor." Then run the three cases as live practice.

cases.title: Case 1 — Square wave in pressure; set RR present; Pcontrol 20, PEEP 5, FiO₂ 21%. Peak 25, VT 345, RR 21, VE 7.2.
cases.reveal: Pressure control. Square wave in pressure = pressure mode; a mandatory RR = control mode.

cases.title: Case 2 — Square wave in flow; set RR; VT targeted, PEEP 18, FiO₂ 100%. Peak 27, VT 355, RR 25, VE 8.9.
cases.reveal: Volume control. Square wave in flow = volume mode; mandatory RR = control mode.

cases.title: Case 3 — Square wave in pressure with blunted, negative-deflection breaths; no mandatory RR. Psupport 10, PEEP 5, FiO₂ 30%. Peak 28, VT 355, RR 25, VE 8.9.
cases.reveal: Pressure support. Square wave in pressure = pressure mode; no mandatory RR = support mode. Blunted waves and negative deflections = patient-initiated breaths.

mcqs.stem: A ventilator shows a square waveform in the pressure tracing, a set respiratory rate of 16, and the clinician has set a target tidal volume; the screen also displays an "achieved VT." Which mode is this?
mcqs.exp:
C. Pressure waveform square (pressure-delivery) + a set tidal-volume target + an "achieved VT" readout = PRVC, the volume-targeted hybrid of pressure control.

A — classic volume control squares the flow wave and targets VT directly without an algorithm. B — pressure control targets pressure, not volume. D — pressure support has no mandatory rate.

mcqs.stem: A patient in AC-VC has a rising peak pressure. An inspiratory hold gives Peak 40, Plateau 22, PEEP 8. What is the problem?
opts: Resistance problem (e.g., mucus plug or bronchospasm)
mcqs.exp:
B. Peak − Plateau = 40 − 22 = 18 (>3–5) with a near-normal Plateau − PEEP (22 − 8 = 14) points to high airway resistance: mucus plug, kinked tube, or bronchospasm.

A — a compliance problem elevates the plateau (Plateau − PEEP markedly >10) with a smaller Peak−Plateau gap. C — a large Peak−Plateau gap is not normal. D — auto-PEEP is diagnosed with an expiratory hold and a flow tracing that doesn't return to zero, not this pattern.

mcqs.stem: Which two ventilator settings primarily determine the level of oxygenation support, in every mode?
mcqs.exp:
B. FiO₂ and PEEP set oxygenation regardless of mode; the ARDSNet ladder pairs them.

A — VT and RR drive ventilation (CO₂), not oxygenation. C/D — affect breath delivery/synchrony, not the primary oxygenation knobs.

mcqs.stem: A patient with ARDS is in AC-VC at 6 mL/kg PBW. Plateau is 24, PEEP 10. Per current evidence, which single parameter is most strongly associated with survival and should guide further adjustment?
mcqs.exp:
B. Driving pressure (here 24 − 10 = 14 cm H₂O) is the ventilator variable most strongly associated with survival in ARDS (Amato, NEJM 2015); target ≤14–15.

A — peak pressure includes a resistive component and is less predictive. C — FiO₂ tracks oxygenation, not VILI risk per se. D — mean airway pressure matters for oxygenation but is not the survival-linked lever here.

mcqs.stem: A ventilator shows no mandatory respiratory rate, a square wave in the pressure tracing with negative deflections preceding each breath, and the only set inspiratory parameter is a pressure above PEEP. What is the mode, and what is its most common use?
opts: Volume control — for ARDS lung protection
Pressure control — for precise airway-pressure control
Pressure support — for spontaneous breathing trials
PRVC — for combined volume target with pressure comfort
mcqs.exp:
C. No mandatory rate = a support mode; the set pressure above PEEP with patient-triggered (negative-deflection) breaths = pressure support, commonly used for SBTs.

A/B/D — all are control modes with a mandatory rate, which is absent here.

anki: The 4 questions for reading any ventilator?
(1) What mode? (2) What level of O2 support? (3) Is ventilation appropriate? (4) How are the respiratory mechanics?
Dials vs measured values on the screen?
Right-side dials = clinician-set; left-side numbers = ventilator-measured. They differ when the patient breathes over the set rate.
Square wave in PRESSURE means…?
A pressure-targeted mode: PC, PRVC, or PS.
Square wave in FLOW means…?
Classic volume control (modern vents can change the flow shape, so its absence does not exclude VC).
Control vs support mode — the tell?
Control mode = a set/mandatory RR. Support mode (PS) = no mandatory RR; patient triggers all breaths.
Pressure Control — target and monitor?
Target Pcontrol/deltaP; monitor tidal volume and minute ventilation.
Volume Control — target and monitor?
Target tidal volume; monitor peak and plateau pressure. Used for ARDS lung-protective VT.
PRVC — what is it?
Hybrid: set a target VT; the vent algorithm sets the pressure to achieve it. Square pressure wave; monitor peak/plateau + achieved VT.
Pressure Support — use and risk?
Spontaneous mode for SBTs/minimal support; no mandatory RR, so apnea triggers a backup control mode.
Which 2 settings set oxygenation in every mode?
FiO2 and PEEP (read against the ARDSNet PEEP/FiO2 ladder).
Minute ventilation equation and normal range?
VE = VT x RR. Normal 4-6 L/min (often higher in disease).
Target pH range when judging ventilation?
Generally keep pH 7.2-7.5.
Patient-driven tachypnea — causes to look for?
CNS injury/suppression, pain, salicylates, metabolic acidosis.
Volume mode, deltaP > 10 — next step?
Perform an inspiratory hold to obtain the plateau pressure.
Peak minus Plateau > 3-5 indicates…?
A RESISTANCE problem: mucus plug, kinked tube, bronchospasm.
Elevated Plateau minus PEEP (>10) indicates…?
A COMPLIANCE problem: pneumothorax, alveolar filling/edema, fibrosis, mainstem intubation, distended abdomen.
lessons.dot:
#Vent check (4 questions):
1) MODE: * [AC-VC / AC-PC / PRVC / PS] — clue * [square wave in flow=VC / pressure=PC,PRVC,PS]; * [mandatory RR=control / no set RR=support].
2) O2 SUPPORT: FiO2 *% / PEEP * cmH2O (ARDSNet ladder: 30/5, 50/8, 70/10, 100/18-24). SpO2 *% / PaO2 *.
3) VENTILATION: VT * mL (*mL/kg PBW), set RR * / measured RR *, VE * L/min (nl 4-6). ABG pH * / pCO2 * (goal pH 7.2-7.5). trigger: * [vent / patient].
4) MECHANICS: Ppeak * / (insp hold) Pplat * / PEEP *. ΔP(peak-PEEP) *; driving pressure(Pplat-PEEP) * cmH2O (goal ≤14-15). Peak-Plat * [>5 → resistance: plug/kink/bronchospasm] | Plat-PEEP * [>10 → compliance: PTX/edema/fibrosis/mainstem/abdomen].
A/P: lung-protective 6 mL/kg PBW, Pplat <30; titrate PEEP/FiO2 to SpO2 88-95%; * [SBT readiness / sedation target / proning if P:F<150].
takehome: Read any ventilator with the same four questions: mode, O₂ support, ventilation, mechanics.
Mode in two glances: square wave in pressure = pressure mode; square wave in flow = classic volume control. A mandatory RR = control mode; no mandatory RR = support (PS).
Set pressure → monitor VT/VE; set volume → monitor peak/plateau. FiO₂ and PEEP set oxygenation in every mode.
Mechanics: ΔP > 10 in a volume mode → inspiratory hold. Peak − Plateau gap = resistance; elevated Plateau − PEEP = compliance.

extras.label: Modern Framing — Lung-Protective Ventilation 2015–24 evidence
extras.html:
The mode-reading framework taught here is timeless and unchanged. For learners who go on to manage ARDS, layer in the current lung-protective targets (also on the appended deck slide):

Low tidal volume 6 mL/kg predicted body weight (range 4–8) remains the foundation — the original ARDSNet mortality benefit (NEJM 2000) is still standard of care. Plateau pressure <30 cm H₂O (many target ≤28).
Driving pressure (ΔP = Plateau − PEEP) is the ventilator variable most strongly associated with survival in ARDS (Amato et al., NEJM 2015; mediation analysis of 3,562 patients). Target ≤14–15 cm H₂O where achievable. Note the deck's quick ΔP = Peak − PEEP is a bedside screen; true driving pressure uses the plateau from an inspiratory hold, not peak.

Sources: ARDS Network, NEJM 2000;342:1301. Amato MBP et al., NEJM 2015;372:747. Lung-protective targets unchanged through 2024.

lessons.refs:
1. ARDS Network. Ventilation with lower tidal volumes for ALI/ARDS. NEJM 2000;342:1301–8.
  2. Amato MBP, et al. Driving pressure and survival in ARDS. NEJM 2015;372:747–55. (newly cited)
  3. ARDSNet ventilator protocol / PEEP-FiO₂ ladder (ardsnet.org).
  4. Original TeachIM lesson teaching script and references — retained from the source page.

lessons.title: Work-up of Anemia
lessons.subtitle: Optimized lesson package · Facilitator-led interactive chalk talk for IM residents · 45–50 min (modular: underproduction 25 min / overproduction 25 min)
lessons.credit: Adapted from TeachIM.org — Work-up of Anemia (Krimmel-Morrison, Keel; exec. eds. Zhang, Fainstad; Mar 2020, deck rev. Dec 2022), CC BY-NC 4.0. Optimized June 2026: framework verified current, one deck typo corrected, iron-cutoff & TMA refinements added, pedagogy restructured, assessments added. Companion deck: Work_up_of_Anemia_Optimized.pptx (original interactive board, surgically corrected + 3 appended slides).
objectives: Construct a framework for the evaluation of anemia (underproduction vs. overproduction).
Differentiate underproduction anemias using the MCV and iron studies.
Differentiate immune-mediated from non-immune causes of hemolysis.
Interpret the reticulocyte index, hemolysis labs, DAT, and peripheral smear to reach a diagnosis.

prework.q: Q1. What single test best separates a marrow problem from blood loss/hemolysis, and how do you read it?
prework.a: The reticulocyte index (corrected retic count). RI <2 = underproduction (the marrow isn't responding); RI ≥3 = appropriate response to blood loss, hemolysis, or sequestration. (Beware: a concurrent underproduction process can falsely lower the RI.)

prework.q: Q2. Ferritin is 18 ng/mL in a patient with active rheumatoid arthritis. Does that confirm iron deficiency?
prework.a: Yes — a low ferritin (<30) is specific for iron deficiency even in inflammation, because inflammation only raises ferritin. The harder problem is the reverse: a normal/high ferritin does NOT exclude iron deficiency when inflammation is present (use TSAT <20%, sTfR, or a hepcidin/iron trial).

prework.q: Q3. Schistocytes + thrombocytopenia on a smear — what must you not miss?
prework.a: A thrombotic microangiopathy (TTP/HUS) — a hematologic emergency. Send ADAMTS13 and start plasma exchange when TTP is likely; check coags/fibrinogen to separate DIC and consider HELLP/atypical HUS.

segments.label: Hook (2 min)
segments.html: Put a CBC on the board: Hgb 7.9, MCV 89, with the rest blank. "Same Hgb, same MCV — but two of these patients need a GI scope, one needs plasma exchange today, and one just needs IV fluids stopped. How do you tell them apart?" The retic index is the fork in the road.

segments.label: Objective 1 — The framework: retic index first
segments.html:
ASK: "Before MCV, before iron studies — what's the first branch point in any anemia?" Reveal: underproduction (marrow problem, RI <2) vs. overproduction (RI ≥3: blood loss, hemolysis, sequestration). Hemodilution (aggressive IVF inpatient, hypervolemia/HF outpatient) drops the Hgb without deranging the RI.
Pearl: The retic index can be falsely low/normal if an underproduction process coexists with hemolysis or bleeding — don't let a "normal" retic talk you out of an obvious bleed.

segments.label: Objective 2 — Underproduction: MCV + iron studies
segments.html:
ASK: "Sort underproduction by MCV." Build the three buckets (note overlaps):
MCVCauses
Macrocytic (>100)Megaloblastic (B12/folate; drugs — hydroxyurea, allopurinol, HIV meds), liver disease/ETOH, severe hypothyroidism, primary marrow pathology (MDS, esp. MCV >120)
Normocytic (80–100)Anemia of chronic inflammation, anemia of CKD (↓EPO), early iron deficiency, acute blood loss, primary marrow pathology (aplastic anemia, leukemia, pure RBC aplasia, myeloma)
Microcytic (<80)Iron deficiency, thalassemia, sideroblastic anemia (congenital; acquired — lead, Zn, INH/linezolid), anemia of chronic inflammation
ASK: "When other cell lines are also down, where does your differential shift?" → toward primary marrow pathology (MDS, aplastic anemia, leukemia, myeloma).
ASK: "Read the four iron studies — how do iron deficiency and ACD differ?" Reveal the toolbox:
Serum FeTIBC% satFerritin
Iron deficiencyLowHighLow (<20% suggestive, <10% very)Low (<30 ng/mL diagnostic)
ACD/inflammationLowLow–normalLow–normal (pseudo-normal if TIBC low)Normal–high (acute-phase)
ThalassemiaNormal–highNormal–highNormal–highNormal
Pearl: 20–85% of patients with ACD also have iron deficiency. A very low TSAT is the clue; sTfR, hepcidin, or a documented response to an iron trial settle it. Ferritin <30 is diagnostic of iron deficiency even in inflammation — inflammation only raises ferritin, never lowers it.

segments.label: Objective 3 & 4 — Overproduction: confirm hemolysis, then DAT + smear
segments.html:
ASK: "RI is ≥3 — what are the three buckets?" Blood loss (GI/"floor", long bones, thorax, abdomen/retroperitoneum), hemolysis, splenic sequestration.
ASK: "Which labs confirm hemolysis?" ↑LDH, ↓haptoglobin, ↑indirect bilirubin, and the smear. Then the DAT (direct Coombs) splits immune from non-immune.
DATCategoryKey typesSmear clue
PositiveImmune (AIHA)Warm (IgG) — cancer/lymphoma, autoimmune (SLE), drugs; Cold (IgM) — lymphoma, mycoplasma, mono (also PNH, syphilis)Spherocytes
NegativeNon-immuneMAHA (fragmentation) vs non-MAHA (intrinsic/extrinsic to RBC)Schistocytes (MAHA)
ASK: "Non-immune, DAT-negative — how do you split it?" MAHA (schistocytes → if ↓platelets, think TMA: primary TTP/HUS, drug-induced; secondary DIC [↓fibrinogen], HELLP; other — malignant HTN, prosthetic valves/LVAD/TIPS, scleroderma renal crisis) vs non-MAHA (intrinsic — membrane [HS, elliptocytosis, stomatocytosis, PNH], Hb [sickle, thalassemia], metabolism [G6PD, PK deficiency]; extrinsic — liver disease/hypersplenism, infection [malaria/Babesia/Bartonella; Clostridium/leishmania toxins], oxidative-stress meds).
Emergency: Schistocytes + thrombocytopenia = possible TMA. Calculate the PLASMIC score, send ADAMTS13 before plasma exchange, and don't wait for the result if TTP is likely — confirmed TTP is treated with PEX + steroids + caplacizumab.

segments.label: Close (2 min)
segments.html: Hide the board. Have the group rebuild the algorithm from the top: retic index → (underproduction → MCV + iron studies) or (overproduction → hemolysis labs → DAT → smear). Then show the take-home slide.

cases.title: Case 1 — Fevers, menorrhagia, gum bleeding
cases.stem: 33-year-old with 2 weeks of fevers/chills, nausea/vomiting, non-bloody diarrhea; + fatigue, menorrhagia, gum bleeding. Exam: blood blister on the lip. Hgb 7.9, MCV 89, platelets low, Cr 1.0, fibrinogen normal. Retic index 3.5. LDH 1508, haptoglobin <30, Tbili 1.0.
cases.reveal: Overproduction (RI 3.5) with hemolysis (↑LDH, ↓haptoglobin); smear shows schistocytes → MAHA. With concurrent thrombocytopenia and normal fibrinogen, this is a primary TMA.

cases.title: Case 2 — Fall with femur fracture, confusion, constipation
cases.stem: 56-year-old with a ground-level fall and femur fracture; weakness, fatigue, mild confusion, constipation, easy bruising. Cr 1.6, calcium 11.5. Retic index 1.1, MCV 89. Iron studies show ↓Fe, ↓%sat, ↓ferritin. LDH normal, smear shows rouleaux.
cases.reveal: Multiple myeloma — the classic CRAB tetrad (hyperCalcemia, Renal dysfunction, Anemia, Bony lesions). Rouleaux reflects the paraprotein.

cases.title: Case 3 — SLE, chest pain, jaundice
cases.stem: 45-year-old with SLE, chest pain and dyspnea; + jaundice, joint pains, fatigue. Retic index 4.5, MCV 115. LDH 2504, haptoglobin <30, Tbili 4.6. DAT positive, IgG positive. Smear: spherocytes.
cases.reveal: Warm autoimmune hemolytic anemia (IgG, DAT-positive, spherocytes) associated with SLE. The high MCV reflects reticulocytosis; iron studies suggest concurrent anemia of chronic disease.

cases.title: Case 4 — 6 months of progressive fatigue, no overt bleeding
cases.stem: 73-year-old, minimal prior care, 6 months of fatigue and exertional dyspnea; normal diet, no overt blood loss. Retic index 1.1, MCV 68. Iron studies: ↓Fe, %sat 1%, TIBC normal, ferritin 150 ng/mL. LDH 254, smear: hypochromic RBCs.
cases.reveal: Concurrent iron deficiency (very low % saturation) AND anemia of chronic disease (normal TIBC, elevated ferritin from inflammation masking the depleted stores).

mcqs.stem: A 68-year-old man with rheumatoid arthritis has Hgb 10.2, MCV 84. Ferritin 95 ng/mL, TSAT 9%, TIBC low-normal. Which is the best next step to resolve whether iron deficiency coexists with his anemia of chronic disease?
opts: Conclude pure anemia of chronic disease; no iron needed
Soluble transferrin receptor (sTfR) or a trial of iron with repeat labs
Bone marrow biopsy for iron stores
mcqs.exp: B. A TSAT of 9% is very low and suggests concurrent iron deficiency even though ferritin (an acute-phase reactant) is "normal" from his RA. sTfR (or sTfR/log-ferritin index), hepcidin, or an empiric iron trial with repeat CBC/iron studies is the practical next step. Calling it pure ACD (A) misses treatable iron deficiency; marrow biopsy (C) is invasive and rarely needed; an ESA (D) is premature before repleting iron.

mcqs.stem: A 28-year-old woman has Hgb 8.0, retic index 4, ↑LDH, ↓haptoglobin, platelets 22k, normal PT/PTT and fibrinogen, and schistocytes on smear. What is the most appropriate immediate action?
opts: Transfuse platelets to >50k before any procedure
Send ADAMTS13 and start plasma exchange
Give vitamin K and fresh frozen plasma for DIC
mcqs.exp: B. MAHA (schistocytes, hemolysis) + thrombocytopenia with normal coagulation points to a primary TMA, most urgently TTP. Send ADAMTS13 and begin plasma exchange without waiting for the result when TTP is likely (PLASMIC score); confirmed TTP gets PEX + steroids + caplacizumab. Platelet transfusion (A) is generally contraindicated in TTP (can worsen thrombosis). Normal fibrinogen/coags argue against DIC (D); this is not primarily an infection (C).

mcqs.stem: A 35-year-old develops back pain and dark urine 2 days after starting trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole. Hgb fell from 14 to 9, retic index 5, ↑LDH, ↓haptoglobin, DAT negative, smear shows bite cells. What is the most likely diagnosis?
mcqs.exp: B. Oxidant-drug-triggered, DAT-negative intravascular hemolysis with bite cells (and Heinz bodies) is classic for G6PD deficiency — a defect in RBC metabolism. Warm AIHA (A) is DAT-positive with spherocytes; hereditary spherocytosis (C) is DAT-negative but chronic with spherocytes and a family history, not acute drug-triggered bite cells; MAHA (D) shows schistocytes, not bite cells.

mcqs.stem: A 22-year-old man of Mediterranean descent has Hgb 10.5, MCV 66, normal RDW, RBC count high-normal, and normal iron studies. Which is most likely?
mcqs.exp: B. Marked microcytosis out of proportion to a mild anemia, a normal/high RBC count, a normal RDW, and normal iron studies are classic for thalassemia trait (confirm with hemoglobin electrophoresis). Iron deficiency (A) gives low iron studies and a high RDW; ACD (C) is usually normocytic with low TIBC; sideroblastic anemia (D) typically shows elevated ferritin/iron and ringed sideroblasts.

mcqs.stem: An inpatient's Hgb drops from 11 to 8.5 over 12 hours after aggressive IV fluid resuscitation. Retic index is 1.5, MCV normal, no bleeding source identified, LDH and haptoglobin normal. What best explains the drop?
mcqs.exp: B. A rapid Hgb fall after large-volume IV fluids, with a non-elevated retic index and no hemolysis labs, is dilutional (hemodilution) — the red cell mass hasn't actually changed. A true bleed (A) or hemolysis (C) would typically drive a brisk reticulocytosis (RI ≥3) and, for hemolysis, ↑LDH/↓haptoglobin. ACD (D) develops over weeks, not hours.

anki: First branch point in any anemia work-up?
Reticulocyte index: <2 = underproduction (marrow); >=3 = overproduction (blood loss, hemolysis, sequestration).
What drops Hgb without deranging the retic index?
Hemodilution -- aggressive IVF (inpatient) or hypervolemia/HF (outpatient).
Caveat to the retic index?
May be falsely low/normal if an underproduction process coexists with bleeding/hemolysis.
Macrocytic underproduction causes?
Megaloblastic (B12/folate, drugs: HU/allopurinol/HIV meds), liver/ETOH, severe hypothyroidism, primary marrow (MDS, esp. MCV >120).
Microcytic underproduction causes?
Iron deficiency, thalassemia, sideroblastic (lead, Zn, INH/linezolid), anemia of chronic inflammation.
When do you shift toward primary marrow pathology?
When other cell lines are also affected (MDS, aplastic anemia, leukemia, myeloma).
Iron studies in iron deficiency?
Low Fe, high TIBC, low %sat (<20% suggestive, <10% very), low ferritin (<30 ng/mL diagnostic).
Iron studies in anemia of chronic disease?
Low Fe, low-normal TIBC, low-normal %sat, normal-high ferritin (acute-phase reactant).
Does a low ferritin confirm iron deficiency in inflammation?
Yes -- inflammation only raises ferritin; ferritin <30 is diagnostic even with active inflammation.
ACD + concurrent iron deficiency -- how common and how to tell?
20-85% of ACD. Clue: very low TSAT; confirm with sTfR, hepcidin, or response to an iron trial.
Labs that confirm hemolysis?
High LDH, low haptoglobin, high indirect bilirubin, + peripheral smear.
What does the DAT (direct Coombs) distinguish?
Immune (DAT-positive, antibodies on RBCs -> AIHA) vs non-immune (DAT-negative) hemolysis.
Warm vs cold AIHA?
Warm = IgG (cancer/lymphoma, SLE, drugs); Cold = IgM (lymphoma, mycoplasma, mono; also PNH, syphilis). Smear: spherocytes.
Smear clue for MAHA, and what it means with low platelets?
Schistocytes; with thrombocytopenia -> thrombotic microangiopathy (TTP/HUS) -- an emergency.
Primary vs secondary TMA?
Primary: TTP/HUS, drug-induced. Secondary/TMA-like: DIC (low fibrinogen), HELLP. Other: malignant HTN, prosthetic valve/LVAD/TIPS, scleroderma renal crisis.
Suspected TTP -- what to do?
PLASMIC score, send ADAMTS13 BEFORE plasma exchange, start PEX if TTP likely; treat confirmed TTP with PEX + steroids + caplacizumab.
Intrinsic non-immune (non-MAHA) hemolysis categories?
Membrane (HS, elliptocytosis, stomatocytosis, PNH), Hb (sickle, thalassemia), metabolism (G6PD, pyruvate kinase deficiency).
New iron deficiency in an older adult -- next step?
GI evaluation for occult blood loss (colon cancer until proven otherwise).
lessons.dot:
#?Anemia: * yo, Hgb * (baseline *), MCV *, plt *, retic % *, retic index *. Acuity [acute/chronic], sx [fatigue/DOE/lightheaded/none], bleeding hx [GI/GU/menorrhagia/none]. HDS.
- STEP 1 retic index: <2 -> UNDERPRODUCTION (marrow) | >=3 -> OVERPRODUCTION (bleed/hemolysis/sequestration). r/o hemodilution (recent IVF/hypervolemia -> RI not elevated)
- UNDERPRODUCTION by MCV:
  - macro (>100): B12/folate, drugs [HU/allopurinol/HIV], liver/ETOH, hypothyroid, MDS (esp >120) -> B12, folate, TSH, retic, smear, ?marrow
  - normo (80-100): ACD, CKD (low EPO), early Fe-def, primary marrow (if other lines down) -> iron studies, Cr, ?SPEP/FLC, ?marrow
  - micro (<80): Fe-def, thalassemia, sideroblastic [Pb/Zn/INH/linezolid], ACD -> iron studies, ?Hgb electrophoresis
  - iron studies: Fe-def = Fe low / TIBC high / TSAT low (<20%, <10% strong) / ferritin <30 dx. ACD = Fe low / TIBC low-nl / TSAT low-nl / ferritin nl-high (APR). *ferritin <30 dx even in inflammation*; ACD+Fe-def common (20-85%) -> sTfR/hepcidin or iron trial
  - *new Fe-def in older adult -> GI w/u for occult blood loss*
- OVERPRODUCTION: confirm hemolysis [LDH high, hapto low, indirect bili high, smear] -> DAT:
  - DAT+ (immune/AIHA): warm IgG [cancer/lymphoma, SLE, drugs] / cold IgM [lymphoma, mycoplasma, mono; PNH, syphilis]; smear spherocytes -> heme c/s, steroids for warm, avoid cold exposure
  - DAT- (non-immune): MAHA (schistocytes) -> if low plt = TMA [PLASMIC score, ADAMTS13 BEFORE PEX, start PEX if TTP likely; DIC if low fibrinogen; HELLP] *EMERGENCY*; non-MAHA -> intrinsic [membrane HS/ellipto/stomato/PNH, Hb sickle/thal, metabolism G6PD/PK] vs extrinsic [liver/hypersplenism, infection malaria/Babesia/Bartonella/Clostridium, oxidant meds]
  - bleed w/u if RI high + no hemolysis: GI (floor), long bones, thorax, abd/retroperitoneum
- mgmt: treat cause; transfuse if symptomatic/threshold *; iron repletion [PO ferrous sulfate qod / IV ferric carboxymaltose or derisomaltose if malabsorption/CKD/intolerance]; *
takehome: Start every anemia work-up with the reticulocyte index: <2 = underproduction (marrow problem), ≥3 = overproduction (blood loss, hemolysis, or sequestration).

For underproduction, use the MCV (macro/normo/microcytic) plus iron studies; ferritin <30 ng/mL is diagnostic of iron deficiency, but a normal/high ferritin doesn't exclude it in inflammation (use TSAT, sTfR, or an iron trial).

For overproduction, confirm hemolysis (↑LDH, ↓haptoglobin, ↑indirect bilirubin) then use the DAT + smear: DAT-positive = autoimmune (warm IgG / cold IgM, spherocytes); DAT-negative with schistocytes = MAHA.
MAHA with thrombocytopenia is a hematologic emergency — think TMA (TTP/HUS), send ADAMTS13, and don't delay plasma exchange when TTP is likely.
Concurrent iron deficiency + anemia of chronic disease is common, and new iron deficiency in an older adult warrants a GI work-up for occult blood loss.

TypeChange
Currency checkVerified June 2026. The diagnostic framework (retic index → MCV + iron studies / hemolysis labs → DAT + smear) is timeless and remains standard. No guideline reversed the teaching; refinements added as context, original teaching slides unchanged.
Correction (deck)Slide 6, non-MAHA membrane defects: "stomacytosis" → "stomatocytosis" (the run was already spell-flagged). Single-run XML edit; all 10 original animated/click-to-reveal slides and graphics preserved.
Content addedInflammation-adjusted iron interpretation (ferritin <30 diagnostic even in inflammation; use TSAT/sTfR/hepcidin or an iron trial when ACD + iron deficiency coexist; Weiss Blood 2019, Camaschella Blood 2019); IV iron (ferric carboxymaltose/derisomaltose) preference; PLASMIC score + ADAMTS13-before-plasma-exchange and caplacizumab for confirmed TTP. All source-cited.
PedagogyAdded 5-min pre-work; same-CBC hook; timed segments keyed to the board's cursor prompts; MCV, iron-study, and DAT tables; retrieval-based close; the four original cases kept and converted to click-to-reveal.
AssessmentAdded 5 ABIM-style MCQs with explanations and 18 Anki-importable cards.
Clinical toolAdded attending-level EMR dotphrase — the full retic-index → MCV/iron-study and hemolysis → DAT/smear algorithm with repletion and TMA-emergency steps. (Dotphrase lives in the HTML only — not on the one-pager.)
One-pagerAdded dense single-page printable diagnostic reference (Work_up_of_Anemia_OnePager.pdf/.html) — the retic-index fork, MCV buckets, iron-study table, and the hemolysis/DAT/smear algorithm; two-column Letter layout.
DeckOriginal interactive board kept intact (13 slides total; 2 worksheet slides hidden in the original): 10 original animated slides + appended framework-update, take-home, and attribution/changelog slides.
Companion filesPage-linked companion reviewed (no errors found) and copied into this folder unchanged, linked from the launcher: learner handout with fill-in schematic (Anemia-Handout-12.22.pdf). Note: Anemia-Presenter-1.pptx and Anemia-Learner-board-1.pptx in Source Files are NOT the page-linked deck (embed names Anemia-12.22.pptx) → excluded per the no-guess rule; Anemia-flowchart.jpg is the page cover image → excluded.
Human review neededNone. Iron cutoffs and TMA thresholds are context-dependent — apply local lab references and have content clinician-reviewed before teaching.
